question:
A 52‐year‐old man with Addison’s disease on lifelong corticosteroid replacement and a history of lateral epicondylitis presented with a 7‐day history of severe redness around his right elbow accompanied by intense burning and stinging. The redness began after he had been gardening on a cloudy summer day. Over the next days, his elbow became swollen, blisters formed and then ruptured, leaving crusted lesions. His general practitioner suspected cellulitis and prescribed dicloxacillin. Two days after starting antibiotics, he developed an itchy rash on his chest and abdomen. On examination, there was a bright red, edematous, crusted erythema over the right elbow and a maculopapular rash on the trunk. Laboratory studies, including C‐reactive protein and complete blood count, were within normal limits.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

An 18-year-old woman presented with a 1-year history of slowly enlarging gingival overgrowth in the left posterior mandible that interfered with chewing but was painless. Intraoral examination revealed a 3×4 cm exophytic mass extending from the left mandibular second molar to the retromolar pad, buccally into the vestibule and inferiorly to the floor of the mouth. Panoramic radiograph showed a well-defined radiolucency around the impacted left third molar. The lesion and the impacted tooth were excised en bloc. 

Grossly, the specimen included both intraosseous and extraosseous components. Histologic examination demonstrated cords, interconnecting strands, and islands of odontogenic epithelium embedded in a cell-rich, myxoid mesenchymal stroma. The epithelial strands and cords were lined by a double layer of cuboidal cells. The islands exhibited peripheral tall columnar cells with polarized nuclei and clear, vacuolated cytoplasm surrounding central stellate reticulum-like cells. Juxtaepithelial hyalinization was noted around some islands. No hard-tissue (enamel or dentin) formation was seen. The cellularity varied, with focal hypercellular areas and other sparsely cellular, myxoid regions. A thin fibrous capsule partially surrounded the lesion. No cytologic atypia or mitotic figures were observed on multiple sections.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 37-year-old man presented with a 3-month history of progressive skin thickening, initially on his torso and then spreading diffusely, accompanied by a 20–30-lb weight loss and fatigue. He denied Raynaud’s phenomenon, dyspnea, or wheezing. His blood pressure at presentation was 100–110 mmHg systolic, with a serum creatinine of 0.8 mg/dL. He had a history of treated hepatitis B without active disease.

Serologic studies showed a negative antinuclear antibody, negative anti–Smith and anti–ribonucleoprotein antibodies, and low-level anti–topoisomerase I (3–4 AU/mL). Nailfold capillaroscopy was suggestive of systemic sclerosis, and a skin biopsy was read as suspicious for morphea versus systemic sclerosis. Echocardiography revealed no pulmonary hypertension or pericardial effusion. He was started on mycophenolate mofetil.

An IgG λ monoclonal protein of 1.1 g/dL was detected. Bone marrow biopsy showed 10% λ-restricted plasma cells without high-risk cytogenetics besides 1q and 5q gains, monosomy 13, and 14q deletions. Three months after presentation, for unclear reasons, he was started on high-dose prednisone (60 mg daily). Shortly thereafter, his systolic blood pressure increased to 140–150 mmHg and serum creatinine rose to 1.1 mg/dL. He developed blurry vision; ophthalmologic examination revealed cotton-wool spots. He received two intravitreal injections of bevacizumab (1.25 mg each).

One week after the injections, he was admitted with severe hypertension (systolic blood pressures 200–220 mmHg), a rise in serum creatinine to 1.4 mg/dL, and new proteinuria (urine protein-creatinine ratio 1 g/g). Renal ultrasound with Doppler showed normal-sized kidneys and no evidence of renal artery stenosis. Given the abrupt hypertension, worsening renal function, proteinuria, recent corticosteroid exposure, and intravitreal VEGF blockade, scleroderma renal crisis was suspected, and a complement-mediated thrombotic microangiopathy related to VEGF inhibition could not be ruled out. A renal biopsy was planned after blood pressure control.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

options.C: Chronic kidney disease with hypertensive nephropathy

question:
A previously healthy 5‐year‐old girl presented with 9 hours of intermittent, moderate–severity epigastric pain radiating to the right lower quadrant. The pain was unchanged by position and was associated with multiple episodes of nonbloody vomiting. She was afebrile, had normal urination and bowel movements, and reported a similar, self‐limited episode 1 month earlier.

On examination, she was alert, without signs of systemic infection. Abdominal palpation elicited tenderness in the epigastrium; there was no guarding or rebound. Murphy’s sign was positive, and there was no jaundice.

Laboratory studies showed normal hepatic and biliary function tests and an elevated C-reactive protein level of 30.2 mg/L. A supine abdominal radiograph was unremarkable.

Initial abdominal ultrasound demonstrated an enlarged gallbladder (54 × 34 mm) with a 3.2 mm wall thickness, pericholecystic fluid, increased pericholecystic fat, and no gallstones or intraluminal nodules. On repeat ultrasound 24 hours later, the gallbladder measured 53 × 33 mm with a 3.1 mm wall, lacked vascular flow, contained biliary sludge, and showed a cone-shaped hypoechoic structure at the neck; the fundus was displaced to the left of its fossa and moved with patient repositioning.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed a 53.5 × 22.8 × 31.5 mm gallbladder with an irregular, poorly enhancing wall, an intraluminal hyperdense area suggestive of hemorrhage, a 3 mm hyperdense nodule, fundus deviation to the left of the gallbladder bed, pericholecystic fluid and fat stranding, and focal hepatic perfusion abnormalities.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 51-year-old woman with Crohn’s disease on infliximab presented with a 2-day history of a bullous rash on her left arm, axilla, and lateral chest wall accompanied by subjective fever. Two days before presentation, she received her second dose of the recombinant adjuvant Shingrix vaccine. She denied new medications or topical products and had no prior similar rashes. Her Crohn’s disease was at baseline with intermittent loose stools. On examination, there was diffuse erythema and swelling from the midchest to the axilla and upper arm, with multiple bullae, some with central dusky areas; mucosal surfaces were spared. She was referred to dermatology and underwent punch biopsy; PCR testing of a bulla for herpes simplex virus types 1 and 2 and varicella zoster virus was negative.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 69-year-old woman with metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (peritoneal and hepatic metastases) enrolled in a trial of SBP-101 combined with nab-paclitaxel and gemcitabine presented with a 6-month history of progressive blurred vision and visual field loss. She had no diabetes or hypertension. Baseline ophthalmologic evaluation before SBP-101 showed 20/20 visual acuity (VA) in both eyes, normal intraocular pressures, and normal macular structure on optical coherence tomography (OCT). After the second monthly cycle of SBP-101, her VA fell to 20/50 in the right eye and 20/40 in the left eye. Fundus examination revealed pigmentary changes in the macula, with punctate hypo- and hyperautofluorescent spots on blue-autofluorescence. OCT demonstrated ellipsoid zone disruption and hyperreflective material between the Bruch membrane and retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) in both eyes. SBP-101 was discontinued, and intravitreal dexamethasone was administered bilaterally without improvement. One month later, VA was 20/50 OD and 20/32 OS. Full-field electroretinography (ERG) showed normal scotopic responses and photopic amplitudes at the lower limit of normal. Serum anti-recoverin and anti-enolase autoantibodies were within normal limits. Over the next five months, VA declined to 20/5000 OD and 20/4000 OS, with photopsias and severely impaired central vision. Ultra–wide-field fundus photographs revealed bilateral roundish macular areas of irregular pigment loss extending beyond the vascular arcades, with scattered dot-blot hemorrhages. Blue-autofluorescence showed central hypoautofluorescence surrounded by a ring of alternating hyper- and hypoautofluorescent dots. OCT of the macula demonstrated incomplete RPE and outer retinal atrophy with signal hypertransmission to the choroid, ellipsoid zone disruption, and perifoveal hyperreflective sub-RPE deposits; choroidal thickness was reduced. Optic nerve OCT and RNFL thickness were normal. Visual fields showed bilateral central relative scotomas (VFI 26% OD, 31% OS). ERG per ISCEV standards revealed preserved dark-adapted 0.01 cd·s/m2 and mixed rod–cone responses, relatively preserved a-waves on light-adapted single flashes, but reduced amplitudes and slightly prolonged implicit times on 30 Hz flicker.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old man of African descent was brought to the emergency department unresponsive after a generalized tonic–clonic seizure. On examination he was comatose with a blood pressure of 178/121 mmHg, heart rate 105 beats/min, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, and a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 3 of 15; he required intubation and ICU admission. He had no history of epilepsy, head trauma, recreational drug use, smoking, or alcohol consumption. His medical history was notable for resistant hypertension diagnosed 20 years ago, treated with atenolol 50 mg, spironolactone 100 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, and valsartan 160 mg daily, with poor compliance, and for type II diabetes mellitus treated with a sulfonylurea. Family history was positive for hypertension but negative for renal disease or adrenal tumors. Body mass index was 31. Thyroid and visual-field examinations were normal, and there were no cushingoid or acromegaly features. Initial laboratory studies showed a potassium level of 2.6 mmol/L (normal 3.6–5.1), creatinine 113 μmol/L, metabolic alkalosis on blood gas analysis, urea 5.7 mmol/L, and creatine kinase 2.2 μg/L. Urinalysis was negative for leukocyte esterase and nitrites. A chest radiograph showed no signs of heart failure. ECG demonstrated sinus rhythm. Troponin-T and BNP were within normal limits. Calcium was 2.46 mmol/L. CT of the brain showed no lesions, and MRI the next day revealed no mass-occupying lesion; EEG was normal. Repeat potassium remained low at 2.6 mmol/L; plasma aldosterone was 36.2 ng/dL (normal 2.8–15.8), and plasma renin was < 0.081 ng/dL (normal 0.4–2.3). A 24-hour urine collection excluded Cushing’s syndrome and pheochromocytoma. Renal ultrasound and cardiac echocardiography excluded renovascular and cardiac causes of secondary hypertension.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 53-year-old man with no significant medical history presented with a headache. Neurologic examination revealed that he was alert and had no motor or sensory deficits, but he noted narrowing of the left lower visual field quadrant. Noncontrast CT of the head showed a 5-cm, well-circumscribed, high-density mass in the right parietal region without calcification. MRI demonstrated an extra-axial lesion that was iso‐intense on T1-weighted imaging, uniformly very low in signal on T2-weighted imaging, and showed marked enhancement after gadolinium administration. There was no diffusion restriction after correction for a T2 blackout effect. The mass was separate from the ventricles and was associated with a large anterior cyst whose contents had a signal different from cerebrospinal fluid; the cyst wall did not enhance, consistent with a peritumoral cyst, and there was surrounding edema. The tumor contacted the dura mater convexity and the falx cerebri but lacked an obvious attachment or dural tail sign. Time-resolved MR angiography (4D-TRAK) showed no tumor staining or feeding vessels, suggesting low vascularity. Routine blood tests, including tumor markers, were unremarkable. Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed no extracranial source of malignancy.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 69-year-old woman reported a 5-year history of recurrent febrile episodes every 6–8 weeks. Each episode featured a temperature of 38.5–40 °C lasting up to 7 days, accompanied by sore throat, cervical and submandibular lymphadenopathy and pain, headache, myalgia, fatigue, and dysphagia. She was asymptomatic between episodes and could predict their onset. She had a history of childhood tonsillitis treated with tonsillectomy. She worked in black metal analysis for over 30 years.  

During episodes, laboratory studies showed CRP 64 mg/L, serum amyloid A 140 mg/L, leukocytes 2.94 × 10^9/L, and neutrophils 3.19 × 10^9/L; procalcitonin, immunoglobulins, and ANA titres were normal. HIV, CMV, EBV, and other infection indicators were negative, and blood and throat cultures showed no growth. PET-CT demonstrated increased glucose metabolism in the sigmoid colon, but colonoscopy was normal. Pharyngoscopy revealed pharyngitis with white exudate. Ultrasound showed enlarged bilateral cervical, submandibular, and supraclavicular lymph nodes (up to 21 × 6 mm), and a supraclavicular lymph node biopsy revealed reactive hyperplasia. Bone marrow biopsy and cytogenetics were unremarkable. Despite multiple courses of antibiotics, febrile episodes persisted.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 65-year-old man was brought to the emergency department because of progressive fatigue, diffuse myalgia, and altered mental status. His medical history included chronic ischemic heart disease status post coronary-artery bypass grafting, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and stage 3 chronic kidney disease.  

On arrival, he reported several days of worsening leg and shoulder pain with an inability to walk independently. Vital signs showed hypotension, and an electrocardiogram revealed atrial fibrillation, a left bundle-branch block, ST-segment depression, and peaked, high T waves. Laboratory tests demonstrated a troponin T level of 532 ng/L (normal < 14 ng/L) and NT-proBNP of 5579 ng/L (normal < 125 ng/L). A temporary pacemaker was placed because of bradyarrhythmia.  

Urgent coronary angiography showed severe three-vessel atheromatous disease with an 80% stenosis in the left anterior descending artery and sub-occlusive lesions in the right coronary artery, which was successfully stented. During the procedure, he developed cardiogenic shock, treated with vasopressors and inotropic support. Arterial blood gas analysis disclosed a metabolic acidosis (pH 7.26) and a serum potassium concentration of 7.5 mmol/L.  

He was admitted to the intensive care unit. Further laboratory results included a creatine phosphokinase level > 22,000 U/L (normal 39–308 U/L), a myoglobin level > 30,000 μg/L (normal < 72 μg/L), aspartate aminotransferase 1406 U/L (normal < 50 U/L), lactate dehydrogenase 1969 U/L (normal 50–250 U/L), blood urea nitrogen 304 mg/dL (normal 10–71 mg/dL), and serum creatinine 6.58 mg/dL (normal 0.67–1.17 mg/dL).

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 32-year-old man presented with excessive tearing of the left eye and mild ocular pain. Over the previous 4–5 months he had noticed progressive left-eyelid drooping and intermittent diplopia. He reported a mild left-sided head trauma 1.5 years earlier but had no other medical history or family history of neurological or autoimmune disease.

On ophthalmologic examination, visual acuity, intraocular pressure, and funduscopy were normal. The left eyelid was ptotic, and there was limitation of adduction, elevation, and depression of the left eye, with a dilated left pupil. No other neurological deficits were found. Fluorescein angiography, indocyanine green chorioangiography, and optical coherence tomography were unremarkable. The patient noted that his ptosis fluctuated during the day, with marked improvement when washing his face with cold water.

To evaluate for myasthenia gravis, an ice pack was applied to the left eyelid for 2 minutes, after which his ptosis improved significantly, with near-complete eyelid opening; the improvement lasted only a few minutes.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

An 8-year-old girl was brought to the emergency department with reduced consciousness and an 8-day history of high fever, accompanied by vomiting, epigastric pain, and a mild cough. On arrival, her pulse was 112/min, respiratory rate 19/min, blood pressure 93/62 mm Hg, and temperature 36 °C. Physical examination was unremarkable. Neurologically, she was drowsy and disoriented (GCS 14/15), with impaired memory, visual hallucinations, and an unsteady gait; deep-tendon reflexes were normal, and plantar responses were flexor. Initial blood tests showed acute respiratory acidosis and mildly elevated liver enzymes; serum sodium was 135 mEq/L, BUN 11 mg/dL, and creatinine 0.49 mg/dL. Chest radiography revealed a slight increase in bilateral lung markings. Brain CT was normal. 

In the pediatric ICU, lumbar puncture showed CSF with protein 26 mg/dL, glucose 82 mg/dL, and 5 lymphocytes/μL. Twelve hours after admission, she developed complex partial seizures with secondary generalization and was started on phenytoin and acyclovir. By day 3, seizures increased in frequency with auditory and visual hallucinations; MRI was normal. Serologies were negative for HSV I/II, CMV, and EBV IgM, but Mycoplasma pneumoniae IgM was positive (3.44), and a 5-day course of azithromycin was begun. Blood and CSF cultures remained sterile. EEG showed diffuse slowing, rhythmic parietal–temporal discharges, and left parietal spikes. By day 4, seizures progressed to generalized myoclonic type despite levetiracetam, phenytoin, oxcarbazepine, and valproate, and her consciousness declined to GCS E2V2M5. 

On day 6, she developed marked polyuria: intake 1324 mL versus output 2606 mL, with signs of dehydration (decreased skin turgor, tachycardia, 0.6 kg weight loss). Laboratory evaluation revealed serum sodium 112 mmol/L, serum osmolality 227 mOsm/kg, urine sodium 129 mmol/L, urine osmolality 437 mOsm/kg, and a fractional excretion of sodium of 2.76×10⁻².

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

options.B: Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion

question:
A 69-year-old Hispanic woman was referred for progressively worsening blurry vision, right eye greater than left. Her past ocular history included bilateral epiretinal membranes. Medical history was notable for nonspecific joint disease managed with low-dose prednisone and methotrexate, and pulmonary symptoms of unclear etiology for which she was also on low-dose prednisone. 

On examination, best-corrected visual acuity was 20/40 in the right eye and 20/25 in the left. Intraocular pressures were 13 mm Hg OU. Slit-lamp examination showed nuclear sclerotic cataracts OU (2+ OD, 1+ OS) without cell or flare. Dilated fundus examination revealed 0.5+ vitreous haze OU and mild vessel attenuation OU. Spectral-domain OCT showed preserved foveal contour without sub- or intraretinal fluid. Wide-angle fluorescein angiography demonstrated mild bilateral peripheral perivascular leakage.

Initial laboratory tests including liver function, basic metabolic panel, hemoglobin A1c, angiotensin-converting enzyme, lysozyme, QuantiFERON, syphilis serologies, anti–dsDNA, and chest radiography were within normal limits, except for a positive antinuclear antibody titer of 1:80. Complete blood count showed leukocytosis (11.6 K/µL) and anemia (hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL; previously 10.2 g/dL one year earlier).

Methotrexate was discontinued; the patient was started on mycophenolate mofetil 1000 mg twice daily and prednisone 20 mg daily. Two months later, visual acuity remained stable (20/25 OD, 20/20 OS), but repeat fluorescein angiography showed worsening peripheral perivascular leakage.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 6-year-old girl was admitted with a 5-day history of fever (to 39.5 °C) and a 2-week history of unilateral clear tearing from the right eye. She had been treated elsewhere for presumed bacterial conjunctivitis with topical ofloxacin without improvement. Over the previous 4–6 weeks she had chronic nasal obstruction, noisy breathing, and rhinolalia, and for 7 days she had purulent anterior rhinorrhea.  
On examination, she had erythematous, tender, and edematous right upper and lower eyelids with an S-shaped palpebral swelling and overlying erythema at the site of the lacrimal sac. There was painful palpation at the nasal canthus but no ocular discharge. Nasal mucosae were swollen. Bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy was present, more marked on the right. Neurologic examination, ocular motility, and funduscopy were normal, with visual acuity 20/20 bilaterally and no proptosis or relative afferent pupillary defect. Throat and abdominal examinations were unremarkable.  
Laboratory studies showed a normal white-cell count with a left shift, C-reactive protein 22 mg/L, and normal procalcitonin.  
Contrast CT of the head revealed right preseptal soft-tissue infiltration and a well-delimited ipsilateral 11 × 18 × 13 mm collection with rim enhancement suggestive of a periorbital abscess; there were no signs of acute sinusitis or intracranial extension. Multiple bilateral cervical lymph nodes were also noted.  
Intravenous co-amoxiclav (100 mg/kg/day) and topical neomycin–polymyxin B were initiated. Over the next 3 days, fever persisted, and a fluctuating lump developed in the nasolacrimal region. Systemic corticosteroids were added. No spontaneous purulent discharge occurred, and blood cultures plus eye-swab cultures remained negative. Surgical drainage was considered, and EBV serology was sent.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 47-year-old woman with HIV-1 infection presented with a 10-day history of diffuse maculopapular rash, fever, myalgias, arthralgias, choluria, pruritus, and right upper-quadrant abdominal pain. Three days before admission she developed nausea, vomiting, and decreased oral intake. Her HIV had been well controlled on zidovudine, lamivudine, and lopinavir–ritonavir for the past three years, with CD4 counts consistently >500 cells/mm3. Forty-five days before presentation her regimen was changed to zidovudine, lamivudine, and nevirapine because of gastrointestinal intolerance. Twenty days after starting nevirapine the rash and jaundice began. 

On examination: temperature 39.0°C; blood pressure 95/66 mm Hg; marked conjunctival and skin icterus; diffuse maculopapular rash covering >50% of body surface; no lymphadenopathy; abdomen soft with tenderness in the right hypochondrium; no hepatosplenomegaly; neurologic examination normal.

Laboratory studies showed: normal complete blood count and differential; normal glucose, electrolytes, and pancreatic enzymes; elevated aspartate aminotransferase, alanine aminotransferase, bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase, and γ-glutamyl transpeptidase; acute kidney injury with a threefold increase in serum creatinine and a 75% decrease in estimated glomerular filtration rate. Testing was negative for cytomegalovirus antigenemia, hepatitis A IgM, hepatitis B and C antibodies. An electrocardiogram was normal. Abdominal ultrasonography showed a liver of normal size and shape with homogeneously increased echogenicity of the parenchyma.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 22-year-old athletic man with no other medical history presented with two episodes of acute tetraparesis.

First episode (military service): During intense physical exercise and a stringent diet, he developed hyperventilation, hypocapnia, severe hypophosphatemia without rhabdomyolysis, and acute tetraparesis. Cerebral and spinal MRI findings were unremarkable. Workup for myasthenia gravis was negative, with normal nerve conduction studies and no acetylcholine receptor antibodies. Hypokalemic periodic paralysis was ruled out by short/long exercise tests and genetic analysis of SCN4A, KCNJ18, KCNJ2, and CACNA1S. He underwent a 4-week in-patient rehabilitation program and fully recovered within 6 months.

Second episode (one year later): He presented to the emergency department with a panic attack manifesting as acute hyperventilation and anxiety, accompanied by paresthesia and tetany in all four limbs. It was unclear whether the sensorimotor symptoms preceded or followed the panic attack. He reported normal physical activity and a balanced diet in the preceding months.

On examination, he had tetraparesis with muscle strength of 4/5 (Medical Research Council scale), a positive Trousseau’s sign on the right hand, normal cranial nerves, no sensory deficit, and normal deep tendon and plantar reflexes. Gait and balance were not assessed in the acute setting.

Arterial blood gas analysis showed acute respiratory alkalosis: pH 7.57 (NR 7.35–7.45), PaCO₂ 19.2 mmHg (NR 30–45 mmHg), PaO₂ 105 mmHg (NR 82.5–105 mmHg), HCO₃⁻ 17.7 mmol/L (NR 22–26 mmol/L), and a base excess of –1.3 mmol/L (NR ±2–3 mmol/L). Blood chemistry revealed severe hypophosphatemia (0.2 mmol/L) and rhabdomyolysis (creatine kinase 44 913 U/L; AST 397 U/L; ALT 133 U/L). Other blood tests, including sodium, potassium, magnesium, calcium, vitamins B1, B2, B6, B9, B12, amino acids, and TSH, were normal. Urinalysis showed no renal phosphate loss. Nerve conduction studies and myography were normal.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 63-year-old woman presented with a 1-year history of atypical right upper-quadrant abdominal pain, significant fatigue, and weight loss. Her medical history was unremarkable, with no cirrhosis, viral hepatitis, or tuberous sclerosis complex. On examination she had mild hepatomegaly without palpable mass or jaundice. Liver function tests and tumor markers (AFP, CEA) were within normal limits.  
Abdominal ultrasonography revealed a solitary, heterogeneous, hypoechoic mass in the liver with an ill-defined margin; color Doppler imaging demonstrated abundant blood flow at the lesion’s periphery. Contrast-enhanced CT showed a 6.4 × 8.0 cm low-density mass in segment IV with inhomogeneous enhancement in the portal phase. MRI confirmed a lesion that was hypointense on T1-weighted images, hyperintense on T2-weighted images, and showed strong heterogeneous enhancement after gadolinium administration. There was no lymphadenopathy, portal vein involvement, or other lesions.  
Given the hypervascular nature of the mass, its lack of adipose tissue, and absence of thick-walled vessels, the lesion was initially interpreted as a hemangioma, an atypical hepatocellular carcinoma, or a metastatic focus. Because of the patient’s worsening symptoms and indeterminate imaging findings, a central segmentectomy of the liver with cholecystectomy was performed.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 76-year-old man presented with a 5-year history of progressive leg weakness. He reported right foot slap and tripping on uneven ground, later developing bilateral foot drop, exercise-related leg fatigue, and mild difficulty climbing stairs or rising from a low chair. There was no bulbar or upper-limb involvement. He also described sensory loss in both feet to mid-shin level with mild superficial dysesthesia. A prior workup suggested bilateral L5 radiculopathy, but lumbar MRI was normal.

On examination, there was no obvious muscle atrophy. Manual strength testing revealed isolated distal lower-limb weakness: ankle dorsiflexion 2/5 on the right, 4–/5 on the left; inversion and eversion 4/5; plantar flexion difficulty when standing on toes. Hip girdle and thigh strength were 5/5. Pinprick and light-touch sensation were reduced in a length-dependent distribution; vibration and proprioception were intact. Reflexes were 1+ in the upper limbs and absent at the knees and ankles.

Laboratory studies showed normal CBC, vitamin B12, TSH, serum protein electrophoresis, hemoglobin A1c, paraneoplastic antibodies, and renal and liver function tests. Creatine kinase was mildly elevated at 508 U/L. A 30-gene Charcot–Marie–Tooth panel was negative. Because EMG showed myotonic potentials, genetic testing for myotonic dystrophy types 1 and 2 was performed and was negative.

Nerve conduction studies revealed mildly reduced peroneal and tibial CMAP amplitudes with normal distal latencies and conduction velocities. Sensory nerve conduction studies were within normal limits. Needle EMG demonstrated fibrillations, positive sharp waves, and polyphasic, long-duration motor-unit potentials with reduced recruitment in the tibialis anterior (most prominent) and gastrocnemius. Prominent myotonic discharges were also noted in several muscles. The biceps brachii and vastus lateralis were normal. Overall, these findings were interpreted as consistent with a predominantly motor length-dependent polyneuropathy.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 62‐year‐old woman presented with angular stomatitis and intermittent mild-to-moderate abdominal pain for 6 months. She was found to be severely anemic (hemoglobin, 6 g/dL) but denied hematuria, postmenopausal bleeding, or gastrointestinal bleeding; occult blood in stool and red cells in urine were negative. Physical examination was otherwise unremarkable. Renal ultrasonography demonstrated left hydronephrosis; all other abdominal organs appeared normal. Intravenous pyelogram showed an opacity in the left renal region exerting mass effect on the pelvi‐calyceal system. Contrast‐enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed a 9.0 × 7.5 cm solid, enhancing soft‐tissue mass with focal necrosis, inseparable from the medial left kidney, displacing the kidney laterally and compressing the collecting system. At exploratory laparotomy, a radical nephrectomy specimen measured 17 × 12 × 8 cm. The cut surface showed a well‐circumscribed, lobulated, firm, gray‐white mass (10 × 9 × 8 cm) in the lower pole, with a whorled appearance, focal necrosis, and myxoid degeneration, displacing the pelvis. Microscopically, the tumor was composed of interlacing fascicles and whorls of spindle cells with moderate eosinophilic cytoplasm, oval-to‐cigar‐shaped nuclei with moderate pleomorphism, 20–25 mitoses per 10 high‐power fields, and approximately 20% of the tumor showing hemorrhage and necrosis; no epithelial component or perinephric infiltration was identified. Immunohistochemical analysis showed diffuse strong positivity for smooth muscle actin, desmin, and vimentin; epithelial membrane antigen was focally positive, while pancytokeratin and high‐molecular‐weight cytokeratin were negative.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 16-year-old Iranian girl presented with sudden-onset bilateral horizontal gaze palsy that developed overnight. She reported paresthesia in both feet beginning 2 months earlier, progressing over 2 weeks to her trunk at the level of the umbilicus, and a similar episode in both hands about 1 year prior. She had no significant past medical history, took no medications, and denied illicit drug, tobacco, or alcohol use. On examination, she had complete bilateral horizontal gaze palsy in the horizontal plane while vertical gaze was intact aside from slightly decreased saccadic velocity. Pupils were midsize and reactive; there was no nystagmus, diplopia, or blurred vision. Mild bilateral peripheral facial nerve palsy was present. Hyperreflexia was noted in all four limbs without clonus; plantar reflexes were downgoing; muscle strength, tone, and coordination were normal. Brain MRI showed multiple hyperintense subcortical and periventricular lesions on FLAIR and T2-weighted images, and bilateral hyperintensities in the paramedian pontine reticular formation (PPRF) at the level of the pons. Spinal MRI revealed two hyperintense lesions, one at C7 and another adjacent to T4. After gadolinium, the PPRF lesions did not enhance, whereas the T4 lesion enhanced. Visual evoked potentials were normal. Serum anti–aquaporin-4 and anti–myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein antibodies were negative. All other laboratory tests and routine studies were unremarkable.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 70-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation on enoxaparin, liver cirrhosis, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and a history of venous thrombus presented with a 5-day history of left arm weakness, numbness in the left fourth and fifth digits and medial palmar surface, and confusion. She reported chronic vision loss in the right eye but denied headache or trauma. On examination, she was alert; oriented to person, place, and time; and disoriented to situation. Visual acuity was reduced in the right eye. Muscle strength was 4/5 in the left triceps, wrist flexors, wrist extensors, and finger flexors, and 3/5 in the left finger extensors. The left triceps reflex was absent. Sensation to pinprick and light touch was decreased in the left fourth and fifth digits. There was no dysmetria or dysdiadokokinesia. Head CT without contrast showed a 1 cm hyperdense round lesion in the suprasellar cistern.

On hospital day 2, she developed a pupil-sparing right third cranial nerve palsy, which progressed the next day to a dilated, nonreactive right pupil. Gadolinium-enhanced MRI of the brachial plexus and spine was unrevealing. Gadolinium-enhanced brain MRI revealed enhancing lesions with heterogeneous signal intensity in the suprasellar, pineal, and right periatrial regions, without evidence of acute ischemic stroke. One week later, repeat brain MRI demonstrated interval enlargement of these lesions and focal right frontoparietal dural thickening. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast showed multiple thyroid nodules concerning for metastases. Two serial lumbar punctures yielded lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, and an elevated IgG index but no malignant cells or other inflammatory or infectious markers.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 33 4/7-week, 2.22-kg female neonate was born after spontaneous vaginal delivery following premature rupture of membranes. She had an unremarkable physical examination at birth and passed meconium within 24 hours. Maternal breast milk feeds were initiated by gavage on day 1 of life. 

On day 4, after episodes of bilious emesis, she was made nil per os. Examination showed a distended, tender abdomen without rebound and normoactive bowel sounds; the remainder of her exam, including genitourinary assessment, was normal. Laboratory studies on day 4 revealed a white-cell count of 21.1×10^9/L with 14% eosinophils. 

By day 5, she developed frank bloody stools. Serial abdominal radiographs showed nonspecific bowel gas fullness without pneumatosis. An upper gastrointestinal series ruled out malrotation. Although abdominal distention resolved by day 6, leukocytosis, eosinophilia, and hematochezia persisted. Family history was notable for a sister with milk-protein intolerance who developed hematochezia after milk exposure. 

Broad-spectrum antibiotics (ampicillin and cefepime) were started on day 5. On day 7, ampicillin was replaced with vancomycin after an abdominal ultrasound was performed to evaluate for ischemia, intussusception, and colitis; the ultrasound showed several subcentimeter hypoechoic, hypovascular liver lesions characterized as microabscesses, scattered loops of thick-walled bowel with minimal ascites, a sludge-filled gallbladder, and no biliary ductal dilatation. There was no pneumatosis, pneumoperitoneum, portal venous gas, or complex ascites. 

On day 8, a contrast-enhanced CT scan confirmed irregular hypodensities in the anterior right hepatic lobe consistent with microabscesses and demonstrated mild mucosal hyperenhancement and bowel-wall thickening of the rectosigmoid.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 60-year-old man presented with a 1-month history of right lower back and abdominal pain. He had a 10-year history of poorly controlled diabetes. Physical examination was unremarkable. Laboratory tests showed CA 19-9 of 99.98 μmol/mL, creatinine 73.5 μmol/L, and split glomerular filtration rates of 34.9 mL/min bilaterally. Renal ultrasound revealed a 0.9 × 0.8 cm round hypoechoic mass in the mid-to-lower parenchyma of the right kidney with no internal blood flow. CT angiography demonstrated a 1 cm nodular iso-dense lesion in the mid-right kidney abutting the capsule, with continuous, obvious but inhomogeneous arterial-phase enhancement and no prominent feeding vessels. Thyroid ultrasound showed bilateral TI-RADS 3 nodules.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

question:
A male infant was born at 39 weeks’ gestation via non-elective cesarean delivery for failed induction of labor in the setting of intrauterine growth restriction. Birth weight was 2730 g (<10th percentile) and Apgar scores were 8 and 8. Within the first hour of life the infant developed progressively worsening biphasic stridor, respiratory distress, and hypoxemia with oxygen saturations falling to 60%. He was started on nasal continuous positive airway pressure at 6 cm H₂O with FiO₂ 0.21 and a nasogastric tube was placed. Despite this support, there was continued respiratory compromise, and he was transferred to a tertiary pediatric center.

On arrival, the respiratory rate was 70 breaths/min with marked intercostal recession and tracheal tug; he had a weak cry. A venous blood gas revealed a pH of 7.10 and a PaCO₂ of 75 mm Hg despite escalation of respiratory support. Fibreoptic nasendoscopy performed at the bedside demonstrated a normal supraglottic anatomy and vocal cords but revealed a large subglottic mass causing near–complete occlusion of the airway.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 54-year-old man presented with right flank pain of eight months’ duration. On examination, a palpable mass was noted in the right upper abdominal quadrant. Contrast-enhanced CT showed an enlarged right kidney (20×15 cm) with hydronephrosis, nodular thickening of the pelvic lining, and enlarged hilar lymph nodes; multiple renal stones were also present. The findings were suggestive of urothelial carcinoma with lymph-node metastasis. There was no history of other neoplastic disease or prior hormonal treatment. The patient underwent right nephroureterectomy; residual matted lymph nodes with aortic encasement were left in situ. 

On gross examination, the kidney was cystically dilated, and the pelvicalyceal surface was almost entirely replaced by pale-tan papillary and solid nodules up to 2.5 cm in diameter, with only a thin rim of renal parenchyma remaining. The ureter was uninvolved. 

Microscopically, the tumor showed complex papillary structures lined by neoplastic cells with clear cytoplasm, large round nuclei with distinct nucleoli, and frequent hobnail morphology. Papillary cores contained variable stroma with hyaline basement membrane–like material, and psammoma bodies were present focally. The tumor invaded into atrophic renal parenchyma and peripelvic fat. The entire pelvicalyceal surface was lined by these clear and hobnail cells. No invasive or in situ component of conventional urothelial carcinoma or non–clear cell adenocarcinoma was identified, and no benign Müllerian-type epithelium was seen. 

Immunohistochemical studies showed tumor-cell positivity for cytokeratin 7, HNF-1β, and PAX8, with focal CA-125 expression; stains for RCC marker antigen, CD10, uroplakin III, p63, and CK20 were negative.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 37-year-old previously healthy man presented with four days of progressive weakness of all four limbs, dysphagia, drooling, nausea, and vomiting. One month earlier, he had received the first dose of an inactivated SARS-CoV-2 vaccine. In the days after vaccination he developed lethargy and intermittent myalgia with partial improvement. He had no recent infection, no known COVID-19 exposures, and a negative SARS-CoV-2 PCR. 

On examination, temperature was 37.0 °C, heart rate 88 bpm, blood pressure 115/80 mm Hg, and respiratory rate 20 /min. He was alert and oriented. Cranial-nerve examination showed bilateral facial paralysis; pupils were 3 mm and reactive bilaterally. There was no sensory level. Muscle strength was 2/5 in all four limbs, reflexes were preserved, and plantar responses were extensor. 

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 14.1 g/dL, WBC 11,600/µL, platelets 305,000/mm3, PT 17.5 s, PTT 37.2 s, INR 1.27, ESR 23 mm/hr, negative CRP, and normal chemistry. Blood and urine cultures were negative. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis showed 2 WBCs, 32 RBCs, protein 56 mg/dL, glucose 97 mg/dL (serum glucose 159 mg/dL), and no oligoclonal bands. Chest radiography and ECG were unremarkable.

On hospital day 2, brain MRI revealed poorly marginated T2-FLAIR hyperintense foci in the left cerebral peduncle, bilateral pons, and medulla with slight T1 hypointensity and faint contrast enhancement. Diffusion-weighted imaging showed restricted diffusion in the pons. MR spectroscopy showed elevated choline and myoinositol peaks. Cervical spine MRI was normal.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 68-year-old man with high-grade lymphoma diagnosed four months earlier presented with confusion. He had lost 13 kg in the month before diagnosis and, following diagnosis, developed severe depression with anorexia, losing a further 23 kg (BMI 14.5) over four months. He received chemotherapy from April to August and had a partial response. Three weeks after his last cycle, he was admitted with a three-day history of confusion; his capillary glucose was 1.9 mmol/L, and he received intravenous dextrose until normoglycaemia. He had no history of alcohol use. Initial labs showed WBC 11.9×10^9/L (neutrophils 10.9×10^9/L); chest X-ray, urine studies, blood cultures, and stool cultures were unremarkable. Head CT showed no acute intracranial abnormality. Over the next four days, his confusion resolved with IV dextrose, nutritional supplements, and antibiotics. On hospital day 6, he acutely worsened with new-onset confusion accompanied by ataxia, agitation, and visual hallucinations. Over the following three days, he became withdrawn and drowsy, developed episodes of hypothermia, and remained normoglycaemic; repeat infectious work-up was negative. Laboratory studies at that time showed serum magnesium 0.34 mmol/L and serum folate 3.5 ng/mL.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 43-year-old man with no prior lung disease presented to the emergency department with sudden-onset left-sided chest pain and progressive shortness of breath. He was afebrile, mildly tachycardic, and normotensive. On examination, breath sounds were decreased bilaterally and pulse oximetry showed an SpO2 of 90% on room air. Supplemental oxygen was started, and chest CT revealed a simultaneous bilateral pneumothorax that was larger on the left side. In addition, there were numerous elliptically shaped lung cysts of various sizes distributed bilaterally, predominantly in the basilar medial, basilar lateral, and subpleural regions. Bilateral chest tubes were placed, resulting in full reexpansion of both lungs with no persistent air leak after 48 hours. Radiographic findings suggested an underlying multiple cystic lung disease. Family history was notable for a maternal aunt with bullous emphysema and two episodes of pneumothorax and a deceased grandfather who had an unspecified pulmonary disease.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 41-day-old female infant was referred for evaluation of recurrent electrolyte disturbances. She was born at 37 weeks’ gestation to consanguineous parents by uncomplicated vaginal delivery (birth weight 3 kg). At 6 days of age, she developed poor feeding, lethargy, and frequent vomiting. In the emergency department, she was severely dehydrated with an arterial pH of 7.20, serum bicarbonate 11 mmol/L, serum potassium 10 mmol/L, and serum sodium 119 mmol/L. Cardiopulmonary resuscitation was required, and she was intubated. Initial hormonal studies were obtained, and she received hydrocortisone (stress dose), a fluid bolus, sodium bicarbonate, and measures to lower potassium. She was admitted to the neonatal ICU, tested negative for sepsis and metabolic disorders, and was discharged on hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone without sodium supplementation.  

At 20 days of age, she was readmitted with recurrent vomiting, poor feeding, and lethargy, again with hyperkalemia and hyponatremia. Treatment included hydrocortisone, fluid resuscitation, sodium bicarbonate, potassium‐lowering measures, and emergency peritoneal dialysis.  

Upon transfer at 41 days, she was alert, well hydrated, with normal female genitalia and no dysmorphism. Vital signs were stable. Laboratory studies showed sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 5 mmol/L, and bicarbonate 23 mmol/L; urinalysis and urine culture were normal. Fluorescence in situ hybridization was negative for SRY, and karyotype was 46,XX. Abdominal and pelvic ultrasonography were normal. Repeat hormonal studies were inconsistent with congenital adrenal hyperplasia, and she did not respond adequately to corticosteroids, requiring ongoing mineralocorticoid therapy.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 3-year-old girl presented with gradual-onset abdominal pain and non-bilious vomiting over 2 days. She was irritable but active; vital signs were stable (heart rate 98 beats/min, respiratory rate 22 breaths/min, blood pressure 90/65 mmHg, oxygen saturation 99% on room air); she was afebrile. The abdomen was soft, non-tender, with normal bowel sounds and no signs of obstruction. There was no history of fever, diarrhea, dysuria, trauma, or prior similar episodes. Family history was negative for bleeding or gastrointestinal disorders.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 7.3 g/dL (11.5–17.5), leukocyte count 16.1×10^9/L (5.0–15.0), MCV 72.4 fL (76–96), RBC count 3.69×10^6/μL (4–6), platelets 150×10^9/L (150–400), prothrombin time 12.7 s (11–15), activated partial thromboplastin time 27 s (25–40), and bleeding time 2 min (1–10). Liver and renal function tests were within normal limits except for an elevated AST of 337 U/L and CRP 43 U/L. Factor XIII activity and von Willebrand factor antigen levels were pending.

The pediatric surgical team performed an ultrasound, which showed no intussusception but revealed “a rounded hypo to iso-echoic lobulated area adjacent to the porta hepatis and head of the pancreas, devoid of vascularity.” A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the abdomen demonstrated a large intramural/submucosal duodenal hematoma along its entire length, causing luminal narrowing.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presented with a 6-day history of diffuse abdominal pain radiating to the flanks, nausea, and chronic constipation. She denied fever or vomiting. She had used NSAIDs for the past week for lower-limb discomfort. Her history included recent treatment for pneumonia, progressive exertional dyspnea over 1 year without functional limitation, an asymptomatic cardiac murmur diagnosed 11 years earlier, appendectomy, hysterectomy, and a 90 pack-year smoking history.

On examination, she was afebrile, pale, and hemodynamically stable without peripheral edema. Lung auscultation was clear. Cardiac exam revealed a systolic ejection murmur best heard in the aortic area. Abdominal examination showed a flat but mildly tender abdomen with normal bowel sounds and no peritoneal signs.

Laboratory tests showed creatinine 7.6 mg/dL (0.4–1.3), urea 191 mg/dL (10–50), hemoglobin 8 g/dL, hematocrit 27%, and an iron profile consistent with iron-deficiency anemia. Potassium was 5.6 mEq/L; other electrolytes and pH were normal. Urinalysis revealed hematuria and 24-hour proteinuria of 689 mg. Renal ultrasound demonstrated normal kidney size with parenchymal thickening.

Echocardiography showed left atrial enlargement (47 mm), left ventricular ejection fraction 62%, septal thickness 17 mm, posterior wall thickness 14 mm, inferolateral hypokinesia, and a calcified aortic valve with severe stenosis (peak gradient 92 mmHg).

She was treated with prednisone 1 mg/kg/day for presumed NSAID-induced acute interstitial nephritis and severe aortic stenosis but did not improve and required hemodialysis. A renal biopsy showed crescentic glomerulonephritis with medullary lymphomonocytic infiltration; of five glomeruli, four were sclerotic. ANA and anti–double-stranded DNA were negative; complement levels were normal. Serologies for HIV, hepatitis B, and hepatitis C were negative. p-ANCA was positive at > 1 / 80 (nonreactive normal), and myeloperoxidase antibody was 120 U (normal < 20).

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 49-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes, dyslipidemia, and hypertension presented with several weeks of cramping abdominal pain, vomiting, and frequent watery, nonbloody diarrhea. The pain began as generalized cramping and later localized to the epigastrium without radiation; there were no alleviating or aggravating factors. She denied dysphagia, odynophagia, fever, chills, rashes, joint pains, recent travel, sick contacts, or antibiotic use. She had been admitted twice previously for similar symptoms; a prior CT scan showed fluid‐filled bowels and colonic wall thickening, and she received empirical antibiotics without improvement.

On examination, she appeared uncomfortable. Blood pressure was 146/93 mmHg, pulse 88/min, temperature 36.5 °C. The abdomen was tender, worst in the epigastrium, without rebound or distention; bowel sounds were normal, and there was no organomegaly. Other systems were unremarkable except for xanthelasmas on the eyelids.

Laboratory studies showed a WBC count of 9,000/µL with 28% eosinophils (absolute count 2520/µL). Hemoglobin was 14.7 g/dL. Lipase was 2831 IU/L (previously 2415 IU/L five days earlier). Alkaline phosphatase was 391 IU/L, AST 57 U/L, ALT 54 U/L; bilirubin, kidney function, lactate, and electrolytes were normal. ESR was 10 mm/h and CRP 0.673 mg/dL. Peripheral smear demonstrated mature‐appearing eosinophils without atypia. HIV test was negative; IgG4 was 40 mg/dL (normal <86). Chest X-ray showed no infiltrates or adenopathy.

Stool PCR panel was negative for bacterial and viral pathogens; ova and parasites were negative. Review of her medications excluded drug-induced eosinophilia. Contrast CT of the abdomen and pelvis revealed diffuse fluid-filled loops of small and large bowel without obstruction; the pancreas appeared normal.

Esophagogastroduodenoscopy with biopsies from the esophagus, stomach, duodenum, and jejunum was performed. Histologic examination revealed eosinophilic-predominant mucosal inflammatory infiltrates in all specimens. Helicobacter pylori testing was negative. No villous abnormality, cryptitis, or crypt abscesses were identified.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 73-year-old woman with a history of poorly differentiated caecal adenocarcinoma (T4N2M0) treated by right hemicolectomy, incomplete adjuvant chemotherapy, and radiotherapy to para-aortic lymph nodes, presented three years after radiotherapy with a rapidly enlarging, painless, left-sided neck lump. She had no dysphagia, odynophagia, dysphonia, weight loss, or appetite change. On examination, there was a smooth, nonfluctuant, non-tethered 6-mm level IV lymph node. Routine blood tests, including complete blood count, liver and renal function, were normal; carcinoembryonic antigen was elevated at 24.0 mg/L (normal <2.5 mg/L). Ultrasound of the lump showed a 6-mm level IV cervical lymph node with intranodal calcifications, loss of hilar architecture, and increased vascularity. Fine-needle aspiration cytology demonstrated clusters of atypical cells with pleomorphic nuclei and moderate cytoplasm. Immunohistochemical analysis of the cell block preparation showed cells positive for cytokeratin AE1/AE3 and CDX2. Whole-body PET-CT revealed elevated tracer uptake in an 8.4-mm left level IV node and a 7-mm left supraclavicular node, with no further evidence of thoracic or abdominal disease.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old man with psoriatic arthritis (on adalimumab for 6 years), hereditary spherocytosis, and a history of cutaneous herpes zoster infection presented with two weeks of intermittent fevers and headaches and a 10-lb weight loss over 9 months. He had temporarily discontinued and then restarted adalimumab and, one month after returning from travel to Mexico, developed worsening headache, persistent fever, left facial paresis, and loss of taste.

On examination, visual acuity was 20/20 in the right eye and 20/50 in the left eye, with a left seventh-nerve palsy. Anterior segment examination showed only exposure keratopathy of the left eye, with no anterior chamber cell or flare. Funduscopic examination revealed dozens of 100–200 µm, round, creamy yellow-white choroidal infiltrates disseminated throughout both fundi, without vitritis, vasculitis, or optic nerve edema. Optical coherence tomography demonstrated hyporeflective choroidal lesions without macular cysts or thickening.

Laboratory studies showed negative serum testing for Lyme disease, syphilis, tuberculosis, and West Nile virus. Complete blood count and chemistry panel were within normal limits. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis revealed 8 white blood cells/mm3 (91% lymphocytes), normal glucose and protein, and negative cryptococcal antigen and other organisms. MRI of the brain and orbits demonstrated enhancement of the left facial nerve. Chest CT was performed and revealed mediastinal lymphadenopathy.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 52-year-old man presented with progressive right upper limb weakness and sensory disturbance. In 2018, he had right facial paralysis that resolved with corticosteroids; two years later, he noted numbness of the left face and neck. In February 2022, he developed persistent pain and numbness in his right hand, and by June 2022 he was unable to raise his right arm. He was initially treated with two courses of intravenous methylprednisolone (1,000 mg daily for three days) and one course of intravenous immunoglobulin (0.4 g/kg daily), without improvement. The weakness progressed, and his right facial paralysis recurred, prompting referral in July 2022.

On examination, he had right facial paralysis and diffuse right upper limb weakness (Medical Research Council grade 0–1/5) with severe muscle atrophy and areflexia from C5 to T1 distributions. There was severe dysesthesia and hypoesthesia in the right upper limb and mild dysesthesia in the left face and anterior neck. Vitals were normal.

Blood tests showed negative vasculitis-associated immune markers, including antinuclear and antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies, and an elevated soluble interleukin-2 receptor level (1,198 U/mL). Cerebrospinal fluid analysis revealed 14 cells/μL and a normal protein level.

Conventional nerve conduction studies demonstrated absent compound muscle and sensory nerve action potentials in the right median and ulnar nerves, with markedly reduced amplitude in the right axillary nerve; studies in the lower limbs were normal. The pattern indicated axonal degeneration involving postganglionic segments.

Nerve ultrasound (18 MHz) of the right median nerve at the forearm showed marked enlargement (cross-sectional area, 54 mm²) with swollen fascicles and perineurium, and a fusiform hypoechoic mass between the nerve and thickened epineurium. The right ulnar and musculocutaneous nerves also showed segmental enlargement with loss of the normal fascicular pattern. Color Doppler ultrasound revealed massive enlargement and increased vascularity of the right brachial plexus and cervical nerve roots. Subsequent MRI confirmed massive swelling of the right median nerve, nerve roots, and brachial plexus. Fluorine-18 fluorodeoxyglucose PET/CT demonstrated abnormal FDG uptake in these same structures, suggesting neurolymphomatosis.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 39-year-old Japanese man presented with three weeks of left chest pain and a rash on his hands and feet. His history included allergic rhinitis; he smoked 5–10 cigarettes per day and drank socially. Three months earlier, he had sexual intercourse with a commercial sex worker; he had not traveled abroad. 

He denied fever, cough, sputum production, or weight loss. A local plain CT scan performed three weeks earlier showed multiple nodular shadows in the left lower lobe, and metastatic lung cancer was suspected. Contrast-enhanced thoracic CT one week later again showed multiple nodular shadows in the left lower lobe, slight enlargement of left mediastinal and bilateral axillary lymph nodes, and a small left pleural effusion; upper and lower endoscopy were normal. He was referred for further evaluation.

On arrival, temperature was 36.8°C, pulse 85/min, blood pressure 102/60 mmHg, and SpO2 99% on room air. Examination revealed several millimeter oral aphthae, numerous soft, non-tender cervical lymph nodes bilaterally, and multiple pale red, scaly, nodular erythematous patches 3–8 mm in size on the palms and soles. Heart and lung examinations were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed a white blood cell count of 10,600/μL, platelet count of 621,000/μL, γ-glutamyl transpeptidase of 161 U/L, C-reactive protein of 3.13 mg/dL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 62 mm/h, and soluble interleukin-2 receptor of 1180 U/mL. Tests for HIV antigen/antibody, hepatitis B and C, HTLV-1 antibody, and tumor markers (CEA, SCC, CYFRA, Pro-GRP, NSE) were negative. β-D glucan, rheumatoid factor, MPO-ANCA, and PR3-ANCA were all negative.

Chest radiography showed blunting of the left costophrenic angle without discrete lung field opacities. Doppler ultrasonography of the neck confirmed multiple enlarged lymph nodes with preserved hila. PET-CT demonstrated high uptake in bilateral cervical, subclavicular, axillary, mediastinal, and pelvic lymph nodes but no abnormal uptake elsewhere.

Cervical lymph node biopsy revealed lymphoid follicular hyperplasia, enlarged germinal centers, paracortical dilation, vascular proliferation, and fibrosis. Bacterial and acid-fast bacillus smears and cultures were negative, as was PCR for Mycobacterium DNA. Immunostaining of germinal centers was negative for B-cell lymphoma, and flow cytometry and genetic studies supported reactive lymphadenopathy.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 58-year-old woman presented with a history since February 2008 of painful left axillary lymphadenopathy accompanied by low-grade fever. Initial excisional biopsy showed changes suggestive but not diagnostic of Castleman’s disease. A CT scan revealed an 8×6 cm colliquative mass in the left axillary and supraclavicular regions, smaller reactive nodes in the right axilla and inguinal areas, and subcentimeter nodules in the lung and liver. Repeat lymph node biopsies through 2010 demonstrated nonspecific necrotizing lymphadenitis; microbiologic and virologic studies, including HHV-6 and HHV-8, were negative. A subsequent CT scan confirmed persistent axillary and supraclavicular lymphadenopathy, bilateral inguinal enlargement, and a perianal mass. Review of prior histology noted necrotizing lymphadenitis with epithelioid and giant cells and vasculitis without lymphoproliferation.  

In January 2011, rheumatologic evaluation revealed nasal chondritis with crusted lesions, chronic conjunctivitis, violaceous purpuric areas on the extremities, ulcerative palmar and gluteal lesions, and persistent evening fevers. Examination showed mobile, nontender axillary and inguinal nodes. Nasal mucosal biopsy demonstrated acute and chronic inflammation but no vasculitis or granulomas. Laboratory studies revealed neutrophilic leukocytosis and elevated acute-phase reactants. Antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies were negative, whereas IgG and IgM anti–β2-glycoprotein I and anticardiolipin antibodies were positive. Cytomegalovirus IgM and Epstein–Barr virus IgM were detected, but antigenemia was negative and PCR non-significant. Bartonella henselae serology returned a titer of 1:128.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 22-year-old Chinese woman was referred for evaluation of oligomenorrhea. Menarche was at age 12 with previously regular 30-day cycles. Over the past year, she noted spotting two to three times weekly. She denied weight gain, hirsutism, Cushingoid features, or thyroid symptoms. She denied medications or exogenous androgens.

On examination, blood pressure was 128/70 mm Hg, BMI 18.1. She had mild facial acne, deeper voice, and mild clitoral enlargement (10 × 5 mm); there was no hirsutism by Ferriman–Gallwey score. Abdominal, cardiac, respiratory, thyroid, and neurologic examinations were normal.

Initial laboratory evaluation showed:
– Total testosterone 5.35 nmol/L (154.3 ng/dL) [reference 0.48–1.85 nmol/L]  
– 17-hydroxyprogesterone (17OHP) 18.9 nmol/L (623.7 ng/dL) [follicular phase 0.3–3.3; luteal phase 2.9–15.2 nmol/L]

A transabdominal pelvic ultrasound demonstrated a normal uterus and a left ovarian cyst measuring 18.2 × 16 mm, interpreted as a simple physiological cyst; the right ovary was normal.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 34-year-old Japanese man with a history of gastric ulcer treated with esomeprazole presented with 2 days of fever to 40 °C, headache, myalgia, fatigue, and vomiting starting 2 days after a finger laceration during sewage-plumbing work. Influenza A/B tests were negative; he received amoxicillin and ibuprofen without improvement. 

On day 6, he was alert with temperature 38.5 °C, heart rate 88 bpm, blood pressure 133/69 mmHg, neck stiffness, positive Kernig sign at 60°, and tender bilateral cervical and inguinal lymphadenopathy. White blood cell count was 8200/mm3 (6.0% eosinophils). Serum creatinine was 0.91 mg/dL; BUN 6.0 mg/dL. ESR was 55 mm/hr; CRP 11.58 mg/dL. Urinalysis showed no protein or cells. Head CT was normal. CSF opening pressure was 235 mmH2O; CSF had 1 cell/µL, glucose 66 mg/dL, protein 31 mg/dL; Gram stain and cultures of CSF and blood were negative. Oral levofloxacin was started.

Despite CRP falling to 0.67 mg/dL by day 17, creatinine rose to 2.74 mg/dL and peripheral eosinophils reached 12.5%. Levofloxacin and esomeprazole were stopped on day 17. He was transferred on day 20 with creatinine 2.09 mg/dL, urinary β2-microglobulin 1589 µg/L, N-acetyl-β-d-glucosaminidase 7.9 IU/L, and FeNa 2.1%. Renal sonography was unremarkable.

Renal biopsy on day 22 showed focal interstitial edema, mononuclear and eosinophilic infiltrates, mild tubulitis, and four noncaseating granulomas; glomeruli and vessels were preserved. Immunofluorescence was negative. Acid-fast and Grocott stains were negative. ACE level was normal. Chest X-ray and CT were normal; gallium scan showed no uptake. Autoantibodies including ANA, ANCA (MPO and PR3), anti-SS-A/SS-B, anti-dsDNA, and anti-GBM were negative; viral serologies (HIV, hepatitis, EBV, CMV, mumps) were not consistent with acute infection.

On day 29, he developed bilateral eye pain, conjunctival hyperemia, photophobia, and blurred vision. Best-corrected visual acuity was 1.2 OD and 1.5 OS; intraocular pressures were normal. Slit-lamp examination revealed bilateral iritis with keratic precipitates and anterior chamber cells (1+ OD, 0.5+ OS). Dilated funduscopic exam was unremarkable.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 64-year-old woman was found unresponsive by her husband in their non–air-conditioned home during a heatwave, with outdoor temperatures above 33°C (90°F). Her medical history included bipolar depression, diabetes mellitus, migraine headaches, and a known seizure disorder. Home medications were:
• Amitriptyline 175 mg daily  
• Cyclobenzaprine 10 mg daily  
• Lurasidone 80 mg daily  
• Benztropine 1 mg three times daily  
• Topiramate extended release 100 mg daily  
• Clonazepam 0.5 mg daily  
• Trazodone 100 mg daily  
• Sitagliptin 25 mg daily  
• Erenumab 70 mg monthly

Emergency medical services noted that she was febrile en route. In the emergency department, her blood pressure was 84/42 mmHg, and rectal temperature was 42°C. Her skin was warm and dry, and her mucous membranes were dry. On neurologic examination she was responsive only to painful stimuli, with no focal deficits, hyperreflexia, or rigidity. Initial laboratory studies showed a leukocyte count of 11 400 cells/mm3 and a normal creatine phosphokinase level. A screening electrocardiogram was unremarkable.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 25-year-old Caucasian woman with a history of moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma of the tongue, treated 16 months earlier with hemi-glossectomy and chemoradiation, presented with a 1-month history of progressively worsening severe, sharp, constant right shoulder and chest pain, associated with right arm numbness, productive cough, and dyspnoea on exertion. The pain was exacerbated by right arm movement. She denied trauma or travel.

Her medications included pembrolizumab and recent head and neck radiation. She had a 1.5-year smoking history (0.5 pack per day), social alcohol use, and no significant family history of cancer aside from a grandmother with brain cancer.

On examination, she appeared frail with weight loss. Vital signs: heart rate 86 bpm; blood pressure 121/74 mm Hg. Pulmonary auscultation revealed coarse rales and rhonchi in the left lower lung field. Cardiac examination showed a regular rhythm without murmurs, gallops, or rubs. She had tender right supraclavicular lymphadenopathy and swelling with erythema over the right suprascapular area.

Laboratory studies showed haemoglobin 9.2 g/dL, haematocrit 28.8%, total calcium 10.5 mg/dL, ionized calcium 1.51 mmol/L. Admission ECG demonstrated ST-segment elevation in the inferior leads and T-wave inversions in the anterolateral leads. Troponin I peaked at 0.29 ng/L (normal <0.05 ng/L). Chest radiograph revealed a left lower lobe infiltrate, progression of a known left upper lobe cavitary lesion, and a new right clavicular lesion.

Differential diagnosis at this point included acute coronary syndrome, myocarditis due to pembrolizumab, and Takotsubo cardiomyopathy in a cancer patient.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

An 84-year-old woman was admitted with a  one-day history of left lower abdominal pain and nausea without fever. She had a history of hysterectomy, femoral neck fracture repair, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. Her body mass index was 18.2 kg/m2; vital signs were within normal limits. On examination, there was tenderness in the left lower abdomen without peritoneal signs and no Murphy sign. Laboratory studies, including inflammatory markers, liver function tests, and biliary enzymes, were all within normal limits. 
A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the abdomen at admission showed an enlarged gallbladder with wall thickening but no gallstones, along with prominent small-bowel gas, mild intestinal dilatation, and slight wall thickening. Abdominal ultrasonography confirmed an enlarged, thick-walled gallbladder without stones. She was diagnosed with acute enterocolitis or subileus and managed conservatively with bowel rest and intravenous fluids.
Six hours later, she developed sudden severe right upper quadrant pain and vomiting unresponsive to analgesics or antiemetics. A repeat CT scan demonstrated that the gallbladder had become more distended and thickened, had rotated approximately 180°, and had shifted toward the midline; the cystic duct was now located on the right side of the gallbladder.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 41-year-old woman with no prior medical history was referred for evaluation of suspected early breast cancer. She was asymptomatic. On examination, there were no palpable masses in the breasts, axillae, or abdomen. Laboratory studies, including complete blood count and serum tumor markers (CEA 1.8 ng/mL, CA15-3 11.4 U/mL, BCA225 30.7 U/mL, NCC-ST-439 1.3 U/mL, soluble IL-2 receptor 322 U/mL), were all within normal limits.

Mammography revealed a high-density mass with clustered microcalcifications in the inferior lateral right breast. Chest CT confirmed a well-enhancing right breast mass without axillary lymphadenopathy. Ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration cytology of the breast lesion yielded class V cells, consistent with malignancy.

As part of staging, abdominal ultrasound demonstrated a heterogeneous hypoechoic mass with internal blood flow signals arising from the splenic periphery. Contrast-enhanced abdominal CT showed a 40-mm well-circumscribed splenic lesion with less enhancement than the surrounding parenchyma in the arterial and portal phases, becoming isodense in the delayed phase. MRI of the abdomen revealed a lesion isointense to the spleen on T1- and T2-weighted images, with slow centripetal enhancement reaching parity with the spleen at 10 minutes post-contrast. PET-CT showed no significant FDG uptake in the splenic lesion.

The preoperative differential diagnoses for the splenic mass included benign vascular neoplasms (hemangioma, lymphangioma, littoral cell angioma, hamartoma, inflammatory pseudotumor), angiosarcoma, solitary splenic metastasis of breast cancer, and primary splenic lymphoma. Given the inability to establish a definitive diagnosis noninvasively, the decision was made to perform a partial mastectomy with sentinel node biopsy and a simultaneous laparoscopic splenectomy for therapeutic diagnosis.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 33-year-old woman was found unresponsive and diaphoretic by paramedics. A point-of-care blood glucose measurement was 18 mg/dL. She received 12.5 g of intravenous dextrose, regained consciousness, and her glucose rose to 109 mg/dL. 

On arrival at the hospital, she was alert. Vital signs were blood pressure 117/68 mm Hg, heart rate 73 beats/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Physical examination showed mild distress, moist oral mucosa, and a nonfocal neurologic examination with normal pupils.

In the emergency department, her venous blood glucose was 15 mg/dL. She received two additional doses of dextrose 50% in water, improving her glucose to 45 mg/dL, followed by an infusion of dextrose 5% in water, which raised her glucose to 96 mg/dL.

Once stable and alert, she reported that the previous evening at 5 pm she took two light blue pills given by a friend for anxiety, believing they were diazepam. She felt unwell overnight with nausea and diaphoresis, awoke at 4 am with tremulousness, improved after a large lunch at 11 am, then used cocaine and consumed three beers before losing consciousness at dinner around 7 pm.

Her history was notable for substance use disorder and anxiety. She denied personal or family history of diabetes or use of hypoglycemic medications. Current medications included methadone; she had no allergies. Social history included daily tobacco use and occasional beer consumption.

Urine toxicology was positive for benzodiazepines, ethanol, and cocaine. Laboratory studies revealed a blood glucose of 44 mg/dL, insulin level of 47.4 mIU/mL (normal 2.6–24.9), and C-peptide of 5.4 ng/mL (normal 1.1–4.4). Morning cortisol, thyroid-stimulating hormone, free thyroxine, and insulin antibodies were within normal limits.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 46-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of new-onset heart failure. She reported exertional dyspnea (NYHA class II) and fatigue. Her history was notable for lichen planus dermatitis treated with immunosuppression several years earlier and TPO antibody–positive hypothyroidism on levothyroxine. One month earlier, she had mild COVID-19 confirmed by RT-PCR, with no hypoxemia or pulmonary involvement, which resolved with outpatient therapy. Three weeks later, she developed severe chest discomfort and shortness of breath. ECG showed ST-segment elevations in leads V2–V6 and troponin-I was elevated, but coronary angiography revealed normal epicardial arteries. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a normal LV size, apical hypokinesia, and an EF of 35–40%; RV size and function were normal. Cardiac MRI showed mildly enlarged LV size (LVEF 35%), a thin and aneurysmal apex, and widespread patchy subepicardial to mid-wall fibrosis in inferolateral and septal segments with subendocardial fibrosis at the apex; the report recommended evaluation for recent viral, autoimmune, or sarcoidosis-related myocarditis.

On exam in January, blood pressure was 115/75 mmHg, heart rate 90/min, oxygen saturation 96%, and bi-basilar crackles were present. Labs showed hemoglobin 12.1 g/dL, normal renal and liver function, NT-proBNP 885 pg/mL, normal thyroid tests, and elevated anti-TPO. She was started on guideline-directed heart failure therapy. A few days later, she developed worsening dyspnea, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, abdominal pain, and nausea. On admission, blood pressure was 85/60 mmHg, heart rate 120/min, jugular venous distention, hepatomegaly, mild ascites, and 2+ pitting edema. Creatinine was 1.7 mg/dL, ALT 46 U/L, AST 40 U/L, total bilirubin 1.5 mg/dL, NT-proBNP >18,000 pg/mL, and troponin-I remained elevated. ECG showed sinus tachycardia with low-voltage QRSs, Q waves in leads I, III, aVF, and V1–V6, ST elevations, and T-wave inversions. Echocardiography revealed mild LV enlargement, EF 15–20%, global hypokinesia, apical akinesia, mild RV enlargement with severe dysfunction (TAPSE 10 mm, RV Sm 6 cm/s), moderate to severe mitral and tricuspid regurgitation, a plethoric IVC (23 mm), and TR gradient 25 mmHg. She received IV milrinone and diuretics. Right heart catheterization showed a cardiac index of 1.4 L/min/m2, CVP 16 mmHg, PCWP 17 mmHg, and mixed venous O2 saturation 48%. She was scheduled for emergent endomyocardial biopsy.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 63-year-old woman with metastatic myoepithelial carcinoma of the left parotid gland, type 2 diabetes (managed with metformin until recently), hypertension, atrial fibrillation, and hypothyroidism presented for routine chemotherapy infusion and was found to have a preinfusion serum glucose level of 30 mg/dL. She reported several episodes over the past week of morning and evening confusion and tremulousness after fasting several hours; finger-stick glucometers confirmed values in the 30s and symptoms resolved with sugar-containing foods, fulfilling Whipple’s triad. Metformin had been discontinued after hypoglycemia began. On admission, renal function was normal. Liver enzymes were stably elevated, with normal bilirubin, INR, and a mildly decreased albumin. During a hypoglycemic episode (glucose 33 mg/dL), laboratory evaluation showed insulin <1 μU/mL (reference 3–25), C-peptide <0.5 ng/mL (reference 1.1–4.3), β-hydroxybutyrate <1.0 mmol/L (reference <3), and random cortisol 23 μg/dL (reference morning 8–25). Thyroid studies showed TSH 8.3 mIU/L (reference 0.3–4.7) and free T4 0.80 ng/dL (reference 0.80–1.70), and her levothyroxine dose was increased. Insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) was 15 ng/mL (reference 41–279), and IGF-2 was 147 ng/mL (reference 180–580), yielding an IGF-2:IGF-1 molar ratio of 10.

A 72-year-old Caucasian woman with Sézary syndrome, previously treated with interferon alfa-2b and photopheresis, began romidepsin therapy (14 mg/m2 on days 1, 8, and 15) with bexarotene. Five hours after the second infusion, she noted bright, hot, salmon-pink, nonscaly, edematous nummular patches and plaques in body folds, on the face, upper chest, and back. She described her skin as feeling like a heat rash with minimal pruritus. She had no fever, skin pain, lymphadenopathy, mucositis, arthralgia, transaminitis, neutrophilia, or eosinophilia. Topical desonide and triamcinolone were applied. A punch biopsy showed a neutrophilic infiltrate in the papillary and superficial reticular dermis with mild spongiosis and no epidermal necrosis.

A 16-year-old boy noted a progressively enlarging lump at his posterior left knee over one year. MRI revealed a 13 cm mass in the left posteroinferior thigh, centered in the semitendinosus muscle and extending to the biceps femoris. Fine-needle aspiration showed a heterogeneous population of ovoid to tapered spindle cells with densely eosinophilic cytoplasm and occasional multinucleated cells; no lipogenic differentiation was seen. Immunohistochemistry was positive for desmin and myogenin and negative for CD68, keratin, S-100, SOX-10, HMB-45, and Melan-A. FISH for a FOXO1 13q14 rearrangement was negative. A diagnosis of embryonal rhabdomyosarcoma was made, and the patient received 10 weeks of vincristine/irinotecan chemotherapy per protocol ARST0531. 

The tumor was then completely excised. Grossly, it was a mixed cystic and solid mass with a thin white capsule. H&E sections revealed diffuse sheets of round cells with eosinophilic rhabdomyoblastic cytoplasm and large nuclei. Throughout the tumor, a subset of cells exhibited a spectrum of cytoplasmic vacuolation, from small vacuoles to multiple vacuoles indenting the nucleus, resembling adipocytes or lipoblasts. These vacuolated cells were positive for adipophilin, Oil Red O, and S-100 protein; the vacuoles were negative with periodic acid–Schiff stain, with or without diastase. Immunostaining for desmin was positive in the vacuolated cells, whereas myogenin was negative in their nuclei. There was cystic change, scattered histiocytes, extracellular hemosiderin, low mitotic activity (1 mf/10 hpf), and no coagulative necrosis.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 17-year-old male presented with several hours of right lower quadrant abdominal pain associated with nausea and subjective fevers. He had a heart rate of 106 beats per minute, blood pressure of 130/90 mm Hg, and was afebrile. He appeared comfortable at rest. On abdominal examination, he had normal bowel sounds and isolated right lower quadrant tenderness without peritoneal signs. Laboratory tests showed a normal white blood cell count, normal C-reactive protein level, and normal creatinine. A point-of-care ultrasound of the right lower quadrant was performed to evaluate for appendicitis and revealed a hyperechoic circular focus with acoustic shadowing within a hypoechoic, non-peristalsing, non-compressible, tubular structure adjacent to the iliac vessels. There was no surrounding mesenteric fat stranding, free fluid, or hyperemia, and the walls of the structure were thinner and lacked the alternating layered appearance typical of intestinal structures.
