A 49-year-old man presented with a 6-month history of headache. Physical, neurologic, and ophthalmologic examinations, including visual acuity, fundoscopy, and visual fields, were unremarkable. Magnetic resonance imaging revealed a sellar cystic lesion measuring 18 × 14 × 14 mm that extended from the suprasellar cistern through the diaphragma sellae to the dorsum sellae and compressed the optic chiasm. The lesion was hypointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2-weighted images; in contrast studies, rim-like peripheral enhancement was noted. The normal pituitary gland and stalk enhanced typically and were displaced laterally to the right by the lesion. Computed tomography of the sellar region showed a hypodense cystic lesion with no contrast enhancement. Laboratory studies, including complete blood count, routine biochemistry, pituitary hormone levels, and urinalysis, were within normal limits.

A 55-year-old man presented with 1 month of edema and 2 weeks of decreased urine output, fever with chills, breathlessness, anorexia, nausea, and recurrent vomiting. He denied hematuria, cough, sore throat, skin rashes, or joint pains.  
On examination, blood pressure was 180/90 mmHg; there was no organomegaly.  
Laboratory studies revealed: hemoglobin 8.8 g/dL; WBC 9,300/mm3; platelets 120,000/mm3; serum creatinine 7.0 mg/dL; total protein 6.9 g/dL; albumin 3.4 g/dL. Urine output was 1800 mL in 24 hours, with 4+ protein, 15–20 RBCs/hpf, and 12–15 WBCs/hpf. Twenty-four-hour proteinuria was 4.2 g. Complement levels were C3 68.8 mg/dL (normal 60–120) and C4 17.6 mg/dL (normal 15–25). Serologies for ANA, anti-GBM, and ANCA were negative; HIV, HBsAg, HCV, and cryoglobulins were negative.  
A renal biopsy was performed after hemodialysis.  
Light microscopy showed 22 enlarged glomeruli with lobular accentuation, capillary-wall thickening, endocapillary proliferation, segmental neutrophilic exudate, occasional loop necrosis, hyaline thrombi, and no crescents. Silver stain revealed diffuse basement-membrane splitting. Tubules exhibited acute tubular necrosis; interstitium and vessels were unremarkable.  
Immunofluorescence demonstrated 4+ granular capillary-wall and mesangial deposits of IgA and C3, 2+ IgG with a similar pattern, and negative IgM and C1q; kappa and lambda chains were equal.  
Electron microscopy showed extensive subendothelial electron-dense deposits, GBM duplication with mesangial interposition, some capillary-luminal and mesangial deposits, focal foot-process effacement, and no subepithelial deposits.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 47-year-old woman was admitted with a 10-day history of remittent fevers up to 39°C, fatigue, and sore throat. Initial outpatient treatment with azithromycin followed by garenoxacin yielded no improvement; she then developed muscle pain and lower-extremity edema. 

On admission: temperature 39.7°C; heart rate 135 bpm; respiratory rate 26/min; oxygen saturation 91% on 1 L nasal cannula; mild respiratory failure. Abdominal examination revealed distension, tympany on percussion, and near-absent bowel sounds. Lung auscultation detected wheezing. Both proximal interphalangeal and ankle joints were swollen and tender, with lower-extremity edema. No rash was present.

Despite treatment with sulbactam/ampicillin, fevers persisted above 39°C. Abdominal radiography showed marked colonic gas and upward diaphragms. Contrast-enhanced CT demonstrated mediastinal lymphadenopathy, dilated colon with fluid levels, hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, and a small pericardial effusion—without mechanical obstruction. Colonoscopy confirmed no obstructing lesion. A diagnosis of acute intestinal pseudo-obstruction was made on hospital Day 5; nasogastric decompression and metoclopramide yielded no improvement. Clostridium difficile tests were negative, and metronidazole produced no response.

On Day 6 her respiratory status worsened (PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio 177), and she was intubated for presumed ARDS. A salmon-pink, edematous erythema appeared on the trunk and extremities. Discontinuation of all potential culprit drugs failed to improve the rash.

Laboratory studies: WBC 18 560/µL (89% neutrophils); AST 36 U/L; ALT 45 U/L; LDH 430 U/L; ferritin 3551.4 ng/mL; CRP 27.6 mg/dL; ESR 134 mm/h; procalcitonin 0.4 ng/mL; ANA <1:40; rheumatoid factor 29 IU/mL; C3 and C4 normal; blood cultures and viral serologies (EBV, hepatitis) negative.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 60-year-old postmenopausal woman with a history of total abdominal hysterectomy 20 years earlier for fibroids presented with a 1-year history of clitoral enlargement, hirsutism, deepening of voice, decreased libido, and increasing abdominal girth. Baseline renal and hepatic chemistries, TSH, and lipid panel were unchanged from 1 year prior except for mildly elevated LDL. Transvaginal ultrasound showed an anechoic, nonvascular, cystic left adnexal lesion measuring 10.5 × 5.9 × 6.2 cm. CT of the abdomen and pelvis confirmed the cystic left adnexal mass and demonstrated no adrenal mass or other intra-abdominal abnormalities. Initial hyperandrogenism workup revealed a free testosterone of 4.2 pg/ml (normal) but a total testosterone of 264.3 ng/dl (elevated), with DHEA-S 29.2 μg/dl, CA-125 5 U/ml, and inhibin-A 1 pg/ml all within normal limits. Three months later, the cyst was unchanged but symptoms persisted. On referral to endocrinology, repeat labs showed free testosterone 4.7 pg/ml, total testosterone 276 ng/dl, FSH 41.7 IU/L, LH 26.6 IU/L (postmenopausal range), suppressed 8 am cortisol of 1.0 μg/dl, and normal DHEA, DHEA-S, and estradiol. Repeat ultrasound showed slight increase of the adnexal mass to 11.7 × 5.5 × 8.3 cm. A multidisciplinary team recommended laparoscopic bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 17-year-old Asian woman from a rural, animal‐husbandry family presented with a 1‐month history of abdominal pain, nocturnal fevers, and worsening lower back pain. She had no chronic illnesses or relevant family history. Initially, she was diagnosed elsewhere with urinary infection and lumbar discopathy. After persistent symptoms, a standard tube agglutination test (STA) returned positive at 1/320, and she was started on doxycycline and rifampicin. Despite treatment, her back pain intensified, and she was referred to our emergency department.

On admission: temperature 37.8 °C; other vitals stable. Examination revealed diffuse tenderness over the thoracolumbar spine; otherwise unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL, white blood cell count 10,900/mm3 (53% neutrophils), C‐reactive protein 92.9 mg/L (0–5), erythrocyte sedimentation rate 71 mm/h, and Rose Bengal plate test negative. Chest radiograph was normal. Blood cultures were obtained.

Lumbar MRI was normal. Contrast‐enhanced thoracic MRI demonstrated bone marrow edema in T11 and T12, a hyperintense T2 signal in the corresponding disc space with intense contrast enhancement of these vertebrae, and a 2 mm rim‐enhancing collection consistent with epidural and anterior paravertebral abscess at T11–T12. Ceftriaxone was added; neurosurgery declined surgical intervention given absence of neurologic deficits. A Brucella Coombs test on serum returned positive at >1/1280. Tuberculin skin testing was negative, and blood cultures remained sterile.

On hospital day 4, she developed fever to 39 °C, white blood cell count rose to 21,000/mm3, and CRP to 420 mg/L. Evaluation for a new infectious focus revealed a left pleural fluid collection. Thoracic surgery placed a chest tube, yielding hemopurulent, exudative fluid with numerous leukocytes but no organisms on Gram stain and no growth on culture.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 71-year-old man with coronary artery disease, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, gout, and benign prostatic hyperplasia was transferred from a long-term acute care facility with a 2-day history of right wrist and hand pain, tenderness, and swelling. Eighteen days earlier, he was admitted for COVID-19 pneumonia with acute hypoxemic respiratory failure requiring high-flow nasal cannula and bilevel positive-pressure ventilation. During a 16-day ICU stay at the referring hospital, he underwent frequent arterial blood gas sampling from both radial arteries, multiple venous blood draws, and peripheral IV placements but never had an indwelling arterial line. He reported fatigue, shortness of breath, generalized weakness, and easy bruising and bleeding. His medications included aspirin, atorvastatin, carvedilol, lisinopril, sublingual nitroglycerin as needed, and therapeutic enoxaparin 90 mg subcutaneously twice daily. On admission, his temperature was 96.7°F, blood pressure 158/70 mm Hg, heart rate 104 beats/min, respiratory rate 30 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 88% on 10 L nasal cannula. Examination revealed diffuse bilateral inspiratory crackles, anasarca, and mild tenderness and swelling of the right wrist and hand with bruising on the volar wrist. Radial and ulnar pulses were palpable; sensation and motor strength were intact; the right fingers felt cool. Electrolytes and renal function were normal. The admission chest radiograph showed bilateral infiltrates consistent with COVID-19 pneumonia.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 65-year-old woman with metastatic malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor on a clinical trial agent and no known germline mutations presented with 6 weeks of nausea, progressive gait imbalance, and falls. On examination, she had no focal deficits beyond gait ataxia. MRI of the brain showed nonenhancing T2 FLAIR hyperintense white matter abnormalities in deep structures. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis revealed a normal cell count and protein, cytology with atypical lymphoid cells, and flow cytometry with an abnormal mature B-cell population that could not be further characterized. CSF viral studies, CSF oligoclonal bands, and both CSF and serum paraneoplastic panels were negative. A whole-body PET scan showed no evidence of new disease, and ophthalmologic examination was normal.

Her clinical trial drug was held, but over the next month she developed intermittent diplopia, neck-extensor weakness, and worsening inability to ambulate. Repeat MRI demonstrated new contrast-enhancing lesions in the corpus callosum. Repeat CSF again showed atypical cells and an abnormal B-cell population, with persistently normal cell count and protein. The differential diagnosis included an inflammatory or autoimmune process, a drug-related process from her MPNST treatment, or a new neoplastic process. Given the deep location of the lesions, brain biopsy was initially deferred, and she received high-dose methylprednisolone for presumed autoimmune or drug-related disease.

Over the next several months, her MRI findings fluctuated, and she underwent a stereotactic brain biopsy of an enhancing lesion that showed gliosis with mild–moderate parenchymal and perivascular mixed inflammatory infiltrate. Genomic testing of the biopsy sample was nondiagnostic. She was treated with intravenous immunoglobulin and a prednisone taper, but her symptoms continued to worsen. She then underwent plasma exchange followed by additional intravenous immunoglobulin and rituximab, without clinical or radiographic improvement.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 40‐year‐old woman with asthma, hypertension, and a 20‐year history of psoriatic arthritis involving both temporomandibular joints presented with a 4‐month history of a hard, tender swelling (1.5×2.0 cm) in the left preauricular region. Her prior TMJ interventions included bilateral discectomy and synovectomy 14 years earlier, right‐side arthroplasty with synovectomy 3 years ago, and left‐side arthroplasty with dermis‐fat interpositional graft 1 year ago, during which surgeons noted and removed whitish “chalky” material. A year later, examination showed a well‐defined, tender preauricular nodule with normal overlying skin. An initial MRI demonstrated a well‐defined encapsulated lesion adjacent to the TMJ, and fine‐needle aspiration cytology was nondiagnostic. A dedicated TMJ MRI showed progressive bilateral calcification around both TMJs. Subsequent CT scans revealed extensive calcification with deformation and arthrosis of both TMJs, and a well‐circumscribed calcified nodule caudal to the left TMJ.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 26-year-old man was admitted for evaluation of recurrent hepatitis and persistent cytopenias. One year earlier, he developed acute hepatitis (AST 1750 U/L, ALT 3110 U/L, T-bil 8.9 mg/dL) one month after influenza vaccination; a liver biopsy at that time showed prominent lymphocytic portal infiltration. Six months later he had a second episode of hepatitis (AST 957 U/L, ALT 1970 U/L) accompanied by leukocytopenia and thrombocytopenia; viral serologies (hepatitis A, B, C, E, cytomegalovirus) and autoantibodies were negative, and liver enzymes normalized without treatment, but cytopenias persisted. Seven months later, he presented with fatigue and again had elevated transaminases (AST 434 U/L, ALT 1017 U/L). 

On admission he was afebrile, normotensive, without jaundice or hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory findings included: AST 501 U/L, ALT 1165 U/L, ALP 429 U/L, γ-GTP 103 U/L, T-bil 1.0 mg/dL, albumin 4.5 g/dL, PT-INR 0.96. Complete blood count: WBC 2.35×10^3/μL, neutrophils 0.94×10^3/μL, hemoglobin 14.0 g/dL, reticulocytes 8.0×10^4/μL, platelets 3.0×10^4/μL. Serologies remained negative for HBsAg, HCV IgG, CMV IgM, EBV IgM, antinuclear and antimitochondrial antibodies; immunoglobulin levels were within normal limits. Abdominal ultrasound and contrast-enhanced CT showed mild splenomegaly but unremarkable liver morphology. 

A transjugular liver biopsy was performed and compared with the specimen from one year earlier. The earlier specimen showed prominent lymphocytic infiltration in portal areas; the current specimen showed disappearance of lymphocytes, replacement by histiocytes, bridging hepatic necrosis in portal regions, and new centrilobular fibrosis.

A 58-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of 8 months of progressive shortness of breath, intermittent fever and chills, productive (non–blood-stained) cough, and a 25-kg unintentional weight loss over 6 months. She had no night sweats, dysphagia, or hoarseness. Her medical history was notable only for hypothyroidism treated with levothyroxine. She was a passive smoker, denied tuberculosis exposure, and had no family history of malignancy or pulmonary disease. She had been empirically treated with antituberculosis therapy at another center without improvement.

On examination, she was afebrile (37.8 °C), blood pressure 130/80 mmHg, pulse 84/min, respiratory rate 12/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Lung auscultation was clear, and no thyroid nodules, neck masses, or cervical lymphadenopathy were palpable.

Laboratory studies showed TSH 0.08 mU/L (normal 0.35–4.9), free T4 0.7 ng/dL (normal 0.76–2.24), and serum calcitonin 128 pg/mL (elevated). Other blood tests were normal.

Chest radiography revealed diffuse bilateral 1–3 mm nodular infiltrates with a miliary pattern. Noncontrast chest CT showed innumerable small nodules with a random distribution and a confluent mass in the right lung. Smears and cultures for acid-fast bacilli were negative, Mycobacterium tuberculosis PCR was negative, and blood cultures were sterile.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 16-year-old girl presented with a 4-day history of increasing difficulty swallowing solid foods and a painless globus sensation. She denied ingestion of caustic substances or foreign bodies. An ENT examination was unremarkable; she could swallow saliva and small amounts of water. A barium swallow study the next day was normal.

Two weeks later, her dysphagia persisted. She was limited to soft foods and liquids, with no weight loss or reflux. She described three choking episodes in the month before her first visit and anxiety about solids lodging in her throat. She denied additional trauma. Her history included mild anxiety and depression managed in outpatient psychiatry after self-harm one year earlier; she had no current psychiatric treatment. Repeat ENT examination was again normal; she declined fiberoptic endoscopy but agreed to evaluation by a speech and language pathologist (SLP).

Three weeks later, SLP assessment showed normal oral sensorimotor function, an EAT-10 score of 10/40 (normal <3), and a prolonged swallowing capacity of 150 ml water in 26.9 s (normal <15 s). A video fluoroscopic swallow study (VFSS) performed four weeks later was inconclusive: she could ingest only minute solid or semisolid pieces, and liquids were within normal limits but taken in very small sips.

At a pediatric clinic consultation, neurological, cardiac, ENT, and abdominal examinations were normal. Laboratory studies, including electrolytes, liver enzymes, blood count, thyroid function, vitamins, and celiac serology, were all within reference limits. She was referred for gastro-esophagoscopy with biopsy and psychological evaluation. Endoscopic biopsies of the esophagus were normal.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 10-year-old boy of West African origin, born to non-consanguineous parents, presented with a one-year history of bilateral knee pain radiating into his legs. The pain began in the right knee after a long walk, was of fairly rapid onset, and was associated with swelling; within weeks, the left knee became similarly affected. The pain was localized to the knees and was continuous with intermittent exacerbations, being worse in the mornings and at rest. Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs provided only mild relief.

Two months after symptom onset, laboratory studies showed an ESR of 5 mm/hr, CRP of 4 mg/L, and normal ACE level; sickle-cell trait testing was negative. MRI of the right knee demonstrated a small joint effusion without synovial thickening. Synovial fluid aspiration yielded an almost acellular specimen; cultures were negative. A transient ecchymosis-like rash around the knee was attributed to warm compresses.

Over the next nine months, he was admitted several times with severe knee pain but no fevers, systemic rash, or other systemic features. On initial rheumatology evaluation, his vital signs were normal. Examination revealed a small right knee effusion and pain on flexion; the knee posture was held in slight flexion but could be extended to neutral.

One month later, he reported difficulty standing and required assistance with dressing because of lower-limb weakness. Examination revealed bilateral calf and thigh hypertrophy (more pronounced in thighs). Strength was 5/5 in neck, upper limbs, abdominal, and shoulder muscles; assessment of lower limbs was limited by pain, but quadriceps strength appeared reduced. Timed rises from the floor and from lying were within normal limits. He had tight long flexors of the arms, hamstrings, and gastrocnemius. Hyperpigmented patches were noted over the right knee and thigh, thought secondary to compresses.

Subsequently, he experienced worsening nocturnal leg pain. Laboratory studies showed ALT 145 U/L, CK 13 126 U/L, LDH 2245 U/L, with persistently normal ESR and CRP; ANA was negative. Muscle ultrasound of the quadriceps was normal. MRI of the thigh and calf muscles revealed mildly reduced posterior-compartment muscle bulk with asymmetric edema. On fat-suppressed STIR images, there was signal hyperintensity in the right sartorius and medial gastrocnemius extending into adjacent fascia, and similar edema in other selective thigh and calf muscles.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 25-year-old Asian woman presented with a 3-week history of constant bilateral heel pain and difficulty walking. She also reported migratory pain involving her ankles, knees, and hips of the same duration; the pain worsened with activity and was not associated with morning stiffness. She had no history of sexual activity, no family history of tuberculosis or rheumatologic disease, and had received BCG vaccination at birth. 

On examination, she was afebrile. There was mild swelling of the left ankle without erythema, tenderness, or limitation of movement; all other joints and the remainder of her examination were normal. Initial complete blood count and creatine phosphokinase level were normal; radiographs of the hands and knees showed no erosions. She was treated with NSAIDs and other symptomatic measures, but her pain worsened over the next week, now involving her wrists and small joints of the hands bilaterally. She failed to respond to escalated analgesic therapy, and developed low-grade fevers to 37.8 °C, anorexia, fatigue, a 2-kg weight loss, and a mild nonproductive cough. 

Laboratory testing revealed an ESR of 72 mm/hour and a CRP of 4.9 mg/dL. A tapering course of corticosteroids produced partial relief of pain but persistent daily fevers. Ten days after steroids were discontinued, she returned with worsening polyarthritis, fevers to 37.8 °C, an additional 2-kg weight loss, and cough. Her ESR was 70 mm/hour, CRP 5.3 mg/dL, and ferritin 204 mg/dL; ANA was positive, but ASMA, AMA, anti–dsDNA, and RF were negative. Chest radiography revealed patchy right perihilar and lower-lobe infiltrates without lymphadenopathy or effusion. A PPD skin test showed 22 mm of induration at 72 hours, compared with a negative test 2 years earlier. Attempts to obtain sputum and synovial fluid for AFB were unsuccessful, and the patient declined invasive procedures.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 13‐year‐old boy was referred to pediatric dentistry for irregularly placed upper and lower front teeth and unerupted teeth. His medical history was otherwise noncontributory, aside from an epileptic attack in childhood; dental history included extraction of his lower front teeth one year earlier.  
On examination, he had hypertelorism, a flat nasal bridge, saddle nose, mild webbing of the neck, pectus excavatum, arachnodactyly, and clubbing of the nails. Cardiovascular examination was unremarkable, and electrocardiography was normal. A hand–wrist radiograph showed a missing carpal bone with no fusion of the remaining carpal bones. Hematology revealed an elevated alkaline phosphatase level (179 U/L).  
Intraoral examination showed multiple submerged and retained deciduous teeth (numbers 53, 54, 55, 63, 64, 65, 73, 74, 75, 84, and 85), a deep bite, a high‐arched palate, gingival overgrowth with supernumerary teeth in the lower anterior region, and grade I mobility of the lower permanent incisors. Numerous permanent teeth (12, 14, 15, 22, 24, 25, 33, 34, 35, 43, 44, 45, and 46) were unerupted; all erupted teeth were caries‐free.  
An orthopantomogram revealed three supernumerary lower anterior teeth, congenitally missing teeth (12, 22, 38, and 48), multiple impacted permanent teeth, and an impacted mandibular right first permanent molar (tooth 46) with divergent roots. Cephalometric analysis demonstrated a skeletal Class I malocclusion with orthognathic maxilla and mandible, a horizontal growth pattern, and retroclined upper and lower incisors.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 61-year-old homeless African American man with hypertension presented with 1 week of nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain. He had presented 6 days earlier with similar symptoms but left against medical advice. On arrival, he reported intractable nausea, vomiting, fatigue, headache, photophobia, anuria, and myalgia; he denied cough, fevers, chills, or night sweats. He used cocaine regularly but denied alcohol, tobacco, and intravenous drugs. He had no recent travel.

Vital signs: afebrile; blood pressure 177/65 mm Hg; heart rate 77/min; respiratory rate 16/min; oxygen saturation 100% on room air. Examination revealed scleral icterus, conjunctival suffusion, jaundice of the soft palate, and a supple neck. Cardiopulmonary exam showed a heaving, laterally displaced point of maximal impulse with regular rate and rhythm. Abdominal exam disclosed epigastric tenderness without guarding or rigidity, normal bowel sounds, and a negative Murphy’s sign.

Laboratory studies showed: 
• Lipase >3× upper limit of normal  
• Mixed hepatocellular-cholestatic liver injury (elevated bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase, AST, ALT)  
• Thrombocytopenia  
• Acute renal failure (creatinine 10.4 mg/dL)  
• Elevated creatine kinase  

Urinalysis revealed hematuria, pyuria, leukocyte esterase, and hyaline casts.

Initial differential: hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS) and thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP).  
• Peripheral-blood smear: thrombocytopenia without schistocytes.  
• ADAMTS13 activity: normal.  
• Stool cultures: negative for Escherichia coli, Shigella, and Salmonella.

Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia was suspected but anti–platelet factor 4 antibodies were negative. Serologic and PCR testing for CMV, EBV, and HIV were negative. Workup for glomerulonephritis and other autoimmune renal disease (ANA, anti–dsDNA, p-ANCA, c-ANCA, complement levels) was unremarkable. Evaluation for chronic and autoimmune liver disease, including hepatitis panel, QuantiFERON, CMV, EBV, HIV, α₁-antitrypsin, and ceruloplasmin, was negative.

Given elevated lipase and epigastric pain, acute pancreatitis was diagnosed. Investigations for causes of pancreatitis included autoimmune markers (negative), urine toxicology (negative), undetectable blood ethanol level, abdominal CT (only subtle gallbladder sludge vs small stones), and MRCP (unremarkable).

Right upper-quadrant ultrasound demonstrated isolated hepatofugal flow in the right portal vein suspicious for portal vein thrombosis, whereas the left portal vein had normal directional flow.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 22-month-old Caucasian girl presented with low-grade fever, cough, decreased activity, poor oral intake, and bilateral jaw-line swelling. She was born at term without complications; her maternal aunt had autoimmune disease. Vaccinations were incomplete (no MMR, varicella, or fourth DTaP). History included recurrent otitis media (with tubes) and eczema. Initial tests showed positive mumps IgM; elevated IgM, IgG, IgA, IgE, and IgG subclasses; positive rhinovirus and enterovirus; negative CMV, HIV, and hepatitis panels. She was leukopenic with cold-agglutinin autoimmune hemolytic anemia. Exam revealed mild hepatosplenomegaly and small mobile cervical nodes. EBV serologies indicated acute infection, with plasma EBV load 9 000–20 600 copies/μL. Flow cytometry showed reduced B, T, and NK cells. Despite broad-spectrum antibiotics and symptomatic improvement, chest radiography and CT demonstrated persistent bilateral perihilar and right middle lobe infiltrates. One month later, she was readmitted with febrile neutropenia, rash, elevated soluble IL-2, but did not meet five of eight HLH criteria. Lymphocyte proliferation studies showed poor response to PHA but normal response to pokeweed mitogen.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 28-year-old man presented with a tonic–clonic seizure, left arm paresis, paraparesis, two months of gait disturbance, and fever to 38 °C. He had been diagnosed with HIV-1 three months earlier; his CD4+ T-cell count was 669 cells/μL, and the viral load was 23,800 copies/mL. He was ART‐naïve. On exam, he was confused and somnolent with complex visual hallucinations. Strength was 3/5 in the left arm and 2/5 in both lower limbs; myotatic reflexes were symmetrically increased, and Babinski reflexes were absent. Cranial nerves, funduscopic and sensory exams were normal, with no meningeal signs. 

CSF was clear with protein 27 mg/dL, glucose 74 mg/dL (serum 90 mg/dL), and 20 WBC/mm3 (54% lymphocytes); there were no malignant cells or oligoclonal bands. CSF studies, including cryptococcal antigen, PCR for Mycobacterium tuberculosis, VDRL, bacterial cultures, and Gram and Ziehl–Neelsen stains, were negative. Serum IgG for Toxoplasma gondii was negative. 

Brain MRI showed multiple white-matter lesions in the centrum semiovale, frontal lobes, and left parietal lobe: hypointense on T1, hyperintense on T2 and FLAIR, restricted diffusion on DWI, and a ring‐enhancing lesion in the left parietal lobe after gadolinium.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 4-year-old boy was evaluated for a 1-year history of a slowly enlarging mass in the right cheek. He reported no pain, tenderness, dysphagia, dysphonia, dyspnea, trauma, or systemic symptoms. No relevant medical, family, or psychosocial history was noted.

On examination, there was a 3 × 2 × 1 cm firm, non-tender, non-fluctuant, non-pulsatile mass on the right cheek, extending from the right oral commissure to the pterygomandibular raphe. The overlying mucosa was normal in color, margins were distinct, and the lesion was freely mobile between the buccal mucosa and skin. Aspiration yielded no fluid. No submandibular or submental lymphadenopathy was present.

Ultrasonography confirmed the presence of a soft-tissue mass. Contrast-enhanced CT of the right cheek demonstrated a well-defined, hypodense lesion within the muscle layer. Complete blood count and metabolic panel were within normal limits.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

An 81-year-old man presented to the emergency department with presyncope. Two months earlier he had undergone a Whipple procedure for pT2N1M0 carcinoma of the ampulla of Vater. His medical history also included hypertension. He was receiving prophylactic low-molecular-weight heparin.  
On examination, he had ecchymoses and swelling of the right shin and both forearms. Digital rectal examination was negative for bleeding.  
Laboratory studies showed normocytic normochromic anemia, mildly elevated lactate dehydrogenase, a prolonged activated partial thromboplastin time, a normal prothrombin time, a normal fibrinogen level, and no schistocytes on peripheral smear.  
Ultrasound of the right shin excluded deep-vein thrombosis and confirmed a hematoma.  
Differential diagnoses included an adverse reaction to heparin use, antiphospholipid syndrome, and a bleeding disorder due to an acquired inhibitor in the clotting cascade. Heparin was discontinued, and mixing studies were performed; mixing with normal plasma did not correct the aPTT, and the lupus anticoagulant test was negative.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

options.A: Bleeding disorder due to an acquired inhibitor in the clotting cascade

question:
A 74-year-old woman presented with a 2-day history of dyspnea and bilateral chest pain, continuous and worse on exertion; she denied cough, hemoptysis, fever, night sweats, or weight loss. Her past medical history included glaucoma, hip arthrosis, and cystocele repair. She was a non-smoker with no relevant exposures. Medications: boric acid and timolol eye drops. Physical examination was normal. Laboratory studies showed WBC 4860/μl, hemoglobin 133 g/l, platelets 205 000/μl, normal PT and aPTT, D-dimer 5360 ng/ml, calcium 2.35 mEq/l, albumin 4.3 U/l, potassium 4.3 mEq/l, CRP 3.4 mg/l, creatinine 0.852 mg/dl, LDH 306 UI/l, and normal liver tests. Chest radiography revealed multiple bilateral nodules. CT pulmonary angiography confirmed bilateral pulmonary emboli and multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules, some calcified, the largest measuring 35 mm in the right middle lobe. She was anticoagulated with enoxaparin. Flexible bronchoscopy was normal; bronchoalveolar lavage was nondiagnostic, with no AFB on microscopy and negative cultures for bacteria, nocardia, mycobacteria, and fungus. CT-guided lung biopsy demonstrated amorphous eosinophilic material with surrounding plasma cells and no neoplastic cells. 18F-FDG PET-CT showed intense uptake in all pulmonary nodules (SUVmax 8.8) without hypermetabolic lesions elsewhere. Autoimmune serologies were negative (ANA, ANCA); ACE was normal. Serum and urine protein electrophoresis were normal, but immunofixation revealed an isolated monoclonal kappa IgM. Bone marrow biopsy showed 3% plasma cells and 9% lymphocytes with slightly reduced cellularity. Pulmonary function tests and arterial blood gases were normal.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 13-year-old Sudanese girl presented with a 2-day history of colicky abdominal pain, absolute constipation, and vomiting of all ingested food. Since age 5, she had recurrent nocturnal abdominal pain exacerbated by spicy foods, frequent yellowish diarrhea, dyspepsia, and daily loud audible bowel sounds. She is the seventh of 10 siblings, with two of her siblings and four nephews having died in early childhood. 

On examination, she appeared ill, in pain, and tachypneic, with a low-grade fever. Her abdomen was distended and tender. Laboratory investigations were within normal limits. 

Supine abdominal radiography showed a tubular gas-filled structure originating in the right iliac fossa, crossing obliquely upward to the left hypochondrium. Erect abdominal radiography revealed multiple air-fluid levels. A diagnosis of acute intestinal obstruction was made, and the patient was taken for emergency exploratory laparotomy.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A previously healthy woman was admitted with a 20-day history of recurrent headache and fever and a 3-day history of dyspnea. Twenty days before admission, she developed intermittent left occipital headache lasting 2–3 minutes per episode, followed by low-grade fever, sore throat, and dry cough. Initial outpatient treatment provided only transient relief. Fifteen days before admission, fever recurred (to 38 °C), with marked fatigue, persistent dry cough, nausea, and non-projectile vomiting. One week before admission, she noted memory loss, bilateral hearing loss, and limb weakness; urinary incontinence developed the next day without altered consciousness. Three days before admission, dyspnea worsened, and she was transferred to the MICU after rapid progression to severe hypoxemia and lethargy, requiring intubation and mechanical ventilation.

Her medical history was notable for hypothyroidism (on levothyroxine) and chronic hepatitis B (on entecavir), with normal thyroid and liver function pre-illness. She had no diabetes, head trauma, psychiatric disease, tuberculosis, or malignancy. She denied smoking, drug abuse, recent travel, tick bites, and any known contact with birds, poultry, or horses.

On MICU admission (day 1): temperature 39.0 °C; pulse 128 beats/min; respiratory rate 20 breaths/min; blood pressure 105/78 mmHg on dopamine infusion; SpO₂ 96.4% on FiO₂ 0.90. She appeared lethargic under sedation. Lung auscultation revealed coarse breath sounds and bilateral wet rales; no murmurs, hepatosplenomegaly, rash, or definitive neck stiffness; Kernig sign was equivocal.

Laboratory results: WBC 6.37 ×10⁹/L (neutrophils 94.7%); CRP 175 mg/L; PCT 6.87 ng/mL; ESR 65 mm/h; lactate 1.3 mmol/L; albumin 29.4 g/L; AST 218 U/L; ALT 97 U/L. Arterial blood gas: pH 7.378, PaO₂ 79.4 mmHg, PaCO₂ 32.8 mmHg, PaO₂/FiO₂ ≈ 88.2. Interferon-gamma release assay was negative. Hepatitis B serologies showed HBsAg+, HBeAb+, HBcAb+; HBV DNA 610 IU/mL. Tests for influenza A and B viruses, cytomegalovirus, Epstein-Barr virus, Toxoplasma, rubella virus, and herpes simplex virus were negative. Cryptococcal antigen, galactomannan, and β-D-glucan tests were negative. Autoimmune panels (ANA, anti-ENA, ANCA) were negative. Lymphocyte subsets: CD3 76/mm³, CD4 44/mm³, CD8 28/mm³. 

Lumbar puncture: opening pressure 212 mm H₂O; CSF clear; WBC 6 ×10⁶/L; RBC 124 ×10⁶/L; protein 0.267 g/L; glucose 5.83 mmol/L; chloride 125.9 mmol/L; ADA 1 U/L. Abdominal ultrasound was unremarkable. Chest CT showed multiple patchy shadows in both lungs with bilateral pleural effusions. Head CT was normal. Two sets of peripheral blood cultures during febrile episodes were negative.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 72-year-old woman was referred for progressive symmetrical proximal muscle weakness. Two months earlier, she had undergone transurethral resection for early bladder cancer and then began weekly intravesical BCG instillations. Eight days after the third instillation, she noted abrupt leg weakness that gradually worsened over the next three weeks until she was bedridden.

Her medical history included dyslipidemia treated with rosuvastatin for over two years and treated rectal and endometrial cancers without recurrence. On readmission, vital signs were stable. Neurologic examination showed symmetric proximal weakness (modified MRC grade 2/5 in arms, 3/5 in legs), with normal reflexes and sensation; she denied myalgia or urine‐color changes.

Laboratory tests revealed hemoglobin 6.7 g/dL, WBC 12,850/μL (89% neutrophils), AST 2,235 IU/L, ALT 1,189 IU/L, BUN 146.4 mg/dL, creatinine 5.74 mg/dL, potassium 8.0 mmol/L, and CK >13,000 IU/L. Nerve conduction studies were normal; EMG showed short‐duration, small‐amplitude motor units without spontaneous activity. Muscle MRI demonstrated diffuse T2 hyperintensity in skeletal muscles and subcutaneous fat infiltration. Biopsy of the vastus lateralis revealed variability in fiber size with splitting, type 1 fiber atrophy, mild lymphocytic infiltration, and faint sarcolemmal staining for MHC-1 and C5b–C9 in non‐necrotic fibers.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 35‐year‐old man with a 5‐year history of recurrent palpitations and multiple emergency department visits. ECGs during episodes demonstrated a wide‐complex tachycardia (QRS ~130 ms) with right bundle branch block morphology and left‐axis deviation. Intravenous verapamil consistently terminated the tachycardia. Transthoracic echocardiography showed a structurally normal heart.

During electrophysiological study, catheters were placed in the high right atrium, His‐bundle region, right ventricular apex, and coronary sinus. Baseline conduction intervals were normal, with no evidence of dual AV nodal physiology, manifest preexcitation, or concealed accessory pathway conduction. The clinical tachycardia was reproducibly induced by right ventricular burst pacing and isoprenaline infusion. It was regular with a cycle length of 290 ms and a QRS duration of 135 ms showing right bundle branch block and left‐axis deviation. There was 1:1 ventriculoatrial association with earliest atrial activation near the His bundle and a negative HV interval. Atrial pacing at a cycle length much shorter than the tachycardia cycle length dissociated atrial from ventricular activity, whereas atrial overdrive pacing demonstrated progressive fusion consistent with entrainment. Based on these findings and consistent termination with overdrive RV pacing and verapamil, a diagnosis of the left posterior fascicular variant of ILVT was made.

An octapolar catheter was positioned retrogradely to map the left ventricular septum during tachycardia. Presystolic Purkinje potentials were recorded 28 ms before QRS onset in the distal third of the left posterior apical septum. Radiofrequency energy was applied to the mid‐apical posterior interventricular septum. Immediately during energy delivery, the wide‐complex tachycardia (QRS width 135 ms; cycle length 299 ms) abruptly transitioned to a regular narrow‐complex tachycardia (QRS width 85 ms; cycle length 281 ms) with 1:1 ventriculoatrial conduction, a relatively short VA interval, and concentric atrial activation.

What is the most likely mechanism and diagnosis of this narrow‐complex tachycardia?

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 36-year-old woman with no prior liver disease presented in August 2008 with a 5-day history of constant, sharp right upper quadrant abdominal pain associated with nausea and fatigue but no vomiting. Initial labs showed an ALT of 42 IU/L. Right upper quadrant ultrasound demonstrated hepatomegaly and steatosis. Two weeks later she returned with worsening abdominal pain, low-grade fevers, nausea, and vomiting. MRI of the abdomen revealed a 2 cm enhancing lesion in the right hepatic lobe. Fine-needle biopsy showed a nonspecific mixed inflammatory infiltrate and steatohepatitis without evidence of malignancy; fungal and mycobacterial cultures were negative, and she was discharged without treatment.

Two months later she presented again with worsening right upper quadrant pain and fever. Abdominal CT revealed a 4.8 × 4.7 cm mass in the right hepatic lobe suspicious for malignancy, prompting a partial right hepatectomy. Histopathology revealed florid necrotizing granulomatous inflammation with pseudotumor formation; immunohistochemical stains for mycobacteria, fungal organisms, and cytomegalovirus were negative. Her symptoms initially resolved.

In April 2009, she experienced recurrent right upper quadrant pain with low-grade fevers. An extensive evaluation—including serologies for acute viral hepatitis, toxoplasmosis, HIV, Entamoeba histolytica, Borrelia burgdorferi, Bartonella spp.; testing for urinary Histoplasma and Blastomyces antigens and cryptococcal antigens; and tuberculin skin testing with Quantiferon—was entirely negative. A random liver biopsy showed mild steatosis and nonspecific chronic inflammation; special stains and cultures remained negative.

In July 2009, she again developed right upper quadrant pain, nausea, and fevers up to 102.4 °F. CT of the abdomen revealed multiple low-attenuation lesions in both hepatic lobes. On transfer, she was afebrile with RUQ tenderness; labs showed AST 93 IU/L, ALT 74 IU/L, alkaline phosphatase 193 IU/L, and total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL. Blood, urine, and stool cultures were negative. ANA was 1:40 and anti-mitochondrial antibody was negative. On hospital day 3 she developed a fever of 101.1 °F. A percutaneous liver biopsy revealed extensive granulomatous hepatitis with occasional fibrin rings on a background of mixed micro- and macrovesicular steatosis.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 65‐year‐old woman presented with two months of persistent left upper‐quadrant abdominal pain and multiple episodes of nonbilious, nonbloody vomiting of recently ingested food. The pain worsened with eating, leading to a 5 kg weight loss. She denied fever, night sweats, skin lesions, known allergies, atopy, or family history of similar illness. Initial evaluation elsewhere included an upper gastrointestinal endoscopy that revealed an antral ulcer; no biopsy was taken. Empiric clarithromycin‐based and then levofloxacin‐based Helicobacter pylori regimens were prescribed without improvement. 

On examination, she was hemodynamically stable, with conjunctival pallor and tenderness in the left upper quadrant; no lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, or ascites were noted. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 9.1 g/dL, total leukocyte count and differential within normal limits, and an absolute eosinophil count of 147/mm3. A fecal occult blood test was positive. 

A repeat upper endoscopy demonstrated a large ulcer on the lesser curvature of the antrum. Contrast‐enhanced CT of the abdomen showed focal, asymmetrical thickening of the gastric wall (6.5 mm thickness over a 2.6 cm length) along the lesser curvature without perigastric lymphadenopathy.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A term female newborn (3.700 kg) was born vaginally without prenatal complications. Family history was negative for heart disease. Before discharge, transthoracic echocardiography showed moderate tricuspid regurgitation due to valvular dysplasia; all other findings were normal. She was discharged for follow-up.

On day 6 of life she was admitted with tachypnea, sweating, and failure to thrive; weight was 3.220 kg. Signs of heart failure and respiratory distress were present: O2 saturation 96% on 6 L/min, venous blood gas revealed metabolic acidosis (pH 7.03, pO2 46 mmHg, pCO2 29 mmHg, HCO3– 12.3 mmol/L, BE –19). Mechanical ventilation was initiated. On arrival: BP 70/30 mmHg, HR 130 bpm, diuresis preserved. Examination showed a grade 3/6 systolic murmur, weak femoral pulses, and hepatomegaly (liver palpable 3 cm below costal margin).

Laboratory studies: high-sensitivity troponin T 1001 ng/L, CK-MB 37.4 mcg/L; venous blood gas on admission pH 7.1, pO2 65 mmHg, pCO2 28 mmHg, lactate 14.8 mmol/L. Viral studies were negative. Chest radiograph showed cardiomegaly and mild pulmonary venous congestion.

ECG demonstrated sinus rhythm at 133 bpm, right atrial enlargement, inferior Q waves, and lateral repolarization abnormalities. TTE revealed a normal-sized left ventricle with hypokinesis of the inferior wall, dyskinesis of the septum, and mildly depressed ejection fraction; the right ventricle was enlarged with mildly impaired systolic function. Moderate mitral regurgitation and severe tricuspid regurgitation were present. The right coronary artery origin was normal; the left coronary artery was not well visualized.

Selective coronary angiography confirmed normal origin and anatomy of both coronary arteries and normal hemodynamic parameters. With mechanical ventilation and diuretics, hemodynamics improved and heart failure resolved. Within hours, the patient was weaned from ventilation; ECG normalized except for a prolonged corrected QT interval (480 msec). Cardiac markers fell (high-sensitivity troponin T 163.2 ng/L, CK-MB 5 mcg/L). TTE showed normal biventricular systolic function, no wall motion abnormalities, moderate tricuspid regurgitation, and no mitral regurgitation.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 29-year-old woman presented with lower abdominal pain. She had undergone laparoscopic myomectomy at age 25, during which uterine leiomyoma fragments were removed with surgical scissors in a collection bag. Four laparoscopic scars were noted; the mass was not palpable. Laboratory studies, including serum CA 19-9 (≤1.2 U/mL), CEA (0.6 ng/mL), CA-125 (9.5 U/mL), and sIL-2R (206 U/mL), were within normal limits, as were other routine tests. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed a well-defined 38 × 32 × 42 mm pelvic mass with central enhancement in the early phase and peripheral enhancement in the late phase, with a feeding vessel from the second sigmoidal artery and no evidence of invasion or metastasis. T2-weighted MRI demonstrated high signal intensity at the tumor margins and low signal centrally, while T1-weighted images showed heterogeneous low signal. Abdominal ultrasonography revealed a hypoechoic, heterogeneous mass with a clear capsule and pulsatile internal blood flow. FDG PET–CT showed nodular uptake in the pelvis (SUVmax 3.2) without distant lesions. Colonoscopy was unremarkable. The preoperative differential diagnosis included schwannoma, gastrointestinal stromal tumor, leiomyoma or leiomyosarcoma, desmoid tumor, and solitary fibrous tumor. Because it was difficult to differentiate benign from malignant, surgical resection en bloc was planned without breaching the capsule.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 37-year-old woman presented with a 1-week history of pain in the toes of her left foot. The pain was persistent, worse with movement, and associated with numbness, tingling, and a bluish-pale discoloration of the left toes. She had no prior medical history or medication use. On examination, heart rate was 102 beats/min; left femoral pulsation was weakened, distal pulses were absent, and the left toes were dry, cold, and pale. Motor function was intact, but sensation was reduced in the left foot. Electrocardiogram, chest radiograph, and transthoracic echocardiography showed no abnormalities or intracardiac thrombus. Doppler ultrasound demonstrated triphasic flow in the right superficial femoral artery, and a thrombus in the left common femoral artery with poor distal flow. Laboratory studies revealed a platelet count of 812 × 10^3/μL; other routine blood tests were normal. Peripheral blood smear showed marked thrombocytosis with giant platelets, leukocytosis, and immature granulocytes. A JAK2 mutation assay was obtained.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 35-year-old African American man presented with a one-month history of worsening low back and bilateral leg pain, intermittent night sweats, and a 32-kg unintentional weight loss over one year. He denied saddle anesthesia or bowel/bladder incontinence. He was treated empirically for sciatica with prednisone 10 mg daily without relief. He then experienced an episode of leg weakness and was admitted for evaluation. 

On examination, vital signs were stable. There was no peripheral lymphadenopathy. Neurologic examination showed intact strength and sensation in both lower extremities. Laboratory studies revealed a normal complete blood count, normal serum electrolytes, and a normal serum calcium level of 8.1 mg/dL. HIV serologies were negative.

Magnetic resonance imaging of the cervical, thoracic, and lumbar spine demonstrated multiple enhancing lesions in the T11, T12, L3, and L4 vertebral bodies, right sacrum, and ilium, with retropulsion causing effacement of the lateral recesses at L3–L4 and L4–L5; a small epidural lesion was noted at T5 without significant cord compression. Additional osseous lesions were seen in the sternum and multiple ribs.

Computed tomography of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis showed bilateral hilar and mediastinal lymphadenopathy, mild cardiomegaly, a dilated main pulmonary artery (3.6 cm), hepatomegaly (18.1 cm), splenomegaly (12.4 cm), and multiple enlarged pelvic sidewall, external iliac, and inguinal lymph nodes. Testicular ultrasound was normal. Serum protein electrophoresis, urine immunofixation, β-hCG levels, and fecal occult blood testing were all negative.

A CT-guided core biopsy of a left iliac crest lesion revealed noncaseating and necrotizing granulomas; Grocott’s methenamine silver and Ziehl–Neelsen stains were negative. Endobronchial ultrasound–guided transbronchial needle aspiration of a mediastinal lymph node demonstrated nonnecrotizing granulomas without malignant cells; fungal and acid-fast cultures were negative. Serum angiotensin-converting enzyme was 62 U/L (reference 14–82).

One month later, an excisional biopsy of a left inguinal lymph node again showed necrotizing and nonnecrotizing granulomatous lymphadenopathy without evidence of fungal or acid-fast organisms. An ^18F-FDG PET scan revealed extensive hypermetabolic osseous and nodal uptake.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 50-year-old non-smoker Caucasian woman with no past medical history presented with chronic mild right upper quadrant abdominal pain for one year. Physical examination was unremarkable. Laboratory studies, including complete blood count, liver function tests, and alpha-fetoprotein level, were within normal limits. Viral hepatitis B and C serologies were negative. An abdominal ultrasound showed a 15 mm homogeneous hyperechoic nodule in segment VI of the liver. Contrast-enhanced MRI revealed a well-circumscribed 17 mm nodule in segment VI that was moderately hyperintense on T2-weighted images, hypointense on T1-weighted images, and hyperintense on diffusion-weighted images with apparent diffusion coefficient restriction. The lesion demonstrated arterial-phase enhancement, washout in the portal and delayed phases, capsule enhancement, and a signal drop on opposed-phase imaging, indicative of fat content.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 15-month-old boy was referred for evaluation of a right lower limb deformity. He was born at term following induction; growth parameters were normal. On examination, the right foot had medial splaying and duplication of the hallux with mild anterolateral bowing of the right tibia. The left index finger had radial deviation. There were no café-au-lait macules, neurofibromas, or other cutaneous stigmata of a neurocutaneous disorder. Initial radiographs of the right leg showed anterolateral bowing of the tibia, a medullary cyst, and duplication of the first ray; the fibula appeared straight. At 7 months of age, an MRI of the brain and right lower extremity was performed: the brain was normal; the right tibia showed midshaft cortical thinning with continuous anterior cortex and posterior callus formation without evidence of pseudarthrosis or medullary canal duplication; a supernumerary digit arising from the first cuneiform was noted.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

options.B: Congenital anterolateral tibial bowing and polydactyly

question:
A 23-year-old man from Cameroon presented with acute-onset, right-sided scrotal pain and swelling while in the United States for military training. He denied any current or prior sexual activity, penile discharge, hematuria, fevers, night sweats, weight loss, or cough.  
Initial urinalysis showed pyuria; no urine culture was performed. Urine nucleic acid amplification testing (NAAT) was negative for Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis. He was diagnosed with acute epididymitis and treated with ceftriaxone and doxycycline, with symptomatic improvement. Six weeks later, he had recurrent pain; scrotal ultrasound again showed epididymitis. Repeat urinalysis (no culture) and gonorrhea/chlamydia NAAT were negative, and he received a 30-day course of ciprofloxacin, which improved the pain but not the swelling.  
Five months after the initial presentation, he returned with another recurrence. On examination, there was prominent right scrotal swelling and tenderness. Ultrasound demonstrated right epididymitis with possible necrosis, a complex hydrocele, and a focal scrotal abscess. Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed a complex right scrotal fluid collection, an enlarged heterogeneous prostate, an enlarged juxtaesophageal lymph node, and right apical lung nodular scarring. Although TB was considered in the differential diagnosis, a unilateral orchiectomy was performed because malignancy was also high on the differential given his age.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 43-year-old woman presented with a 2-week history of progressive dyspnea. She was afebrile, with a heart rate of 87 beats per minute, blood pressure of 97/70 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation of 94% on room air. Physical examination revealed elevated jugular venous pressure and a muffled mechanical closure click. Her medical history was notable for rheumatic heart disease, mitral valve replacement with a 27-mm bileaflet mechanical prosthesis 9 years earlier, a prior episode of prosthetic valve thrombosis treated with tPA 8 years ago, and a reported allergy to porcine products. Home warfarin therapy was chronic, but her INR on admission was subtherapeutic at 1.3.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL and brain natriuretic peptide 241 pg/mL. Electrocardiography revealed normal sinus rhythm with nonspecific ST-segment changes. Chest radiography showed mild interstitial prominence. CT scan of the chest with contrast demonstrated bilateral moderate pleural effusions and an enlarged pulmonary trunk. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 50% and significant stenosis across the mechanical mitral valve with restricted leaflet motion; the mean mitral gradient was 23 mm Hg at a heart rate of 79 beats per minute. Transesophageal echocardiography confirmed an immobile anterior leaflet and restricted posterior leaflet with a mean gradient of 22 mm Hg at 78 beats per minute, and it showed an echo-dense mass on the anterior leaflet. Further CT angiography identified a possible thrombus measuring ≤1.0 cm in greatest dimension attached to the mitral valve.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 79-year-old white man presented with sudden onset of right-sided ophthalmoplegia, diplopia, and ptosis accompanied by nausea, vomiting, and headache. The symptoms began two days after receiving an inactivated seasonal influenza vaccine containing A/New Caledonia/20/99 (H1N1)-like, A/Wisconsin/67/2005 (H3N2)-like, and B/Malaysia/2506/2004-like viruses. 

He had no history of diabetes, glucose intolerance, arterial hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, systemic vasculitis, smoking, obesity, or other vascular risk factors. There was no personal or family history of neurologic disorders. 

On examination, temperature and other vital signs were normal; blood pressure was 130/80 mmHg. Neurologic examination revealed right oculomotor nerve palsy with pupil sparing; all other cranial nerves were intact. Motor strength, reflexes, tone, coordination, sensation, gait, and Romberg test were normal.

Laboratory studies showed normal red and white blood cell counts, platelet count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, electrolytes, renal function, lipid panel, fasting glucose, glucose tolerance, and glycosylated hemoglobin. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis was normal. Noncontrast head CT, brain MRI, and MR angiography were unremarkable.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 51-year-old Japanese man presented with a 2-month history of painless right vision loss. He had type 2 diabetes mellitus managed with oral agents without retinopathy and a 60–pack-year smoking history; there was no family history of sarcoidosis. On examination, cutaneous nodules were noted on his back; the neurologic examination was otherwise unremarkable. Visual field testing showed slight enlargement of the right blind spot. Funduscopic examination of the right eye revealed optic disc edema with hemorrhages; best-corrected visual acuity was 20/25. There was no evidence of uveitis. MRI of the orbits with contrast demonstrated enhancement of the right optic nerve; brain FLAIR images showed no demyelinating lesions. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis revealed protein 74 mg/dL, 19 mononuclear cells/µL, glucose 77 mg/dL; there were no oligoclonal bands or elevated IgG index, and bacterial, viral, and fungal cultures were negative. Laboratory studies showed an elevated serum angiotensin-converting enzyme level of 34.2 IU/L with normal serum calcium; antinuclear antibody, interleukin-2 receptor, immunoglobulin G4, antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody, T-spot, and aquaporin-4 antibody tests were negative. Contrast-enhanced chest CT revealed bilateral mediastinal and hilar lymphadenopathy.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 73-year-old man with ischemic heart disease and a bioprosthetic aortic valve implanted in 2009, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, stage 4 chronic kidney disease, and peptic ulcer disease presented with a 2-week history of worsening exertional dyspnoea, orthopnoea, and intermittent presyncope. His medications included lifelong clopidogrel, antihypertensives, statin therapy, and insulin.

Initial ECG showed a stable borderline left bundle branch block without ischemic changes. He was admitted for intravenous diuresis. A few days later, he awoke with new right-sided visual disturbance. Vital signs were within normal limits. Neurologically, he had a right homonymous hemianopia; tone, reflexes, cranial nerves, strength, and sensation were otherwise normal. Cardiovascular examination revealed a regular rate with no rub, gallop, or murmur; there were coarse crepitations at the lung bases and bilateral ankle pitting oedema. Abdominal and dermatologic findings were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 102 g/L, white-cell count 15.3×10^9/L, and C-reactive protein 121 mg/L. Five sets of blood cultures were negative. Serologic tests for Coxiella and Bartonella were unremarkable. Coagulation studies, a prothrombotic panel (protein C, protein S, factor V Leiden), and blood film were normal.

Diffusion-weighted brain MRI demonstrated a left occipital lobe infarct at the junction of the posterior and middle cerebral artery territories without hemorrhage or old infarcts. Transoesophageal echocardiography revealed a 10×7.5 mm heterogeneous mobile echogenicity attached to the bioprosthetic aortic valve with moderate stenosis, severe paravalvular regurgitation, and leaflet degeneration; no valvular abscess was seen. Cardiac positron-emission tomography showed no FDG uptake at the bioprosthetic valve.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

options.B: Embolization of a calcific valve fragment

question:
A 17-year-old Caucasian woman presented with acute right lower abdominal pain. Ultrasonography and CT revealed a 20-cm hyperdense cystic right adnexal mass with solid components up to the umbilicus. Emergency laparotomy showed torsion of the right ovary; a right salpingo-oophorectomy removed a 1600-g tumor. Histopathology disclosed a benign cystic struma ovarii confirmed by thyroglobulin immunostaining, with lymphocytes and lymphoid follicles among thyroid follicles. Three days after surgery, thyroid function tests showed TSH 0.001 mIU/mL (normal 0.27–4.2), free T4 1.32 ng/dL (normal 0.74–1.52), and free T3 3.6 pg/mL (normal 2.3–4.2). Two months later, she remained asymptomatic but had persistent subclinical hyperthyroidism. Thyroid ultrasound demonstrated a normal-sized gland with mildly heterogeneous echogenicity. Thyroid scintigraphy showed focal uptake in the left lobe with suppression elsewhere, and I-131 uptake was reduced to 16% at 4 hours and 27% at 24 hours.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 66-year-old man with sleep apnoea and a history of junctional tachycardia ablation presented with acute dyspnoea shortly after undergoing urgent percutaneous coronary intervention for an inferior ST-elevation myocardial infarction. His medications included aspirin, clopidogrel, bisoprolol, atorvastatin, and ramipril. Initial examination prior to PCI was unremarkable, and transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) showed no valvular abnormalities. Coronary angiography revealed a complete occlusion of the distal right coronary artery, which was stented without difficulty, despite a shepherd’s crook configuration of the proximal vessel.

Soon after the procedure, he developed shortness of breath and a short decrescendo early diastolic aortic murmur. NT-pro–brain natriuretic peptide was 1784 pg/mL. Chest radiograph was normal, and CT angiography excluded dissection and pulmonary embolism. Repeat TTE showed new moderate-to-severe aortic regurgitation (vena contracta 0.6 cm; jet >50% of outflow tract; pressure half-time 340 ms) with an abnormal mobile structure on the aortic valve. Left ventricular ejection fraction was 45% with inferior wall hypokinesis.

Transoesophageal echocardiography identified a 17 mm fine, filamentous mobile lesion attached to the non-coronary cusp, causing a coaptation defect and severe eccentric regurgitant jet. The valve was tricuspid without root dilation. There was no clinical or laboratory evidence of infective endocarditis. After multidisciplinary discussion, the patient was referred for surgical evaluation.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

An 82-year-old man with chronic atrial fibrillation and type 2 diabetes mellitus presented with a 3-month history of worsening peripheral edema and pleural effusion despite escalating diuretic therapy. He had stable heart failure with preserved ejection fraction for 2 years on bisoprolol, losartan, spironolactone, furosemide, and empagliflozin. Laboratory studies showed total bilirubin increased from 0.9 to 1.7 mg/dL, γ-glutamyl transferase from 32 to 197 U/L, and alkaline phosphatase from 70 to 176 U/L. He developed sialadenitis.  
Electrocardiography revealed chronic atrial fibrillation. Transthoracic echocardiography showed an ejection fraction of 50%, biatrial enlargement, a septal bounce, and a 40.1% respiratory variation in left ventricular E-velocity. Contrast-enhanced CT demonstrated partial pericardial calcifications without aortic wall thickening or hydronephrosis. Right heart catheterization showed equal right and left ventricular end-diastolic pressures, a dip-and-plateau pattern, and a systolic area index of 2.5.  
Autoimmune serologies—including anti-nuclear, anti-DNA, P-ANCA, C-ANCA, and anti-RNP-1 antibodies—and C-reactive protein were not elevated. QuantiFERON-TB Gold was negative. His serum IgG4 level was 1168 mg/dL, and a minor salivary gland biopsy showed >10 IgG4-positive cells per high-powered field with an IgG4+/IgG-positive cell ratio of about 40%.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 37 year-old man with HIV infection and a recent history of cryptococcal meningitis, treated with amphotericin followed by fluconazole, began ART (zidovudine, lamivudine, efavirenz) 21 days after meningitis treatment completion. His baseline CD4 count was 5 cells/µL, rising to 29 cells/µL by day +21 of ART. On day +21, he noted hypogastric abdominal pain and was treated empirically for urinary tract infection with ciprofloxacin, with some improvement. By day +35, the pain recurred, now associated with low-grade fevers and a dry cough; physical examination showed tachypnea (28 breaths/min) and right iliac-fossa tenderness. A complete blood count, chest radiograph, and abdominal ultrasound were unremarkable. He was treated for possible atypical pneumonia with doxycycline; his respiratory symptoms improved, but abdominal pain persisted with occasional vomiting. On day +84, a repeat chest radiograph to exclude tuberculosis was unremarkable. His CD4 count had fallen to 14 cells/µL, and HIV-1 viral load was 22,000 copies/mL despite verified adherence. On day +112, abdominal ultrasound showed an appendicular mass and para-aortic lymphadenopathy; CD4 was 8 cells/µL and viral load 15,475 copies/mL. He consented to ultrasound-guided biopsy on day +140, and a second ultrasound demonstrated ileal wall thickening (6 mm) with adjacent 1 cm lymph nodes. Two lymph-node biopsy specimens showed no abnormality on histology. By day +168, abdominal pain and vomiting persisted, CD4 was 24 cells/µL, viral load 23,755 copies/mL, and he was presumed to have ART failure possibly due to malabsorption from thickened gut. An empiric trial of anti-tuberculosis therapy was started.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 70-year-old Caucasian man with IgG-kappa multiple myeloma diagnosed one year ago (initial K-FLC 685 mg/dL, λ 0.13 mg/dL; no lytic bone lesions or organ involvement) presented with 3 months of worsening dyspnea, dysphagia, and weight loss. His history also included hypertension, type II diabetes, atrial fibrillation, and chronic kidney disease with Bence Jones proteinuria. On examination, he was hypoxic (78% on room air), tachypneic with accessory muscle use, and had bibasilar crackles and an irregular rhythm with a holosystolic grade III/VI apical murmur. Arterial blood gas showed pH 7.31, pCO2 60 mmHg, pO2 60 mmHg, and bicarbonate 33 mmol/L. Laboratory studies revealed creatinine 2.55 mg/dL (baseline 1.2), hemoglobin 10.3 g/dL, WBC 7.4×10^3/μL, BNP 572 pg/mL, and troponin I levels of 0.22–0.21 ng/mL. Current free light chains were K-FLC 1379 mg/dL and L-FLC 0.65 mg/dL (K/L ratio 2121). A chest radiograph showed mild pulmonary vascular congestion and small bilateral pleural effusions. Echocardiography demonstrated a pulmonary artery systolic pressure of 64 mmHg, biatrial dilatation, normal ventricular size, and preserved ejection fraction. A ventilation–perfusion scan was negative for acute or chronic pulmonary embolus. After diuresis, right heart catheterization revealed a pulmonary artery pressure of 34/9/18 mmHg and a pulmonary capillary wedge pressure of 10 mmHg. Despite diuresis, he developed worsening hypercapnic respiratory failure requiring continuous noninvasive positive-pressure ventilation. Pulmonary function tests showed restrictive physiology (FEV1 61%, FVC 58%, FEV1/FVC 104%). A nerve conduction study suggested bilateral phrenic axonal neuropathy.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

options.D: Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction

question:
A 19-year-old male, born in 1990 to consanguineous parents, was referred for evaluation of multisystem involvement. His father had renal failure and familial Mediterranean fever. Beginning at 6 months, he had recurrent fevers and an absence of sweating. At 8 months, with eruption of his first teeth, he began biting his fingers and toes and sustaining tongue and skin injuries. By age 6, he had learning difficulties, multiple bone fractures from repeated trauma, and by age 9 was reported to have Lesch–Nyhan syndrome. At age 10, he underwent multiple hip‐reduction surgeries for recurrent dislocations. By age 16, he had deformed limbs, a sacral decubitus ulcer, and chronic osteomyelitis of the right elbow, both ankles, and both feet; cultures from right‐elbow ulcers grew methicillin‐resistant Staphylococcus aureus, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Acinetobacter. At 19 years, he underwent bilateral below-knee amputations. Two months after surgery, he developed ascites. Laboratory evaluation showed uric acid 4.1 mg/dL, creatinine 0.1 mg/dL, serum albumin 1.3 g/dL, ascitic fluid albumin 0.1 g/dL, and a 24-hour urine protein excretion of 6.29 g. Six months later, he developed generalized edema and new-onset diarrhea. Colonoscopy revealed multiple erythematous, ulcerating lesions throughout the colon; biopsy demonstrated severe crypt abscesses, goblet cell depletion, and lamina propria inflammation consistent with idiopathic inflammatory bowel disease suggestive of Crohn’s disease, and mesalamine was initiated. During the same admission, he had generalized tonic–clonic seizures; brain MRI and EEG were normal.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

options.B: Hereditary sensory and autonomic neuropathy type I
options.D: Congenital insensitivity to pain and anhydrosis

question:
A previously healthy 9-month-old boy was transferred after 8 days of fever and new-onset macroscopic hematuria. He had an initial self-limited diarrheal illness, then developed visible blood and pyuria on repeat urine microscopy. Empiric antibiotics for presumed urinary tract infection produced no defervescence. He had reduced oral intake and irritability but normal development and immunizations. 

On examination, temperature was 103 °F; he was ill-appearing, irritable, and well-hydrated. A 1.5 cm right cervical lymph node was palpable, and a diffuse maculopapular rash was present. No Bacillus Calmette–Guérin reaction was noted. Other systems were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies revealed white blood count 24 × 10^3 cells/mm^3 (80% neutrophils), hemoglobin 8 g/dL, platelets 440 × 10^3 cells/mm^3, C-reactive protein 96 mg/dL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate 80 mm/hr, alanine transaminase 98 IU/dL, aspartate transaminase 120 IU/dL, γ-glutamyl transferase 156 IU/dL, total protein 5.8 g/dL, albumin 2.4 g/dL; renal function, ferritin, and lipid profile were normal. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis was normal. Serologies for Epstein–Barr virus, cytomegalovirus, influenza, and Mycoplasma were negative. Urine, blood, and CSF cultures were sterile. Chest radiograph was normal. Abdominal ultrasound showed mild hepatomegaly. 

On illness day 12, echocardiography demonstrated dilatation of the left coronary artery (4.75 mm).

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

An 8-month-old child presented with progressive head enlargement since birth, global developmental delay (developmental age equivalent to 3 months), and cranial bruits. The occipitofrontal circumference was 54 cm (normal 43–45 cm). Brain MRI showed a well-circumscribed, markedly T2-hypointense midline lesion dorsal to the third ventricle with aqueductal obstruction, resulting in dilation of the lateral and third ventricles and a normal fourth ventricle. Digital subtraction angiography confirmed a mural type vein of Galen aneurysmal malformation with feeders from both posterior cerebral arteries and the left anterior cerebral artery. The child underwent transarterial embolisation via Onyx injection into the posteromedial choroidal branches of the right posterior cerebral artery, achieving occlusion of the venous sac. On day 3 post-embolisation MRI showed minimal reduction in sac size, mild peripheral rim enhancement, and no internal restricted diffusion. One-month follow-up MRI demonstrated a residual aneurysmal sac with thick peripheral mural enhancement, marked restricted diffusion, and persistent hydrocephalus.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 26-year-old man with no history of hypertension or heart disease presented with a 3-day history of upper abdominal pain. He reported epigastric tenderness with rebound and a positive Murphy’s sign. He was afebrile. Laboratory tests showed mild jaundice and a mildly elevated leukocyte count; procalcitonin and C-reactive protein levels were normal. An abdominal CT scan demonstrated a hyperdense gallbladder, mild gallbladder wall swelling, and fluid collections in the chest, pericardial sac, and abdomen. Electrocardiography showed ST-segment depression with T-wave inversion in V1–4 and a prolonged QT interval. The patient was taken to the operating room for an emergency laparotomy; intraoperatively, there was no evidence of bowel perforation, gallbladder enlargement, or cholangitis, but there was ascites and bowel wall edema. Approximately 1000 mL of peritoneal fluid was drained, and the patient was transferred to the intensive care unit.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 34-year-old woman presented with a 6-year history of a painless swelling of the lower jaw, initially 1 cm in diameter on the left, which gradually enlarged to involve both sides of the face. She reported only difficulty in chewing. On examination, there was gross facial asymmetry with fullness of the cheeks and lower lip and obliteration of the mentolabial sulcus and nasolabial folds. A well-demarcated, hard, lobulated, nontender swelling measured approximately 8×6×5 cm over the left cheek and a diffuse hard, lobulated, nontender swelling extended from the left mandibular ramus to the right mandibular body, obliterating the inferior border. No cervical lymphadenopathy was present.

Intraorally, a diffuse, hard, lobulated swelling extended from the left to the right retromolar regions, with bilateral buccal, labial, and lingual cortical expansion and intact mucosa. A provisional diagnosis of a benign fibro-osseous lesion of the mandible was made, and the differential diagnoses included fibrous dysplasia, ossifying/cementifying fibroma, and central giant cell granuloma (non-aggressive).

Laboratory tests, including complete blood count, serum protein, calcium, phosphorus, and alkaline phosphatase levels, were within normal limits.

Panoramic radiograph showed a well-defined mixed radiopaque and radiolucent lesion from the left ramus to the right angle of the mandible, measuring about 11×7 cm, with a “cotton-wool” pattern of calcification, concentric growth producing a ball-like outline, and bowing—but no erosion—of the inferior mandibular border. Occlusal radiograph revealed buccal and lingual cortical plate expansion with lingual displacement of teeth. Posterior–anterior mandible view confirmed a mixed radiopaque–radiolucent expansile lesion extending from the left ramus to the right mandibular angle. Axial and 3D CT scans demonstrated a large (11.8×7.0×7.3 cm) expansile lesion of the right and left mandibular bodies and left ramus, with mixed sclerotic and lucent areas and central low-density foci.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 30-year-old man first noted right calf muscle cramps in 2013. Twelve months later, he developed right foot drop, fasciculations in the right leg, and wasting of the right anterior compartment. Neurological examination in 2014 showed 2/5 strength of the right ankle extensors, right leg amyotrophy, brisk reflexes in the lower limbs, a positive right Babinski sign, and no sensory deficits.

By 2015, fasciculations were present throughout the body, painful cramps and weakness appeared in the left shin, and thigh muscle atrophy developed. Deep tendon reflexes remained brisk except for absent Achilles reflexes bilaterally; Hoffmann sign was positive. There were no bulbar or respiratory symptoms.

In 2015, T2-weighted MRI of the thoracic spine demonstrated a slitlike syrinx (central canal dilation) extending from T3 to T9 without Chiari malformation or extramedullary compression; brain and cervical MRI were normal. EMG in 2015 revealed fibrillation potentials, fasciculations, and large‐amplitude, long‐duration motor–unit potentials in both upper and lower limbs without conduction block. GM-1 and GM-2 antibody titers were negative, and routine blood and CSF studies were normal. Follow-up spinal MRI in 2016 and 2019 showed no change in the cavity size.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 67‐year‐old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus and nephropathy presented with a 4‐month history of an asymptomatic rash and swelling over his right thigh. He had undergone a cadaveric renal transplant six years earlier and was maintained on cyclosporine, mycophenolic acid, and prednisone. He wore a prosthetic right lower limb after a below‐knee amputation; the eruption began distally on his residual limb and spread proximally. Empiric antifungal cream was ineffective, and topical corticosteroid worsened the lesions. He had traveled to Thailand, Vietnam, and Panama. 

On examination, there were multiple erythematous papules and plaques, some coalescing into an annular pattern on the right thigh. A punch biopsy showed dermal nonnecrotizing tuberculoid granulomas with lymphocytes and rare neutrophils. Acid‐fast staining revealed rare acid‐fast bacilli and demonstrated granulomatous involvement of a peripheral nerve bundle. Period acid‐Schiff stain was negative for fungal elements.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 4-week-old male infant, born at 30 weeks’ gestation (birth weight 1665 g; Apgar scores 8 and 9), was admitted at birth for respiratory distress and cyanosis requiring intubation. On initial examination, he had generalized edema and a grade III–IV holosystolic murmur. Chest radiographs and echocardiography revealed a moderate-to-large patent ductus arteriosus, moderate-to-severe pulmonary hypertension, and biventricular hypertrophy suggestive of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy; a bedside PDA ligation was performed. Subsequent evaluation showed Coxsackie B viremia by PCR, and TORCH serologies were negative. He was treated with intravenous immunoglobulin, loop diuretics, a phosphodiesterase-3 inhibitor, and dopamine for presumed viral cardiomyopathy. Despite therapy, daily chest radiographs demonstrated progressive nonspecific calcification of the chest soft tissues and the vessels of both arms, and his condition worsened. He died at 4 weeks of age from intraventricular hemorrhage and multiorgan failure.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 35-year-old woman presented with a one-year history of mobile teeth in the lower left molar region and progressive difficulty chewing associated with pain and reduced mouth opening.
