Laboratory tests showed a normocytic, normochromic anemia (hemoglobin 5.4 g/dL), thrombocytopenia (42×10^9/L), a reticulocyte count of 101 500 cells/µL (index 1.1), hypercalcemia (2.9 mmol/L), elevated alkaline phosphatase (509 IU/L), and lactate dehydrogenase (547 IU/L). Renal and liver function tests were normal. Serum protein electrophoresis revealed no M-protein, and urine was negative for Bence Jones proteins. Urinalysis showed mild proteinuria and microscopic hematuria; urine culture grew Escherichia coli sensitive to cefuroxime. The prostate-specific antigen level was >100 ng/mL.

Radiographs of the right humerus demonstrated osteolytic lesions. Computed tomography of the spine, pelvis, and femur showed multiple osteolytic lesions. A lateral skull radiograph was normal.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 23-year-old Caucasian man presented with 5 months of persistent epigastric pain, intermittent nausea, nonbloody emesis, and a 20-lb weight loss. His vital signs were notable only for a heart rate of 97 beats/min. Examination revealed conjunctival pallor and epigastric tenderness. Laboratory studies showed iron deficiency anemia (hemoglobin 5.8 g/dL, MCV 67.8 fL, iron < 10 µg/dL, ferritin 2 ng/mL) and a hemoccult-positive stool; carcinoembryonic antigen, cancer antigen 19-9, and α-fetoprotein levels were normal. HIV and EBV PCR were nonreactive. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis demonstrated a markedly thickened heterogeneous loop of jejunum in the left hemiabdomen with mesenteric adenopathy and small-volume pelvic ascites. Push enteroscopy revealed a single ulcerated, malignant-appearing mass measuring 45 mm × 45 mm in the proximal jejunum; biopsy showed a high-grade malignant neoplasm, and fluorescence in situ hybridization was normal. PET–CT confirmed a hypermetabolic, multilobulated, ill-defined mass involving multiple jejunal loops.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

An 86-year-old woman was referred for several months of worsening vision. She had cataract surgery two years earlier and a 25-year history of open-angle glaucoma treated for seven years with brimonidine tartrate 0.1% twice daily in both eyes and artificial tears. She denied contact lens use or lid disorders and had no history of corneal disease. She reported gradual vision decline since her cataract surgery, most noticeable over the past three months, and experienced photosensitivity in both eyes. She had no history of hyperlipidemia or systemic disease.

On examination, best-corrected distance visual acuity was 20/60 in the right eye and 20/70 in the left. Manifest refraction was +0.75–1.00 × 160° OD and +0.50–1.25 × 165° OS; intraocular pressures were within normal limits. Slit-lamp examination revealed dermatochalasis, superficial punctate keratitis, and bilateral peripheral corneal opacities from the 4- to 5-o’clock and 7- to 8-o’clock positions with overlying neovascularization. Crocodile shagreen was noted bilaterally, with no guttata or lattice lines. Dilated fundus examination showed a blunted macular reflex in both eyes and cup-to-disc ratios of 0.65 OD and 0.75 OS.

An infectious and autoimmune workup was performed, including CBC with differential, ESR, CRP, ANA, c-ANCA, p-ANCA, rheumatoid factor, anti-SSA/Ro, anti-SSB/La, PPD, interferon-gamma release assay, chest radiography, ACE and lysozyme levels, MRSA swabs, stool ova and cysts, Lyme titers, syphilis testing, and review by the primary care physician for systemic rheumatologic disease. All test results were negative or within normal limits, and there were no systemic symptoms to suggest autoimmune disease.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 37-year-old woman presented 2 weeks after cesarean section with a 2-week history of cough, dyspnea, and head and neck swelling. Symptoms had begun in pregnancy and worsened over the last few days. The cough was nonproductive, with no hemoptysis. She had no history of cardiac or pulmonary disease or risk factors such as alcohol, tobacco, or illicit drugs.

On examination, heart rate was 110/min, blood pressure 110/75 mmHg, and she was afebrile. There was slight respiratory discomfort but no cyanosis. Lung fields were clear; heart sounds revealed wide fixed splitting of S2 without murmur. There was no clinical evidence of right heart failure.

Transthoracic echocardiography showed a severely dilated right atrium and right ventricle, a nonmobile 6.5 cm² mass attached to the right atrial wall, and a secundum-type atrial septal defect measuring 2.44 cm with a left-to-right shunt (QP/QS 2.9). A large extracardiac mass (8.7 × 4.3 cm) anterior to the ascending aorta was also noted.

Chest CT and pulmonary CT angiography demonstrated a lobulated anterior mediastinal soft-tissue mass (105 × 46 mm), suggestive of thymoma, teratoma, thyroid goiter, lymphoma, or germ cell tumor, with extension of thrombosis into the brachiocephalic veins, superior vena cava (SVC), and right atrium, as well as multiple pulmonary nodules. Abdominal and pelvic CT were unremarkable. Serum alpha-fetoprotein was 5000 ng/mL; blood cultures were negative.

At this point, by considering the findings of physical and laboratory examinations, echocardiography, thoracic CT scan, and pulmonary CT angiography, a mediastinal malignancy with cardiac and lung metastasis was at the top of our differential diagnoses. Atrial thrombosis was another potential diagnosis due to the hypercoagulable state because of the post‐partum period and the mediastinal probable malignancy. On the other hand, the absence of fever, negative blood culture, and no history of IV drug abuse made endocarditis vegetation a less likely diagnosis.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

options.D: Mediastinal malignancy with cardiac and lung metastasis

question:
An 18-year-old African-American man presented with a 5-day history of pruritic, maculopapular rash, periorbital swelling, fever, and transaminitis. Five days before presentation, he noted pruritus and rash over his extremities that progressed over several days to involve his chest, back, and face. Thirty-five days earlier, he had begun treatment for new-onset seizures with phenytoin extended-release, 100 mg daily, and levetiracetam, 500 mg twice daily. He had no other significant medical history, drug allergies, or alcohol use. Review of systems was positive for non-productive cough, fever, and tea-colored urine, and negative for chest pain, abdominal pain, shortness of breath, nausea, vomiting, weight loss, chills, or altered mental status.

On examination, his temperature was 40.2 °C, heart rate 88 beats/min, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, and blood pressure 110/55 mmHg. He was well developed and nourished, alert and oriented, and appeared uncomfortable but was not in distress. A fine exanthematous rash was present on the face and sun-exposed upper and lower extremities without involvement of the oral mucosa, palms, or soles. There was profound periorbital edema preventing eye opening. The abdomen was soft, non-distended, and nontender, with no hepatosplenomegaly. Neurological examination showed no focal deficits.

Laboratory studies on admission:
• WBC 7.9 × 10^3/mm^3 (60% neutrophils, 8% lymphocytes, 4% eosinophils; absolute eosinophils 0.32 × 10^3/mm^3)  
• Free phenytoin level 0.4 µg/mL (therapeutic 1.0–2.0 µg/mL)  
• Basic metabolic panel within normal limits  
• AST 778 U/L, ALT 1274 U/L  
• Acetaminophen and salicylate levels undetectable  
• Serologies for hepatitis A, B, and C negative  
• Electrocardiogram and echocardiogram normal  
• EBV, CMV, and HIV testing negative  
• Antinuclear antibody test negative

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

options.A: Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms

question:
A 7-month-old Hispanic male was referred for nystagmus. He was born at term with normal growth but exhibited motor and social developmental delay. Family history was notable for an older sister with visual abnormalities; his parents and two older brothers had no visual problems. 

On examination, he fixed and followed with the left eye but not the right. Cycloplegic refraction was −2.50 + 3.50 × 090° OD and −7.00 + 3.50 × 090° OS. Anterior segments were unremarkable bilaterally. Fundus examination of the right eye revealed a tilted optic nerve with trace pallor, a large posterior serous retinal detachment involving the macula with surrounding demarcation lines and a subretinal fibrotic band, and otherwise thin, atrophic retina without identifiable tears or holes. The left eye showed a tilted optic nerve, tessellated retina, RPE mottling, and central macular atrophy without detachment.

B-scan ultrasound of the right eye confirmed posterior subretinal fluid. Fluorescein angiography of the right eye demonstrated early and late hyperfluorescence consistent with pooling; the left eye showed RPE window defects and staining of drusen-like deposits. A full-field electroretinogram showed moderately to severely depressed responses from both the cone and rod systems. Optical coherence tomography of the right eye showed an elevated retina with subretinal fluid; the left eye showed RPE changes and thinning.

A hereditary retinal dystrophy panel covering ~180 genes revealed a mutation in CNGB3 associated with achromatopsia. This result, however, was inconsistent with the clinical presentation.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 49-year-old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 11.1% one month earlier), untreated hypertension, hyperlipidaemia, morbid obesity (BMI 42 kg/m^2), and obstructive sleep apnoea presented with 4 days of diarrhoea, nausea, vomiting, low-grade fever, and chills. He denied respiratory or chest symptoms. On first presentation, he was tachycardic (102 b.p.m.) but otherwise afebrile and normotensive; chest radiograph showed mild pulmonary congestion. He was tested for SARS-CoV-2 and discharged after intravenous fluids. 

One day later, he returned with fever (39.5°C), diarrhoea, dyspnoea, and severe cough. SARS-CoV-2 testing was positive. On examination, he was tachypnoeic (26 breaths/min), tachycardic (108 b.p.m.), with coarse bilateral breath sounds; cardiac exam was normal. Repeat chest radiograph showed patchy alveolar opacities superimposed on diffuse interstitial changes.

Laboratory results:
- WBC 5.8×10^3/µL, lymphocytes 0.6×10^3/µL  
- CRP 9.79 mg/dL, LDH 296 U/L, ferritin 482 ng/dL, IL-6 83.75 pg/mL  
- D-dimer <150 µg/L  
- Troponin I 0.018 ng/mL, BNP 25 pg/mL  

Arterial blood gas on nasal cannula showed pH 7.38, PaO₂ 66.2 mmHg, PaCO₂ 38.5 mmHg. Electrocardiogram on admission was normal sinus rhythm at 78 b.p.m.

He was admitted to the ICU for respiratory failure and treated with hydroxychloroquine. Despite therapy, inflammatory markers and oxygen requirements worsened, and he was intubated on hospital Day 6. Cardiac monitoring demonstrated episodes of premature ventricular complexes with ventricular bigeminy. 

On Day 7, transthoracic echocardiography revealed global left ventricular hypokinesis with an ejection fraction of 37% (previously 60–65% two months earlier) and markedly impaired global longitudinal strain averaging –1%.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 21-year-old Irish woman presented with a 3-week history of an enlarging, firm, painless right-sided neck mass. She denied dysphagia, stridor, fevers, weight loss, and hypercalcemic symptoms except mild constipation and two presyncopal episodes with exercise. Her medical history was unremarkable. Family history included sudden cardiac death in a maternal grandmother, two maternal aunts with hyperthyroidism and goitres, and early-onset hypertension in her mother.

On examination, vital signs showed a heart rate of 112 bpm and blood pressure of 116/80 mm Hg. There was a firm, non-tender swelling in the right thyroid lobe without lymphadenopathy. Thyroid and renal function tests were normal: serum creatinine 64 μmol/L and eGFR 124 mL/min/1.73 m2. Corrected serum calcium was elevated at 3.96 mmol/L (2.2–2.6), phosphate 0.35 mmol/L (0.8–1.5), and albumin 49 g/L. She received IV fluids and bisphosphonates; calcium fell to 2.46 mmol/L in 3 days. 

Neck ultrasound revealed a 5×4 cm solid and cystic nodule in the lower pole of the right thyroid lobe. Two days later, intact PTH was 138.4 pmol/L (1.6–6.9). Six days after presentation, calcium had rebounded to 2.73 mmol/L, vitamin D was low at 35 nmol/L, and alkaline phosphatase rose from 143 to 240 IU/L. A chest radiograph was unremarkable. 

Differential considerations included parathyroid adenoma, parathyroid hyperplasia, parathyroid carcinoma, or multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1. Tc-99m sestamibi single-photon emission CT/CT demonstrated a 7–10 cm tracer-avid lesion posterior to the right thyroid lobe. Twenty-four–hour urinary calcium was elevated at 9 mmol (2.5–7.5), and prolactin, urinary catecholamines, and metanephrines were normal.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 57-year-old woman presented with recurrent fevers and painful leg ulcers for two months.
Eight months earlier she developed vertigo and a fullness sensation in her left ear; audiometry confirmed left sensorineural hearing loss.
Two weeks later she had fever with chills; chest radiograph showed a right‐lung infiltrate and ESR was elevated; her symptoms improved with oral antibiotics.
Subsequently she noted gingival hypertrophy. CT of the paranasal sinuses revealed subcutaneous thickening of the right buccal area, and she improved with ibuprofen.
Her history included hypothyroidism on levothyroxine. She denied recent travel but cared for sick pets.
On examination, temperature was 37.5°C, blood pressure 139/69 mm Hg, pulse 78/min, respirations 16/min, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Tender erythematous nodules were present on both lower extremities; otherwise normal.
Laboratory tests showed normocytic anemia with normal white‐cell and platelet counts, normal renal and liver function, and an unremarkable urinalysis.
Blood and urine cultures were negative. The test for latent tuberculosis by interferon gamma release assay (IGRA) was also negative. Serology for Brucella abortus and melitensis also came negative. Serological tests for connective‐tissue disease were negative as was the serum angiotensin converting enzyme level.
Abdominal ultrasound revealed multiple hypoechoic cystic lesions in both liver lobes. Contrast‐enhanced CT confirmed multiple oval hypoattenuating hepatic lesions.
Liver biopsy showed cholestasis and large irregular necrotic areas palisaded by epithelioid cells with granulomatous inflammation and plasma cells; acid‐fast bacilli culture remained negative at six weeks.
She continued to spike fevers up to 101°F, and her leg lesions progressed to central necrosis with purulent discharge. Pus cultures were negative for bacterial, fungal, and mycobacterial growth.
Skin biopsy demonstrated surface ulceration, fibrinoid necrosis and vasculitis of superficial and deep dermal vessels with granuloma formation, neutrophilic infiltration, and debris. Immunohistochemistry and PCR for mycobacterial species on the biopsy were negative.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 20-year-old Caucasian woman with no past medical history presented with sudden onset of numbness and weakness of her left arm and leg. On neurologic examination, she had 4/5 strength of the left upper and lower extremities, no cranial-nerve deficits, no cerebellar signs, and cortical sensory loss to light touch and stereognosis on the left; pain and temperature sensation were preserved.

Noncontrast head CT showed a well-demarcated, 2.1-cm hypodense lesion in the right parietal lobe without surrounding edema. Brain MRI demonstrated a concentric mass in the right parietal white matter that was hypointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2-weighted images. After gadolinium, the lesion enhanced inhomogeneously, forming alternating rings of enhancement and nonenhancement.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 37-year-old man presented with headache, psychomotor retardation, and left hemiparesis. Laboratory evaluation revealed HIV infection (CD4+ T-cell count 20 cells/μL; plasma HIV RNA 301,000 copies/mL) and positive serology for Toxoplasma gondii. A brain CT scan showed multiple scattered parenchymal lesions with perilesional edema, mass effect, and ring contrast enhancement. He received anti-toxoplasma therapy without corticosteroids, with clinical and radiologic improvement, and initiated HAART one month after diagnosis (regimen as per institutional protocol). Toxoplasmosis therapy continued (followed by suppressive dosing), and MAC prophylaxis was maintained.

Three months after HAART initiation, he reported abrupt onset of right lower-limb numbness that progressed over one month to involve his entire right lower limb and right hemi-abdomen. He denied fever or other systemic symptoms and affirmed full adherence to all therapies. On examination, mental status and cranial nerves were normal. Apart from his prior left hemiparesis, there was a distinct sensory level at T9 on the right, with loss of touch and pain sensation below this level; strength, proprioception, and reflexes in the affected areas were preserved. 

A follow-up brain CT scan revealed that some prior lesions had improved, others had slightly worsened with ring enhancement and edema, and several new lesions had appeared. MRI of the dorsal spinal cord demonstrated T2-weighted hyperintense lesions extending from T3 to T9, with a probable posterolateral lesion at T7–T8. At this time, CD4+ T-cell count had risen to 128 cells/μL and plasma HIV RNA had fallen to 121 copies/mL. CSF analysis showed 5 cells/μL, protein 58 mg/dL, glucose 51 mg/dL; Gram and acid-fast stains, microbiological cultures, cryptococcal antigen, VDRL, and PCR assays for JC virus, BK virus, cytomegalovirus, herpes simplex virus, human herpesvirus 6, varicella-zoster virus, and enterovirus were negative. CSF PCR for T. gondii was not performed. EBV PCR was positive, but a normal CSF lymphocyte phenotype and a whole-body PET scan showed no hypermetabolic cerebral or medullary lesions. HTLV-I/II serology was negative.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

An 8-year-old previously healthy girl presented with sudden onset of mid–cervical back and neck pain followed by bilateral hand weakness. Earlier that evening, she had received repetitive kicks to her upper back while playing. She did not lose consciousness but noted progressive inability to move her hands later that night. By the following morning, grip strength was markedly diminished, making it difficult to pick up objects.

On arrival, vital signs were normal. She complained of neck and back pain but denied sensory changes. Neurologic examination revealed 0/5 strength in bilateral triceps, wrist flexors, wrist extensors, and intrinsic hand muscles; biceps and shoulder abductors were 5/5. Lower extremity motor and sensory function was intact. There were no bladder, bowel, or gait abnormalities; perianal sensation and rectal tone were normal.

A cervical spine MRI without contrast was obtained. Sagittal T1-weighted images showed a posterior disc protrusion at C5–C6. Sagittal STIR and T2 sequences demonstrated increased signal (edema) in the anterior spinal cord from C4 through C7 with minimal cord expansion. Axial T2 images localized the edema to the anterior two-thirds of the cord. Diffusion-weighted imaging revealed increased signal in the anterior cord spanning C4–C6 with corresponding signal drop-out on the apparent diffusion coefficient map, consistent with diffusion restriction.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 66‐year‐old African‐American woman, 4 years after bilateral lung transplantation for end‐stage pulmonary fibrosis, presented with 2 months of progressive dyspnea, wheezing, and 4 weeks of cough productive of scant blood‐tinged sputum. She denied fever or weight loss but reported nightly sweats for 6 months. Her immunosuppression regimen consisted of tacrolimus (goal trough 8–10 ng/mL) and prednisone 5 mg daily; azathioprine had been discontinued years earlier because of pancytopenia. Her medical history included chronic pancytopenia, stage IV chronic kidney disease, diabetes, hypertension, and gastroparesis. 

On examination, she was afebrile, respiratory rate 22/min, oxygen saturation 97% on room air; focal end‐expiratory wheezes were heard over the left lung. Laboratory studies showed pancytopenia (WBC 0.9×10^3/mL, hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL, platelets 134×10^3/mL) and elevated creatinine (2.37 mg/dL). CMV and EBV PCR were undetectable. 

Chest CT showed a 2.2×1.1×0.9 cm polypoid soft‐tissue lesion arising from the roof of the left mainstem bronchus at the anastomotic site, with severe luminal narrowing to 3 mm and tree‐in‐bud nodularity in the left lower lobe. 

Rigid bronchoscopy revealed a fibrinous mass protruding into the left mainstem bronchus proximal to the anastomosis. Multiple biopsies were obtained, and a bronchial wash was performed; samples were sent for histopathologic and microbiologic evaluation.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 19-year-old Hispanic woman with acne, asthma, and psychosocial distress presented to the ED with lightheadedness, generalized weakness, diaphoresis, diarrhea, and vomiting. 

First ED visit:  
• She reported a home glucose of 53 mg/dL, rising to 80 mg/dL after eating.  
• Triage vitals: BP 98/65 mmHg; HR 81 bpm; RR 18/min; SpO₂ 100% on room air; T 37.0°C.  
• Exam: well-developed, obese woman in no acute distress.  
• Point-of-care glucose was 60 mg/dL (confirmed by lab); other labs and examination were unremarkable.  
• She received IV saline, ondansetron, dietary advice for hypoglycemia, and was discharged.

Second ED visit (next day):  
• Found unresponsive at home with prehospital glucose 46 mg/dL treated with D50W.  
• Triage vitals: BP 122/75 mmHg; HR 100 bpm; RR 18/min; SpO₂ 100%; T 36.7°C.  
• Exam and labs again unremarkable. She was treated symptomatically and discharged.

Third ED visit (three weeks later):  
• Found unresponsive at work with glucose 23 mg/dL; EMS administered D50W to 172 mg/dL.  
• In the ED her glucose repeatedly fell despite multiple D50W boluses and escalating dextrose infusions (D5½NS then D10W), prompting ICU admission.  
• She had recurrent episodes of weakness, confusion, diaphoresis, palpitations, and altered mental status.  
• ECG: normal sinus rhythm, no acute changes.  
• Laboratory studies across visits showed hypoglycemia with otherwise normal hematologic, renal, and electrolyte panels (Table 2).  
• A standard drug screen was negative.  
• Further tests: hemoglobin A1C 5.1%; C-peptide 9.9 ng/mL; free insulin 370 mIU/L; total insulin 377 mIU/L.  
• Noncontrast head CT: no intracranial abnormalities.  
• Abdominal CT: right ovarian cyst; no pancreatic mass.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

options.C: Hypoglycemia due to exogenous insulin administration

question:
A 9-year-old boy with no significant medical history presented for dental evaluation of carious teeth and a two-month history of painless swelling in the right posterior maxilla. On examination there was a non-tender, expansile swelling of the right maxillary posterior region associated with carious primary maxillary canine and first and second molars. Panoramic radiograph showed a well-corticated, unilocular radiolucent lesion measuring approximately 4 × 3 cm from the distal aspect of the permanent right lateral incisor to the mesial aspect of the permanent right second premolar. Aspiration of the cystic cavity yielded golden-yellow fluid. Based on the patient’s age, lesion location, and radiographic appearance, both an inflammatory dentigerous cyst and unicystic ameloblastoma were considered in the preoperative differential.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 44-year-old man with a history of colonic diverticulitis presented with acute abdominal pain. On examination, his abdomen was distended with right lower quadrant tenderness. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed small-bowel loops dilated up to 4.3 cm in diameter, with a transition point in the mid-lower abdomen concerning for moderate enteric obstruction and asymmetric wall thickening of some ileal loops, along with a few borderline mesenteric lymph nodes. Diagnostic laparoscopy identified a nonperforated Meckel diverticulum in the mid-ileum that appeared prominent, inflamed, and indurated. 

Histologic examination of the resected diverticulum showed a true diverticulum lined by intestinal mucosa without ectopic gastric or pancreatic tissue. Microscopy revealed extensive infiltration of sheets of small lymphocytes with abundant cytoplasm (monocytoid B-cells) forming lymphoepithelial lesions in the mucosa and submucosa and focally involving the serosal adipose tissue. In some areas, there were nodular proliferations with occasional reactive germinal centers and an expanded marginal zone. Immunohistochemical studies demonstrated that the lymphocytes were positive for CD20 with weak coexpression of CD43 and positive for BCL-2, and negative for CD10, Cyclin-D1, CD3, and CD5. The reactive germinal centers were BCL-2–negative and BCL-6–positive, with no evidence of follicular colonization by tumor cells. In situ hybridization for kappa and lambda showed scattered polyclonal plasma cells, and the Ki-67 proliferation index was less than 5%.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 44-year-old man presented with diffuse left knee pain 3 years after a medial opening wedge high tibial osteotomy (HTO) fixed with a TomoFix plate for varus-associated medial compartment overload. He described persistent anterior tibial and lateral knee pain; his preoperative medial joint pain had resolved. Two years after HTO the hardware was removed, but the pain continued.

On examination there were no signs of infection; the previous medial wound had healed. There was tenderness over the anterior and medial tibia and the lateral-posterior knee joint line, with mild hypesthesia around the scar. Vascular examination was normal. Varus stress and rotational provocation tests elicited severe pain that necessitated termination of testing. Active range of motion was 0° to 120°.

Standard non–weight-bearing anteroposterior and lateral radiographs of the knee showed a well-healed osteotomy site with a distally placed plate and generalized osteoarthritic changes (Kellgren–Lawrence grade II–IV), particularly in the patellofemoral joint. Long-leg alignment films showed the mechanical axis (Mikulicz line) passing through the lateral tibiofemoral compartment.

Because the cause of persistent pain remained unclear, a 99mTc-HDP SPECT/CT was obtained. There was no increased tracer uptake in the tibiofemoral joint or osteotomy site, but there was markedly increased uptake in the proximal tibiofibular joint. Axial CT images demonstrated that the proximal tibial screws were excessively long and protruded into the proximal tibiofibular joint, which also showed advanced degenerative changes.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman was admitted with a one-month history of fever, asthenia, and inflammatory polyarthralgia involving small, medium, and large joints. She later developed chest pain, a productive cough with whitish sputum (worse at night), progressive exertional dyspnea, and bilateral lower-limb edema.  

Her medical and family history were unremarkable except for obstetric history: five pregnancies, four live births, and termination at 21 weeks.  

On examination, temperature was 37.2 °C, heart rate 110 beats/min, blood pressure 180/100 mm Hg. She appeared fatigued and anorexic. Hands and feet showed intermittent acrosyndrome without ulcers. There was no synovitis or tenosynovitis. Findings included painful hepatomegaly, jugular venous distension, a positive hepatojugular reflux, bilateral pitting edema of the lower limbs, dullness to percussion and absent breath sounds at the right lung base, and mild ascites. No splenomegaly or lymphadenopathy was noted.  

Initial laboratory studies revealed:  
• Hemoglobin 8.9 g/dL, leukocytes 3.28 G/L, platelets 83 G/L  
• ESR 85 mm in first hour, CRP 96 mg/L  
• Creatinine 141 µmol/L; AST 84 U/L, ALT 90 U/L  
• Triglycerides 2.41 g/L; D-dimer 9300 ng/mL  
• Sodium 121 mmol/L; albumin 13.4 g/L; total protein 43 g/L  
• 24-h proteinuria 0.65 g; ferritin 2675.6 ng/mL  
• Polyclonal hypergammaglobulinemia; normal liver synthetic function; prothrombin time 100%; aPTT 53.7 s  

Serologic and microbiologic tests:  
• HBsAg positive; hepatitis C, HIV, and COVID-19 serologies negative; AFB smear negative  
• ANA 1:1280 (speckled); anti–double-stranded DNA 380 IU/mL  

Bone-marrow aspiration showed a hypercellular marrow with cell lysis and numerous macrophages.  

Chest radiograph demonstrated a moderate right pleural effusion and cardiomegaly (cardiothoracic index 0.6). Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a 13.2 mm noncompressive pericardial effusion and a dilated heart with an ejection fraction of 35%.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 19-year-old unmarried woman was found to have hypertension (200/120 mmHg) six months earlier, when she was hospitalized for a febrile illness. She has been on losartan 50 mg twice daily without blood-pressure control. She denies muscle weakness, polyuria, nocturia, weight gain, bruising, acne, hirsutism, episodic headaches, or rheumatologic symptoms. There is no family history of early hypertension or intracerebral hemorrhage. She takes no other medications or supplements.

On examination, her BMI is 22 kg/m2. Pulse is 88 beats/minute and regular. Cardiovascular examination is unremarkable; there are no radio-radial or radio-femoral delays, murmurs, or bruits. Fundoscopy shows silver wiring of retinal vessels; other systems are normal.

Laboratory studies:
• Potassium 2.8 mmol/L (3.5–5.2) despite replacement  
• Sodium 136 mmol/L (135–145)  
• Bicarbonate 24.8 mEq/L (22–26)  
• Overnight dexamethasone suppression test <11 nmol/L (normal <50)  
• Aldosterone 49.7 ng/dL (1.76–39.2)  
• Plasma renin concentration 1275 μIU/mL (2.8–32.9)  
• Plasma renin activity 106.3 ng/mL/hr  
• Aldosterone-to-renin ratio (ARR) 0.5 (<10 suggests secondary aldosteronism)  
• TSH, 24-h urinary metanephrines, creatinine, ESR, and urine microscopy all normal  

Cardiac echocardiogram shows normal biventricular function without coarctation. Renal ultrasound shows normal renal size and parenchyma; renal artery Doppler is normal. CT angiogram excludes renal artery stenosis but reveals a 1.2 × 1 cm well-defined, exophytic, iso-dense lesion in the lower pole of the left kidney with delayed contrast enhancement.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 70-year-old Caucasian man, a non-smoker, presented with a 3-month history of 12 kg weight loss and recent generalized pruritus and jaundice. Serum biochemistry and abdominal ultrasonography confirmed obstructive jaundice. Contrast CT of the abdomen showed a 4.6×4.1 cm mass in the head of the pancreas without radiologic evidence of distant metastases. He underwent ERCP with stent placement, followed two months later by a pylorus-sparing pancreaticoduodenectomy, with an uneventful recovery.

Gross examination revealed a 5.5 cm lobulated tumor in the pancreatic head extending to the superior mesenteric vessel margin; the entire lesion was submitted for histology. Microscopically, the neoplasm consisted of large nests of basaloid cells with central necrosis and scattered small foci of squamoid differentiation. There was no morphologic evidence of glandular or acinar formation. The tumor showed extensive intravenous and intralymphatic invasion, foci of perineural invasion, extension into the duodenum, ampullary region, pancreatic duct, and peripancreatic fat, and microscopic involvement of the superior mesenteric vessel margin. Metastatic deposits with similar morphology were identified in 6 of 43 regional lymph nodes.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

options.C: Squamous cell carcinoma of the pancreas

question:
A 68-year-old Asian woman with a history of osteoporosis was admitted for vertebroplasty of a compression fracture of the third lumbar vertebra. Her surgical history included appendectomy, thyroid nodule removal, and rotator cuff repair under general anesthesia. Preoperative evaluation was unremarkable except for grade I diastolic dysfunction on echocardiography. Vertebroplasty was performed in the prone position under spontaneous ventilation with sevoflurane sedation; there were no intraoperative events. 

In the post–anesthesia care unit, she had sudden hemoptysis—enough to drench four to five gauze pads—and dyspnea, with oxygen saturation falling to 80%, which improved to 93% with 5 L/min of oxygen by mask. She was hemodynamically stable, with coarse breath sounds bilaterally. Postoperative chest radiography showed new bilateral perihilar consolidation with air bronchograms. On transfer to our institution, she was afebrile; pulse, 82/min; respiration, 20/min; blood pressure, 120/75 mmHg; and oxygen saturation, 97% on supplemental oxygen. Computed tomography of the chest revealed diffuse ground-glass opacities and interlobular septal thickening in the central portions of both lungs. Bronchoscopy was not performed at the patient’s request.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 64-year-old Caucasian woman presented with a 4-day history of painless vision loss in the right eye. Her medical history included major depressive disorder, hypercholesterolemia, and bladder surgery; she also reported annual oral herpangina and white skin patches on her forearms. She works as a real estate agent with recent exposure to old, damp, moldy homes, smokes one pack of cigarettes daily, and previously lived in Iran, Turkey, and Switzerland.

On examination, best-corrected visual acuity was counting fingers at 1.5 ft in the right eye and 20/25 in the left eye. Intraocular pressures were normal bilaterally. Slit-lamp examination of the right eye showed fine keratic precipitates, 2+ conjunctival injection, 3+ cells and flare, and inferonasal posterior synechiae; the left eye had 1+ conjunctival injection, no cells, and 1+ flare. Fundus examination of the right eye revealed 3+ vitreous haze with “snowballs” and a “string-of-pearls” configuration and a possible chorioretinal lesion; the left eye had 1+ vitreous haze, mild peripheral retinal hemorrhage, and periphlebitis without definite infiltrates. Fluorescein angiography was inconclusive in the right eye and showed disc and inferior vessel leakage in the left eye.

Initial laboratory studies, including complete blood count, angiotensin-converting enzyme level, lysozyme level, C-reactive protein, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and urinalysis, were all within normal limits. B-scan ultrasonography demonstrated hyperechogenicity of the vitreous and possible chorioretinal lesions. Chest radiograph showed diffuse fine linear and interstitial pulmonary infiltrates, minimal mediastinal widening, and no hilar lymphadenopathy.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 16-year-old adolescent presented with an extensive, pruritic rash that began abruptly 1 week earlier. The lesions were annular, arciform, or polycyclic, erythematous with an edematous peripheral border and a pale or ecchymotic center, distributed on the trunk, limbs, and face, with sparing of the palms and soles. Individual lesions were labile, resolving entirely within 24 hours, and dermographism was present. There was no edema of the extremities. There were no ulcerations of the oral or genital mucosa, and no target lesions. The throat was mildly erythematous. He was in good general condition and reported a 10-day history of cough and rhinorrhea. He had no significant past medical history, no known allergies, no recent medications, and no recent vaccinations. Laboratory studies revealed a C-reactive protein level of 50 mg/L (normal <5 mg/L) and a normal leukocyte count. Viral serologies for EBV, CMV, parvovirus, and hepatitis were negative, as was the antinuclear factor. A SARS-CoV-2 PCR test was also negative.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 68-year-old woman was referred for gradually increased facial hirsutism and deepening of the voice over 6 months. She had entered menopause at age 52 and had an unremarkable family history. Her medical history included hypertension managed with telmisartan and osteoarthritis treated with topical NSAID creams. On examination, blood pressure was 140/90 mmHg, pulse 84 beats/min, and temperature 36.9 °C. She had plethoric facies and terminal hair growth on the upper lip, chin, and upper abdomen (Ferriman–Gallwey score 4). There was no clitoromegaly or temporal balding. The remainder of her examination was normal. Laboratory tests showed polycythemia (hemoglobin 18 g/dL, hematocrit 54%), normal electrolytes and biochemistry, and a serum testosterone level of 25.40 nmol/L (normal range for women, 0.38–1.97 nmol/L). Further testing revealed a 17-hydroxyprogesterone level of 302.57 pmol/L and a cortisol level of 2.7 nmol/L after a 1 mg dexamethasone suppression test. Pelvic MRI showed ovarian dimensions of 22×12 mm and 22×14 mm without masses, and adrenal CT demonstrated normal gland size and configuration. Serial measurements on hospitalization days 4, 6, 8, and 10 showed a decline in testosterone levels to 12, 7.4, 4.3, and 2.1 nmol/L, respectively.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 46-year-old man with hypertension, dyslipidemia, and a 2-year history of arthralgias under rheumatologic surveillance (no arthritis, fever, uveitis, or systemic autoimmune symptoms) was referred for evaluation of renal dysfunction. Routine laboratories showed serum creatinine 1.7 mg/dL (baseline 1.3 mg/dL two years earlier) and subnephrotic proteinuria (protein/creatinine ratio 2.3 g/g) with a bland urine sediment. On examination, his BMI was 35 kg/m2 and blood pressure 150/90 mmHg. Repeat testing demonstrated creatinine 1.4 mg/dL, albumin 3.5 g/dL, LDL 85 mg/dL, triglycerides 272 mg/dL, and 24-hour urine protein 2.7 g with normal sediment. Serologic tests for HIV and hepatitis B/C were negative; hepatic profile was normal. An immunologic panel showed a low‐titre anti-M-type phospholipase A2 receptor (anti-PLA2R) antibody (1:10), with other tests within normal limits. Renal ultrasonography was unremarkable. Antiproteinuric therapy was intensified with an angiotensin receptor blocker and an SGLT2 inhibitor. Concurrently, rheumatology diagnosed rheumatoid arthritis (RF 35 IU/mL; anti-CCP 432 U/mL), and methotrexate was initiated.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 43-year-old man presented with progressive respiratory distress. He was a heavy smoker. On admission, CT of the chest showed a 55 mm mass in the right upper lobe extending into the anterior mediastinum, with multiple enlarged regional lymph nodes. An ultrasound-guided lymph node biopsy was performed, and the initial pathological diagnosis was poorly differentiated non–small-cell lung carcinoma, adenocarcinoma type. Despite systemic chemotherapy, the tumor was refractory, and the patient died six months after admission. 
At autopsy, the thoracic mass measured 10 cm and extended from the right upper lobe to the anterior mediastinum, with widespread lymph node metastases. On gross examination, the tumor was solid, opalescent, and necrotic. Microscopically, it showed sheets of monotonous epithelial-like cells with loose intercellular connections, distinct nucleoli, rhabdoid-like cells, frequent mitoses, and areas of necrosis. 
Immunohistochemical staining showed the tumor cells were negative for desmin, S100P, TTF-1, p40, CK7, CK20, SMARCA4, and SMARCA2, and positive for SOX2, CD34, p53, and AE1/AE3. Molecular testing for EGFR mutations and ALK and ROS1 rearrangements was negative.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 21-year-old man was brought to the emergency department by his aunt after first presenting with sudden-onset severe anxiety and the sensation that he was dying. He has high-functioning autism and obsessive-compulsive disorder; there is no other psychiatric or medical family history. He reported using cocaine that day, but collateral history denied this. He was diagnosed with a panic attack and discharged. A few hours later he returned, agitated, pacing, and nonverbal.  

Initial vital signs and physical examination were unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed normal complete blood count, liver function, renal profile, and inflammatory markers. A urine toxicology screen was negative. Notable findings included a low serum phosphate of 0.35 mmol/L and an elevated prolactin of 737 mu/L. Skull radiograph showed no fractures. MRI of the head was unremarkable. Lumbar puncture analysis returned with normal protein and glucose and was negative for bacteria or viruses.  

He was referred for psychiatric assessment and admitted to the acute psychiatric unit 9 days after initial presentation. On admission, he was severely disoriented to time, place, and person. He exhibited major thought block with markedly limited, stilted speech. He appeared dazed and had increased muscle tone, spastic movements, and preserved withdrawal to painful stimuli. His speech content was disjointed and bizarre; examples included “this is not the original,” “two people joined in the middle, different timelines,” and “I am not the soft science in the Jurassic thoughts.” He occasionally answered yes/no questions with great effort but could not recall his responses. Mental status examination showed a well-kept young man with poor eye contact and an intense stare, normal tone and volume of speech but extremely limited and incoherent content. Mood was euthymic, but thoughts were chaotic, and he lacked insight and capacity regarding his mental state.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

options.C: Acute psychosis due to substance abuse

question:
A 62-year-old man presented to the emergency department with severe right upper‐quadrant abdominal pain, moderate epigastric pain, and chest pain. He reported a COVID-19 infection 14 days earlier, after which he developed mild right upper‐quadrant discomfort that progressed over 3 days to severe pain aggravated by eating and unrelieved by changes in position. The chest pain was described as atypical for cardiac origin and was accompanied by nausea and vomiting. He denied alcohol use, trauma, and had no known chronic diseases; family history was notable for cholecystectomy in his mother and sisters.

On arrival, blood pressure was 130/80 mmHg, pulse 80/min, respiratory rate 20/min, temperature 37°C, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Examination revealed elevated jugular venous pressure without peripheral edema, normal abdominal percussion, and a liver span of 14.5 cm at the midclavicular line.

Electrocardiogram showed bradyarrhythmia, T-wave inversion in lead V1, and ST‐segment elevation in leads V2 through V6. Chest and abdominal radiographs were unremarkable, with no evidence of pneumonia, air under the diaphragm, or biliary air; gallbladder sludge was noted. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated trivial mitral regurgitation and an ejection fraction of 55%.

Laboratory tests revealed elevated high-sensitivity troponin (0 h: 1547.6 pg/ml; 6 h: 1342.8 pg/ml), amylase 2420 U/L, lipase 987 U/L, AST 800 IU/L, ALT 760 IU/L, alkaline phosphatase 361 IU/L, total bilirubin 3.27 mg/dl, direct bilirubin 2.37 mg/dl, blood sugar 203 mg/dl, and urea 68 mg/dl.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 42-year-old Sri Lankan shopkeeper with diet‐controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus presented with three weeks of generalized malaise, nightly “feverishness,” evening sweats, anorexia, and 3-kg weight loss. He denied cough, hemoptysis, or tuberculosis contacts. There was nausea without vomiting or abdominal pain and no features of autoimmune disease. He had no significant environmental or travel exposures and no history of recurrent infections.

On examination he was afebrile, mildly pale, with mild nontender hepatomegaly but no lymphadenopathy, rashes, or splenomegaly. Blood pressure was 110/70 mmHg without postural drop. Initial labs showed pancytopenia (WBC 3.0×10^9/L, platelets 120×10^6/L, hemoglobin 10 g/dL), CRP 8 mg/L, ESR 60 mm/h, normal liver and renal profiles, and HbA1c 6.5%. Chest radiograph and echocardiogram were normal. Blood and urine cultures, Mantoux test, and bone marrow biopsy (including fungal and tuberculosis studies) were negative. Abdominal ultrasound showed mild hepatosplenomegaly, para‐aortic lymphadenopathy, and bilateral hypoechoic adrenal masses.

One week later he returned with three days of severe nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, and postural dizziness. He was hypotensive (90/60 mmHg with a 30 mmHg postural drop), tachycardic (100 bpm), and dehydrated. Despite fluids his blood pressure remained low. Labs showed hyponatremia (114 mmol/L), hyperkalemia (5.0 mmol/L), random cortisol 1.3 µg/L, and improved blood counts. An ACTH stimulation test showed persistently low cortisol (<1 µg/L). CT of the chest and abdomen revealed bilateral, well‐defined, homogeneously enhancing adrenal masses (left 6.9×3.7×5.0 cm; right 5.3×3.4×5.4 cm), mild hepatosplenomegaly, and patchy para‐aortic lymphadenopathy.

Ultrasound‐guided adrenal biopsy showed necrosis with plasma cells, lymphocytes, and histiocytes. Gomori–Grocott methenamine silver staining demonstrated numerous fungal spores morphologically similar to Histoplasma.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

An 81-year-old Korean man was seen urgently for a 2-month history of a temporal C-shaped scotoma in his left eye. He had a 3-year history of neovascular age-related macular degeneration (nAMD) treated with anti-VEGF in the right eye (last injection 9 months prior) and controlled nonproliferative diabetic retinopathy in both eyes. Other medical history included type 2 diabetes, hypothyroidism, and extensive-stage small-cell lung cancer (T2b/3, N3, M1a) diagnosed 5 months earlier. He had received five cycles of durvalumab (1,500 mg every 3 weeks). Before cancer treatment, best-corrected visual acuity was 20/100 in the right eye and 20/20 in the left; intraocular pressures were normal. 

On presentation, visual acuity was 20/200 OD and 20/25 OS. The anterior segments were unremarkable. Fundus examination showed an atrophic macula in the right eye from prior nAMD and only mild pigmentary changes in the left eye. Fundus autofluorescence revealed broad C-shaped hypoautofluorescent areas with hyperfluorescent margins in both eyes. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) demonstrated loss of the peripapillary ellipsoid zone in the left eye corresponding to the visual field defect. Fluorescein angiography showed normal vascular perfusion.

Routine laboratory tests, including a CBC and renal function, were normal except for mildly elevated AST and ALT. Abdominal ultrasound showed moderate fatty liver. Thyroid function tests indicated subclinical hypothyroidism. Serologies for hepatitis B, hepatitis C, syphilis, and HIV were negative. F-FDG PET/CT revealed pleural seeding and multiple lymph node metastases. Brain MRI noted a left superior frontal gyrus metastasis, but these findings did not explain the patient’s visual field defects.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 9-year-old Caucasian male with preexisting diagnoses of autism spectrum disorder (ASD) and inattentive subtype attention deficit disorder (ADD) presented as an emergency to our inpatient service due to minimal dietary intake. He was detained under Section 2 of the Mental Health Act and admitted to a specialist psychiatric ward for children under 12. At presentation, his most prominent symptoms were refusal of food and fluids, mutism, school refusal, and self‐neglect, including refusal of personal care. His dietary restriction led to severe weight loss with a body mass index of less than 12 kg/m2 (<0.5th percentile), requiring nasogastric tube feeding. His sole method of communication was typing on an iPad, and his personal care was limited to wearing pajamas and a coat for several days without washing or changing. His mother reported incidents of urinary and fecal incontinence. He would not sleep in his bed but instead slept on the floor outside his parents’ bedroom in the “fetal position.” When family members attempted to touch him, he became physically aggressive and hit out at them. In May 2015, his stepfather gave him a haircut that he particularly disliked. Following this event, he became more controlling of his dietary intake and selectively mute with his stepfather, refusing to eat his food or speak to him.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 51-year-old man with chronic myeloid leukemia (diagnosed in 2007) treated initially with imatinib then nilotinib (achieving a 4.5 log molecular response for 6 years) presented in March 2019 with 2 weeks of left upper quadrant abdominal pain, anorexia, and easy fatigability. He had elected a treatment-free remission in 2013. On exam he was pale with hepatosplenomegaly but no bleeding. Initial laboratory studies showed WBC 90.2×10^9/L (neutrophilia, eosinophilia, basophilia), hemoglobin 6.6 g/dL, MCV 62 fL, platelets 497×10^9/L, normal renal and liver function, and uric acid 745 μmol/L. Anemia workup demonstrated a positive direct antiglobulin test, mildly elevated lactate dehydrogenase, and a normal reticulocyte count. Viral serologies (EBV, CMV, HIV) were negative. Peripheral blood smear showed 1% blasts consistent with CML and hypochromic microcytic anemia. Bone marrow biopsy confirmed CML relapse in chronic phase with erythroid hyperplasia. He was started on hydroxyurea and allopurinol and received 2 units of matched RBCs, but his hemoglobin rose only to 7.0 g/dL. Two weeks later he returned with increased fatigue and a hemoglobin of 5.8 g/dL. After transfusion of 2 additional units (hemoglobin 7.7 g/dL), repeat studies showed a hemolytic picture with elevated reticulocyte count, elevated lactate dehydrogenase, and a positive direct antiglobulin test.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 62-year-old Hispanic woman with no significant past medical history presented with a 3-week history of progressive dyspnea, palpitations, profound weakness, new hot flashes, and right flank pain. Vital signs showed a heart rate of approximately 100 beats per minute and a temperature of 37.7 °C. Examination revealed pale conjunctivae without erythema or exophthalmos, a thyroid gland of normal size without nodules, a systolic tricuspid murmur, decreased basal breath sounds, and tender hepatomegaly extending 5 cm below the right costal margin. Neurologic examination demonstrated hyperreflexia and tremor. Initial laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 8.1 g/dL, lactate dehydrogenase 3054 U/L (122–222), alkaline phosphatase 434 U/L (40–129), elevated total and free thyroxine levels, and suppressed TSH. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed pulmonary hypertension (65 mmHg), right atrial and ventricular dilation, tricuspid regurgitation, preserved left ventricular function, and an atrial septal defect with right-to-left shunt. Contrast-enhanced 18 F-FDG PET-CT demonstrated hepatomegaly (22 cm) with multiple FDG-avid liver masses up to 12 cm (SUVmax 13.5), FDG uptake in the gastric antrum (SUVmax 9.4) without corresponding wall thickening, mesenteric lymphadenopathy, and small pulmonary infiltrates. A technetium-99m pertechnetate thyroid scan showed diffusely increased uptake, and assays for thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulin and TSH receptor antibodies were negative. The patient was started on propranolol and methimazole for thyrotoxicosis.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 15-year-old girl presented with a 2-year history of painless, gradually progressive swelling of the upper and lower lips and of the gums.
She had no relevant medical history and no history of allergy to foods, drugs, or cosmetics; no asthma or eczema; and no family history of similar findings.
On extraoral examination, there was diffuse, soft, nontender swelling of both lips with normal temperature, with mild fissuring and a cobblestone appearance of the upper lip.
Intraoral examination revealed enlargement of the gingiva extending from tooth 13 to 23, involving marginal, interdental, and attached gingiva to the mucogingival junction; the gingiva was red, smooth, shiny, and lacked stippling. Probing revealed bleeding on probing, mild local deposits, and false pockets without clinical attachment loss.
Panoramic radiograph showed no alveolar bone loss in the maxillary or mandibular anterior region.
Laboratory studies, including complete blood count, differential, platelet count, and serum angiotensin-converting enzyme level, were within normal limits.
Chest radiograph was unremarkable, and the Mantoux test was negative.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 65-year-old Caucasian woman presented with a two-week history of left lower extremity swelling and pain that improved slightly with leg elevation and NSAIDs. She denied shortness of breath, recent trauma, fever, chest pain, or unexplained weight loss. Her medical history included inflammatory polyarthritis (on methotrexate and adalimumab), hyperlipidemia, hypertension, asthma–COPD overlap, osteopenia, and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation managed with warfarin (therapeutic INR). She had a three–pack-year smoking history, quitting 40 years prior.

On examination, the left lower extremity had pitting edema; peripheral pulses were strong, and the abdomen was soft and nondistended. Laboratory studies showed hypokalemia (2.8 mEq/L), which was repleted, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 35 mm/hr, and a C-reactive protein level of 30.1 mg/L. Doppler ultrasound of the left leg was negative for deep venous thrombosis. Computed tomography angiography of the abdomen and pelvis revealed a large heterogeneous left pelvic retroperitoneal mass centered at the left iliac chain, measuring 8.7 × 6.9 × 8.5 cm, with probable compression or occlusion of the adjacent iliac vein. Based on imaging, the differential diagnosis included sarcoma, lymphoma, ovarian neoplasm, or germ cell tumor.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 65-year-old man presented with 3 months of lower-jaw and submandibular pain occurring only with eating. He described acute, sharp, stabbing pain (8/10) over the lower jaw and submandibular glands, more pronounced on the right, triggered by the first bite of each meal, subsiding over subsequent bites and resolving minutes after the meal. Episodes lasted less than 2 minutes with no associated hoarseness, facial sweating, rhinorrhea, odynophagia, or dysphagia. Symptoms were independent of time of day or position. He had no history of surgery, trauma, underlying disease, medications, or family history of similar symptoms. Vital signs were normal. 

Head and neck examination revealed dentures in place, no ulcers, deformities, masses, or lymphadenopathy. The pharynx, tonsils, soft palate, tongue, parotid glands, nose, temporomandibular joint, and neck were all normal on inspection and palpation. 

Laboratory tests, including complete blood count, viral serologies, fasting blood sugar, thyroid-stimulating hormone, inflammatory and rheumatologic markers, electrolytes, and renal function, were all within normal limits. 

Contrast and noncontrast CT scans from the skull base to the neck showed no space-occupying lesions, masses, or other pathologic findings.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

An 80-year-old man presented with dizziness. MRI of the head showed a high-density area in the pons, and he was diagnosed with cerebral infarction and started on ozagrel sodium and clopidogrel; his dizziness improved. Continuous ECG monitoring documented frequent nonsustained ventricular tachycardia and 7,888 premature ventricular contractions per day, all asymptomatic. He was referred for cardiovascular evaluation. 

On examination, his heart rate was 71 beats/min, blood pressure 109/72 mmHg, lungs were clear, and a fourth heart sound was present. Laboratory tests showed a D-dimer level of 5.70 μg/mL and a B-type natriuretic peptide level of 525.7 pg/dL. A chest radiograph showed no pulmonary congestion. ECG demonstrated ST-segment elevation in leads V5 and V6 and T-wave inversion in leads I, aVL, and V4–V6. 

Transthoracic echocardiography revealed asymmetric left ventricular hypertrophy and an apical aneurysm containing an intracavitary mass. Doppler echocardiography showed a paradoxical jet flow from the apex toward the base during early diastole, with midventricular peak velocities of 2.00 m/s in systole and 3.67 m/s in diastole; a pseudonormal transmitral flow pattern (E/A ratio 1.12); and an E/Ea ratio of 19.2. Enhanced CT confirmed an apical aneurysm with a 21×14 mm thrombus. Coronary CT demonstrated no significant stenosis. Serum amyloid A protein, angiotensin-converting enzyme, and α-galactosidase A levels were all within normal limits.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 46-year-old woman presented with progressive fatigue and shortness of breath. Laboratory studies revealed leukocytosis (WBC 27×10^3/mm^3) with neutrophilia and monocytosis (monocytes 14%), anemia (hemoglobin 7.8 g/dL), and thrombocytopenia (platelets 96×10^3/mm^3). A bone marrow biopsy showed a markedly hypercellular marrow with myeloid hyperplasia, approximately 4% blasts, and 10% monocytes; cytogenetics were normal, and next-generation sequencing detected pathogenic NPM1 and NRAS mutations and an ETV6 variant of uncertain significance. The findings were interpreted as MDS/MPN unclassifiable, and the patient was started on hydroxyurea. Two months later, a repeat marrow biopsy demonstrated myelomonocytic hyperplasia, trilineage dysplasia, mild reticulin fibrosis, and a karyotype of 47,XX,+21/46,XX, consistent with CMML-2. Despite treatment, monocytosis, anemia, thrombocytopenia, and leukocytosis persisted, with 6% blasts in peripheral blood. CT imaging of the neck, chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed bilateral axillary, mediastinal, right hilar, and inguinal lymphadenopathy, prominent nasopharyngeal lymphoid tissue, and hepatosplenomegaly.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

options.B: Myelodysplastic syndrome with excess blasts in transformation

question:
A 52-year-old man presented with a 5-year history of progressive exertional dyspnea, fatigue, pleuritic chest pain, abdominal distension, and peripheral edema. One year earlier, coronary angiography for chest pain was normal. Six months before admission, thoracentesis revealed a lymphocyte-predominant exudative right pleural effusion; cytology was nondiagnostic, and pleural biopsy was nondiagnostic.

Over the past 10 days, he noted worsening abdominal distension and pain. On examination, blood pressure was 110/80 mmHg, pulse 78/min, jugular venous pressure was markedly elevated, and heart sounds were muffled. Right lung base breath sounds were diminished. Abdominal examination showed mild hepatomegaly, ascites, and peripheral edema.

Laboratory tests were within normal limits. Analysis of ascitic fluid demonstrated protein 4.1 g/dL and serum–ascites albumin gradient 1.6 g/dL. Abdominal ultrasound showed congestive hepatomegaly, mild splenomegaly, ascites, and signs of portal hypertension; upper endoscopy revealed no esophageal varices, and viral hepatitis serologies were negative. Chest and abdominal CT demonstrated right pleural effusion, pericardial thickening with calcification, ascites, and inferior vena cava dilation.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 59-year-old woman presented with a 2-month history of sore throat, cough, hoarseness, and a 10-kg weight loss. She was a never-smoker, had undergone left quadrantectomy for ductal carcinoma, and had been treated for stage II pulmonary sarcoidosis with hydroxychloroquine (400 mg/day) and oral prednisone (15 mg/day) for 5 years. Physical examination was unremarkable, and laboratory studies revealed a normal complete blood count and metabolic panel. Flexible laryngoscopy showed a centimetric polylobulated lesion on the free border of the epiglottis, inframillimetric lesions on the laryngeal surface of the epiglottis, and a millimetric ulcerated area on the right arytenoid mucosa; vocal-fold and arytenoid motility were preserved, and neck examination was normal. Contrast-enhanced MRI of the neck demonstrated a small low-enhanced lesion of the epiglottic border without other laryngeal enhancements. Whole-body PET-CT revealed multiple ground-glass lung masses and bilateral nodules increased from prior imaging of known sarcoidosis.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 20-year-old man fell and struck the back of his head. He reported severe neck and shoulder pain with difficulty raising both arms and in gripping objects. He was alert (GCS 15), with normal vital signs and no signs of head injury or respiratory distress. On cervical palpation, there was tenderness at the upper cervical spine and marked paraspinal muscle spasm. Neurologically, he had weakness of both arms (right worse than left) in the C5–T1 distribution with brisk reflexes but no sensory deficits. The lower limbs had brisk reflexes but normal strength. Cranial nerves were intact. An open-mouth odontoid radiograph and cervical CT scan showed an undisplaced type III odontoid fracture.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 38-year-old male cook presented with a 2-day history of blistering on both hands and feet. One week earlier, he had been admitted for epistaxis and nasopharyngeal myiasis and treated with gentamicin, crystalline penicillin, metronidazole, and turpentine oil instillations. Two days after starting antibiotics, he developed painless hemorrhagic edema and bullae over the dorsum of the left hand, which rapidly involved the right hand, both feet, and both earlobes. He denied fever, arthralgia, or other constitutional symptoms but reported a 1-year history of recurrent blistering of his hands and feet.

On examination:
• Multiple 1–2 cm hemorrhagic bullae with surrounding purpuric macules on the dorsa of both hands, feet, toes, and ears, which ruptured within 2 days to form deep irregular ulcers with angulated margins.  
• Diffuse facial infiltration with saddle-nose deformity; shiny infiltrated skin on the back with intact sensation.  
• Symmetrical, non-tender thickening of peripheral nerves; glove-and-stocking anesthesia distally.  
• Ulnar claw deformities bilaterally, resorption of the right little finger, pseudo-ainhum of the right fourth toe, and amputation of the left second toe.  
• Cranial nerves intact.

Investigations:
• Slit-skin smear from earlobes: globi present; bacteriological index 4+.  
• Hemogram: neutrophilic leukocytosis; ESR 56 mm in the first hour.  
• Liver and renal function tests: normal.  
• Urine porphyrins: negative.  
• Serologic tests: VDRL, HIV ELISA, and hepatitis B surface antigen negative.  
• Autoantibodies: serum cryoglobulins, rheumatoid factor (RF), and antinuclear antibody (ANA) negative.  
• Radiographs of the hands and feet: no evidence of osteitis or osteomyelitis.

A provisional diagnosis of diffuse non-nodular lepromatous leprosy with grade 2 deformities was made, and the differential diagnosis included Lucio phenomenon, drug-induced pseudoporphyria, drug-induced vasculitis, cryoglobulinemic vasculitis, and necrotic erythema nodosum leprosum.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 32-day-old girl was brought with a 4-day history of watery diarrhea (12–13 nonbloody, nonmucoid stools in 24 hours) and fever. There was no vomiting, poor feeding, or respiratory symptoms. She had contact with a COVID-19–positive case 4 weeks earlier. She was born at term, breastfed, and had normal growth parameters.

On admission she appeared toxic, tachypneic, and irritable. Temperature was 38.4°C, pulse 140/min, respiratory rate 30/min, and blood pressure 90/60 mmHg. Signs of dehydration included tenting skin, sunken eyes, and a soft fontanelle. Neurologic exam and development were normal.

Initial labs showed leukocytosis with neutrophilia, elevated ESR, CRP, ammonia, and D-dimer. A venous blood gas revealed metabolic acidosis (pH 7.22, HCO3– 11.5 mEq/L). Blood glucose and liver tests were normal. She received IV fluids and sodium bicarbonate.

On hospital day 2, repeat venous blood gas showed persistent metabolic acidosis (pH 7.26, HCO3– 11.5 mEq/L) with a normal anion gap (11 mEq/L). Chest CT, echocardiography, and an abdominopelvic ultrasound were unremarkable. Urine and stool cultures were negative; blood culture grew Klebsiella pneumoniae. SARS-CoV-2 RT-PCR was positive, and COVID-19 IgM was elevated.

By day 4, diarrhea had improved but a venous blood gas still showed hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis (pH 7.21, HCO3– 15 mEq/L, Cl– 115 mEq/L). Urine pH was 6.5 with a sodium of 105 mEq/L, specific gravity 1.012, and a positive urine anion gap. Serum potassium was 3 mEq/L, and ammonia was 235 mg/dL.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 74-year-old man was brought to the emergency department with generalized shaking, tremors, clonus, and altered level of consciousness. He had been seen normal nine hours earlier by his spouse. His medical history included type 2 diabetes and remote traumatic brain injury (subdural frontal and temporal hemorrhage) from a fall 3 years ago; he had no history of seizures.

On examination, he had jugular venous distention and bilateral pitting edema. Lung auscultation revealed decreased breath sounds without crackles. Cardiac auscultation was unremarkable. He demonstrated proximal limb weakness (manual muscle testing score 3/5) and an unsteady gait.

A 65-year-old woman presented with several weeks of peri-umbilical and epigastric discomfort, anorexia, 10-pound unintentional weight loss, and night sweats. Her medical history included treated hypothyroidism, hypertension, chronic hepatitis C infection (genotype 1a), and a low-grade rectal neuroendocrine tumor resected in 2012 with negative re-excision margins in 2017. Physical examination revealed mild diffuse abdominal tenderness without palpable masses or hepatomegaly. Laboratory tests showed elevated serum chromogranin A at 450 ng/mL (normal < 93 ng/mL), α-fetoprotein (AFP) of 2198 ng/mL (normal 10–20 ng/mL), and AFP-L3% at 86.6%. Liver enzymes were mildly elevated (AST 72 U/L, ALT 86 U/L). 

Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed two hepatic lesions: a 2.2 × 2.1 cm peripherally enhancing mass with central hypodensity in segment 4, and a 1.4 × 1.0 cm low-density lesion in segment 7, with prominent portacaval lymph nodes. MRI with gadolinium confirmed a 2.7 × 2.5 × 2.3 cm T2-hyperintense lesion in segment 4 with peripheral rim arterial phase enhancement and persistent venous phase enhancement; the smaller lesion was too small for enhancement characterization. FDG-PET showed avidity in the segment 4 mass (SUV 6.6) only. Gallium-68 dotatate PET showed no uptake in the liver or lymph nodes. 

A CT-guided core biopsy of the segment 4 lesion demonstrated a high-grade malignant tumor with neuroendocrine morphology and synaptophysin positivity; Ki-67 index was 30%. Non-cirrhotic liver parenchyma was confirmed on biopsy. No extrahepatic disease was found on chest CT. The main differential diagnoses considered at this point were metastatic neuroendocrine tumor versus a primary hepatic malignancy, most likely hepatocellular carcinoma.

An infected intra-abdominal cyst was diagnosed.

A 31-year-old right-hand–dominant man originally from Afghanistan presented with over 3 months of progressive right wrist pain. He had no hypertension, diabetes, smoking history, or cardiovascular disease. He weighed 64 kg (BMI 19.8 kg/m2) and was ASA class II. Preoperative airway examination showed Mallampati I without restriction or pain on neck extension, though a note advised careful extension due to osteoporosis.

Intubation with a conventional laryngoscope in slight neck extension revealed a Cormack–Lehane grade II view. The patient was supine with a small pillow under the shoulders for slight neck extension and arms alongside the body. A bilateral SCPB was performed using anatomical landmarks with 10 mL of 0.5% ropivacaine (5 mL per side). The 103-minute surgery was uneventful, and vital signs remained stable. The patient was extubated and transferred to recovery. 

Shortly after transfer, painless left arm weakness was noted. Neurological examination 3.5 hours after surgery showed strength 2/5 in shoulder elevation, 4/5 in elbow flexion/extension, and 4/5 in finger abduction and wrist extension; fist closure was normal. There was no headache, altered consciousness, dysarthria, or leg weakness. Vital signs were normal. Given the mild symptoms and absence of signs suggesting stroke or cervical spine complication, no CT or MRI was performed.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

– A 72-year-old man with a 12-year history of idiopathic Parkinson disease presented with progressively worsening low back pain.  
– Eight years earlier, he developed chronic back pain aggravated by movement, which became associated with bilateral radicular leg pain and weakness.  
– His past medical history included coronary artery disease, a pacemaker, and hemochromatosis.  
– Neurologic examination showed 3/5 strength in the right L4 and L5 myotomes and hypoesthesia in the L4 dermatome.  
– Electromyography demonstrated irritation of the right L4, L5, and S1 nerve roots.  
– He underwent L3–L5 laminectomies for presumed degenerative lumbar stenosis, with only transient relief; six months later, low back pain and right leg pain recurred with right foot drop.  
– Over the next three years, medical therapy and epidural corticosteroid injections failed to provide lasting benefit, and thoracolumbar kyphosis developed.  
– Plain radiographs revealed extensive destruction and collapse of the L2 vertebral body with paravertebral hypertrophic ossifications at the superior margin of the prior laminectomy.  
– Dynamic radiographs showed gross instability at L2–L3.  
– Computed tomography of the spine demonstrated severe lateral dislocation of the L2–L3 articulation and a reduction in the local kyphosis angle.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 24-year-old Japanese man was referred for evaluation of hypocalcemia. He was born at low birth weight (1114 g) with ptosis and joint contractures but without neonatal asphyxia or cerebral anomalies. At age 4, he was noted to have moderate bilateral sensorineural hearing loss requiring hearing aids. Beginning around age 10, he experienced facial spasms, tetany, and leg cramps, which improved during puberty but recurred in adulthood, prompting evaluation. Initial testing showed adjusted serum calcium 6.1 mg/dL (normal 8.8–10.1) and phosphate 5.4 mg/dL (2.7–4.6), and he was started on oral vitamin D and calcium, which normalized his calcium and relieved symptoms. There was no family history of parathyroid disease, tetany, deafness, kidney dysplasia, or congenital anomalies. On admission, while on supplementation his serum calcium was 8.3 mg/dL, phosphate 4.9 mg/dL, and intact PTH was low at 15 pg/mL (18.5–88.0). Other hormones were normal. Renal function was normal without proteinuria or hematuria. Neck ultrasound showed no thyroid or parathyroid abnormalities. Brain CT demonstrated multiple basal ganglia calcifications, and abdominal CT showed morphologically normal kidneys.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

options.C: Genetic abnormality in PTH or CaSR

question:
A 59-year-old woman presented with a 2-month history of progressive upper‐quadrant pain and postprandial vomiting. The pain became continuous, worsened after meals, and was relieved only by vomiting. She reported a 5-kg weight loss. There was no history of alcohol abuse. On examination, she had severe epigastric tenderness without abdominal distension. Laboratory tests showed an amylase level of 156 U/l (reference ≤100) and a CA 19-9 level of 52 U/ml (reference ≤37); complete blood counts were normal. 

Abdominal ultrasonography revealed normal abdominal parenchyma, a normal gallbladder, and no common bile duct dilation. Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy was unremarkable. 

Multidetector CT (portal phase) demonstrated cystic lesions encircling the second portion of the duodenum with circumferential duodenal wall thickening, and a soft‐tissue component in the pancreaticoduodenal groove abutting the pancreatic head; the pancreatic head and pancreatic duct appeared normal. Signs of inflammatory change, including fat stranding and fluid, surrounded the groove area. 

Contrast‐enhanced MRI confirmed intramural fluid‐signal lesions within the thickened duodenal wall and sheet‐like tissue in the groove with enhancement patterns similar to the pancreas. MR cholangiopancreatography showed a normal gallbladder and no dilation of the common bile duct or pancreatic duct.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 69-year-old woman presented with a 3-month history of mild lumbar and right hip pain without fever, chills, or weight loss. On examination, lumbar flexion was 60°, extension 10°, lateral bending 10° bilaterally; mild tenderness was noted near the L4 spinous process. Neurologic testing, including cervical reflexes, femoral nerve stretch, and straight leg raise (45° on the left, 50° on the right), was unremarkable except for hypoesthesia of the left lateral leg. Routine tumor markers (CA 19-9, CEA, AFP, NSE, and others) were within normal limits.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the lumbosacral region revealed a well-demarcated, homogeneous soft-tissue mass (3.2 × 7.5 cm) within the right multifidus muscle. Noncontrast attenuation was 55 HU, with heterogeneous enhancement (arterial phase 61–85 HU; venous phase 67–95 HU) and a central low-density area. The mass compressed but did not infiltrate the sacrum; the adjacent bone showed compressive sclerosis without destruction.

MRI showed an intramuscular lesion from L4 to S3. On T1-weighted images, the mass was isointense to muscle with patchy hypointense areas. STIR images demonstrated slightly hyperintense signal with patchy low-signal foci. Post-contrast sequences revealed heterogeneous enhancement with lamellar nonenhancing regions.

At surgery, the tumor was found within the right multifidus muscle, firm, poorly circumscribed, infiltrating surrounding muscle but without bone destruction. Grossly, it measured 7.5 cm, was yellowish-gray and slightly firm.

Histologic sections showed large polygonal cells with abundant eosinophilic granular cytoplasm, marked nuclear pleomorphism with prominent nucleoli, and approximately 2 mitoses per 10 high-power fields; there was no definite invasion of nerves, vessels, or bone. Immunohistochemical stains were positive for S100 protein, SOX-10, MDM2, and Bcl-2, and negative for Desmin, MyoD1, Myogenin, CD99, Synaptophysin, CK-pan, CD34, ERG, and CD163. The Ki-67 proliferation index exceeded 10%.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 13-month-old girl with no prior health issues was found unresponsive at home following 2 days of vomiting and 1 day of lethargy. Paramedics recorded a Glasgow Coma Score of 3, and she was intubated and transferred to a regional hospital. A noncontrast head CT was unremarkable. On admission she was hypothermic (core temperature 33.2 °C). Initial laboratory studies revealed:  
• Arterial pH 6.6, bicarbonate 6 mmol/L  
• Urine ketones positive; blood glucose 33 mmol/L  
• Platelet count 99 × 10^9/L; hemoglobin 106 g/L; creatinine 82 μmol/L  
• Fibrinogen, prothrombin time, and activated partial thromboplastin time within normal limits  

She was managed according to DKA protocols and transferred to a tertiary pediatric ICU. Over the next 48 hours, serum ketones normalized and blood glucose ranged 6–12 mmol/L, but metabolic acidosis persisted and she remained obtunded without fever. By day 7 she developed oliguria with urea 27.5 mmol/L and creatinine 175 μmol/L; renal ultrasound showed bilaterally enlarged kidneys, and she developed pulmonary edema with pleural effusions.  

By day 5, platelet count fell to 20 × 10^9/L and hemoglobin to 77 g/L despite transfusions. Blood film demonstrated red cell fragments and fibrin strands; reticulocyte count was 0.2%; coagulation studies remained normal; lactate dehydrogenase was elevated at 800 U/L.  
These biochemical features in conjunction with her clinical symptoms of renal failure and decreased neurological function led to a presumptive differential diagnosis of thrombocytopenia-associated multiorgan failure.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 49-year-old man presented with vague pain in the left mandibular area and lower-lip paresthesia of several weeks’ duration and occasional low-grade fevers. He was edentulous. On examination, there was a firm mass of the left edentulous mandibular ridge covered by intact mucosa. Cone-beam CT showed an ill-defined radiolucent lesion extending from the left alveolar crest to the inferior alveolar canal, with buccal cortical plate invasion. An incisional biopsy yielded tan, soft to elastic tissue fragments. Histologically, the lesion consisted of sheets of malignant small to medium-sized round cells with hyperchromatic, occasionally clefted nuclei, prominent nucleoli, scant cytoplasm, frequent mitoses, perineural invasion, and crush artifact. Immunohistochemistry revealed tumor cells positive for CD45, CD20, C-MYC, CD10, and BCL-6; scattered CD3 positivity; and negative staining for CD5, BCL-2, Cyclin D1, CD19, TdT, and MPO. The Ki-67 proliferation index was >95%, and in situ hybridization for Epstein–Barr virus was negative.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

An 8-year-old boy born to consanguineous parents was evaluated for markedly delayed development. Antenatal and perinatal history were unremarkable; birth weight was 2.5 kg. At 4 months of age, a white reflex was noted bilaterally; lens aspiration for congenital cataracts yielded no visual improvement. The child has not attained neck control, vocalizes by turning his head to sound, and has a developmental quotient of 3.6%. There is no history of seizures.

On examination, he is wasted and stunted (weight 8 kg [<3rd percentile]; height 95 cm [<3rd percentile]; head circumference 45 cm [<3rd percentile]).
