Dysmorphic features include microcephaly with plagiocephaly, frontal hypertrichosis, microphthalmia, microcornea, broad nasal root, long philtrum, retrognathia, low-set prominent ears, high-arched palate, and poor dentition. There is bilateral undescended testes and micropenis. Musculoskeletal findings include bilateral simian creases, syndactyly of the second and third toes of the left foot, equinus deformity of the right foot, and scoliosis with left-sided concavity. Neurologic examination shows hypotonia of the upper limbs, hypertonia of the lower limbs, and brisk deep tendon reflexes. Pupils are atonic and unresponsive to light and mydriatics. Brainstem auditory evoked responses and visual evoked potentials were abnormal. Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain revealed right frontal pachygyria, right ventriculomegaly, and hypoplasia of the corpus callosum with age-appropriate myelination.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 37-year-old white woman with no past medical history presented with 4 months of progressive dyspnea and back pain. Four months earlier, chest radiography and CT at an outside facility had shown right upper lobe consolidation, and she was treated for presumed pneumonia. Two months later, bronchoscopy with BAL and transbronchial biopsy revealed chronic inflammation without malignancy. On re-presentation, she complained of neck pain and was tachypneic on exam. Contrast CT of the chest showed near-complete collapse and consolidation of the right upper lobe with enlarged subcarinal and station 6 lymph nodes. Spine MRI demonstrated a 50% compression fracture at C7 and diffuse lytic vertebral lesions. CT of the abdomen revealed a solitary 1.5-cm hypodense lesion in the right hepatic lobe. A transthoracic biopsy of the right upper lobe showed interstitial fibrosis and lymphocytic infiltrates without malignancy. EBUS with transbronchial lymph node sampling and BAL cytology demonstrated poorly differentiated neoplastic cells but was insufficient for immunostains. A CT-guided liver biopsy revealed an undifferentiated epithelioid neoplasm forming nests and cords within a hyalinized sclerotic stroma, with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round‐to‐ovoid nuclei. Initial immunohistochemical stains showed weak synaptophysin positivity, membranous CD99, GATA-3 positivity in a subset, and weak PAX-8. The tumor cells were nonreactive to ER and SATB2 and negative for pankeratin, CK7, CK20, MOC-31, TTF-1, WT-1, HMB45, Melan-A, SOX-10, S100, Inhibin, SMA, MyoD1, CD117, CD45, CD30, and CD34. On expert review, the cells were reactive for MUC4, with preserved BRG1 and INI1 expression and negative claudin-4, ERG, CD31, CAMTA1, P40, ETV-4, and NUT.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 21-year-old woman with a history of type 1 diabetes mellitus (onset at age 15 years) and hyperlipidemia presented with a 3-month history of gradually worsening difficulty walking. She and her family denied any cognitive complaints. On neurological examination, she was alert and oriented, with no overt encephalopathy. Her speech was normal. There was moderate-to-severe truncal ataxia and appendicular ataxia in both arms and legs. Eye-movement testing showed no square-wave jerks, mild left-beating gaze-evoked nystagmus on extreme left gaze, normal pursuit without saccadic intrusions, and normal saccades. Deep-tendon reflexes were normal throughout. There was no pes cavus.

Laboratory studies revealed an anti–thyroid peroxidase antibody titer of 1:1600 (normal <1:100) and a borderline anti–thyroglobulin antibody titer of 1:40 (normal <1:20). Thyroid function tests were within normal limits. Serum anti–glutamic acid decarboxylase, antinuclear antibody, anti-Ro, anti-La antibodies, and ceruloplasmin were all normal or negative. 

Cerebrospinal fluid analysis showed a protein level of 46 mg/dL, a glucose level of 112 mg/dL (serum glucose 196 mg/dL), 1 white cell per mm3, and 570 red cells per mm3. CSF oligoclonal bands, anti–aquaporin-4 antibodies, and a CSF paraneoplastic panel (including anti-Hu, anti-Ri, anti-Yo, anti–Purkinje cell cytoplasmic antibody type 2 [PCA2], anti-Tr, anti-MAG, anti-Ma, anti-GAD, anti–CRMP5, and anti-amphiphysin) were negative.

Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain was normal without evidence of cerebellar atrophy.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 70-year-old woman with hypertension, hyperlipidemia, eczema, and obstructive sleep apnea presents with 4 days of fever and chills, with a maximum temperature of 104 °F, though she is afebrile on admission. Vital signs are normal. Initial laboratory studies reveal troponin 14 552 ng/L (0–30), C-reactive peptide 96.2 mg/L (< 8.0), ESR 23 mm/h (0–20), BNP 895 pg/mL (≤ 100), CK 2 584 U/L (25–190), and WBC count 8.7 × 10^3/µL (3.7–11.0). Peak absolute eosinophil count is 0.67 × 10^3/µL (≤ 0.50). Contrast CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis is unremarkable. Transthoracic echocardiogram shows concentric left ventricular hypertrophy. Cardiac MRI demonstrates diffuse mid-wall to near-transmural patchy late gadolinium enhancement of the left ventricle without subendocardial enhancement in a vascular distribution, consistent with nonischemic myocarditis.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 64-year-old man with a history of seropositive rheumatoid arthritis on methotrexate, hypertension, stable lung nodules, and a prior right thalamic ischemic stroke (treated with thrombolysis) presented after multiple witnessed seizures. The events began on awakening with headache and progressed to brief (<1 min) focal motor seizures of the left arm and leg, occurring every few minutes with preserved awareness; there was no incontinence, tongue biting, or postictal confusion. He reported left-sided tingling and numbness but denied visual, speech, or cognitive changes. He had been compliant with levetiracetam since a single seizure at the prior admission and had no further events until this presentation.

In the emergency department, he was hemodynamically stable. Examination revealed repeated left-sided shaking involving the neck and extremities with preserved consciousness, a left upper-extremity pronator drift, and 3/5 strength in the left lower extremity; the rest of the neurologic and general exam was normal. Lorazepam and a loading dose of levetiracetam controlled the seizures. 

Noncontrast head CT showed sulcal effacement with isodense material suggestive of a leptomeningeal process. EEG demonstrated cortical hyperexcitability with focal seizure activity over the right frontocentral and parasagittal regions. MRI of the brain ruled out acute infarction but revealed focal right frontal pachymeningeal and leptomeningeal enhancement.

Laboratory tests, including CBC, electrolytes, liver function, hepatitis B and C panels, vitamin D, HIV, angiotensin-converting enzyme, proteinase 3, C3, C4, extractable nuclear antigens, immunoglobulin G4, myeloperoxidase, rapid plasma reagin, and anti-dsDNA, were all negative. Rheumatoid factor was mildly elevated; anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody was >250 U/mL. 

Lumbar puncture showed normal cell counts, protein, and glucose. CSF studies—including rheumatoid factor, angiotensin-converting enzyme, Lyme serology, syphilis testing, Mycobacterium tuberculosis PCR, adenosine deaminase, cytology, and flow cytometry—were negative, and bacterial, fungal, and mycobacterial cultures showed no growth. 

CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed only small stable pulmonary nodules; MRI of the spine was unremarkable.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 33-year-old man presented with a 1-week history of progressive weakness and difficulty swallowing. He had daily heroin use administered by skin popping and untreated hepatitis C. On arrival, he could open his eyelids only 2–3 mm with marked ptosis, had diplopia, pooling of secretions after 1–2 words, dysarthria, difficulty holding up his head, and proximal upper-extremity weakness (4/5 bilaterally). Vital signs were blood pressure 136/80 mmHg, pulse 106 beats/min, respiratory rate 19 breaths/min, temperature 36.5°C, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Five deep-cavity abscesses over his hips and thighs were incised and drained, and wound cultures were obtained. Over the next few hours, his neck and extremity weakness worsened and breathing became very shallow, prompting intubation for airway protection and impending respiratory failure. A urine drug screen was positive for amphetamines, opiates, and acetone. The constellation of symptoms was suggestive of botulism, with differential diagnoses that included Guillain–Barré syndrome and myasthenia gravis. The patient denied ingestion of bulging canned food and recent travel.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 72-year-old woman with hyperthyroidism (controlled on oral therapy), a history of diffuse large B-cell lymphoma in complete remission, and a penicillin allergy received her first dose of the BNT162b2 mRNA COVID-19 vaccine at 09:00 on day X. Pre-vaccination screening showed a temperature of 35 °C and no other abnormalities. Laboratory testing one month before vaccination revealed normal liver and kidney function and a normal blood count; absence of thrombocytopenia and anemia was confirmed 10 days before vaccination. She felt unwell immediately after vaccination but improved with rest and returned home. At approximately 16:00 on day X + 1, she developed fatigue, nausea, chest pain, and back pain. She was last known alive around 20:00 via social media activity. She was found deceased at home on day X + 2 with rigor mortis present, and a medicolegal autopsy was initiated to determine the cause of death.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 36-year-old man was referred to our hospital because of an abnormal finding on screening colonoscopy. He had a history of tuberculosis-related lung destruction with pneumothorax and an 8-year smoking history; there was no other relevant family or medical history. He had no gastrointestinal symptoms. On examination, his vital signs were normal and laboratory tests, including alpha-fetoprotein, carcinoembryonic antigen, and carbohydrate antigen 19-9, were within normal limits.

Colonoscopy revealed an approximately 10-mm yellowish, sessile protruding lesion in the proximal ascending colon covered by intact mucosa without a rolling sign or cushion sign. Endoscopic ultrasonography showed a 10-mm homogeneous hypoechoic-to-isoechoic mass mainly in the mucosal layer with irregular extension into the submucosal layer; the muscularis propria appeared intact. On the basis of the EUS features, a colonic carcinoid tumor was initially suspected. Abdominal CT did not detect the lesion or any enlarged lymph nodes. An endoscopic biopsy specimen was consistent with a granular cell tumor, although the possibility of a malignant tumor could not be completely excluded based on the endoscopic evaluation.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 51-year-old woman with a history of long-term smoking presented with left hip pain. She denied cough, dyspnea, fever, night sweats, or weight loss. Physical examination was unremarkable aside from hip tenderness. 

Laboratory tests revealed a leukocyte count of 21,200 cells/μL, hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL, platelets 337,000/μL, and C-reactive protein 19.5 mg/L. A CT scan of the left hip demonstrated extensive osteolysis of the left acetabulum with suspected pathological fracture, confirmed on MRI. A CT-guided biopsy of the acetabular lesion showed poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma, confirming metastatic disease. Immunohistochemical stains were pan-cytokeratin positive; TTF-1 and other lineage markers were negative. 

Subsequent CT imaging of the chest revealed a 2.3-cm right hilar mass encircling the right main bronchus, with enlarged mediastinal nodes and pleural, pericardial, and diaphragmatic metastases; abdominal imaging showed a 2.0-cm left adrenal lesion. Bronchoscopic biopsy confirmed a moderately to poorly differentiated lung adenocarcinoma. Molecular testing revealed wild-type EGFR and a KRAS mutation. 

The patient underwent radiotherapy to the hip lesion and concurrent chemoradiation (cisplatin and vinorelbine) to the primary tumor and mediastinum. During treatment, she developed left flank pain due to adrenal mass growth, and her leukocyte count rose to 146,200 cells/μL. Differential showed neutrophils 85%, lymphocytes 1%, monocytes 2%, metamyelocytes 6%, and myelocytes 5%. CRP was 49.3 mg/L and procalcitonin 0.4 ng/mL; she remained afebrile.

Bone marrow aspiration revealed massively increased, left-shifted granulopoiesis up to myeloblasts with displacement of erythropoiesis and normal megakaryopoiesis. Flow cytometry showed mature granulocytic cells with partial CD56 expression and no immature blasts. Cytogenetic analysis showed a normal female karyotype (46,XX). FISH excluded t(9;22) translocation, and PCR was negative for BCR-ABL and JAK2 V617F mutation. A CT-guided biopsy of the iliopsoas lesion, along with repeated blood and urine cultures, were sterile. Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics did not reduce leukocytosis or inflammatory markers.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 79-year-old woman was admitted for a prolonged episode of confusion. Over the past months, she had recurrent episodes of confusion, disorientation, dysphasia, and fluctuating levels of consciousness; nonconvulsive seizures and status epilepticus had been considered, and antiseizure drugs including levetiracetam and gabapentin were ineffective. Intravenous valproic acid led to severe stupor that resolved after withdrawal. Her medical history included marginal zone lymphoma in remission (previous rituximab and bendamustine) and a pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy 5 years earlier for intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm.

On examination, she was disoriented and dysphasic without focal motor or sensory deficits. Electroencephalography showed generalized, irregular delta-theta activity with triphasic waves. Laboratory testing revealed a serum ammonia concentration of 121 μg/dl (reference 19–55 μg/dl) despite normal liver and kidney function. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis and brain MRI were unremarkable. Hyperammonemic encephalopathy was suspected, and lactulose, rifaximin, and daily enemas were initiated. Serum ammonia levels decreased, and her clinical status improved under this regimen. Abdominal ultrasound with elastography and abdominal MRI showed only minor signs of chronic liver disease, without evidence of cirrhosis or significant portal hypertension.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old man with an 8-year history of type 2 diabetes managed with oral agents and a 30–pack-year smoking history, but no known exposure to tuberculosis, was referred for worsening low back pain. Four months earlier, he developed diffuse abdominal pain with altered bowel habits and insidious inflammatory low back pain, accompanied by night sweats and a 36-kg weight loss over 6 months without fever. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis showed retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy without mediastinal or pulmonary lesions. A surgical biopsy of a retroperitoneal lymph node revealed rare epithelioid granulomas without caseous necrosis. Acid-fast bacilli were not detected in sputum, but an interferon-gamma release assay was positive, and the patient was started on a 6-month course of anti-tuberculous therapy.

After 4 months of treatment, he reported progressive low back pain radiating to the buttocks, now constant and disturbing sleep. On examination, he had lumbar spinal tenderness without neurologic deficit or palpable superficial lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. Plain radiographs of the lumbar spine and pelvis were unremarkable. MRI demonstrated an L4 vertebral body lesion (T1 hypointense, T2 and STIR hyperintense), disruption of the inferior L4 cortex with adjacent disc involvement, and a 46 × 32 mm paravertebral soft-tissue collection extending into the left psoas, along with persistent retroperitoneal lymphadenopathies.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

An 86-year-old man presented with acute shortness of breath and several months of unexplained weight loss. He reported recent high-impact falls, having undergone right hip hemiarthroplasty for a femoral fracture. On examination he was hypoxic with peripheral edema but had no fever, night sweats, or other constitutional symptoms. Laboratory studies showed elevated alkaline phosphatase and D-dimer levels; hemoglobin was chronically low without an acute drop, and the white-cell count, neutrophil count, and C-reactive protein were normal. A chest radiograph was unremarkable. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis revealed a 13 × 12 × 24 cm heterogeneous complex cystic hypoenhancing lesion in the left retroperitoneum posterior to the left kidney, with attenuation values of 9–32 Hounsfield units, displacing the kidney, encasing the renal vasculature and collecting system, and causing mild hydronephrosis. MRI showed heterogeneous T2-weighted hyperintensity with multicystic areas; multiple T1-weighted hyperintense foci consistent with hemorrhage; chemical shift imaging demonstrated microscopic lipid content by signal loss on out-of-phase images in the superior portion of the cystic component; there was diffusion restriction in the peripheral solid regions; and post-contrast fat-saturated T1 images revealed avid enhancement of the thick peripheral walls and internal septations.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 55-year-old man with chronic hypertension and gout presented to the emergency department with acute-onset chest tightness and a burning sensation radiating from the chest into both upper extremities, accompanied by sensory impairment at the T4 (nipple) level. Minutes after arrival, he developed bilateral arm weakness. He reported receiving a fourth dose of the Pfizer COVID-19 vaccine three weeks earlier. Cardiovascular and vascular etiologies were excluded on initial evaluation.

Over the next few hours, his left lower extremity strength declined from 5 to 2 on Lovett’s scale, followed by progressive weakening of the right lower limb (to 3/5) and both upper limbs (to 3/5). Cervical spine MRI revealed an inflammatory focus centrally at C5–C6 without contrast enhancement; brain and thoracic spine MRI were unremarkable. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis showed elevated protein (0.91 g/L) and albumin (656.7 mg/L) levels without intrathecal oligoclonal band synthesis. CSF culture and PCR for pathogens were negative. Serum aquaporin-4 antibody was negative. Anti–myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein (MOG) antibody titers were 1:10 on October 13 and <1:10 on October 18. A connective-tissue–disorder workup was unremarkable.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 48-year-old man with a longstanding history of central serous chorioretinopathy presented with a sudden onset of tiny floaters in the field of view of his left eye. He had episodic blurred vision in both eyes. His past medical history was notable for ethanol-related liver cirrhosis with decompensation and hepatorenal syndrome; he had no history of diabetes mellitus or cardiovascular disease. Blood tests excluded diabetes and infectious causes of neovascularization. 

On ophthalmologic examination, best-corrected visual acuity was 20/20 in both eyes, and intraocular pressures were normal. Slit-lamp examination showed clear lenses and no iris neovascularization or anterior segment inflammation. Funduscopy revealed normal optic discs but extensive pigment epithelial and retinal alterations in both maculae. 

In the right eye, there was a pigmented epithelial detachment and surrounding subretinal fluid near the temporal superior arcade, suggesting active choroidal leakage. In the left eye, there was a vitreous hemorrhage and a branched vascular proliferation projecting into the vitreous from the inferior temporal arcade in an area of retinal pigment epithelium atrophy; the remaining retinal vasculature and peripheral retina were normal. 

Spectral-domain OCT confirmed extensive photoreceptor and RPE degeneration in both eyes and the pigmented epithelial detachment with subretinal fluid in the right eye. Fundus autofluorescence showed large areas of reduced autofluorescence surrounded by increased autofluorescence (gravitational tracks) in both maculae, consistent with chronic CSCR. Fluorescein angiography demonstrated widespread RPE window defects, an active leakage point at the temporal superior arcade in the right eye, and an active vascular proliferation at the inferior temporal arcade in the left eye. Indocyanine green angiography of the left eye revealed multifocal confluent choroidal atrophy with reduced vessel density beneath the neovascular complex. En-face OCT angiography of the left eye confirmed a flow-positive vascular network protruding into the vitreous and underlying choriocapillaris and choroidal vessel voids, with surrounding retinal atrophy.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 40-year-old Asian woman presented with a 3-month history of watery discharge from her left nostril and dull, throbbing headaches localized to the left side, which worsened when leaning forward or straining. The nasal discharge was clear, moderate in amount, nonfoul, and nonbloody. She denied fever, nasal obstruction, sneezing, itching, facial fullness, or other sinonasal symptoms. She had been diagnosed elsewhere with allergic rhinitis and treated with antiallergic medication without improvement.

Her medical history included an episode of sinusitis 5 years earlier and a childhood nasal injury at age 12. Surgical history comprised cesarean section, appendectomy, and sterilization. She had no other comorbidities, was a non-smoker, and did not drink alcohol.

On examination, she was afebrile with stable vital signs. Nasal inspection revealed a normal external contour and a reddish vestibule with clear left-sided discharge. The discharge could not be sniffed back and did not stiffen a handkerchief. There was no septal deviation, polyps, or mucosal abnormalities. Neurologic and other system examinations were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed mild anemia (hemoglobin 10 g/dL) but were otherwise normal. The handkerchief test was positive. Analysis of the nasal fluid demonstrated a glucose concentration exceeding 30 mg/dL.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 66-year-old woman was referred for decreased visual acuity in her right eye. Three years earlier she had undergone bilateral laser iridotomy for primary angle closure and subsequently developed bullous keratopathy. On initial examination, the right cornea was edematous with Descemet’s membrane folds and a central corneal thickness of 702 µm. Anterior-chamber depths were 2.36 mm OD and 2.23 mm OS; the iridotomy site was patent without inflammation or hyperemia. Best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) was 20/40 OD; endothelial cell density (ECD) was unmeasurable OD and 1,773 cells/mm2 OS. 

The patient underwent phacoemulsification with intraocular lens implantation OD. Her BCVA was 20/50 at 1 week and 20/125 at 2 months with no further improvement, so Descemet’s membrane endothelial keratoplasty (DMEK) was performed. The graft was fully attached and corneal clarity improved except for progressive peripheral anterior synechiae at the temporal periphery. One month post-DMEK, BCVA was 20/20, ECD was 1,984 cells/mm2, and central corneal thickness was 477 µm. 

Oral prednisolone was discontinued at 1 month and topical 0.1% betamethasone was tapered from four times to once daily when the cornea was clear (BCVA 20/20, thickness 486 µm, ECD 1,114 cells/mm2). Fifteen months after DMEK, the regimen was changed to topical 0.1% fluorometholone once daily. Shortly thereafter, corneal edema recurred, with hyperemia, multiple Descemet’s membrane folds, diffuse, inferiorly located mutton-fat keratic precipitates on the graft, and anterior-chamber cells. BCVA worsened to 20/32, central corneal thickness was 704 µm, and ECD, which had been 980 cells/mm2 two months earlier, was now unmeasurable. 

Anterior-segment optical coherence tomography confirmed peripheral anterior synechiae in the recipient cornea. The patient declined PCR testing. Blood tests showed no elevation of antibodies against herpes simplex virus or varicella zoster virus.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 33-year-old Guinean woman with a history of diabetes and prior splenectomy presented with chronic left upper-quadrant abdominal pain and a progressively enlarging mass. On examination, there was a fixed deep mass in the left upper quadrant extending below the costal margin. Carcinoembryonic antigen and CA19-9 levels were within normal limits. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed a 104 × 77 × 44 mm, rounded, well-defined lesion appearing attached to the gastric wall, with both cystic and solid components and scattered calcifications; there were no signs of local invasion or metastases.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 44-year-old man with a 50 pack-year smoking history was referred for 1 year of progressive dyspnea, dry cough, fatigue, 10 kg weight loss, and recurrent “pneumonias.” He drank approximately 49 units of alcohol per week and worked in an office with no known inhalational exposures. On examination he had finger clubbing and bibasilar crackles. Spirometry showed an FEV₁ of 2.79 L (69% predicted), FVC of 4.04 L (81% predicted), and an FEV₁/FVC ratio of 69%. DLCO was 22% predicted and KCO was 32% predicted. Resting oxygen saturation on room air was 84–90%, and a 6-minute walk of 490 m caused desaturation from 89% to 78%. Lactate dehydrogenase was 339 U/L (reference 105–205 U/L). Serial chest radiographs showed a reticular pattern and bilateral infiltrates. High-resolution CT of the thorax revealed interlobular septal thickening with diffuse ground-glass opacities and areas of consolidation in a “crazy paving” pattern. Bronchoalveolar lavage fluid appeared milky white, and microbiological studies of the lavage were negative.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 19-year-old previously healthy male presented with chest pain and fever. Initial evaluation revealed an enlarged cardiac silhouette on chest radiograph, ST-segment elevation in leads aVF and III on ECG, and a 2.2 cm pericardial effusion on transthoracic echocardiography. He was diagnosed with acute pericarditis and treated with ibuprofen and colchicine; colchicine was continued after discharge. One month later, echocardiography showed persistent pericardial effusion (0.7 cm) and ongoing fatigue. Seven months after initial presentation, he experienced recurrent pericarditis with effusion and asthenia, prompting cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (CMR). CMR demonstrated a 54×42 mm mass adjacent to the epicardial surface of the basal and mid anterior left ventricular wall that was hypointense with a peripheral hyperintense rim on T2-weighted images, hyperintense on T1-weighted images, and showed peripheral gadolinium enhancement with a nonenhancing central core; diffusion-weighted imaging showed no signal increase. Contrast-enhanced cardiac CT confirmed a hypervascular mass with a central hypodense scar and showed that the proximal left anterior descending artery coursed through the lesion without chamber invasion. Laboratory testing revealed marked elevations of plasma and urine 3-methoxytyramine and increased chromogranin A levels.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 76-year-old Caucasian woman with hypertension and glaucoma presented with a three-month history of a productive cough and progressive dyspnea. She was initially treated for bronchitis with antibiotics and a methylprednisolone dose pack without improvement; allergy testing was negative. She denied fevers, chills, weight loss, chest pain, palpitations, hemoptysis, gastrointestinal symptoms, asthma, rash, arthritis, recreational drug use, bird exposure, or occupational exposures (silicon, beryllium, asbestos). Medications included timolol eye drops, losartan, and pantoprazole.

On examination, her blood pressure was 128/68 mm Hg, heart rate 72 bpm, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, temperature 98.8 °F, and oxygen saturation 84% on room air. Pulmonary exam revealed bibasilar rales; the remainder of the examination was unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed normal complete blood counts and chemistry panels. Blood cultures remained negative for 5 days; sputum cultures were negative for acid-fast bacilli, fungi, and Streptococcus pneumoniae. Chest CT demonstrated a slight mosaic attenuation pattern suggestive of air trapping. High-resolution CT of the chest revealed ground-glass opacities and a mosaic pattern consistent with bronchial air trapping. Bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) showed 89% neutrophils, 8% lymphocytes, 2% monocytes, and 1% macrophages. Transbronchial biopsy revealed focal giant cell reaction without malignancy; the CD4:CD8 ratio was 2.8:1. Pulmonary function testing showed an FEV1 of 75% of predicted and a decreased DLCO, consistent with restrictive physiology.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 49-year-old woman presented with a 2-year history of pain and swelling of the cartilaginous portions of both ears, swelling of the nasal bridge, and progressive dyspnea. She had been treated for presumed relapsing polychondritis with oral methotrexate (20 mg weekly) and mycophenolate mofetil (1 g twice daily) without sustained improvement. On examination, there was bilateral swelling of the ear helices with overlying red-brown nodules, firm swelling of the nasal bridge, and mild inspiratory stridor. Vital signs were within normal limits apart from mild tachypnea. No lymphadenopathy or other cutaneous lesions were noted.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

An 18-year-old Thai woman, G1P0 at 31 weeks’ gestation, presented with 6 hours of fever and severely painful swelling of her wrists, hands, and feet. She was unable to make a fist and had difficulty walking. She also reported a headache but denied nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or myalgia. A pruritic, erythematous rash was noted on her abdomen. She lived in a community where neighbors had experienced fever and arthralgia in the past week. She was not on any medications, had no recent hospitalizations, and no family members were ill.

On examination, temperature was 38.1 °C, blood pressure 100/40 mmHg, pulse 126 beats/min, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Maculopapular rashes were present on the forehead, cheeks, nose, and abdominal wall. There was arthritis of both wrists, ankles, and all metatarsophalangeal joints. Arthrocentesis of the left wrist yielded clear yellow synovial fluid with high viscosity; Gram stain showed no organisms.

Laboratory studies revealed: hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL; hematocrit 33%; white cells 9300/mm3 (91% neutrophils); platelets 180 000/mm3; BUN 8.3 mg/dL; creatinine 0.38 mg/dL; total protein 6.2 g/dL; albumin 3.5 g/dL; globulin 2.7 g/dL; total bilirubin 0.4 mg/dL; direct bilirubin 0.1 mg/dL; AST 15 U/L; ALT 8 U/L; ALP 102 U/L; ESR 52 mm/h. Serology for dengue NS1 antigen and IgM and IgG antibodies was negative.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old woman with severe rheumatoid arthritis, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and hypothyroidism presented with two weeks of progressive temporal disorientation and unsteady gait. Her rheumatoid arthritis had been treated for 10 years with prednisolone (10 mg daily), leflunomide (20 mg daily), and weekly methotrexate (7.5 mg). Two months earlier, adalimumab (40 mg every two weeks) was added due to worsening joint pain and deformity.

On examination, she was afebrile. Neurologic assessment showed moderate left hemiparesis (Medical Research Council grade 3) without sensory deficits or cranial nerve abnormalities. Laboratory tests revealed a normal white-cell count (9100/µL) with neutrophilia (89%) and lymphopenia (7%), hyperglycemia (136 mg/dL), and an HbA1c of 6.9%. HIV testing was negative. Toxoplasma serology was IgG positive and IgM negative. Electrocardiogram and chest radiograph were unremarkable.

Noncontrast CT of the head demonstrated a focal hypodense lesion in the right basal ganglia with surrounding edema and mild midline shift. Brain MRI showed a heterogeneous T2/FLAIR lesion with peripheral gadolinium enhancement and perfusion‐weighted imaging revealed decreased cerebral blood volume in the lesion.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 38-year-old woman with cystic fibrosis, status post bilateral lung transplant and with stage 3B chronic kidney disease and CF-related diabetes, was transferred to the intensive care unit for acute-on-chronic hypoxemic respiratory and renal failure. Three weeks prior, she had been treated for acute-on-chronic sinusitis caused by methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus and Aspergillus fumigatus. Bronchoalveolar lavage cultures subsequently grew Stenotrophomonas maltophilia, and she was started on intravenous colistimethate. 

On the second day of colistin therapy, she reported new-onset numbness and tingling in her fingers, toes, and perioral region. Neurologic examination revealed intact cranial nerves, preserved sensation and proprioception, and full motor strength. Deep-tendon reflexes were decreased bilaterally at the knees and ankles but were normal in the upper extremities. Given a recent upper respiratory infection and the combination of subjective weakness, paresthesia, and diminished lower-extremity reflexes, Guillain-Barré syndrome was suspected. 

A lumbar puncture was performed. Cerebrospinal fluid contained 1 erythrocyte, no neutrophils, 71 lymphocytes, 29 monocytes, a glucose level of 81 mg/dL, and a protein level of 23 mg/dL.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of microscopic hematuria and a rise in creatinine from a baseline of 59 to 94 µmol/L. Over three months, she had developed a bilateral petechial rash on her lower extremities, intermittent right knee swelling and pain, and a two-year history of subjective fevers and night sweats. A short course of oral prednisone produced transient improvement in her rash and systemic symptoms, but these recurred on tapering. She was restarted on prednisone 30 mg daily one week before nephrology consultation for concern of vasculitis or connective tissue disease.

Her medical history included a left femoral crossover graft (2005), gastroesophageal reflux, dyslipidemia, depression, and at least six months of microscopic hematuria with normal cystoscopy. Medications were rabeprazole, ASA, simvastatin, citalopram, and bupropion. On exam, she had palpable petechiae on her lower extremities and warm moderate effusions in the right knee and ankle; vital signs were normal.

Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 102 g/L, WBC 11.1×10^9/L, creatinine 94 µmol/L, ESR 115 mm/hr, CRP 94 mg/L, and rheumatoid factor 176 IU/mL. Urinalysis had trace protein, 58 RBCs/HPF with acanthocytes, and 7 WBCs/HPF. ANCA, C3, and C4 were normal. A skin biopsy demonstrated leukocytoclastic vasculitis with dermal capillary IgM and C3 deposition. Knee arthrocentesis was noninfectious and noncrystalline.

Serologies for hepatitis B, hepatitis C, HIV, and syphilis were negative; blood cultures were negative. SPEP showed no monoclonal protein; cryoglobulins and anti-CCP were negative. Kappa and lambda light chains were modestly elevated. ANA was weakly positive with RNP 1.1 AI, dsDNA was negative, and lupus anticoagulant was normal. Twenty-four–hour urine protein was 0.32 g. Renal ultrasound showed normal-sized kidneys.

Because her creatinine and symptoms initially improved on prednisone, biopsy was deferred. Upon prednisone withdrawal, her creatinine rose to 111 µmol/L and symptoms recurred, prompting a renal biopsy. Light microscopy revealed diffuse global mesangial hypercellularity. Immunofluorescence showed trace mesangial IgG and kappa, 1+ IgM, lambda, and C1q, and 2+ C3. Electron microscopy demonstrated mesangial electron-dense deposits. These findings were thought to be consistent with mesangial proliferative lupus nephritis, although her clinical and serologic profile was atypical.

She was treated with prednisone and azathioprine, then mycophenolate mofetil, hydroxychloroquine, and rituximab in various combinations, each time showing creatinine improvement on steroids and relapse on tapering. Given her atypical clinical features and inability to wean prednisone, the lupus diagnosis was reconsidered. Repeat transthoracic echocardiogram, CT of the abdomen and pelvis, and blood cultures showed no infectious focus. She was referred for further infectious evaluation.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old woman presented with a 1-year history of gradually increasing lower abdominal pain and irregular vaginal bleeding. She was postmenopausal (age at menopause, 56), with no significant medical, surgical, or family history. She had no history of HPV vaccination.

On examination, she was pale and underweight. Abdominopelvic CT without contrast showed an enlarged uterus. Laboratory evaluation revealed a white blood cell count of 32,200/mm3, a C-reactive protein level of 17 mg/dL, and hemoglobin of 5.5 g/dL; 6 units of red blood cells were transfused. Tumor markers were elevated: SCC antigen, 5.3 ng/mL (normal ≤2.5); CA-125, 112.7 U/mL (normal ≤35).

Speculum examination demonstrated a large bleeding tumor occupying the vaginal canal; bimanual exam revealed an enlarged, fixed uterus with the fundus midway between the symphysis pubis and the umbilicus. Contrast-enhanced CT showed multiloculated, thick-walled cystic lesions in the uterus and pelvic fluid suggestive of an abscess. Empiric antibiotics were begun.

Pelvic MRI revealed an 11×9-cm mass with central necrosis confined to the cervix; the endometrial cavity was spared and distended with fluid. There was no parametrial, vaginal wall, or rectal involvement on MRI; bladder mucosal invasion was not excluded.

Colposcopy-directed biopsy of the cervical lesion showed endometrioid adenocarcinoma. Immunohistochemistry was diffusely positive for vimentin and estrogen receptor, almost negative for progesterone receptor, and patchy positive for p16. Tests for high-risk HPV (16 subtypes) and microsatellite instability were negative. Cystoscopy confirmed intact bladder mucosa.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 46-year-old Japanese woman presented with a 1-month history of worsening dry cough and slight loss of appetite without weight loss. She had no significant medical or family history, was a non-smoker, and drank alcohol socially. On examination, vital signs were normal, lung and heart auscultation were unremarkable, there was no peripheral lymphadenopathy or skin lesions, and neurologic findings were non-specific. Ophthalmologic evaluation revealed bilateral anterior uveitis. Laboratory studies showed: white blood cell count 5300/µL, hemoglobin 14.7 g/dL, platelets 201 000/µL, C-reactive protein 0.76 mg/dL, serum calcium 9.7 mg/dL, angiotensin-converting enzyme 35.1 U/L (elevated), soluble interleukin-2 receptor 1693 U/mL (elevated), and normal antinuclear antibody. Urinalysis was normal. Blood and sputum cultures were negative. A Mycobacterium tuberculosis–specific interferon-γ release assay was negative, but serum anti-glycopeptidolipid core immunoglobulin A antibody for MAC was positive. Tests for Aspergillus galactomannan antigen, beta-D-glucan, Cryptococcus antigen, p-ANCA, c-ANCA, and HIV were all negative or within normal limits. Chest radiography showed upper mediastinal widening. Contrast-enhanced thoracic CT demonstrated enlarged mediastinal and hilar lymph nodes with low-density areas and hepatosplenomegaly. Bronchoscopy revealed edematous bronchial mucosa, but endobronchial ultrasound‐guided needle aspiration yielded insufficient tissue. A surgical biopsy of a mediastinal lymph node was performed. Histology showed epithelioid cell granulomas with marked necrosis. Ziehl–Neelsen staining and PCR for Mycobacterium tuberculosis and MAC on the biopsy specimen were negative.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman presented with persistent swelling of her lower lip for 2 years. She had no history of tuberculosis or contact with patients with tuberculosis or leprosy. Routine blood biochemistry and hematological parameters were within normal limits. A slit skin smear examination, Mantoux test, and chest radiograph were unremarkable. On the basis of these findings and the clinical presentation, a diagnosis of cheilitis granulomatosa Miescher (CGM) was made, and multiple intralesional corticosteroid injections were administered into the lower lip without improvement. Four weeks later, she developed erythematous, non-pruritic, non-scaling plaques above her upper lip and on the right lower chin, each measuring approximately 1 cm × 1.5 cm. Systemic examination was otherwise normal. Incisional biopsy specimens from the lower lip and an erythematous plaque revealed granulomas composed of epithelioid histiocytes with periadnexal and perineural lymphocytic infiltration on histopathological examination.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 60-year-old man presented with fever and confusion. One month earlier, he had been admitted for slurred speech, right hemiparesis, and weight loss; MRI at that time showed multiple left temporoparietal enhancing lesions, and biopsy diagnosed glioblastoma (WHO grade IV), for which he received radiation and temozolomide. His past medical history included diabetes mellitus.
On current admission, temperature was 39.7 °C, pulse 88/min; examination revealed left facial weakness and decreased strength in the right upper and lower extremities. Laboratory tests showed WBC 0.4 K/mm3 (absolute neutrophil count 0.2 K/mm3), hemoglobin 12.3 g/dL, and platelets 20 K/mm3; other routine tests were unremarkable. Chest radiograph suggested a possible right lower lobe infiltrate.
Noncontrast head CT demonstrated stable left frontal and corpus callosum abnormalities compared to prior MRI. He received platelet transfusion and was started on meropenem for febrile neutropenia; dexamethasone was tapered. After platelet count rose, lumbar puncture showed 0 WBC/mm3, 0 RBC/mm3, protein 100 mg/dL, glucose 33 mg/dL, LDH 38 IU/L, and negative Gram stain. EEG revealed generalized background slowing with left frontotemporal low-amplitude sharp waves. Repeat brain MRI with contrast showed a new signal abnormality in the right temporal lobe.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 68-year-old woman presented with a dry cough of more than 1 month’s duration, associated with dyspnea, fever, chills, myalgia, and intermittent diarrhea. She had type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and a history of COVID-19 diagnosed by PCR 40 days earlier that required hospitalization and treatment with antipyretics, corticosteroids, anticoagulants, and intravenous fluids. She denied known exposure to tuberculosis. On examination, she had crackles in the right lung, an oxygen saturation of 80% on ambient air, a blood pressure of 118/80 mm Hg, and a pulse rate of 97 beats per minute. Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 33 000/mm3 and a C-reactive protein level of 194 mg/dl. A chest radiograph demonstrated a cavitated density in the right upper lobe. Computed tomography of the thorax revealed bilateral diffuse parenchymal densities, two cavities in the right lung (one in the right upper lobe and one in the right lower lobe), and residual ground-glass opacities.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 47-year-old woman presented with a 4-year history of wheezing and productive cough, initially diagnosed as late-onset asthma. Despite standard asthma therapy, her symptoms worsened, with new episodes of left chest pain and dyspnea. She had no relevant past medical or family history. On examination, breath sounds were bilaterally vesicular but decreased at the left lung base. A chest radiograph showed marked volume reduction of the left lung. Computed tomography of the chest revealed a rounded lesion at the carinal region, partially obstructing the ostium of the left main bronchus.  

Rigid bronchoscopy demonstrated a mobile polypoid lesion with a smooth, shiny, non-friable surface that obstructed the left main bronchus; the lesion was removed endoscopically. Grossly, the oval, encapsulated mass measured 2.3 × 1.7 × 1.5 cm, had a fibroelastic consistency, and a smooth, homogeneous brownish cut surface.  

Microscopic examination showed a low-grade mesenchymal neoplasm composed of spindle cells in small, loose fascicles, with alternating hypercellular and hypocellular areas. Hypocellular regions contained dense collagen bands and focal myxoid change. Focal aggregates of mature adipose tissue were present. Blood vessels were irregular with a “staghorn” configuration. Mitotic activity was 2 per 10 high-power fields.  

Immunohistochemical stains revealed that the neoplastic cells were negative for cytokeratin (AE1/AE3), S100 protein, smooth muscle actin, and desmin, and were positive for CD34, CD99, and BCL2.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 15-year-old boy was referred for evaluation of a 3-day history of dizziness, gait instability, nausea, and headache. On examination, he had mild diplopia on left lateral gaze and on convergence, an inability to sustain upward vertical gaze, mild right upper- and lower-extremity weakness (Medical Research Council grade 4), right-sided hypoesthesia, and mild gait instability without frank cerebellar signs. Brain MRI showed a well-defined, lobulated cystic lesion in the right middle cerebellar peduncle adjacent to the fourth ventricle, causing mild mass effect on the cerebellar vermis. The lesion contained multiple thin septations and was uniformly isointense to cerebrospinal fluid on all sequences, with no definite enhancement or diffusion restriction. There was mild surrounding edema in the right middle cerebellar peduncle. Single-voxel proton MR spectroscopy demonstrated unremarkable concentrations of N-acetylaspartate, choline, and creatine, with normal choline/creatine and choline/N-acetylaspartate ratios.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

An 11-year-old girl presented with an 18-month history of progressive abdominal distention. She had no pain, gastrointestinal, urinary, respiratory, or constitutional symptoms. She reached menarche one year earlier; menstrual cycles were infrequent, with the last period two weeks before presentation. There was no history of menorrhagia or dysmenorrhea. On examination, she was in no distress with normal vital signs. Her weight was 64.5 kg (>95th percentile); she was otherwise thin. There was no clubbing, pallor, icterus, edema, or lymphadenopathy, and no stigmata of chronic liver disease. Abdominal examination showed a uniformly distended abdomen from the pelvis to the xiphisternum with full flanks; fluid thrill was positive, and there was no tenderness or palpable mass. Cardiovascular, respiratory, and neurologic examinations were unremarkable. Urine dipstick was negative for proteinuria. Laboratory studies—including CBC, peripheral smear, serum electrolytes, renal function, and liver function tests—were within normal limits. Tumor markers (CA-125, CEA, alpha-fetoprotein, beta-hCG), uric acid, and LDH were all within normal limits. A transabdominal ultrasound was reported as massive ascites. MRI of the abdomen and pelvis demonstrated a large, homogeneous, well-defined unilocular cystic abdominopelvic mass measuring 39 × 29 × 18 cm, occupying the entire abdomen and pelvis and bulging into the anterior abdominal wall; no septations or solid components were seen.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 55-year-old Japanese man with a six-month history of left hilar lung cancer (suspected squamous cell carcinoma based on sputum cytology and tumor markers) treated with radiotherapy and chemotherapy presented one year after diagnosis with fever and chest pain. On admission (day 1), he was hypotensive (BP 69/58 mmHg), tachycardic (146/min), tachypneic (24/min), and had an altered mental state. Chest CT showed a left hilar mass invading the left atrium and left main bronchus, with small low-density areas within the tumor and around the intestinal membrane. Echocardiography confirmed tumor invasion into the left atrium without vegetations. Blood cultures grew Staphylococcus aureus. He received noradrenaline and broad-spectrum antibiotics. On day 2, he developed acute difficulty speaking, left-sided weakness, and left unilateral spatial neglect, with an NIHSS score of 7. Head CT revealed multiple small low-density areas in the right cortex and bilateral subcortex interpreted as air emboli. Brain MRI showed faint hyperintensity along the right parietal cortex, subcortex, and left frontal cortex on diffusion-weighted images and small dot-like low signals in the right frontal cortex and left parietal subcortex on susceptibility-weighted images.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 63-year-old woman was referred with progressive generalized fatigue, proximal muscle weakness, insomnia, flushing, hypertension, impaired concentration, memory difficulties, and hypomanic symptoms. She reported a 5-kg weight loss over several months despite features of Cushing’s syndrome. Her history included breast cancer 16 years earlier and a superficial melanoma with no recurrence.

On examination she had moon facies, supraclavicular fat, a buffalo hump, chin hirsutism, and proximal myopathy. Laboratory studies showed a 24-hour urinary free cortisol of 1300 nmol (reference <183 nmol), serum cortisol levels of 600–1000 nmol/L without diurnal variation, and plasma ACTH levels of 30–45 ng/L throughout 24 hours. There was no suppression on 1-mg or 8-mg dexamethasone tests.

An MRI of the sella turcica showed no pituitary lesion. Bilateral inferior petrosal sinus sampling after CRH stimulation demonstrated no central-to-peripheral ACTH gradient and no ACTH increase. Fasting CRH was normal at 3.5 pmol/L. Urinary catecholamines and hydroxyindoleacetic acid were normal. Chromogranin A was mildly elevated.

A CT scan of the neck, chest, and abdomen revealed only slight enlargement of the left adrenal gland with an attenuation of 25 Hounsfield units. A whole-body planar 111In-pentetreotide somatostatin receptor scintigraphy scan demonstrated no uptake typical of an ectopic ACTH source.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 76-year-old woman with hypertension and dyslipidaemia presented with two weeks of intermittent palpitations, orthopnoea, and NYHA class III–IV dyspnoea. On examination, her extremities were cool and clammy, jugular venous pressure was elevated to the angle of the jaw with a positive abdominojugular reflux, and auscultation revealed bilateral crackles. Heart sounds were normal without murmurs, but the rhythm was irregularly irregular at approximately 100 b.p.m. 

In the emergency department, she was found to be in atrial fibrillation with left bundle branch block on ECG and clinical evidence of decompensated heart failure, confirmed by chest X-ray showing pulmonary oedema and pleural effusions. Initial management included IV furosemide and rate control with metoprolol and digoxin. 

Over the next few hours, she became increasingly hypotensive (60/30 mmHg), tachycardic, diaphoretic, and developed anuric acute kidney injury and lactic acidosis. High-sensitivity troponin rose from 13 ng/L to 2040 ng/L. Labs showed worsening metabolic acidosis, elevated liver enzymes, and a lactate of 7.1 mmol/L. She was started on dual antiplatelet therapy and vasopressors for suspected acute coronary syndrome. Point-of-care ultrasound demonstrated significant biventricular systolic dysfunction, a plethoric non-collapsible IVC, and diastolic flattening of the interventricular septum. 

Differential diagnoses considered included acute coronary syndrome with RV infarction, underlying cardiomyopathy or pulmonary hypertension with decompensation, and massive pulmonary embolism. 

Given her hemodynamic instability, she was intubated and taken for emergent cardiac catheterization. Angiography showed very large, ectatic left main, LAD, and LCx arteries with no flow-limiting stenoses, and the RCA ostium could not be engaged. Contrast injection into the left system revealed retrograde filling of the RCA via multiple collateral vessels. On further review, it was felt that the RCA was emptying back into the pulmonary artery/right ventricular outflow tract.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

options.B: Acute coronary syndrome with right ventricular infarction
options.D: Anomalous right coronary artery from the pulmonary artery

question:
A 12-year-old African American girl with known iron deficiency anemia presented with 2 months of weight loss, decreased appetite, diffuse abdominal pain, and bilious vomiting. On examination, she had generalized abdominal tenderness and epigastric fullness. Laboratory studies showed thrombocytosis and elevated inflammatory markers. An upper gastrointestinal series revealed severe gastric distention with failure of contrast to pass beyond the stomach, consistent with gastric outlet obstruction. Magnetic resonance enterography demonstrated marked distention of the stomach and the first and second portions of the duodenum with decompression of the third portion; the remainder of the small and large bowel appeared normal. Upper endoscopy showed complete obstruction at the second portion of the duodenum. Biopsies from the stomach and proximal duodenum demonstrated chronic gastritis and mildly active duodenitis proximal to the stricture, without evidence of granulomas, viral inclusions, or malignancy.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

options.A: Gastric outlet obstruction due to peptic ulcer disease

question:
A 63-year-old man with ischaemic cardiomyopathy and New York Heart Association class IV symptoms, on maximal medical therapy, was referred after 22 days in an intensive care unit for inability to wean from dobutamine support (INTERMACS III), with evidence of low cardiac output (acute on chronic kidney injury, hepatic dysfunction, and respiratory failure). 

On transfer, he was bed-bound, afebrile, blood pressure 100/60 mmHg, heart rate 90 beats/min (on dobutamine), and oxygen saturation 95% on 40% FiO2 by facemask. Cardiovascular examination revealed a 2/6 apical systolic murmur and signs of volume overload with low output. 

His history included a myocardial infarction at age 48 (managed conservatively), coronary artery bypass grafting with left internal mammary artery grafts and left ventricular aneurysmectomy 6 months earlier, stage IV chronic kidney disease (peak creatinine 3.0 mg/dL, eGFR 21 mL/min/1.73 m²), and obesity (BMI 30.4 kg/m²). The most recent angiogram before surgery showed patent left main, mid left anterior descending artery occlusion, atheromatous circumflex and right coronary arteries, and 70% stenosis of the posterior descending artery. 

Laboratory tests on transfer showed B-natriuretic peptide 1328 pg/mL, INR 1.44, albumin 2.9 g/dL, total bilirubin 1.8 mg/dL, γ-glutamyl transferase 114 U/L, urea 98 mg/dL, creatinine 2.3 mg/dL, sodium 137 mmol/L, high-sensitivity troponin 79 ng/L, and CK-MB 1.8 IU/L. Chest radiography revealed cardiomegaly and interstitial pulmonary oedema. 

Transthoracic echocardiography was suboptimal. Transoesophageal echocardiography showed a dilated left ventricle (end-diastolic diameter 72 mm), ejection fraction ~20% on dobutamine, and a severe, centrally directed mitral regurgitant jet from A2–P2 attributed to annular dilatation and leaflet tethering (Carpentier types I and IIIb). The MR was considered disproportionate, with an effective regurgitant orifice area of 80 mm² and a regurgitant volume of 76 mL. Systolic pulmonary artery pressure was 60 mmHg. Left atrial haemodynamic tracing demonstrated giant V waves up to 65 mmHg (mean 32 mmHg).

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 4-year-old girl with bilateral congenital glaucoma underwent examination under anesthesia. Three years earlier, she had trabeculotomy and trabeculectomy in both eyes, and two years ago she suffered a retinal detachment in the right eye. The left eye had also undergone three sessions of transscleral cyclophotocoagulation. She was on topical timolol/dorzolamide twice daily, apraclonidine thrice daily, and latanoprost nightly in the left eye. There was no history of intraocular inflammation.

Visual acuity was count fingers in the right eye and no light perception in the left eye. Examination of the right eye revealed a quiet eye, horizontal corneal diameter 11.5 mm, deep anterior chamber, cataractous lens, corectopia, and chronic retinal detachment. Examination of the left eye showed a quiet eye with white conjunctiva, corneal diameter 12.5 mm with a faint diffuse scar, deep anterior chamber, clear lens, and a healthy optic disc (cup–disc ratio 0.5).

Gonioscopy of the left eye revealed several quadrants of densely hyperpigmented, dark brown, raised mounds within the trabecular meshwork, resembling iris tissue. No retrocorneal pigment deposition or iris transillumination defects were noted. Anterior segment OCT showed a deep anterior chamber, open angle recess, slightly posteriorly bowed iris, and confirmed elevated tissue within the angle. No ciliary body or iris masses were seen. Intraocular pressure in the left eye was 11 mmHg, and axial length was stable at 24.70 mm. Six months later, the appearance of the angle structures remained unchanged.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 27-year-old woman presented with two weeks of nocturnal fever, arthralgia, right upper–quadrant abdominal pain, and icterus. She is a sheep farmer who consumes nonpasteurized cheese and has no history of drug use. On examination, temperature was 38.5°C, blood pressure 70/45 mm Hg, pulse 70 beats/min, respirations 18 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 96% on room air. She was euvolemic, had a palpable liver edge 5 cm below the costal margin, diffuse right upper–quadrant tenderness, and marginal splenomegaly. The remainder of the examination was normal.

Laboratory studies showed WBC 5400 cells/mm³, hemoglobin 10.6 g/dL, platelets 216 000/mm³, AST 224 U/L, ALT 159 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 2340 U/L, γ-GGT 597 U/L, direct bilirubin 3.1 mg/dL, albumin 3.3 g/dL, globulin 4.3 g/dL, and uric acid 1.4 mg/dL. Serum sodium was 124 mmol/L, potassium 4.4 mmol/L, chloride 95 mmol/L, plasma osmolality 262 mmol/kg, urine osmolality 274 mmol/kg, and urine sodium 64 mmol/L. Serum pituitary, thyroid, and adrenal function tests were normal. ESR was 47 mm/hr and CRP 26 U/L. Serologic tests for Epstein–Barr virus, cytomegalovirus, and hepatitis viruses were negative.

Abdominal ultrasonography and CT showed hepatomegaly, mild splenomegaly, and small pericardial effusion without biliary dilatation. CT of the thorax and brain was normal. Echocardiography confirmed mild pericardial effusion without cardiac dysfunction.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 64-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of chronic progressive gait disturbances that began approximately 1 month after completing four cycles of docetaxel-based chemotherapy for breast cancer 2 years earlier. Before chemotherapy, she had walked normally. At the time of chemotherapy, she developed mild, distal extremity numbness that remained stable over 2 years. She denied headache, dizziness, speech changes, cognitive difficulties, visual loss, abnormal movements, or bladder or bowel symptoms, and there was no evidence of cancer relapse.

Electrophysiological studies were inconclusive but indicated polyneuropathy. Despite stable sensory deficits, her gait disturbance continued to worsen, prompting neurologic evaluation. On examination, she walked with short steps, rotational instability, and an increased base of support; the Romberg test was negative. There was mild impairment of superficial and deep sensation in both feet and hands. Cranial nerves, strength, deep tendon reflexes, cerebellar testing, and sphincter function were normal. Cognitive testing, including the Mini-Mental State Examination and verbal fluency, was within normal limits.

Brain MRI showed marked enlargement of the supratentorial and infratentorial ventricles without cortical atrophy and no evidence of metastatic or meningeal disease. A lumbar puncture was performed, and 30 mL of CSF was drained. Gait was assessed before and 24 hours after the tap: a 10-m walking test demonstrated a clear reduction in the number of steps and time to completion, and the number of steps needed for a 360° rotation decreased. CSF cytology was unremarkable.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 54‐year‐old Hispanic woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and peripheral vascular disease presented with a 3‐day history of severe watery diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, and altered mental status. Her medications included metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril, metoprolol, pravastatin, aspirin, hydrochlorothiazide, and nightly insulin detemir; she had not administered her prandial insulin for the preceding 24 hours due to poor oral intake. In the emergency department, her serum glucose was 47 mg/dL, for which glucagon was administered. She was hypotensive (70/39 mmHg), confused, and disoriented, with dry mucous membranes and decreased bowel sounds. Shortly after arrival, she became unresponsive and developed pulseless electrical activity; return of spontaneous circulation was achieved after 3 minutes of cardiopulmonary resuscitation, including intubation and mechanical ventilation. In the intensive care unit, her blood pressure was 98/37 mmHg on norepinephrine (0.3 µg/kg/min). Laboratory studies showed arterial pH 6.57, serum bicarbonate 2 mEq/L, anion gap 30 mmol/L, osmolar gap 21 mOsm/kg, lactate 16.3 mmol/L, hemoglobin A1c 7.3%, and creatinine 8.07 mg/dL (baseline 0.86 mg/dL two months earlier). A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis revealed only nonspecific gallbladder wall thickening. Vasopressin was added to maintain a mean arterial pressure above 60 mmHg.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old right-hand–dominant Polynesian man (BMI 42.6) with a history of crystal-proven gout and poorly controlled type 2 diabetes presented with progressive right forearm, hand, and shoulder swelling and pain. Four days after two days of light weight exercise, he noted moderate forearm swelling and pain unrelieved by his usual oral anti-inflammatories. Four days later, during a long car trip, his right shoulder pain and forearm/hand swelling worsened. In the emergency department he received IV ketorolac, oral prednisone, and tramadol. The next morning he was admitted for presumed severe acute gout flare and emerging compartment syndrome of the right forearm and hand.

He was afebrile, blood pressure 140/73 mm Hg, heart rate 89 bpm. Examination showed tense volar forearm compartments, reduced finger sensation, decreased capillary refill, and severe pain with passive finger stretch, with overlying erythema suggestive of cellulitis.

Laboratory studies revealed serum uric acid 7.7 mg/dL, leukocytosis (13 800/μL), normal initial blood glucose (84 mg/dL), and mildly elevated CK (245 U/L). Tests for rheumatoid factor, HIV, and blood toxicology were negative. CT of the right upper extremity demonstrated fluid in the olecranon bursa and elbow joint; MRI of the shoulder was unremarkable. Aspiration of the elbow joint yielded monosodium urate crystals and a white-cell count of 91 650/mm3; wrist aspiration showed calcium pyrophosphate crystals with no bacterial growth. CT angiogram and Doppler ultrasound excluded vascular pathology.

With clinical signs of compartment syndrome and compartment pressures of 20–24 mm Hg in the volar forearm and 45–48 mm Hg in the carpal tunnel and palm, urgent fasciotomies of the volar compartments and carpal tunnel were performed. Intraoperatively the volar forearm muscles were tense, bulging, and contractile; there was no necrosis, pus, or odor. Small 0.25–0.5 mm tophi were noted in the synovial sheaths of the flexor tendons. Wounds were dressed with negative-pressure therapy.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 59-year-old Hispanic man with known neurofibromatosis type 1 presented with a 1-month history of episodic epigastric pain, pruritus, a 15-pound weight loss, and progressive jaundice. On examination he was deeply icteric; pulse 118 /min, respiratory rate 20 /min, blood pressure 116/80 mm Hg, temperature 98.3 °F. The abdomen was mildly distended with an enlarged, non-tender liver and widespread excoriations. His skin showed >10 cutaneous fibromas, multiple café-au-lait spots >1.5 cm, and extensive axillary freckling, meeting criteria for NF 1. 

Initial laboratory studies demonstrated a cholestatic liver profile with normal blood counts. Abdominal ultrasound showed marked intrahepatic and extrahepatic biliary dilatation with the proximal common bile duct measuring 17 mm. Contrast-enhanced CT revealed a 4.5 × 5 × 3.4 cm hypoenhancing soft-tissue mass in the porta hepatis encasing the portal vein and common hepatic artery and extending toward the pancreatic head. MRCP confirmed an abrupt occlusion in the mid common bile duct; the pancreatic duct was normal. Additional imaging identified a 5 cm hypoenhancing splenic mass and a 4.5 × 4.5 × 2.8 cm retroperitoneal hypodense mass.

On hospital day 2, a lipid panel showed total cholesterol >1,000 mg/dL, HDL 19 mg/dL, triglycerides 233 mg/dL, and LDL >1,000 mg/dL; a repeat test confirmed these values. ERCP demonstrated a tight 6 cm proximal CBD stricture with marked biliary dilatation. After biliary sphincterotomy and placement of self-expanding metallic stents, the patient’s jaundice improved and serum cholesterol levels declined steadily.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 14-year-old boy was referred for failure to gain weight and restrictive eating behaviors since early childhood. Parents described early breastfeeding difficulties and persistent aversions to certain textures and smells. His diet was limited to very plain and smoothly textured foods (for example, burgers without toppings, purees) and a narrow list of items (for example, Cheerios, chicken nuggets). He displayed rigid routines (insisting on the same seat at dinner, folding clothes in a particular way) and marked distress when routines were disrupted. He was anxious, with worsening anxiety after parental separation at age 5, and had inattention affecting school. His weight had tracked along the 3rd percentile until age 5, then plateaued for 8 years; bone age was delayed by one year. He was seen 2 months earlier by a pediatric endocrinologist, with no clear endocrine diagnosis. One month before admission, a psychiatrist’s formulation included sensory integration disorder, communication and other learning disorders, eating disorder, and autism spectrum disorder. Occupational therapy and psychoeducational assessments were recommended but not completed. On admission, weight was 24.4 kg (<0.01 percentile), height 143.5 cm (0.39 percentile), BMI 11.8 kg/m2 (≈68% of ideal weight). Laboratory studies showed no electrolyte abnormalities. The gastroenterology service did not find an organic cause for his failure to thrive.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 45-year-old woman from Eastern Nepal presented with a 10-day history of painful erythematous nodules along the radial border of her left hand, forearm, and arm, and a 5-day history of swelling of the left hand. For the preceding 10 months, she had been treated with oral corticosteroids, methotrexate, and hydroxychloroquine for presumed pain in the left arm and forearm. She denied any antecedent trauma.

On examination, there were six tender, warm, erythematous nodules in a linear distribution along the radial border of the left upper extremity, with edema extending from the proximal third of the fingers to the wrist joint. Vital signs were within normal limits.

Laboratory investigations revealed a normal complete blood count, blood glucose, chest radiograph, urine microscopy, and serum cortisol. C-reactive protein was elevated; rheumatoid factor and serologies for HIV, hepatitis B, and hepatitis C were negative. Liver transaminases were mildly elevated (1.6× upper limit of normal), and vitamin D level was low. A wound swab culture was sterile, and a microfilaria test was negative.

An incisional skin biopsy was performed and sent for histopathology and tissue culture for bacteria and fungi. Histopathological examination showed lymphohistiocytic infiltrates admixed with neutrophils and activated macrophages forming epithelioid aggregates and ill-defined granulomas, with acid-fast bacilli on staining. A slit-skin smear for Mycobacterium leprae and sputum examination for acid-fast bacilli were negative. Culture of the biopsy specimen yielded no growth.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 70-year-old woman with a history of rheumatic mitral stenosis status post percutaneous mitral valvotomy in 2012, hypertension, persistent atrial fibrillation, and prior amiodarone-induced hepatotoxicity underwent successful radiofrequency pulmonary vein isolation for symptomatic atrial fibrillation and was discharged off antiarrhythmics. Two days later, she was admitted in atrial fibrillation, converted to sinus rhythm with 360 J, and started on flecainide 100 mg every 12 hours. 

Approximately five hours after the first dose of flecainide, she developed fever (38.7 °C), chills, and a dry cough, and her oxygen requirement increased from 2 L/min by nasal cannula to high-flow nasal cannula. Laboratory studies revealed a leukocytosis of 12 000 cells/mm3. Portable chest radiography showed increased interstitial markings compared with baseline. She received 20 mg intravenous furosemide, and her symptoms initially improved.

Three hours after the second 100 mg dose of flecainide, fever recurred with worsening dyspnea, and her oxygen support escalated to bilevel positive airway pressure. Leukocytosis rose to 16 000 cells/mm3. Computed tomography of the chest demonstrated diffuse bilateral ground-glass opacities predominantly in the upper lobes, multifocal perilobular consolidation in the lower lobes, and mild traction bronchiectasis and bronchiolectasis. Transthoracic echocardiography showed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 65–70%, no mitral valve stenosis, and a small, collapsed inferior vena cava; serum brain natriuretic peptide was 186 pg/mL. Flecainide was discontinued.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 59-year-old man with no significant medical or surgical history presented with a 6-week history of an enlarging, painful mass on the dorsum of his right hand. Six weeks earlier, he had sustained a penetrating injury from a galvanized culvert, resulting in a small laceration. Despite antibiotic therapy for presumed Staphylococcus abscess, the lesion continued to enlarge. On examination, there was a 2.5 × 2.0 cm solid, mobile mass over the dorsal right hand. The lesion was nonpulsatile, nonfriable, nonfluctuant, not adherent to underlying structures, and appeared limited to the cutaneous and subcutaneous tissues. There was no surrounding erythema, no expressible drainage, and no palpable lymphadenopathy. A central area of keratinaceous-appearing material was noted. Radiographs of the hand were unremarkable, and routine laboratory values were within normal limits.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 73-year-old man with a history of heart failure with recovered ejection fraction (60%), hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and diabetes mellitus presented with 2 days of severe, exertional, pressure-like chest pain rated 9/10. His physical examination was unremarkable. Vital signs were: heart rate 60 bpm, blood pressure 119/58 mm Hg, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min. An electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm. Initial laboratory tests revealed hemoglobin 16 g/dL, platelet count 380 K/cm3, white-cell count 8.1 K/cm3, creatinine 2 mg/dL, high-sensitivity troponin >5000 ng/L, and B-type natriuretic peptide 2810 pg/mL. He was admitted for management of non–ST–elevation myocardial infarction and started on a heparin drip and antiplatelet therapy. 

On hospital day 2, he developed severe left lower abdominal and thigh pain. CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed marked swelling of the left psoas and iliacus muscles with heterogeneous density, consistent with hemorrhage. Anticoagulation and antiplatelet agents were discontinued, and a CT angiogram revealed active contrast extravasation into the left psoas muscle. Interventional radiology performed angiography, which showed no arterial source of active bleeding. After stabilization, his hemoglobin rose to 18 g/dL.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 65-year-old African American man with hypertension, COPD, mitral valve prolapse, CKD, osteoarthritis, PTSD, two prior strokes, nephrolithiasis, and chronic back pain presented to the emergency department with 24 hours of worsening headaches, retro-orbital pressure, decreased vision in the left eye, white flashes and floaters, and palinopsia in both eyes. He was afebrile, without leukocytosis, and other signs of infection.  
On ophthalmologic evaluation, best-corrected visual acuity was 20/40 OD and 20/60 OS. Extraocular movements were full, intraocular pressures were 20 mm Hg OD and 21 mm Hg OS, and there was a 1+ relative afferent pupillary defect OS. CT of the head was unremarkable. Slit-lamp and fundus examinations were normal except for 2+ nuclear sclerotic cataracts bilaterally. He was discharged with a presumptive diagnosis of migraine with aura.  

Ten days later, he returned with bilateral worsening vision loss, headaches, retro-orbital pressure, and periorbital swelling. Visual acuity was 20/150 OD and counting fingers at 1 m OS. Intraocular pressures were 26 mm Hg OD and 27 mm Hg OS. Extraocular movements were reduced in all gazes. Hertel measurements were 22 mm OD and 26 mm OS (base 118 mm).  
MRI of the orbits showed significant proptosis with straightened optic nerves and enlargement of all extraocular muscles, including their tendon sheaths. Serum IgG, ACE, TSI, TSH, and T4 levels were all within normal limits.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 32-year-old woman, two years after simultaneous pancreas–kidney transplant for type I diabetes, presented with sudden onset of constant, dull, generalized abdominal pain accompanied by fever, nausea, and vomiting. She denied trauma, alcohol use, and changes in bowel or urinary habits. She had undergone MRI of the brain with gadobutrol 48 hours earlier. Her medications included mycophenolate mofetil, tacrolimus, prednisolone, and other maintenance therapies. 

On arrival, she was hypotensive (90/29 mmHg), tachycardic (100 bpm), and hypothermic (34.3°C), with cool extremities. Blood glucose was 6.5 mmol/L. Examination revealed tenderness over the transplanted pancreatic graft without renal graft tenderness. Arterial blood gas showed metabolic acidosis (pH 7.24; lactate 3.2 mmol/L). Laboratory tests revealed WBC 22.3 × 10^9/L, creatinine 159 µmol/L (baseline 85 µmol/L), urea 12.4 mmol/L, and serum lipase 3368 U/L (normal 0–60). Liver tests and CRP were unremarkable. Tacrolimus trough was 6.2 µg/L, and donor-specific antibody testing was negative. 

CT of the abdomen demonstrated inflammatory changes of the transplanted pancreatic graft with poor enhancement, suggestive of graft pancreatitis, with no hydronephrosis. Cytomegalovirus PCR was negative.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 48-year-old man with no prior comorbidities was transferred to the ICU after emergent craniotomy and intracranial pressure monitoring for traumatic epidural hematoma and subarachnoid bleeding. He had a 3–4-day history of fever and nonproductive cough following contact with a person known to have COVID-19; SARS-CoV-2 PCR on admission was positive. Seventy-two hours after ICU admission and decompressive craniectomy, he developed a maculopapular erythematous exanthem predominantly on the trunk and extremities, with sparing of mucosae, palms, and soles. Over the next 24–48 hours, the rash evolved into a papulovesicular eruption. Concurrently, he became hypotensive requiring high-dose vasopressors and developed progressive respiratory failure with rising oxygen requirements. Routine laboratory tests showed mildly elevated inflammatory markers and lymphopenia. Arterial blood gas on FiO2 0.55 revealed an oxygenation index of 490. Initial chest CT showed no infiltrates; on day 5, repeat CT demonstrated patchy ground-glass opacities and consolidations typical of COVID-19 affecting 23% of lung volume, along with small bilateral pleural effusions. At that time, his oxygenation index had fallen to 97.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

options.A: Drug-induced rash with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms

question:
A 17-year-old farmer from Kankossa, Mauritania, presented with severe chronic arthralgia of both hands and bilateral perforating ulcers on the soles, evolving over 7 years. His symptoms began with hypopigmented maculopapular skin lesions and paresthesia at the finger and toe tips and on the plantar surfaces. He had seen multiple dermatologists and traditional healers without improvement despite analgesic, antibiotic, and topical therapies. 

On examination, there was distal phalangeal atrophy with severe hand deformities, hypochromic anesthetic macules on the skin, atrophy of the metatarsals and phalanges with marked foot deformity, and bilateral plantar perforating ulcers. Palpable thickening of the ulnar and popliteal nerve trunks was noted. Visual acuity was normal. Routine laboratory tests were within normal limits. Radiographs of the hands and feet demonstrated osteoporosis.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 29-year-old woman, 20 days postpartum after a cesarean delivery at 35.6 weeks’ gestation, presented with a 2-day history of fever (up to 38.5°C), body aches, sore throat, and a mild cough. On admission, her temperature was 39.8°C, pulse 104/min, respiratory rate 18/min, and blood pressure 104/68 mmHg. A complete blood count showed a white-cell count of 9.3×10^9/L with 78.3% neutrophils. She was treated with oseltamivir and paracetamol.
