On the third day of fever, she developed generalized convulsions and an altered mental state. Neurologic examination revealed dysarthria, nystagmus, and ataxia of the trunk and all extremities. Meningeal signs were absent.

On hospital day 4, brain MRI demonstrated well-defined lesions in the bilateral middle cerebellar peduncles and the splenium of the corpus callosum that were hypointense on T1-weighted images, hyperintense on T2-weighted images, and hyperintense on diffusion-weighted imaging with low apparent diffusion coefficient values. There was no contrast enhancement. MR angiography and venography were normal.

Cerebrospinal fluid analysis showed 29 white cells per mm^3; Gram stain was negative, and tests for Epstein–Barr virus, cytomegalovirus, and VDRL were negative. Electroencephalography revealed diffuse slow waves with focal spike waves.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old woman with no significant medical history noted a left laterocervical mass enlarging over 3 months. CT imaging showed a 25 × 28 × 44 mm laterocervical mass. In September 2019, a core-needle biopsy was reported as paraganglioma. Octreotide scintigraphy revealed uptake in the left cervical region and in the body of the stomach; endoscopic biopsies of the stomach were negative. In October 2019, the cervical mass was resected, and the initial diagnosis was extraosseous Ewing sarcoma. Upon referral, an anatomopathological review described a well-demarcated tumor composed of oval cells with clear eosinophilic cytoplasm arranged in nests separated by thin fibrous septa. Immunohistochemistry was positive for S100, SOX10, synaptophysin, and CD57; negative for Melan-A, HMB45, MITF, CD99, and multiple other markers. FISH detected an EWSR1 rearrangement that next-generation sequencing confirmed as an EWSR1-ATF1 t(12;22) fusion. A diagnosis of malignant gastrointestinal neuroectodermal tumor (GNET) was rendered. In January 2020, PET-CT showed uptake in the left laterocervical surgical bed consistent with local recurrence.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 42‐year‐old right‐handed man was admitted with a 6-year history of painless, progressive stiffness and wasting of the back and proximal limb muscles. He reported increasing difficulty with walking (short‐stepping gait), rising from supine, bending in any direction, lifting his arms, and abducting his thighs beyond 20°. He did not experience weakness, sensory changes, spasms, or pain.

Six years earlier, he began self-injecting pentazocine (60 mg daily, intramuscularly into buttocks and upper arms) combined with promethazine for abdominal pain and continued in a dependent fashion. He never injected the thighs, calves, abdomen, shoulder girdle, or forearms. He stopped the injections 6 months after symptom onset. He had a 13-year history of alcohol dependence but had been abstinent for at least 6 years. He was normotensive and non-diabetic.

On examination, there was marked wasting and woody induration of the deltoids, biceps, glutei, quadriceps, and paraspinal muscles. Active range of motion was markedly reduced: arm abduction to 45–50°, thigh abduction to 20°, elbow flexion to 15°, and thigh flexion to 10°. Distal joint movements were normal. Muscle power was full within the limited ranges, and sensation was intact. Other systems were unremarkable.

Laboratory tests, including complete blood count, liver and renal function, serum calcium, phosphate, and creatine phosphokinase, were within normal limits. Roentgenograms of the lumbar spine, pelvis, thighs, knees, chest, and shoulders revealed multiple soft‐tissue calcifications with muscle hyperdensity. Electromyography of affected muscles was normal.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 52-year-old woman with known unrepaired Type II tricuspid atresia with d-transposition of the great arteries, hypoplastic right ventricle, unrestricted atrial and ventricular septal defects, and multi-level pulmonic stenosis presented with sudden-onset severe right upper quadrant abdominal pain without nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or dysuria. Two months earlier, she had flu-like symptoms treated with doxycycline, and she reported poor dentition with no dental care for two years. On examination, her blood pressure was 90/54 mmHg, she was afebrile, heart rate was 80 bpm, and oxygen saturation was 79% on room air. A grade-2 systolic ejection murmur was heard at the left upper sternal border. She had cyanotic fingers bilaterally but no Osler nodes, Janeway lesions, or splinter hemorrhages. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 17.9 g/dL and C-reactive protein 2.2 mg/dL; lactate, white blood cell count, and creatinine were within reference ranges. Computed tomography of the abdomen demonstrated an acute right renal infarct; cerebral computed tomography was negative. She was anticoagulated with heparin.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 46-year-old healthy woman was referred for evaluation of a painful soft-tissue mass on the right plantar midfoot. She first noticed the swelling 2–3 years earlier; it had enlarged and become increasingly painful over the past 4 months, and she described walking on a hard stone. There was no history of trauma or family history of similar problems. On inspection, the overlying skin was normal without signs of infection. Examination revealed bilateral planovalgus feet with bilateral hallux valgus (worse on the right), and a tender, subcutaneous mass on the right plantar midfoot associated with sensitivity and numbness.  

To provide temporary relief, she received an ultrasound-guided injection of 80 mg depo-medrone and 1 ml of 1% lignocaine into the second metatarsophalangeal region. Plain radiographs showed no bony injury or malignancy. Ultrasound demonstrated a well-defined, hypoechoic tender lesion within the plantar subcutaneous tissue, measuring approximately 17 × 7 × 11 mm, extending deep to but not arising from the plantar fascia. Contrast-enhanced MRI revealed a 2 cm × 5 mm tubular oblique lesion in the plantar subcutaneous fat and ruled out sinus tarsi syndrome or internal derangements. She was provided with orthotic medial arch support and referred to a sarcoma multidisciplinary team, which found no radiologic features of sarcoma. Despite these measures, she continued to experience sharp, stabbing pain (10/10) and elected to proceed with surgical excision biopsy.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 39-year-old right-handed woman was evaluated for recurrent seizures that began in early 2014. The initial events were dyscognitive and progressed to secondary generalized tonic-clonic seizures. Some weeks before the first seizure, she developed subacute bifrontal headaches and noted progressive short-term memory impairment. In May 2015, her husband observed a transient personality change with disinhibited behavior, which did not recur. 

Her medical history was notable for Crohn’s disease diagnosed in 2006 by ileocolonoscopy, treated with steroids and mesalazine until 2008, then methotrexate (15 mg weekly) through 2016, with no gastrointestinal symptoms since 2013. She smoked 25 pack-years and had no other comorbidities, family history of neurological disease, or exposures. 

In early 2014, a transient dyscognitive event was interpreted as a transient ischemic attack, and a head CT excluded hemorrhage. By August 2014, after a secondarily generalized seizure, MRI showed multiple supratentorial Gd-enhancing cortical lesions. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis revealed pleocytosis (14 cells/μl) with negative oligoclonal bands. Bronchoalveolar lavage and chest CT were normal. Laboratory studies showed normal levels of IL-2 receptor, ACE, neopterin, calcium, ANCA, ANA, and antiphospholipid antibodies; an interferon-gamma release assay was negative.

Two months later, MRI demonstrated progression of cortical lesions. Methotrexate was supplemented with prednisolone, leading to stable lesion load on MRI in February 2015. Seizures recurred intermittently without changes in therapy. In August 2016, MRI revealed increased bihemispheric cortical and deep gray matter hemorrhagic lesions, gadolinium enhancement in multiple cortical and limbic regions, and bilateral hippocampal atrophy with hyperintensity. Repeat CSF showed pleocytosis (8 cells/μl), elevated protein, and now positive oligoclonal bands. Extensive serologic and CSF testing for neural autoantibodies (including paraneoplastic and autoimmune encephalitis panels) was negative. Complement levels, rheumatoid factor, and immunoglobulins were normal. Neuropsychological testing showed depressive symptoms with attentional, executive, and memory deficits.

In September 2016, further evaluation included CSF testing for Borrelia burgdorferi, Treponema pallidum, PCR for HSV-1/2, VZV, Tropheryma whipplei, and HIV (all negative). Ultrasonography of extra- and intracranial arteries showed no large-vessel vasculitis. Sarcoidosis biomarkers, PR3-ANCA, p-ANCA, and blood cultures were negative. Fluorescein angiography of the retina excluded small-vessel vasculitis or Susac’s syndrome. Given the progressive cortical lesions and nondiagnostic noninvasive workup, a stereotactic brain biopsy was planned.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 42-year-old woman presented with a painless, pigmented growth on the left anterior maxillary gingiva for 3 months. The lesion began as a pinpoint spot and gradually enlarged, extending to the palatal gingiva. She reported no systemic illness or head and neck trauma. Vital signs were within normal limits.

Extraoral examination revealed a tender, firm left submandibular lymph node fixed to the mandible and a separate 3 × 3 cm firm, non-tender, mobile right cervical lymph node. Intraoral examination showed a 2 × 3 cm sessile, rough-surfaced, black pigmented lesion on the left maxillary attached gingiva near the central and lateral incisors, extending to the hard-palate rugae and causing displacement of those teeth.

Laboratory studies, including complete blood count, biochemical tests, and urinalysis, were within normal limits. Intraoral periapical and panoramic radiographs demonstrated a radiolucent area with displacement of the left central and lateral incisors. Neck ultrasonography revealed a highly vascular mass with a low-velocity waveform indenting major vessels. Contrast-enhanced CT of the head and neck showed a well-defined, heterogeneously enhancing hypodense nodule in the left premaxillary region without bony erosion, and a 34 × 32 mm necrotic left level II cervical lymphadenopathy compressing the internal jugular vein.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 32-year-old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus and dyslipidemia presented with a 2-day history of continuous, progressive epigastric pain that began after a heavy meal. He described the pain as sharp (10/10), radiating to his back, and worsened by leaning back or lying down. He reported nausea and several episodes of non-bloody, non-bilious vomiting. He denied recent dietary changes, binge drinking, hospitalizations, or medication changes.  

Home medications included fenofibrate 48 mg daily, metformin 500 mg twice daily, insulin glargine 30 units at bedtime, and insulin lispro 7 units three times daily. He admitted occasional noncompliance with his medications and poor adherence to a diabetic diet. He led a sedentary lifestyle.  

On examination, temperature was 98.9 °F, heart rate 91 bpm, blood pressure 127/84 mmHg, and respiratory rate 18 breaths/min. He sat leaning forward in mild distress. He was alert and oriented. Lung fields were clear. Cardiac examination was unremarkable. Abdominal examination revealed epigastric tenderness with voluntary guarding; there was no peripheral edema.  

Chart review revealed a hospitalization 2 years earlier for diabetic ketoacidosis complicated by hypertriglyceridemia and pancreatitis.  

Laboratory studies on this admission showed a normal complete blood count, blood glucose 256 mg/dL, an elevated anion gap >17, and a beta-hydroxybutyrate level of 74.5 mg/dL. Serum lipase was 257 IU/L. Abdominal ultrasonography revealed a normal gallbladder and no dilation of the common bile duct.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 3-year-old Caucasian sister and brother were referred for evaluation of severe, pruritic, nonexudative erythroderma affecting nearly the entire skin surface, ongoing since 8 months of age. At birth their skin and general health were normal, and both had grown regularly. On examination, both children had generalized scaling erythema with severe pruritus and onychodystrophy; the sister also had bilateral lower-lid ectropion. Their father and his twin offspring have plaque psoriasis, and their father's twin sister has psoriatic arthritis. Routine blood tests, metabolic panels and skin‐culture studies were all within normal limits. A punch biopsy of lesional skin was performed for further evaluation.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 46-year-old woman presented for preoperative evaluation before a planned hysterectomy. She reported a 2-year history of dry cough and an unintentional 10-lb weight loss over the past year. Her medical history was unremarkable; she was a non-smoker with no history of cancer, recent travel, or significant occupational exposures. On examination, she was afebrile and denied dyspnea or shortness of breath on exertion. Pulmonary-function testing showed mild nonspecific restrictive and obstructive defects. A preoperative chest radiograph demonstrated innumerable nodular opacities throughout both lungs without lymphadenopathy or osseous lesions. A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the chest revealed innumerable soft-tissue–density lesions of varying sizes throughout the lungs, the largest measuring 4 cm in the right infrahilar region, with no mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy, pleural effusions, or bone lesions. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed a large central pelvic soft-tissue mass measuring 17 × 15 × 13 cm with a necrotic component and multiple thin enhancing septations, as well as a 6.5-cm exophytic right adnexal lesion; there was no fat attenuation, calcification, lymphadenopathy, or osseous involvement.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

options.D: Pulmonary metastases from an occult primary tumor

question:
A 41-year-old man presented with a 1-month history of daily high-grade fevers, night sweats, a 20-kg weight loss, anorexia, asthenia, progressive lower-limb weakness, and recent jaundice. His history included hypertension managed with captopril, remote heavy alcohol use, and active smoking. On examination, he was febrile (38.0 °C), slightly dehydrated, and icteric; blood pressure was 120/75 mmHg, pulse 106 bpm, and respiratory rate 18 breaths/min; BMI was 21.6. No lymphadenopathy was noted. Cardiac exam revealed a mild systolic murmur at the mitral area; lung fields were clear. Abdominal examination showed a liver edge 1 cm below the right costal margin and a tender spleen 5 cm below the left costal margin.  

Laboratory studies revealed: hemoglobin 14.8 g/dL, WBC count 4.78×10^3/mm^3 with normal differential, platelets 91.7×10^3/mm^3; AST 443 U/L, ALT 404 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 719 U/L, γ-GT 368 U/L, total bilirubin 9.6 mg/dL, LDH 1274 U/L; INR 1.69; fibrinogen 67 mg/dL; triglycerides 617 mg/dL; ferritin >16 500 ng/mL. Serologic tests were negative for HIV, CMV (IgG/IgM), hepatitis B and C, syphilis, Schistosoma mansoni (IgM); EBV IgG was positive, IgM negative; toxoplasmosis IgG positive, IgM negative; rheumatoid factor and antinuclear antibodies were negative.  

Upper endoscopy showed enanthematic pangastritis. Transthoracic echocardiography was normal. Abdominal ultrasound and CT demonstrated splenomegaly with multiple hypoechoic areas. Bone marrow aspiration was hypercellular with granulocytic predominance; biopsy showed megakaryocytic hyperplasia and a microgranuloma. Ziehl–Neelsen, Grocott, and PAS stains were negative. Immunophenotyping showed no evidence of lymphoma.  

Despite broad supportive care—including blood product transfusions—the patient remained febrile and developed progressive hepatic failure, acute renal failure requiring hemodialysis, and respiratory failure necessitating mechanical ventilation.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 79-year-old white man with PD-L1–positive stage IV non–small cell lung cancer began pembrolizumab, 200 mg every 3 weeks. His history included a 50-pack-year smoking history, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, hypothyroidism, and oral lichen planus managed with doxycycline, 20 mg daily, with good control. He had no prior cutaneous lichen planus or history of skin cancer.

Approximately 6 weeks after starting pembrolizumab, he noted a mildly pruritic, enlarging rash on his arms, legs, and upper back. On examination, there were innumerable hyperkeratotic, necrotic papulonodules and ulcerations on the extremities and scattered multicentimeter, serpiginous, nonscaly, erythematous annular plaques on the upper back and forearms. Concurrently, his oral lichen planus flared, and his doxycycline was increased to 40 mg twice daily. He denied genital lesions or systemic symptoms.

Punch biopsies of an extremity papulonodule and an annular plaque were performed.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 57-year-old man with recently diagnosed stage IV non-mucinous adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon (multiple lung, liver, and nodal metastases) underwent palliative ileocolic bypass and was discharged in stable condition. Four days later, he was readmitted with fever and scrotal pain; a scrotal abscess was drained and antibiotics given. During the last three days of this admission, his wife noted that he developed small-stepped gait, slowed movements, clumsiness, and rigid posture. These neurologic signs worsened after discharge. 

On neurologic examination, he was mask-faced with slow eye movements and dysarthria. He had a resting tremor in both hands, bilateral bradykinesia, postural instability, a wide-based, festinating gait with freezing, absence of arm swing, and a bilaterally dystonic posture. Generalized hyperreflexia and plastic rigidity with cogwheeling were present; there were no cranial nerve palsies. Cognition was intact but responses were delayed. Family reported new nighttime urinary incontinence, and he was fully dependent for activities of daily living. He had no prior neurologic disease or hypertension. 

High-dose intravenous corticosteroid therapy was initiated, and an obvious improvement in the neurologic signs was observed.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 40-year-old man presented with right upper quadrant pain and fevers up to 38 °C. Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 17.9 × 10^9/L, C-reactive protein of 425 mg/L, and normal liver-function tests. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen demonstrated acute cholecystitis with an 8 mm stone impacted at the gallbladder neck and surrounding inflammation involving the duodenum and hepatic flexure. He received intravenous antibiotics, improved, and returned four weeks later for laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

Intraoperatively, dense adhesions of the omentum and transverse colon obscured the gallbladder and dissection was converted to open. The gangrenous gallbladder was separated from the duodenum, which showed patchy bile discoloration but no serosal tear. A subtotal cholecystectomy was completed without encountering a bile leak.

An intraoperative cholangiogram (IOC) demonstrated an unexpected streak of contrast just inferior to the cystic duct. The duodenum was flushed with saline and pressurized methylene blue via the cystic duct catheter, with no visible leak. Metal clips were placed on the discolored duodenal areas. A repeat IOC performed 10 minutes after the first showed no contrast leak and the disappearance of the previously noted streak.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

options.D: Retrograde contrast reflux into the duodenum

question:
A 69-year-old man presented with a several-year history of purple spots on both feet that gradually increased in number and size. He had well-controlled hypertension for 10 years. Dermatologic examination revealed multiple purple-colored plaques and papules, 0.5–1.5 cm in diameter, some with crusted surfaces on the dorsum of the feet and around the ankles. He had no systemic symptoms, lymphadenopathy, or visceral involvement. Laboratory and serologic tests, including HIV-1 serology, were unremarkable. A punch biopsy was obtained from a lesion on the dorsum of his foot.

Histopathological examination of hematoxylin and eosin–stained sections showed a dilated, blood-filled vascular proliferation lined by flattened endothelium, extending from the papillary dermis under hyperkeratotic epidermis to the reticular dermis in a lobular pattern. A few thick-walled vessels were interspersed within predominantly thin-walled vascular spaces. Hemosiderin pigment was noted in vessel walls and surrounding stroma. Chronic inflammatory cells were present, and focal areas of immature vascular structures forming clefts, containing extravasated erythrocytes, and mild endothelial cytologic atypia were identified, especially in the papillary dermis. No spindle cell proliferation was observed.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man with a 12-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and poor glycemic control presented four years ago with involuntary, irregular movements of the right upper and lower limbs two weeks after a fall. At that time, his blood glucose level was 712 mg/dL. A noncontrast CT scan of the brain showed no acute abnormalities. Brain MRI on fluid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) sequences demonstrated a tiny hyperintense lesion in the left basal ganglion. His hemichorea resolved spontaneously the next day, and he had no residual neurologic deficits.

Four years later, he returned with a two-week history of involuntary movements involving his right upper limb and jaw, which abated during sleep. Neurologic examination revealed normal strength and sensation in all extremities. Laboratory studies showed a blood glucose level of 671 mg/dL without ketoacidosis, a normal serum creatinine concentration, and an HbA1c of 17.3%. A CT scan of the brain demonstrated increased density in the left basal ganglia. Subsequent MRI revealed increased signal intensity in the left basal ganglion on T1-weighted imaging.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 26-year-old woman presented with a 3-month history of altered sensation on the right side of her body, beginning in the lower leg as pins and needles with hyperalgesia and progressing proximally. The leg symptoms resolved before similar sensations appeared in the right arm, tongue, and face. There was no left-sided involvement, no identifiable trigger, and no systemic symptoms. One month after resolution of dysesthesia, she developed true vertigo and was referred for neurologic evaluation.

On examination, she was alert and slightly anxious. Visual acuity and fields were normal, with no diplopia, nystagmus, or internuclear ophthalmoplegia. Pupils were equal and reactive. Cranial nerves were intact except for right-sided facial sensory loss. Motor strength was 5/5 in all limbs, tone and reflexes were normal, and plantar responses were downgoing. Proprioception and cerebellar testing were normal. She had a normal tandem gait but swayed briefly on Romberg testing. She had no significant past medical history, took no medications, and had no vascular risk factors.

Two months after the onset of vertigo, MRI of the brain and cervical spine demonstrated bilateral periventricular T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesions, some ovoid and radiating perpendicular to the corpus callosum, and additional lesions in the left medulla and upper cervical cord. 

Laboratory studies showed normal blood counts, electrolytes, and thyroid function. Tests for Borrelia, syphilis, HIV, anti-myeloperoxidase, and anti-proteinase 3 were negative. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis revealed an opening pressure of 15 mmHg, normal protein and glucose, and oligoclonal bands detected in CSF but absent in serum.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

An 18-month-old boy was evaluated for intermittent spiking fevers, constipation, and gradually progressive abdominal distension since 1 month of age. His birth history and immunizations were unremarkable. A sibling had died at age 2 with a similar illness.

On examination, he was below the fifth percentile for height and weight, jaundiced, and had abdominal distension. The liver and spleen were palpably enlarged.

Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 8.0 g/dL, white blood cell count 12,000/µL, alkaline phosphatase 2117 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 121 U/L, γ-glutamyl transferase 301 U/L, elevated total bilirubin, and prolonged prothrombin time/international normalized ratio. TORCH screening was negative. Serum α-fetoprotein was markedly elevated at 14,831 ng/mL.

Abdominal ultrasound revealed a coarse, nodular-appearing liver with multiple small hypoechoic avascular nodules in a cirrhotic background. Contrast-enhanced CT confirmed multiple non-enhancing hepatic nodules on arterial, venous, and delayed phases. The portal vein was patent; splenomegaly was present without other signs of portal hypertension.

Both kidneys were bilaterally enlarged and markedly echogenic on ultrasound, with post-contrast CT demonstrating poor enhancement and loss of corticomedullary differentiation. A chest radiograph showed expansion of the anterior rib ends (rachitic rosary), and wrist radiographs demonstrated widened, flared growth plates with metaphyseal fraying consistent with rickets.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 32‐year‐old Asian man presented with a 1‐year history of a right neck mass. His past medical history was noncontributory. On extraoral examination, there was a soft, slightly movable mass in the right submandibular region with normal overlying skin; the remainder of the physical examination was unremarkable. Computed tomography of the neck revealed a well‐defined, mixed‐density lesion measuring approximately 5 × 3 cm adjacent to the right submandibular gland. Subsequent image segmentation suggested that the lesion contained components of fat, connective tissue, and osteoid. Primary differential diagnoses based on imaging included pleomorphic adenoma of the submandibular gland, teratoma, and osteo/chondroid lipoma.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 45-year-old woman with a history of well-controlled Graves’ disease presented with palpitations and dyspnoea on minimal exertion. Her family history was notable for a brother and sister with lupus and a father with sarcoidosis. On presentation, she was normotensive, bradycardic (heart rate 38 b.p.m.), eupnoeic, normoxaemic, and afebrile. Cardiac examination revealed bradycardia with an irregular rhythm and good pulses; pulmonary, musculoskeletal, neurologic, and cutaneous examinations were unremarkable.

A 12-lead electrocardiogram demonstrated second-degree atrioventricular block with a ventricular rate of 48 b.p.m. She was admitted for further evaluation and pacemaker placement for symptomatic bradycardia. Transthoracic echocardiography showed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 60–65% and suggested two intracardiac masses. Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging confirmed two right atrial masses with delayed gadolinium enhancement. An ^18F-fluorodeoxyglucose positron emission tomography scan demonstrated that both atrial masses were FDG-avid.

To seek a diagnosis, she underwent endobronchial lung biopsy and transbronchial needle aspiration of a left hilar lymph node; both were negative for malignant cells and organisms. Two subsequent endomyocardial biopsies were either negative or non-diagnostic.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 54-year-old woman presented with a 6-month history of progressively enlarging bilateral parotid gland swellings, dryness of the mouth and eyes, and a dry cough. She reported a transient enlargement of her bilateral submandibular glands that resolved spontaneously. She denied fever, sputum production, or known exposure to tuberculosis; however, she noted decreased appetite and weight loss. 

Two months earlier, a chest radiograph had shown bilateral hilar prominences. A fine-needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) of the parotid swellings performed at that time was inconclusive, and polymerase chain reaction for Mycobacterium tuberculosis on the parotid aspirate was negative.

On examination, both parotid glands were diffusely enlarged without palpable lymphadenopathy. Repeat FNAC of the parotid glands revealed smears containing noncaseating epithelioid granulomas and multinucleated giant cells against a background of lymphoid cells and salivary ductal and acinar cells. A few giant cells contained refractile crystalline structures. There was no evidence of caseous necrosis, and an acid-fast bacillus stain was negative.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 22-year-old right-hand–dominant man presented with a 6-month history of gradual shortening of the left middle finger and inability to extend that digit, accompanied by reduced grip strength. He recalled an incidental hand trauma treated conservatively at the onset of symptoms. On examination, there was an extension lag of the middle finger, poor grip strength, and reduced height of the third ray. Laboratory studies, including serum calcium and parathyroid hormone levels, were within normal limits. Plain radiographs of the left hand showed a pathological fracture of the third metacarpal with an osteolytic lesion at the base of the second metacarpal and carpal bones. Magnetic resonance imaging revealed near-complete osteolysis of the third metacarpal (with only its base visible), a fracture of the fourth metacarpal with thinning and resorption of bone and adjacent soft-tissue changes, and a pathological fracture of the capitate with patchy areas of osteolysis. Serial radiographs at 4, 8, 12, and 18 weeks and at 6 months demonstrated progressive concentric reduction of the shafts of the third and then the fourth metacarpals, producing a “sucked candy” appearance.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 37-year-old woman with metastatic HER2-positive breast cancer presented with a 2-week history of episodic hemoptysis, odynophonia, and odynophagia. She described awakening choking on blood and a sensation of “water in her throat” with cough. She also had recent rhinorrhea and cough. Her medical history included lung, neck, and bone metastases and chemotherapy-related iron deficiency anemia, menorrhagia, hematochezia, and thrombocytopenia. Initial therapy with docetaxel, trastuzumab, and pertuzumab was changed to trastuzumab emtansine (T-DM1), zoledronic acid, and tamoxifen; her last T-DM1 infusion was 26 days before presentation (total of 15 infusions). Nasopharyngolaryngoscopy revealed a friable, excoriated nasal septum; bilateral vocal fold edema with ulceration; epithelial bleeding on supraglottic and glottic surfaces; no submucosal hemorrhage; and no surrounding ecchymosis. CT imaging of the head, neck, and chest showed no parenchymal lung lesions or other sources of hemorrhage. The pulmonology team determined that this was not related to progression of metastatic lung lesions or lung-related hemoptysis.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 39-year-old man presented to his general practitioner for a routine check-up. He had a history of extranodal NK/T-cell lymphoma of the nasal type treated with chemotherapy and local radiation and assessed as in clinical remission two months earlier. During the examination, the physician noted a heart rate of 155 beats per minute. An ECG demonstrated a regular wide-complex tachycardia interpreted as a hemodynamically stable ventricular tachycardia with an apical-anterior-lateral exit. 

He was transferred urgently to the emergency department with ongoing VT. On arrival, he developed cardiogenic shock. One synchronized biphasic shock of 100 J failed to terminate the arrhythmia and induced ventricular fibrillation, requiring chest compressions and three defibrillations to restore an unstable sinus rhythm that soon degenerated back into VT. Intravenous antiarrhythmic therapy with ajmaline, landiolol, and electrolyte substitution was initiated, maintaining a ventricular rate of 120–150 beats per minute with brief sinus rhythm intervals.

Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a 3 × 2 cm hyperechogenic zone in the apex of the left ventricle, regional hypokinesia in that area, and a small pericardial effusion. Coronary angiography showed no stenosing coronary artery disease.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

options.C: Extranodal NK/T-cell lymphoma of the nasal type

question:
A 28-year-old normotensive woman reported a 2-year history of paresthesia over the left side of her chest. Over the past 8 months, she noted partial ptosis of her left eyelid and anhidrosis of the left face. On neurologic examination, the left pupil was dilated and nonreactive to light, consistent with Horner’s syndrome. Sensory testing revealed hyperesthesia in the left D2 and D3 dermatomes; the remainder of the neurologic examination was unremarkable. Magnetic resonance imaging of the cervicodorsal spine demonstrated, at the D1–D2 level on the left, a large paravertebral extrapulmonary mass that was heterogeneously enhancing on short-T1 inversion recovery images, extended into the neural foramen, and compressed the thecal sac. T2-weighted images showed multiple flow voids within the lesion and heterogeneous signal intensity.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

Twin male patients, born after an uncomplicated pregnancy, presented with idiopathic, progressive loss of vision and hearing during early childhood, culminating in complete blindness and deafness by the first decade of life. There was no family history of similar cutaneous or audiovisual abnormalities. On examination, both brothers had multiple smooth, gelatinous papules and coalescing plaques on the face, numerous small erythematous papules on the buttocks, and scattered, well-defined red-brown waxy papules with coalescing patches on the lower extremities, accompanied by areas of mottled hypopigmentation over the knees and popliteal fossae. They denied arthritis, constitutional symptoms, abdominal or bone pain, and respiratory complaints. A punch biopsy of a knee lesion revealed a histiocytic cellular proliferation with plasma cells and multinucleated giant cells. Initial differential diagnoses included reactive histiocytoses (leishmaniasis, syphilis, atypical mycobacterial infection) and nonreactive histiocytoses (Rosai-Dorfman disease, xanthoma disseminatum, Langerhans cell histiocytosis). Empirical doxycycline (100 mg twice daily) was initiated without sustained clinical improvement, and therapy was discontinued after 6 weeks. Laboratory studies showed a normal complete blood count, metabolic panel, thyroid and liver function tests. A lipid panel demonstrated low triglycerides, normal cholesterol and LDL, and low HDL. Antinuclear antibodies and rapid plasma reagin testing were negative, and a urine Histoplasma antigen assay was negative.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old Japanese man was evaluated for several months of progressive anorexia. His medical history was notable for sublingual adenocarcinoma treated with resection and radiation 6 years earlier, supraclavicular lymph-node metastasis resection and radiation 5 years earlier, and subsequent lung and bone metastases treated with platinum-based chemotherapy. He was currently receiving nivolumab. He had no history of smoking or significant family illnesses and was taking sertraline. 

On initial evaluation, laboratory testing demonstrated secondary adrenocortical insufficiency and secondary hypothyroidism without evidence of diabetes insipidus. Serum IgG4 was 52.0 mg/dL; angiotensin-converting enzyme, 13.6 U/L; soluble interleukin-2 receptor, 684 U/mL; and T-SPOT was negative. 

Magnetic resonance imaging showed an isointense, homogenously enhancing enlargement of the pituitary gland and stalk with extension into the cavernous sinus and the planum sphenoidale dura, without compression of the optic chiasm. No other intracranial lesions were identified. Autoimmune hypophysitis related to immune checkpoint inhibition was suspected, and the patient was started on glucocorticoids and thyroid hormone replacement. 

One month later, he returned with new right-eye visual impairment (visual acuity 20/200) attributed to invasion of the optic canal. Pulse corticosteroid therapy was administered as a diagnostic treatment. Repeat MRI showed no clear reduction in the pituitary lesion, and he subsequently developed a right oculomotor nerve palsy.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

An 11-year-old previously healthy boy presented with 1 day of severe abdominal pain localizing to the right iliac fossa. He underwent a laparoscopic appendectomy for a perforated gangrenous appendix and was started on intravenous cefuroxime, gentamicin, and metronidazole. 

On postoperative day 5, his C-reactive protein remained elevated at 281 mg/L, and on day 8 he spiked fevers up to 38 °C. Abdominal ultrasound revealed a 4 cm × 2 cm hypoechoic fluid collection in the right iliac fossa. Antibiotics were changed to intravenous cefotaxime plus metronidazole. CT on day 13 confirmed the persistent collection, which was drained. Culture of the fluid grew ESBL-producing, carbapenem-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae (resistant to ertapenem but sensitive to meropenem), Enterococcus faecalis, and Enterococcus avium. 

On day 20 postappendectomy, a PICC line was placed and therapy was switched to high-dose intravenous meropenem (40 mg/kg every 8 h) plus teicoplanin. He defervesced and serial imaging showed resolving collection.

On day 7 of meropenem administration (day 27 postappendectomy), he developed a rapidly spreading pruritic, non-tender maculopapular blanching rash with occasional petechiae over his trunk and upper limbs, and fevers up to 38 °C recurred.  
 
Complete blood count on that day showed a platelet count drop to 101 × 10^9/L from 322 × 10^9/L; by day 8 of meropenem it had fallen further to 14 × 10^9/L. Leukocyte and hemoglobin levels were stable. Coagulation studies (aPTT ratio, INR, fibrinogen) were within normal limits. Repeat abdominal CT showed improvement of the fluid collection. The PICC line was removed; line tip and blood cultures were negative, as were stool and urine cultures. Serial viral serologies and molecular tests for Epstein–Barr virus, cytomegalovirus, and parvovirus remained negative.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 46‐year‐old woman with intractable complex partial seizures presented with increasing episodes of sudden falls over the past 2 years. She had been diagnosed with epilepsy at age 21 and, despite maximal antiepileptic drug therapy, continued to have monthly seizures. A left‐sided vagal nerve stimulator had been implanted in 2004, with a generator change in 2012; over the past 2 years, the output current had been gradually increased to control breakthrough seizures.  

Her new spells occurred randomly, without prodrome or postictal period, were not position related, and were not accompanied by urinary or bowel incontinence. She reported brief loss of consciousness and an average of two falls per month, resulting in injuries. This pattern differed from her prior episodes of blank staring and automatic behavior.  

On examination, vital signs were normal. Cardiopulmonary auscultation was unremarkable, and there were no signs of heart failure. Brain MRI showed left mesial temporal sclerosis and increased signal in the posterior right frontal region. Three prolonged EEG studies demonstrated spikes in the F7–T3 distribution, consistent with her known hemispheric epileptiform focus. A Ziopatch monitor disclosed no arrhythmias; ECG showed normal sinus rhythm; echocardiography and myocardial perfusion stress testing were unremarkable.  

The VNS settings were adjusted (decreased current strength and increased OFF time), but her spells persisted. A LINQ implantable loop recorder was placed, and on interrogation several sinus pauses of 10 to 12 seconds were documented. Review of her records showed that these pauses corresponded exactly to the times of her falls and injuries.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man was brought by his wife to the emergency department with a 2-week history of intermittent disorientation, nervousness, pressured speech, emotional lability, sexual disinhibition, decreased appetite, unquantified weight loss, and insomnia. The onset of symptoms coincided with a recent dental procedure. 

Initial evaluation included complete blood count, electrolytes, renal and liver panels, coagulation studies, urinalysis with drug screen, ECG, and noncontrast head CT; all were normal. He was diagnosed with a hypomanic episode and discharged on olanzapine and lorazepam. 

Three days later, he returned with worsening paranoia, third-person imperative auditory hallucinations, severe irritability, and aggression. He was involuntarily admitted. Repeat blood tests remained normal, and he was started on olanzapine, lorazepam, and lithium. 

On hospital day 2, he had a witnessed coma with loss of consciousness and sphincter relaxation. He was intubated in the ICU; head CT remained normal. After stabilization, he returned to the acute mental health unit where he was disoriented in time and space, with formal thought disorder, incoherent speech, psychomotor agitation, and dysphoria. His regimen was changed to aripiprazole, valproic acid, dipotassium clorazepate, and levetiracetam. 

Over the next five days, disorientation persisted, speech became further disorganized with thought blocking, and soliloquies appeared. On day 7, neurology ordered an EEG (showing normal background, no epileptiform changes) and brain MRI (no abnormalities). Routine labs were unchanged. 

On day 8, he developed a low-grade fever (37.4°C), hypertension, sinus tachycardia, and fluctuating consciousness progressing to stupor, chewing movements, and minimal response to pain. New labs showed leukocytosis (15.28 × 10^3/µL) with neutrophilia, INR 1.19, and D-dimer 4902 ng/mL. Blood and urine cultures later returned negative. CT pulmonary angiography revealed a segmental thrombus in the left lower-lobe artery. 

Although there were no meningeal signs, the fever and altered consciousness prompted lumbar puncture, which showed:  
• Red cells: 2000/mm^3  
• White cells: 47/mm^3 (10% polymorphonuclear, 80% monocytes)  
• Protein: 0.27 g/dL  
• Glucose: 93 mg/dL (blood 115 mg/dL)  

He was provisionally diagnosed with limbic encephalitis of probable infectious or paraneoplastic origin.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 21-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of dry skin and generalized spiny skin lesions. She had been treated empirically for eczema with a medium-strength topical corticosteroid without improvement. She had no other medical problems, medications, atopy, allergies, or family history of similar skin findings.

On examination, she had multiple minute, digitate, folliculocentric hyperkeratotic papules symmetrically involving the face, neck, trunk, and upper extremities. There was no perilesional erythema, palmoplantar keratoderma, scarring, alopecia, or signs of nutritional deficiency.

A 3-mm punch biopsy from the back showed keratin plugs in hair follicle infundibula, sparse perifollicular lymphocytes, and cystic spaces in the outer root sheath of hair follicles and sebaceous glands. Alcian blue–periodic acid–Schiff stain highlighted mucin within these spaces. Immunohistochemistry demonstrated CD3- and predominantly CD4-positive small T lymphocytes, few CD8-positive T cells, and inconspicuous CD20-positive B cells and CD30-positive cells. T-cell receptor gene-rearrangement testing failed for technical reasons.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 25-year-old woman with insulin-treated diabetes mellitus presented 4 weeks after an uncomplicated vaginal delivery with a 1-week history of progressive vision loss in her right eye. Vital signs were stable. Visual acuity was 20/80 in the right eye and 20/20 in the left. Intraocular pressures were 14 mmHg OD and 12 mmHg OS. Slit-lamp examination of the right eye revealed fine keratic precipitates, 3+ anterior chamber cells, and a clear lens. Dilated fundus examination showed 1+ vitreous haze, a hyperemic optic disc, and multiple yellow-white retinal infiltrates. The left eye examination was normal. Fluorescein angiography of the right eye demonstrated diffuse retinal vascular leakage and optic-disc staining. Optical coherence tomography showed a dry macula. 

She was admitted with a provisional diagnosis of right acute nongranulomatous panuveitis, probably representing atypical toxoplasmosis. A uveitis workup—including complete blood count, renal and liver function tests, purified protein derivative skin test, chest CT, syphilis and toxoplasmosis serology, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, C-reactive protein, and urine analysis—was entirely negative. Hemoglobin A1c was 6.7%. Empirical therapy with clindamycin, co-trimoxazole, and systemic prednisolone resulted in only transient mild improvement, followed by persistent inflammation. Repeat toxoplasmosis serology remained negative. 

Further history revealed persistent vaginal discharge during the peripartum period without fever. A gynecologic evaluation including a vaginal swab showed heavy growth of Candida albicans. Infectious disease consultation prompted blood and urine cultures, contrast CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis, and cardiac echography, all of which were unremarkable.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 71-year-old Colombian man underwent uneventful phacoemulsification and in-the-bag intraocular lens implantation in the right eye. Preoperatively, best-corrected visual acuity was 20/100 OD and 20/20 OS, with no other ocular abnormalities or systemic disease. On postoperative day 1, uncorrected visual acuity in the right eye was 20/40, intraocular pressure was 10 mmHg, and slit-lamp examination showed diffuse conjunctival hyperemia, anterior chamber cells, mild Descemet folds, medium keratic precipitates, and a poorly defined intraocular foreign body at 12 o’clock. The cornea was otherwise clear. He was treated with dexamethasone eye drops hourly, ciprofloxacin eye drops four times daily, and dexamethasone–tobramycin ointment at bedtime. One week later, visual acuity improved to 20/25, and inflammation decreased to 1+ cells. Ciprofloxacin was stopped at two weeks, steroids were tapered over one month, and ointment continued for one month. Each time steroids were tapered, inflammation recurred and improved upon restarting therapy. Removal of the foreign body was recommended but declined by the patient due to good vision and minimal symptoms. Over six months, topical steroids were modulated to control the inflammation. Nongranulomatous anterior uveitis was considered the leading diagnosis. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate was 24 mm/h; C-reactive protein and antinuclear antibodies were within normal limits, and rheumatologic diseases were excluded on laboratory and physical evaluation. At six months after surgery, further evaluation of the anterior chamber foreign body was planned with gonioscopy.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 56-year-old woman with no prior medical history presented with an 18-month history of isolated dry cough and severe asthenia. Physical examination was unremarkable. A chest radiograph showed a poorly defined, heterogeneous right parahilar opacity measuring approximately 5 cm in length. Initial considerations included a pulmonary neoplasm or pseudotumoral tuberculosis. Sputum samples were negative for Koch’s bacillus. The erythrocyte sedimentation rate was 10 mm in the first hour, and plasma protein electrophoresis indicated an inflammatory syndrome. 

Contrast-enhanced thoracic CT revealed a right hilopulmonary tissue infiltrate with multiple parenchymal nodules and mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Bronchoscopy demonstrated thickening of the right lower lobar bronchial spurs and a flattened, barely passable middle lobar bronchus; the left bronchial tree appeared normal. Multiple bronchial biopsies showed only chronic inflammatory remodeling. Bronchoalveolar lavage fluid analysis revealed a lymphocytic alveolitis (~38%) without neoplastic cells and was negative for acid-fast bacilli. 

Because the etiology remained unclear, a CT-guided percutaneous lung biopsy was performed, yielding confluent epithelio-giganto-cellular granulomas without caseous necrosis. Thoracic MRI with diffusion sequences showed a right upper lobe tissue mass with multiple nodules without hypercellularity.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 37-year-old woman underwent uterine curettage for fetal death at 9 weeks and 6 days of gestation. Six days later she developed fever up to 39.8 °C, generalized arthralgia without joint swelling, muscle fatigue, and dyspnea on minimal exertion. Initial laboratory studies showed pancytopenia (moderate microcytic anemia with circulating myelocytes), C-reactive protein elevated, procalcitonin normal, elevated D-dimer and ferritin levels. Vaginal swab grew Acinetobacter baumannii. ECG showed no right-ventricular overload or left-ventricular dysfunction. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated an ejection fraction of 65% and no pericardial fluid. Chest CT angiography revealed no emboli but an 11-mm pleural effusion. Broad-spectrum antibiotics yielded minimal improvement despite sterile blood cultures. Bone marrow biopsy showed reactive changes with cells containing eosinophilic amorphous nuclear material. Further tests revealed hypoalbuminemia, decreased C3 and C4 complement, ANA with granular fluorescence at 1:1280 (anti–SS-A and SS-B positive).  

After 16 days she was transferred to rheumatology, reporting persistent fatigue, arthralgia with 1-hour morning stiffness, dyspnea on minimal exertion, and low-grade fever up to 37.4 °C. Exam showed limb muscle weakness, decreased breath sounds at lung bases, tachycardia (100/min), and bilateral lower-leg edema. Labs: WBC 3.05×10^3/mm^3, hemoglobin 7.3 g/dL, platelets 117×10^3/mm^3, ESR 27 mm/h, CRP 199 mg/dL, procalcitonin normal, D-dimer 21 μg/mL, ferritin 953 ng/mL, NT-proBNP 798 pg/mL, triglycerides 268 mg/dL, fibrinogen 142 mg/dL, C3 0.67 g/L. ANA ≥1:10,000, anti-dsDNA 1:3200, lupus anticoagulant slightly elevated, anticardiolipin IgM positive, proteinuria 0.7 g/24 h. ANCA negative, CK normal, Coombs test negative, cultures negative. She met 2018 ACR/EULAR criteria for systemic lupus erythematosus (score 24).  

She received heparin prophylaxis, antipyretics, IV methylprednisolone 1 g/day for 6 days, then oral prednisone 1 mg/kg. Despite this, she developed worsening dyspnea, right-upper-quadrant pain, increased leg edema, and fever. Exam again showed reduced breath sounds at lung bases and epigastric tenderness. Repeat labs demonstrated rising ferritin, fibrinogen falling to 94 mg/dL, further cytopenias, NT-proBNP rising to 4600 pg/mL, and troponin T at the upper limit of normal. CT showed pleural effusions widened to 25 mm and small pelvic fluid; abdominal organs were unremarkable. Echocardiography again showed no right-ventricular overload or left-ventricular dysfunction, with only mild mitral regurgitation. The calculated H Score was 195, corresponding to an 80–88% probability of macrophage activation syndrome.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 40-year-old woman with a 17-month history of schizophrenia was admitted for adjustment of antipsychotic medication. Her regimen had been switched from risperidone to perospirone, and over three weeks the perospirone dose was increased to 40 mg/day. Shortly after reaching this dose, she developed tremor, muscle rigidity, akathisia, heavy sweating, difficulty swallowing and speaking, tachycardia, fluctuating blood pressure, a fever of 39.8 °C, restlessness, and confusion.  
Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 18.3 × 10^9/L, serum sodium 129 mmol/L, potassium 3.2 mmol/L, creatine phosphokinase 813.2 U/L (normal 26–140 U/L), and myoglobin 486 µg/L (normal 12–76 µg/L). A lumbar puncture revealed normal cerebrospinal fluid. Neuroimaging was unremarkable. Electroencephalography demonstrated diffuse slow waves, consistent with metabolic encephalopathy.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old Caucasian man presented with a dull, long-standing pain in the dorsolateral region of the left chest. He denied trauma, fever, night sweats, or weight loss. On examination, he had limited movement at the waist without motor or sensory abnormalities; the neurological examination was otherwise normal. Laboratory tests—including blood cell count, serum electrolytes, renal and liver function, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and C-reactive protein—were all within normal limits. Chest radiographs were unremarkable. 

Contrast-enhanced CT of the thorax showed a widening of the left T10 costovertebral joint with scalloping and mild sclerosis of the left aspect of the T10 vertebral body, partially involving the left pedicle and adjacent costal bone. FDG PET-CT revealed no significant tracer uptake at this site. MRI of the thoracic spine demonstrated non-specific changes at the left T10 costovertebral junction, including bone marrow edema in the T10 vertebral body, pedicle, and adjacent costal bone (low signal on T1-weighted images and high signal on T2-weighted fat-saturated images) with non-specific contrast enhancement and no spinal canal invasion.

A fluoroscopy-guided needle biopsy of the T10 vertebral body yielded fibrous/myxoid tissue with reactive lymphocytes and plasma cells and rare histiocytic elements resembling a non-specific chronic osteomyelitic pattern. A CD1a immunostain was performed to further characterize the lesion.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 33-year-old Thai man with a heavy smoking habit presented with a 3-week history of left eyelid droop without other complaints. Visual acuity was 20/20 bilaterally; intraocular pressure and ocular motility were normal. Examination revealed mild ptosis of the left upper eyelid and anisocoria, with the left pupil measuring 2 mm versus 3 mm on the right in bright light, and 2.5 mm versus 4 mm on the right in dim light. Both pupils reacted briskly to light without an afferent defect. Anhidrosis was noted on the medial aspect of the left forehead. A 10% cocaine test increased the pupillary difference to 2.5 mm (6.0 mm on the right, 3.5 mm on the left) and produced right upper-eyelid retraction. Neurologic examination was otherwise unremarkable. A provisional diagnosis of left-sided, painless, isolated Horner syndrome was made, and MRI and MRA of the neck and brain plus CT of the chest were scheduled.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 58-year-old woman presented with multiple episodes of coffee-ground emesis over 24 hours, preceded by nearly 20 episodes of greenish, nonbloody emesis beginning 3 days earlier. She reported a 7-month history of similar 2- to 3-day episodes of moderate, continuous, dull, midline abdominal pain associated with nausea, anorexia, and a mild headache without visual changes or aura; these episodes required three prior hospital admissions. Between admissions she was symptomatic enough to need assistance with activities of daily living. She denied fever, chills, bowel‐habit changes, weight loss, or skin findings. Her medical history included coronary artery disease treated with a drug-eluting stent 2 months earlier; she was on aspirin and ticagrelor. Prior outpatient therapies (omeprazole, ondansetron, sucralfate, meclizine, scopolamine, topiramate) had failed. 

On examination, her abdomen was soft, mildly distended, and tender in the epigastric and periumbilical areas without guarding or rebound. Initial laboratory studies showed potassium 3.0 mEq/L, glucose 264 mg/dL, anion gap 20 mEq/L, venous pH 7.412, HCO3 18.6 mmol/L, and beta-hydroxybutyrate 2.25 mmol/L. Management for presumed starvation ketosis and possible diabetic ketoacidosis corrected her metabolic abnormalities. Low hemoglobin and coffee-ground emesis raised concern for an upper GI bleed, so aspirin was held and intravenous fluids and antiemetics were administered.

A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis revealed a small hiatal hernia and no acute changes in the bowel or mesentery. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy showed no ulcers, strictures, or active bleeding, and biopsies were negative for malignancy. A gastric-emptying study was normal. Brain MRI demonstrated only a stable chronic cerebellar infarct without new findings. Blood cultures and urinalysis were normal, and chest radiography showed no pulmonary process. Cardiac evaluation revealed a normal ECG and negative troponins.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 10-year-old female was born full term and noted on day 4 of life to have bilateral congenital cataracts, which were removed by anterior vitrectomy at 5–6 weeks of age, with secondary membranous cataract removal two months later. At 3 months, she was referred for elevated intraocular pressure (30 mm Hg OD); examination revealed a hemangioma lateral to the right eyebrow, a posterior scalp protuberance consistent with a hemangioma, bilateral multifocal congenital hypertrophy of the retinal pigment epithelium, and left microphthalmia. Despite maximal medical therapy, she required a Baerveldt anterior chamber shunt and pars plana vitrectomy in the left eye at 17 months, with additional vitrectomies for inflammation; intraocular pressure has since remained controlled on topical therapy in both eyes.

Cardiac evaluation at 1 month identified an atrial septal defect and possible Wolff–Parkinson–White pattern on ECG. At 11 months, neurology assessment of a 5 × 6 cm parietal scalp vascular lesion raised suspicion for PHACE syndrome. MRI/MRA at 12 and 25 months showed a large right parietal scalp hemangioma (initially 3.9 × 2.3 cm, later involuting to 1.4 cm thickness) and a smaller right frontal scalp lesion, both without intracranial arterial communication, plus an aberrant right subclavian artery. Growth and development were otherwise unremarkable, with no endocrinopathies, hearing loss, or structural brain anomalies.

At 9 years, genetic consultation rendered a definite clinical diagnosis of PHACE syndrome based on two forehead/scalp hemangiomas and the presence of arterial, cardiac, and eye anomalies. During routine ophthalmic follow-up, the combination of congenital cataracts, glaucoma, bilateral CHRPE, and parental report of long dental roots, one missing tooth, and delayed primary tooth loss prompted consideration of an alternative diagnosis and molecular testing.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

options.B: Microphthalmia with coloboma and seagull facies

question:
A 54-year-old man presented with recurrent palpitations and retrosternal pain radiating to the back, accompanied by nausea and vomiting. On initial admission, his heart rate was 130 b.p.m.; blood pressure and other vital signs were stable, and physical examination was unremarkable. He had no history of hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidaemia, or cardiovascular disease, and no family history of syncope, coronary heart disease, malignant arrhythmia, or sudden death.

At a local hospital, ECG showed ventricular tachycardia at 213 b.p.m. Troponin and myocardial enzyme levels were normal. Coronary angiography revealed normal coronary arteries. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated thinning and bulging of the left ventricular (LV) apex with localized dyskinesia. Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (CMRI) confirmed abnormal systolic activity at the LV apex; the right ventricle was normal. Non-coronary obstructive acute myocardial infarction was initially considered, and he was treated with metoprolol, rosuvastatin, and amiodarone, with symptomatic improvement.

One year later, he experienced recurrent palpitations with hypotension (85/56 mmHg) but no chest pain or syncope. ECG again revealed ventricular tachycardia at 156 b.p.m. He declined implantable cardioverter-defibrillator implantation and was managed medically with beta-blocker and amiodarone.

Two months later, he returned with chest pain, palpitations, and diaphoresis. On ECG, there was T-wave inversion in leads V1–V4 (depth >1 mm, deepest ∼5 mm in V1–V2). During palpitations, ECG showed paroxysmal ventricular tachycardia of left bundle-branch-block morphology with negative precordial concordance and a notched, wide QRS, suggesting an inferoapical origin. Transthoracic echocardiography now demonstrated left atrial enlargement and an LV apical aneurysm. CMRI revealed LV apical dilation, akinesis with paradoxical systolic motion, increased fat signal at the apex on fat-suppressed T1-weighted images, and increased adipose signal with late gadolinium enhancement at the LV apex on fat-suppressed T2-weighted and gadolinium sequences. Cardiac enzymes remained within normal limits (troponin 0.007 ng/mL, NT-proBNP 85.5 pg/mL, CK-MB 9 U/L, CK 82 U/L).

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

An 18-year-old man presented with a 6-month history of progressively worsening dyspnea and, over the past 5 days, dyspnea at rest with orthopnea and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea. He reported low-grade intermittent fevers, polyarthralgias of the ankles, knees, and elbows, and intermittent ankle swelling for 2 months. He also had productive cough and one episode of hemoptysis. On examination, he was febrile (up to 39°C), tachycardic (105 beats/min), hypotensive (92/59 mm Hg), and hypoxemic (SpO₂ 89% on room air). Right supraclavicular lymph nodes were palpable. The apex beat was displaced; heart auscultation revealed a pansystolic murmur at the mitral area radiating to the axilla.  
Initial transthoracic echocardiography showed possible vegetations on the mitral valve, and he was treated with IV vancomycin, gentamicin, and ceftriaxone without clinical improvement. On transfer, he required norepinephrine and IV furosemide. Transesophageal echocardiography demonstrated a flail A2 segment of the anterior mitral leaflet with severe mitral regurgitation, a dilated left ventricle with preserved systolic function (EF 60%), and no vegetations. Blood cultures remained negative. During the first week, the white cell count fell to 1.69 ×10⁹/L with neutropenia; antibiotics were stopped. Bone marrow biopsy showed hypocellularity with trilineage hematopoiesis and no granulomas or malignancy. He remained febrile; by the third week, leukocytosis (20.25 ×10⁹/L), ESR 68 mm/h, CRP 9 mg/L, and deranged liver enzymes were noted. Ferritin was 25 499.4 ng/mL. Tests for ANA, RF, ANCA, and viral infections were negative. A whole-body PET scan revealed no inflammatory focus.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 48-year-old man with moderate haemophilia A, transfusion-acquired HIV (on maraviroc/efavirenz/raltegravir; CD4+ 1200 cells/mm3; undetectable viral load) and treated genotype 1a hepatitis C virus infection (completed ledipasvir/sofosbuvir 2 years earlier) was referred for evaluation of new-onset proteinuria. He has portal hypertensive changes with splenomegaly, oesophageal varices and mild thrombocytopenia (106×10^9/L). He reports one year of foamy urine but is otherwise asymptomatic. Physical examination shows palpable splenomegaly without peripheral oedema.  
Laboratory studies reveal:  
• 24-h urine protein 4.35 g; microscopy 24×10^6 red cells  
• Serum creatinine 98 μmol/L; albumin 51 g/L  
• ANA 1:160, homogeneous; ANCA, dsDNA, anti-GBM negative  
• Serum anti-PLA2R and cryoglobulins negative; SPEP without monoclonal bands  
Renal ultrasonography shows normal-sized kidneys, preserved corticomedullary differentiation, bilateral simple cysts, and no hydronephrosis or calculi.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman with a history of ovarian cancer completed six cycles of postoperative chemotherapy with paclitaxel and carboplatin. After her 5th and 6th cycles, she developed myelosuppression and received filgrastim (75 µg/day) for 3 and 4 days, respectively. On the night after her last filgrastim dose, she developed high fever. 

She was prescribed acetaminophen and levofloxacin as an outpatient but remained febrile and was admitted on day 4. She had no cough, chest pain, abdominal pain, rash, or visual symptoms. Her temperature was 38.0 °C, blood pressure 92/50 mmHg, pulse 70 beats/min, and oxygen saturation 96% on room air. The head and neck were without bruit or tenderness, chest and cardiac auscultation were unremarkable, and the abdomen was soft without tenderness. There were no skin lesions or peripheral edema. 

Laboratory studies showed a white blood cell count of 8780/µL, hemoglobin 7.2 g/dL, platelet count 70,000/µL, and C-reactive protein 19.39 mg/dL. Serum ferritin was elevated (883 ng/mL), β-D glucan was normal, and blood cultures were negative. Serologic tests including STS, tuberculosis interferon-γ, and viral panels were negative. Autoimmune serologies were negative (MPO-ANCA <1.0 U/mL, PR3-ANCA <1.0 U/mL, fluorescent ANA negative, IgG4 21 mg/dL). 

Electrocardiography, echocardiography, and chest radiography were unremarkable. Abdominal aortic ultrasonography demonstrated circumferential wall thickening with a surrounding hypoechoic rim. Contrast-enhanced CT showed new soft-tissue stranding around the aortic arch and abdominal aorta compared with pre-chemotherapy images. Gadolinium-enhanced MRI revealed thickening and enhancement of the aortic wall and periaortic tissue. These imaging findings suggested active inflammation of the aorta.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 41-year-old Iranian woman with no known immunodeficiency presented with a 10-month history of slowly enlarging, crusted, painful ulcerative lesions on her lower lip. She had no history of smoking, alcohol, opium use, trauma, cosmetic lip tattooing, or burns. She had intermittent bronchitis treated with a corticosteroid nasal spray. On examination, there was an ill-demarcated, tender, thick-crusted ulcer measuring 3 × 1 cm on the lower lip, which discharged fluid when pressed. There were no signs of onychomycosis or interdigital intertrigo; she was afebrile, with no lymphadenopathy, and the remainder of her exam was unremarkable. HIV antibody, hepatitis B surface antigen, and hepatitis C antibody tests were negative. An upper endoscopy performed for dysphagia was normal. Despite local wound care, topical antifungal and antimicrobial therapies, and empiric immunosuppressive and antibiotic treatments, the ulcer worsened. Clinical differential diagnoses included squamous cell carcinoma, other malignant lesions, ulcerative leishmaniasis, ulcerative mycobacterial infection, erosive lichen planus, immunobullous disorders, and opportunistic fungal infection. Two incisional biopsies of the lip lesion showed parakeratotic hyperkeratosis, severe acanthosis, spongiosis, exocytosis of inflammatory cells in the epidermis, and a mixed dermal infiltrate without evidence of malignant transformation. Direct immunofluorescence was negative for immunobullous diseases. A direct smear for leishmaniasis was negative, and the nitroblue tetrazolium test was normal.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 26-year-old Caucasian man with a history of diabetes mellitus presented with a two-month history of diffuse headache, progressive nausea, and vomiting. He denied fever, chills, or photophobia. He was born in Mexico and had lived in California for eight years. On examination, his temperature was 37 °C; he had mild neck stiffness, but neurologic and ophthalmologic examinations were otherwise normal. Laboratory studies showed a white blood cell count of 6500/mm3 (75% neutrophils, 15% lymphocytes, 8% monocytes), serum glucose of 90 mg/dL, and normal electrolytes and liver–renal function tests. Noncontrast head CT revealed dilated lateral and third ventricles consistent with hydrocephalus. Lumbar puncture showed an opening pressure of 400 mmHg, 330 white blood cells/mm3 (14% neutrophils, 45% lymphocytes, 38% monocytes, 3% eosinophils), protein 84 mg/dL, glucose 43 mg/dL, and 3 red blood cells/mm3. Gram stain and India ink of cerebrospinal fluid were negative, and bacterial, viral, fungal, and mycobacterial cultures were negative. Empiric antimycobacterial therapy was initiated for presumed tuberculous meningitis. MRI of the brain demonstrated hydrocephalus with dilation of the lateral and third ventricles, stenosis of the cerebral aqueduct, absence of meningeal enhancement, and a subtle cystic area in the region of the aqueduct.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 6-year-old boy was brought for evaluation of 2 months of persistent, throbbing right thigh pain and progressive swelling. The pain worsened at night and during inactivity, and he had difficulty walking for weeks. Parents noted generalized lethargy and low-grade fever for 2 weeks before presentation. There was no history of trauma or family history of cancer. Dietary history revealed minimal fruit and vegetable intake. On examination, he appeared irritable and malnourished (weight <1st percentile). There were hemorrhagic findings on the gingiva and labia. Both hip and knee joints were held in a semi-flexed position, with pain on attempted extension. The right thigh was edematous but not erythematous; a firm, tender mass extended across the mid-thigh. Laboratory studies showed WBC 11,740/µL (83.5% neutrophils), hemoglobin 4.8 g/dL, hematocrit 16.3%, normal MCV, MCH, and MCHC, and platelets 751,000/µL. Coagulation studies, metabolic panel, and electrolytes were normal. Given the clinical presentation of a painful thigh mass, the orthopedic team considered a primary bone neoplasm. A radiographic assessment of the right femur and a fine-needle aspiration biopsy (FNAB) were requisitioned to substantiate this.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman with a history of recurrent patellar dislocations and progressive right anterior knee pain underwent patellofemoral arthroplasty with concomitant medial patellofemoral ligament reconstruction. The surgery was uneventful. Four weeks postoperatively, she noted an erythematous, blanchable rash around the arthrotomy incision, associated with warmth, swelling, and stiffness limiting range of motion to 0–30°. She was afebrile, and vital signs were normal. A 7-day course of trimethoprim–sulfamethoxazole was initiated for presumed skin and soft-tissue infection. Two days later, she returned with increased pain, persistent rash with mild urticaria and warmth, tenderness along the joint line, and a trace effusion; the surgical incision sites were well healed. She was referred to the emergency department, where she received intravenous vancomycin and diphenhydramine. Laboratory tests showed normal inflammatory markers, and MRI of the knee showed no evidence of infection; the rash and swelling subjectively improved.  

Over postoperative weeks 7 through 21, she had recurrent episodes of knee warmth, swelling, a reticular rash over the limb, and throbbing pain rated up to 10/10 with activity, significantly impairing daily activities. At week 14, she presented to the emergency department with fatigue and headache; workup for infection and thromboembolism was negative. Outpatient allergy evaluation considered implant allergy, mastocytosis, and hereditary angioedema; patch testing was negative, and serum C4 complement and tryptase levels were within normal limits. Vascular surgery evaluation for May–Thurner syndrome and popliteal entrapment included MRI angiography and abdominopelvic CT with contrast, which demonstrated patent vasculature. On further questioning, she reported intermittent bloating, fatigue, and headaches. Allergy specialists recommended maintenance of a rigorous food diary before pursuing further testing.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 40‐year‐old man with a history of juvenile polyposis syndrome (post–partial colectomy) and alcohol use disorder in remission presented in June 2018 with sudden‐onset right calf pain, swelling, and erythema. Venous ultrasound showed an acute, occlusive thrombus in the right popliteal, tibial, and peroneal veins. He was started on rivaroxaban. One week later, he returned with 3 days of melena. Laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 5.3 g/dL and MCV 55.7 fL; he received 3 units of packed red blood cells. Anticoagulation was held, and abdominal CT showed transmural thickening of the gastric antral and pyloric walls protruding into the duodenal bulb, multifocal bilateral segmental and subsegmental pulmonary emboli, and a nonocclusive thrombus from the right renal vein into the suprarenal IVC. An IVC filter was placed. Upper endoscopy demonstrated diffusely irregular, raised gastric mucosa throughout the gastric body, resembling a soft carpeted mass. Biopsy revealed prominent foveolar hyperplasia, focal granulation tissue, ulceration, reactive glandular changes, and chronic active inflammation, with no evidence of dysplasia or malignancy. Immunostaining was negative for Helicobacter pylori. A probable diagnosis of Ménétrier disease was established, and he was discharged on high‐dose oral pantoprazole.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

An 11‐week‐old intact male mixed‐breed dog was referred for severe anemia and suspected babesiosis. Three weeks earlier, he had been transported from Texas. One week after transport, he developed acute lethargy and fever (40.4°C). In‐house CBC showed severe microcytic, normochromic, non‐regenerative anemia (HCT 0.147 L/L; RI 0.373–0.617 L/L) and severe thrombocytopenia (23 × 10^9/L; RI 148–484 × 10^9/L). He received 15 ml/kg pRBC over 4 h and was discharged on omeprazole, sucralfate, and amoxicillin/clavulanate. The next day, PCV was 23%. Eight days later, he was lethargic with PCV 15%; a blood smear was suspicious for Babesia, and clindamycin was started. When he became too weak to stand, he was referred.

On presentation, the dog was nonambulatory, pale, mildly icteric, and splenomegaly was noted. Weight was 5.75 kg; temperature 38.2°C; heart rate 150 beats/min; respiratory rate 60 breaths/min. Point-of-care testing showed PCV 14%, total protein 5.4 g/dL, and compensated metabolic acidosis with lactate 5.2 mmol/L. Ultrasound revealed volume contraction, no effusions or pulmonary B-lines, and subjective splenomegaly. He was blood‐typed (DEA 1.1 positive), cross-matched, and transfused with 14 ml/kg pRBCs. Cytologic evaluation demonstrated intra-erythrocytic piroplasms consistent with Babesia canis. He was treated with IM imidocarb and IV fluids.

After the first transfusion, PCV remained 13% and lactate rose to 8.2 mmol/L. He received diphenhydramine for suspected transfusion reaction and an additional 13 ml/kg pRBCs. Fourteen hours after admission, his respiratory rate exceeded 100 breaths/min; he was placed in an oxygen chamber, then developed diarrhea and hypersalivation treated with atropine. Tachypnea worsened further, necessitating emergent endotracheal intubation. During intubation, a large volume of serosanguineous/icteric fluid was produced through the endotracheal tube. Initial manual ventilation with 100% FiO₂ yielded SpO₂ values of 88%–92%, after which he was transitioned to mechanical ventilation.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 38-year-old woman presented with a 2-year history of progressive abdominal distension. She noted her abdomen, initially tennis-ball size, gradually increased to soccer-ball size. She had no prior medical illnesses, was a lifelong non-smoker without secondhand-smoke exposure, and had no occupational carcinogen exposure. She was nulliparous with regular 28-day menstrual cycles. 

On examination, there was a huge, immobile suprapubic mass equivalent to a 30-week pregnancy. Chest examination revealed reduced air entry and vocal resonance with stony dullness over the left lower lung zone. Breast examination was normal, with no palpable masses; there was no lymphadenopathy and no other abnormalities on systemic review.

Transabdominal ultrasonography showed a 20 × 20 cm solid-cystic ovarian mass. Contrast-enhanced CT of the thorax, abdomen, and pelvis revealed a large solid-cystic pelvic mass filling most of the lower abdomen, bilateral ovarian cysts, left hydrosalpinx, a solid irregular lesion in the left upper lung lobe, ipsilateral pleural effusion, and multiple small bilateral lung nodules.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 65-year-old woman was admitted for evaluation of progressive forgetfulness that had been present for five years. She reported difficulty preparing meals, repetitive conversations, and repeated purchasing of identical items. Neuropsychological testing showed an MMSE score of 19/30, an HDS-R score of 20/30, and a Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale–Revised total IQ of 87 (verbal IQ 83, performance IQ 92).

Her past medical history included uterine myoma at age 42 and cerebral aneurysm clipping at age 58. She graduated from junior high school and had worked in agriculture, construction, and food service. There was a family history of brain calcification: one brother had mental retardation with documented calcifications and another brother had alcoholism; her parents were asymptomatic.

On examination, there were no pyramidal or extrapyramidal signs, and the Albright sign was negative. Laboratory studies showed normal levels of thyroid hormones, parathyroid hormone, serum calcium, serum phosphate, and cerebrospinal fluid constituents. A treponemal hemagglutination assay was negative.

Noncontrast cranial CT revealed symmetric calcifications in the globus pallidus, pulvinar thalami, right frontal subcortical white matter, and the occipital cortex–white matter junction. T1-weighted MRI showed small patchy hyperintensities in the globus pallidus and pulvinar thalami, and T2-weighted MRI demonstrated corresponding hypointensities in the globus pallidus. Brain perfusion SPECT demonstrated decreased perfusion in the bilateral basal ganglia, thalamus, and right frontal lobe.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?
