Blood cultures were negative, and tests for ANA, ANCA, anti-GBM, dsDNA, EBV, and CMV were unremarkable.

Chest radiography demonstrated multifocal pneumonia. CT of the chest revealed diffuse emphysematous changes, ground-glass infiltrates, and a 1.6-cm left upper lobe nodule. Abdominal ultrasound showed hepatomegaly and splenomegaly. Transesophageal echocardiography identified a 1.5×0.9-cm mobile echodensity on the anterior mitral leaflet.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

options.C: Drug rash with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms (DRESS)

question:
A 7-year-old boy was referred for evaluation of intermittent exotropia in his left eye. His medical history was notable for mild supravalvular pulmonary stenosis discovered on echocardiography during a routine exam. Best-corrected visual acuity was 20/100 in the right eye and 3/200 in the left eye. Intraocular pressures were normal in both eyes, and anterior segment examination was unremarkable. Fundus examination of the right eye revealed subretinal hard exudates at the superotemporal arcade with minimal foveal involvement and clinically visible aneurysms in the midperiphery. The left eye showed a subtotal exudative retinal detachment with marked vascular telangiectasia superotemporally. Fluorescein angiography of the right eye highlighted subtle beading of a major arterial trunk, mid-peripheral and peripheral aneurysms, and capillary dropout in all quadrants. Fluorescein angiography of the left eye demonstrated peripheral aneurysms and areas of non-perfusion.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 38-year-old man presented with a 2-year history of a nonpruritic rash and 1 year of bilateral knee pain. The rash began as small red macules on the right thigh and progressed to brownish-red papules on both thighs, neck, shoulders, back, chest, abdomen, and arms. Treatments for presumed eczema had no effect. Over the subsequent year he developed firm, smooth, brownish-red nodules (2–8 mm) on the dorsal hands and “gooseneck” deformities of the fingers, with difficulty making a fist. He also reported worsening knee pain, swelling, and limited squatting but denied fever, myalgia, oral ulcers, or weight loss.

On examination, he had densely distributed brown-red maculopapular lesions behind the ears, neck, shoulders, chest, back, waist, abdomen, arms, and inner thighs. Multiple 2–8 mm hard, nontender nodules were present on the dorsal hands, with limited interphalangeal motion and flexion deformities of digits 2–5. Thumb interphalangeal joints were swollen bilaterally. Knee joints were swollen and tender with a reduced range of motion.

Laboratory studies showed normal liver function, biochemistry, lipids, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and tumor markers. ANA, rheumatoid factor, anti-CCP, ANCA, anti-MPO, and anti-PR3 were negative. Platelet and monocyte counts were normal. Immunoglobulin G and C3 were within reference ranges.

Radiographs of the hands revealed narrowing of interphalangeal and intercarpal joint spaces, reduced bone density, and cystic translucent areas. Left radial–carpal joint space was narrowed. Knee MRI demonstrated degeneration of the menisci, osteochondral injury of the patellofemoral surface, possible synovitis, and joint effusion. Chest CT showed bilateral interstitial inflammation, interlobular septal nodules, fibrous foci in the lower lobes, and cystic foci in the right subscapularis muscle.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 64-year-old woman with no previous medical history was referred for suspected pulmonary hypertension after 18 months of progressive exertional dyspnea and bilateral lower extremity edema. On examination, she had jugular venous distension, lower extremity edema, and an accentuated pulmonic component of the second heart sound. Chest radiography showed decreased right pulmonary volume, a shrunken right hilum, and left lung hyperinflation. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated right ventricular enlargement with impaired function. Lung perfusion scintigraphy revealed absent perfusion of the right lung and normal perfusion of the left lung. Right heart catheterization showed a mean right atrial pressure of 14 mm Hg; right ventricular pressure of 119/22 mm Hg; pulmonary artery pressure of 130/42 mm Hg (mean, 74 mm Hg); pulmonary artery wedge pressure of 12 mm Hg; pulmonary vascular resistance of 35.4 Wood units; and a cardiac index of 1.3 L/min/m². Pulmonary angiography revealed absence of the right pulmonary artery and diffuse dilation of the left pulmonary artery without pouch defects, intimal irregularities, or abrupt narrowing. Four-dimensional computed tomography confirmed absence of the right pulmonary artery from the main trunk in the pulmonary artery phase and demonstrated collateral flow from the right lateral thoracic and right inferior phrenic arteries into the underdeveloped right lung in the aortic phase.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

options.B: Unilateral absence of a pulmonary artery

question:
A 45-year-old woman presented with a 1-month history of fever and a 7-day history of cough, dyspnea, and a painful swelling over the right side of her neck. She had a 10 pack-year smoking history and no significant past medical or family history. On examination, multiple firm, palpable right cervical lymph nodes were noted, and prominent dilated veins were visible on the right anterior chest wall. No other abnormalities were detected on systemic examination.

Laboratory studies showed a leukocyte count of 13,000/mm3 with 510 eosinophils/mm3, an IgE level of 779 IU/mL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 120 mm/hr, and a positive C-reactive protein. Tests for HIV, hepatitis B surface antigen, rheumatoid factor, antinuclear antibodies, anti–double-stranded DNA, antiphospholipid antibodies, lupus anticoagulant, anti-cardiolipin antibodies, protein C and S, antithrombin III, factor V Leiden, and homocysteine were all within normal limits or negative. Two-dimensional echocardiography revealed a mild pericardial effusion. Abdominal ultrasonography was unremarkable. Sputum studies, including acid-fast bacilli smear, GeneXpert for Mycobacterium tuberculosis, Gram stain, KOH mount, and cultures for pyogenic bacteria, mycobacteria, and fungi, were negative.

Neck ultrasonography demonstrated multiple necrotic right cervical lymph nodes and thrombosis of the entire right internal jugular vein. A chest radiograph revealed right middle lobe collapse and consolidation. A CT venogram of the neck and chest showed multiple heterogeneous lymph nodes in the cervical, pretracheal, and mediastinal regions; thrombus in the right internal jugular, subclavian, brachiocephalic veins, and superior vena cava; compression of the right internal jugular vein by an enlarged lymph node; extensive collateral venous channels; a small right pleural effusion; and mild pericardial effusion.

Fine-needle aspiration cytology of a right cervical lymph node yielded few lymphocytes and histiocytes, with no granulomas or malignant cells. Staining and GeneXpert for Mycobacterium tuberculosis and cultures were negative.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

An 18-year-old woman presented with a 4-year history of severe, global headaches (10/10) that worsened over the past 2 years. Eighteen months ago, she developed polydipsia of approximately 15 L per day with matching polyuria, waking 10 times per night to urinate. She also reported secondary amenorrhea for 18 months, a weight loss of 10 kg over the past year, and intermittent epigastric discomfort. She denied trauma, surgery, radiation exposure, fever, cough, or osmotic diuretics. Menarche occurred at age 13. She had no personal or family history of diabetes mellitus or other endocrine disorders. Medications included occasional antacids and analgesics.

On examination, vital signs were normal; BMI was 16 kg/m2. Axillary and pubic hair were normal, and the remainder of the exam was unremarkable.

Initial labs: urine specific gravity 1.005; random urine osmolality 150 mOsm/kg. After overnight water deprivation, serum osmolality rose to 298 mOsm/kg while urine osmolality remained low at 210 mOsm/kg; urine output was 3 L over 8 h and urine specific gravity 1.006. Desmopressin challenge by IV was unavailable; however, on low-dose oral desmopressin, her urine specific gravity normalized to 1.020 and urine osmolality to 600 mOsm/kg.

Additional labs: βhCG 1.2 mIU/mL, AFP 7 ng/mL, IgG4 28 mg/dL, soluble IL-2 receptor 203 U/mL; anterior pituitary hormones (TSH, T3, T4, prolactin, cortisol) were within normal limits.

Brain MRI showed partial filling of the pituitary fossa by CSF with a flattened pituitary gland measuring 3.68 mm, consistent with a partial empty sella. There was no evidence of intracranial hypertension. Pelvic ultrasound was normal.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 72-year-old man presented for routine follow-up of sigmoid colon cancer. Two years earlier, he had undergone laparoscopic sigmoidectomy for a pStage I, moderately differentiated tubular adenocarcinoma with submucosal and venous invasion. His father died of gastric cancer. 

He was asymptomatic, with no abdominal pain, palpable masses, or lymphadenopathy on examination. Laboratory studies showed:
- Lactate dehydrogenase 239 U/L (elevated)
- Creatinine 1.14 mg/dL (elevated)
- Carbohydrate antigen 19-9 41 U/mL (elevated)
- Other serum biochemistry and tumor markers within normal limits
- Urine cytology: no atypical cells (class II)

Enhanced abdominal CT demonstrated a 30 × 13.7 mm soft-tissue mass involving the left ureter at the level of the common iliac bifurcation; the lesion had grown from 16.7 × 10.3 mm over 17 months. There was no hydronephrosis or lymphadenopathy. FDG-PET–CT showed focal uptake in the same region (SUVmax 8.93) and no other abnormal accumulations. Ureterography and colonoscopy were unremarkable.

Given the slow growth and significant FDG uptake, concern arose for lymph node recurrence or peritoneal seeding of colon cancer, and a decision was made to proceed with laparoscopic excisional biopsy of the mass.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 76-year-old man presented with progressive confusion and long-standing blindness of the left eye. His medical history included type II diabetes mellitus and hypercholesterolemia; he had no urological complaints. On examination, he was afebrile, hemodynamically stable, and neurologically nonfocal except for left-eye amaurosis. 

An axial contrast-enhanced brain CT demonstrated an irregular infiltrative hyperdense mass in the left temporal region with necrotic components, extensive osseous destruction, and extension into the orbit, sinuses, and adjacent musculature; there was left-sided exophthalmos, vasogenic edema, and a mild midline shift. T2-weighted MRI confirmed a hyperintense lesion in the left temporal lobe with osseous involvement. 

Serum PSA was 194 ng/mL.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old African woman, with three prior full-term pregnancies and recent immigration from East Africa, presented with a large, tender left axillary mass. She first noticed a small, nontender lump 3 years earlier—6 months after placement of a left-arm etonogestrel implant, 8 months after her most recent delivery, and 2 years after arriving in the United States. The mass grew slowly without pain, drainage, skin changes, or neurologic symptoms, and did not interfere with breastfeeding. Six months before presentation, the mass enlarged rapidly and became tender, limiting full adduction of the left arm. Her implant had been replaced 2 months before this acceleration. She denied breast symptoms, trauma, infection, systemic complaints, or other exposures; her only medication was the implant device.

On exam, there was a softball-sized, mobile, nonreducible mass in the left axillary fossa with overlying skin striations; no regional lymphadenopathy; intact vascular, sensory, and motor function; and mild discomfort with arm adduction. Vital signs and the remainder of the examination were unremarkable.

Prior MRI of the left axilla (with and without contrast) demonstrated a 16 × 11 × 11 cm multiloculated subcutaneous cystic lesion displacing but not invading adjacent structures, without intrathoracic communication or lymphadenopathy. MR lymphangiogram—after subcutaneous contrast injection into the interdigital web spaces—showed no lymphatic communication with the mass, although lymphatic drainage led to nodes over the lesion. Echinococcal IgG antibody testing was equivocal. Initial laboratory studies were otherwise unremarkable, without evidence of systemic infection.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 5-year-old boy was brought with a 40-day history of pain, swelling, and redness in both legs. He had been treated 30 days earlier elsewhere with a 10-day course of oral antibiotics for fever. He had no history of chronic illness or trauma. On examination, both legs showed swelling, erythema, warmth, and purulent discharge from the proximal tibiae. Vital signs were within normal limits except for mild tachycardia. Laboratory studies revealed a white-cell count of 15,640/mm3, C-reactive protein 5.29 μg/mL, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate 63 mm/h. Serologic tests for Brucella and Salmonella and purified protein derivative tests for tuberculosis, previously negative twice, were again negative; chest radiograph was normal. Radiographs of both tibiae showed cortical destruction and periosteal reaction at the proximal tibiae and distal left tibia. Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrated bone-marrow edema throughout both tibiae, purulent debris within the medullary canals and metaphyses, and adjacent pyomyositis. Radionuclide bone scanning revealed increased technetium uptake in the metaphyses of both tibiae.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 44-year-old man with end-stage kidney disease secondary to congenital oligomeganephronia underwent a living-donor kidney transplant 20 years earlier. Twenty-nine days before presentation, his maintenance immunosuppression (cyclosporine and prednisone) was stopped after an 8-cm clear cell carcinoma was diagnosed in the transplant. Six days before presentation, he received his first doses of nivolumab and ipilimumab.  

He presented to the emergency department with a 1-day history of fever. Vital signs showed a heart rate of 146 beats per minute; examination revealed tenderness over the transplant site, otherwise unremarkable.  

Laboratory studies demonstrated a serum creatinine concentration of 275 μmol/L (baseline 150–180 μmol/L), urinalysis with 3.0 g/L protein and a small amount of blood, negative urine and blood cultures, and no laboratory evidence of tumor lysis syndrome. Chest radiograph was unremarkable.  

Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed minimal interval increase in the size of the transplant mass and transplant renal vein findings consistent with tumor thrombus. Doppler ultrasound confirmed a near-occlusive thrombus in the transplant renal vein.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 51-year-old Caucasian woman with no significant medical history was admitted to a psychiatric unit for new‐onset auditory hallucinations and paranoid delusions, leading to a diagnosis of paranoid schizophrenia. Trials of olanzapine and haloperidol yielded no symptom remission and caused sedation and extrapyramidal side effects, respectively. After two ineffective antipsychotics over adequate durations, clozapine was initiated and titrated to 200 mg daily over 14 days. Baseline ECG and blood tests were normal aside from mild lipid elevations. 

During the first two weeks of clozapine therapy, she developed known side effects including hypersalivation, sedation, constipation, lethargy, and persistent tachycardia (100–120 bpm). She reported a dry cough but three consecutive COVID-19 PCR tests were negative. ECGs showed only sinus tachycardia, and troponin and CRP remained within normal limits, leading to no evidence of myocarditis. 

On day 15, monitoring labs revealed an increase in neutrophils (8.48 × 10^9/L), monocytes, and CRP (23 mg/L). That night, she developed coryzal symptoms, fever (37.8 °C), and left‐sided pleuritic chest pain. Vital signs were: HR 136 bpm, RR 18/min, BP 132/78 mm Hg, SpO2 95% on room air. Examination of chest, heart, abdomen, and calves was normal. Repeat ECGs remained unchanged. CRP rose to 81 mg/L; troponin was 5 ng/L. Urinalysis was unremarkable. As her symptoms resolved spontaneously, she was started on amoxicillin for a presumed lower respiratory tract infection.

On day 18, she acutely deteriorated with HR 150 bpm, RR 24/min, BP 92/46 mm Hg, SpO2 97% on room air, and temperature 38.7 °C. Arterial blood gas showed pO2 6.0 kPa and pCO2 4.2 kPa. Labs demonstrated lymphopenia (0.77 × 10^9/L), neutrophilia (8.85 × 10^9/L), CRP 197 mg/L, and D-dimer 506 mg/L; coagulation studies were normal. Chest radiograph showed no airspace disease. A third COVID-19 swab was negative. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed normal ventricular size and function without pericardial or valvular abnormalities.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 19-year-old gravida 2, para 1 woman, with no significant medical or surgical history and a nonconsanguineous marriage, attended routine prenatal visits. 
20-week ultrasound showed hyperechogenic lungs, ascites, a normal right kidney, a three-vessel umbilical cord, and a normal sucking reflex. 
At 25 weeks, ultrasound demonstrated relative shortening of the upper and lower limbs, nuchal edema, bilateral syndactyly of both hands and feet, hydrops fetalis, lung enlargement, and cardiac compression; estimated fetal weight was 4000 g. 
At 39 weeks’ gestation, the mother underwent cesarean delivery for cephalopelvic disproportion; the fetus was stillborn, weighing 3600 g, with respiratory insufficiency.

Gross examination of the stillborn revealed bilateral anophthalmia, pseudo-hypertelorism, low-set ears, flat nasal bridge, bilateral cutaneous syndactyly of the hands and feet, generalized edema, large-volume ascites, and ambiguous external genitalia.

Autopsy findings:
- Airways were patent; lungs were enlarged; no significant cardiac anomalies.
- Right renal agenesis; on the left, a gonad and Müllerian structures were noted.
- Liver capsule was congested.

Microscopic examination:
- Lung: dilated pleural lymphatic vessels, interstitial fibroblast hypertrophy, and vascular wall thickening.
- Spleen: congestion of red pulp with hemosiderin and immature white pulp.
- Liver: cholestasis with biliary atresia, biliary plugs, portal-space fibrosis, diffuse congestion, and chronic lymphocytic infiltrates.
- Kidney: increased subcapsular nephrogenic structures, reduced glomerular subcapsular spaces, and cuboidal change of Bowman's capsule parietal epithelium.
- Gonad/Müllerian derivative: clusters of crowded immature primordial follicles and epithelium resembling Fallopian tube.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 40-year-old woman was evaluated for a Raynaud-like disorder when a chest radiograph revealed a peripheral 8-cm lesion involving the third right rib with cortical expansion; a concurrent CT scan confirmed this finding. She reported oligomenorrhea since age 26 but was otherwise asymptomatic. Physical examination was unremarkable. 

Further evaluation included 99mTc bone scintigraphy, which showed focal uptake in the rib lesion and the medial condyle of the right femur. MRI of the chest demonstrated an aneurysmal cystic bone lesion at the same rib site. A percutaneous biopsy of the rib lesion revealed numerous osteoclast-like multinucleated giant cells without necrosis, mitoses, or histologic features of malignancy, a picture compatible with a giant cell tumor of bone.

Six months later, the lesion was excised with local curettage, including resection of the right pectoralis major and portions of the II, III, and IV ribs. Grossly, the specimen was a well-circumscribed, tan, fleshy mass measuring 6.0 × 4.5 × 2.2 cm, confluent with two rib segments. 

Microscopic examination showed:
- A diffuse distribution of multinucleated giant cells  
- A background of spindle-shaped mononuclear stromal cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and oval, non-atypical nuclei  
- No necrosis, hemorrhage, or atypical mitoses (mitotic index 6 per 10 HPF)

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 69-year-old man with a 7-year history of refractory anemia–type myelodysplastic syndrome presented in 2008 with macrocytic anemia (hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, MCV 114 fL), a normal white-cell count, normal platelets, and a low reticulocyte count; B12, folate, TSH, and liver function tests were normal, and bone marrow biopsy showed refractory anemia with blasts <5%. By 2011, his hemoglobin fell to 7.0 g/dL and platelets to 50,000/µL despite normal vitamin and thyroid studies; repeat marrow showed hypercellularity with dysplasia, blasts <5%, and a normal karyotype. He required monthly red-cell transfusions to maintain a hemoglobin >8 g/dL and occasional platelet transfusions. 

In October 2013, he noted painless gross hematuria. His hemoglobin was 7.5 g/dL, platelets 13,000/µL, and coagulation studies were normal. CT urography revealed a 16 mm lobulated lesion near the left ureteral orifice. With transfusional support, cystoscopy demonstrated five superficial bladder tumors; transurethral resection showed low-grade papillary urothelial carcinoma without invasion. After resection, transfusion requirements decreased but did not resolve. 

Beginning March 2015, he received intravesical BCG (weekly ×6, biweekly ×4, then monthly ×12). While receiving BCG, his hemoglobin rose to 13.7 g/dL, platelets to 317,000/µL, and he became transfusion independent.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old man presented with a 5-month history of right lumbar pain, intermittent hematuria, and weight loss. He is a chronic smoker (11 pack-years) with an otherwise unremarkable medical history. On examination, there was a palpable right flank mass appreciated bimanually. Routine laboratory studies were normal. Contrast-enhanced abdominal CT revealed a 25 × 11 × 8 cm heterogeneous mass replacing the right kidney and extending into the right renal vein lumen, with compression of the inferior vena cava; no other intra-abdominal abnormalities were noted. Chest CT showed multiple nodular lesions suggestive of metastatic disease. The patient underwent a right radical nephrectomy with resection of the thrombus in the right renal vein.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 25-year-old woman was referred after new-onset hypertension (systolic 180–225 mmHg, diastolic 110–130 mmHg) was noted during hospitalization for repair of a tibia-fibula fracture. She was asymptomatic with blood pressure refractory to hydrochlorothiazide and amlodipine. Her body mass index was 23 kg/m2, with no features of endocrine hypertension. Pulses were equal; no bruits were heard. Serum creatinine was 67 µmol/L.

Renal ultrasound showed the left kidney measured 72 mm (poor perfusion), and the right kidney measured 117 mm (resistive index 60%). CTA demonstrated the left renal artery encased in fibrous tissue with near-complete occlusion from 8 mm beyond the ostium to the hilum, and a 50% stenosis of the right renal artery 17 mm from its ostium; the remainder of the aorta and its branches appeared normal. A diagnosis of fibromuscular dysplasia was made.

Over the next months, her serum creatinine rose to 87 µmol/L with resistant hypertension on multiple agents. In January 2016, she developed malignant hypertension, flash pulmonary edema, and a serum creatinine of 1807 µmol/L despite a urine output of 700–1000 mL/day. Repeat ultrasound showed the right kidney had decreased to 92 mm with a resistive index of 70%. A diuretic renogram revealed 80% function in the right kidney and 20% in the left, with poor global function. Hemodialysis was initiated, and creatinine fell to 650 µmol/L; dialysis was then stopped. Three weeks later, malignant hypertension and pulmonary edema recurred, creatinine rose to 1509 µmol/L, and hemodialysis was resumed. Throughout, urine output remained at ~1000 mL/day.

Repeat CTA showed extensive collateral blood supply to the right kidney. A biopsy of the right kidney revealed normal-appearing, viable tissue.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

options.A: Renal artery stenosis due to atherosclerosis

question:
A 24-year-old Hispanic man was brought to the emergency department after a witnessed tonic–clonic seizure with urinary incontinence, which resolved after intravenous lorazepam. He had a remote history of seizures but was not on antiepileptic medications. On arrival he was postictal, afebrile, tachycardic (124 bpm), and had a blood pressure of 144/66 mmHg. Initial laboratory studies showed a lactate level of 17 mmol/L that normalized rapidly. A routine urine toxicology screen was positive for cannabinoids and benzodiazepines and negative for cocaine, phencyclidine, opiates, and amphetamines.

On hospital day 1, he developed abdominal pain, diarrhea, vomiting, and progressive agitation with confusion. His serum lipase was normal, but the serum creatinine rose from 1.4 mg/dL on admission to 4.3 mg/dL and continued to increase. Several hours later, he became hypoxic with an arterial oxygen saturation of 70%, febrile to 38.9°C, tachycardic at 140 bpm, and hypertensive at 163/104 mmHg. He failed a trial of noninvasive ventilation and was intubated. A chest radiograph showed bilateral alveolar infiltrates. Despite high–fractional inspired oxygen, his oxygenation improved only with high positive end-expiratory pressure.

Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics were started. Bronchoscopy revealed normal airways and clear secretions; bronchoalveolar lavage fluid was obtained. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a left ventricular ejection fraction of 30% with global wall-motion abnormalities, suggesting cardiac dysfunction and fluid overload as contributors to his respiratory failure. The causes of his acute kidney injury and congestive heart failure remained unclear. He underwent three sessions of hemodialysis, after which his oxygenation, urine output, and creatinine level improved. He was extubated on hospital day 8.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman presented with abnormal vaginal bleeding. Hysteroscopy identified and resected a 2 cm polypoid mass in the right lateral uterine wall. Histologic examination of the biopsy showed mucinous adenocarcinoma with glandular and solid architecture, intra- and extra-glandular necrosis, neoplastic cells with pale eosinophilic to clear cytoplasm, distinct cell borders, variable intracytoplasmic mucin, mild to moderate nuclear atypia, and a high mitotic rate with atypical figures. Immunohistochemistry revealed diffuse positivity for CK20, CDX2, and p16; focal positivity for CK7 and PAX8; negative estrogen and progesterone receptors; a wild-type p53 pattern; focal (< 10%) chromogranin A and synaptophysin positivity; and intact MLH1, PMS2, MSH2, and MSH6 expression. A diagnosis of high-grade adenocarcinoma with mucinous features, highly suspicious for metastatic origin, was initially rendered. Subsequent ultrasound demonstrated only a superficial minimal residual endometrial lesion without myometrial or adnexal involvement. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy, colonoscopy, PET, and MRI were all unremarkable.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presented to the emergency department 10 days after receiving a third dose of the Pfizer–BioNTech COVID-19 vaccine with pain in both eyes, numbness in both legs, difficulty walking, and dysuria. She had no prior similar episodes or cognitive impairment. PCR tests were negative for SARS-CoV-2, adenovirus, Epstein–Barr virus, cytomegalovirus, and HIV. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis showed elevated total protein at 101.3 mg/dL but was otherwise unremarkable. A T2-weighted spinal MRI revealed an intramedullary hyperintense lesion extending from T2 to T12. On hospital day 2, muscle strength in both lower extremities deteriorated to the point that no voluntary movements were observed. Brain MRI demonstrated hyperintense lesions in the optic chiasm and optic nerve, and visual evoked potentials showed central conduction delay.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old man with idiopathic hypertension presented with a 1-month history of right aural fullness and subjective hearing loss. Examination and audiometry confirmed a conductive hearing loss consistent with otitis media with effusion; on placement of a ventilation tube, the effusion was thick and viscid with a red–yellow hue. CT of the temporal bones showed partial opacification of both the middle ear and mastoid cavities; incidental findings suggested chronic rhinosinusitis. Nasal endoscopy was unremarkable and his nasal symptoms were mild. Despite spontaneous extrusion of the first grommet and placement of a larger tube, otorrhea persisted; a swab was negative and a biopsy of the postnasal space showed nonspecific inflammation. MRI with gadolinium demonstrated a large T2-hyperintense, T1-hypointense effusion in the right middle ear and mastoid with patchy mucosal enhancement extending along the Eustachian tube into the nasopharynx and sinonasal cavity. The extensive mucosal inflammation, persistent effusion, and lack of clear infectious or atopic cause raised suspicion for an autoimmune process. Blood tests showed normal RF, ANCA, ANA, serum IgE, CCP, renal and liver function, and ESR; ACE was elevated, as was total IgG (16.21 g/L; normal 6.2–14.4 g/L). A chest radiograph and QuantiFERON Gold test were unremarkable, and repeat ACE testing normalized.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

An 11-year-old spayed female Golden Retriever was referred for evaluation of an incidentally detected hepatic mass. Five days earlier, she had pollakiuria and mild abdominal discomfort; abdominal ultrasound then showed a 5.2 × 5.0 cm round hypoechoic mass in the left lateral liver lobe and a smaller hypoechoic lesion near the right middle lobe. Urinalysis was notable only for struvite crystals and mild proteinuria; empirical amoxicillin resolved her urinary signs. CBC and serum chemistry were within reference limits. 

Her only chronic issues were bilateral elbow osteoarthritis and cruciate ligament injuries, for which she was receiving tramadol, amantadine, meloxicam, and supplements. On presentation, she was bright, alert, obese (BCS 8/9), with a left forelimb lameness. Abdominal palpation and peripheral lymph nodes were unremarkable. Repeat abdominal ultrasound confirmed a 4.5 × 5.7 cm heterogeneous, well-vascularized hypoechoic mass in the left lateral liver lobe. Thoracic radiographs were normal.

Ultrasound‐guided fine‐needle aspiration of the hepatic mass yielded a mixed lymphoid population: predominantly intermediate‐sized lymphocytes (12–15 μm, indented nuclei, coarse chromatin, pale blue cytoplasm) and fewer small lymphocytes (8–12 μm, condensed chromatin). There was a lymphoglandular background, few hepatocyte clusters with mild hydropic change, and no expanded lymphocytes in adjacent parenchyma. The cytology was most consistent with lymphoma, and marked inflammation was deemed unlikely.

Flow cytometry on the aspirate demonstrated predominantly small CD3+ T cells (mixed CD4+ and CD8+), about 5% small CD21+ B cells, and 15% CD45+ MHC class II+ leukocytes lacking lineage markers. PCR for antigen receptor rearrangements (PARR) revealed a reproducible clonal immunoglobulin heavy‐chain rearrangement and polyclonal T‐cell receptor rearrangements.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 35-year-old man in a drug rehabilitation center presented with a 4-month history of headaches and one seizure the day before admission. He reported a 2-month history of gradual, painless, progressive visual loss that acutely worsened in the days before hospitalization. He had no history of immunodeficiency, autoimmune disease, or malignancy and denied ongoing substance abuse. On examination, he was afebrile, normotensive, and fully conscious. Neurologic assessment showed bilateral visual acuity reduced to light perception on the right and hand motion on the left, with mild to moderate bilateral optic disc swelling. Pupils were mid-size, isochoric, and reactive to light.

Noncontrast head CT revealed bilateral temporal-lobe hypodensity with loss of gray–white differentiation extending into the occipital lobes and centrum semiovale, with mild left uncal herniation but no mass lesion or hemorrhage. Empiric IV aciclovir was started for presumed herpes simplex encephalitis, and corticosteroids with mannitol were administered for raised intracranial pressure. Lumbar puncture showed clear cerebrospinal fluid, an opening pressure of 38 cm H₂O, mild glucose elevation, and otherwise normal cell counts and chemistry. CSF PCR for HSV and blood and urine toxin assays, as well as HIV and hepatitis B and C serologies, were negative.

Brain MRI showed bilateral T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the temporal lobes, insular regions (worse on the left), with extension into the corpus callosum, centrum semiovale, left basal ganglia, left thalamus, optic chiasm, and periventricular occipital white matter. The lesions were mildly expanded, with no diffusion restriction on DWI, no enhancement on postcontrast images, and no hemorrhagic foci on SWI.

Ophthalmologic testing with optical coherence tomography revealed increased retinal nerve fiber–layer thickness consistent with disc swelling, and visual evoked potentials showed absent bilateral waveforms.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man with no significant medical history presented with 4 hours of severe cramping abdominal pain in the periumbilical and right iliac regions, associated with dry heaving, nausea, and three episodes of non-bilious, non-bloody vomiting. He reported a three-month history of milder, intermittent right-sided abdominal discomfort attributed to indigestion. He denied fever, changes in bowel habits, hematochezia, flatus difficulty, weight loss, or urinary symptoms.

On examination, he was hemodynamically stable and afebrile. Abdominal examination revealed a soft, non-distended abdomen with tenderness on deep palpation in the periumbilical and right iliac regions; no masses, guarding, organomegaly, or ascites were noted.

Initial management included intravenous fluids, ondansetron, a proton-pump inhibitor, and analgesia, but his pain persisted. Differential diagnoses considered included acute gastritis, appendicitis, incarcerated or strangulated inguinal hernia, kidney stones, mesenteric ischemia, and small-bowel obstruction.

Investigations:
– Ultrasound of the abdomen and pelvis: normal.  
– Urinalysis: unremarkable except 1+ ketones.  
– Complete blood count, electrolytes, lipase, amylase, liver function tests, troponin I, D-dimer, and C-reactive protein: within normal limits.  
– Serum lactate: 2.9 mmol/L.  
– CT KUB: small left renal calcific density suggestive of a non-obstructing calculus; otherwise normal.  
– Abdominal radiograph: small-bowel distension and absence of ileocecal gas.  
– CT angiography of the abdomen and pelvis with contrast: small intramural lipoma in the terminal ileum, thought to be an incidental finding; no evidence of ischemia or obstruction.  
– Esophagogastroduodenoscopy and colonoscopy on hospital day 3: normal, although the colonoscope could not be advanced beyond the terminal ileum.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 43-year-old woman presented with a two-year history of a slowly enlarging swelling on the left side of her jaw that was asymptomatic until two months ago, when she experienced dull, nonradiating pain that intensified during meals and resolved spontaneously. Her medical history was notable for type 1 diabetes mellitus and dyslipidemia under treatment. On examination, there was a hard, non-tender mass adherent to the lower border of the left posterior mandible. Intraoral examination and bimanual palpation of the submandibular gland were unremarkable. Panoramic radiography revealed a well-defined, irregular radiopaque lesion in the left posterior mandible. Ultrasonography showed a normal submandibular gland and duct. Cone-beam computed tomography demonstrated a 19×12 mm radiopaque mass arising from the lingual cortex of the left posterior mandible.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 60-year-old woman presented with a 3-month history of easy fatigability and a persistent, dull, right-sided throbbing headache, which intermittently worsened. During the same period, she noted a nodular growth over her right forehead that measured approximately 2.5 × 2.5 cm; it was initially excised by a local practitioner but recurred and enlarged over 1½ months. She denied changes in appetite, bowel or bladder habits, and had no weight loss. Her surgical history was notable for a total abdominal hysterectomy performed 6 months earlier for multiple uterine fibroids.

On examination, the forehead lesion was fixed, nonpulsatile, and non-tender, with a nodular surface and an overlying scar from prior excision. A second firm, fixed swelling measuring 4 × 4 cm was palpated on the right chest wall. There was no organomegaly. Routine blood tests and serum chemistry were within normal limits.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the head showed a 5 × 4 cm heterogeneously enhancing mass in the right frontal region with destruction of both the outer and inner tables of the frontal bone and compression of the underlying frontal lobe. CT of the chest and abdomen revealed a 5.7 × 4.1 × 3.5 cm heterogeneously enhancing lesion in the superior/posterobasal segment of the right lower lobe, with invasion of a branch of the inferior pulmonary vein; multiple spiculated nodules in both upper lobes; and a pleura-based soft tissue mass in the right posterolateral chest wall destroying the 10th rib and infiltrating subcutaneous fat.

Fine-needle aspiration was performed from both the forehead and chest-wall nodules. Smears from both sites showed cohesive overlapping clusters of pleomorphic tumor cells with hyperchromatic oval to bizarre nuclei, some embedded in pink matrix material. The features were interpreted as those of a poorly differentiated malignant tumor.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 64-year-old female teacher presented with six weeks of burning pain and sensitivity in both inner thighs, the left buttock, left leg, and sole of the left foot. Six weeks earlier she had been treated empirically for a urinary tract infection, but her dysuria persisted and was soon followed by acute bilateral vulval blisters. She was diagnosed with genital herpes and treated with valacyclovir 500 mg twice daily for one week, with resolution of blisters in five days. Despite a further week of valacyclovir, she developed persistent burning and exquisite hypersensitivity in the S1–S3 dermatomes on the left. After six weeks of valacyclovir with minimal improvement and no recurrent ulcers, she was referred. 

She denied systemic or prodromal symptoms. She was in a monogamous relationship; her partner was HSV-2 positive and on suppressive therapy. She had no prior history of genital herpes or shingles; childhood chickenpox only. Her medications included escitalopram started four months earlier. 

Examination showed normal genital skin and hyperaesthesia in left S1–S3 dermatomes without motor deficit or other sensory loss. A PCR swab of healed vulval sites was negative for HSV and VZV; first‐catch urine PCR was negative for Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis. She declined HSV serology. 

Pain management was discussed; four days later she began amitriptyline, titrated to 20 mg at night, with complete resolution of pain after four weeks. Three months later she had a milder recurrence of vulval blisters, self-treated with topical acyclovir; lesions healed in two days, but she again experienced hyperaesthesia in the same distribution, which resolved after three weeks of amitriptyline.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 14-year-old girl with type 1 diabetes mellitus since age 7 was admitted for a 2-month history of progressive asthenia, weight loss, headaches, and glycemic instability characterized by multiple unexplained hypoglycemic episodes. Over the preceding 2 weeks she noted increased urine output and polydipsia without a clear relationship to blood glucose levels. She also reported a 2-month history of secondary amenorrhea. Past medical history was notable for an appendectomy at age 8 and chronic autoimmune thyroiditis with normal thyroid function. She was on multiple daily insulin injections at 0.8 U/kg/day.  
On examination, she was Tanner stage V, 159.8 cm (48th percentile), and 51.7 kg (55th percentile). Vital signs, neurologic examination, and funduscopic exam were normal.  
Initial studies—including chest radiography, abdominal ultrasonography, and electrocardiography—were unremarkable. Laboratory evaluation showed HbA1c 7.18% (54 mmol/mol), hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL, normal electrolytes, liver and lipid panels, and negative inflammatory markers.  
Endocrine testing revealed:  
• Morning ACTH 13.94 pg/mL and cortisol 3.81 μg/dL; low-dose ACTH stimulation yielded a peak cortisol of 338 nmol/L, consistent with central adrenal insufficiency.  
• Free T4 0.91 ng/dL and TSH 0.03 μIU/mL, indicating central hypothyroidism.  
• Urine osmolality 59 mOsm/kg with 5–6 L/day water intake/output, diagnostic of diabetes insipidus.  
• Undetectable 17-β-estradiol and elevated prolactin (≈90 ng/mL) on serial measurements.  
Cerebrospinal fluid analysis was normal. Visual field testing showed an upper central isopter defect and enlarged blind spot.  
Brain magnetic resonance imaging demonstrated a solid sellar-suprasellar mass with postcontrast enhancement, central necrotic area without hemorrhage, thickening of the pituitary stalk, and absence of the normal posterior pituitary bright spot. Computed tomography confirmed sella turcica enlargement without evidence of sinus disease.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 26-year-old woman with a history of unspecified bipolar disorder and recent antipsychotic treatment was brought to the emergency department after 10 days of worsening mental status. Collateral history revealed progressive fatigue, inability to feed herself, and dependence for activities of daily living. On arrival, she was unresponsive; blood pressure was 175/108 mm Hg, heart rate 170 beats per minute, temperature 37.8 °C, respiratory rate 20 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. She had adequate urine output but required assistance with toileting. Physical examination showed rigidity of all four extremities, areflexia, inability to follow commands, and warm skin. Laboratory studies revealed a white-cell count of 12,000 per mm³ and a creatine kinase level of 6,500 IU/L. A noncontrast CT scan of the head was unremarkable. The initial differential diagnosis included neuroleptic malignant syndrome, malignant catatonia, encephalitis, and sepsis.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 73-year-old woman with intermediate-2–risk MDS in complete remission after azacitidine and on venetoclax presented with acute odynophagia beginning 2 days earlier, followed by left-sided neck pain at the carotid bifurcation and swelling. She had received G-CSF 4 days prior for severe neutropenia (neutrophils 290/mm^3). She remained SARS-CoV-2 PCR–positive but without hypoxia. On day 6 of neck pain, she had fever to 38.5 °C and a C-reactive protein of 328 mg/L. Contrast CT demonstrated circumferential thickening around the left internal carotid artery at its bifurcation extending into the common carotid artery, and concentric thickening of the aortic arch. Cervical ultrasound confirmed an eccentric, isoechoic, circumferential perivascular infiltrate at the left carotid bifurcation. Two cytopunctures of the pericarotid tissue yielded predominantly blood with neutrophils and lymphocytes, but no giant cells or malignant cells. She was transferred to the internal medicine and vascular department for further evaluation.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old man with a history of hypothyroidism and alcohol abuse was brought to an outside hospital after being found unresponsive by his wife. Over the preceding month, he had experienced a subacute, rapidly progressive cognitive decline. He was treated for sepsis and presumed aspiration pneumonia in the setting of a suspected accidental opioid and benzodiazepine overdose; his course was complicated by acute respiratory failure requiring intubation and a 4-day ICU stay. Although he recovered from his respiratory failure and completed rehabilitation, his mental status did not return to baseline. One week after discharge, he developed behavioral changes, including leaving water running, walking around naked, and defecating in the closet. Twenty-four days after his initial admission, he was readmitted with continued neuropsychiatric decline progressing to near akinetic mutism.

During the second hospitalization, laboratory testing—including ammonia, C-reactive protein, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, antinuclear antibody, striated muscle antibody, acetylcholine-receptor antibody, and N-methyl-d-aspartate receptor antibody—a paraneoplastic antibody panel, and lumbar puncture (normal opening pressure; CSF glucose, encephalitis panel, VDRL, cytology, West Nile virus, and protein 14-3-3) were unremarkable except for an elevated CSF protein of 86 mg/dL. Head CT, brain MRI with and without gadolinium, MR angiography of the head and neck, and cerebral angiography showed only T2-weighted hyperintensities in the bilateral basal ganglia and diffuse increased signal in the cerebral white matter, without enhancement or restricted diffusion. Initial EEG showed intermittent left frontotemporal slowing and sharp waves; one week later, it showed diffuse slowing. A trial of lacosamide and a 3-day course of IV methylprednisolone (1 g/day) produced no clinical improvement. He was then transferred to Tampa General Hospital.

On arrival, he was awake with eyes open but nonverbal, could not follow commands, and had only limited purposeful movements; there was no focal or asymmetric weakness. Extensive serum studies—including CBC, CMP, urinalysis, thyroid function tests, vitamin and micronutrient levels, homocysteine, ammonia, ceruloplasmin, methylmalonic acid, hemoglobin electrophoresis, serum and urine protein electrophoreses, heavy-metal panel, autoimmune serologies, and infectious serologies—were unrevealing; inflammatory markers were mildly elevated (CRP 0.79 mg/L; ESR 23 mm/h). A repeat lumbar puncture (opening pressure 22 cm H₂O) showed CSF analyses negative for meningitis panel, cryptococcal antigen, Lyme, arboviruses, CMV, HSV, VDRL, gram stain, and cultures, with oligoclonal bands absent and protein mildly elevated at 70 mg/dL. Brain MRI again demonstrated extensive nonenhancing white matter hyperintensities. A 24-hour continuous EEG revealed a mild to moderate diffuse encephalopathy without epileptiform activity. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was negative for malignancy, mass, or lymphadenopathy.

Steroid-responsive encephalopathy with thyroiditis (SREAT) was considered, and the patient received a 5-day course of IV methylprednisolone (1 g/day) without improvement. He then received intravenous immunoglobulin (2 g total over 4 days), but his clinical status remained unchanged.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 15-year-old girl with congenital intellectual disability and blindness presents with primary amenorrhea and a 2-year history of cyclical lower abdominal and pelvic pain lasting 5 days each month. The pain has worsened over the past 2 months and is partially relieved by oral analgesics. She exhibits irritability, reduced attention span, impaired perceptual reasoning, and limited functional independence. A sister has a similar clinical profile.

Vital signs: blood pressure 100/60 mmHg; pulse 96/min; respiratory rate 18/min. Examination shows pallor, moon face, submental fat, height 151 cm, weight 85 kg (BMI 37.2). Post-axial polydactyly is present on the left hand. Tanner stage 5 secondary sexual characteristics. Genital exam reveals vaginal atresia, a rudimentary uterus, and a transverse vaginal septum. Ophthalmologic exam shows astigmatism, strabismus, and on funduscopy numerous whitish-yellow equatorial spots, optic nerve pallor, retinal vessel constriction, and macular degeneration.

Abdominal and pelvic ultrasonography demonstrates a 13.9-cm cystic structure posterior to the bladder within the vaginal canal, communicating with a small amount of fluid in the endometrial cavity. MRI confirms hematometrocolpos with moderate bilateral hydrosalpinx. Laboratory studies reveal normocytic normochromic anemia (hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL); renal, hepatic, metabolic profiles, and electrolytes are normal.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 76-year-old Japanese man with a 40 pack-year smoking history was referred after an abnormal chest radiograph at a routine exam. He reported no respiratory symptoms. Laboratory studies revealed elevated squamous cell carcinoma antigen (24.5 ng/mL; normal <1.5), cytokeratin 19 fragment (10.3 ng/mL; normal <3.5), carcinoembryonic antigen (7.3 ng/mL; normal <3.5), and surfactant protein D (299 ng/mL; normal <110). Chest radiograph showed an irregular opacity in the right lower lobe. Chest CT demonstrated a large area of consolidation with air bronchograms in the right lower lobe and bilateral reticular opacities with honeycombing. FDG-PET/CT revealed high uptake in the right lung lesion (SUVmax 9.38 at 1 hour; 13.03 at 2 hours). Bronchoscopic examination showed no endobronchial lesion. Transbronchial lung biopsy and bronchial brushing yielded cytology interpreted as squamous cell carcinoma, but occasional ciliated epithelial cells and mucin-containing columnar cells without atypia were also noted.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

options.B: Mixed squamous cell and glandular papilloma

question:
A 35-year-old man with no past medical history presented with intermittent fever and myalgia of 1 month’s duration. On examination, his temperature was 39.2°C. Both upper arms were swollen and exquisitely tender, but the overlying skin appeared normal.  
  
Laboratory studies revealed a white-cell count of 20 440/mm3 with a differential of 7% segmented neutrophils, 22% lymphocytes, and 40% blast cells, and a lactate dehydrogenase level of 977 U/L.  

Ultrasonography of the upper arms showed diffusely increased echogenicity and thickening of the triceps and brachialis muscles. MRI confirmed diffuse muscle edema without abscess formation.  

A muscle biopsy of the left triceps and brachialis revealed severe myositis with dense infiltration of mature neutrophils, disruption of muscle architecture, and tissue necrosis. Microbiological cultures and stains for bacteria and fungi were negative, and serologic testing for autoantibodies was also negative.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A previously healthy 13‐year‐old girl was evaluated on day 40 of illness with fever and rash. Four weeks after a mild febrile illness, she developed fever to 39–41 °C, fatigue, sore throat, cough, shortness of breath, profuse sweating and diarrhea. On day 40, she awoke with a red rash on her palms, which rapidly spread to her arms, feet and knees.

On arrival to the emergency department, vital signs were: blood pressure 87/56 mmHg; heart rate 137 bpm; respiratory rate 16/min; oxygen saturation 95% on room air; temperature 39.1 °C. She was alert but fatigued with mild respiratory distress. Physical examination revealed conjunctival injection, slight bilateral eye redness, minimally erythematous throat, dry mucous membranes, dry cracked lips, no lymphadenopathy, a grade II apical systolic murmur, clear lungs, and an erythematous rash on palms, arms and feet.

Initial laboratory studies showed a C-reactive protein of 255 mg/L, white blood count 8.1×10^9/L, hemoglobin 117 g/L. A nasopharyngeal swab for SARS-CoV-2 PCR was negative.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 37-year-old man with no past medical history developed fever, headache, and a painful left-sided neck swelling while working offshore. He denied cough, dyspnea, chest pain, or recent travel and had no known COVID-19 contacts. On admission: temperature 41 °C, heart rate 115 beats/min, blood pressure 119/76 mm Hg, respiratory rate 12–22 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 100% on room air. Physical examination revealed a tender, non-fluctuant swelling extending from the left mandibular angle to the sternocleidomastoid muscle and a red area on the posterior palate; the remainder of the examination was unremarkable.  
ECG showed sinus tachycardia with moderately flattened T-waves. Chest radiograph was normal. Arterial blood gas on room air demonstrated respiratory alkalosis (pH 7.55, pCO2 3.9 kPa, pO2 8.4 kPa, HCO3 23 mmol/L). Laboratory studies revealed CRP 230 mg/L (0–5), procalcitonin 2.1 µg/L (0–0.10), WBC 7.1 × 10^9/L (3.5–11.0), troponin T 90 ng/L (0–15), and NT-proBNP 160 ng/L (0–85).  
Contrast CT of the neck showed left-sided subcutaneous edema with multiple enlarged lymph nodes up to 2.7 cm. Transthoracic echocardiography on admission demonstrated normal ventricular function without hypokinesia. On day 2, echocardiography revealed a reduced left ventricular ejection fraction of 40%, while CRP rose to 344 mg/L and procalcitonin to 12.9 µg/L, with persistently normal WBC count.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 20-year-old Japanese woman presented with a slowly enlarging subcutaneous nodule on her left forehead, first noticed at age 15. On examination, a firm, poorly mobile 7-mm nodule was palpable beneath the skin. Ultrasonography showed a well-defined, dome-shaped, solid subcutaneous mass (~7 mm) without vascular flow on Doppler; there was apparent continuity with the frontal bone, though the interface with subcutaneous tissue was unclear. MRI of the head demonstrated a 7-mm lesion within the frontalis muscle that was uniformly low signal on both T1- and T2-weighted sequences, with no obvious connection to the frontal bone. Noncontrast CT revealed a high-attenuation nodule beneath the frontalis muscle, measuring approximately 7 mm, with a small zone of decreased attenuation separating it from the underlying bone. Based on these findings, benign entities such as calcifying epithelioma and skull osteoma were considered in the preoperative differential.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 6-year-old boy presented after awakening with nonbloody, nonbilious emesis, lightheadedness, and approximately one minute of eye fluttering with unresponsiveness but no tonic–clonic movements or incontinence. Post-ictally, he had headache, photophobia, and vision changes. He had intermittent morning vomiting for one month but no fevers, abdominal pain, urinary symptoms, or prior seizures. Family history was notable for brain tumors in maternal relatives. 

On exam, blood pressure was 161/102 mm Hg, heart rate 90–110 beats/min, temperature 37.9 °C, and respiratory rate 16 breaths/min. He was neurologically intact with bilateral upgoing toes. Abdominal exam revealed suprapubic distension without tenderness or organomegaly.

Noncontrast head CT showed a left‐hemisphere hypodense region with artifact. Brain MRI demonstrated bilateral parieto-occipital subcortical T2 prolongation and gyral swelling consistent with posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome. EEG captured subclinical seizures from bilateral occipital regions, which resolved with lorazepam, levetiracetam, and fosphenytoin.

Initial laboratory studies showed WBC 29.7×10^6/mL, hemoglobin 12 g/dL, platelets 265×10^6/mL, BUN 16 mg/dL, creatinine 1.3 mg/dL. Urinalysis revealed 30 mg/dL protein without blood. Lumbar puncture yielded 0 nucleated cells, 0 red blood cells, protein 43 mg/dL, and glucose 69 mg/dL. Renal ultrasound revealed bilaterally enlarged (11.6 cm) echogenic kidneys without Doppler abnormalities and a distended bladder. Transthoracic echocardiogram was normal without left ventricular hypertrophy. Despite blood pressure control with intravenous nicardipine transitioned to oral agents, the etiology of his hypertension and renal enlargement remained unclear.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 28-year-old Iranian woman presented with a 3-year history of bilateral vision loss and photopsia. Three years earlier, she had accidentally ingested four 1250 mg boluses of veterinary niclosamide. A few days after ingestion, she noted visual disturbances and was treated empirically with a short course of high-dose oral corticosteroids for presumed bilateral optic neuritis; her vision neither improved nor worsened. Over the next 3 years, her visual symptoms remained stable. She denied other medication use, alcohol, smoking, night blindness, or photophobia. There was no family history of vision loss or retinal disease, and parents were nonconsanguineous. 

On examination, vital signs were normal. Best-corrected visual acuities were 20/50 in the right eye and 20/63 in the left eye. Color vision and pupillary reactions were normal. Anterior segment and vitreous examinations were unremarkable; intraocular pressures were normal. Fundus examination revealed pink optic discs, cup-to-disc ratios of 0.2, and parafoveal retinal pigment epithelial atrophy manifesting as bilateral bull’s-eye maculopathy. Fundus autofluorescence demonstrated annular hyperautofluorescence in the parafoveal region. Optical coherence tomography showed parafoveal ellipsoid zone disruption, outer retinal thinning, and relative foveal sparing (“flying saucer” sign). Full-field electroretinography revealed decreased scotopic and photopic amplitudes with normal waveforms in both eyes. Electrooculography showed Arden ratios of 240% (right eye) and 202% (left eye).

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 22-year-old Caucasian woman was first evaluated in 2014 for dyspnoea, chronic cutaneous eruptions, and symmetrical polyarthritis. High-resolution chest computed tomography showed pulmonary fibrosis, and lung biopsy led to a diagnosis of hypersensitivity pneumonitis in the setting of significant mould exposure. Skin biopsies were consistent with spongiotic dermatitis (eczema). Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated right ventricular dilatation with mild tricuspid regurgitation and normal left ventricular size and function. Right heart catheterization revealed normal pulmonary arterial pressures.

Over the next 4 years, she received methotrexate, sulfasalazine, and intermittent prednisone. In mid-2018, she reported dyspnoea on walking 50 m and four episodes of syncope at rest. On examination, blood pressure was 130/70 mmHg, heart rate 88 b.p.m. (regular), and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Jugular venous pressure was elevated with prominent V waves, and there was a right ventricular heave. A loud pansystolic murmur was audible at the left sternal edge, with pulsatile hepatomegaly and fine crackles at both lung bases.

Electrocardiogram showed atrial fibrillation with a right bundle branch block. Repeat echocardiography demonstrated severe right ventricular dilatation and severe tricuspid regurgitation; left ventricular size and function remained normal, with no significant left-sided valvular disease. Telemetry captured non-sustained ventricular tachycardia at 150 b.p.m. Right heart catheterization again showed normal pulmonary arterial pressures (22/10 mmHg), elevated right atrial pressures (16/14 mmHg), and normal cardiac output and pulmonary vascular resistance. 

Transesophageal echocardiography excluded atrial thrombus and intracardiac shunts. Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging showed no late gadolinium enhancement and excluded congenital anomalies. Cardiac positron emission tomography did not reveal increased metabolic activity. A chest CT demonstrated unchanged pulmonary fibrosis.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 60-year-old man with a 20-year history of hepatitis B and 1-year history of liver cirrhosis presented initially with chronic abdominal pain. His alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) level was 2494.2 ng/mL (normal <10.9). CT scans showed a 4×3 cm mass in the left liver lobe and multiple pulmonary nodules. He underwent transarterial chemoembolization (TACE) and two cycles of gemcitabine–oxaliplatin, but his liver tumor enlarged and AFP rose to 8683.0 ng/mL. A bean-sized gingival nodule was noted, which had mild pain, discharged “pus” on puncture, and recurred quickly. He received further TACE and sorafenib, with stable disease for 6 months, then disease progression. 

On readmission, he reported breathlessness, progressive weight loss, severe back pain, and right upper quadrant pain. Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 91 g/L, platelets 97×10^9/L, albumin 33.4 g/L, AFP 4325.0 ng/mL, AST 66 U/L, LDH 296 U/L, direct bilirubin 9.4 µmol/L, indirect bilirubin 13.2 µmol/L, and normal coagulation. 

Physical examination revealed a 2×2 cm reddish nodule on the alveolar mucosa distal to the upper right first premolar; it was soft, sharply demarcated, centrally ulcerated, swollen, mildly tender, and bled occasionally. Radiography showed no bony destruction.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 37-year-old man was referred for evaluation of elevated liver enzymes and cirrhosis on imaging. He reported that over the past 4–5 years he had persistently elevated aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), and gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT) levels but had not undergone further evaluation. He denied symptoms, alcohol intake was two to three drinks per month, and he was a current smoker (seven pack-years). There was no history of liver disease, medication use, or recent travel. 

On examination, he was afebrile, without jaundice or stigmata of chronic liver disease; the liver and spleen were not palpable. His body mass index was 29.7 kg/m2. Laboratory studies showed AST 133 IU/L (ULN 40), ALT 158 IU/L (ULN 40), ALP 378 IU/L (ULN 129), GGT 2162 IU/L (ULN 73), total bilirubin 0.71 mg/dL, albumin 5.0 g/dL, and normal prothrombin time. 

Abdominal CT revealed cirrhotic-appearing nodules in both hepatic lobes without evidence of portal hypertension. Chest radiograph was unremarkable. Serologic tests for hepatitis A, B, and C viruses were all negative. Autoimmune markers—including antimitochondrial antibody (AMA), anti–smooth muscle antibody, anti–liver kidney microsome type 1, and ANCA—were within normal limits. Alpha-1-antitrypsin, ceruloplasmin, antinuclear antibody, and serum copper were normal. Serum immunoglobulins (IgG, IgA, IgM) were not elevated. A 5-month course of ursodeoxycholic acid (600 mg/day) did not improve liver enzyme levels. 

A transjugular liver biopsy was performed. Histopathology showed chronic granulomatous inflammation; staining for fungi and acid-fast bacilli was negative, and PCR testing for Mycobacterium tuberculosis was negative.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 15-year-old man presented to the emergency department with acute lightheadedness, diaphoresis, and palpitations in the evening after a 7-hour fast. He had experienced recurrent episodes of dizziness for 2 months without weight change. Point-of-care blood glucose was 1.5 mmol/L, and his symptoms resolved after IV dextrose.

He had no family history of insulinoma or multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1. Vital signs were normal. On abdominal examination, there was a large, firm, palpable mass in the left upper quadrant.

Serum testing during hypoglycemia showed an inappropriately elevated insulin level of 13.34 μU/mL (normal <3 μU/mL) and a C-peptide level of 2.2 ng/mL (normal <0.6 ng/mL). Serum intact parathyroid hormone, calcium, and prolactin levels were all within reference ranges.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed a well-defined, lobulated, hypervascular mass measuring 12 cm in the pancreatic tail with areas of low attenuation and calcifications. There was no pancreatic duct dilatation, no hepatic lesions, and no significant lymphadenopathy.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A woman in her late forties presented with a 1.5-week history of gradually worsening pain and stiffness in both thighs, limiting her ability to walk. Her history was notable for childhood-onset type 1 diabetes mellitus complicated by retinopathy, neuropathy, nephropathy, and end-stage renal disease; she also had hypertension. She denied trauma, rash, joint symptoms, or respiratory complaints. On examination, she had reduced strength in ankle dorsiflexion and plantarflexion and hallux extension and flexion bilaterally. Her temperature was 37.9 °C. Laboratory studies showed a C-reactive protein level of 203 mg/l, creatine kinase of 700 IU/l, and myoglobin of 1011 μg/l. Urinalysis revealed microscopic hematuria and pyuria, and a urine culture grew Escherichia coli in the absence of urinary symptoms. An MRI of the lower limbs demonstrated extensive, symmetric edema in muscles of the hips, thighs, shins, and calves, without significant muscle atrophy or fatty replacement. Serologic testing was unremarkable: antinuclear antibodies, myositis-associated and myositis-specific antibodies (including anti-HMGCR and anti-SRP), and viral hepatitis serologies were all negative.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 40-year-old man was referred for evaluation of newly diagnosed, asymptomatic left ventricular (LV) systolic dysfunction discovered after premature ventricular contractions were noted on a pre-sports electrocardiogram. His history was notable for cutaneous and pulmonary sarcoidosis diagnosed at age 25 and in remission since 2017 after corticosteroid therapy. He had no known cardiovascular disease, no relevant family history, and took no regular medications. Physical examination was unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed NT-proBNP 110 pg/mL, high-sensitivity troponin T 20 ng/dL, soluble interleukin-2 receptor 2287 pg/mL (normal 458–1997), and angiotensin-converting enzyme 155 U/L (normal 8–52). A 24-hour Holter monitor revealed sinus rhythm with 9 polymorphic PVCs per hour. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a severely dilated LV (indexed end-diastolic volume 112 mL/m²), basal thinning (“scooping”) of the anterior septum, and moderate systolic dysfunction (LVEF 41%, global longitudinal strain –15.4%). The right ventricle was slightly dilated but had normal systolic function. No valvular abnormalities or pericardial effusion were noted.

Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging showed diffuse myocardial edema (T2 mapping 51 ± 6 ms; institutional cut-off 50 ms) in anterior, anterolateral, and inferolateral walls, and extensive LGE (26% of LV mass) with intramural septal and subepicardial anterior, inferior, and lateral involvement, forming a “ring-like” pattern. Coronary CT angiography was normal. A presumptive diagnosis of cardiac sarcoidosis was made based on imaging. Whole-body 18F-FDG PET/CT revealed diffuse myocardial uptake without extracardiac involvement. Right ventricular endomyocardial biopsy was negative for granulomas.

The patient was started on prednisolone, methotrexate, ramipril, bisoprolol, and dapagliflozin. At three months, CMR showed normalization of T2 values (49 ms) and recovery of LVEF to 52%, but the LGE pattern persisted. A six-month 18F-FDG PET/CT scan demonstrated resolution of myocardial uptake.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 58-year-old woman with a 4-year history of chronic lymphocytic leukemia treated with fludarabine, cyclophosphamide, and rituximab presented in May 2021 for induction therapy of newly diagnosed, therapy-related acute myeloid leukemia (decitabine plus a 21-day venetoclax ramp-up). Prophylactic levofloxacin, isavuconazonium, acyclovir, and allopurinol were started for neutropenia and tumor-lysis risk. On cycle 1 day 3, she developed neutropenic fever and was switched to meropenem; a CT pulmonary angiogram showed a pulmonary embolism, small bilateral pleural effusions, and basilar atelectasis. Chemo was held on day 4 due to persistent tachycardia and rising oxygen requirements; a repeat chest CT revealed worsening patchy infiltrates in the upper and middle lobes and consolidation versus atelectasis in the lower lobes. Pleural effusions improved with diuresis, but tachypnea persisted despite broad antibiotics and sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim for Pneumocystis coverage. She was discharged after stabilization.

Twelve days after chemotherapy was held, she returned with acute, left-sided facial swelling in the setting of neutropenia. Dermatologic exam revealed a solitary subcutaneous nodule without epidermal ulceration. An initial biopsy was non-specific, showing mild inflammation but no evidence of infection, leukemic infiltration, or neutrophilic dermatosis. Two days later, similar erythematous plaques appeared on her right eyelid and eyebrow; a repeat biopsy showed dense neutrophilic infiltration consistent with Sweet syndrome, with negative bacterial and fungal stains. Prednisone was started, and the skin lesions improved rapidly.

In October 2021, approximately four months later and on day 35 of azacitidine plus venetoclax, she developed facial pain, fevers to 38.1 °C, and neutropenia (ANC 390 cells/μL). She had bilateral purpuric papules on the lower extremities and periorbital tenderness. Bone marrow biopsy showed 2% blasts. Empiric cefepime, doxycycline, acyclovir, and isavuconazonium were instituted, and prednisone 30 mg daily plus topical hydrocortisone were added for presumed recurrence of cutaneous Sweet syndrome. Maxillofacial CT demonstrated maxillary sinus opacification with hyperdense material and orbital fat stranding, prompting amphotericin B and daptomycin. Nasal endoscopy suggested bacterial sinusitis but cultures remained negative. Despite optimized antimicrobials, fevers and dyspnea persisted. Chest CT revealed new bilateral ground-glass opacities in the upper and middle lobes with consolidative opacities in the lower lobes. She developed increasing oxygen requirements, ultimately requiring high-flow nasal cannula. Bronchoscopy with cultures was unrevealing.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 20-year-old Filipino man with systemic lupus erythematosus diagnosed 6 months earlier and maintained on hydroxychloroquine presented with sudden dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, fatigue, dry cough, fevers, and a chronic progressive rash. He denied recent travel, sick contacts, high-risk sexual activity, intravenous drug use, smoking, or alcohol use. On examination, his temperature was 100.8 °F, blood pressure 118/72 mmHg, heart rate 118 bpm, and respiratory rate 22 breaths per minute. Cardiovascular examination was unremarkable. Skin assessment revealed hyperpigmented to violaceous, scaly plaques with excoriated papules on the extremities, back, and chest.

Laboratory studies showed leukopenia, anemia, non-nephrotic proteinuria without active sediment, elevated troponin and N-terminal pro-BNP, high inflammatory markers, hypocomplementemia, and positive ANA with anti–Smith, RNP, chromatin, SS-A, double-stranded DNA, pANCA, and MPO antibodies. Initial chest radiograph was normal. Blood cultures grew methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus. Transthoracic echocardiogram at admission showed a normal left ventricular ejection fraction. He was treated with intravenous antibiotics and high-dose corticosteroids for presumed sepsis and SLE flare.

On hospital day 5, after clearance of bacteremia, he developed worsening dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, and hemoptysis. Chest radiograph and CT of the chest showed bilateral pleural effusions, dense basal consolidations, pulmonary nodules, possible left subsegmental pulmonary emboli, and pericardial effusions. Repeat transthoracic echocardiogram revealed an LVEF of 25%, moderate global hypokinesis, a small pericardial effusion without tamponade, right atrial pressure of 15 mmHg, and mild pulmonary hypertension (43 mmHg). Cardiogenic shock was favored over septic shock.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 43-year-old man with a 6-month history of chronic urticaria presented to clinic. He had no systemic symptoms initially. Laboratory studies showed normal blood counts, electrolytes, liver and thyroid function tests, ESR, and CRP. Serum immunoglobulins revealed a polyclonal increase in IgG and IgA, and a small (<1 g/L) IgM kappa paraprotein. The rash was refractory to high-dose nonsedating antihistamines.

Six months later, he reported generalized fatigue, daily fevers, night sweats, arthralgias (notably right knee and tibial pain), weight loss, and bilateral axillary and inguinal lymphadenopathy. Investigations revealed a persistent neutrophilia (>15×10^9/L), elevated ESR and CRP, ongoing polyclonal IgG, IgA, and IgM elevations with the IgM kappa paraprotein increased to 3.3 g/L, and a mildly elevated ferritin (361 μg/L; normal 18–325). While an inpatient, he had daily fevers above 38 °C. 

Isotope bone scan showed increased uptake at the proximal right tibia. MRI of the right knee demonstrated nonspecific marrow signal changes at the distal femur and proximal tibia. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed florid bilateral axillary and inguinal lymphadenopathy. Autoimmune serologies (ANA, ENA, dsDNA, CCP, ANCA), rheumatoid factor, and complement levels were all normal or negative. Beta-2 microglobulin and plasma viscosity were slightly elevated; lymphocyte subsets and kappa/lambda ratios were normal; urine Bence Jones protein was negative. Extensive infectious workup, including serologies for EBV, CMV, HIV, hepatitis viruses, syphilis, Borrelia, Brucella, Chlamydia, Q fever, Mycoplasma, and HHV8, as well as mycobacterial cultures, was negative.

Skin biopsy showed an inflammatory epidermal infiltrate suggestive of rheumatoid arthritis or Still’s disease. Lymph node biopsy revealed polyclonal reactive hyperplasia with abundant plasma cells; Castleman disease was considered but further expert review excluded it. Bone marrow biopsy showed only reactive changes. Genetic testing for MVK, TNFRSF1A, and NLRP3 mutations was negative, making Hyper‐IgD syndrome, TNF receptor–associated periodic syndrome, and Muckle Wells/familial cold urticaria unlikely. 

A tentative diagnosis of Adult Onset Still’s Disease was made.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 57-year-old woman presented with 6 months of progressive right hip and groin pain without trauma or steroid use. She had a 3 cm right leg-length discrepancy, severely limited right hip motion, and no neurovascular deficits. Over 5 months she became unable to ambulate. She also had a 3-year history of chronic metatarsophalangeal joint and toe pain with deformities and recent swelling, followed by right knee pain with warmth and swelling. Morning stiffness lasted approximately 2 hours. On examination her swollen joint count was initially 8/66 (right knee and multiple MTPs), rising to 11/66 on subsequent visits. She had an unintentional 50 lb weight loss. Laboratory studies showed elevated inflammatory markers, a weakly positive ANA, and negative RF and anti-CCP. All blood cultures (aerobic, anaerobic, fungal, mycobacterial) were negative. Metabolic panel and ACE level were normal. Malignancy work-up—including serum and urine electrophoresis, CEA, CA-125, total‐body PET scan, and bone scan—was normal. CT thorax showed two benign pulmonary nodules. CT abdomen and pelvis revealed a soft‐tissue calcified mass in the right sacroiliac fossa and gluteal muscles. Initial right hip and pelvis radiographs showed femoral head lucencies compatible with subchondral cysts, moderate diffuse joint-space loss, and flattening of the femoral head. Five months later films demonstrated complete destruction of the femoral head, acetabular erosion, and lateral subluxation of the proximal femur. CT pelvis with contrast showed fragmented bone within the acetabular fossa. MRI of the right hip revealed extensive synovial hypertrophy, minimal bone marrow edema, an iliopsoas bursal fluid collection extending to the sciatic notch, and hip joint–capsule enlargement. Foot radiographs showed erosive changes in the MTPs; hand radiographs showed periarticular osteopenia of the MCPs and ulnar deviation. Imaging of the knees, shoulders, and spine revealed degenerative changes. Three hip aspirations yielded bloody fluid with 0.6 × 10^9 nucleated cells (17% neutrophils) and only extracellular CPPD crystals; a right knee aspiration showed bloody fluid with 35 × 10^9 nucleated cells (96% neutrophils) and no crystals.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 64-year-old man with a 35-year history of back pain, stiffness, and hip pain was evaluated for persistent symptoms despite previous treatments.  
• At age 29, he was diagnosed with ankylosing spondylitis based on progressive back pain, stiffness, hip pain, joint-space narrowing, and a history of ankle surgery for enthesopathy at age 19.  
• Imaging showed diffuse spinal and Achilles enthesophytes, interspinous ligament calcification, bilateral sacroiliac joint fusion (“severe AS”), and he had undergone bilateral hip arthroplasties.  
• He reported ongoing pain managed with hydrocodone and had derived no benefit from indomethacin or other nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs; he had also failed adalimumab therapy.  
• Atypical features included bilateral bowing of the proximal femoral shafts with subtrochanteric lateral proximal insufficiency fractures and progressive hearing loss since age 9.  
• A 20-year dental record review revealed chronic poor dentition with multiple abscesses, dental fractures, and extractions.  
• Laboratory studies showed inorganic phosphate 1.9 mg/dL (2.5–4.5), alkaline phosphatase 148 U/L (40–129), creatinine 0.74 mg/dL (0.7–1.3), spot urine creatinine 76 mg/dL (20–320), parathyroid hormone 115 pg/mL (16–77), 1,25(OH)₂ vitamin D 49.9 pg/mL (19.7–79.3), and serum calcium 9.8 mg/dL (8.4–10.5).  
• A 24-hour urine phosphate was 1300 mg (226–1797), inappropriately normal in the setting of hypophosphatemia.  
• HLA-B27 testing was negative.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 53-year-old man was found unarousable by his wife after two weeks of progressive fatigue without fevers, weight loss, nausea, or abdominal pain. His medical history included benign prostatic hypertrophy and hyperlipidemia; medications were terazosin, tamsulosin, and a recent short methylprednisolone taper; simvastatin was started one month earlier.
