He denied alcohol abuse, liver disease, recent surgery, or chemotherapy, and had no symptoms after using a permethrin‐based insecticide.

On initial evaluation at an outside hospital he was afebrile with normal vital signs but intermittently arousable only to verbal stimuli; the remainder of the neurologic exam was nonfocal. A head CT, MRI/MRA of the brain, and routine labs—including CBC, electrolytes, liver function tests, alcohol and drug screens—were unremarkable except an ammonia level of 202 µmol/L (normal 11–35). AST was 19 U/L, ALT 70 U/L, total bilirubin 1.4 mg/dL, and INR 1.12. Abdominal CT showed fatty infiltration of the liver without cirrhosis.

Despite aggressive lactulose therapy, his mental status worsened and ammonia remained elevated. He was intubated for airway protection and transferred. On arrival his Glasgow Coma Scale score was 10. Labs showed leukocytosis (16.6 × 10^9/L), sodium 155 mEq/L, calcium 8.8 mg/dL, AST 34 U/L, ALT 50 U/L, ammonia 215 µmol/L, and INR 1.21. Brain and cervical‐spine MRI remained normal. EEG demonstrated diffuse slowing with triphasic waves. Over the next 12 hours ammonia rose to 516 µmol/L, and moderate‐flux continuous venovenous hemodialysis was initiated, reducing ammonia to 291 µmol/L without clinical improvement.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 45-year-old man on maintenance hemodialysis for 3 years (ESRD due to chronic glomerulonephritis) presented with a 4-month history of progressively enlarging, painless neck masses. He denied fever, night sweats, or weight loss. His past history included tuberculous pleurisy 25 years earlier and hepatitis B surface antigen carriage; he reported no allergies.

On examination, there were firm, movable, non-tender masses (largest 3×3×2 cm) in the right anterior cervical triangle, as well as palpable lymph nodes in both axillary and inguinal regions; there was no hepatosplenomegaly or overlying skin changes.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 7.3 g/dL, WBC count 6.2×10^9/L with 12.1% eosinophils, platelet count 200×10^9/L, serum IgE 261 IU/mL, BUN 56 mg/dL, creatinine 4.8 mg/dL. Stool examinations, including ova and parasite evaluations, were negative. Fine-needle aspiration cytology of a cervical node revealed nonspecific reactive lymphadenitis.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the neck and chest demonstrated enlarging lymph nodes adjacent to both sternocleidomastoid muscles and numerous nodes (>1 cm) in axillary, paratracheal, subcarinal, and intrapulmonary regions. CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed multiple enhancing lymph nodes in the hepatic hilum, peripancreatic, celiac, and paraaortic regions (largest 4.5 cm). A Gallium-67 scan revealed increased uptake in cervical, axillary, paraaortic, iliac, and inguinal lymph nodes.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 73-year-old man was referred for evaluation of a slow-growing mass of the left mandible noted over the past 15 months. His medical history was notable only for well-controlled hypertension. On intraoral examination, there was a painless bony expansion of the buccal aspect of the left posterior mandible; the overlying mucosa was intact, and no tooth mobility was present. Palpation of the neck revealed no lymphadenopathy. A panoramic radiograph showed a multilocular radiolucent lesion, measuring approximately 6.0 × 3.5 cm, extending from the ascending ramus to the periapical region of the left canine and involving the inferior alveolar canal. CT scans demonstrated a large expansile lytic lesion of the left mandibular body with cortical destruction. Ultrasound of the neck showed only reactive lymph nodes on the left. A contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed no evidence of distant metastatic disease.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 23-year-old Malay man was referred for sudden-onset, painless, central blurring of vision in the right eye without floaters, photopsia, or eye-movement pain. Two days before visual symptoms, he had headache, low-grade fever (37.7 °C), and painful cervical lymphadenopathy treated with amoxicillin/clavulanic acid. He had no notable travel, sexual history, or sick contacts. Examination showed right-eye BCVA counting fingers at 1 meter, left-eye 6/6; a right grade-1 relative afferent pupillary defect with red desaturation; and normal left optic nerve function. Funduscopy of the right eye revealed diffuse optic disc swelling and macular edema, with a normal left fundus. Left submandibular lymphadenopathy was noted. No cranial nerve deficits were present. Laboratory tests showed leukocytosis (12.76×10^9 cells/L), neutrophilia (9.29×10^9/L), elevated C-reactive protein (62.6 mg/L), and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (44 mm/hr). A pre-steroid workup was unremarkable. Autoimmune antibodies (anti-aquaporin 4, anti–myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein, antinuclear antibody, anti–double-stranded DNA) were negative. Initial Bartonella and Borrelia IgG titres were both <1:64. Static automated perimetry demonstrated a right centrocecal scotoma. Optical coherence tomography of the right eye suggested intraretinal and subretinal fluid at the optic nerve extending to the macula. Fundus fluorescein angiography showed right-disc leakage. MRI of the brain, orbits, and anterior visual pathways with contrast and lumbar puncture were unremarkable. The patient was observed as an inpatient; his right-eye visual acuity improved spontaneously to 6/60-1. Five days after onset, hard exudates formed a partial macular star, progressing temporally.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

An 11-year-old boy was referred for evaluation of a nodule on the palatal mucosa behind the right upper central incisor. Eight months earlier, he had sustained dental trauma with a crown fracture of that tooth without pulp exposure. On examination, there was a sessile‐based nodule, narrow in the anteroposterior dimension, with a smooth surface, slight white coloration, and diffuse posterior erythema; it was non‐tender on palpation. Panoramic and periapical radiographs showed no bone involvement. Thermal sensitivity testing confirmed that the adjacent teeth were vital, effectively ruling out an acute or chronic periapical lesion.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 49-year-old man was referred for evaluation of two liver tumors detected on routine abdominal ultrasonography. He had no medical history, medication use, alcohol intake, or known viral hepatitis. On examination, he was afebrile, had no stigmata of chronic liver disease, and his body-mass index was 22.7. Laboratory tests, including alpha-fetoprotein and protein induced by vitamin K absence-II, were within normal limits. Abdominal sonography showed two well-circumscribed, hypoechoic lesions measuring 2.1 cm in segment 8 and 2.2 cm in segment 7 in a mildly fatty liver. Plain CT demonstrated both lesions as iso- to hyperdense relative to liver. On contrast-enhanced CT, the lesions showed arterial‐phase enhancement with delayed washout in the portal venous phase. On MRI, each lesion exhibited high-intensity rims and low-intensity centers on T1-weighted images, low intensity on T2-weighted images, and signal drop on opposed‐phase compared with in-phase images, indicating intralesional fat. Both lesions retained ethoxybenzyl contrast on hepatobiliary-phase imaging. Based on these findings, well-differentiated hepatocellular carcinoma was suspected, with benign nodules such as nodular regenerative hyperplasia or focal nodular hyperplasia–like lesions in the differential diagnosis.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 62-year-old woman presented with a two-day history of acute onset shortness of breath and productive cough, associated with fever, chills, generalized body aches, and pleuritic chest pain. She is a lifetime non-smoker with no alcohol use. Her medical history is notable for hypertension, gastroesophageal reflux disease, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, asthma, osteoporosis, and multiple hospital admissions for recurrent pneumonia since childhood, including infections with Nocardia and Moraxella and one episode of Mycobacterium avium‐intracellulare in the past five years. She has no history of pulmonary tuberculosis or known tuberculosis contacts; her father had emphysema.

On examination, her temperature was 38.1 °C, pulse 112 beats/min, blood pressure 112/56 mm Hg, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 91% on room air. She was in mild respiratory distress with expiratory wheezes; examination of other systems was unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 13,500/µl with immature granulocytosis and no eosinophilia; the basic metabolic panel was normal. A respiratory viral PCR panel and blood and urine cultures were negative.

Chest radiographs demonstrated hyperexpansion of the lungs, bronchiectasis in the right suprahilar region and left lung base, and a right-sided aortic arch. An inspiratory noncontrast CT scan of the chest showed hyperinflation of both lungs and multiple ballooning cystic bronchiectasis distal to the third-generation bronchi bilaterally. End-expiratory CT images revealed collapse of these bronchiectatic airways. Coronal minimal-intensity-projection images in inspiration confirmed cystic bronchiectasis in the fourth- to sixth-order bronchi in the right upper and left lower lobes, with calcified hilar lymph nodes and a right-sided aortic arch; expiratory coronal images again demonstrated airway collapse. An oblique volume-rendered reconstruction showed cystic bronchiectasis in the left lower lobe.

Pulmonary-function testing revealed an obstructive pattern with progression to fixed airway obstruction and no reversibility after bronchodilator administration. Quantitative immunoglobulins (IgG, IgA, IgM, IgE), serum α-1 antitrypsin levels, and sweat chloride were all within normal limits. Sputum acid-fast bacilli smear and fungal cultures were negative. Anti–nuclear antibody, rheumatoid factor, and Aspergillus‐specific immunoglobulins were negative.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 44-year-old African American man with a family history of systemic lupus erythematosus presented with progressive fatigue, severe anemia, and night sweats over several months. He also reported photosensitivity and nonspecific arthralgias. His vital signs were stable, and physical examination was unremarkable.

Laboratory evaluation:
• He required red blood cell transfusions every 1–2 weeks for a full year.  
• Lactate dehydrogenase was mildly elevated.  
• Peripheral blood smear did not show leukoerythroblastosis.  

Bone marrow studies:
• Aspirate and core biopsy showed 95% hypercellularity with panhyperplasia and moderate reticulin fibrosis (grade 2).  
• There was a slight increase in immature precursors and megakaryocytic proliferation without atypia or dysplasia.  
• Touch preparations revealed 3% blasts.  
• Molecular testing for JAK2 (V617F and exon 12) and MPL mutations was negative.

Clinical course:
He remained transfusion-dependent for one year, then unexpectedly became transfusion-independent with normalization of hemoglobin. One year later, anemia recurred and he again became transfusion-dependent. A direct Coomb’s test was negative, and his reticulocyte count and haptoglobin level were within normal limits. He developed multiple red cell alloantibodies and was treated with dexamethasone, resulting in resolution of anemia for several months. After steroids were discontinued, anemia worsened. The absence of typical PMF-related bone marrow findings, family history of SLE, and connective tissue disease–related symptoms prompted an evaluation for SLE.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 33-year-old Chinese woman (G1P1) presented in January 2018 with 8 months of intermittent abdominal discomfort and 4 months of worsening pelvic pain and a palpable lower-abdominal mass. Her history was notable for four prior surgeries for uterine leiomyomas without morcellation (2007, 2010, 2012, and 2015), including myomectomies and a total hysterectomy.

On examination, there was an 8 × 8 cm, non-tender, firm mass in the right lower quadrant of the anterior abdomen. Routine laboratory tests, including tumor markers and sex hormones, were within normal limits.

A core-needle biopsy of the mass showed a spindle-shaped smooth-muscle cell tumor without necrosis or atypia. Immunohistochemical staining was positive for Caldesmon, smooth-muscle actin (SMA), Desmin, estrogen receptor (ER), and progesterone receptor (PR) and negative for SOX-10 and CD117; the Ki-67 index was 5%. Next-generation sequencing of 120 cancer-related genes revealed no germline variants.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis demonstrated multiple nodules involving the abdominal wall and pelvic cavity measuring 3–7 cm in diameter; the largest lesion measured approximately 78 × 66 × 40 mm. There was no evidence of distant metastasis or intravenous proliferation.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 40‐year‐old woman with a history of migraine headaches presented with fatigue, easy bruising, frequent nosebleeds, and the complaint “I am almost bleeding to death.” She was taking only an oral contraceptive. A complete blood count showed a platelet count of 12 × 10^9/L (normal, 150–425 × 10^9/L). She was diagnosed with idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura and received prednisone 60 mg per day for one week without improvement. A bone marrow biopsy revealed a hypercellular marrow with trilineage hematopoiesis but decreased megakaryocytes; erythroid and myeloid maturation were normal. She received weekly platelet infusions and prednisone; after each transfusion, the platelet count rose to ~70 × 10^9/L and fell back to ~12 × 10^9/L over 2–3 days. 

On referral, physical examination showed scattered petechiae and ecchymoses on the extremities and no hepatosplenomegaly or lymphadenopathy. A complete blood count showed a platelet count of 25 × 10^9/L and a white blood cell count of 13.9 × 10^9/L (absolute neutrophil count, 12.09 × 10^9/L; absolute lymphocyte count, 1.39 × 10^9/L). The peripheral blood smear showed rare giant platelets and no platelet clumps. A repeat bone marrow biopsy showed a normocellular marrow without dysplasia and an absence of megakaryocytes, confirmed by lack of CD61 staining.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 37-year-old woman with no prior cardiac history presented with palpitations. Twelve-lead electrocardiography during tachycardia showed a regular, narrow QRS rhythm with a long RP interval at a rate of 161 beats per minute. Intracardiac recordings revealed a tachycardia cycle length of 340 ms and the earliest atrial activation site at the His‐bundle electrogram. An 8 mg bolus of adenosine 5′-triphosphate terminated the supraventricular tachycardia without atrioventricular block. During tachycardia, ventricular burst pacing demonstrated ventriculoatrial dissociation. Differential atrial pacing from the high right atrium and proximal coronary sinus showed no ventriculoatrial linking, with a difference in postpacing VA interval of 22 ms (>14 ms). Entrainment pacing at a cycle length 30 ms shorter than the tachycardia cycle length was then performed: pacing from the 6 o’clock position at the tricuspid annulus resulted in antidromic capture of the coronary sinus and high right atrium and orthodromic capture of the earliest atrial activation site; pacing from the posterior high right atrium and proximal coronary sinus produced antidromic capture of the earliest atrial activation site, coronary sinus, and high right atrium, suggesting that the entrance to a slow conduction zone lay lateral to the earliest atrial activation site.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 13-year-old previously healthy girl presented with fever, bilateral foot pain, and a mottled purpuric rash. Over the prior 6 months, she had intermittent episodes lasting 2–3 days, improving with ibuprofen. In the weeks before presentation, she developed fatigue, hip and calf pain attributed to sports, and an episode of abdominal pain with diarrhea and emesis. She denied headaches, visual changes, oral ulcers, or other systemic symptoms. Family history was negative for autoimmune disease. 

On examination, temperature was 39.2 °C, heart rate 90 bpm, respiratory rate 20 bpm, blood pressure 114/62 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 97% on room air. She had blanchable, retiform violaceous patches with areas of true purpura on her lower extremities and duskiness of the right second toe. Cardiac, pulmonary, joint, and neurologic examinations were otherwise normal.

Laboratory studies showed elevated creatine kinase, lactate dehydrogenase, C-reactive protein, and transaminases. A skin biopsy of a purpuric lesion demonstrated subtle ischemia, vascular congestion, purpura, and focal eccrine gland necrosis without evidence of vasculitis. Direct immunofluorescence was negative. An extensive work-up—including hepatitis panel, cryoglobulins, lupus anticoagulant panel, cardiolipins, beta-2-glycoprotein, complements C3 and C4, antistreptolysin O titers, anti-DNAse B, and aldolase—was unremarkable. Abdominal and renal Doppler ultrasonography showed no abnormalities.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 57-year-old right-handed man with essential hypertension and a history of COVID-19 infection 3 weeks earlier presented with a 1-day history of acute bilateral lower extremity numbness, paresthesia, paraparesis, and urinary retention. He felt mildly headachey but denied fever, chills, respiratory, chest, abdominal, or gastrointestinal symptoms. 

On examination, he had no voluntary movement against gravity in either lower extremity; deep tendon reflexes were normal and symmetrical. Pinprick sensation was diminished bilaterally beginning one fingerbreadth below the umbilicus. Upper extremity strength, sensation, and coordination were normal. A Foley catheter was placed, and urinalysis showed no infection.

SARS-CoV-2 infection was confirmed on admission by nasal swab antigen testing and RT-PCR; lung imaging was deferred in the absence of respiratory symptoms. Initial MRI of the cervical, thoracic, and lumbosacral spine (with and without contrast) and CT and MRI of the brain showed no lesions. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis revealed protein 90 mg/dL, red blood cells 40/mcL, white blood cells 9 cells/mm³ with 14% neutrophils, and no oligoclonal bands. A comprehensive antinuclear antibody panel was negative.

He was admitted with a working diagnosis of acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy versus postinfectious radiculopathy and started on intravenous immunoglobulin, which was discontinued after development of subsegmental pulmonary emboli. He then underwent five sessions of plasmapheresis. 

On hospital day 15, repeat MRI of the cervical and thoracic spine demonstrated diffuse increased T2 signal throughout the spinal cord and multiple enhancing lesions with edema extending over most of the thoracic cord, consistent with longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis. He was treated with a 5-day course of IV methylprednisolone with slight improvement.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

options.C: Myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein antibody associated transverse myelitis

question:
A 35-year-old man presented with a 10-day history of syncope occurring each time he changed position from supine to sitting. One day before presentation he developed fever to 39 °C but denied cough or dyspnea. He was unable to sit or stand without transient loss of consciousness. In the emergency department, when he assumed an upright seated posture his heart rate initially rose to 110 bpm in sinus rhythm with maintained blood pressure; over the next 2–3 minutes his rate gradually declined to marked sinus bradycardia, at which point he yawned, experienced dizziness and visual blurring, and then lost consciousness. There was no PR-interval prolongation and no AV-conduction abnormality on ECG. His only past medical history was asthma treated with salbutamol; there was no family history of unexplained syncope or sudden cardiac death.

On examination, temperature was 39 °C, heart rate 107 bpm, blood pressure, respiratory rate, and oxygen saturation were normal, and bilateral basal crackles were noted. Arterial blood gas showed mild hypoxemia (pO2 10.1 kPa). Laboratory tests demonstrated leukopenia (3.7 Gpt/L), elevated C-reactive protein (32.9 mg/L), ferritin (1874 µg/L), procalcitonin (0.10 µg/L), and mild transaminase elevation, with normal troponin T and TSH. A nasopharyngeal swab was positive for SARS-CoV-2. Chest CT showed bilateral, patchy ground-glass opacities consistent with COVID-19.

He was admitted to the ICU. A 24-h Holter ECG demonstrated two sinus pauses up to 3.4 seconds, episodes of idioventricular rhythm, and a 35-second run of slow ventricular tachycardia. Transthoracic echocardiography showed normal ventricular size and function without structural abnormalities. A lumbar puncture to evaluate possible COVID-19-related central dysautonomia (eg, Guillain–Barré syndrome) was unremarkable.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 73-year-old man was transferred for evaluation of persistent fever despite 7 days of doxycycline for scrub typhus, diagnosed by an axillary eschar and an indirect immunofluorescence antibody titer of 1:1,280. He had no prior illnesses or medications. He lived in a rural area and worked as a farmer. On admission, his temperature was 36.9°C, pulse 72/min, respiratory rate 21/min, and blood pressure 97/54 mmHg. Examination revealed hepatosplenomegaly, a maculopapular rash on the abdomen, and a 1-cm eschar in the left axilla.  

Laboratory studies showed WBC 2,610/mm3 (41.7% neutrophils, 55.8% lymphocytes), hemoglobin 11.9 g/dL, platelets 237,000/mm3, lactate dehydrogenase 1,500 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 675 U/L, AST 245 U/L, ALT 106 U/L, total protein 6.6 g/L, albumin 2.3 g/L, γ-glutamyl transpeptidase 444 U/L, total bilirubin 0.65 mg/dL, total cholesterol 61 mg/dL, ferritin 2,000 ng/mL, fibrinogen 70 mg/dL, triglycerides 138 mg/dL, CRP 85.2 mg/L, BUN 18.4 mg/dL, creatinine 0.67 mg/dL, and uric acid 3.4 mg/dL. Serologies for HIV, cytomegalovirus, parvovirus, Hantaan virus, leptospira, hepatitis A, B, and C were negative. ANA and rheumatoid factor were negative. Blood and urine cultures were negative. A repeat O. tsutsugamushi IgG titer was 1:5,120. Abdominal CT confirmed hepatosplenomegaly.  

Azithromycin was administered for 5 days for possible doxycycline resistance. Four days after admission, the fever persisted and his condition deteriorated. Repeat labs showed WBC 1,800/mm3, hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, platelets 105,000/mm3, AST 591 U/L, and ALT 365 U/L. Bone marrow aspiration and biopsy were performed.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 37-year-old man presented with severe, localized right maxillary pain. He had no facial swelling or tenderness on examination. His history was notable for dental implants placed in the missing right maxillary premolar and molar region; although these implants were loaded, functional, and had an intact periodontium, an implant-related complication was initially suspected. 

A panoramic radiograph demonstrated severe marginal alveolar bone loss around the implants and inferior extension of the maxillary sinus floor. Adjacent maxillary sinus mucosal thickening was noted. 

The patient localized his pain to just below the right orbital rim and reported that it was severe and predominantly nocturnal. A CT scan revealed a small (3.5 mm diameter), well-defined, corticated, spherical radiolucency within the superior anterior wall of the right maxillary sinus, just below the infraorbital rim and medial to the infraorbital foramen, causing minimal bony expansion. The lesion’s CT attenuation was similar to soft tissue. 

These findings suggested a benign bone neoplasm, and an excisional biopsy was planned.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 36-year-old man presented with a 3-year history of pain and swelling on his back. Over time, he developed numbness and weakness in both lower limbs, hypoesthesia, constipation, and difficulty squatting. Neurologic examination showed bilateral lower-limb strength of 4/5 and hypoesthesia below the xiphoid level. Babinski and Romberg signs were positive. He had no prior trauma, surgery, or relevant family history.

On examination of the posterior chest, there was a deformity with a gibbous lump and a post-biopsy scar from an incisional biopsy performed 2 months earlier, after which the mass enlarged and became more painful. Tenderness was present along the midline, and hypoesthesia was noted at the T4 level; otherwise, sensation and movement were intact.

Conventional radiographs of the thoracolumbar spine showed a lytic-sclerotic expansile lesion on the right posterolateral aspect of T5–T6, with normal alignment and other vertebrae appearing unremarkable. A CT scan revealed an expansile sclerotic mass measuring 4.85 × 5.03 × 11.87 cm with a chondroid matrix, involving the right posterolateral T5–T6 vertebrae, transverse process, costovertebral junction, and ribs 5–6, with surrounding soft-tissue swelling and calcification. MRI of the thoracolumbar region showed an inhomogeneous mass with sharp margins and necrosis, extending from T4 to T8, infiltrating intradurally and intramedullary up to T4–T5, causing spinal canal stenosis and obliteration of posterior ligaments; anteriorly it bordered the aortic arch and entered the thoracic cavity.

Based on these imaging findings, the leading consideration was a primary bone tumor, with differential diagnoses of chondrosarcoma, osteosarcoma, and osteochondroma.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 25-year-old woman with a history of severe childhood abuse believed, based on cultural teachings, that marriage would heal her past wounds. She married at age 21. After one year, her husband was emotionally and physically abusive, and she underwent an abortion related to the abuse. Shortly thereafter, she experienced persistent stomach pain, severe headache, neck pain, sleeping difficulties, and profound bitterness and loss of trust in others. One month later, she developed sudden, temporary paralysis of both legs and was unable to walk. She remained fully conscious and communicated through gestures, having lost her voice.

On return to her biological family, she underwent extensive evaluations: radiography of the legs, magnetic resonance imaging of the brain, and routine blood tests (including a complete blood count), all of which were normal. She was treated with prednisolone 5 mg daily, tramadol for pain, and physiotherapy, with only minimal improvement. After two weeks of hospitalization and inpatient physiotherapy, she was discharged with the understanding that her symptoms might resolve spontaneously in the absence of identifiable neurological pathology. Her family then sought traditional healing when her condition failed to improve and subsequently worsened. She was brought back to the hospital, and, given her inability to speak, was referred to a psychotherapist for further assessment.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 60‐year‐old man with no history of liver disease, pancreatitis, or abdominal trauma was admitted for severe anemia (hemoglobin, 4.8 g/dL) after one episode of hematemesis and two episodes of melena over the prior 24 hours. He was hemodynamically unstable (pulse, 110 bpm; blood pressure, 100/50 mmHg) but afebrile, with an unremarkable abdominal examination and no stigmata of portal hypertension. Digital rectal examination revealed melanic stool. Other laboratory tests, including coagulation, liver, and renal function, were normal.

After resuscitation with transfusion and high‐dose proton‐pump inhibitor, emergent esophagogastroduodenoscopy was performed. A moderate amount of fresh blood was aspirated from the stomach. In retroflexion, the gastric fundus showed varicose‐shaped, pulsating, tortuous submucosal vessels without active bleeding, and a small erosion over one vessel suggested recent rupture. There were no endoscopic signs of portal hypertension.

To further characterize the lesion, computed tomography angiography was obtained. There was complete thrombosis of the splenic artery and tortuous arterial structures arising from the left gastric and left gastroepiploic arteries and coursing into the gastric fundus. No active contrast extravasation was seen.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 33-year-old right-handed man presented for presurgical evaluation of drug-resistant seizures. He had experienced bilateral tonic–clonic seizures at awakening beginning at age 16. After initiation of antiseizure medications, he continued to have focal hyperkinetic seizures with mild impairment of awareness, occurring 4–8 times per month despite lacosamide (600 mg/day), levetiracetam (3000 mg/day), and valproate (1000 mg/day). 

Brain magnetic resonance imaging at 1.5 T showed increased T2 and FLAIR signal intensity in the right frontal lobe “due to possible gray matter heterotopias or focal cortical dysplasia.” Long-term video-EEG monitoring with surface electrodes over three days captured five seizures lasting 16–30 seconds, localizing the epileptogenic network to the right frontal region. 

Subsequently, subdural grids, strips, and depth electrodes were implanted over the right frontal lobe to delineate the caudal boundaries of the seizure focus and to map motor cortex. After further video-EEG recording and cortical stimulation, a tailored resection of the right frontal lobe respecting the kinetic area was performed.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 66-year-old man with Gorlin syndrome and a 10-year history of daily vismodegib therapy (150 mg) for locally advanced and metastatic basal cell carcinoma presented with worsening watery diarrhea. Two years after starting vismodegib, he developed loose stools 2–3 times daily (Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events grade 1), with no interruptions in therapy. A colonoscopy after 5 years of vismodegib was unremarkable; at 9 years it showed mildly granular mucosa with loss of vascular pattern in the ascending and transverse colon, and random biopsies exhibited chronic inactive colitis without erosions, ulcers, granulomas, metaplasia, or dysplasia.

At 10 years of therapy, he had grade 2 diarrhea only partially responsive to loperamide (up to 5 doses daily). He also had alopecia, dysgeusia, and muscle spasms, although these did not correlate with diarrheal episodes. He denied abdominal pain, weight loss, or other systemic symptoms. Physical examination was unremarkable. Laboratory studies, including complete blood count, comprehensive metabolic panel with electrolytes, thyroid-stimulating hormone, tissue transglutaminase antibody, immunoglobulin A, and a multiplex stool PCR panel for bacterial and viral pathogens, were normal or negative.

A repeat colonoscopy revealed diffuse mild ileal edema and granularity with punctate erosions and exudate, as well as colonic edema, granularity, and loss of vascular pattern. Ileal biopsies showed mild villous blunting, active ileitis, chronic lamina propria inflammation, and epithelial apoptosis. Biopsies from the right colon, left colon, and rectum demonstrated crypt architectural distortion, mixed inflammation, epithelial apoptosis, and a crypt abscess. Immunostains for cytomegalovirus were negative.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 71-year-old woman presented with a 1-month history of symmetric proximal muscle weakness of the lower limbs, causing difficulty climbing stairs and rising from a seated position. She denied systemic complaints or trauma. Her medical history was notable for hypercholesterolemia treated with atorvastatin (up to 40 mg daily for 5 years, discontinued at end of 2017) and T4N0M0 ductal breast cancer treated with surgery, epirubicin, cyclophosphamide, paclitaxel, trastuzumab, and radiotherapy, with no evidence of recurrence.

On examination, muscle strength was 4/5 in both proximal lower extremities with slight left-sided amyotrophy; Gowers’s sign was positive. There were no fasciculations, reflex abnormalities, skin changes, or chest findings. Laboratory studies showed serum creatine kinase elevated 40-fold, mild transaminase elevation, elevated lactate dehydrogenase, and dyslipidemia; there was no inflammatory syndrome and renal function was normal. Antinuclear antibodies were positive with a nucleolar pattern, and anti-Th/To antibodies were detected at high titer; myositis-specific and hepatobiliary autoimmune antibodies were negative. Viral serologies for HIV, HCV, HBV, and EBV were negative.

Electroneuromyography revealed myotonic and pseudo-myotonic discharges at rest in several muscles without myogenic activity. A right quadriceps femoris muscle biopsy showed necrotic fibers with degeneration and regeneration, surrounded by macrophages, and without lymphocytic infiltrate or endomysial fibrosis. MRI could not be performed due to claustrophobia. A PET-CT scan revealed diffuse hypermetabolic uptake in skeletal muscles consistent with myositis, with no evidence of cancer recurrence, rendering paraneoplastic myositis unlikely.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 52-year-old woman presented with half-day episodes of abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting that began 3 weeks prior and recurred weekly. The pain was dull, poorly localized across the central abdomen from the epigastrium to the lower quadrants, intense enough to interrupt her daily activities, and resolved spontaneously after approximately 12 hours. She denied diarrhea, constipation, weight loss, photophobia, phonophobia, or visual auras. Her medical history included migraine since age 30 and an ectopic pregnancy; her mother and brother have migraine. She had a recent episode of infectious enteritis treated symptomatically. Initial treatment by a local physician with lansoprazole, bifidobacteria probiotic, and daikenchuto provided no relief; subsequent outpatient therapy with oxethazaine, dimeticone, and domperidone also failed, and the pain recurred multiple times over one week.  
On examination: temperature 36.6℃, blood pressure 122/73 mmHg, heart rate 74/min, respiratory rate 18/min. The abdomen was soft with mild tenderness from the epigastrium to the lower abdomen without rebound or guarding.  
Laboratory tests revealed a slight amylase elevation (157 U/L) and otherwise normal blood counts, chemistry, liver enzymes, pancreatic enzymes, and CRP. Serologic testing for Helicobacter pylori IgG was negative.  
Abdominal ultrasound, contrast-enhanced CT, and upper and lower endoscopy (performed during pain episodes except colonoscopy) showed no abnormalities.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 30-year-old man presented with a 2-day history of rapid-onset right-sided retro-orbital headache and new difficulty navigating on his left side, bumping into objects. At age 5, he underwent resection of a right parieto-occipital anaplastic astrocytoma, followed by 3D conformal external beam radiotherapy (6000 cGy in 30 fractions). His last MRI at age 15 showed post-surgical changes and giant Virchow–Robin spaces, attributed to prior radiotherapy. He had no prior history of headache, seizures, or neurologic deficits.  

On examination, he was normotensive and afebrile, alert and oriented, with no meningism. Visual testing revealed a dense left homonymous hemianopia; the remainder of the neurologic and systemic examination was normal.  

Noncontrast CT of the head showed multiple cystic lesions consistent with previous imaging. On day 3, brain MRI revealed multiple periventricular and posterior subcortical cystic lesions with surrounding nonspecific T2 hyperintensity; T1-weighted post-contrast images demonstrated no enhancement. A vasculitis screen was positive for antinuclear antibody at 1:160 but negative for extractable nuclear antigen, double-stranded DNA, p-ANCA, and c-ANCA.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 44-year-old woman with no prior medical history presented with a one‐month history of transient numbness of her right face and arm. She also reported intermittent episodes of left‐arm weakness over several years previously attributed to psychosomatic causes. On neurologic exam, plantar responses were indifferent bilaterally, and deep‐tendon reflexes were brisk. Brain MRI demonstrated multiple T2 and FLAIR hyperintense lesions in the subcortical white matter and periventricular regions, with mild linear cortical enhancement in the left parietal lobe. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis and laboratory studies for inflammatory or autoimmune disease were unremarkable. A detailed review of the MRI revealed that the lesions were predominantly located in the watershed zone between the anterior cerebral artery and middle cerebral artery territories, without restricted diffusion. Given this watershed distribution, magnetic resonance angiography of the brain was recommended to exclude a cerebrovascular etiology.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 13-year-old boy presented with a 6-month history of a rapidly enlarging, painless, diffuse swelling of the right midface and parotid region. On examination there was slight deviation of the right oral commissure, trismus, and mandibular asymmetry. There was no clinical or radiographic evidence of regional or distant metastasis. His medical history was notable for an attempted surgical excision in Honduras three months earlier.

Magnetic resonance imaging showed a 7×5 cm hyperintense mass on T1-weighted images in the right masticator space, extending into the mandibular ramus, masseter and pterygoid muscles, the parotid gland, and the parapharyngeal space. On contrasted T2-weighted sequences the lesion demonstrated a high signal intensity with a multinodular, infiltrative pattern.

An intraoral incisional biopsy was performed. Histologic examination revealed multiple, well-demarcated nodules of tumor cells immersed in abundant myxoid stroma separated by fibrous septa. The cells ranged from round to elongated forms with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and eccentrically placed round to ovoid nuclei with homogeneously distributed chromatin and inconspicuous nucleoli. Occasional spindle, epithelioid, and lipoblast-like cells were present. The mitotic rate was low (approximately two mitoses per 10 high-power fields). Periodic acid–Schiff staining, before and after diastase digestion, confirmed that the cytoplasmic granules represented glycogen.

Immunohistochemical studies showed tumor cells positive for vimentin, neuron-specific enolase, and chromogranin, and negative for AE1/AE3 cytokeratin, epithelial membrane antigen, S-100 protein, desmin, muscle-specific actin (HHF35), CD57, GLUT-1, synaptophysin, p53, p63, GFAP, and podoplanin (D2-40). The Ki-67 proliferation index was 42% in 1 000 evaluated tumor cells.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 46-year-old woman presented with a 1.5-year history of progressive weakness in her left upper extremity that had impaired her work as a hairstylist. She had a background of bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS) and cervical intervertebral disc degeneration. Ten weeks earlier, she underwent left carpal tunnel release without symptomatic improvement.

Initial EMG/NCS (October 2020) showed moderate-to-severe left CTS, mild right CTS, and mild cervical radiculopathy. On examination, she had bilateral hyperreflexia in upper and lower extremities (3+ biceps, brachioradialis, triceps, patellar, Achilles), bilateral positive Hoffman signs, and weakness more pronounced on the left (deltoid 3/5 bilaterally; biceps 4/5 right, 3/5 left; triceps 4/5 right, 3/5 left; wrist extensors/flexors 4/5 left; hand intrinsics 3/5 left). Lower extremity strength was 4/5 in tibialis anterior and extensor hallucis longus bilaterally. Sensation was intact.

Cervical MRI revealed degenerative disc disease at C5–C6 and C6–C7 with a small C6–C7 disc herniation causing moderate bilateral foraminal stenosis, worse on the left, but no central canal stenosis.

Given the combination of marked bilateral weakness (left > right), hyperreflexia, positive Hoffman signs, and fasciculations, she was referred to neurology for repeat EMG/NCS and to a neurosurgeon for surgical consideration.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 41-year-old multiparous woman (G6P5) with a history notable only for an open appendectomy at age 12 years presented in active labor. Seventeen hours after admission she had an uncomplicated vaginal delivery with 400 mL blood loss and received methylergonovine and misoprostol for uterine atony. On postpartum day 1 she tolerated oral intake, voided, and ambulated. Approximately 11 hours after delivery she developed severe, cramping, generalized abdominal pain. Vital signs were: temperature 36.8 °C, heart rate 80 bpm, blood pressure 147/80 mm Hg, respiratory rate 18/min, oxygen saturation 100% on room air, and blood glucose 98 mg/dL. Examination revealed moderate tenderness in all quadrants without rebound or guarding. Bedside abdominal ultrasound showed a thin endometrial stripe, a well-contracted uterus, and no free intraperitoneal fluid. Hemoglobin was 11.7 g/dL. She was treated conservatively with antacids, soft diet, and analgesics with initial symptomatic improvement. On postpartum day 2 she became intolerant of oral intake, with persistent pain. Electrolytes, liver function tests, amylase, and lipase were normal; white-cell count was 15.2 × 10^9/L. An abdominal radiograph showed mildly distended, gas-filled large bowel loops and absence of gas in the rectum, “possibly due to ileus or early obstruction.”

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 14-year-old Asian girl presented with occasional slight pain in her left elbow but no pain in daily activities. She was normally developed, with no relevant past medical, surgical, or family history and no history of trauma. She participated in school basketball but was not a competitive athlete.

On examination, there was no tenderness over the elbow. Vital signs were normal.

Plain radiographs of the left elbow showed a well‐defined osteolytic lesion with an osteosclerotic rim in the distal humeral epiphysis. The rim was absent at the articular periphery but continued into the subarticular bone without irregularity. There was no periosteal reaction. The right elbow appeared normal on radiographs.

Magnetic resonance imaging of the left elbow demonstrated a solid lesion adjacent to the joint cartilage. The lesion had low- to isointense signal on T1-weighted images, slightly high signal on T2-weighted fat‐suppressed images, and high signal on STIR images. There was no evidence of bone marrow edema.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 13-year-old girl presented with a 5-year history of gradually progressive diminution of vision in both eyes, associated with mild ocular pain and photophobia. She denied ocular trauma, surgery, or contact-lens use and had no family history of eye disease. She had a 7-year history of migratory joint pains with swelling; rheumatic fever had been ruled out, and she had been on treatment for over a year.

On examination, visual acuity was finger counting at 5 meters in both eyes. Slit-lamp examination revealed a circumferential peripheral corneal lesion in both eyes, characterized by an overhanging epithelial edge (more prominent in the right eye), peripheral subepithelial stromal infiltrates with scarring, and corneal thinning; there was no fluorescein staining, no vascularization crossing the area, no descemetocele, and no scleral involvement. Corneal sensations were intact; intraocular pressure and extraocular movements were normal. There was no uveitis, dry eye, or blepharitis, and the fundus examination was unremarkable.

Anterior segment optical coherence tomography showed, in both eyes, areas of thinned, hyporeflective epithelium overlying focal stromal hyperreflectivity with a sharp demarcation; these changes were more pronounced in the right eye.

Laboratory studies revealed a C-reactive protein level <6 mg/L and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 24 mm/h. Antinuclear antibody testing was positive; rheumatoid factor and anticyclic citrullinated peptide antibody were negative. Complete blood count, liver and kidney function tests, serum electrolytes, total protein, albumin, and infection screening (Mantoux, VDRL, HIV, hepatitis B and C) were all within normal limits or negative. Chest radiography and electrocardiography were unremarkable.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 16-month-old boy presented with sudden-onset difficulty in breathing for 1 day. He had a 3-day febrile illness with coryzal symptoms that resolved 2 days before presentation. There was no history of foreign-body aspiration or trauma. His birth and developmental histories were unremarkable.

On examination, he was afebrile, with moderate respiratory distress (subcostal, intercostal, and suprasternal retractions) and a respiratory rate of 50 breaths per minute. He had a loud expiratory grunt; chest expansion was reduced on the right, dull to percussion, with markedly diminished air entry over the mid and lower zones and no added sounds. Oxygen saturation was 98% on room air. Heart rate was 140 beats per minute, and blood pressure was 94/60 mmHg; heart sounds and the remainder of the examination were normal.

A chest radiograph showed a well-demarcated opacity occupying the right mid and lower hemithorax, with mediastinal shift to the left and obscuration of the right cardiac and diaphragmatic borders. Ultrasound of the chest suggested a septate right pleural effusion. Laboratory studies revealed a white-cell count of 17,000/mm³ (neutrophils 7300/mm³, lymphocytes 8100/mm³, eosinophils 300/mm³), hemoglobin 7.3 g/dL, platelets 607,000/mm³, and C-reactive protein 10.5 mg/L; electrolyte, renal, and liver tests were normal.

He was started on intravenous cefotaxime for presumed pneumonia with parapneumonic effusion. Because of the absence of fever, minimal inflammatory findings, and the atypical radiographic appearance, a contrast-enhanced CT scan of the thorax was obtained. The scan demonstrated a large, multiloculated, extrapulmonary cystic lesion occupying the right hemithorax, with leftward mediastinal shift, displacement of right-sided mediastinal structures (including the superior and inferior vena cava), narrowing of the right main pulmonary artery, and compression of the right lung; the lung parenchyma was otherwise normal.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 44-year-old man presented with a 3-month history of fever and a 2-week history of cough and dyspnea. His past medical history included cutaneous lupus erythematosus, membranous nephropathy, and a 24-pack-year smoking history. On examination, temperature was 38.0°C, blood pressure 116/51 mmHg, heart rate 97/min, respiratory rate 20/min, and oxygen saturation 97% on room air; bilateral rhonchi were noted without rash, joint swelling, or edema. Laboratory studies revealed leukopenia (WBC 3,200/μL), normal platelet count, elevated lactate dehydrogenase (1,362 IU/L), C-reactive protein (7.34 mg/dL), ferritin (10,570 ng/mL), and soluble interleukin-2 receptor (4,865 U/mL); evidence of renal and hepatic dysfunction; coagulopathy not meeting DIC criteria; elevated tumor markers CEA (8.8 ng/mL) and SLX (71.7 U/mL); antinuclear antibody with a speckled pattern (160×) and anti-ribonucleoprotein antibody (2×) positivity; and negative blood and sputum cultures, β-D-glucan, and viral serologies for Epstein–Barr virus and cytomegalovirus. Chest radiography showed diffuse reticulated shadows in the lower lobes and a left hilar mass; CT of the chest revealed diffuse bilateral ground-glass opacities and a left hilar mass without lymphadenopathy or interlobular septal thickening.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 70-year-old Sri Lankan man with a 6-year history of well-controlled diabetes mellitus and hypertension presented with acute high-grade intermittent fever, headache, arthralgia, myalgia, and nausea without an apparent focus of infection. On day 2 of illness, he developed right eyelid ptosis and dysarthria. On day 3, he noted bilateral asymmetric ptosis, dysphagia, and unsteadiness impairing gait; he denied sensory symptoms, limb weakness, or sphincter dysfunction. 

On examination (day 3), temperature was 38.5°C, heart rate 76/min, blood pressure 140/90 mmHg; general, respiratory, cardiovascular, and abdominal examinations were unremarkable. Neurologic evaluation revealed bilateral asymmetric ptosis (worse on the right), mid-dilated pupils with sluggish light reactions, complete bilateral external ophthalmoplegia without diplopia, palatal weakness, rightward tongue deviation; limb strength and sensation were normal, but tendon reflexes were absent throughout, and there was a broad-based ataxic gait with dysdiadochokinesia and dysmetria.

Laboratory studies on day 3 showed a platelet count of 106 × 10^9/L (declining to 17 × 10^9/L by day 6), a white-cell count of 4 200 × 10^9/L, AST 115 U/L, and ALT 55 U/L. Dengue NS1 antigen and RT-PCR on day 3 were positive; serum IgM antibodies to West Nile and Japanese encephalitis viruses were negative on day 7. Noncontrast CT of the head on day 4 and MRI of the brain on day 6 were normal. Nerve conduction studies revealed mild axonal polyneuropathy without a decrement on repetitive stimulation.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 56-year-old woman presented with a 1-month history of an intermittent throbbing headache in the left temporal region that had worsened at night. She also noted bilateral temporal visual field defects. Her medical history was significant only for hypertension of more than 20 years; there was no personal or family history of autoimmune disease. On examination, her temperature was 36.5 °C, blood pressure 140/108 mmHg, and neurologic examination was unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed a serum potassium of 3.2 mmol/L, microscopic proteinuria, and a urinary specific gravity of 1.025. She was negative for antinuclear antibodies, immunoglobulin G (IgG), IgM, IgA, and IgG4, and had no antithyroid antibodies. Other biochemical and coagulation tests were within normal limits. Endocrine evaluation revealed deficiencies of the hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal axis, thyroid axis, and gonadal axis. Chest CT was normal, and ECG showed flattened T waves. Magnetic resonance imaging of the pituitary gland demonstrated symmetrical enlargement with suprasellar “dumbbell” extension, significant homogeneous enhancement after gadolinium, low signal in the anterior pituitary on T1, high signal on T2, a preserved bright spot in the posterior lobe, thickening of an undeviated pituitary stalk, bilateral cavernous sinus encirclement of the internal carotid arteries, and a parasellar dural tail sign.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 74-year-old Asian woman presented with several months of right knee pain without antecedent trauma. Her medical history was notable for papillary thyroid carcinoma treated by resection 5 years earlier, with no recurrence. On examination, there was mild tenderness over the medial femoral condyle without erythema or warmth; the range of motion was slightly reduced.

Radiographs of the right knee demonstrated a 4.0 × 4.5 cm osteolytic lesion within the medial femoral condyle without cortical breach or periosteal reaction. MRI showed the lesion to be hypointense on T1-weighted images, low-to-intermediate signal on T2-weighted images, and avidly enhancing after gadolinium; there was no extraosseous extension.

An open biopsy revealed clusters of oval to spindle-shaped epithelial cells with squamous differentiation, set within a bland fibrous stroma. The epithelial nuclei were uniform with a low mitotic rate, and reactive bone formation surrounded the clusters.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A woman in her late 70s presented with a 2-week history of progressive bilateral leg weakness, unstable gait, and fluctuating drowsiness with moments of confusion. This followed a 4-week history of progressive bilateral lumbosacral herpes zoster infection that spread to her right groin and right leg. Her medical history included primary biliary cirrhosis with two liver transplants, immunosuppression with tacrolimus, post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder in remission, and chronic pancreatitis.

On examination, she was drowsy but could converse and obey simple commands; when verbal stimulus ceased, she fell asleep, indicating hypersomnolence. Cranial-nerve evaluation revealed bilateral upward gaze palsy and horizontal nystagmus on smooth pursuit. Deep-tendon reflexes were absent, plantar responses were extensor bilaterally, and there was a symmetrical flaccid paraparesis (2/5) of the lower limbs with preserved tone and power in the upper limbs. Sensory testing showed a non-painful glove-and-stocking paraesthesia; otherwise, sensation was intact. There was no history of foreign travel or tick exposure.

Laboratory studies showed a normal complete blood count, liver and renal function, and undetectable SARS-CoV-2 RNA; tacrolimus level was 3.3 µg/L (reference 5.0–15.0). Serum anti-mitochondrial M2, anti-M2-3E, and anti-Ro52 antibodies were strongly positive. MRI of the brain and spinal cord with and without contrast was unremarkable. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis revealed protein 0.37 g/L, glucose 3.2 mmol/L, and zero white cells; viral PCR for herpes simplex virus types 1 and 2, varicella zoster virus, enterovirus, and adenovirus was negative. Nerve conduction studies at 8 weeks after symptom onset showed no demyelinating or axonal features. Serum anti-ganglioside testing was positive for anti-GQ1b IgM and negative for anti-GM1 and anti-GD1b subtypes.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 56-year-old man with a 12-year history of IgA lambda multiple myeloma, currently on daratumumab and dexamethasone, presented with sudden moderate vision loss in his right eye for one day. He denied diabetes, uncontrolled hypertension, prior radiation, or carotid disease. Recent laboratory evaluation showed an elevated IgA monoclonal peak.  
On examination, best corrected visual acuity was 20/25 in the right eye and 20/400 in the left eye. Anterior segment examination was unremarkable in the right eye; the left eye had a known central corneal scar. No intraocular inflammation was present.  
Fundus examination of the right eye revealed a discrete vascular irregularity in the perifoveal region, seen as a red dot on binocular ophthalmoscopy, without hemorrhages, cotton-wool spots, or arteriovenous changes.  
Fluorescein angiography demonstrated an isolated perifoveal aneurysm with mild late leakage and no evidence of neovascularization. A few peripheral microaneurysms were noted bilaterally.  
Spectral-domain OCT of the right macula showed a large oval lesion spanning the inner nuclear and outer plexiform layers, with a hyperreflective wall and internal reflective material, associated with cystic intraretinal fluid; the outer retinal layers were preserved. OCT of the left eye was normal.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 32-year-old woman, 13 weeks postpartum with no prior medical history, presented with a 6-week history of an intensely pruritic rash, daily high-grade fevers, and worsening pain in her wrists and fingers. Four weeks earlier, she had been treated for group A streptococcal pharyngitis without improvement, was hospitalized for presumed acute rheumatic fever and Moraxella bacteremia, received 11 days of IV ceftriaxone with transient hemodynamic stabilization, but left against medical advice due to persistent symptoms.

On arrival, she was afebrile but tachycardic to 114 beats/min; blood pressure was normal. Examination revealed a faint, diffuse maculopapular rash over the trunk and extremities (spared palms and soles) with desquamation, plus prominent linear erythematous plaques on the upper back. Both wrists and proximal interphalangeal joints were swollen and tender. Abdominal examination was benign.

Laboratory studies showed WBC 11,000/μL (ANC 6.3×10^3/μL), hemoglobin 8.6 g/dL, platelets 115×10^3/μL; AST 535 U/L, ALT 161 U/L, alk phos 986 U/L, total bilirubin 2.1 mg/dL; CRP 101.9 mg/L, ESR 58 mm/h. Coagulation tests revealed D-dimer 57,547 ng/mL, INR 1.4, aPTT 23.5 s, fibrinogen 81 mg/dL. Ferritin was > 15,000 ng/mL; soluble IL-2 receptor and IL-18 were > 4000 pg/mL and 11,000 pg/mL, respectively. ANA, ANCA, and rheumatoid factor were negative. 

A HIDA scan was negative for cholecystitis. Chest CT showed enlarged hilar lymph nodes without infiltrates. TEE revealed no valvular vegetations. Viral hepatitis panel, HIV, EBV, HSV, RSV, SARS-CoV-2, and QuantiFERON assays were all negative. Blood and urine cultures yielded no growth.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

options.B: Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms

question:
A 25-year-old North African man with a 3-year history of schizophrenia, deemed treatment-resistant after inadequate responses to risperidone, haloperidol, and haloperidol plus olanzapine, was started on clozapine 300 mg daily in March 2022, along with electroconvulsive therapy. Pre-clozapine evaluation, including a complete blood count (leukocytes 9110/mm3, eosinophils 0/mm3), serum electrolytes, and electrocardiogram, was unremarkable. After rapid psychiatric improvement, he was discharged on clozapine with routine hematological monitoring.

Four months after clozapine initiation, he developed a generalized rash of erythematous, pinpoint papules with intense pruritus that appeared after any episode of heat exposure or exercise-induced sweating and resolved within an hour at rest. He was taking no other medications and denied environmental exposures; photosensitivity was considered but lesions occurred during evening hours. Treatment with cetirizine 10 mg daily had no effect. Reduction of clozapine to 150 mg daily failed to prevent recurrence. Switching to haloperidol led to complete resolution of the rash but insufficient psychiatric control. One month after initiating olanzapine 20 mg daily, his psychotic symptoms remitted but the identical cutaneous reaction recurred.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

An 8-year-old girl presented with a 10-day history of left-sided otalgia, otorrhea, and ipsilateral hearing loss. On the day of admission she noted binocular diplopia and strabismus. She had taken paracetamol and ibuprofen regularly, had a single day of fever on day 4 of her illness, and vomited the night before presentation without headache or ocular pain. Her past medical history was unremarkable.

On examination her temperature was 38.0°C. Otoscopic examination showed a suppurative left-sided acute otitis media. Tuning-fork testing demonstrated bilateral conductive hearing loss. Neurologic exam was notable for an ipsilateral abducens nerve palsy; trigeminal sensation was intact, there were no other focal deficits, and meningeal signs were absent.

Laboratory studies revealed a white blood cell count of 26,200/µL with 93.4% neutrophils and a C-reactive protein level of 88.8 mg/L. Renal function and coagulation studies were within normal limits.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the head demonstrated left mastoiditis, left petrous apicitis (apical petrositis), ipsilateral septic cavernous sinus thrombosis, and infectious arteritis with stenosis of the internal carotid artery. MRI confirmed diffuse enhancement of the left mastoid air cells and petrous apex with extraosseous extension to the adjacent dura, approximately 50% stenosis of the petrous, lacerum, and cavernous segments of the internal carotid artery, and enhancement and swelling of the cavernous sinus.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 45-year-old woman underwent atraumatic extraction of a fractured maxillary left lateral incisor and immediate implant placement with guided bone regeneration. Preoperative CBCT imaging was performed. A 3.5×10 mm implant achieved 35 N cm of primary stability; no excessive intraoperative bleeding was noted. Within hours, she reported pain and paresthesia of the left maxilla. During the first postoperative week, the pain was mild and responsive to ibuprofen. In the second week, pain became severe, continuous, and unrelieved by analgesics. She described burning in the left canine region, spreading to the palate, nasal area, and occipital scalp. Extraoral and intraoral examinations were unremarkable, with no signs of infection (no fever, exudate, or bleeding). A neurologic etiology was suspected. Palatal, incisive, and infraorbital nerve blocks were performed sequentially; only the infraorbital block provided relief.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 2-year-old boy was hospitalized at an outside institution for acute pancreatitis after 2 days of fever, anorexia, abdominal pain, and vomiting. His serum lipase was 2754 U/L. Abdominal MRI showed a 3-cm lesion in the pancreatic head that was connected to the main pancreatic duct with mild ductal dilation and distorted ductal anatomy; there was no internal enhancement, and the remainder of the pancreas appeared normal. He was managed supportively and discharged with a diagnosis of acute pancreatitis with an acute necrotic collection.

Six months later, he was readmitted with another episode of acute pancreatitis (lipase 242 U/L). Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 15.9 × 10^3/µL with neutrophil predominance, a platelet count of 120 × 10^3/µL, hemoglobin of 10.7 g/dL, and a C-reactive protein level of 10 mg/dL. MRCP demonstrated enlargement of the pancreatic head lesion with compression of the portal vein, along with signs of portal hypertension including gastric varices and splenomegaly. The differential diagnosis included duplication cyst, pseudocyst, pancreatic tumor, congenital pancreatic cyst, and vascular changes secondary to underlying connective tissue disease or vasculitis.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 41-year-old woman with hypertension, lupus nephritis, antiphospholipid antibody syndrome, coronary artery disease, and prior Libman-Sacks endocarditis (status post coronary artery bypass graft and aortic valve replacement) presented with one week of lightheadedness and two days of recurrent nausea, vomiting, and poor oral intake. She also noted two days of right calf pain only when ambulating. She denied chest pain, back pain, or abdominal pain. Her medications included warfarin, and she required hemodialysis. 

On examination, blood pressure was 171/91 mm Hg, heart rate 92 beats/min, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, temperature 37.1 °C, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. She appeared comfortable, alert, and oriented. Neurologic examination was normal. Lungs were clear, and cardiac examination revealed regular rate and rhythm without murmur. The abdomen was soft, non-tender, and non-distended. Lower extremities were warm and well perfused, with normal range of motion, no swelling or calf tenderness, and symmetric palpable peripheral pulses.

Initial working diagnoses included an acute viral process or foodborne illness. However, given her right calf pain in the context of a chronic procoagulant state, a point-of-care ultrasound two-point compression examination of the right leg was performed.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 26-year-old woman was admitted with a 3-week history of fever and painful right-sided neck swelling. She first noted a painful right cervical node, then developed fever up to 40 °C, nausea, and fatigue despite courses of amoxicillin–clavulanate, metronidazole, clindamycin, and naproxen. She had no comorbidities, recent travel, or relevant family history. On examination, she was hemodynamically stable and had three tender enlarged lymph nodes on the right side of her neck; the overlying skin was normal. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL, white blood cell count 6.4 × 10^3/mL, C-reactive protein 77 mg/L, erythrocyte sedimentation rate 42 mm/hr, and lactate dehydrogenase 519 U/L. Blood and urine cultures showed no growth, and a tuberculin skin test was negative. Serologic tests demonstrated evidence of past Epstein–Barr virus infection but were negative for HIV, hepatitis B and C, toxoplasmosis, VDRL, ANA, and rheumatoid factor. Chest radiography and CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis were unremarkable. Ultrasound and CT of the neck revealed multiple lymph nodes bilaterally extending to the base of the neck and intraparotid region; on the right, posterior-triangle nodes measured up to 18 × 14 mm with rim enhancement and central hypodensity, and on the left, nodes up to 12 × 5 mm appeared reactive. An ultrasound-guided core needle biopsy of an affected lymph node was performed.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 66-year-old Caucasian woman with a 15-year history of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus presented with a 3-week history of generalized malaise, poor oral intake, and intermittent diarrhea. Her medical history included hypertension, asthma, and depression. Medications were metformin 3 g daily, modified-release gliclazide 60 mg daily, aspirin 100 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, ramipril 10 mg daily, and hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily.

Prehospital vital signs: Glasgow Coma Scale score 15, blood glucose 2.8 mmol/L, blood pressure 90/40 mmHg, pulse 54 beats/min, respiratory rate 32 breaths/min, SpO2 98% on 8 L oxygen via mask. On arrival, she was confused (GCS 14) with similar hemodynamics.

Laboratory studies: Na 140 mmol/L, K 7.3 mmol/L, Cl 91 mmol/L, HCO3 1 mmol/L, anion gap 55 mmol/L, glucose 2.3 mmol/L, urea 30.8 mmol/L, creatinine 768 µmol/L; normal liver function tests; troponin 50 ng/L. Venous blood gas: pH 6.58, PCO2 38.6 mmHg, HCO3 3.6 mmol/L, glucose 2.0 mmol/L, lactate 16.7 mmol/L.

Initial management included 2 L of 0.9% saline, calcium gluconate, insulin with dextrose, and intravenous bicarbonate. Minutes later, she suffered a pulseless electrical activity arrest with 25 minutes of downtime. She was intubated, ventilated, and required vasopressor support after return of spontaneous circulation. Chest radiograph, electrocardiogram, and toxicology screen were unremarkable.

With a presumed diagnosis of ischemic bowel based on vague abdominal symptoms and profound lactatemia, she was admitted to the ICU for preoperative optimization. She was sedated and ventilated (FiO2 0.40, peak inspiratory pressure <25 cm H2O), received 10 L of crystalloid (CVP from 8 to 16 cmH2O), and high‐dose noradrenaline and adrenaline. Continuous veno-venous hemodiafiltration was initiated at 50 mL/kg/h, and broad-spectrum antibiotics were started. Twenty-six hours after admission, she underwent exploratory laparotomy, which revealed no significant findings.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 33-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus diagnosed one month earlier (photosensitive rash, arthritis, anemia, lymphocytopenia, albuminuria, hematuria, positive ANA) was admitted for a tonic seizure. Since diagnosis, she had been adherent to prednisone (1 mg/kg/day), hydroxychloroquine, and antihypertensives. She reported no prior headaches, cognitive changes, or seizures, but noted poor sleep over the past week.

On admission, her blood pressure was 160/100 mmHg; other vital signs were normal. Examination showed moon facies, facial acneiform lesions, supraclavicular fullness, grade 1 bipedal edema, and left shoulder tenderness. Neurologic examination was unremarkable.

Laboratory studies revealed microcytic hypochromic anemia (Hb 99 g/L), thrombocytopenia (platelets 100×10^9/L), positive direct and indirect Coombs tests, normal creatinine and electrolytes, and urinalysis with +3 proteinuria, mild pyuria, and coarse granular casts (24 h urine protein 4.47 g). Initial assessment was neuropsychiatric SLE flare with renal involvement and hypertension; steroids were switched to IV hydrocortisone, mycophenolate mofetil was initiated, and antihypertensives were continued.

On hospital day 2, she developed sudden holocranial headache, confusion, and bilateral total vision loss (after peripheral blurring), with blood pressure 190/110 mmHg. She was disoriented; cranial nerve exam revealed no light perception bilaterally; strength, reflexes, and sensation were normal.

A contrast-enhanced cranial CT scan showed an acute parenchymal hemorrhage in the right occipital lobe (18.4 cc) extending into the right parietal lobe, with surrounding cerebral edema, subfalcine herniation, and early uncal herniation.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 16-year-old girl presented with a one-week history of persistent low-grade fever and slight cough treated with ibuprofen. A general practitioner noted an increase in abdominal girth from a giant palpable mass and referred her urgently. She reported progressive abdominal enlargement for one year, associated with intermittent mild pain; her menstrual cycles were normal. On examination, she was afebrile; there was a giant, tense, relatively mobile abdominal mass with a regular surface, an umbilical hernia, and a brown-black lesion on the right thorax. 

Abdomino-pelvic ultrasound showed a thin-walled, multilocular cystic mass occupying nearly the entire abdomen and pelvis, with transonic fluid and multiple septa; the ovaries were not visualized, and there was no free fluid. MRI demonstrated a well-defined 115 × 224 × 350 mm cystic tumor originating from the right ovary, multiloculated, containing some T1-hyperintense loculi, with moderate mural and septal enhancement after gadolinium; the suggested diagnosis was ovarian cystadenoma.

Laboratory results revealed hemoglobin 10.1 g/dL, hematocrit 29.4%, platelets 656 × 10^3/µL, CRP 26.45 mg/dL, D-dimers 1.21 mg/L, ALP 213 U/L, ferritin 480 ng/mL, iron 28 µg/dL, AST 70 U/L, ALT 77 U/L, GGT 353 U/L. Endocrine studies, including thyroid function, were normal. Tumor markers CEA, AFP, β HCG, LDH, and CA 19-9 were normal; CA 125 was elevated at 124.7 U/mL. Urine culture and nasal and pharyngeal swabs were negative, and chest radiography was normal.

On hospital day 3, she developed high-grade fever (up to 39.5 °C) unresponsive to intravenous antibiotics and antipyretics, without localizing symptoms. Otorhinolaryngeal, respiratory, digestive, urinary, and cutaneous sources were excluded; blood cultures and SARS-CoV-2 testing were negative. Intracystic hemorrhage or infection was suspected, but abdominal ultrasound showed unchanged cyst contents. A pediatric oncology consultation raised concern for paraneoplastic syndrome and sepsis, and she received meropenem, gentamicin, and metronidazole; her temperature normalized after 2 days. Given a presumptive diagnosis of serous or mucinous cystadenoma, surgical removal of the cyst was planned.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 4-year-old Egyptian girl was referred for evaluation of bilateral breast budding and pubic hair of 4 months’ duration, followed by two episodes of vaginal spotting over the preceding 2 weeks. Her medical and birth histories were unremarkable, with no family history of endocrinologic disorders. She was alert, irritable, 115 cm tall (>97th percentile), and weighed 20 kg (90th percentile). Vital signs were normal. Examination revealed Tanner stage III breast development, Tanner stage III pubic hair, estrogenized vulvar mucosa with clear discharge, slight clitoral enlargement, and a firm, ill-defined, nontender suprapubic mass without ascites.  
Abdominal ultrasonography showed an enlarged uterus with a prominent endometrium and a 7.8 × 5.2 cm irregular, inhomogeneous right adnexal mass crossing the midline; the left ovary and other abdominal organs were normal. CT of the abdomen confirmed a large right ovarian mass. Brain MRI showed a normal-sized pituitary and infundibulum with a normal posterior pituitary bright spot. Bone age was 5 years on left hand/wrist radiograph.  
Laboratory studies revealed elevated estradiol (280–375 pmol/L; normal <73), progesterone (5.3 nmol/L; normal 0–1.3), testosterone (38.9 pg/mL; normal 1–5.2), and androstenedione (4.1 ng/mL; normal 0.3–3.1); basal LH and FSH were low (0.4 and 0.7 IU/L) with no response to LHRH stimulation. Total inhibin was 1,069 U/L (normal <100), alpha-fetoprotein 987 μg/L (normal 1–200), and β-hCG, LDH, and ALP were negative. Karyotype was 46,XX.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 58-year-old Japanese man presented with a three-month history of a painless, swollen mass in the right submandibular region. On examination, the right submandibular gland was enlarged, elastic-hard, nonfixed, with several enlarged, elastic-hard, movable lymph nodes. Salivary flow from the right submandibular gland was poor. His medical history was notable only for recurrent gastric ulcer disease. Laboratory studies were normal except for: lactate dehydrogenase 214 U/L (normal 105–210), γ-glutamyl transpeptidase 88 U/L (11–80), triglycerides 199 mg/dL (42–168), leucine aminopeptidase 83 U/L (19–69), and serum IgG 924 mg/dL (870–1700). With consent, serum IgG4 was measured at 16.9 mg/dL (4.8–105); other immunoglobulin subclasses and autoantibodies were not evaluated. Contrast-enhanced CT of the neck showed a 2.8 × 2.8 cm homogeneously hyperdense, enhancing mass in the right submandibular region with invasive extension into adjacent tissues and several enhancing lymph nodes. MRI revealed a 3.0 × 3.0 × 4.0 cm mass that was hypointense on fat-saturated T1-weighted images, heterogeneous hyperintense on gadolinium-enhanced T2-weighted images, and mid-hyperintense on fat-saturated T2 images; the submandibular lymph nodes were also hyperintense. PET scan demonstrated intense uptake only in the right submandibular region, with no other lesions. The clinical and radiological appearance suggested malignant neoplasm of the submandibular gland with metastatic spread. An excisional biopsy of a level II lymph node showed reactive follicular hyperplasia, and flow cytometry revealed polyclonal B-cell populations (kappa 40.1%, lambda 27.1%).

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 66-year-old man with familial dilated cardiomyopathy and a CRT-D device presented with transient faintness and palpitations. His history included juvenile sick sinus syndrome treated with a permanent pacemaker at age 25; his son died of juvenile dilated cardiomyopathy. On examination, pulse was 90 beats/min and systolic blood pressure 102 mm Hg. A 12-lead ECG showed a regular wide QRS rhythm at 90 beats/min, QRS duration 200 ms with a right bundle branch block pattern, marked left axis deviation, and no obvious P waves. Device interrogation revealed ventricular sensing without CRT pacing at a V-V interval of 700 ms, which was shorter than the regular right atrial rhythm. The CRT-D did not classify this as ventricular tachyarrhythmia because its detection zone was programmed for rates over 150 beats/min. From these observations, the initial speculation was that this was an accelerated idioventricular rhythm with ventriculoatrial dissociation, and catheter ablation was planned to restore effective CRT pacing.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 54-year-old woman was referred by her dentist for sudden onset of pain in the right mandible and numbness of the right chin. Her medical history was notable for an early breast carcinoma diagnosed in 2008 (pT1a N1a, estrogen- and progesterone-receptor positive, HER-2 negative) treated with wide excision, axillary lymphadenectomy, adjuvant chemotherapy (3 cycles of FEC-100 and docetaxel), radiotherapy, and subsequent letrozole (2 years) and tamoxifen (5 years). In the same year, she underwent a total nephrectomy for a stage T1a clear-cell renal carcinoma. In October 2015, pulmonary and osteoclastic bone metastases from breast cancer were identified, and treatment with monthly zoledronic acid began in January 2016; she also received exemestane followed by fulvestrant after disease progression. 

In June 2016, she noted right mandibular pain and chin numbness, without involvement of the lower lip. Examination revealed no cervical lymphadenopathy. Intraoral inspection showed a firm bony elevation in the region of the right first and second premolars; the lower right second molar was tender to percussion but pulp sensibility was intact. A panoramic radiograph was unremarkable. Cone-beam CT and PET-CT were obtained; the PET-CT demonstrated bony radiolucent areas in the right mandibular ramus suggestive of osteoclastic lesions. Skeletal scintigraphy revealed diffuse metastases in the spine, ribs, pelvic bones, sacrum, proximal femur, and right mandibular ramus. MRI of the head was performed to evaluate possible trigeminal involvement and showed a small lesion in the right cavernous sinus, interpreted as a hemangioma or leiomyoma, with no apparent role in the patient’s neuropathy.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 25-year-old man with a 5-year history of adult‐onset Still’s disease, maintained on anakinra (100 mg/day) and prednisolone (5 mg/day), presented to an outside hospital with a 3-day history of high-spiking fevers, sore throat, and arthralgias. He developed acute kidney injury and liver dysfunction and was transferred to the ICU with suspected sepsis. His immunosuppressive therapy was stopped, and empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics were started. 

Laboratory studies showed:
- Anemia and thrombocytopenia  
- Markedly elevated ferritin and C-reactive protein levels  
- Hypertriglyceridemia and hypofibrinogenemia  
- Elevated soluble interleukin-2 receptor level  
- Splenomegaly on imaging  

A bone marrow biopsy demonstrated hemophagocytosis, and treatment with etoposide and dexamethasone (HLH-94 protocol) was initiated. After one week, he redeveloped high fevers, pancytopenia, and rising inflammatory markers. On transfer to our hospital, examination revealed cervical lymphadenopathy and severe tonsillopharyngitis. Abdominal CT showed hepatosplenomegaly and cervical and mesenteric lymphadenopathy.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 46-year-old perimenopausal woman presented with a 4-month history of right axillary swelling that was insidious, gradually progressive, and associated with pain, foul-smelling discharge, and intermittent bleeding.
