Three months earlier, fine needle aspiration cytology of the axillary mass revealed metastatic carcinoma favoring adenocarcinoma, and she received three cycles of epirubicin and cyclophosphamide without response; the mass enlarged with increased bleeding and ulceration. She had no medical comorbidities or family history of malignancy. Menarche was at age 14, with regular cycles; she had two children (first birth at age 22). 

On examination, her performance status was ECOG 1; vital signs were stable, and pallor was present. Both breasts and the left axilla were unremarkable. In the right axilla there was a 7 cm × 6 cm ulceroproliferative, firm, tender mass with limited mobility, overlying ulceration, and bleeding. A single 2-cm, mobile, firm, nontender level I axillary lymph node was palpable. Other systems were normal. Laboratory studies showed a hemoglobin level of 6 g/dL. Review of the prior cytology confirmed metastatic adenocarcinoma. 

Whole-body FDG-PET showed a large lobulated, heterogeneously enhancing soft tissue mass in the right axillary tail (8.8 × 9.5 × 7 cm; SUV max 27.9) infiltrating skin and subcutaneous tissue of the proximal arm, with FDG-avid right axillary and subpectoral lymph nodes (largest 2.4 × 1.4 cm; SUV max 13.6). Both breasts appeared normal, and no other FDG-avid lesions were identified. 

Contrast-enhanced MRI of the breast and axilla revealed a well-defined, lobulated, heterogeneously enhancing hyperintense lesion (10.6 × 9.7 × 7.6 cm) epicentered in the right axillary recess, probably arising from the axillary tail of the right breast, closely abutting the lateral chest wall muscles and axillary vessels, with a few enlarged axillary nodes; both breasts were unremarkable.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

options.A: Occult breast cancer with axillary lymph node metastasis
options.B: Carcinoma of the axillary tail of Spence

question:
A 24-year-old Southeast Asian woman presented with a 2-month history of pruritic blisters and bullae initially on her hands that gradually involved her face and trunk. She also reported weight loss, myalgia, fatigue, intermittent fever, self-limiting epistaxis, and hair loss. She denied dyspnea, hematuria, decreased urine output, muscle weakness, joint pain, or new medications.

On examination, temperature and respiratory rate were normal; pulse was 112 beats per minute; blood pressure was 80/60 mmHg; oxygen saturation was 96% on room air. Findings included non-cicatricial hair loss, pallor, a malar rash sparing the nasolabial folds, and discoid lesions on the ears. Numerous tense, clear vesicles and bullae on an erythematous base involved her face, nape of neck, trunk, and hands. Superficial erosions were noted on the buccal mucosa, hard palate, and lips. The Nikolsky sign and bulla spread sign were positive; the remainder of the exam was unremarkable.

Initial laboratory studies revealed a white-cell count of 2600/mm³, hemoglobin 8.1 g/dL, aspartate aminotransferase 194 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 53 U/L, albumin 2.9 g/L, active urinary sediments with white cells and albumin, and proteinuria of 0.42 g/24 h. Peripheral smear showed microcytic hypochromic anemia with pencil and teardrop cells. Antinuclear antibody testing was positive; erythrocyte sedimentation rate was 18 mm/h; C-reactive protein was 1.8 mg/L (normal 0–4.8); and complement levels were slightly decreased.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 72-year-old man was admitted with a 1-day history of right flank pain and massive gross hematuria with clot retention. He was a heavy smoker and denied any history of trauma, recent medical intervention, known urinary stones, bleeding disorders, or use of anticoagulants. On examination, his blood pressure was normal. Laboratory studies showed a normal complete blood count, normal serum biochemistry, and normal coagulation parameters. A rinsing urinary catheter was placed, and the hematuria resolved over the next few days. Ultrasonography of the kidneys and bladder was unremarkable. 

Subsequently, contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen showed a small soft-tissue filling defect within the right renal pelvis that did not significantly enhance in the arterial phase and was not associated with dilation of the collecting system. There was no evidence of calculi. 

Urine cytology was negative for malignant cells. Cystoscopy with placement of a right ureteral catheter was performed to obtain selective upper-tract urine cytology and to perform a retrograde pyelogram. Upper-tract cytology was negative, and the retrograde study revealed no filling defect in the right renal pelvis or calyces.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 5-year-old Caucasian boy was referred for pediatric ophthalmology evaluation after magnetic resonance imaging of the brain—performed as part of an endocrine work-up for growth deficiency—revealed abnormalities of the optic pathways. 

His medical history was remarkable for:  
• Right unilateral cleft lip and palate (repaired, with subsequent rhinoplasty)  
• Anal atresia  
• Vesicoureteral reflux  
• Hypospadias  
• Presence of an 11th pair of ribs  
• Growth hormone deficiency and hypothyroidism  
• Pes planus  
• Transsphenoidal encephalocele  

Family history was noncontributory.  

Ophthalmic examination revealed:  
• Visual acuity 20/250 in the right eye and 20/150 in the left eye  
• Restricted extraocular movements of the right eye, with an upshoot on left gaze  
• A small-amplitude, high-frequency pendular horizontal nystagmus with a superimposed right-beating component  
• A right relative afferent pupillary defect  
• A right exotropia of 35 prism diopters at near and 30 prism diopters at distance  
• Slit-lamp examination showing an inferior iris coloboma in the right eye  
• Posterior segment examination demonstrating a large retinochoroidal and optic nerve coloboma in the right eye; the left eye was structurally normal  

Chromosome microarray analysis showed a small deletion at 6p23 of uncertain significance.  

Magnetic resonance imaging of the orbits and brain demonstrated hypoplasia of both optic nerves with absence of the optic chiasm (achiasma) and a sphenonasopharyngeal/transsphenoid basal cephalocele containing meninges and sellar contents, with thin flattened enhancing pituitary tissue along the cephalocele floor.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 21-year-old white woman presented with a 2-week history of arthralgias and myalgias involving wrists, ankles, and knees bilaterally. During the past week, she noted daily fevers up to 102 °F and an almost simultaneous salmon-colored rash on her trunk, arms, and legs, accompanied by sore throat, nausea, and vomiting. She reported hiking in North Georgia in March and removing a tick from her leg and pubis three days before the onset of joint symptoms; no local rash was noted at the bite sites.  
Her past medical history was unremarkable. Medications included metronidazole, acetaminophen, and diphenhydramine. She had a penicillin allergy described as causing a “bad rash.”  
On examination, blood pressure was 142/72 mm Hg, pulse 81 beats/min, respirations 18 breaths/min, and temperature 102 °F. Oral and pharyngeal mucosa were erythematous without exudate; mild conjunctival injection and cervical lymphadenopathy were present. The abdomen was tender with a mildly enlarged spleen. Multiple joints were tender to palpation and passive motion. A salmon-colored, raised maculopapular rash involved the trunk and extremities.  
Laboratory data revealed a WBC count of 13.93 × 10^3/mm^3 with 83% neutrophils; hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL; platelets 233 × 10^3/mm^3; AST 134 U/L; ALT 156 U/L; LDH 430 U/L; CRP 21.1 mg/dL; ESR 119 mm/hr. ANA and rheumatoid factor were negative. Blood, urine, and vaginal/rectal cultures were negative. HIV test was negative. Rocky Mountain spotted fever titers were 1:16 on admission and rose to 1:64. Serologies for Mycoplasma and Chlamydia were negative.  
CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed moderate splenomegaly and mesenteric adenitis.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 73-year-old woman presented with a 4-month history of a painless swelling on the tongue that had gradually increased in size. She denied dysphagia, odynophagia, speech changes, or breathing difficulty. Her medical history was notable for well-controlled hyperthyroidism. Examination revealed stable vital signs and no lymphadenopathy. Intraorally, there was a 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm pale-pink, round, soft-to-firm, sessile, nonpulsatile, nontender, slightly mobile nodule with intact mucosa on the dorsum of the tongue near the left lateral border. Oral hygiene was good, with a broken lower molar and sharp opposing cusp noted. Routine blood tests were within normal limits. Based on the clinical presentation, a benign fibrous lesion was suspected, and a broad differential diagnosis was considered. After informed consent, an excisional biopsy under local anesthesia was performed.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 28-year-old man with a history of nodular lymphocyte-predominant Hodgkin lymphoma (stage IA treated with radiotherapy five years earlier) presented with three weeks of fatigue, jaundice, and intermittent epigastric pain. He denied fevers, sweats, weight loss, alcohol excess, toxin or mushroom ingestion, intravenous drug use, recent travel, or new medications apart from an over-the-counter multivitamin. There was no family history of liver disease.

On examination, he was alert and oriented but visibly jaundiced, with mild epigastric tenderness, ecchymoses, and no stigmata of chronic liver disease or asterixis. There was no lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, or ascites. His BMI was 28.7 kg/m^2.

Laboratory studies showed: total bilirubin 156 μmol/L (normal <20), direct bilirubin 100 μmol/L (<4), ALT 2190 U/L (≤45), AST 1750 U/L (≤35), ALP 143 U/L (30–110), GGT 214 U/L (<55), albumin 36 g/L (35–50), INR 1.9 (0.9–1.2), hypoglycemia (<3 mmol/L), ferritin 3380 μg/L (40–440), CRP 8.4 mg/L (<5), globulins 26 g/L (25–45). Complete blood count was unremarkable. Viral serologies for hepatitis A, B, C, HIV, EBV, CMV, and HSV were negative. Antinuclear, smooth-muscle, antimitochondrial, and anti–liver/kidney microsomal antibodies were all negative. Alpha-1-antitrypsin, ceruloplasmin, and copper studies were normal. Peripheral blood flow cytometry showed no monoclonal B-cell population.

Abdominal ultrasound showed a heterogeneous liver (span 14 cm), gallbladder wall thickening without cholelithiasis or biliary dilation, patent portal vein, nonvisualized hepatic veins, and splenomegaly (15 cm). Contrast CT of neck, chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed heterogeneous hepatic enhancement without discrete lesions, multiple enlarged upper abdominal lymph nodes, and splenic lesions; portal and hepatic veins were patent though the left hepatic vein was poorly visualized. There was no biliary obstruction and echocardiography showed normal cardiac function.

Given the acute hepatocellular pattern with coagulopathy, hypoglycemia, and negative noninvasive workup, the leading considerations were lymphomatous infiltration of the liver, Budd–Chiari syndrome, and autoimmune-mediated hepatitis. On hospital day 2, a transjugular liver biopsy with hepatic venous pressure measurement was performed.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 22-year-old man presented with one month of mid–thoracic back pain and progressive difficulty walking. Over 48 hours in the hospital, he developed spasticity in both lower extremities and worsening weakness to grade 2–3, consistent with paraplegia. He had no history of trauma or prior neurologic disease. Vital signs were normal, and routine hematologic studies were within normal limits. MRI and CT of the thoracic spine revealed a well-defined, lytic, expansile mass measuring approximately 25 × 18 × 33 mm arising from the posterior elements of the T7 vertebra, including the left facet joint, invading the transverse process and lamina, and protruding into the spinal canal with compression of the cord. Intravenous contrast produced homogeneous enhancement of the lesion.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 53-year-old woman presented to the emergency department with sudden, severe abdominal pain 60 minutes earlier, preceded by anchovy ingestion 48 hours before. She also reported weakness, diaphoresis, and hypotension. Her pain was rated 8/10 and was diffuse, with maximal tenderness in the periumbilical and epigastric areas associated with guarding.  

Her blood pressure was 84/49 mmHg, pulse 110/min, respirations 20/min, and SpO2 98% on room air. She was pale and diaphoretic. The neck was supple, chest clear, and there was no abdominal ecchymosis or palpable masses.  

She had a 6-month history of epigastric pressure unrelated to food, with intermittent nausea and vomiting; surgical causes had been previously excluded. Medical history included hyperlipidemia, hypertension, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and type 2 diabetes; she was on fluvastatin, lansoprazole, irbesartan, metformin, and aspirin. She had no history of trauma.  

Four months earlier she had elevated serum-specific IgE against Anisakis simplex. On admission her white blood cell count was 13 160/μl with eosinophilia, hemoglobin 7.9 g/dl, hematocrit 22.4%, C-reactive protein > 67 U/L, and D-dimer 550 ng/ml. Other laboratories were notable for normal liver enzymes and coagulation studies.  

Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen showed a splenic subcapsular hematoma with loss of normal splenic architecture and perisplenic free fluid, consistent with acute spontaneous splenic rupture, and a thickened, inflamed retrocecal appendix with mesenteric fat stranding, suggestive of acute appendicitis.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 53-year-old woman had a 3-year history of a burning sensation in her mouth and esophagus, leading to a 50-pound weight loss, followed by the development of skin lesions. On examination, there was diffuse intense erythema of the oral mucosa—most pronounced on the gingiva and cheeks—with a reddish, glazed tongue without atrophy and a crusted cheilitis. There was also diffuse vulvar erythema and an anal marisca. The skin of the trunk, pubis, and limbs showed crusted, hyperkeratotic follicular plaques, open comedones, and papulopustules. Both palms and soles had well-demarcated erythematous plaques. Gastroscopy revealed linear ulcerations of the esophagus. Antifungal therapy with oral fluconazole and amphotericin B solution was ineffective. Laboratory studies showed no vitamin B12 or iron deficiency, and serum zinc levels were normal. Topical and systemic corticosteroids did not help. Colonoscopy was normal. Serum glucagon levels were normal, and pancreas CT and MRI showed no abnormalities. Direct and indirect immunofluorescence studies were negative, and antibodies against desmoglein 1 and 3, envoplakin, and periplakin were undetectable. A thoracoabdominal and pelvic CT scan was normal.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

options.B: Folliculotropic and Syringotropic CD8 Mycosis Fungoides

question:
A 20-year-old G2P1 woman presented 4 days after an emergency caesarean delivery with a 1-day history of headache and new-onset generalized tonic–clonic seizures. Her antenatal course was unremarkable. During labour she received what was noted to be a difficult lumbar epidural, although no dural puncture was recorded. Post-operatively on day 1, she developed a frontal, postural headache that responded to bed rest, intravenous fluids, and simple analgesics, and she was discharged on day 3.

On day 4, she returned with nausea, headache, and generalized seizures. There was no history of visual change, fever, tinnitus, or neck stiffness. Paramedics administered diazepam en route. On arrival her Glasgow Coma Scale score was 8/15; pupils were equal and reactive, and peripheral neurological reflexes were normal. She was afebrile; pulse 114 beats/min; blood pressure 108/46 mmHg; SpO₂ 98% on 5 L/min oxygen. Chest and abdominal examinations were unremarkable.

Obstetricians administered magnesium sulphate and obtained a head CT scan, which did not reveal any abnormality. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 10 g/dL; WBC count 15.9×10⁹/L; platelets 102×10⁹/L; normal electrolytes, renal and liver function tests; uric acid normal; and no proteinuria. Her mental status improved to GCS 15/15 over several hours, and magnesium was discontinued. A lumbar puncture with a 27-gauge needle revealed clear cerebrospinal fluid, elevated protein (715 mg/L; normal 120–600 mg/L), and no cells. She was started on phenytoin, with no further seizures. Over the next 24 hours her only symptom was a severe postural headache, worse upright and relieved supine. Neurology consultation prompted magnetic resonance imaging and MR venous angiography of the head, and both studies were reported as normal with no intracranial pathology.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

An 81-year-old man with atrial fibrillation on warfarin, heart failure with moderately reduced ejection fraction, coronary artery disease status post–coronary artery bypass graft, pulmonary hypertension, severe tricuspid regurgitation, obstructive sleep apnea, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a thoracic aortic aneurysm presented with one day of numerous grossly bright-red bloody bowel movements. He reported epigastric discomfort that had resolved and denied dyschezia, hematemesis, or lightheadedness. His last colonoscopy was 16 years earlier.

On examination, he was afebrile, blood pressure 101/63 mmHg, heart rate 73 beats/min, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 90% on home 3 L/min oxygen. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal examinations were unremarkable. Digital rectal examination revealed bright-red blood and a positive stool guaiac test. He had 3+ pitting edema of the lower extremities.

Laboratory tests showed an international normalized ratio of 3.1, creatinine 1.37 mg/dL (baseline 1.1–1.3), and hemoglobin 12.9 g/dL. A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis showed no source of gastrointestinal bleeding. Intravenous vitamin K was given, but he continued to have hematochezia.

On hospital day 2, he developed small-volume hemoptysis and hematemesis while remaining hemodynamically stable, with rising oxygen requirements to 4–6 L/min. Chest imaging demonstrated a new focal opacity in the left lower lobe, suspicious for pulmonary hemorrhage. Bronchoscopy revealed old blood in the left lower lobe without an identifiable bleeding source. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy showed erosive gastropathy in the gastric body and antrum, with no active bleeding lesions.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 51-year-old man with poorly controlled asthma, diabetes mellitus, and hypertension presented with a 1-week history of productive cough, worsening dyspnea, and chest pain. The chest pain was constant, worse when supine, and relieved by leaning forward. He had been hospitalized for SARS-CoV-2 pneumonia one month earlier. 

On examination, he was afebrile; blood pressure was 176/105 mmHg, heart rate 117 beats/min, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 87% on room air. Findings included jugular venous distention, bilateral lower extremity pitting edema, tachypnea, and bilateral expiratory wheezing.

Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 26.6 × 10^3/μL with 32% eosinophils (8,512 cells/μL) and a troponin I level of 1.45 ng/mL (reference 0.00–0.05 ng/mL). Electrocardiography revealed subtle ST-segment depressions in the anterolateral leads. He was started on a heparin infusion for presumed non–ST-elevation myocardial infarction, and coronary angiography demonstrated nonobstructive coronary artery disease. 

Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 60–64% with severely increased wall thickness, normal diastolic and right ventricular function, and a small pericardial effusion. A computed tomographic angiogram of the chest showed no pulmonary embolism but scattered ground-glass opacities.

The initial working diagnosis was myopericarditis, possibly related to recent SARS-CoV-2 infection. He was treated with aspirin 325 mg three times daily, intravenous methylprednisolone 40 mg twice daily, bronchodilators, and empiric cefepime. By hospital day 5, his absolute eosinophil count had risen to 18,150 cells/μL (55%), prompting evaluation for autoimmune, fungal, parasitic, and other infectious causes.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 25-year-old Japanese woman was brought to the emergency department with severe chest pain and transient loss of consciousness. She had no history of smoking, hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidemia, connective-tissue disease, or prior cardiac disease. Four days before presentation, she noted exertional chest discomfort that gradually worsened, sometimes with diaphoresis. On the day of admission, she developed severe chest pain with diaphoresis, fainted at a local clinic, and was transferred to a nearby hospital. 

Electrocardiography showed marked ST-segment elevation in lead aVR and widespread ST-segment depression elsewhere. Echocardiography revealed diffuse left ventricular hypokinesis with preservation of basal inferior wall motion. On arrival at our hospital, she was hypotensive and agitated (GCS E1V1M5), heart rate 113 bpm and irregular, jugular venous distension, coarse crackles bilaterally, and no murmurs. 

Laboratory studies revealed WBC 19,400/μL, CRP 8.18 mg/dL, CK 1100 IU/L, CK-MB 89 IU/L, troponin T elevated, lactic acidosis (pH 7.016, lactate 10.3 mmol/L), normal lipid profile, and negative autoantibodies. Chest radiograph showed pulmonary congestion. 

During evaluation, she developed pulseless electrical activity and ventricular tachycardia. VA-ECMO was initiated, and emergent coronary angiography demonstrated 99% stenosis of the left main trunk. Intravascular ultrasound showed no calcification and a poorly defined three-layered structure with concentric thickening of the vessel wall. A 3.5×12 mm bare-metal stent was placed with restoration of TIMI grade 3 flow. An intra-aortic balloon pump was instituted, and she was admitted to the coronary care unit.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 49-year-old man presented with a 1-week history of pain in the right laterocervical region over the carotid bifurcation. The pain was a severe dull discomfort radiating to the ipsilateral ear and was triggered by head and neck movements. He had mild fever, fatigue, and myalgia at the onset but denied migraine or other neurological symptoms.

On examination, he was afebrile and in good general condition. There was localized swelling and tenderness at the right carotid bifurcation, worsened by palpation. No carotid bruits were audible. There was no cervical lymphadenopathy or induration along the jugular vein.

Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 9910/mm3, C-reactive protein of 0.10 mg%, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 20 mm/h. All other laboratory parameters were within normal limits.

Ultrasonography of the neck demonstrated an asymmetric hypoechoic thickening of the right distal common carotid artery extending into the proximal internal carotid artery. No intimal plaque or stenosis was present, and Doppler studies showed normal flow parameters. The thyroid, parathyroid, and salivary glands appeared normal; no neck mass or cervical lymphadenopathy was seen.

MRI of the neck revealed a thin rim of hyperintense tissue surrounding the right carotid artery on STIR sequences, which showed diffusion restriction on DWI and marked enhancement after contrast administration. There was no significant luminal narrowing.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 46-year-old healthy woman of Turkish ancestry was referred for persistent ocular symptoms. One month earlier, she had acute redness, purulent discharge, and discomfort in the left eye (OS), with the right eye (OD) affected four days later. She was diagnosed with bilateral bacterial conjunctivitis and treated with topical moxifloxacin 0.5%/dexamethasone 0.1% every 2 hours for one week. Three weeks after completing that course, she developed ocular pain, severe photophobia, mild hyperemia, and decreased vision in OS; intraocular pressure (IOP) was elevated. She was diagnosed with presumed autoimmune hypertensive uveitis in both eyes and treated with topical prednisolone acetate 1% every 2 hours, atropine 1% twice daily, a topical carbonic anhydrase inhibitor/β-blocker, and oral steroids (1 mg/kg/day, tapered over 3 weeks) with no improvement.

On examination at our clinic, best-corrected visual acuity was 20/25 OD and 20/200 OS, with intact color vision. Slit-lamp examination revealed severe pigment dispersion in the anterior chamber, diffuse iris transillumination defects, and mydriatic atonic pupils in both eyes. There were no keratic precipitates or anterior chamber cells, and corneal sensation was normal. Gonioscopy showed wide-open angles with heavy trabecular meshwork pigmentation and no peripheral anterior synechiae. Despite ongoing topical and oral antiglaucomatous therapy, IOP was 30 mm Hg OD and 45 mm Hg OS.

Fundus examination showed tilted discs with increased cup-to-disc ratios in both eyes. In OS, there was optic disc edema and peripapillary retinal hemorrhages suggestive of nonarteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy, findings confirmed by optical coherence tomography and high-frequency B-scan ultrasonography. Fluorescein angiography showed no signs of inflammation.

Laboratory evaluation including complete blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (11 mm/h), VDRL, FTA-ABS, herpes simplex virus and cytomegalovirus serologies, QuantiFERON-TB Gold, homocysteine, anticardiolipin antibody, lupus anticoagulant, antinuclear antibody, and HLA-B27 testing were all negative. Chest, orbital, and brain MRI and magnetic resonance angiography were unremarkable.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 72-year-old man with a history of stage IIIA non–small cell lung cancer (cT4N0M0) treated 13 months earlier with concurrent cisplatin/docetaxel and radiotherapy followed by 1 year of durvalumab presented for evaluation of an incidental pelvic nodule. Surveillance CT showed a 15-mm, round, contrast-enhancing lesion adjacent to mesorectal vessels. FDG PET/CT revealed solitary uptake in this lesion with an SUVmax of 10.3. There was no other abnormal uptake. Laboratory tests demonstrated normal carcinoembryonic antigen, sialyl Lewis X, squamous cell carcinoma antigen, neuron-specific enolase, cytokeratin fragment, progastrin-releasing peptide, and normal coagulation studies. Total colonoscopy revealed no neoplastic lesions, and urinary cytology showed only nonmalignant urothelial cells. Endoscopic or CT-guided biopsy was deemed infeasible due to anatomical restrictions and risk of complications.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 65-year-old man with a history of low-risk prostate cancer managed by active surveillance was referred for restaging when his prostate-specific antigen (PSA) level rose to 26.6 μg/L in 2022. He had no significant comorbidities aside from prior cholecystectomy. Initial prostate biopsy in 2013 showed high-grade prostatic intraepithelial neoplasia at a PSA of 6.2 μg/L. A repeat biopsy in 2015 revealed Gleason 3+3 adenocarcinoma in 1 of 14 cores; digital rectal examination was unremarkable, and the cancer was staged as T1c. Surveillance over the next eight years included biennial biopsies that were negative for malignancy. 

When his PSA increased to 26.6 μg/L, a 3-Tesla multiparametric MRI of the prostate identified two PI-RADS 4 lesions (12 mm in the right transition zone and 6 mm in the right peripheral zone). Repeat biopsy detected Gleason 3+3 adenocarcinoma in 2 of 15 cores without perineural invasion, and his prostate volume was estimated at 86 mL. He experienced postbiopsy urinary retention and developed a left lower-limb deep vein thrombosis and bilateral pulmonary emboli, which were treated with apixaban. He otherwise had no symptoms of metastatic disease.

For staging, CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis showed no nodal or visceral metastases. A 99m-Tc bone scan demonstrated moderate focal uptake in the right frontal calvarium. Noncontrast CT of the head revealed a 2.5 cm sclerotic lesion in the right frontal bone corresponding to the area of uptake, suspicious for a solitary sclerotic metastasis.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 21-year-old woman presented with 7 days of abdominal pain. She had no recent travel, no history of asthma or allergies, and no family history of similar symptoms. On examination, she had tenderness in the right lower abdomen; cardiopulmonary findings were normal. Vital signs were: temperature 36.7°C, heart rate 102 beats/min, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, blood pressure 124/90 mmHg. Laboratory tests showed white blood cells 60.64×10^9/L, neutrophils 10.06×10^9/L, lymphocytes 6.84×10^9/L, eosinophils 42.80×10^9/L, and total IgE 1109.3 IU/mL. A stool routine examination was normal, and allergen testing for 20 common allergens was negative. Abdominal CT revealed bowel-wall thickening and pelvic and abdominal ascites. Diagnostic paracentesis yielded yellowish fluid with a high eosinophil count; bacterial and tuberculosis cultures were negative and cytology showed no malignant cells. Bone marrow aspiration demonstrated active myelopoiesis with an abnormally high percentage of eosinophils. Gastroscopy revealed mild gastric mucosal erythema, and colonoscopy showed mucosal edema, erythema, erosions, and a villous appearance of the terminal ileum.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

An 8-year-old autistic boy presented with severe bilateral leg pain and difficulty walking of recent onset. He also had gum swelling and bleeding, a low-grade fever, and a maculopapular rash on both upper and lower extremities. Routine blood tests, tick titers, and an autoimmune workup were all normal. A whole-body bone scintigraphy examination showed increased radiotracer activity in the bilateral shoulders, wrists, hips, knees, and ankles, most severe in the knees. Radiographs of these areas were normal. Differential considerations included infiltrative processes such as leukemia, neuroblastoma metastases, and multifocal osteomyelitis; multifocal fractures were felt to be unlikely. Contrast-enhanced MRI of both femurs demonstrated intense metaphyseal signal abnormality and enhancement in the bilateral proximal and distal femurs and proximal tibiae, as well as subperiosteal signal abnormality and enhancement along the metaphyses.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 29‐year‐old woman presents with a 20‐year history of chronic anemia and progressive fatigue over the past 4 months. She reports no menstrual abnormalities, gastrointestinal bleeding, or nutritional deficits. Examination reveals pallor of the mucous membranes; no organomegaly or lymphadenopathy is noted.

Initial laboratory studies show: hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, hematocrit 31.6%, MCV 68.1 fL, MCH 21.1 pg, RDW 18.1%. Iron studies reveal serum iron 19 µg/dL (50–170), TIBC 430 µg/dL (250–450), ferritin 6 ng/mL (10–291). Peripheral smear shows anisopoikilocytosis and hypochromia. Reticulocyte count is 2%.

Autoimmune and infectious workup is negative, including ANA, anti–double‐stranded DNA, anti–MPO, anti–PR3, anti–gliadin, anti–transglutaminase, and anti–Helicobacter pylori antibodies. Hemoglobin A2 and F are normal; no circulating erythroblasts. Genetic testing for α‐ and β‐thalassemia is negative.

Upper endoscopy and colonoscopy are unremarkable; duodenal biopsies show preserved villous architecture with a light intraepithelial lymphocytic infiltrate but no villous atrophy.

Therapy with daily oral ferrous sulfate (90 mg) and folic acid is initiated. At 6 months, symptoms improve but hemoglobin rises by only 0.4 g/dL to 10.2 g/dL. After 18 months, hemoglobin is 10.7 g/dL, MCV 75.4 fL, and serum iron 16 µg/dL with ferritin 11 ng/mL; the patient remains anemic and iron‐deficient.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 60-year-old Japanese woman presented with a one-month history of slightly pruritic, tender subcutaneous nodules in both temporal areas. She denied systemic symptoms, including fever, weight loss, visual changes, or musculoskeletal stiffness. Her medical history was unremarkable for allergic disease or asthma; she was a 20–30 pack-year smoker. Family history was notable only for neurofibromatosis in her son.

On examination, her temperature was 35.7°C, pulse 61/min, and blood pressure 131/71 mmHg. The nodules corresponded to enlargement of the superficial temporal arteries with reduced pulsation. Cardiopulmonary, abdominal, and neurologic examinations were normal; there was no peripheral edema.

Ultrasound of the temporal arteries showed hypoechogenic wall thickening and luminal stenosis. Initial laboratory studies revealed a white blood cell count of 10,300/μL with 5.4% eosinophils (556/μL); ESR and CRP were within normal limits. Serologic tests including ANA, RF, ANCA, complement levels, and hepatitis serologies were unremarkable. Stool examinations were negative for parasites. Ophthalmologic evaluation showed no ischemic changes.

Over the next three months, her absolute eosinophil count rose to 1,954/μL. She developed coolness and cyanosis of the fingertips and small pruritic nodules on the sides of her fingers. CT of the chest and echocardiography were normal. Color duplex ultrasonography of the hands revealed occlusion of the ulnar arteries and a corkscrew pattern of the digital arteries. Angiography confirmed occlusion of the ulnar artery, palmar arch, and proper digital arteries.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 79-year-old woman presented with five days of right orbital pain. She had a ruptured vesicle at the right nasal root (the Hutchinson sign) and was diagnosed with herpes zoster ophthalmicus (HZO); oral famciclovir was started. On hospital day 2, she developed nausea, malaise, right conjunctival hyperemia, right upper-eyelid swelling with ptosis, and dysesthesia in the ophthalmic nerve distribution; there was no extraocular movement restriction or pupillary dilation. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis showed no pleocytosis or elevated protein, but PCR was positive for varicella-zoster virus DNA. Oral famciclovir was changed to intravenous acyclovir (30 mg/kg/day). She then developed neck pain, CSF pleocytosis, and elevated protein, consistent with meningitis, and received intravenous methylprednisolone. By day 23, CSF PCR was negative for viral DNA.

On day 32, despite ongoing acyclovir, she abruptly developed diplopia and right eye pain. Visual acuity and fields were normal; there was no relative afferent pupillary defect and no pupil dilation. On the right, abduction was moderately restricted, and adduction and elevation were slightly restricted. Tendon reflexes were diminished; Romberg sign was negative. Serum autoantibodies (antinuclear, ANCA, SS-A/Ro, aquaporin-4, anticardiolipin) were negative. Repeat CSF showed a normal cell count, elevated protein, negative PCR for VZV, and negative oligoclonal bands. Brain MRI with gadolinium showed no orbital apex mass, no cranial nerve enhancement, no extraocular muscle swelling, and no brainstem signal abnormalities. Neurophysiological studies were normal aside from known left carpal tunnel syndrome. A blink reflex study showed absence of right R1 and prolongation of bilateral R2 upon bilateral stimulation.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 58-year-old Grenadian woman was referred after her primary care physician found elevated liver enzymes on routine blood tests. She reported 10 days of fatigue, nausea, and intermittent non-bilious, non-bloody vomiting; 4 days before admission she noted scleral icterus and dark urine, along with a 10-lb weight loss and decreased appetite. She denied abdominal pain, alcohol use, or other supplement intake, but admitted to drinking a “green juice” containing watercress, garlic, and ginger for 3 days before admission.

Her medical history included hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and hyperlipidemia; she took her statin irregularly. She had no family history of liver or autoimmune disease. On examination, blood pressure was 168/98 mmHg, heart rate 77/min, temperature 37.1 °C. She was alert, without asterixis, had mild bilateral scleral icterus, and a non-tender, non-distended abdomen without hepatomegaly or fluid wave.

Laboratory studies on admission showed:  
– ALT 2500 U/L (RR 10–49)  
– AST 3159 U/L (RR 8–34)  
– Alkaline phosphatase 714 U/L (RR 46–116)  
– Total bilirubin 6.4 mg/dL (RR 0.3–1.2), direct bilirubin 4.4 mg/dL (RR 0.1–0.3)  
– Prothrombin time 12.7 s, INR 1.07  

Serological testing was negative for hepatitis A, B, C, and E. Further serologies for cytomegalovirus, Epstein–Barr virus, herpes simplex virus 1 and 2, and HIV were negative. Autoimmune markers—including ANA, smooth muscle antibody, anti-mitochondrial antibody, anti–liver kidney microsome-1 antibody, and soluble liver antigen antibody—were all negative. Ceruloplasmin was slightly elevated at 62 mg/dL (RR 18–53).

Abdominal ultrasound with Doppler showed normal hepatic parenchyma and vascular flow; the gallbladder was contracted without cholecystitis. MRCP demonstrated no biliary stones, filling defects, or ductal dilatation. 

A percutaneous liver biopsy revealed moderate to severe active hepatitis with focal confluent necrosis, predominately lymphocytic infiltrate with few eosinophils, plasma cells, and neutrophils, scattered acidophil bodies, periportal fibrosis, lobular disarray, and no evidence of cholestasis, granulomas, or malignancy.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A Caucasian man in his early 20s with schizophrenia spectrum disorder and a history of monthly crystal methamphetamine use was transferred to a forensic psychiatric unit for aggression and erratic behavior. He had been on oral ziprasidone for several weeks but stopped it briefly and then restarted at a lower dose. Over the next month, he refused medications, and his behavior worsened. He was switched to oral aripiprazole, with low‐dose clonazepam added. After a requested increase in aripiprazole, his irritability improved. Two weeks later, staff noted that he was wearing a thick pullover despite warm temperatures, was sweating, and refused to speak, responding only by head movements. Concerned about oversedation, clonazepam was reduced. Over the next two days, he developed bizarre persecutory delusions, undressed, paced, and became noncompliant. A single dose of intramuscular zuclopenthixol was given; the next day he was calmer but had increased paranoia, vague suicidal thoughts, slowed movements, and disordered thinking. He was thought to be in a psychotic state with catatonic‐like features. That night, he exhibited ambitendency and mild posturing. His temperature and blood pressure were at baseline; heart rate was 135 beats per minute. Physical examination revealed mild rigidity of the arms. To rule out neuroleptic malignant syndrome, blood was drawn for measurement of creatine kinase.

A 43-year-old Hispanic woman with a history of left-leg monoparesis from poliomyelitis, long-standing diabetes mellitus, hypertension requiring three agents, nonproliferative diabetic retinopathy, proteinuria, and osteoporosis was evaluated for suspected Cushing’s syndrome. She first underwent evaluation 13 and 5 years earlier, including inferior petrosal sinus catheterization, without localization of an ACTH source. Three years ago she presented with an 18-month history of polymenorrhea, and examination showed moon facies, buffalo hump, prominent supraclavicular fat, purple striae on arms and abdomen, truncal obesity, an umbilical hernia, and proximal muscle wasting. Laboratory evaluation revealed urinary free cortisol 186.5 μg/day, morning serum cortisol 21.8 μg/dL, midnight serum cortisol 16.1 μg/dL, serum cortisol after a 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test 16.6 μg/dL, and plasma ACTH 32.6 pg/mL. An 8 mg high-dose dexamethasone suppression test showed cortisol suppression to 2.69 μg/dL (84.8%). Thyroid function was normal. Two pituitary MRIs were reported as normal. Abdominal CT revealed a 68 × 60 × 76 mm bilobed intra-abdominal mass protruding through the umbilicus, adjacent to a small-intestinal loop, with unenhanced attenuation of 3, 7, and 45 HU rising to 6, 16, and 88 HU postcontrast, raising the possibility of a mesothelioma. Pelvic ultrasound confirmed uterine fibroids but no ovarian masses. Exploratory laparotomy was planned.

A 62-year-old woman presented with a 4-month history of progressive gingival enlargement and bilateral tender submandibular lymphadenopathy. Her medical history was notable only for 10 years of hypertension treated with antihypertensive medications. Intraoral examination showed generalized maxillary and mandibular gingival enlargement of firm consistency covering two-thirds of the teeth, without bleeding or purulence. Teeth exhibited generalized attrition and Grade-3 mobility of the mandibular second molars, which were tender to percussion. Extraoral examination revealed bilateral tender, mobile submandibular lymph nodes. A panoramic radiograph demonstrated generalized alveolar bone loss, severe resorption around the mandibular second molars resembling floating teeth, and periapical radiolucencies in the maxilla. Complete blood counts and biochemical tests were within normal limits except for an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. The initial clinical differential diagnosis included drug-induced gingival enlargement, idiopathic gingival enlargement, and inflammatory gingival enlargement, the latter of which was considered unlikely given the firmness of the tissue. An incisional biopsy was performed from the mandibular anterior facial gingiva and submitted for histological evaluation.

A 12-year-old girl with a history of anxiety treated with sertraline and no prior seizures presented with acute onset of altered mental status and possible seizure activity. Her parent found her hiding under her bed, hitting herself, jerking, and crying. On arrival, her temperature was 103.1°F, and her Glasgow Coma Scale score was 12 (she opened her eyes to voice, spoke with confused speech, and localized to pain). Examination was otherwise unremarkable, with no pharyngitis despite a positive rapid group A streptococcus antigen test. Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 14.6/µL with 18% bands; urine drug screen, serum metabolic panel, cerebrospinal fluid cell counts, urinalysis, and head CT scan were all normal. Empiric vancomycin, ceftriaxone, acyclovir, and levetiracetam were initiated. On hospital day 2, she was noted to have flaccid paralysis of the left upper extremity and urinary incontinence. Brain MRI with and without contrast and MR angiography were normal except for mild paranasal sinus inflammation. A routine EEG demonstrated slowing over the right hemisphere. Blood, urine, and CSF cultures showed no growth; CSF PCR assays for herpes simplex virus and varicella virus were negative. West Nile virus IgM and Epstein–Barr virus PCR were negative. Serum Mycoplasma pneumoniae IgM was elevated at 1861 U/mL, but PCR was negative. Anti-streptolysin O titer was 675 IU/mL, and anti–DNAse B was 583 U/mL. An arbovirus panel was negative, and serum Bartonella henselae titers were sent.

A 44-year-old man presented in January 2014 with a solitary yellowish plaque on the right temple that had been stable for 5 months and was mildly itchy on touch. His history was notable for a superficial spreading melanoma excised from the right upper leg in 2012; there was no history of pediatric cutaneous mastocytosis. Physical examination revealed a solitary plaque on the right temple without other skin lesions or systemic abnormalities. Laboratory studies showed a normal serum tryptase level (2.9 μg/L; normal <11.4 μg/L). 

A punch biopsy of the lesion revealed diffuse sheets of epithelioid cells in the dermis without epidermal involvement. The cells had abundant, finely granulated cytoplasm and slight nuclear polymorphism, with scattered spindle-shaped and bilobed/multilobed nuclei. Mitotic activity was low (up to 1 mitotic figure/mm2). Giemsa stain highlighted metachromatic granules. Immunohistochemistry was positive for CD117, tryptase, and MiTF; CD30 showed heterogeneous positivity; a few cells were CD25 positive; CD2 was negative. Next-generation sequencing of the lesion showed no KIT mutations (exons 8, 9, 11, 13, 14, 17).

In July 2014, the lesion was excised. The histopathologic features were similar but with increased nuclear pleomorphism, up to 9 mitotic figures/mm2, and a Ki-67 index of 29.5%. CD117 and tryptase staining demonstrated small clusters of mast cells extending into the resection margin. No clinical or imaging evidence of systemic disease was found. 

Based on these findings, a provisional diagnosis of mastocytoma was made.

A 43-year-old man presented with a 2-year history of slowly progressive vision loss in both eyes, which had markedly worsened over the past 3 months. He also noted floaters and a rapid decrease in vision in the left eye. His medical history was notable for chronic kidney failure from post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (on peritoneal dialysis and erythropoietin) and well-controlled systemic hypertension (blood pressure 115/80 mmHg on beta- and alpha-blockers). He denied diabetes, prior ocular surgery, or significant eye disease; he was allergic to trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole.

On examination, best-corrected visual acuity was 20/200 in the right eye and 20/50 in the left eye. Intraocular pressure was 12 mmHg bilaterally. Slit-lamp examination showed clear corneas, rubeosis iridis, and mild nuclear sclerotic cataracts. Funduscopic examination revealed engorged, pale retinal arterioles; attenuated venules; diffuse RPE pigmentary changes; small vitreous hemorrhage and subhyaloid hemorrhage sparing the fovea, with neovascularization of the optic disc in the right eye; and old inferior vitreous hemorrhage with fibrovascular proliferation on the optic disc in the left eye.

Fluorescein angiography demonstrated a leopard-spot pattern of choroidal hyperfluorescence, optic-disc neovascular leakage, and peripheral capillary nonperfusion in both eyes. Spectral-domain OCT showed posterior vitreous detachment with hyperreflective intraretinal flecks, and visible hyperreflective arterial vessel walls. Enhanced depth imaging OCT revealed thickened choroidal vessel walls and lumenal enlargement.

The working diagnosis was bilateral proliferative retinopathy from an occlusive retinal vasculopathy. Laboratory studies showed normocytic normochromic anemia, elevated creatinine (12.9 mg/dL), elevated alkaline phosphatase, and hypoalbuminemia. Syphilis serologies and tuberculosis testing were negative. ANA, RF, anti-Sm, antiphospholipid, ANCA subtypes, complement levels, anti-DNA, anti-RNP, anti-Ro, and anti-La antibodies were all negative. A thrombophilia panel for factor V Leiden, protein C and S deficiencies, and antithrombin III deficiency was negative. Carotid and orbital Doppler ultrasonography showed mild carotid and vertebral artery stenosis with reduced flow in the ophthalmic and central retinal arteries.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

An 11-year-old boy with a history of decreased visual acuity in the right eye presented with severe headaches and confusion evolving subacutely over 3 weeks. He was disoriented to time and place; motor and sensory examinations were normal. Brain MRI showed multiple nodular lesions primarily in the central corpus callosum and supratentorial subcortical white matter, appearing hypointense on T1-weighted images, hyperintense on T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences, with restricted diffusion, no contrast enhancement after gadolinium, and no abnormalities on MR angiography. Spinal MRI was normal. Fluorescein angiography revealed bilateral branch retinal artery occlusions. EEG demonstrated diffuse slowing of background activity. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis showed an elevated protein level (170 mg/dL) with no oligoclonal bands; tests for systemic vasculitis were negative. Audiometry confirmed bilateral sensorineural hearing loss.

A 9-year-old Limousine beef cow, non-pregnant and 4 months postpartum, was referred for a 10-day history of moderate hyperthermia (39.5–39.6 °C), dry feces, decreased rumination and appetite, an arched back, and attenuated heart and lung sounds. Initial treatment with amoxicillin and dexamethasone produced no improvement.

On arrival, the cow was depressed but in fair body condition (BCS 3.5/5) and weighed 738 kg. She exhibited complete inappetence (rumen-fill score 2), a persistently arched back, dewlap edema, and bilateral jugular distension with a positive jugular compression test. Mucous membranes were pale. Vital signs: heart rate 105 bpm, respiratory rate 44/min (costo-abdominal breathing), rectal temperature 38.7 °C. Right-sided pulmonary auscultation was attenuated; cardiac auscultation revealed a duplicated S1, loud on the left and attenuated on the right. Rumination was absent. A wither-pinch test elicited pain. External lymph nodes: moderate enlargement of the right superficial cervical node. Rectal and urine exams were unremarkable; feces appeared normal.

Laboratory studies showed normal plasma fibrinogen, mild hypoalbuminemia, increased total protein (chronic inflammatory syndrome), leukocyte count at the upper limit with neutrophilia, and lymphopenia. 

A right-sided thoracic ultrasound revealed a 30 cm × 25 cm echogenic mass severely compressing the right heart, reducing right ventricular size, and an adjacent anechoic pleural fluid collection; the pericardium and valves appeared normal. Reticular ultrasonography showed normal structure but absent contractions. Pleural fluid, aspirated under ultrasound guidance, was moderately cloudy, orange-brown; analysis indicated a non-specific septic exudate.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman with hypothyroidism and cochlear hydrops took nitrofurantoin 100 mg twice daily for presumed uncomplicated urinary urgency and frequency. She had no prior exposure to nitrofurantoin. 

In less than 24 hours, she developed abrupt fever (101.7 °F), chills, lightheadedness, chest congestion, pleuritic chest pain, generalized weakness, and hypotension (90/51 mm Hg). In the emergency department, she received 2 L normal saline, ceftriaxone IV, ondansetron, and had blood and urine cultures drawn.  
– WBC 17.1 × 10^3/µL (83% neutrophils, 9% bands)  
– Lactate 1.4 mmol/L  
– CMP notable only for K^+ 3.4 mmol/L  
– Urinalysis: 2+ blood, trace leukocyte esterase, 5–10 WBCs, 0–4 RBCs, 1+ bacteria  

She was discharged on levofloxacin for suspected acute pyelonephritis. All symptoms resolved within 24 hours, and she completed 7 days of levofloxacin.

Two months later, she again took nitrofurantoin 100 mg twice daily for similar urinary complaints. Within 24 hours she developed fever (102.6 °F), chills, myalgias, pleuritis, weakness, nausea, vomiting, hypotension (80 mm Hg systolic), and tachycardia (120 beats/min), with a positive tilt test but no rash or urticaria. She received 2 L normal saline and was again prescribed levofloxacin after blood and urine cultures were obtained.  
– WBC 22 × 10^3/µL (94% granulocytes)  
– Urinalysis: 500 leu/µL, 250 ery/µL  
– CMP notable only for K^+ 3.2 mmol/L

A 56-year-old woman with a 23-year history of thyrotoxicosis treated intermittently with antithyroid drugs presented with recurrent symptoms two weeks after total thyroidectomy. On initial evaluation she had bilateral exophthalmos, an enlarged thyroid with nodules, and an elevated TSH-receptor antibody level of 3.86 IU/L (normal < 1.00 IU/L). A Tc-99m thyroid scan had shown diffusely increased uptake with one hyperfunctioning nodule in the right upper pole and a hypofunctioning nodule in the right midlobe. Histopathology of the 57.7 g thyroidectomy specimen confirmed Graves’ disease and benign nodules.

Two weeks after surgery, she developed symptoms of thyrotoxicosis. Thyroid-function testing confirmed elevated free T4 and suppressed TSH. A radioiodine uptake in the thyroid bed was 0.2 % (reference, 15 %–45 %). An I-131 whole-body scan demonstrated intense radiotracer uptake with a “star” artifact in the pelvic region. SPECT/CT of the pelvis revealed an 8.5 × 7.2 cm mixed cystic–solid mass with internal calcification in the right adnexa. The plasma cancer antigen 125 level was 48.55 U/mL (reference, 0–35 U/mL).

A 75-year-old woman with extensive‐disease small-cell lung cancer presented with 5 days of back pain. Two months earlier she had been diagnosed with stage IVB ED-SCLC and received first-cycle carboplatin, etoposide, and atezolizumab, complicated by febrile neutropenia. The second cycle was given at a reduced dose, and pegfilgrastim was administered 15 days before presentation. 

On admission she was afebrile (36.8°C) with blood pressure 168/74 mmHg, heart rate 68/min, respiratory rate 24/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Physical examination was unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed WBC 21,000/μL (neutrophils 87%), CRP 23.99 mg/dL, and IL-6 83.6 pg/mL (normal <4.0 pg/mL). Serologic tests were negative for ANA, PR3-ANCA, MPO-ANCA, and rheumatoid factor.

A 60-year-old man presented with a 3-month history of lethargy, weight loss, cough, and night sweats. He had no personal or family history of tuberculosis. He was born in Turkey and had lived in France for 30 years. On admission, temperature was 37.2 °C, heart rate 80 bpm, respiratory rate 20 /min, blood pressure 100/70 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Physical examination was unremarkable, with no palpable peripheral lymphadenopathy.

Chest radiography showed mediastinal enlargement without pulmonary infiltrates. Contrast-enhanced CT revealed large mediastinal and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy and a hypodense splenic collection. Complete blood count, renal, and liver function tests were within normal limits; HIV serology was negative. Acid-fast bacilli were identified in a sputum sample, and molecular analysis did not suggest antibiotic resistance. A standard four-drug antituberculous regimen (rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol) was initiated, with some clinical improvement.

Two weeks later, the patient experienced recurrent fever and marked asthenia. Repeat blood cultures and bronchoalveolar lavage were negative. A second CT scan showed a decrease in mediastinal lymph-node size but persistence of the splenic lesion, which remained enlarged and necrotic in appearance.

A woman in her 70s with longstanding mild systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE)—manifested by discoid lupus erythematosus of the scalp, positive anti–double‐stranded DNA antibody, and otherwise quiescent systemic disease—presented for treatment of a recalcitrant scalp lesion. Physical examination revealed a single well-demarcated red-purple scaly plaque on the scalp with follicular plugging and scarring alopecia, consistent with active DLE. No other classic DLE lesions were noted on the head or neck. On the right lower eyelid, there was a focal area of erythema involving the temporal lid margin and adjacent palpebral conjunctiva, without loss of lashes. A biopsy of the eyelid margin showed atrophic lichenoid inflammation with basement membrane thickening, consistent with a lichenoid presentation of DLE. The patient was started on hydroxychloroquine, with partial improvement of her lesions, and topical plus intralesional steroids were used for her scalp. Over the next 3 years, she experienced recurrent inflammation of the right lower eyelid accompanied by right-sided episcleritis. She ultimately developed a focal symblepharon between the bulbar and palpebral conjunctiva. Because the etiology of the ongoing ocular inflammation—active mucosal DLE, friction or trauma from her prior biopsy, or another process—remained unclear, a diagnostic conjunctival biopsy of the symblepharon area was planned.

A 54-year-old woman presented in 2023 after discovering a firm, movable, nontender mass in her right breast. She had no systemic symptoms such as fever, weight loss, or dyspnea. She underwent breast-conserving surgery with sentinel lymph node biopsy; pathology showed invasive carcinoma, ER 5%, PR negative, HER2 0+, Ki-67 45%, pT2N0M0, with a close margin (<1 mm at 3–6 o’clock). She received one cycle of docetaxel and then, at our institution, five cycles of docetaxel (120 mg/m2 q3w) plus cyclophosphamide (600 mg/m2 q3w) and pembrolizumab (200 mg q3w). After the fifth cycle, she noted numbness in her hands and edema of her lower limbs, followed by swelling and fibrotic changes of the skin on both lower extremities. On examination, there was bilateral leg skin induration without sclerodactyly, Raynaud’s phenomenon, or dysphagia. Laboratory testing revealed negative antinuclear antibodies. A 50-year-old Filipino woman with a history of disseminated Mycobacterium avium–intracellulare infection, IgG lambda monoclonal gammopathy, mixed connective tissue disease with leukocytoclastic vasculitis, IgA nephropathy, SAPHO (synovitis, acne, pustulosis, hyperostosis, osteitis) syndrome, and treated breast cancer presented with intensely pruritic, pink-to-brown, edematous, finely scaly, coin-shaped thin plaques on the left lower leg and back that gradually spread over her body. HIV and syphilis serologies were negative. She was initially treated for presumed eczematous dermatitis with triamcinolone 0.1% ointment twice daily, a sensitive skin care regimen, and discontinuation of over-the-counter topicals and unnecessary supplements. After 1 month without improvement, a skin biopsy showed spongiotic vesicular dermatitis with eosinophils. Given her monoclonal gammopathy, she was diagnosed with suspected eosinophilic dermatosis of hematologic malignancy and began narrow-band ultraviolet B phototherapy twice weekly, cetirizine, hydroxyzine, pramoxine and camphor/menthol lotions, and was switched to clobetasol 0.05% ointment twice daily. Over the next 7 months, she experienced no improvement, so methotrexate 15 mg weekly (with folate) was added and topical tacrolimus 0.1% ointment replaced steroids. After 6 months of systemic immunosuppression her lesions worsened. An elevated absolute eosinophil count (1.6 × 10^3/µL) prompted a single dose of oral ivermectin 0.2 mg/kg for possible scabies, but there was no clinical improvement and stool ova and parasite tests were negative. A second skin biopsy revealed a superficial and deep perivascular infiltrate with numerous eosinophils, and the report noted that “the differential diagnosis of such histopathology is broad and can include a primary spongiotic process, contact dermatitis, and dermal hypersensitivity (including autosensitization, drug, or arthropod assault).” During a routine PET scan to monitor her monoclonal gammopathy, she was found to have widespread new lymphadenopathy. Biopsy of a right cervical lymph node was negative for malignancy.

An 85-year-old woman was admitted with acute abdominal pain. Laboratory tests showed elevated cholestasis parameters and a C-reactive protein level of 99 mg/dL (reference <5 mg/dL). A 47-year-old Asian woman was transferred with a two-month history of episodic chest pain, dyspnea, bilateral lower-extremity swelling, cyanosis, and transient loss of consciousness. Two years earlier she had undergone an abdominal hysterectomy for uterine fibroids. Two months before transfer she had a brief loss of consciousness; two weeks before she noted gradual pleuritic chest pain and leg edema; and one day before she developed shortness of breath and a nonproductive cough. At the outside hospital she appeared cyanotic and hypotensive, with a heart rate of 110 bpm, systolic blood pressure of 70 mm Hg, respiratory rate of 34 breaths per minute, and dusky skin. An electrocardiogram and serum troponin were normal. Arterial blood gas on 100% oxygen showed pH 7.25, PaCO₂ 22 mm Hg, and PaO₂ 506 mm Hg. A 74‐year‐old man with dementia was brought to the emergency department one day after a mountain fall. He had no headache, seizure, or visual symptoms but had multiple bruises and an abrasion on the head. There was no history of infection. On arrival his Glasgow Coma Scale was 13 (E3V4M6); heart rate 94/min; blood pressure 149/75 mmHg; and temperature 37.9°C (persisting near 38°C for two weeks). Physical examination showed bruises on the chest and abdomen and right lower‐leg tenderness; muscle strength was 4/5 in the right lower leg. Laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 13.5 g/dL; leukocytes 19,200/μL (neutrophils 15,398/μL); urea 25 mmol/L; creatinine 0.76 mg/dL; potassium 3.8 mmol/L; sodium 141 mmol/L; chloride 108 mmol/L; creatine kinase 20,518 IU/L; and C‐reactive protein 13.43 mg/dL. He received a tetanus vaccine and intravenous tazobactam/piperacillin from hospital day 6 to 20. Plain radiographs showed no fractures. Head CT demonstrated subarachnoid hemorrhage, an acute subdural hematoma, and bilateral parietooccipital hypodense areas without cranial fractures. Chest and abdominal CT scans were unremarkable. On hospital day 2, he developed ascending weakness of the extremities and bulbar weakness. A 31-year-old Mestizo-Mexican woman presented to the emergency department with a 1-week history of asthenia, adynamia, polydipsia, nausea, vomiting, food intolerance, impaired visual acuity, and abdominal pain. A 10-month-old girl born at 29 weeks’ gestation with trisomy 21 and chronic lung disease of prematurity was transferred for otolaryngologic evaluation of persistent stridor. A 1.3-year-old boy was referred for evaluation of abnormal visual behavior and global developmental delay. He had mild hypotonia and plagiocephaly. Neurodevelopmental testing at 1.3 years showed skills equivalent to a 7–9-month-old. He began walking at 2.8 years and at age 4 continued to have delayed gross motor and cognitive skills. On examination, he had short stature, normal weight, brachycephaly, minor facial dysmorphism, and fifth-finger brachydactyly. He carried diagnoses of hypotonic cerebral palsy and moderate to severe intellectual disability. Additional issues included gastroesophageal reflux, constipation, presumed calcaneal osteomyelitis, and oropharyngeal dysphagia.

Family history was notable for a paternal great-grandfather with nyctalopia and two first-degree cousins with congenital stationary night blindness. Both parents and a younger sibling were healthy with no vision problems.

At 1.3 years, ophthalmic examination revealed that he could fix and follow but had unsteady gaze with small-amplitude nystagmus. Retinoscopy showed mild hyperopia and astigmatism. Under anesthesia, the fundus examination demonstrated bilateral steep temporal-sloping optic discs, mottled macular pigmentation, and a blonde fundus appearance; these findings remained stable at ages 1.9, 2.9, and 3.9 years, although nystagmus evolved into a pendular type.

Handheld spectral-domain OCT showed foveal hypoplasia with retained inner retinal layers at the foveola; segmentation revealed that the photoreceptor/RPE layer (REC+) was thicker than the 95% confidence interval of age-matched controls, while inner retinal layers (RGC+ and INL) were thinner. Repeat OCT from 1.3 to 3.9 years showed no structural progression.

Full-field ERG at 1.3 years showed rod-dependent responses more severely attenuated than cone-dependent responses, normal implicit times, and an electronegative b-to-a wave ratio on the mixed rod-cone stimulus. Between 1.9 and 3.9 years, rod responses remained stable, whereas cone responses declined by 20–30% per year.

Prior work-up was negative for fragile X syndrome, chromosomal microarray, brain MRI, EEG, and audiometry. Metabolic labs were normal except for mild elevations of creatine kinase and lactate. Enzyme assays for neuronal ceroid lipofuscinoses types 1 and 2 were negative, congenital disorders of glycosylation testing was normal, and TRPM1 mutation analysis was negative.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 62-year-old Caucasian man was admitted with six days of cough, fever, myalgia, and chills. He had tested positive for SARS-CoV-2 three days after symptom onset. His only medical history was hyperlipidemia managed with atorvastatin; he had no known allergies and denied tobacco, alcohol, or illicit drug use. On admission his renal function was normal. Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis showed diffuse bilateral ground-glass pulmonary infiltrates, mediastinal lymphadenopathy, moderate pleural effusions, and normally sized kidneys with preserved perfusion.

Two days later he developed acute respiratory distress syndrome requiring intubation, prone positioning, and empiric amoxicillin–clavulanate; hydroxychloroquine was also given after exclusion of G6PD deficiency. His course was complicated by atrial fibrillation, paralytic ileus, and oliguria. By hospital day 9, he had acute kidney injury with a serum creatinine peak of 519 μmol/L and severe metabolic acidosis, prompting initiation of continuous veno-venous hemodiafiltration.

Over the next two weeks he developed a maculopapular rash on his trunk and lower extremities and a Coombs-negative hemolytic anemia with schistocytes, high lactate dehydrogenase, undetectable haptoglobin, and transfusion dependence. Laboratory studies revealed ferritin >11,000 μg/L, elevated C-reactive protein and procalcitonin, worsening hypoalbuminemia, persistent proteinuria (urine protein/creatinine ratio 72.6 mg/mmol), microscopic hematuria, but minimal leukocyturia and no marked eosinophilia. Serologic screening for HIV, hepatitis B and C, antinuclear, anti–DNA, antineutrophil cytoplasmic, and anticardiolipin antibodies, complement levels, and ADAMTS13 activity were unremarkable. Echocardiography showed preserved ventricular function; imaging excluded urinary obstruction. In the absence of renal recovery, nephrology was consulted on hospital day 26 to consider renal biopsy.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 40-year-old man presented with a four-week history of a rapidly growing nodule at the site of a scratched surgical scar on his right elbow. His oncologic history included a malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor of the right forearm in 2003 treated with surgery (negative margins), radiation, and chemotherapy, and a recurrence over the biceps in 2015 treated with resection (positive margins) and additional radiotherapy. He had no personal or family history of neurofibromatosis. On examination, there was a 2.0 cm violaceous nodule with a 1 cm red, friable papulonodule centrally located on the previous surgical scar. Histopathologic examination of the nodule showed mixed hypercellular and hypocellular areas composed of atypical spindled and epithelioid cells in a myxoid stroma. The Ki-67 proliferation index was high, PHH3 staining revealed more than 30 mitoses per high-powered field, and S-100 immunostaining was focally positive. Contrast-enhanced MRI of the right forearm demonstrated a complex, dumbbell-shaped soft-tissue mass measuring approximately 5.4 × 4.3 × 3.5 cm that displaced the ulnar nerve medially and splayed the flexor muscles and tendons, with no evidence of distant metastases.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 40-year-old woman presented with concern for a right breast lump following trauma. Screening mammography was abnormal and concerning for malignancy. Core-needle biopsy revealed granulomatous mastitis. She received a 2-week course of cephalexin without improvement, followed by a prednisone taper with only transient symptomatic relief, and then an oral course of doxycycline with persistent symptoms. On examination, there were multiple red, indurated, tender areas on the right breast, including a suspected fistula on the lateral aspect of the nipple. In-office breast ultrasound identified multiple complex abscess cavities and a fistulous tract. Two aspirations of fluid collections yielded negative wound cultures. The infection did not resolve with further penicillin administration, and concerns for multidrug-resistant organisms and acid-fast bacilli were raised. After more than one year of progressive symptoms, the patient underwent operative incision and drainage with debridement; perioperative antibiotics included vancomycin and a gentamicin/clindamycin irrigant. Intraoperative cultures again failed to isolate an organism.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 4-month-old male infant presented with fresh blood-stained stools, sometimes loose but not frankly diarrhoeic, without fever or vomiting. He had been exclusively breastfed until 3 months of age, after which fresh cow’s milk was added. At 4 months (weight 6.0 kg), stool microscopy at a local clinic reportedly showed Entamoeba histolytica, and he received metronidazole. Over the next two months, he again had blood-stained stools despite two more courses of antiparasitic and antibiotic therapy (metronidazole, cotrimoxazole, tinidazole, chloroquine), with repeat stool microscopy each time reporting E. histolytica. He remained in good general condition, continued to gain weight, and had no diarrhoea, fever, or vomiting.

On referral to the paediatrician at age 6 months (weight 7.5 kg), the history of ongoing cow’s milk supplementation was elicited. Physical examination was unremarkable with no abdominal or perianal lesions. Abdominal ultrasound was normal. Laboratory facilities for immunoglobulin assays, specific antibodies, or skin testing were unavailable. A preliminary diagnosis of cow’s milk protein allergy was made. The antibiotics and antimalarials were discontinued, and a dietary trial was initiated. Cow’s milk was excluded from the infant’s diet, and exclusive breastfeeding was resumed.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 71-year-old normotensive man with a 50–pack-year smoking history was referred by his primary care physician for severe hyponatremia (serum sodium, 117 mMol/L). He reported no active complaints or neurological symptoms. On examination, he appeared euvolemic, had feminine facial features, and was notably short (height, 149 cm). Blood pressure was 131/70 mmHg. Initial laboratory studies showed mild metabolic acidosis (serum bicarbonate, 19 mEq/L), normal potassium, BUN 13.1 mg/dL, creatinine 1.27 mg/dL, and GFR 68 mL/min/1.73 m2. Serum osmolality was 245 mOsm/kg; urine osmolality, 309 mOsm/kg; and urine sodium, 68 mEq/L. A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the chest was obtained and ruled out lung malignancy as a source of ectopic ADH.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

options.B: Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion

question:
A 10-year-old boy was referred for non-resolving redness and photophobia in both eyes. Symptoms began one day after accidental exposure to a herbal insecticide spray; he also developed flu-like symptoms and received oral acetaminophen and cefuroxime for 3 days. One week later, topical moxifloxacin-difluprednate and homatropine were started without improvement.

On presentation, he had extreme photophobia and ocular pain. Best-corrected visual acuity was 20/40 in both eyes; intraocular pressure was normal by digital palpation. Slit-lamp examination revealed diffuse palpebral and bulbar conjunctival congestion, superficial punctate keratopathy in the interpalpebral cornea, and pigment dispersion in the anterior chamber without inflammatory cells. There was pigment deposition on the inferior corneal endothelium. Pupils were round and slightly dilated (on homatropine). The iris showed diffuse stromal atrophy with a granular appearance in the mid and peripheral zones, sparing the peripupillary region, and no transillumination defects.

Gonioscopy demonstrated wide open angles with heavy pigment deposition and regular iris insertion. Anterior-segment OCT confirmed a flat (non-concave) iris contour. Fundus examination and specular microscopy were normal. Systemic examination was unremarkable. Laboratory tests showed a normal haemogram, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 28 mm/hour, and no elevation of IgM levels for CMV, HSV-1, HSV-2, or VZV.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 52-year-old woman with a 10-year history of seronegative rheumatoid arthritis, managed with sulfasalazine, presented with 3 weeks of daily fevers up to 103°F. She had been off adalimumab for 1 year after developing a psoriatic rash. Six months earlier, she had an incidental nondiagnostic biopsy of a left upper lobe pulmonary nodule. 

On admission, she was afebrile and hemodynamically stable. Laboratory studies showed:  
• White-cell count 25 × 10^9/L (elevated)  
• C-reactive protein 6.44 mg/dL (elevated)  
• Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 70 mm/h (elevated)  
• Alkaline phosphatase 179 U/L (elevated)  
• Normal alanine aminotransferase, aspartate aminotransferase, and bilirubin  

A whole-body CT scan revealed that the prior lung nodule had decreased in size but showed multiple new hypodense hepatic lesions, the largest measuring 2 cm. MRI of the liver confirmed numerous hepatic lesions initially concerning for metastatic disease.

During hospitalization, she remained afebrile, her leukocytosis resolved without antibiotics, and blood and urine cultures were negative. Testing for hepatitis B and C, QuantiFERON-TB Gold, cancer antigens (AFP, CA-125, CA 19-9, CEA), and autoantibodies (ANA, rheumatoid factor, antimitochondrial, anti–smooth muscle, anti–topoisomerase I, anti–SSA/SSB) were all unrevealing. A transthoracic echocardiogram showed no endocarditis. She then underwent colonoscopy, which showed no evidence of colonic malignancy.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 29-year-old man with no significant medical history presented with epigastric abdominal pain. He reported a 1-week history of tea-colored urine, pale stools, and diffuse pruritus that preceded the development of a new rash. He denied taking any medications, including over-the-counter herbal supplements or analgesics. He drank alcohol occasionally (2–3 standard drinks several days per month). He identified as a man who has sex with men and reported unprotected sexual encounters with multiple partners several months before symptom onset. On examination, he had a diffuse papular rash involving the palms, soles, trunk, face, and groin, as well as scleral icterus; the abdomen was soft, without hepatomegaly or tenderness. Laboratory studies showed an alkaline phosphatase level of 279 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 70 U/L, alanine transaminase 167 U/L, total bilirubin 5.6 mg/dL (conjugated bilirubin 3.7 mg/dL), and serum albumin 2.6 g/dL. Urinalysis revealed 3+ bilirubin and 4+ protein; serum creatinine was 1.0 mg/dL. The random urine protein-to-creatinine ratio was 6,667 mg/g, and a 24-hour urine collection showed 13.3 g of protein. Five days before presentation, antimitochondrial antibody and antismooth muscle antibody had returned positive (AMA 57 U; ASMA 31 U). Hepatitis A, B, and C serologies were negative. Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography demonstrated moderate intrahepatic biliary dilation and heterogeneous perfusion of the liver. Hepatology was consulted and recommended evaluation for systemic infectious or inflammatory etiologies and repeat autoantibody testing.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 71-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of a cardiac mass identified during work-up for hypertensive urgency. She reported chest tightness and malaise. Her history was notable for well-controlled hypertension, dyslipidaemia, non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, and gallstone disease; there was no fever or signs of infection. Physical examination was unremarkable. Electrocardiography and laboratory tests excluded an acute coronary syndrome. 

Transthoracic echocardiography revealed an echo-lucent, partially calcified lesion measuring 28×24×21 mm at the posterolateral mitral annulus, without mitral regurgitation or stenosis and with normal biventricular function. Given the irregular borders of the mass, further evaluation was undertaken.

Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging demonstrated an 18×23×31 mm heterogeneous mass adjacent to the posterior mitral annulus. First-pass perfusion sequences showed no perfusion of the mass, and late gadolinium enhancement revealed contrast uptake only at the periphery. Native T1 mapping showed a hyperintense central region with elevated values, and T2 mapping was also elevated. 

Transesophageal echocardiography excluded mobile vegetations. An atherosclerotic plaque was noted in the aortic arch on imaging.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 22-year-old man with no significant medical or family history presented with a 2-week history of progressive abdominal distention associated with generalized abdominal pain, myalgia, fatigue, decreased oral intake, nausea, and unintentional weight loss. On examination, his vital signs were within normal limits. Abdominal examination revealed distention with shifting dullness; cardiopulmonary, skin, and scleral examinations were unremarkable. Laboratory tests showed:  
- White-cell count 10.6 × 10^9/L  
- Hemoglobin 7.3 g/dL  
- Platelet count 557,000/µL  

Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis demonstrated moderate ascites, hepatosplenomegaly, and a 4.7 × 3.3 cm lobular omental mass in the lower pelvis. A diagnostic paracentesis yielded 3.2 L of grossly bloody fluid with a low serum–ascites albumin gradient. Ascitic-fluid analysis showed:  
- Red-blood-cell count >900,000/µL  
- Total nucleated cells 3,382/µL (41% neutrophils, 30% lymphocytes, 0% eosinophils, 1% monocytes, 28% other cells)  
- Negative bacterial cultures  
- Cytology revealing mononuclear cells of possible mesothelial origin, inflammatory cells, and histiocytes in a hemorrhagic background

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 47-year-old Japanese woman with no breast symptoms was referred after screening mammography revealed clustered pleomorphic calcifications in the left breast. Her mother had bilateral breast and ovarian cancer. On examination, there was no palpable mass or nipple discharge. Serum tumor markers (CEA, CA15-3, NCC-ST-439, BCA225) were within normal limits. Breast ultrasound showed a 13×12×7 mm irregular, hypoechoic mass with clear margins and internal high-echogenic foci in the left breast. MRI demonstrated a 14×11×12 mm lesion with early arterial enhancement in the same region. PET-CT revealed focal uptake (SUVmax 3.54) in the left breast without abnormal uptake elsewhere. Core-needle biopsy of the mass showed ductal carcinoma in situ, nuclear grade 2, negative for ER, PgR, and HER2, with a Ki-67 labeling index of 32.4%.
