Given her family history, hereditary breast and ovarian cancer syndrome was considered, but the patient declined genetic testing. The patient elected to undergo total left mastectomy with sentinel lymph node biopsy.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 26‐year‐old woman presented with a 2-month history of progressive lower-limb weakness. She initially walked with crutches, but by presentation she was wheelchair bound and had bladder and bowel incontinence. She denied trauma, fever, or prior medical comorbidities. On examination, she had a palpable thyroid nodule and hirsutism. Neurologic examination showed increased lower-limb reflexes, clonus, hypertonia, a positive Babinski sign, and paraparesis worse on the right. There was a T4 sensory level, diminished anal sphincter tone, and reduced perianal sensation. Her upper limbs and cranial nerves were intact, and there were no signs of infection or spinal tenderness. Laboratory studies revealed negative CEA, negative antinuclear antibodies, negative HIV, normal thyroid‐stimulating hormone, thyroxine, triiodothyronine, parathyroid hormone, CA-19-9, CA-125, full blood count, urea and electrolytes, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, liver enzymes, lactate dehydrogenase, and normal urine electrophoresis with Bence-Jones protein of 0. Surveillance CT scans of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis showed no primary lesion. Thyroid ultrasound confirmed a solitary nodule. Spinal CT demonstrated expansile osteolysis and collapse of the T2 and T3 vertebral bodies with residual trabeculae, a sclerotic rim, and segmental kyphosis. Sagittal and axial MRI showed vertebral collapse involving posterior elements, high-grade cord compression, lesions that were T1 isointense to gray matter, slightly T2 hyperintense, and demonstrated gadolinium enhancement. CT-guided biopsy of the thoracic lesion and fine-needle biopsy of the thyroid nodule were performed.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 43-year-old Japanese man noted a gradually enlarging soft tissue mass in his right thigh since January 2015. He had no significant personal or family history. On examination, a hard, elastic, non-tender, non-pulsatile mass measuring approximately 30 cm in diameter was palpated in the right thigh without overlying skin changes. Laboratory studies were unremarkable. Plain radiography showed a large soft tissue mass without calcification, periosteal reaction, or femoral involvement. MRI revealed that the lesion was isointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2-weighted images, with peripheral, septal gadolinium enhancement. CT imaging confirmed the absence of calcification and no obvious cortical invasion of the femur. Differential diagnoses included malignant myxoid soft tissue tumors such as myxoid liposarcoma, myxofibrosarcoma, and metastatic carcinomas (myoepithelial or neuroendocrine). An incisional biopsy demonstrated a lobulated tumor with a myxoid matrix and atypical spindle-shaped cells forming eosinophilic cord-like structures. Immunohistochemical staining showed S-100 positivity; synaptophysin, chromogranin A, and pan-keratin (AE1/AE3) were negative; Ki-67 labeling index was approximately 10%.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 16-year-old male presented with a 3-month history of a gradually enlarging mass over the left lower back and pain in the right pelvic region and bilateral lower back that worsened with activity and radiated into the lower limbs. He had no history of trauma or surgery.

On examination, there was a well-circumscribed, soft, compressible subcutaneous swelling over the left lower back and two vague masses palpable in the bilateral iliac regions.

Ultrasonography of the lower back and abdomen demonstrated sizable multiseptated cystic lesions containing echogenic fluid within both psoas muscles, extending into the subcutaneous tissues and abutting the inferior poles of both kidneys.

Noncontrast CT of the abdomen showed a large multilocular cystic mass involving the bilateral psoas muscles, the left extensor muscles, and the left posterolateral abdominal wall, with attenuation values of +20 to +27 HU and calcifications in the walls and septa but no fatty components. Multiple sharply marginated osteolytic lesions were noted in the lumbar and sacral vertebrae.

MRI revealed multiloculated lesions in both psoas muscles extending to the left extensor muscle and posterolateral abdominal wall, with low signal on T1-weighted images, high signal on T2-weighted images, no restricted diffusion on DWI, and peripheral capsular and septal enhancement after gadolinium administration. Lesions extended into the lumbosacral spinal canal and intervertebral foramina, compressing the cauda equina nerve roots.

Chest CT demonstrated interstitial thickening with nodules in the lung fields, no pleural effusion or mediastinal adenopathy, and well-circumscribed osteolytic lesions in the thoracic vertebrae.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 68-year-old man with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease underwent an uncomplicated bilateral lung transplant four months earlier. His regimen included prednisone 20 mg daily, mycophenolate mofetil 1000 mg twice daily, and tacrolimus 5 mg twice daily (target trough 8–12 ng/mL; prior 3-month average 10.5 ± 4.1 ng/mL). He denied recent infections, immunizations, or new medications. He presented with two weeks of dizziness, generalized weakness, fatigue, and difficulty ambulating. On initial exam, upper-extremity strength was normal, lower-extremity strength was 3/5 symmetrically, there were no sensory deficits, and deep tendon reflexes were normal. Tacrolimus dosing was adjusted to maintain therapeutic levels. Serum PCR for cytomegalovirus and Epstein–Barr virus was negative. Brain MRI with contrast was normal.

Over the next two weeks, his lower-extremity strength declined further to 1/5 bilaterally, and deep tendon reflexes became hypoactive. Lumbar puncture showed cerebrospinal fluid protein 76 mg/dL and glucose 68 mg/dL. CSF, blood, and urine cultures were negative; repeat serum CMV PCR was negative. Spinal MRI demonstrated mild degenerative cervical changes without spinal cord compression. Electromyogram and nerve conduction studies revealed increased latency and decreased amplitude of the left ulnar sensory and left sural sensory potentials and decreased conduction velocity and amplitude of the left tibial and ulnar motor potentials, consistent with an acute demyelinating polyneuropathy with axonal changes.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 64-year-old man presented with two days of worsening pain in the left posterior thigh associated with an enlarging mass. He reported that the mass had been slowly growing for 12 years but had only recently become painful. He denied trauma and was not on anticoagulation. On examination, there was a large, firm, tense, relatively non-tender mass in the left posterior thigh. Strength and range of motion at the knee were full, and sensation was intact distally without paresthesias.

Contrast CT and MRI of the thigh demonstrated a 16.0 × 13.7 × 17.1 cm multiloculated, cystic mass in the posterior compartment with fluid–fluid levels, a thick fibrous rim, and splaying of adjacent muscles; the femoral cortex was abutted without invasion. Proximal and distal segments of the sciatic nerve were visualized, but the nerve was not seen at the mass level. The radiology report favored a chronic hematoma.

Ultrasound-guided aspiration yielded 1.8 L of serosanguinous fluid. Cultures were negative. Cytology showed scattered lymphocytes, blood clot, and hemosiderin-laden macrophages without atypical cells, again suggesting chronic hematoma. Within 48 hours, the fluid had reaccumulated.

An open irrigation and debridement with biopsy was performed. Approximately 1.5 L of bloody fluid was evacuated through a rind-like capsule, and small areas of solid hypertrophic tissue at the periphery were sampled. No large bleeding vessel was identified, but diffuse capillary oozing prompted specimen collection for pathology. A vacuum-assisted closure device was placed.

Four days later, repeat irrigation and debridement revealed another substantial blood collection despite the VAC. Two dilated, thrombosed vessels were debrided, and fibrin sealant was applied to all exposed surfaces. Two drains were placed, the hamstring was imbricated to obliterate dead space, and multilayered closure with compressive wrapping was performed.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A previously healthy 16-year-old female presented with a four-day history of abdominal pain, vomiting, fever, headache, myalgias, and cough. In the referring emergency department, her temperature was 39.4°C, pulse 154 beats/min, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, blood pressure 115/61 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 96%. On examination, she was pale and had tenderness in the right lower quadrant without guarding or rebound. 

Initial laboratory tests showed a white-cell count of 5.8×10^3/µL with 93% neutrophils, hemoglobin 11.9 g/dL, platelets 102×10^3/µL, C-reactive protein 11 mg/dL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate 26 mm/hr, and procalcitonin 0.50 ng/mL. A pregnancy test was negative. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis demonstrated mesenteric edema, an 8-mm dilated appendix, and fat stranding throughout the lower abdomen and pelvis.

She received two intravenous fluid boluses and antipyretics and was transferred to our pediatric hospital. On arrival, the pediatric surgery team felt the presentation was not consistent with acute appendicitis. Further examination revealed mild bilateral conjunctival injection and neck tenderness in addition to right lower quadrant pain. She reported a positive SARS-CoV-2 PCR result one month earlier. Additional tests showed positive SARS-CoV-2 IgG antibody and normal troponin and N-terminal pro–brain natriuretic peptide levels. Given persistent fever, positive SARS-CoV-2 IgG, laboratory evidence of inflammation, and multisystem involvement, she was hospitalized for further monitoring and treatment.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 6-year-old boy was brought by his parents for evaluation of firm, non-fluctuant swellings of the jaws noticed 2 months earlier. He had no family history of jaw disease. On examination, there was bilateral buccal cortical expansion of the mandible from the deciduous canine to the deciduous molar, with intact overlying mucosa. Panoramic radiography showed a large, well-demarcated lesion extending from the right mandibular ramus to the left ramus, characterized by central heterogeneous mineralization surrounded by a thin radiolucent zone with a sclerotic border; several tooth germs were displaced, and multiple primary tooth roots were resorbed. A CT scan confirmed thinning of the inferior mandibular cortex, alveolar bone expansion, and a displaced maxillary tooth germ projecting into the maxillary sinus. Serum calcium, phosphorus, alkaline phosphatase, and parathyroid hormone levels were all within normal limits. Incisional biopsies of the mandibular and maxillary lesions showed fibrous stroma rich in fibroblasts with spherical calcifications; the mandibular specimen had only sparse small calcifications, whereas the maxillary specimen contained more abundant calcified spheres. Direct sequencing of GNAS (Arg201 and Gln227) and HRPT2 genes on lesion tissue showed no mutations.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A previously healthy 3-year-old boy presented with a 3-day history of drooping of both upper eyelids and a 2-day history of unsteady gait with sleepiness and reduced activity. He had no seizures, visual blurring, limb weakness, swallowing or breathing difficulties, or bladder dysfunction. On examination, he was alert (GCS 15), with bilateral ptosis; vision, extraocular movements, pupils, light reflexes, and fundoscopy were normal. Other cranial nerves were intact. He had a broad-based ataxic gait and bilateral dysmetria on finger-nose testing. There was no dysarthria, nystagmus, involuntary movements, or Romberg sign. Limb tone and power were normal, tendon reflexes were present, and plantar responses were flexor. There were no signs of fatigability, sensory loss, or meningeal irritation. Vital signs, cardiovascular, and respiratory examinations were unremarkable. Laboratory tests showed normal complete blood count, C-reactive protein, electrolytes, and calcium; ESR was 22 mm in the first hour. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis revealed 2 polymorphonuclear cells/mm3 and 20 lymphocytes/mm3, with protein 55 mg/dL and negative oligoclonal bands. Electroencephalogram and noncontrast CT of the brain were normal. MRI of the brain demonstrated high signal intensities in the subcortical white matter of the parietal and occipital lobes, midbrain, cerebellar vermis, right cerebellar hemisphere, and right caudate and lentiform nuclei, without diffusion restriction or contrast enhancement.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 64-year-old man with hypertension, diabetes mellitus, and coronary artery disease treated with multiple percutaneous coronary interventions (most recently 2 weeks earlier) presented with sudden-onset precordial chest pain and dyspnoea on exertion similar to his prior angina. The pain was initially relieved by sublingual nitroglycerine but recurred, prompting presentation to the emergency department. On examination, his heart rate was 75 beats per minute, blood pressure 167/72 mmHg, respiratory rate 20 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air; his body mass index was 35 kg/m2. Physical examination showed a regular rate and rhythm, clear lungs, and jugular venous pressure of 9 cm H2O. Serial cardiac enzymes were normal (troponin T 0.02 ng/mL, normal 0–0.03 ng/mL), and electrocardiography demonstrated sinus rhythm with right bundle branch block and lateral T-wave inversions unchanged from prior studies. His home medications included aspirin, clopidogrel, atorvastatin, metoprolol tartrate, amlodipine, ranolazine, and furosemide. A transthoracic echocardiogram revealed a homogeneous, echo-dense intracardiac mass measuring 4 cm × 4 cm, located within or attached to the superior interatrial septum and extending superiorly and posteriorly to the aortic root, moving synchronously with the atrial walls. Right and left atrial sizes and left ventricular size, wall thickness, and systolic function were normal, and no tricuspid regurgitation was seen. There was no mention of a similar mass on a transthoracic echocardiogram performed one year earlier.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

options.A: Lipomatous hypertrophy of the interatrial septum

question:
A 41-year-old woman was referred for therapy-resistant dyspnea and angioedema of unknown origin. Six weeks earlier, she had developed progressive dyspnea and coughing, culminating in a severe coughing attack with syncope. She was initially treated for presumed allergic asthma with inhaled bronchodilators, oral glucocorticoids, and antihistamines without improvement and spent 14 days in a local hospital before referral.

On examination, she was obese (160 cm, 85 kg) with significant swelling of the face and neck, bilateral subconjunctival hemorrhages, and basal rhonchi; there were no urticarial hives. She had a five pack-year smoking history (quit at symptom onset) and reported allergies to dust mites, pollen, hay, straw, cats, and dogs. Her medications included prednisolone 25 mg daily, levocetirizine as needed, inhaled budesonide, fenoterol/ipratropium bromide as needed, dihydrocodeine, transdermal nicotine, and local antifungal therapy for laryngeal thrush.

Initial laboratory studies showed CRP 0.71 mg/dL (normal <0.5) and a leukocytosis of 30–40 ×10^9 cells/L with 44% lymphocytes. Chest radiography and CT of the head, neck, and chest were unremarkable except for an enlarged thyroid gland; thyroid function tests confirmed a euthyroid multinodular goitre without antibodies. Morning cortisol and ACTH levels were normal. A RAST panel was negative for common inhalant and food allergens, though total IgE was elevated at 310 kU/L (normal <100). Multiple lung function tests excluded obstructive ventilatory disease. Complement C4 and C1-INH levels were normal. Echocardiography was unremarkable.

Because of the marked lymphocytosis, hematology evaluated for lymphoproliferative disease: immunophenotyping showed increased T cells but no clonal population; serum electrophoresis, abdominal sonography, and CT of the head, neck, chest, and abdomen revealed no malignancy. Repeat chest CT 13 days later showed peribronchovascular ground-glass opacities in the mediobasal segment of the left lower lobe and minimal tree-in-bud opacities in the left upper lobe. An interferon-γ release assay for Mycobacterium tuberculosis was negative. Extensive autoantibody testing was negative. Oropharyngeal swab cultures and pneumococcal urine antigen testing yielded no new findings. Serologies for respiratory syncytial virus, coxsackievirus, echovirus, adenovirus, Chlamydia pneumoniae, Legionella pneumophila, and Mycoplasma pneumoniae were negative.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 41-year-old woman presented with a 2–3 cm hard, irregular lump in the lower outer quadrant of her left breast. She had no hormonal symptoms, weight loss, or family history of breast cancer. Three-year-old bilateral mammograms were normal. Current imaging showed an asymmetrical density on the left mammogram (BI-RADS M4), and tomosynthesis confirmed an ill-defined 28 mm mass. Ultrasound demonstrated a suspicious ill-defined lesion (U4) with normal axillary nodes. An image-guided core biopsy revealed a grade 2 invasive carcinoma interpreted as apocrine carcinoma (B5b); ER and HER2 were negative. 

The patient underwent wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy. Grossly, there was a well-defined pale tumour measuring 22 mm. Microscopically, the neoplasm was well demarcated with foci of papillary architecture, comprising large tall cells in solid nests with central fibrovascular cores and luminal histiocytes. The cells had eosinophilic cytoplasm and vesicular nuclei, and cystic spaces contained luminal eosinophilic secretions. Notably, in several areas the nuclei of the tall columnar cells were located at the luminal surface rather than the basement membrane (“reversed polarity”). Focal luminal calcifications were present. No lymphovascular invasion or lymph node involvement was identified.

Immunohistochemical studies showed strong CK7 positivity, ER and HER2 negativity, and no myoepithelial layer around lesional structures on p63 and CK5 stains. GATA3 demonstrated moderate nuclear positivity; CK5 was patchy; calretinin was positive; and TTF1 was negative.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.C: Tall cell carcinoma of the breast with reversed polarity

question:
A 10-year-old spayed female domestic shorthair cat was evaluated for a 2-day history of anorexia, vomiting and lethargy. There was a 1-year history of suspected weight loss and intermittent reduced appetite. The cat had outdoor access, was up to date on vaccines and anthelmintics, and was fed a commercial diet. On presentation the cat was lethargic but alert; body condition score was 4/9. Vital signs showed mild tachycardia (200 beats/min), and there was mild diffuse discomfort on abdominal palpation.

Complete blood count was within reference intervals, but blood smear evaluation showed toxic and hyposegmented neutrophils. Serum biochemistry revealed a severe elevation in alanine transaminase activity (456 U/l; RI 12–130), mild hyperglobulinaemia (53 g/l; RI 28–51), moderate hyperbilirubinaemia (14 µmol/l; RI 2–6) and a severe elevation in serum amyloid A (138.6 µg/ml; RI 0–12).

Abdominal ultrasound demonstrated a diffusely hypoechoic liver with thickening and dilation of the intrahepatic biliary tract and cystic duct. In the common bile duct, a hyperechoic spindle-shaped structure measuring 12 × 2.5 mm with linear interfaces and mild acoustic shadowing was seen. A second linear hyperechoic structure extended into the duodenal papilla without distal common bile duct dilation. Severe gallbladder wall thickening (2.1 mm) consistent with cholecystitis was also noted. The ultrasonographic appearance of the two spindle-shaped structures was suggestive of vegetal foreign bodies (grass awns) or a conglomerate of biliary sludge; biliary worms were deemed unlikely given the geographical location.

Fine-needle aspirates of liver and bile showed rare plasmacytes and lymphocytes in liver tissue and septic suppurative inflammation in bile. Culture of bile yielded Escherichia coli sensitive to marbofloxacin, doxycycline, amoxicillin and sulfatrimethoprim. The cat was hospitalized for IV fluids, empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics, antiemetics, analgesia and assisted enteral feeding.

Despite 8 days of treatment, the cat remained lethargic and anorexic. Repeat biochemistry showed persistent elevations in liver enzymes and the development of severe ionized hypercalcaemia (1.87 mmol/l; RI 1.1–1.4) with an increased phosphorus concentration. Follow-up ultrasound supported retrograde migration of the spindle-shaped structures within the biliary tract.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 50-year-old multigravida woman presented with a 10-day history of intermittent, diffuse lower abdominal pain, more pronounced on the right. She had a known history of uterine leiomyomas. There were no urinary or gastrointestinal symptoms. Vital signs were stable, and she was afebrile. On examination, she had focal tenderness in the right lower abdomen. Laboratory tests showed a white-cell count of 10 000 per mm3, hemoglobin 12 g/dL, C-reactive protein 0.6 mg/L, and a negative pregnancy test. Transabdominal ultrasound was inconclusive because of a large abdominopelvic mass with marked posterior attenuation; the patient declined transvaginal ultrasound. Because of persistent pain, an MRI was obtained. On MRI, a solid abdominopelvic mass was seen arising from the region of the right uterine cornua via a broad pedicle. T2-weighted images demonstrated flow voids at the uterine attachment (“bridging-vessel sign”). The mass was isointense to myometrium on T1-weighted images and heterogeneously hyperintense on T2-weighted images without diffusion restriction. After gadolinium administration, most of the mass showed no enhancement, but there was a thin enhancing rim and a small eccentric zone of heterogeneous enhancement near its uterine attachment. The pedicle was thickened and twisted in a spiral fashion. The right ovary was enlarged with heterogeneous T2 signal and peripheral follicles (“string of pearls”), and areas of T1 and T2 hyperintensity in the ovarian stroma suggested hemorrhage. The right pelvic ureter was kinked at the twisted pedicle, causing ipsilateral ureterohydronephrosis.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

options.A: Torsion of a subserosal uterine leiomyoma

question:
A 41-year-old woman with a history of a right-sided accessory pathway ablated at age 19 presented with recurrent palpitations and a regular wide complex tachycardia (WCT) that had terminated with 6 mg of intravenous adenosine at an outside hospital. One week later, she had a similar episode.  
– ECG during tachycardia: left bundle-branch block pattern, left superior axis, cycle length 280 ms.  
– Sinus-rhythm ECG: no preexcitation.  
– Echocardiogram and cardiac magnetic resonance imaging: normal.  

Electrophysiologic study:  
– Baseline AH interval 65 ms, HV interval 37 ms; dual AV nodal physiology present.  
– Rapid pacing from the proximal coronary sinus reproduced the clinical arrhythmia. Retrograde conduction was concentric; adenosine caused ventriculoatrial block.  
– During atrial pacing at a cycle length of 370 ms, conduction initially over the fast AV nodal pathway produced a narrow QRS; when conduction abruptly switched to the slow pathway (AH increased from 105 to 194 ms), one fusion beat occurred, followed by fully preexcited complexes with the His potential displaced into the ventricular electrogram. Incremental atrial pacing showed progressive prolongation of the stimulus-delta interval, consistent with a decremental accessory pathway.  
– In sinus rhythm without preexcitation, right ventricular apex activation preceded tricuspid annulus activation; this relationship reversed with preexcitation.  
– During atrial pacing under isoproterenol infusion, WCT with AV dissociation was consistently induced. The induced tachycardia had identical QRS morphology to the clinical arrhythmia and terminated with rapid ventricular pacing or adenosine. Fusion beats were observed both during induction of tachycardia and during the tachycardia itself.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

options.C: Mahaim pathway tachycardia due to triggered activity
options.D: Passive bystander in AV nodal reentry

question:
A 25-year-old man presented with difficulty fitting his right ocular prosthesis. He had undergone enucleation of the right eye at age 4 for retinoblastoma in France; no orbital implant had been placed. On examination, the right anophthalmic socket showed a thickened bulbar conjunctiva with a central, translucid, round area that was vascularized, and an associated inferior symblepharon. Orbital computed tomography revealed a bilobated, cyst‐like structure in the right orbit, with hyperdense walls and hypodense content. Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrated an ovoid cystic structure with an increased anteroposterior diameter, a T1 signal identical to that of an atrophied optic nerve, and no contrast uptake to suggest an expanding lesion. Comparison with a CT scan obtained six years earlier showed no such lesion.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 50-year-old woman underwent revision total hip arthroplasty (THA) for recurrent dislocation after an initial THA for idiopathic osteonecrosis. She had no personal or family history of bleeding and preoperative coagulation studies were normal (APTT 29.8 s, PT-INR 0.98). No unusual bleeding was seen intraoperatively.

On postoperative day 21, she developed sudden right thigh pain and swelling. Laboratory testing showed C-reactive protein 495 nmol/L, white cells 10.9 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 80 g/L, and normal coagulation (APTT 28.2 s, PT-INR 1.11). An emergency operation evacuated a large hematoma; tissue cultures were sterile.

Ten days later, the wound again discharged bloody fluid. Contrast CT demonstrated a mass extending from the right hip to the proximal femur; angiography showed no active bleeding. Hemoglobin had fallen to 67 g/L, and routine coagulation studies, including von Willebrand factor, remained normal.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 2-day-old full-term male neonate was brought to the emergency department for bilateral breast swelling noted since birth. There was erythema over both breasts, and the parents reported milk-like nipple discharge on the right side. Prenatal and delivery histories were unremarkable. On examination, the infant was afebrile and had normal vital signs but slightly decreased activity. Bilateral breasts were symmetrically enlarged with erythematous overlying skin and palpable, subareolar masses. No axillary lymphadenopathy was present. Laboratory tests showed a normal blood count, liver and renal function, electrolytes, glucose, and immunoglobulin levels. A nasal swab for methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus was negative. With a working diagnosis of neonatal mastitis, parenteral clindamycin was planned. Prior to initiating antibiotics, a breast ultrasound was obtained.  
On ultrasound, both breasts showed well-defined, ovoid retroareolar masses (approximately 24 × 24 × 9 mm on the right and 26 × 25 × 10 mm on the left) composed of central heterogeneously hypoechoic tissue surrounded by multiple small peripheral cysts (largest 2.5 mm). There was mild hyperechogenicity of the adjacent subcutaneous tissue. Color Doppler imaging demonstrated scattered intralesional vascular poles oriented perpendicular to the skin, converging on the retroareolar region. Both lesions were compressible on probe pressure.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 67-year-old man presented in June 2013 with a swollen left hand after a laceration to his third finger sustained while working in soil. He also maintained three large hobby aquariums. He was treated with a 10-day course of cefprozil and clindamycin, with only marginal improvement. In October 2013, he returned with worsening stiffness and swelling of the left hand and digits. Rheumatology consultation led to a diagnosis of seronegative rheumatoid arthritis; all autoimmune serology was negative, and inflammatory markers were normal. A pulsed regimen of prednisone led to partial improvement, but swelling recurred. Sulfasalazine was added, with incomplete benefit and progression to involve the left elbow. Multiple aspirations and intra-articular steroid injections of the left elbow, wrist, and metacarpophalangeal joints yielded minimal improvement, and standard bacterial cultures were negative. Methotrexate and leflunomide were then initiated. By January 2014, firm nodules had developed along the extensor surfaces of the left arm and hand—including the ulnar aspect of the thumb, radial index finger, styloid process, and mid-shaft of the ulna. In February 2014, adalimumab was started and prednisone and sulfasalazine were discontinued; methotrexate and leflunomide were continued. After three months, he still had a swollen olecranon bursa and new lower extremity nodules. Aspirations of the wrist and knee remained microscopy-negative, with no bacterial growth. In December 2014, sulfasalazine and prednisone were restarted alongside a switch to etanercept, after which his nodular lesions dramatically worsened.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 10-month-old girl with an uncomplicated antenatal course and term birth presented in the neonatal period with abdominal distension at 1 week and two episodes of macroscopic haematuria by 2 weeks. On evaluation then, she was pale, hypertensive (blood pressure above the 99th percentile), and had a palpable mass in the left lumbar region. Abdominal ultrasonography revealed a retroperitoneal mass anteromedial to the left kidney. Contrast-enhanced CT demonstrated a renal mass arising from the upper pole of the left kidney without focal invasion or distant metastasis (stage I). At 1 month, she underwent left nephrectomy with mesenteric lymph node excision. Histological examination showed spindle cells arranged in fascicles with scant cytoplasm, moderate pleomorphism, numerous mitotic figures, erratic thin-walled vessels with a hemangiopericytoma-like pattern, and entrapped glomeruli; lymph nodes and surgical margins were free of tumour. Immunohistochemical staining was positive for CD99 and vimentin with a Ki-67 index of 45%, and negative for desmin, caldesmon, SMA, MyoD1, cyclin D1, CD34, WT1, NSE, cytokeratin, and neurofilament. Molecular genetic studies revealed an EGFR internal tandem duplication with BCOR and pan-TRK negativity, confirming a cellular subtype of congenital mesoblastic nephroma. She was followed clinically with abdominal ultrasound every 6 weeks and remained asymptomatic and normotensive until 10 months of age, when she developed gradual-onset progressive dyspnoea, post-feed vomiting, poor feeding, and weight loss. On examination, she was tachypnoeic with reduced expansion of the right hemithorax, dull percussion, reduced vocal resonance, and diminished air entry. Chest radiography showed an opacity occupying the right hemithorax with mediastinal shift to the left. Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest revealed a large (8.6 × 6 × 7 cm) heterogeneous enhancing soft-tissue mass with necrotic regions in the right hemithorax, causing mediastinal shift and collapse of the right lower lobe.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.D: Clear cell sarcoma of the kidney

question:
A 40-year-old Caucasian man presented with a 2-month history of exertional dyspnea and had New York Heart Association class II symptoms. He had been therapeutically anticoagulated for 5 months for a presumed diagnosis of CTEPH based on imaging at a local hospital. Repeat CT pulmonary angiography showed extensive proximal narrowing of both the right and left pulmonary arteries with concentric soft-tissue thickening, and occlusion of the right upper-lobe and left apical segmental branches. Full blood count and renal function were normal. C-reactive protein was 77 mg/L, and the erythrocyte sedimentation rate was 79 mm/hr. Tests for antinuclear antibodies and anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies were negative. MRI pulmonary angiography confirmed smooth plaque-like focal intimal thickening in both proximal main pulmonary arteries. Because of concern for pulmonary artery sarcoma, an 18F-FDG PET/CT was performed and showed intermediate uptake in the distal right main pulmonary artery. Right-heart catheterization revealed a mean pulmonary artery pressure of 32 mmHg with a cardiac index of 2.0 L/min/m2.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

An 18-year-old Asian man with no significant medical history presented with recurrent episodes of left upper back pain, chest wall discomfort, and left upper extremity weakness over a 2-year period. Two years prior, after a minor soccer collision, he had sudden severe left back pain and left arm weakness that resolved in 4 weeks with nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs. Over the ensuing 2 years, he experienced 10 similar episodes, each resolving within weeks. 

On examination, vital signs were normal. He had severe spontaneous pain over the left upper back and left chest that did not conform to a dermatomal distribution. Muscle strength of the left trapezius and entire left upper extremity was Medical Research Council grade 3. There was hyperesthesia in the proximal left upper extremity, also non-dermatomal. Deep tendon reflexes were normal throughout, and there were no bladder or rectal symptoms.

Laboratory tests, including assessments for adrenal insufficiency, thyroid function, electrolytes, and trace elements, were unremarkable. Under the suspicion of thoracic outlet syndrome, Adson and Wright tests were performed and were negative. Cervical spine MRI was normal. Thoracic spine MRI revealed a tiny Th4–5 disc herniation, an unusually short distance between the spinal cord and adjacent vertebral bodies, and loss of cerebrospinal fluid anterior to the spinal cord.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 70-year-old man was referred with a 6-week history of a left-sided scrotal swelling that was not painful but had increased in size. His medical history included hypertension, eczema, and a right orchidectomy with chemotherapy for seminoma 25 years earlier. On examination, he had a large left hemi-scrotal swelling with an impalpable left testis.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 131 g/L, normal inflammatory markers, lactate dehydrogenase 743 IU/L (elevated), alpha-fetoprotein 4 ku/L (normal), and β-hCG 3 IU/L (upper limit of normal 2 IU/L).

Scrotal ultrasound revealed a grossly enlarged left testicle with heterogeneous echotexture, loss of normal architecture, and chaotic intratesticular perfusion on color Doppler. 

Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis demonstrated a left testicular soft-tissue mass extending through the left spermatic cord into the left gonadal vein up to the level of the left kidney, with an additional large left suprarenal soft-tissue mass. 

FDG-PET showed intense uptake in the left testicular mass, the extending gonadal-venous soft-tissue, and the left suprarenal mass.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 64-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of an abdominal mass incidentally detected on routine examination 6 months earlier. She denied fever, abdominal pain or distension, nausea, vomiting, weight loss, or changes in bowel habits. Her history was notable only for type 2 diabetes mellitus of 10 years’ duration; she had no tobacco or alcohol use. Physical examination was unremarkable. Urinalysis showed leukocytes 2+; fasting blood glucose was 6.97 mmol/L; complete blood count, liver and kidney function tests, and tumor markers were all within normal limits. Contrast-enhanced abdominal CT demonstrated a dumbbell-shaped, moderately inhomogeneous soft-tissue shadow measuring approximately 3.6 cm × 1.8 cm in the region of the stomach’s lesser curvature, with apparent origin from the gastric wall, and a mesenchymal stromal tumor was suspected. Two months before planned surgery, upper gastrointestinal endoscopy revealed chronic superficial gastritis without evidence of mucosal lesions or malignancy, and colonoscopy showed no abnormalities, ruling out colorectal metastasis. The tumor’s extraluminal location and lack of mucosal involvement reduced suspicion for a primary gastrointestinal lesion. Preoperative three-dimensional reconstruction showed a 4 cm × 2 cm × 1 cm dumbbell-shaped mass encircling the left gastric artery, splenic artery, and splenic vein to guide surgical planning. Preoperative diagnosis: abdominal mass (suspected gastric stromal tumor) and type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 53-year-old woman was referred for refractory hypertension and acute kidney injury secondary to suspected renal vascular disease. Two weeks earlier, she had presented with a hypertensive emergency (blood pressure, 249/99 mm Hg) and altered mental status attributed to posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome. At that time, her serum creatinine was 5.4 mg/dL (baseline, 0.9 mg/dL). Her blood pressure was controlled with intravenous nicardipine, and hemodialysis was initiated urgently.

Contrast-enhanced computed tomography angiography revealed a large segment of calcific plaque extending from just below the superior mesenteric artery to the inferior mesenteric artery, involving both renal arteries. Both renal arteries were occluded at their origins, with distal reconstitution via collateral vessels (right greater than left), and a subacute thrombus was noted at the orifice of the right renal artery.

On transfer, aortography confirmed extensive calcific plaque in the visceral aorta and bilateral renal artery occlusions. Given the severity of calcification, stenting was deemed unfeasible, and aortic endarterectomy was considered to carry high morbidity.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 69-year-old man with stage IV clear-cell renal carcinoma metastatic to the spine and brain presented with a 4-day history of gross hematuria, oliguria, fever (38.5 °C), fatigue, and decreased appetite. Two weeks earlier, he had started sunitinib 50 mg nightly. He had been on acetylsalicylic acid/dipyridamole for five years for stroke prevention. Hematuria persisted despite stopping aspirin/dipyridamole and a course of amoxicillin for presumed urinary tract infection. 

On arrival, blood pressure was 160/72 mmHg; digital rectal examination was negative for occult blood. Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 11.9 g/dL, platelets 68 000/mm3, sodium 120 mEq/L, blood urea nitrogen 41 mg/dL, and creatinine 2.7 mg/dL (baseline 1.0 mg/dL). Coagulation studies were normal. A urine dipstick was positive for blood and protein. Urine sodium was < 10 mEq/L, and 24-hour urinary protein was 484 mg. Haptoglobin was normal, and peripheral smear showed no schistocytes. Inflammatory markers were elevated (CRP 55 mg/L, ESR 80 mm/hr). Serologies for p-ANCA, c-ANCA (MPO, PR3), complement levels, ANA, anti-dsDNA, rheumatoid factor, hepatitis B and C, and cryoglobulins were all negative.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 12-year-old girl first experienced left lower rib pain; chest radiography was normal and the pain resolved with analgesics. Four months later, she developed right leg pain without trauma; plain radiography was again normal, but MRI showed a stress fracture of the tibia with surrounding inflammation. She was managed with offloading. Two months later, she had left shin and right wrist pain progressing to inability to walk, accompanied by intermittent fevers up to 103°F lasting 1–2 days and a nonpruritic erythematous maculopapular rash on her legs and abdomen. Hospital admission revealed periosteal reactions on X-rays of the left tibia, right fibula, and right distal ulna without fracture. Laboratory studies showed WBC 14,800/mm^3, hemoglobin 11.9 g/dL, platelets 514,000/mm^3, ANA 1:160 speckled, ESR 51 mm/hr, CRP 5.0 mg/dL; tuberculin skin test was negative. A bone scan demonstrated multiple areas of longitudinal linear uptake in the humeri, forearms, ribs, femora, and tibiae. Bone marrow biopsy was unremarkable. Whole-body MRI without contrast showed normal marrow signal without features of osteomyelitis. Over the next 6 months, her left leg pain fluctuated, and contrasted MRI of the left calf revealed periosteal reaction, soft-tissue rind, bone marrow edema, and enhancement around the mid-tibia.  

Three years later, she developed painful right visual loss (20/50 OD, 20/20 OS) with a right relative afferent pupillary defect and optic disc swelling. Fat-suppressed orbital MRI showed enhancement of both optic nerves (right > left). Tests for aquaporin-4-IgG, MOG-IgG, ACE, Bartonella, Lyme, FTA-ABS, p-ANCA, and c-ANCA were negative. She received IV steroids and IVIG followed by a 5-month oral taper, with recovery to 20/20 OD and resolution of disc edema.

Seven months later, she had bilateral disc edema (20/20 OU) and bilateral optic nerve enhancement on MRI despite normal CSF (opening pressure 12 cm H2O; negative aquaporin-4-IgG and MOG-IgG). She declined treatment. Two months later, she noted left visual loss (20/20 OD, 20/40 OS) with pain; OD disc pallor, OS disc edema, and enhancement of both optic nerves and chiasm on MRI. IV and oral corticosteroids were given, and follow-up MRI 17 days later showed resolution of enhancement.

Seventeen months after her first optic neuropathy, she awoke with painless left vision loss (20/20 OD, CF OS) and funduscopic findings of a central retinal artery occlusion with cilioretinal artery sparing in the left eye. Carotid Doppler, echocardiography, fat-suppressed MRI of brain and orbits, brain and neck MRA were all normal. Hematologic testing—including CBC, ESR 17 mm/hr, Sjögren SSa/SSb, c-ANCA, p-ANCA, ACE, lysozyme, aquaporin-4-IgG, repeat MOG-IgG, and a hypercoagulable panel—were normal. Lipid panel showed mildly low triglycerides and elevated HDL ratio.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman was evaluated for 8 months of symptomatic hypoglycemia associated with syncope, relieved by carbohydrate intake. She had type 2 diabetes managed without medications since 2018 and had undergone Roux-en-Y gastric bypass 3 years earlier. On examination, her body-mass index was 29, and the abdominal examination was normal. Laboratory studies showed a hematocrit of 33.9%, potassium 3.4 mmol/L, plasma glucose 114 mg/dL, c-peptide 4.71 ng/mL, insulin 27.9 μU/mL, and hemoglobin A1c 4.9%. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy and endoscopic ultrasound demonstrated a large gastrojejunostomy anastomosis but no pancreatic mass. A selective arterial calcium stimulation test showed a fourfold increase in insulin secretion localized to the gastroduodenal artery. During a supervised 72-hour fast, her glucose level remained above 60 mg/dL. She was discharged on octreotide and a low-carbohydrate diet. Two weeks later, she presented with a blood glucose level of 44 mg/dL. CT of the abdomen again showed no enhancing pancreatic mass but revealed intrahepatic biliary ductal dilation and a pancreatic duct dilated to 4.3 mm concerning for a head mass.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 74-year–old man with no significant past or family history was referred for evaluation of severe osteoporosis. A DEXA scan revealed T-scores of –3.1 at L1–L4, –4.3 at the left femoral neck, –3.4 at the left total hip, –4.7 at the right femoral neck, –4.1 at the right total hip, and –1.3 at the right forearm. He reported 7 years of generalized body pain and progressive proximal muscle weakness, rendering him bed bound for the past 2 years despite calcium and calciferol supplementation. On exam, he had 4/5 strength in shoulder and pelvic girdle muscles, generalized muscle wasting, severe kyphosis, barrel chest with a flail segment, and used accessory muscles to breathe; vitals were stable.

Laboratory studies showed: serum phosphate 0.39 mmol/L (0.78–1.4), calcium 2.01 mmol/L (2.2–2.56), albumin 46 g/L (35–52), alkaline phosphatase 302 U/L (53–128), PTH 16.8 pmol/L (1.6–6.9), 25-hydroxy-vitamin D 45.2 nmol/L (> 72.5 replete), creatinine 59 µmol/L (64–104), and ESR 4 mm/hour (0–20). Fractional excretion of phosphate was 29% (< 5%), and TmP/GFR was 0.33 mmol/L (0.8–1.35), confirming marked renal phosphate wasting. Full blood count, liver function, thyroid function, uric acid, lipids, serum protein electrophoresis, Bence–Jones proteins, and testosterone were normal; celiac serologies (tissue transglutaminase, antigliadin, endomysial antibodies) were negative. Urine studies excluded Fanconi syndrome (normal aminoaciduria, no glycosuria, normal sodium and potassium excretion).

He was started on phosphate supplementation plus calcitriol and alfacalcidol. Despite normalization of calcium and vitamin D levels, he continued to have low serum phosphate and elevated alkaline phosphatase and parathyroid hormone concentrations.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 20-year-old man from northwest Iran presented with progressive visual field deterioration. Two years earlier, at age 18, he had ingested hand-made alcohol and suffered methanol poisoning that required eight hours of emergency hemodialysis. He had no prior medical issues, denied exposure to other toxins, and had no visual complaints before the poisoning.  

On examination, best-corrected visual acuity was 160/200 in each eye. He reported difficulty navigating his surroundings and relied on guidance. Neurologic examination and brain MRI were normal. In both eyes, pupils measured 6 mm in room light, with a dull pupillary reflex and no relative afferent defect. The anterior segments were unremarkable, with no significant refractive error and intact macular reflexes.  

Funduscopic examination revealed near-total optic disc cupping bilaterally, with only a narrow neuroretinal rim remaining. Optical coherence tomography showed severe thinning of the peripapillary retinal nerve fiber layer in both eyes. Intraocular pressure measured 13 mm Hg bilaterally by Goldmann applanation tonometry and by ocular response analysis; central corneal thickness was 535 µm. Gonioscopy demonstrated open anterior chamber angles without signs of previous angle-closure or pigment dispersion.  

Standard automated perimetry (Humphrey 24-2) revealed dense ring scotomas in both eyes.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 7-year-old girl presented with 4 months of progressive lower-limb weakness and spasms. There was no history of trauma, fever, or neurologic symptoms such as blurred vision or speech changes. On examination, she was afebrile and fully conscious. A firm, slightly tender 3×5 cm mass was palpated in the right supraclavicular region. Neurologic examination revealed quadriplegia (Medical Research Council grade 2–3/5), right worse than left, generalized hyperreflexia with sustained clonus, and extensor plantar responses; the jaw jerk was normal. Findings localized the lesion to the cervical spinal cord.

Routine chest radiography showed a widened mediastinum, clear lung fields, and no bone involvement. Hematologic studies, including a complete blood count, peripheral smear, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate, were normal.

Magnetic resonance imaging of the entire spine demonstrated a well-circumscribed, T2-hyperintense extramedullary lesion with a dumb-bell appearance at C6–T1. The lesion caused severe canal stenosis, widened the right C6/7 and C7/T1 neuroforamina, displaced the cord postero-laterally to the left, and extended peripherally along the C6/7 nerve root into the right supraclavicular region. There was increased T2 signal within the spinal cord from C3 to C8. Vertebral alignment was normal, with no bony destruction or marrow abnormalities.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 51-year-old man, former smoker, presented with a palpable mass in the right supraclavicular region. His ECOG performance status was 0, and he denied systemic symptoms. A biopsy of the lymph node revealed TTF1-positive lung adenocarcinoma with a KRAS p.G12C mutation; PD-L1 expression was 80% in tumor cells. Staging CT of the chest and abdomen showed right supraclavicular lymphadenopathy measuring 3.8 × 2.7 cm infiltrating the jugular vein, right hilar and right paratracheal nodes up to 3.8 × 2.7 cm, with tracheal compression; there was no detectable primary lung mass. PET-CT showed hypermetabolism in these nodes (SUVmax 6) without distant metastases, and brain MRI was negative. The disease was staged as cTxN3M0. Pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks was initiated.  

A few days after the first infusion, the supraclavicular mass at least doubled in size, became painful with erythema and edema of overlying skin, and the patient’s ECOG status worsened to 3; he reported hyporexia, asthenia, dysphagia, dysphonia, and syncopal episodes. Over the next three weeks, his symptoms resolved, his ECOG status returned to 0, and the supraclavicular mass visibly regressed. A CT scan performed immediately before the second pembrolizumab dose showed a partial reduction in size of the thoracic lymphadenopathies consistent with a partial response by RECIST 1.1.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 16-year-old boy presented with headache, convulsions, blurring of vision, and vertigo. His vital signs were stable, and he was fully conscious and oriented. Pupils were 2 mm bilaterally and equally reactive. There were no cranial nerve, motor, or sensory deficits. He had scattered melanocytic nevi over his back since birth, none larger than 1 cm, and no family history of melanoma. 

Contrast-enhanced CT of the brain revealed a heterogeneous high-density mass in the anterior left temporal lobe with contrast enhancement. MRI of the brain showed a T1-hyperintense and T2-hypointense mass in the left temporal lobe with perilesional edema, no dural attachment, and mild heterogeneous enhancement after gadolinium administration. The imaging prompted consideration of a hemorrhagic brain tumour.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 63-year-old Caucasian man with a 10-year history of biopsy-proven EGPA, who declined maintenance immunosuppression and was managed intermittently for asthma flares, presented to the emergency department with a 2-week history of sudden onset sharp right-sided lumbar back pain beginning after moving a heavy object. He also reported periumbilical pain worsened by meals, associated with nausea and loose stools.  
On examination: temperature 36.6°C; blood pressure 129/77 mm Hg; pulse 79 beats/min; respiratory rate 12 breaths/min. The abdomen was soft with periumbilical tenderness; the remainder of the examination was unremarkable.  
Laboratory studies revealed a leukocyte count of 14,240/μL (4,000–10,000) with 2% eosinophils, neutrophils 82%; C-reactive protein 104 mg/L (0–5); procalcitonin 7.4 ng/mL (0–0.25); normal renal function; alkaline phosphatase 217 U/L (40–150); normal transaminases. ANCA panel and ANA were negative; IgG4 was 125 mg/dL (2–96). Blood cultures were negative.  
Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed an asymmetric fusiform dilation of the infrarenal abdominal aorta measuring 3.6 × 3.2 cm along the right lateral and anterior margin at L4, with focal wall calcification and surrounding hazy soft-tissue stranding, findings interpreted as consistent with a mycotic aneurysm.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 15-week-old male domestic longhair kitten was evaluated for a 1-month history of intermittent generalized tremors and progressive abdominal distension. The kitten was fully vaccinated, dewormed, had not travelled outside the UK, and was fed a constant mixture of three commercial kitten diets according to manufacturers’ recommendations. No treats were given.

On examination, the kitten was bright and responsive, weighed 1.2 kg (body condition score 3/9), and had moderate muscle wasting. Heart rate, respiratory rate and temperature were normal. The long bones were visibly bowed but non-painful on palpation. The abdomen was markedly distended; palpation revealed a faeces-filled colon. Neurologic examination was otherwise unremarkable.

External haematology showed a mild leukocytosis and monocytosis. Serum biochemistry revealed hypocalcaemia (total calcium 1.42 mmol/L; RI 2.3–2.5) with a mildly decreased creatinine and mildly increased alkaline phosphatase. Pre- and post-prandial bile acids and total thyroxine were within normal limits; serum phosphorus and total protein were considered normal.

Further evaluation demonstrated ionized hypocalcaemia (0.78 mmol/L; RI 1.2–1.4), elevated parathyroid hormone (120 pg/mL; RI <40), normal magnesium, normal feline pancreatic lipase immunoassay, low 25-hydroxyvitamin D3 (75 nmol/L; RI 127–335), and high 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D3 (670 pmol/L; RI 90–342).

CT of the chest, abdomen and long bones revealed generalized marked osteopenia with abnormal metaphyseal mineralization and normal physes; the right femur, left shoulder and left stifle were most affected. There was a markedly distended colon with faeces and a subjectively narrowed pelvic canal. No malformation of the pelvic canal bones or pathologic fractures were identified.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 33-year-old man with a 23-year history of Crohn’s disease presented with 36 hours of myalgias, fatigue, headache, and epigastric pain. He denied any change in bowel habits and had no respiratory symptoms prior to admission. Two weeks earlier, ustekinumab therapy had been restarted for active Crohn’s disease; he had previously discontinued it after 5 months of therapy two years prior.

On presentation, temperature was 39.6 °C, heart rate 120 bpm, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, blood pressure 117/83 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Physical examination was notable only for epigastric tenderness; lung auscultation was clear. Initial chest radiography showed no acute airspace disease.

On hospital day 1, he developed cough, dyspnea, and hypoxemia, requiring 5 L/min of nasal cannula to maintain oxygen saturation > 90%. On hospital day 2, he was transferred to the ICU for high-flow nasal cannula at 75% FiO₂. Repeat chest radiography showed diffuse reticular infiltrates. Chest CT with contrast demonstrated bilateral consolidations and ground-glass opacities without pulmonary embolism. Laboratory testing revealed a white blood cell count of 16.3 × 10⁹/L (92% neutrophils) and C-reactive protein of 296.8 mg/L. Infectious workup—including blood cultures, respiratory pathogen panel, and bronchoalveolar lavage cultures—was negative. Serologic testing for connective tissue disease–associated interstitial lung disease was also negative.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

options.D: Connective tissue disease-associated interstitial lung disease

question:
A 44-year-old man presented with intermittent moderate pain in the lateral aspect of his right shoulder and restricted range of motion with difficulty lifting his arm, worsened by activity. He denied any history of trauma, heavy lifting, or sports participation. On examination, internal rotation and abduction of the shoulder elicited moderate pain; all other shoulder movements were within normal limits. Laboratory studies, including hemogram and serological tests, were within normal limits. Plain radiography of the right shoulder demonstrated a focal area of cortical thickening and irregularity in the middle one-third of the humerus at the deltoid insertion. Coronal CT images of the right humerus showed an intracortical hypodense lesion with expansion and focal protuberance of the overlying cortex. MRI of the shoulder revealed eccentric cortical thickening and intracortical altered signal intensity with cortical expansion and irregularity; the lesion was isointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2-weighted images with loss of the normal cortical low-signal band.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 20-year-old woman presented with back pain after a vertebral fragility fracture and reported 7 kg weight gain over 4 weeks, acne, easy bruising, proximal muscle weakness, and 1 year of oligomenorrhea. Blood pressure was 150/100 mm Hg; height 1.47 m; BMI 22.2 kg/m2. Examination showed thin skin, dorsocervical and supraclavicular fat pads, central obesity, acanthosis nigricans, moon facies, and purplish striae on the abdomen, thighs, and popliteal fossae; she was not hirsute. Secondary sexual characteristics and visual fields were normal. Laboratory studies excluded diabetes and electrolyte abnormalities. An 0800 h plasma cortisol was 808 nmol/L with ACTH <5 pg/mL. A 48-h, 2-mg low-dose dexamethasone suppression test failed to suppress cortisol (post-test 621 nmol/L). Adrenal CT showed a 0.7-cm hypodense left adrenal nodule reported as an adenoma. Dual-energy bone densitometry revealed Z-scores of –4.5 (lumbar spine) and –3.2 (femoral neck).

Her 18-year-old brother presented 3 months later with a 2-year history of purple striae and 4 kg weight gain over 4 weeks. He was normotensive; height 1.63 m; BMI 23.1 kg/m2. He had moon facies and purple striae in axillae, popliteal fossae, and waist but no dorsocervical fat pad or proximal myopathy. Laboratory studies excluded diabetes and electrolyte abnormalities. ACTH was <5 pg/mL. A 1-mg overnight low-dose dexamethasone test failed to suppress cortisol (624 nmol/L), and confirmatory low-dose dexamethasone testing showed cortisol of 513 nmol/L. A 6-day Liddle’s test revealed a paradoxical 6% increase in urinary free cortisol. DXA showed Z-scores of –3.2 (spine) and –2.5 (femoral neck). Adrenal CT revealed a diffusely enlarged left adrenal gland and a nodular right adrenal gland.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.B: Cushing syndrome due to adrenal adenoma

question:
A 17-year-old female presented with prolonged epistaxis and severe menstrual bleeding. She had a history of recurrent childhood epistaxis and a brother with similar complaints. On examination, she had mild ecchymoses without hemarthroses or hematomas and no jaundice. Vital signs were: blood pressure 90/50 mm Hg and pulse 110 beats/min with normal sinus rhythm on ECG.  
Laboratory studies revealed:  
• Blood type A+; platelets 190 000/mm3; hemoglobin 4 g/dL; WBC 6900/mm3  
• Glucose 90 mg/dL; ALT 35 U/L; AST 32 U/L; ESR 11 mm/h; CRP 2 mg/L; LDH 340 U/L  
• Prothrombin activity 97%; PT and bleeding time normal; aPTT markedly prolonged at 95 s  
Screening for von Willebrand disease with an antigen assay was negative.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 38‐year‐old man presented with a 10‐year history of progressive enlargement of both hands and feet, excessive sweating, and prominent folds of skin on the forehead and scalp. He also reported insidious, mild bilateral knee pain relieved by over‐the‐counter medications. He had no history of fever, chest pain, rashes, voice changes, headache, visual symptoms, or known pulmonary, cardiac, endocrine, or rheumatologic disease. He smoked three to four cigarettes daily and drank alcohol twice weekly. There was no family history of similar illness.

On examination, he was alert and hemodynamically stable. Coarse facial features included multiple skin folds on the forehead and “cutis verticis gyrata” of the scalp. There was marked enlargement of the bilateral hands, feet, distal forearms, and swelling of both knees and ankles. Grade IV clubbing was present in all fingers and toes. Muscle bulk was symmetrically reduced in the upper arms and thighs.

Laboratory studies showed normal results for complete blood count, renal and liver function, thyroid function, uric acid, calcium, lipid profile, urine microscopy, antinuclear antibody, rheumatoid factor, and anti–cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody. Chest radiography was obtained to evaluate for underlying lung disease.

Radiographs of the knees demonstrated diffuse cortical thickening of the distal femoral diaphyses and metaphyses with periosteal reaction; similar but milder changes were seen in the tibiae and fibulae. Ankle and foot radiographs showed cortical thickening with a fluffy periosteal reaction in the tibiae and fibulae, with milder involvement of the talus and navicular. Hand radiographs revealed cortical thickening and periosteal reaction of the radius, ulna, and the middle and proximal phalanges of all fingers.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 63-year-old woman was referred for elevated serum liver enzyme levels. For the past six years she had eaten only small meals, and over the last two months her intake had decreased to <400 kcal/day, consisting of bread, cheese, raisins, walnuts, and tofu. She noted numbness in her extremities and on evaluation by her family physician was found to have AST 634 IU/L, ALT 462 IU/L, LDH 769 IU/L, ALP 764 IU/L, and γ-GTP 218 IU/L. She denied alcohol use, was a non-smoker, and had no significant medical history. 

On admission her vital signs were normal. Her height was 161 cm and weight 29.0 kg (BMI 11.2 kg/m2). Physical examination, including neurologic assessment, was unremarkable. Initial laboratory studies showed WBC 5600/μL, hemoglobin 14.1 g/dL, platelets 198,000/μL, total protein 5.5 g/dL, albumin 3.3 g/dL, AST 267 IU/L, ALT 338 IU/L, LDH 557 IU/L, ALP 797 IU/L, γ-GTP 227 IU/L, transthyretin 8 mg/dL, transferrin 113 mg/dL, retinol-binding protein 0.7 mg/dL, vitamin B1 3.0 μg/dL, vitamin B12 >1500 pg/mL, and CRP 0.08 mg/dL. Serologic tests for hepatitis B and C and anti-mitochondrial antibodies were negative. Abdominal ultrasonography was unremarkable. Indirect calorimetry measured resting energy expenditure of 654 kcal/day.

Enteral nutrition was started at 300 kcal/day and increased to 500 kcal/day on day 4, with 100 mg intravenous vitamin B1 daily. Although she remained asymptomatic, her AST and ALT began to rise on day 8, raising concern for refeeding syndrome. Her serum electrolytes remained within normal limits throughout. By day 12, AST had increased to 1288 IU/L and ALT to 1043 IU/L, and her body weight had declined to 27.1 kg. Repeat abdominal ultrasonography again showed a normal-appearing liver.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 78-year-old man with triple-vessel coronary disease status post coronary-artery bypass grafting (LIMA to mid-LAD, SVG to first diagonal, and SVG to second obtuse marginal), heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, and hypertension was admitted after a mechanical fall complicated by left chest pain. On examination, he had a hematoma and swelling of the left chest wall but denied cardiac pain or dyspnea. An initial chest CT angiogram was unremarkable except for an incidental abdominal aortic aneurysm. 

Fourteen days later, he developed moderate, pressure-like chest pain and dyspnea. He was mildly tachycardic without hypoxia. ECG showed normal sinus rhythm with frequent premature atrial complexes. Laboratory studies revealed a high-sensitivity troponin I level of 140 ng/mL (reference 0–12 ng/mL) and a BNP of 3363 pg/mL (reference 0–450 pg/mL). A pulmonary embolism was suspected; a repeat chest CTA was negative for PE but demonstrated a new hyperattenuating structure in the left pulmonic pericardial recess concerning for a hematoma. 

The differential diagnosis for this pericardial hematoma included left anterior descending artery pseudoaneurysm, mycotic aneurysm, pericardial injury, connective tissue disorder, or malignancy with central hemorrhage. Considering recent left-sided chest trauma, imaging, and negative blood cultures, an LAD pseudoaneurysm was the leading possibility.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 51-year-old Caucasian man was admitted with a 20-day history of progressive dyspnea and fatigue without chest pain, cough, or fever. He had a history of Wilson’s disease diagnosed 8 years earlier, treated successfully with D-penicillamine, complicated by well-compensated cirrhosis. Six and a half years later, he was found to have pulmonary hypertension on echocardiography and treated with bosentan and sildenafil.

On examination, he had jugular venous distention, peripheral edema, vascular spiders, a systolic tricuspid murmur, accentuated and split second heart sounds in the pulmonary area, and splenomegaly. ECG showed right-axis deviation and right bundle branch block. Chest radiography revealed a “water bottle”–shaped cardiac silhouette and bilateral hilar enlargement. Echocardiography demonstrated a large pericardial effusion, dilated right heart chambers, mild tricuspid regurgitation, and an estimated RV systolic pressure of 75–80 mmHg (normal <25 mmHg).

Laboratory tests showed leukopenia (3,600/µL), thrombocytopenia (83,000/µL), INR 1.33, and albumin 4.6 g/dL; other liver and renal functions were normal. Viral markers (hepatitis B and C, HIV, EBV, HSV, CMV, Echo, Parvo, Coxsackie, Arbovirus), Mycoplasma pneumoniae, Chlamydia, and Coxiella burnetii tests were negative. Autoimmune profile and tumor markers were normal. A tuberculin skin test was positive (15 mm induration).

Contrast CT of the chest showed a moderate pericardial effusion with enlarged anterior and posterior mediastinal lymph nodes, left hilar and carinal nodes, and dilation of the portal and splenic veins; abdominal CT confirmed cirrhosis and splenomegaly. Serum ACE level was elevated at 68 IU/L (normal 0–52 IU/L).

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 51-year-old woman was referred to the ENT clinic with a 2-week history of a painless lump on the right side of her neck. She reported no dysphagia or change in her voice. Her gynaecological history included four full-term vaginal deliveries and previously normal cervical smears; she denied intermenstrual or postcoital bleeding and had regular cycles. She smoked 20–30 cigarettes per day. Over the past 3 months she had increasing lethargy and severe back pain that prevented her from working. Five years earlier she had significant weight loss and heavy menstrual bleeding requiring five units of blood; an evaluation for colon cancer was negative, and she failed to attend follow-up at a menstrual disorders clinic.

On examination, there were multiple palpable cervical lymph nodes bilaterally. Neck ultrasonography showed two large supraclavicular lymph nodes and several abnormal nodes in the right carotid chain. A chest radiograph was normal. Fine-needle aspiration of a lymph node yielded squamous carcinoma cells. To locate the primary, clinical examination and endoscopy of the nasopharynx, larynx, and hypopharynx showed no tumour. Contrast-enhanced CT of the neck, chest, and abdomen demonstrated marked mediastinal and para-aortic lymphadenopathy and bilateral dilation of the collecting systems with proximal ureteral dilatation, suggesting pelvic obstruction. 

^18F-FDG PET-CT showed intense uptake in the right cervical lymph nodes, right paratracheal and anterior mediastinal stations, lower para-aortic and bilateral iliac chains with a photopaenic centre in an obturator node, and a focal area of increased activity in the pelvis, suggesting a lesion within the rectal wall or vaginal vault.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man with a history of dyslipidemia presented with chronic headaches without vomiting or perceived visual changes. Noncontrast CT of the head showed a suprasellar mass measuring 12.5 mm. MRI revealed a 15 × 17 × 12 mm pituitary lesion that was T1-hyperintense, T2-hyperintense, intensely enhanced with gadolinium, elevated the optic chiasm and stalk, and infiltrated both cavernous sinuses. Automated perimetry demonstrated bitemporal hemianopsia.

On review of systems, he reported longstanding fatigue, cold intolerance, hoarseness, constipation, and weight gain. Vital signs showed a pulse of 55/min. BMI was 30.5 kg/m2. Examination revealed dry skin, a pale puffy face with periorbital edema, thinning of the lateral eyebrows, and macroglossia. The thyroid gland was not palpable.

Laboratory studies showed free T4 of 0 ng/dL (NR, 0.61–1.12), free T3 of 1.88 pg/mL (NR, 2.50–3.90), and TSH > 100 µUI/mL (NR, 0.34–5.60). Prolactin was 22.5 ng/mL (NR, 2.64–13.13), testosterone was 2.04 ng/mL (NR, 2–10), LH was 2.8 UI/L (NR, 1.24–8.62), and FSH was 5.1 UI/L (NR, 1.27–19.26). Anti–thyroid peroxidase antibodies were elevated at 56.9 UI/mL (NR, 0.25–9), and thyroid ultrasound showed an atrophic, hypoechoic gland consistent with autoimmune thyroiditis.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

An 8-year-old girl with a history of bilateral sporadic retinoblastoma diagnosed at 22 months of age (both eyes ICRB group D) initially received nine cycles of systemic polychemotherapy and multiple intravitreal melphalan injections, followed by plaque brachytherapy, cryotherapy, and eventual enucleation of the right eye 9 months after diagnosis. At age 2, she underwent external-beam radiotherapy to the left eye as salvage therapy. Regular ophthalmological follow-up showed no recurrence in the left eye, with best-corrected visual acuity of 20/200. Five years after completion of EBRT, she developed progressive pain and swelling of the right leg and was treated for presumed osteomyelitis with antibiotics, without improvement. On referral, MRI of the right tibia demonstrated an expansive intramedullary lesion with broad surrounding tissue reaction. A core biopsy was performed to further evaluate the lesion.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 12-year-old boy with perinatally acquired HIV, on antiretroviral therapy (lamivudine, efavirenz, abacavir) for 5 years, presented with a 5-day history of fever, oral ulcers, and a widespread non-scaly dusky macular rash involving the face, trunk, and upper limbs. He was thin-built (18 kg) and febrile (100 °F); other systemic examination results were unremarkable. He was treated empirically with a 15-day tapering course of oral prednisolone, azithromycin, cetirizine, and supportive care, with complete resolution of fever, rash, and ulcers over 2 weeks. At that point, ART was resumed with efavirenz. Six days after restarting efavirenz, he redeveloped fever and an erythematous, nonscaly maculopapular rash over the malar areas (sparing the nasolabial folds), forehead, trunk, and extremities, as well as papules on the palms and soles and multiple oral ulcers. There was no history of photosensitivity, arthralgia, or prior similar episodes. Differential diagnoses of drug rash, drug-induced lupus, acute cutaneous lupus erythematosus, and COVID-19 infection were considered, and he was admitted for further evaluation.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 32-year-old woman noted a subcutaneous mass in her left lumbar region that had grown gradually over 5–6 years. She experienced pain and restriction of trunk movement, particularly flexion, extension, and rotation, and was concerned about its appearance. She had no other symptoms, took no medications, and had no family history of similar lesions. Her weight had decreased by about 20 kg on a low-calorie diet, and her body–mass index was 31.8. 

On inspection, there was a large mass altering the silhouette of the left flank; the overlying skin was normal. Palpation revealed a subcutaneous bulk that was painful to touch but mobile over underlying structures. Routine laboratory tests and a lipid profile were within normal limits.

Ultrasound of the left lumbar region showed an oval, echogenic, non-homogeneous image with a fibrolipomatous aspect, measuring approximately 210 × 49 mm. MRI of the abdomen demonstrated a 22 cm subcutaneous, round mass with regular, sharp borders and a lipomatous signal; there was no evidence of infiltration into the abdominal wall or underlying muscles.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 71-year-old man with advanced renal cell carcinoma treated with ipilimumab (1 mg/kg) and nivolumab (240 mg) presented four days after his second infusion with an asymptomatic, sustained mild rise in serum creatine phosphokinase. On the seventh day after infusion, ST-segment elevations were noted on his cardiac monitor. Twelve-lead electrocardiography showed ST-segment elevation in all leads. Laboratory testing revealed elevated inflammatory markers and myocardial biomarkers (creatine kinase MB-isozyme, 23 488 U/L; troponin-I, 100 680 pg/mL; NT-proBNP, 15 964 pg/mL). Echocardiography demonstrated an akinetic apex, a hypokinetic septum, and an ejection fraction of 50% (baseline 72%). Emergency cardiac catheterization excluded relevant coronary disease, and left ventriculography showed that the left ventricular apex was nearly akinetic while the remaining left ventricle was hyperkinetic.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman, gravida 1 para 1, with a history of a loop electrosurgical excision procedure two years earlier for cervical intraepithelial neoplasia 3 and adenocarcinoma in situ, was found on routine prenatal Pap smear to have atypical squamous cells of undetermined significance with positive HPV co-testing. Postpartum colposcopic cervical biopsies revealed necrosis and hyalinization, and endocervical curettage demonstrated atypical trophoblastic cells of the chorionic intermediate trophoblastic type. A transvaginal ultrasound showed no retained products of conception. She underwent hysteroscopy with dilation and curettage: both endometrial and endocervical specimens displayed a similar atypical trophoblastic proliferation. Serum beta-human chorionic gonadotropin and human placental lactogen were both undetectable. Pelvic magnetic resonance imaging showed no evidence of gestational trophoblastic disease. Desiring future fertility, she elected close surveillance. Four months later, a repeat dilation and curettage showed no trophoblastic disease. Six months after that, Pap smear and endocervical curettage were again negative. Approximately six months later, endocervical curettage once more demonstrated atypical trophoblastic disease. Computed tomography of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis and repeat pelvic MRI revealed no evidence of malignancy or metastasis, and β-hCG remained below 1 mIU/mL. Given these persistent atypical findings and negative systemic evaluation, she opted for definitive surgical management.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 71‐year‐old Japanese woman with severe dementia was found by her caregiver on her bed in the morning with a fluorescent pink liquid spilled over her head and an altered level of consciousness. She lived in a supervised facility, and all detergents and disinfectants had previously been removed after a suicide attempt 1 month earlier. She had no known medications aside from those managed by the facility. 

On presentation to a secondary hospital, she was hypotensive (systolic blood pressure < 60 mmHg) and received 2 L of extracellular fluid, 20 mL of 50% glucose for a blood glucose of 66 mg/dL, and 250 mL of 7% sodium bicarbonate for presumed acidosis. Three hours later, she was transferred to our emergency department. 

On arrival, her Glasgow Coma Scale score was 9 (E3V2M4); pupils were sluggishly reactive. She was tachycardic at 128 bpm in sinus rhythm; other vital signs and the physical examination were unremarkable. 

Laboratory tests showed: white blood cell count 18.3 × 10^3/µL; sodium 160 mmol/L; chloride 114.8 mmol/L; creatinine 0.73 mg/dL. Arterial blood gas analysis revealed pH 7.083, bicarbonate 3.2 mmol/L, and an anion gap of 38.8 mmol/L. The calculated plasma osmolality was 334.9 mOsm/kg H_2O. 

A chest radiograph was normal. Noncontrast head CT showed no acute intracranial abnormality.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 54-year-old woman established care complaining of 6 months of severe sinusitis and conjunctivitis, with constant “tears mixed with sticky secretion,” not associated with emotional distress. She has a long-standing history of widespread joint pain attributed to fibromyalgia, with multiple medications including duloxetine, tramadol, and oxycodone/acetaminophen. She denies any prior diagnosis or genetic testing for Ehlers-Danlos syndrome despite a family history of EDS in her father and sister (types unspecified). She reports no history of joint dislocations or subluxations, and no history of systemic symptoms such as fever, chest pain, or abdominal discomfort. 

On examination, vital signs were within normal limits. She had atrophic nasal scarring, midfacial hypoplasia, and micrognathia. Skin was hyperextensible over the elbows, forearms, hands, and doughy at the knees. Musculoskeletal exam showed normal strength but pain on palpation of ankles, knees, hips, lower back, and neck. Beighton score was 5/9: bilateral fifth-digit extension to 90°, bilateral thumb opposition to forearm, bilateral elbow hyperextension, and she could place her palms on the floor with straight legs. Foot deformities included pes planus, valgus, and hallux varus.

Laboratory studies showed normal CBC, BMP, thyroid and liver panels, HbA1c 5.5%, normal vitamin B12 and folate, vitamin D 37 ng/mL, lipid panel within mild elevations, and negative ANA and ESR. A 12-lead ECG revealed normal sinus rhythm without ischemic changes.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A young woman presented to the emergency department with a several-week history of difficulty breathing and shortness of breath. She was ambulatory on arrival and was awake, alert, and oriented. Her past medical history included diabetes, anemia, hyperlipidemia, and asthma; surgical history was notable for endometrial ablation and multiple kidney biopsies. Medications included simvastatin, low-dose aspirin, lisinopril, metformin, and inhaled asthma therapies. Vital signs were within normal limits. On examination, chest auscultation, palpation, and percussion were unremarkable. Laboratory studies revealed microcytic anemia and a mild leukocytosis with neutrophilia; other results were unremarkable. An initial chest radiograph showed two possible granulomas in the left lung base but was otherwise normal.
