Contrast-enhanced computed tomography of the chest demonstrated a 4.5 × 3.3-cm mildly enhancing soft-tissue mass in the right posterior mediastinum at the cardiophrenic angle, separate from the orthotopic liver; the radiology report suspected an enlarged lymph node or an esophageal mass.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 50-year-old Caucasian man presented with several months of vague upper abdominal discomfort that progressed to postprandial stabbing pain and intermittent night sweats. He reported an unintentional weight loss of 1.5 kg without anorexia. Four months earlier, a small tubular adenoma in the sigmoid colon had been resected during an otherwise normal colonoscopy. His medical history was unremarkable, with no family history of cancer.

On examination, he was afebrile with stable vital signs. Findings were notable for hepatomegaly and a single prominent right axillary lymph node; the remainder of the exam was normal.

Laboratory studies showed:  
• C-reactive protein 40 mg/L (reference <5.0)  
• Lactate dehydrogenase 300 U/L (reference ≤250)  
• Mild elevations in liver enzymes  
• Normal carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA)  
• Markedly elevated cancer antigen 19-9 (CA19-9) at 14 968 kU/L (reference ≤34)

Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed multiple lesions in the liver and lungs, as well as enlarged axillary, infradiaphragmatic, and hilar lymph nodes. 18F-FDG PET-CT confirmed widespread metastatic involvement of the liver, lungs, kidneys, adrenal glands, skeletal muscle, bone, and lymph nodes but showed no identifiable primary lesion.

Ultrasound-guided biopsy of a liver lesion demonstrated adenocarcinoma. Immunohistochemical staining of the liver specimen was positive for CK7 and CK19, negative for CK20 and TTF1, and variably positive for SATB2. Mismatch-repair proteins were intact. Upper endoscopy and endoscopic ultrasound of the esophagus, stomach, proximal duodenum, pancreas, and biliary tract revealed no primary tumor.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 61-year-old woman presented with a productive cough of six weeks’ duration. She had no smoking history or prior medical conditions. Initial outpatient treatment with a quinolone antibiotic, a bronchodilator, and expectorants for presumed acute bronchitis failed to improve her symptoms. She subsequently developed low-grade fever and was admitted.  
On admission, temperature was 37.0 °C, blood pressure 115/66 mmHg, heart rate 83 beats/min, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Physical examination revealed coarse crackles bilaterally; no peripheral edema was noted.  
Chest radiograph showed diffuse ground-glass opacities, reticular shadows in both lung fields, and right hilar enlargement. Chest CT revealed multiple small nodules, interlobular septal thickening, irregular bronchovascular bundle thickening, ground-glass opacities, reticular shadows, bilateral pleural effusions, and enlarged supraclavicular, mediastinal, and hilar lymph nodes.  
Abdominal CT demonstrated gallbladder wall thickening, a 3 cm mass in the gallbladder body, a low-density lesion in segment 5 of the right hepatic lobe adjacent to the gallbladder bed, porta hepatis and para-aortic lymphadenopathy, and only slight intrahepatic bile duct dilatation.  
Laboratory studies showed a white blood cell count of 14.2×10^3/µL with 17.2% eosinophils (absolute eosinophil count 2442/µL), LDH 517 IU/L, CRP 5.27 mg/dL, serum CEA 9.7 ng/mL, CA 19-9 395 U/mL, and CA-125 160 U/mL.  
Based on the CT and tumor markers, gallbladder cancer with liver metastasis versus acute cholecystitis with liver abscess was suspected. Chest CT findings also raised concern for multiple metastatic lung tumors or lymphangitic carcinomatosis. Peripheral eosinophilia prompted consideration of drug reaction, parasitic infection, or malignancy; pulmonary sarcoidosis was considered but serum ACE and KL-6 levels were not elevated.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 47-year-old woman with a 19-year history of HIV infection presented with a 5-day history of cough, chest pain, and asymptomatic bilateral cheek swelling. She was afebrile, with normal vital signs. On examination, there were mild bibasilar crackles, bilateral parotid enlargement, and a palpable 3-cm left superior cervical lymph node. Laboratory studies showed a CD4 count of 551 K/mm³ and an HIV-1 RNA load of 16 784 copies/mL. A chest radiograph demonstrated an abnormal contour along the right cardiomediastinal border. Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest revealed a 7.1 × 2.7 × 8.8 cm lobulated, low-attenuation mass draped across the anterior mediastinum without invasion of adjacent structures. The lesion was composed of nonenhancing cystic areas separated by enhancing septations, with average densities of 14 HU in cystic regions and 43 HU in septations.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 3-month-old adopted female infant (weight 4.3 kg) presented with a 2-week history of cough and tachypnea. She had no fever, poor feeding, or lethargy. Born full-term, vaccinated per schedule, she was formula-fed and growing well. Family history was unknown; there was no visible BCG scar. On examination, she had mild tachypnea with subcostal retractions and bilateral wheezes. Cardiovascular and abdominal examinations were normal. Laboratory studies showed a leukocyte count of 13.8 × 10^9/L (42% neutrophils, 49% lymphocytes), and a C-reactive protein level of 7.0 mg/L; HIV testing was negative. Chest radiography revealed right upper zone air-space disease. Empiric ceftriaxone was started for community-acquired pneumonia. Because the radiologic findings appeared disproportionate to the clinical presentation, a contrast-enhanced CT of the thorax was obtained, which demonstrated consolidation of the apical and posterior segments of the right upper lobe and the superior segment of the right lower lobe with multiple cystic nonenhancing areas, along with a peripherally enhancing lobulated lesion in the right paratracheal region contiguous with the involved lung segments and extending into the posterior mediastinum, causing lower tracheal compression and mild left mediastinal shift. A differential diagnosis of congenital pulmonary adenomatoid malformation with secondary infection was considered.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 44‐year‐old previously healthy man presented with chills for 3 days and arthralgias in the knees and hips, preceded by several days of unproductive cough and headache. One week before presentation, he had been treated with a macrolide antibiotic and an NSAID. His medical history was notable only for smoking; there was no family history of cardiovascular disease or long‐term medications. 

On examination, temperature was 38.5°C, heart rate 113/min, blood pressure 126/64 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Auscultation revealed no pericardial rub or crackles; there was epigastric tenderness. ECG showed sinus tachycardia with first‐degree AV block (PQ 210 ms). Laboratory studies demonstrated WBC 13.4×10^3/µL, CRP 16.9 mg/dL, ESR 95 mm/h, and high‐sensitivity troponin T 656.2 ng/L; the differential count showed no eosinophilia. Electrolytes, liver, and kidney function were normal. Blood cultures, serology, and PCR for pathogens were negative; vasculitis‐associated autoantibodies were absent.

A 13-year-old previously healthy boy presented with abrupt onset palpitations at rest. His smartwatch recorded a heart rate >200 beats/min. He was alert, warm, and well perfused. An ECG showed a wide complex tachycardia with a right bundle branch block morphology and superior axis, with subtle variation in QRS duration and morphology on the precordial leads. He received IV adenosine 6 mg then 12 mg without effect on rhythm or ventriculoatrial conduction, and IV verapamil 5 mg also had no effect. Synchronized cardioversion with 50 J failed to terminate the tachycardia. He was transferred to the cardiac ICU; echocardiography showed normal intracardiac anatomy and biventricular systolic function. An IV amiodarone bolus and infusion slowed but did not terminate the tachycardia, with decreased peripheral perfusion. A second synchronized cardioversion (50 J) restored sinus rhythm, revealing ventricular pre-excitation with a left bundle branch block morphology distinctly different from the morphology during tachycardia.

A 62-year-old woman with a 3-year history of non–insulin-dependent type 2 diabetes and inconsistent metformin use presented with 2 days of involuntary “jerking” movements of her left arm and leg. She reported increased urination and a 14-kg weight loss over the prior month. Her medical history was notable for hypertension, hyperlipidemia, peripheral vascular disease, bronchiectasis, resolved hepatitis C, and steatohepatitis without cirrhosis. She smoked 20 pack-years, consumed about 10 alcoholic drinks per week, and had a remote history of intranasal cocaine use. 

On examination, her temperature was 98.1 °F, blood pressure 159/64 mmHg, heart rate 54 beats/min, respiratory rate 19 breaths/min, and BMI 23.6 kg/m2. She was alert and oriented. Neurologic examination demonstrated nonrhythmic, circular, writhing movements of the left upper and lower extremity with intermittent ballistic jerks, consistent with chorea.

Laboratory testing showed: blood glucose 776 mg/dL, HbA1c 15.8%, venous pH 7.37, bicarbonate 23 mEq/L, anion gap 13, BUN 16 mg/dL, creatinine 1.48 mg/dL, ALT 17 U/L, AST 20 U/L. A noncontrast head CT revealed asymmetric hyperattenuation of the right lentiform nucleus with sparing of the internal capsule. She was admitted and glucose was controlled with insulin; within 48 hours, the movements were suppressible with concentration. MRI performed 4 days later showed only chronic microvascular ischemic changes without focal basal ganglia abnormalities.

One month later she returned with increased frequency of left-sided chorea and ambulatory dysfunction. Blood glucose was 62 mg/dL and HbA1c 9.9%. She was treated with alprazolam, tizanidine, and haloperidol without improvement. Antinuclear antibody and paraneoplastic autoantibody tests were negative. Ceruloplasmin and copper levels were normal. It was noted that the chorea dissipated when she was not observed, and she was diagnosed with a conversion disorder.

Within 24 hours she returned with persistent chorea (blood glucose 200 mg/dL) and was diagnosed with diabetic hemichorea hemiballismus. Two days later her blood glucose was 40 mg/dL, and repeat MRI showed hyperintensity of the right lentiform and caudate nuclei with corresponding intrinsic T1 hyperintensity.

A 69‐year‐old woman with multiple myeloma complicated by an L4 vertebral fracture began localized radiotherapy and systemic therapy with lenalidomide (25 mg Days 1–21), bortezomib (1.3 mg/m2 Days 1, 4, 8, 11), and dexamethasone (20 mg on scheduled days) in a 28‐day cycle, along with enoxaparin for thromboprophylaxis. Nineteen days after treatment initiation, she presented with a 12‐hour history of right upper‐quadrant abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and general fatigue. She denied chest pain, dyspnea, muscle pain, or fever. She reported pruritus since Day 8 and an erythematous, pruritic rash over her chest, abdomen, and back for one week. 

On examination, blood pressure was 114/54 mmHg, heart rate 76 bpm, temperature 36.8°C, and oxygen saturation 100% on room air. Abdominal palpation revealed tenderness in the right hypochondrium with guarding. Skin examination showed an extensive erythematous rash. Cardiorespiratory examination was unremarkable without rales, edema, or elevated jugular venous pressure. 

CT of the abdomen excluded acute intra‐abdominal pathology. Laboratory tests showed C‐reactive protein 47.4 mg/L (normal <5.0), lactate dehydrogenase 718 U/L, creatine kinase 2122 U/L, N‐terminal pro‐BNP 1440 ng/L, and high‐sensitivity troponin T 5.47 µg/L (normal <0.013). Repeat troponin after 1 hour was 5.40 µg/L. ECG demonstrated sinus rhythm with convex ST elevation in leads I and aVL and reciprocal ST depression in II, III, and aVF. 

Transthoracic echocardiography revealed mild concentric left ventricular hypertrophy, normal ejection fraction, no regional wall motion abnormalities, and a localized posterolateral pericardial effusion measuring 8 mm without hemodynamic compromise. Global longitudinal strain was subtly reduced in the anterolateral segments.

A 92-year-old man with diet-controlled diabetes, coronary artery disease status post angioplasty and stent, gastroesophageal reflux disease, hyperlipidemia, dementia, and hypertension presented with two days of lower abdominal pain, generalized weakness, nausea, vomiting, and absence of flatus; his most recent bowel movement had been three days earlier. He had no history of abdominal surgery. On admission he was stable but agitated and confused, with tachycardia, a low-grade fever of 38.1 °C, and a distended, tympanic abdomen with diffuse tenderness worst in the midepigastrum. The radiologist initially assumed a prior right-sided hemicolectomy based on the anatomy. On review, the majority of the small bowel lay on the right, the right colon was centralized in the midepigastrum, the duodenum did not cross midline, and a fecalith was noted.  
A surgical consultation was requested to evaluate for possible small-bowel obstruction. The working diagnosis was obstruction secondary to adhesive disease versus internal hernia or Ladd’s bands in the setting of malrotation. Given the clinical peritonitis and lactic acidosis, urgent exploratory laparotomy was planned.

A 27-year-old White man presented with a 2-year history of episodic fevers and tender erythematous rash predominantly on his trunk. A 55-year-old Chinese woman was admitted with a 2-week history of fever and 1 week of bilateral thigh edema. Her medical history was unremarkable. On examination, there was marked edema and diffuse induration of both thighs, with prominent dendritic-like telangiectasia overlying the skin. Multiple firm subcutaneous nodules were palpable in the thighs without tenderness. There was no lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, or neurologic deficit; her mental status was normal. Her temperature fluctuated between 36.2 °C and 39.2 °C. She was started on intravenous methylprednisolone 40 mg daily. After one week, her fever resolved, but the telangiectasia rapidly spread to the abdomen, chest, and neck, and new subcutaneous nodules appeared on the right arm.

Patient is a 40-year-old man with a history of reactive arthritis, Graves’ disease, type 2 diabetes mellitus, mitral valve prolapse, and Gilbert’s disease who presented with one month of progressive jaundice, fatigue, lightheadedness, and exertional dyspnea. A 28-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of pingueculae in both eyes. On examination: temperature 38°C; vital signs otherwise normal; icteric sclera; epigastric tenderness without guarding, rigidity, or palpable mass.  
Laboratory data showed normocytic anemia (Hb 12.3 g/dL), total bilirubin 2.9 mg/dL, AST 167 U/L, ALT 443 U/L, ALP 424 U/L; serum lipase and amylase were normal. Chest radiograph was normal.  
Abdominal CT revealed a 3.8×2.7 cm solid mass posterolateral to the pancreatic head, suggestive of peripancreatic lymphadenopathy. MRCP showed a 4.1×2.6 cm mass in the pancreatic head encasing the portal vein with central nonenhancement, a 1.5 cm narrowing of the mid–common bile duct, mild intrahepatic ductal dilation, and no pancreatic duct dilation. The initial differential diagnosis included malignant pancreatic tumor, autoimmune pancreatitis, neuroendocrine tumor, and gastrointestinal stromal tumor.  
Endoscopic ultrasound with fine-needle aspiration demonstrated a heterogeneous hyperechoic lesion in the pancreatic head. Histopathological examination revealed areas of coagulation necrosis, lymphocytes, and epithelioid histiocytes suggestive of granulomatous inflammation.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

An 84‐year‐old man presented with acute onset abdominal and back pain that began during the night without vomiting or gastrointestinal disturbance. One day earlier he had completed a 10-day course of clindamycin for left calf cellulitis. His medical history included atrial fibrillation, congestive cardiac failure, ulcerative colitis, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and antiphospholipid syndrome with recurrent deep vein thromboses; he was on maintenance warfarin. On examination he was afebrile, blood pressure 200/132 mm Hg, heart rate 68 beats/min, and capillary blood glucose 9.1 mmol/L. Chest auscultation was clear; abdominal examination showed mild to moderate epigastric tenderness with normal bowel sounds; mild resolving cellulitis of the right calf was noted. Admission studies revealed hemoglobin 10.3 g/dL, white blood cell count 12.9×10^9/L, platelets 303×10^9/L, INR 6.8, urea 8.7 mmol/L, creatinine 111 μmol/L, sodium 144 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, CRP 75 mg/L, calcium 2.02 mmol/L. ECG showed a paced rhythm and chest radiograph demonstrated slight fluid overload, cardiomegaly, and interstitial shadowing. The initial impression was possible gastro-oesophageal reflux disease. Warfarin was withheld, vitamin K administered, and intravenous fluids started. A CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed subtle, non-specific bilateral adrenal enhancing lesions. His INR normalized to 2–3, but he remained hypotensive with ongoing abdominal pain, nausea, lethargy, and abdominal distension; capillary blood glucose remained normal despite withholding diabetic medications. A short synacthen test showed no appreciable rise in cortisol (baseline 95 nmol/L; 60 min 113 nmol/L; 90 min 132 nmol/L).

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 60-year-old man presented with a 2-year history of nasal stuffiness, multiple episodes of epistaxis, and persistent bilateral pedal edema. For the past 15 days he had developed painful purpuric patches on his extremities that progressed to hemorrhagic blisters and then to deep necrotic ulcers with purulent discharge; he also reported low-grade fever and migratory joint pains.

On examination he was cachectic and pale, with bilateral pitting pedal edema. Cutaneous examination revealed multiple stellate purpuric patches, angular infarcts, areas of gangrene, and overlying hemorrhagic bullae and deep jagged necrotic ulcers predominantly on the extremities; a few purpuric patches were also noted on the abdomen, back, and ears. All peripheral pulses were palpable. There was diffuse infiltration of the face and ears with ciliary and supraciliary madarosis, and nasal septal perforation. No lepromatous nodules or plaques were observed. Neurologic examination showed glove-and-stocking anesthesia and symmetrically thickened peripheral nerves (ulnar, radial cutaneous, ulnar cutaneous, lateral popliteal, posterior tibial, and sural nerves); motor and cranial-nerve functions were intact. Ophthalmologic examination was unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed neutrophilic leukocytosis and elevated liver enzymes (AST 63 U/L [normal <37], ALP 154 U/L [normal <116], GGT 132 U/L [normal <55]), with HCV RNA 1×10^5 copies/mL; tests for hepatitis B surface antigen and HIV were negative. Abdominal ultrasonography revealed splenomegaly and cholelithiasis with a normal liver echotexture; upper GI endoscopy was normal. Arterial Doppler studies of the lower limbs were within normal limits. Anticardiolipin antibodies were elevated (IgM 100 IU/mL [normal <20], IgG 22 IU/mL [normal <20]); lupus anticoagulant and anti-β2-glycoprotein I were negative. ANA, ANCA, cryoglobulins, protein C, and protein S levels were normal.

A slit-skin smear stained with Ziehl–Neelsen stain showed a bacteriological index of 6+ and a morphological index of 5%.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 45-year-old Caucasian man with chronic nicotine dependence and no other comorbidities was admitted after a minor forestry injury to the left middle finger. He was diagnosed with phlegmon of the extensor tendons and reactive osteomyelitis of several fingers bilaterally, debrided surgically, and treated with clindamycin. During hospitalization, he developed severe toothache and underwent extraction and resection of a radiculodental cyst in the right upper jaw. He was then transferred for persistent skin and joint symptoms. On examination, there were multiple infiltrated, livid, subcutaneous nodules on the lower legs and swollen, erythematous fingers with ulcers covered by yellowish fibrinous material. Intraoperative cultures from the hands and jaw prompted escalation of antibiotics to penicillin, imipenem, and amikacin; topical therapy with fusidic acid and betamethasone valerate was applied. Serologic tests were negative for hepatitis, HIV, and tuberculosis. A chest radiograph showed no evidence of sarcoidosis or tuberculosis. Laboratory studies revealed an elevated lipase level (933 µkatal/L; normal 1.59–6.36), leukocytosis, and mild anemia. Antinuclear antibodies, antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies, extractable nuclear antigens, protein electrophoresis, and immunofixation were unremarkable.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 55-year-old man presented with a 3-month history of right-sided abdominal pain. MRI of the abdomen revealed a 9.7 × 9.2 × 8.0 cm right renal mass invading the hepatic lobe. Chest CT demonstrated multiple small bilateral pulmonary nodules. He underwent cytoreductive right nephrectomy with partial hepatic resection. Pathology showed 10 cm clear-cell RCC, Fuhrman grade 4 with sarcomatoid differentiation, lymphovascular invasion, direct liver capsule invasion, negative margins, and pT4 N0 M1 stage.

Six weeks postoperatively, CT showed progression of pulmonary metastases. He was intermediate risk by the IMDC model and started sunitinib. After two cycles, he developed severe right upper-quadrant pain. CT at 12 weeks showed increased bilateral lung nodules, an 8 × 5 cm recurrence at the nephrectomy bed, multiple new liver lesions, and a 3.4 cm heterogeneous filling defect in the right ventricle. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a 5.6 × 3.0 cm echogenic mass extending from the RV inflow to outflow tract with mobile components, and a separate 4.0 × 2.2 cm mass in the RV apex; right and left ventricular size and systolic function were normal, ejection fraction 50%, inferior vena cava and pulmonary valve uninvolved.

Cardiac MRI at 1.5 Tesla showed a large RV lesion invading 47 mm of the interventricular septum and extending into the RV outflow tract with no IVC involvement. Further tissue characterization revealed: (a) isointense signal on cine, low intensity on pre-contrast T1-weighted images; (b) high T2 signal intensity with absent first-pass perfusion; and (c) heterogeneous enhancement after contrast.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 72-year-old Japanese man presented with a one-month history of bilateral wrist pain and swelling. Two weeks before referral, he had been seen by an orthopedist, was noted to have bilateral wrist swelling and a positive rheumatoid factor, and was referred for further evaluation. He was a farmer with a 40–pack-year smoking history and was treated for ulcerative colitis with mesalazine and low-dose prednisolone.  

On examination, his blood pressure was 104/64 mmHg, pulse 101 beats/min, and temperature 36.6 °C. The left wrist was markedly swollen on both dorsal and palmar surfaces, and both wrists were tender. Sensation was decreased in the left palm, and Tinel’s sign and Phalen’s test were positive on the left. There were no skin lesions or injuries.  

Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 9,040/mm3, C-reactive protein 4.78 mg/dL, matrix metalloproteinase-3 199.2 ng/mL, rheumatoid factor 63 IU/mL, anti-citrullinated protein antibody 0.7 U/mL, and β-D glucan 9.1 pg/mL. Renal function and glucose levels were normal. Wrist radiographs demonstrated soft-tissue swelling without bone erosions.  

Musculoskeletal ultrasonography of the left wrist revealed marked thickening of the flexor and extensor tendon sheaths with power Doppler signals at the margins and multiple isoechoic rice bodies within the tenosynovium. Aspiration of straw-colored, cloudy synovial fluid yielded no crystals or microorganisms, and polymerase chain reaction analysis for Mycobacterium tuberculosis DNA was negative.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 51‐year‐old woman was admitted with a 3‐day history of continuous, dull, non‐pleuritic, left‐lateral chest pain, associated with diaphoresis, nausea, palpitations, and presyncope. The pain was partially relieved by leaning forward and by paracetamol with codeine. Four days before the chest pain, she had developed an acute gastrointestinal illness, passing 3–4 loose stools daily, which was still ongoing. She had no significant past medical history or recent travel. On examination she was afebrile; heart rate 106 bpm; blood pressure 120/76 mm Hg. Heart sounds were normal without murmur or rub; jugular venous pressure was normal; lungs were clear. The abdomen was soft and non‐tender. Laboratory tests showed C‐reactive protein 30.0 mg/L (normal <5.0), troponin I 70 ng/L (normal <16), normal creatine kinase, ALT 65 U/L (normal <55), ALP 142 U/L (normal 20–110), normal full blood count and electrolytes. ECG demonstrated concave ST‐segment elevations in leads I, II, III, aVF, and V6, PR elevation in aVR, and PR depression in lead II. She was initially treated for an acute coronary syndrome. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed normal biventricular size and function, no pericardial effusion, but an echodense, thickened pericardium. A diagnosis of myopericarditis was made.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 22-year-old Malay woman presented with an 11-year history of asymptomatic, unilateral brown patches on the right arm, right side of the trunk, and right leg. She reported no preceding skin lesions or inflammation and had no significant medical or family history. On examination, there were linear hyperpigmented, slightly atrophic patches following Blaschko’s lines on the right arm, extending over the trunk to the right buttock, and down the right thigh and leg; no induration or active inflammation was noted. Routine laboratory investigations—including complete blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, renal and liver function tests, and a panel of antinuclear and extractable nuclear antibodies—were all normal or negative. A skin biopsy from an affected patch showed mild upper dermal perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate, reduced dermal thickness, and more compact dermal collagen; adnexal structures were unaffected.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 30-year-old man presented with a 2-year history of paroxysmal, acid-like pain and swelling of the waist and legs, worsening over the past 2 weeks and impairing ambulation. Physical examination was unremarkable. Pelvic X-ray showed patchy and nodular high-density shadows in both ilia, ischia, pubic bones, and upper femurs. CT bone-window images revealed uneven, elevated bone density lesions in the thoracic vertebrae below T10, bilateral ribs, scapulae, lumbar vertebrae, and sacrum. MRI demonstrated diffuse abnormal signal shadows in the pelvis, sacrum, L3–L5 vertebral bodies, and bilateral femurs. Whole-body SPECT showed extensive sclerotic lesions with diffusely increased bone metabolism and focal areas of reduced density with increased uptake in the left ninth rib axillary segment, the sixth and tenth thoracic vertebrae, and regions of both femurs and right tibia. Bone marrow aspiration and urine protein electrophoresis were normal. HLA-B27 was negative. Tumor markers revealed a mildly elevated neuron-specific enolase (19.03 μg/mL) with other markers within normal limits. Endocrine evaluation showed a slight increase in parathyroid hormone; parathyroid CT demonstrated only small, low-density nodules near the right thyroid lobe. Given nondiagnostic findings, a CT-guided left iliac bone biopsy was planned.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 28-year-old woman with Turner syndrome (karyotype 45,X/46,X,+mar), diagnosed at age 16 for amenorrhea, was referred after abnormal brain MRI. She denied alcohol, tobacco, or drug use and had no family history of neurologic disease. On arrival, her temperature was normal, blood pressure 180/130 mm Hg, and heart rate 89 bpm. She was obese (BMI 27.3 kg/m2). Neurologic examination showed no focal deficits. She had had sudden-onset severe headache with a recorded blood pressure of 240/140 mm Hg. Noncontrast head CT was unremarkable. Diffusion-weighted MRI demonstrated restricted diffusion in multiple lesions involving different vascular territories. Cerebral MRA and carotid ultrasound revealed no extracranial or intracranial arterial stenosis. Chest radiograph and ECG were normal. Laboratory tests showed a fasting glucose of 219 mg/dL, hemoglobin A1C 11.0%, and LDL cholesterol 189 mg/dL.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

options.C: Multiple cerebral infarcts secondary to hypertension

question:
A 63-year-old woman underwent left knee replacement and was discharged on enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously once daily for thromboprophylaxis without planned platelet monitoring. Fifteen days later she presented with fever (39.5 °C), headache, acute right-sided weakness, and a 2-minute episode of altered consciousness with focal tremors in her right leg and abdomen. Noncontrast head CT was unremarkable. Brain MRI showed an acute infarct in the left precentral and postcentral gyri and a subacute infarct in the right parietal lobe. Laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 10.6 g/dL, platelet count 43 000 × 10^9/L, and D-dimer 34 800 ηg/L; other values were within normal limits. She had no history of hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, smoking, or estrogen use. Physical examination noted right upper quadrant pain without hepatosplenomegaly or mucocutaneous bleeding. ECG demonstrated sinus rhythm, and echocardiography showed a nondilated left atrium with a normal ejection fraction (65%), excluding atrial fibrillation or intracardiac thrombus. Carotid duplex ultrasound was normal. CT pulmonary angiography revealed pulmonary emboli in the left main and segmental arteries. Abdominal CT identified filling defects in branches of the right suprahepatic vein. Venous ultrasound of the left leg showed nonacute thrombosis of the superficial femoral and popliteal veins. A repeat platelet count using citrate-anticoagulated blood confirmed thrombocytopenia.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 34-year-old man presents with three weeks of migratory generalized urticarial wheals that are intensely pruritic, last less than 24 hours each, and occur without relation to foods, drugs, physical stimuli, or exercise. Two months earlier, he had multiple wasp stings leading to acute hives treated with intravenous methylprednisolone and intramuscular chlorpheniramine, with symptom resolution in 24 hours; the next day he developed satellite lymphadenitis that resolved in 5 days with topical steroids. He has no history of allergic or autoimmune disease. Routine blood tests are normal except for a baseline mild leukopenia; basophil and eosinophil counts are normal. Workup excludes Helicobacter pylori antigen, beta-hemolytic streptococci, Chlamydia spp., Mycoplasma spp., hepatitis A/B/C, Epstein–Barr virus, cytomegalovirus, and other herpesviruses. Thyroid peroxidase, TSH receptor, thyroglobulin, anti-endomysial, and anti–tissue transglutaminase antibodies are negative. D-dimer, ESR, CRP, fibrinogen, ferritin, and complement levels are normal; an autologous serum skin test is negative. Skin tests to common inhalant and food allergens, Apis mellifera, and Polistes dominula venoms are negative; intradermal tests to Vespula spp and Vespa crabro venoms are positive at 0.1 μg/mL. Serum specific IgE is elevated for Vespula spp (5.46 kU/L), Vespa crabro (0.47 kU/L), and Polistes dominula (0.69 kU/L); component-resolved diagnostics show rVes v 5 at 6.17 kU/L and rPol d 5 at 1.07 kU/L. Total IgE is 132 kU/L and baseline tryptase is 7 μg/mL. Despite high-dose antihistamines and prednisone tapered to 5 mg daily over three months, his urticaria remains poorly controlled with a high urticaria activity score.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

An 88-year-old woman with arterial hypertension, osteoporosis, and degenerative lumbar disk disease presented with six months of worsening left low back pain radiating to the left lower limb, partially relieved by analgesics. Home medications included indapamide, enalapril, isosorbide mononitrate, and alphacalcidol. She denied smoking and alcohol use.

On examination, she appeared pale and hypertensive (190/80 mmHg); heart rate was 90 bpm, with a grade 2/6 systolic murmur at the right upper sternal border, a grade 2/6 apical systolic murmur, and bilateral carotid bruits. Lung fields were clear. The abdomen was soft and non-tender, with a palpable, non-tender mass in the left flank; there was no edema.

Laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 5.9 g/dL (MCV 66.4 fL; MCH 19.5 pg), serum iron 12 µg/dL, TIBC 221 µg/dL (transferrin saturation 5.4%), ferritin 36.6 ng/mL, creatinine 1.23 mg/dL (eGFR 39 mL/min/1.73 m2), and NT-proBNP 10 545 pg/mL. Inflammatory markers and other studies were normal.

Abdominal ultrasound showed a 100-mm heterogeneous mass adjacent to a calcified aortic wall and next to a 90×50-mm cyst-like structure with internal septations; Doppler detected minimal flow in the mass. Contrast-enhanced CT revealed a normal right kidney and, on the left, a 27-mm cortical cyst plus a giant (109×102×94 mm), saccular, almost completely thrombosed left renal artery aneurysm with parietal calcifications and a small nodular calcification. The aneurysm compressed the pelvis, causing severe hydronephrosis and extreme parenchymal thinning to 2 mm. CT angiography showed scant contrast filling of the aneurysm.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

options.A: Left kidney as driver of hypertension

question:
A 48-year-old man with no specific medical history presented with a 1-month history of intermittent obstructive abdominal pain initially treated symptomatically with slight improvement. Over time, his abdominal pain worsened and he developed weight loss and asthenia. On examination, he was afebrile, hemodynamically stable, and had tenderness in the right iliac fossa; the remainder of the examination was unremarkable. Viral serologic tests for HIV, HBV, HCV, and syphilis were negative; Epstein–Barr virus serology was positive. Contrast-enhanced abdominal CT showed a 36 mm long segment of regular, pseudo-stenotic circumferential wall thickening of the terminal ileum, with discrete infiltration of the adjacent mesenteric fat, associated with distension of proximal small bowel and ascending colon.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 21-year-old previously healthy Sri Lankan woman was admitted with a 1-week history of progressive dyspnea, initially on exertion and later at rest, accompanied by low-grade fever, malaise, and profuse sweating. She denied weight loss, rash, allergies, recent drug exposure, or systemic symptoms. She had no cardiovascular risk factors, no history of allergy, asthma, or connective tissue disease, and no recent medication use.

On examination, temperature was mildly elevated, heart rate 120 bpm, blood pressure 100/70 mmHg, and respiratory rate elevated. She was dyspneic at rest, with jugular venous pressure of 5 cm above the sternal angle and no Kussmaul sign. Cardiac auscultation revealed slightly muffled heart sounds with a gallop rhythm. Lung examination showed bilateral basal fine inspiratory crackles. She had mild, non-tender hepatomegaly and splenomegaly. There was no peripheral edema, rash, or lymphadenopathy.

Laboratory studies showed leukocytosis with an absolute eosinophil count of 21.6×10^3/μL (63.5%), normal hemoglobin and platelets, erythrocyte sedimentation rate 60 mm/hr, and C-reactive protein normal. Troponin I was 35.4 ng/dL (normal <1.0), and brain natriuretic peptide was 1280.5 pg/mL. Electrocardiogram demonstrated sinus tachycardia with widespread ST-segment depressions. Chest radiograph revealed pulmonary congestion. Transthoracic echocardiography showed global left ventricular hypokinesia with an ejection fraction of 40%, a thin pericardial effusion, and no intracardiac thrombus.

Serologic and microbiologic evaluations—including Filaria, Toxoplasma, and Toxocara antibodies; stool examination for parasites; serologies for Epstein-Barr virus, cytomegalovirus, and Mycoplasma; HIV and tuberculosis screening; blood cultures; and autoimmune markers (ANA, dsDNA)—were all negative. Thyroid function, renal function, electrolytes, and lipid profile were normal; transaminases were elevated threefold. Abdominal ultrasound confirmed mild hepatosplenomegaly without lymphadenopathy. Bone marrow biopsy demonstrated marked eosinophilia without lymphoproliferative infiltration; FIP1L1–PDGFRA and BCR–ABL transcripts were negative. The patient declined endomyocardial biopsy and coronary angiography was not performed.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 16-year-old girl was admitted with a 12-hour history of pulsatile frontal headache accompanied by nausea, photophobia, and phonophobia. One hour after headache onset, she developed diplopia, difficulty adducting the right eye, dysarthria, and subtle imbalance. She had a history of migraine with visual aura since age 10: episodes of scotomas, blurred or tunnel vision lasting up to 60 minutes, with complete resolution, but no prior diplopia or ataxia. She denied fever, other neurologic complaints, or use of oral contraceptives.

On examination, she was tachycardic, blood pressure was 150/80 mmHg, and temperature was normal. Neurologic examination showed right-eye adduction paresis on left gaze, binocular diplopia, slow saccadic abduction of the left eye with horizontal nystagmus, and preserved convergence. Other cranial nerves were intact. Strength, sensation, and reflexes were symmetric. In standing, she swayed backward (worse with eyes closed). She had an ataxic gait with a leftward fall tendency. Diadochokinesis and the finger-to-nose test were normal, but the left heel-shin test was dysmetric. 

Noncontrast head CT and CT angiography were normal. On hospital day 2, headache and nausea improved but diplopia persisted. Brain MRI demonstrated a focus of diffusion restriction in the paramedian right dorsal midbrain, corresponding to the ipsilateral medial longitudinal fasciculus, with T2 and FLAIR hyperintensity and no contrast enhancement.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 66-year-old man with a history of Whipple disease, heart failure with cardiomyopathy, cirrhosis, chronic kidney disease, and pulmonary hypertension was referred to the clinic. He was severely malnourished and in cardiogenic shock. Two months earlier, an intestinal biopsy had confirmed Whipple disease, which was treated with 4 weeks of ceftriaxone followed by oral trimethoprim–sulfamethoxazole. On presentation, he had anasarca. Transthoracic echocardiography showed an ejection fraction of 46%, severely reduced right heart function, and severe aortic and mitral valve regurgitation; the left ventricular end-diastolic dimension was 57 mm and end-systolic dimension 44 mm. Right heart catheterization revealed a right atrial pressure of 32 mm Hg, mean pulmonary arterial pressure of 55 mm Hg, pulmonary capillary wedge pressure of 27 mm Hg, and pulmonary vascular resistance of 10 Wood units. Results of Whipple polymerase chain reaction (PCR) in blood culture were negative. Right ventricular endomyocardial biopsy showed no myocardial inflammation or involvement by Whipple disease.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 72-year-old man presented with a 4-day history of diffuse abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea; he had one episode of confusion with slurred speech. His medical history was notable only for left-sided rib fractures sustained in a fall 4 months earlier. On examination he was afebrile, blood pressure 143/83 mmHg, heart rate 85/min, respiratory rate 20/min, and oxygen saturation 96% on room air. Cardiopulmonary and neurologic examinations were unremarkable. Abdominal examination revealed diffuse tenderness with rebound but no hepatosplenomegaly. Mucous membranes were dry, and petechiae were noted on the inner left thigh.  
Initial laboratory results showed: hemoglobin 138 g/L, white blood cell count 14.7×10^9/L (neutrophils 13×10^9/L), platelets 28×10^9/L, creatinine 112 μmol/L, total bilirubin 22.2 μmol/L, AST 95 U/L, LDH 282 U/L, CK 807 U/L, INR 1.1, and PTT 30 s. Urinalysis was positive for blood (20–30 RBCs/HPF) and protein 1.0 g/L.  
Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen showed complete nonenhancement of the spleen consistent with infarction, surrounding soft-tissue stranding, and a small hemoperitoneum. Thirty-six hours later, his platelet count remained low despite transfusion, C-reactive protein was elevated at 346.3 mg/L, and peripheral smear showed reactive granulocytosis without schistocytes. Forty-eight hours after presentation, he became febrile (37.8°C), tachycardic, and tachypneic; platelets fell to <10×10^9/L, hemoglobin to 109 g/L, LDH rose to 306 U/L, and creatinine to 102 μmol/L. A coagulation panel remained normal (fibrinogen 4.30 g/L, INR 1.1, PTT 26 s). On hospital day 3, peripheral smear demonstrated schistocytes, and he developed hypoxic respiratory failure requiring intubation. The nephrology service was consulted and plasma exchange therapy was empirically initiated on the suspicion for TTP as the etiology of his deterioration.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 73-year-old man with hypertension underwent aortic valve replacement for severe aortic regurgitation. After induction and placement of a transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) probe, initial TEE confirmed severe aortic regurgitation with holodiastolic flow reversal in the descending aorta. Measurements in the midesophageal aortic-valve long-axis view showed an aortic root diameter of 3.0 cm, a sinus of Valsalva diameter of 4.0 cm, and a sinotubular junction diameter of 2.7 cm. The eccentric regurgitant jet had a vena contracta of 5.3 mm. Left ventricular ejection fraction was 39%, with only trivial mitral and tricuspid regurgitation. A patent foramen ovale was identified and closed before cardiopulmonary bypass (CPB).

A 23 mm stented bovine pericardial valve was implanted supra-annularly using a non-everting mattress suture technique with pledgets. The patient was weaned from CPB on low-dose dopamine and dobutamine. Post-bypass TEE in the midesophageal long-axis view showed a well-seated prosthesis with good leaflet mobility, but color-flow Doppler revealed a perpendicular turbulent jet originating from between the stent post and the sewing ring. In the midesophageal short-axis view, there was a considerable flow convergence adjacent to the stent post originating from outside the sewing ring. Further color-flow Doppler demonstrated a second turbulent jet arising from outside the sewing ring, running perpendicularly below the ring toward the left ventricular outflow tract. These findings suggested that both transvalvular and paravalvular leakage existed in close proximity.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

An otherwise healthy 8-year-old Bajau boy from Malaysia was evaluated for gradually developing pigmentation over his nails and mucosa. Over the past two years, he noted longitudinal and diffuse melanonychia of all fingernails and toenails. Over the past year, he also developed discrete, asymptomatic brown-black macules on the lower lip and tongue and a well-defined pigmented macule on the nasal aspect of the right sclera. He denied fatigue, weight loss, abdominal pain, vomiting, bleeding, palpitations, or other systemic symptoms. There was no relevant medication history, no consanguinity, and no family history of pigmentary disorders or intestinal polyposis. 

On examination, all 20 nails showed longitudinal melanonychia without dystrophy. There were multiple brown-black macules on the tongue and lower lip, and a single pigmented macule on the right sclera. The remainder of the physical examination was unremarkable. 

Laboratory studies—including complete blood count, serum electrolytes, liver function tests, serum cortisol, and adrenocorticotropic hormone—were all within normal limits. Chest radiograph, abdominal ultrasound, electrocardiogram, and echocardiography were unremarkable. The patient had undergone esophagogastroduodenoscopy and colonoscopy eight months earlier, both of which yielded normal findings.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 58-year-old man with known diabetes mellitus presented with a 3-month history of intermittent fever, night sweats, and a 6-kg weight loss. He noted a hard swelling in the right parotid region that had rapidly enlarged over the past 2 weeks. On examination, a right supraclavicular lymph node was palpable.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed multiple hypodense liver lesions, bilateral small pleural effusions, a right apical lung nodule, and axillary, mediastinal, peripancreatic, and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. Skull and spine radiographs showed no osteolytic lesions.

Fine-needle aspiration of the parotid swelling yielded hypercellular smears composed predominantly of large plasmacytoid cells with central to eccentric nuclei, perinuclear halos, less condensed chromatin, prominent nucleoli, and abundant basophilic cytoplasm. Binucleated and trinucleated forms and atypical mitoses were noted, along with mature plasma cells, scattered neutrophils, eosinophils, and apoptotic bodies. 

Based on these findings, the preliminary differential diagnosis included plasmablastic lymphoma, plasmablastic plasmacytoma/myeloma, and diffuse large B-cell lymphoma with plasmacytic differentiation.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus, dyslipidemia, and moderate aortic regurgitation was referred for resection of a newly diagnosed left adrenal mass after 3 months of spells characterized by anxiety, agitation, headaches, pallor, palpitations, and hypertensive crises. An abdominal CT scan showed a left adrenal nodule compressing the splenic vein. Elevated urinary normetanephrines and metanephrines confirmed a catecholamine-producing tumor. He received 30 days of doxazosin and bisoprolol before surgery and was hemodynamically stable on admission. Preoperative ECG showed sinus tachycardia with T-wave inversions in lateral leads. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated an LVEF of 55% without regional wall motion abnormalities.

He underwent laparoscopic left adrenalectomy. Gentle tumor manipulation elicited transient blood-pressure spikes to 180 mmHg until the main adrenal vein was ligated. Immediately after tumor removal, he became hypotensive to 50/30 mmHg and developed a new left bundle-branch block. Emergent coronary angiography showed no obstructive atherosclerotic disease (TIMI 2 flow, no vasospasm). Left ventriculography revealed circumferential midventricular akinesia. Repeat echocardiography demonstrated midventricular and apical akinesia with an LVEF of 25%. Cardiac biomarkers peaked on postoperative day 2 (troponin 21.9 ng/mL [URL 0.2 ng/mL]; NT-proBNP 7000 pg/mL [URL 300 pg/mL]).

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 63-year-old Caucasian woman, a non-smoker with no relevant drug history, presented during routine follow-up for bullous pemphigoid (in remission) with progressive, asymptomatic blackish pigmentation of both buccal mucosa. She also reported a 7-year history of brownish-black pigmentation of several fingernails. No family members were affected. 

On examination, there was a regular, brownish, reticular pattern on both sides of the buccal mucosa. Multiple longitudinal pigmented bands were present on her fingernails and toenails. Potassium hydroxide preparation and fungal cultures were negative. Digital dermoscopy of the nails showed a pseudo-Hutchinson’s sign, with homogeneous brownish and grayish longitudinal bands and lines with ill-defined margins on the nail plate.

Laboratory tests—including erythrocyte sedimentation rate; complete blood count with differential; urinalysis; lipid profile; renal and liver function tests; serum amylase, lipase, and lactate dehydrogenase; tumor markers (CEA, AFP, CA 19-9); and immunology parameters (antinuclear antibodies, cold agglutinins, cryoglobulins)—were all within normal limits. Chest radiography and abdominal ultrasonography showed no abnormalities. Colonoscopy revealed no tumorous formations. Endocrinologic evaluation, including cortisol levels, antipituitary antibodies, and an ACTH stimulation test, was normal. The patient declined biopsy of the oral lesions.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

An 89-year-old Sudanese farmer presented with 9 days of generalized colicky abdominal pain, distension, anorexia, persistent vomiting, and constipation. The pain began in the left iliac fossa and periumbilical area, became generalized and colicky, was aggravated by eating, and partly relieved by vomiting and analgesia. It was associated with fever, nausea, and anorexia. One day before presentation, the pain became diffuse, constant, and severe, with abdominal guarding, distension, and absolute constipation. He denied weight loss, bleeding, or prior abdominal surgeries; his only past complaint was an episode of left-sided and lower abdominal pain without gastrointestinal symptoms. 

On examination, he was alert and hemodynamically stable (pulse 108/min, blood pressure 130/70 mmHg). His abdomen was distended with full flanks, diffusely tender with guarding, absent bowel sounds, and an empty rectum on digital examination. 

Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 12.6 g/dL, white blood cell count 22,000/mm^3 (neutrophil predominant), C-reactive protein 189 mg/dL, serum albumin 2.3 g/dL, and a normal carcinoembryonic antigen level. Electrolytes and urinalysis were unremarkable. 

Erect chest and abdominal radiographs demonstrated dilated small and large bowel loops, a cecal diameter ≥ 12 cm, and free subdiaphragmatic air. 

Given the clinical signs of peritonitis, free air under the diaphragm, and radiographic evidence of a large-bowel obstruction, the provisional diagnosis was severe peritonitis due to large-bowel perforation from an obstructing left-sided tumor. The patient was started on intravenous fluids, antibiotics, and human albumin and taken for emergency exploratory laparotomy.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

An 88-year-old man with hypertension and chronic kidney disease presented with progressive dry cough, dyspnea, fever, and chills. Twenty-five days earlier, he had started a herbal medicine, Sai-rei-to, and symptoms began two weeks after initiation. He was an ex-smoker, took no other medications, and had no history of pulmonary disease or allergy.

On examination, temperature was 38.0 °C; respiratory rate 20 breaths/min; blood pressure 189/91 mmHg; heart rate 72 bpm; and oxygen saturation was 99% on 12 L/min via reservoir mask. Chest auscultation revealed bilateral late inspiratory crackles, and bilateral pretibial edema was noted.

Laboratory tests showed WBC count 8000/mm3 (78.8% neutrophils, 7.8% eosinophils), platelets 155,000/mm3, BNP 406 pg/mL, CRP 10.51 mg/dL, KL-6 374 U/mL, and SP-D 339 ng/mL. Coagulation was normal. Bacteriological tests were negative; Mycoplasma complement fixation titer rose fourfold, and single-sample Chlamydia pneumoniae antibodies were non-diagnostic. Arterial blood gas analysis revealed severe hypoxemia. Echocardiography showed an ejection fraction of 60% with normal wall motion.

Chest radiography demonstrated bilateral ground-glass opacities. High-resolution CT confirmed bilateral patchy ground-glass opacities, infiltrative shadows, and pleural effusions. Nasal high-flow oxygen therapy was initiated.

Bronchoalveolar lavage revealed diffuse alveolar hemorrhage with a neutrophil predominance (total cell count 325,000/mL: 62% neutrophils, 26% lymphocytes, 12% macrophages; CD4/CD8 ratio 0.49).

Given the lack of known interstitial lung disease, the temporal association with Sai-rei-to, and the exclusion of obvious infection or cardiac failure, all oral therapies including Sai-rei-to were discontinued, and the patient was started on methylprednisolone pulse therapy (1000 mg/day for 3 days), furosemide, ceftriaxone, and azithromycin.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 25-year-old woman with a history of chronic headaches and Kawasaki disease presented with lethargy, blurred vision, confusion, and headache. Her serum sodium was 127 mmol/L; other metabolic and hematologic studies were normal. Non-contrast head CT showed a 2.2 cm heterogeneous mass in the pineal region without calcification, with resultant obstructive hydrocephalus and transependymal CSF resorption; an external ventricular drain was placed. Subsequent brain MRI demonstrated a mildly lobulated, well-circumscribed mass that was isointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on FLAIR, with heterogeneous gadolinium enhancement except for a small nonenhancing anterior component. On the ADC map, the lesion’s mean ADC was 1077.01 ×10⁻⁶ mm²/s (±191.14 ×10⁻⁶ mm²/s), higher than normal brain. MRI of the spine showed no evidence of drop metastases.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

options.C: Pineal parenchymal tumour of intermediate differentiation

question:
A 52-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of a one-month history of constipation, vague abdominal pain, hematochezia, and a pedunculated sigmoid-colon mass found on rigid sigmoidoscopy. Her medical and family histories were unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed mild anemia; other findings were normal. Colonoscopy revealed a fungating obstructive tumor in the distal sigmoid colon (20 cm from the anal verge). Biopsy suggested a gastrointestinal stromal tumor of uncertain malignant potential. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen showed a 5 cm eccentric, irregularly enhancing sigmoid-colon wall thickening with perirectal lymphadenopathy, consistent with T3/T4a N1 M0 (stage IIIB) disease. She underwent a laparoscopic low anterior resection. Grossly, there was a 3.8 cm × 2.5 cm umbilicated polypoid mass in the distal sigmoid colon infiltrating the mesocolic fat. Histologically, the lesion comprised geographic sheets and nests of uniformly small bland cells infiltrating the muscular wall and fat, with prominent small arterioles, extensive lymphovascular invasion, and regional nodal metastases; mitotic figures and necrosis were absent. Immunohistochemistry showed diffuse positivity for CD10, estrogen receptor, and progesterone receptor; negativity for c-kit, CD34, Dog1, α-smooth muscle actin, S100, desmin, and pan-cytokeratin; and a Ki-67 index of 8%.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 35-year-old woman with acute myeloid leukemia underwent allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation in January 2023. One week after graft, she was neutropenic and febrile (up to 40 °C) despite meropenem. Chest CT was negative; head and sinus CT showed acute inflammation of the maxillary, ethmoidal, and sphenoidal sinuses without bone erosion. She received meropenem, vancomycin, and voriconazole without improvement; symptoms transiently improved and she was discharged.

Two months later, she returned with persistent sinus symptoms, worsening mental status (drowsiness), and new left eye pain and swelling worsened by eye movement, with tearing and redness. On exam, she was alert but had left eyelid edema with purulent discharge, proptosis, chemosis, ophthalmoplegia, and a nonreactive left pupil; the right eye was normal. Lab studies showed WBC 4000/mm3, absolute neutrophils 90 × 103/µL, hemoglobin 10 g/dL, platelets 25 × 109/L, and elevated LDH.

Contrast-enhanced brain CT revealed a cortico-subcortical hypodense area in the left frontal white matter without hemorrhage; left proptosis, orbital cellulitis, mild globe tenting, and ongoing left maxillary, ethmoidal, and sphenoidal sinusitis; demineralization of ethmoid septae, conchae, septum, turbinates, and medial maxillary walls. Multiphasic CT angiography showed absent opacification of the left ophthalmic and facial artery and vein; intracranial vessels were otherwise normal. 

Subsequent brain MRI with contrast demonstrated bilateral fronto-basal and left temporo-polar cortico-subcortical restricted diffusion (more extensive on the left), swelling of adjacent gyri, T2 hypersignal, multiple hypointensities on SWI, ischemic signal along the left optic nerve with orbital soft tissue edema and proptosis. Post-contrast images showed minimal pachymeningeal thickening and extensive leptomeningeal enhancement in the left frontal and temporal regions; no intra-axial nodular enhancement. The left cavernous sinus lacked enhancement, consistent with cavernous sinus thrombosis.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 68-year-old woman under follow-up for osteoporosis presented with symptomatic kidney stones, bone fractures, diarrhea, abdominal pain, dysphagia, and dyspnea. Laboratory studies showed a serum calcium level of 8.99 mg/dL (normal, 8.5–10.5 mg/dL), ionized calcium of 4.69 mg/dL (4.64–5.28 mg/dL), parathyroid hormone level of 191.5 pg/mL (10–65 pg/mL), and vitamin D level of 40 ng/mL (20–50 ng/mL), consistent with primary hyperparathyroidism in the setting of a normal serum calcium level. A Tc-99m sestamibi scan demonstrated a focal area of increased uptake in the anterior mediastinum on immediate imaging with decreased activity on delayed images, as well as slight uptake in the lower third of the left thyroid lobe. A subsequent CT scan revealed an 18-mm mass in the upper anterior mediastinum posterior to the sternum. Neck ultrasound identified nodules in the right thyroid lobe. Given the suspicion for an ectopic mediastinal parathyroid adenoma, she was referred for surgical resection.

In the operating room, after induction of general anesthesia with double-lumen intubation, a right uniportal video-assisted thoracic surgery approach was used to resect the mediastinal mass. The lesion was sent for frozen‐section analysis, which showed a nodular pattern composed mainly of epithelial elements mixed with a patchy lymphoid component; the epithelial cells were arranged in solid sheets and nests with occasional pseudoglandular structures, and lymphocytes were mainly at the periphery. These morphological features were interpreted as consistent with an ectopic parathyroid gland. Ten minutes after removal of the mass, the intraoperative parathyroid hormone level was 149.3 pg/mL. At 30 minutes, the parathyroid hormone level had increased to 259.5 pg/mL.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 60-year-old man presented with a 2-day history of headache and right-sided weakness without altered consciousness. He had no fever, prior surgeries, or other medical conditions. On neurologic examination, he had right hemiparesis.  
Cerebrospinal fluid analysis showed 15 cells/mm3 (predominantly lymphomononuclear) with normal glucose, protein, and flow-cytometry immunophenotyping. Viral serologies, tumor markers, and protein electrophoresis were normal. Evaluation for immunodeficiency, including T-3 lymphocyte count, serum immunoglobulin levels, and complement levels, was normal. CT scans of the chest and abdomen showed no abnormalities.  
Brain MRI demonstrated a perivascular enhancing lesion in the frontoparietal white matter with central diffusion restriction, surrounding vasogenic edema, and leptomeningeal enhancement. MR spectroscopy revealed an elevated lipid–lactate peak, and perfusion imaging showed increased microvascular permeability.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 43-year-old woman presented with a 2-month history of painful, blurred vision in her left eye.  
On examination:  
• Visual acuity was 6/6 in the right eye and counting fingers at 1 ft in the left eye; intraocular pressure was 12 mmHg and 6 mmHg, respectively.  
• The left eye had 4+ anterior chamber cells, a 1.7 mm hypopyon, 2+–3+ flare, only mild conjunctival injection, and multiple iris nodules. The fundus view was obscured. The right eye was normal.  
• B-scan ultrasonography of the left eye revealed heterogeneous vitreous echogenicity.  

Initial management and investigations:  
• Intravitreal vancomycin and ceftazidime plus fortified topical drops were administered. Systemic vancomycin and ceftazidime infusions were given.  
• Vitreous gram stain, KOH preparation, acid-fast bacilli stain, aerobic/anaerobic/mycobacterial cultures, and PCR for bacteria, fungus, and mycobacteria were all negative. Two sets of blood cultures were sterile.  
• Mantoux, pathergy, and anergy tests were negative; chest radiograph was unremarkable; paranasal sinus radiograph showed maxillary sinusitis.  
• Four days later, vision had declined to light perception, and intravitreal amikacin was given for presumed atypical mycobacterial infection.  
• Prednisolone at 1 mg/kg/day was started empirically. One day after starting steroids, the hypopyon increased to 2.0 mm.  

On slit-lamp examination at this time, there was scleral thinning and melting at previous injection sites with prolapsed uveal tissue.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 67-year-old African American woman with type 2 insulin-dependent diabetes, hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, gout, CKD stage III, and osteoarthritis presented with one month of proximal upper-extremity weakness. She had been on atorvastatin for six years; it was discontinued one month before evaluation for presumed statin myopathy. At that time, she had low-grade fevers, myalgia, arthralgia, and a runny nose. Baseline CPK on atorvastatin was 230 U/L, peaked at 529 U/L at discontinuation, and rose to 720 U/L twenty days later.

On exam one week after seeing rheumatology, she reported right shoulder pain radiating to neck and wrist but had 5/5 strength in all extremities. She noted occasional dysphagia and photosensitivity but denied lower-limb weakness, rashes, ulcers, Raynaud’s, or dyspnea. Findings included right shoulder impingement signs and mild right wrist swelling. Labs showed normocytic anemia, BUN elevation, CRP 2.5 mg/L, and CPK 720 U/L. Shoulder and wrist x-rays showed severe degenerative osteoarthritis, chondrocalcinosis, and osteopenia.

Given her gout and CKD, she received a right-shoulder steroid injection and a prednisone taper, with complete symptom resolution in two weeks and CPK normalization to 145 U/L at two months. Three months later, pravastatin was started; after three months, she developed recurrent proximal myalgias in upper and lower limbs, CPK 586 U/L, and ESR 51 mm/hr. Pravastatin was stopped.

A myositis panel was negative. Anti–HMG-CoA reductase antibody testing was not performed. EMG showed peripheral sensory neuropathy without myopathic changes. MRI of the right humerus revealed a small bursal effusion, severe osteoarthritis, rotator cuff tear, and tendinosis but no muscle edema. She was referred to neurology; her presentation was not consistent with a neurologic disorder, and her CPK rose further to 1400 U/L.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 65-year-old Japanese man with hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus was admitted with a 2-week history of progressive dyspnea and productive cough. Several days before symptom onset, vildagliptin (100 mg/day) was initiated for uncontrolled diabetes.  
On admission, temperature 35.8 °C, blood pressure 120/79 mmHg, heart rate 66 bpm, and oxygen saturation 98% on 2 L/min oxygen. On chest auscultation, fine crackles were heard bilaterally; there were no signs of connective-tissue disease.  
Laboratory studies showed elevated serum IgE (4216 mg/dL; normal <400 mg/dL) and KL-6 (9781 U/mL; normal <500 U/mL). Autoantibodies including ANA, anti-RNP, anti-Smith, anti-Ro/SSA, anti-La/SSB, anti-centromere, anti-topoisomerase I, and anti–tRNA synthetase antibodies were all within normal limits.  
Arterial blood gas on 2 L/min oxygen showed pH 7.450, PaO2 111.6 Torr, PaCO2 32.1 Torr, and HCO3– 21.8 mmol/L.  
Chest radiography revealed reduced bilateral lung volumes and diffuse reticular shadows. Chest CT demonstrated extensive ground-glass opacities with irregular reticulation in a peribronchovascular and basal-dominant distribution. Pulmonary function testing showed a restrictive pattern with FVC 43.2% of predicted.  
Bronchoalveolar lavage fluid analysis revealed 23% macrophages, 57% lymphocytes, 5% neutrophils, and 12% eosinophils; microbiological studies were negative.  
Transbronchial lung biopsy specimens showed atypical multinucleated regenerative alveolar epithelial cells, infiltration of eosinophils, lymphocytes, and plasma cells, and interstitial fibrosis around thickened alveolar walls, consistent with subacute interstitial pneumonia showing organizing pneumonia with an acute alveolar injury pattern.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old Nigerian woman was referred with a one-month history of recurrent dyspnea, central chest pain, headaches, visual blurring, and dizziness. She reported orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, progressive bilateral leg swelling, and reduced urine output. She had been diagnosed with hypertension five years earlier but was noncompliant with medications; there was no family history of hypertension.

On examination, she was in respiratory distress and pale, afebrile (36.8 °C). Vital signs: blood pressure 220/120 mmHg, pulse 118 beats/min, respiratory rate 50 breaths/min. BMI was 23.8 kg/m^2. Cardiovascular exam revealed arterial wall thickening, a fourth heart sound, and a loud A2. Lung auscultation showed reduced breath sounds in the left lower zones and bibasal fine crepitations. She had bilateral pitting edema to the knees, grade III hypertensive retinopathy, and asterixis. No focal neurologic deficits were noted.

Laboratory studies showed serum creatinine 900 µmol/L (normal 53–115), urea 30 mmol/L (2.1–7.1), Na 136 mmol/L, K 4.0 mmol/L, Cl 98 mmol/L, HCO3 28 mmol/L. Liver viral markers and HIV were negative. Urinalysis revealed ++ proteinuria without hematuria.

Renal ultrasound and Doppler findings:
- Right kidney: 8.63 × 4.59 × 5.07 cm, loss of corticomedullary differentiation; renal artery velocity 73.9 cm/s, resistive index 0.9.
- Left kidney: upper margin appeared intrathoracic and poorly visualized; hypoechoic with loss of corticomedullary differentiation; renal artery velocity 195 cm/s consistent with stenosis.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old Caucasian woman presented with 5 days of nausea, vomiting, and fatigue. She reported a 1-month history of poor appetite, fatigue, 6-kg unintentional weight loss, occasional vague abdominal pain, and recent tanning of her skin. Her medical history was notable for 17 years of hypothyroidism, initially well-controlled on levothyroxine until she switched to animal thyroid extract several months earlier. One week before presentation, she began trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) 160 mg twice daily for a sinus infection.

On examination, she appeared ill and dehydrated. Supine blood pressure was 131/80 mm Hg with heart rate 81/min; standing blood pressure was 107/67 mm Hg with heart rate 96/min. Skin and mucous membranes were dry, capillary refill was 3 seconds, and there was mild epigastric tenderness without guarding. Neurologic examination was normal.

Laboratory studies revealed sodium 101 mmol/L, chloride 73 mmol/L, potassium 5.2 mmol/L, CO2 16 mmol/L, BUN 20 mg/dL, creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, and normal liver function tests, calcium, phosphorus, glucose, albumin, and lipid panel. Plasma osmolality was 225 mOsm/kg (normal 275–295), urine sodium 128 mmol/L, and urine osmolality 128 mOsm/kg. Hyperglycemia and other causes of nonhypotonic hyponatremia were excluded. She was diagnosed with hypotonic hypovolemic hyponatremia with elevated urine sodium and osmolality and no history of diuretic use. According to the diagnostic algorithm, adrenal insufficiency was considered and adrenal function tests were ordered.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

options.D: Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH)

question:
A 24-year-old nulliparous Hispanic woman with an uncomplicated antenatal course presented in active labor at 40 weeks of gestation. Twelve hours after an uneventful vaginal delivery, she developed chills, diaphoresis, right-sided back pain, and a fever of 40.0 °C. Her heart rate was 110 beats/min, blood pressure 136/85 mm Hg, and respiratory rate 20 breaths/min. Examination revealed marked right costovertebral tenderness. Laboratory evaluation showed a WBC count of 18,000/µL; urinalysis demonstrated 3+ blood, 3+ leukocyte esterase, and 49 WBCs per high-power field. She was started on intravenous antibiotics for presumed pyelonephritis. 

Thirty-six hours after delivery, she became dyspneic, with a nonproductive cough and malaise. Her temperature rose to 40.5 °C, heart rate to 136 beats/min, blood pressure 136/82 mm Hg, respiratory rate 32 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation was 76% on room air. Chest radiography showed poor aeration at both lung bases and large bilateral pleural effusions. Blood and urine cultures grew Escherichia coli. Forty hours after delivery, she was intubated. A 12-lead ECG showed sinus tachycardia without ischemic changes; troponin levels were normal. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed biventricular hypokinesis and an ejection fraction of 35% with normal end-diastolic chamber size and normal valvular function. Contrast-enhanced chest CT showed bilateral airway disease and no pulmonary embolus.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 29-year-old Indigenous Australian man presented with severe (8/10) epigastric pain radiating to his back. He had a history of asthma, type 2 diabetes mellitus, obesity, and known hypertriglyceridemia; he had been hospitalized three years earlier for hypertriglyceridemia-induced pancreatitis (triglycerides 39 mmol/L). He was a current smoker and denied alcohol use.  
On arrival: temperature 36.5°C, heart rate 64 bpm, blood pressure 138/83 mmHg, respiratory rate 11/min, oxygen saturation 99% on room air. He appeared clinically dehydrated.  
Initial blood tests were lipemic; after resuscitation, results showed triglycerides 24.1 mmol/L (reference <1.70), cholesterol 8.4 mmol/L (<5.5), lipase 119 U/L (8–78), white blood cell count 12.3×10^9/L (4–12), corrected calcium 2.29 mmol/L (2.22–2.53), creatinine 68 μmol/L (63–111), and random glucose 21.3 mmol/L. No ketonuria was detected.  
Contrast-enhanced abdominal CT performed 24 hours after symptom onset demonstrated fatty liver but no features of pancreatitis.  
Given the absence of imaging evidence of pancreatitis and only mildly elevated lipase, it was considered that his abdominal pain might represent a “hyperlipemic abdominal crisis,” although pancreatitis could not be ruled out.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

An 88-year-old man with metastatic prostate cancer and stage III chronic kidney disease was admitted with one week of severe weakness, anorexia, and jaundice. He was diagnosed with prostate cancer 8 months before admission, with an initial PSA level of 4280 ng/mL, skeletal and lung metastases, and no liver metastases on contrast CT. He began androgen-deprivation therapy with bicalutamide and leuprolide, but his PSA rose rapidly from 357 ng/mL to 3030 ng/mL over several months. One week before admission, bicalutamide was stopped and enzalutamide was initiated. Other medications included leuprolide, finasteride, doxazosin, metoprolol, omeprazole, ferrous gluconate, multivitamin, psyllium, and a nutritional supplement. He denied transfusions, travel, alcohol, tobacco, or illicit drug use and had no personal or family history of liver disease.

On admission, he was afebrile, pulse 91/min, blood pressure 105/47 mm Hg (MAP 66), and visibly jaundiced without hepatosplenomegaly or stigmata of chronic liver disease. Laboratory tests showed total bilirubin 3.8 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 3.1 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 654 IU/L, gamma-glutamyl transferase 988 IU/L, albumin 4 g/dL, and INR 1.2. Serologic tests for hepatitis B and C were negative. Abdominal ultrasonography showed nonobstructing cholelithiasis without cholecystitis or biliary ductal dilatation, with normal portal and hepatic vasculature and no lymphadenopathy. Because of his renal impairment, contrast CT was deferred. Noncontrast MRI and MRCP demonstrated normal liver parenchyma with mild reactive hepatic hilar lymphadenopathy, small benign liver cysts/hemangiomas, nonobstructing pancreatic cysts, and no biliary obstruction or metastatic lesions.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 51-year-old multiparous perimenopausal woman presented with a painless right breast mass noted on self-examination. The mass had not changed in size since detection, and there was no associated pain, nipple discharge, skin discoloration, or axillary swelling. Her medical history was notable for well-controlled hypertension. On examination, there was a nontender, mobile 2 × 2 cm mass in the upper outer quadrant of the right breast, free from the overlying skin and chest wall, and no axillary lymphadenopathy. Two weeks after detection, a blind core biopsy with a 14-gauge needle yielded inadequate tissue for diagnosis. Three weeks later, a repeat blind core biopsy showed acute-on-chronic nonspecific inflammation with fat necrosis and no evidence of malignancy. Three weeks after the second biopsy, because the mass persisted and the patient experienced some discomfort, a breast ultrasound was performed. B-mode imaging demonstrated a thick-walled anechoic lesion measuring 2.07 × 1.02 × 0.9 cm with a pulsating anechoic center. Color Doppler examination revealed swirling flow within the center of the lesion.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 14-year-old girl presented with 2 days of bilateral painful red eyes and mild blurred vision, following a month of persistent low back pain unrelieved by rest. She had a 2-year history of recurrent inflammatory arthritis and morning stiffness treated intermittently with NSAIDs. Ten days before presentation her joint pain became continuous and more intense, accompanied by daily fever peaks; she received prednisolone 20 mg daily for 3 days and a 5-day course of gentamicin, doxycycline, and rifampicin without improvement.  

She had no significant past medical, travel, or family history. On exam, temperature was 38.5 °C, pulse 105 bpm, respiratory rate 23/min, blood pressure 120/80 mm Hg, and BMI 20 kg/m2. She had red eyes, tenderness of the right elbow and both ankles, and a swan-neck deformity of the fifth fingers bilaterally.  

Ophthalmic exam: visual acuity 20/20 OU; slit-lamp showed bilateral endothelial and anterior lens precipitates, 3+ anterior chamber cells, few anterior vitreous cells (anterior uveitis); grade 1 optic disc edema bilaterally; normal macula, peripheral retina, vessels; normal IOP.  

Laboratory studies: WBC 12.4 K/mm3 (85% neutrophils), hemoglobin 12.2 g/dL, platelets 340 K/mm3, ESR 50 mm/h, CRP 4.6 mg/L; normal LFTs, creatinine, urea, urinalysis. Negative ANA, RF, HIV, VDRL, HBsAg, anti-HCV, Brucella IgG/IgM, blood cultures; negative tuberculin test. Hand X-ray: proximal interphalangeal joint space narrowing of fifth fingers; chest, pelvis, lumbosacral spine X-rays normal; echocardiogram normal.  

She was diagnosed with enthesitis-related JIA and treated with prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg/day, with rapid improvement of fever, eye redness, and arthritis. Methotrexate 10 mg weekly was added on discharge.  

Seven days later she returned with a new, generalized persistent tension-type headache, partially relieved by analgesics, and blurred vision. Ophthalmic exam revealed normal visual acuity but grade 3 optic disc edema OU with no anterior chamber or vitreous flare. Vital signs were normal.  
Brain computed tomography (CT) was normal. Lumbar puncture showed an opening pressure of 300 mm H₂O; CSF analysis revealed no cells, protein 40 mg/dL, glucose 56 mg/dL (serum 79 mg/dL). Total blood leukocyte count was 10.4 K/mm3 (86% neutrophils), ESR 20 mm/h; other labs were normal.  
MRI of the brain demonstrated mild optic nerve tortuosity, posterior globe flattening, and prominent subarachnoid space around the optic nerves; magnetic resonance venography (MRV) was normal.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 35-year-old man presented to the emergency department with acute onset confusion and transient left-arm tingling that resolved before arrival. Initial head CT and CT angiography of the head and neck were normal, and he was discharged. Two weeks later, he returned with fever, headache, and altered mental status characterized by waxing and waning attention. On examination, he was delirious but had no focal neurologic deficits; there was a diffuse petechial rash on his extremities. Laboratory studies showed a platelet count of 7,000/mcL, hemoglobin of 10.6 g/dL (baseline 15.6 g/dL), lactate dehydrogenase of 817 U/L, and haptoglobin less than 10 mg/dL. Serum creatinine was 1.0 mg/dL, and estimated glomerular filtration rate was greater than 60 mL/min/1.73 m2. He was started on high-dose steroids and plasmapheresis, and an ADAMTS13 activity assay was sent.

On hospital day 1, a brain MRI (1.5T) demonstrated multiple patchy T2- and FLAIR-hyperintense lesions in the right perisylvian white matter, right thalamus, left lentiform nucleus, and right midbrain. Susceptibility-weighted imaging showed punctate low-signal foci in these regions, consistent with microhemorrhages. No abnormal enhancement was seen. Review of prior imaging revealed bilateral parotid calcifications and cysts, raising concern for Sjögren’s syndrome. Differential considerations based on MRI included atypical posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome related to TTP or viral encephalitis. A lumbar puncture showed clear cerebrospinal fluid with 2 WBCs/dL, glucose 67 mg/dL, protein 48 mg/dL; cultures were negative, and viral PCR and antibody panels for West Nile virus, herpes simplex virus, varicella-zoster virus, and enteroviruses were negative.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 57‐year‐old Caucasian woman underwent elective revision spinal surgery at L5/S1 without complications. She was discharged 7 days later with normal strength (MRC grade 5/5) and sensation in both lower limbs and was provided below‐knee thromboembolic deterrent stockings to wear for 6 weeks.
