Three weeks postoperatively, she presented with a right‐sided, painless foot drop. She first noticed weakness and numbness in her right foot 1 week earlier and was told by her general practitioner that it was related to her spinal surgery. 

On examination, she had a high‐stepping gait. Sensation was reduced over the distal two‐thirds of the right lateral lower leg and over the dorsum of the right foot, including the first webspace. Plantar sensation, the ankle jerk, and plantar reflexes were preserved. Strength was 1/5 for ankle dorsiflexion, ankle eversion, and toe and hallux extension, and 4/5 for ankle inversion; all other muscle groups were normal. Distal pulses and capillary refill were normal. She had no increase in back pain, no leg pain, and no leg swelling. On removal of the right stocking, there was a well‐demarcated indentation in the skin at the proximal rim at the level of the fibular neck, which was not present on the left side.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

An 86-year-old man was brought to the emergency department for abrupt-onset altered mental status, auditory hallucinations, repetition of words and phrases, and intermittent agitation followed by somnolence. His medical history included Alzheimer’s dementia diagnosed one year earlier, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, osteoarthritis, syringomyelia status post laminectomy, and peripheral arterial disease. Home medications were memantine (started 1 month earlier at 5 mg twice daily), metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily, and meclizine 12.5 mg every 8 hours as needed.

On arrival, blood pressure was 197/79 mm Hg, heart rate 97 beats/min, respiratory rate 15 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation > 95% on room air. Laboratory testing, including complete blood count, comprehensive metabolic panel, lipase level, ethanol level, and drug toxicology, showed no significant abnormalities. An electrocardiogram demonstrated normal sinus rhythm with occasional premature ventricular contractions and a normal QTc interval. Noncontrast CT of the head revealed only chronic microvascular changes and diffuse age-related parenchymal volume loss without acute lesions.

On examination, the patient was alert but oriented only to self, experienced auditory hallucinations, and repeated questions verbatim. There was no nystagmus, tremor, or rigidity. An urgent EEG did not show epileptiform activity. Neurology and psychiatry were consulted, and he was admitted to the intensive care unit for observation.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 37-year-old Caucasian man presented with five days of redness in the right eye, three days of photophobia, ocular pain, and slight blurriness. He denied ocular trauma, recent illness, or cold sores. His ophthalmic history included uncomplicated bilateral small-incision lenticule extraction (SMILE) six months earlier. He had begun nighttime continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapy two weeks prior for newly diagnosed obstructive sleep apnea and noted increased right-eye dryness on awakening. On examination, uncorrected visual acuity was 20/25 in the right eye and 20/20 in the left eye. Slit-lamp examination of the right eye revealed inferonasal conjunctival injection with sectoral ciliary flush and limbitis. At the 4 o’clock position, small peripheral corneal infiltrates were visible without epithelial defect or corneal staining. Diffuse lamellar keratitis (grade I) was noted beneath the SMILE cap margin, corresponding to the area of limbitis. Examination of the left eye revealed mild superficial punctate keratopathy. Both eyes showed inspissated Meibomian glands and posterior marginal telangiectasias.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 45-year-old man presented in December 2010 with one month of left‐eye proptosis and diplopia but no pain or redness. His medical history was notable for HIV and hepatitis B virus infection since 1986, complicated by cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). In July 2006, he underwent orthotopic liver transplantation for end‐stage liver disease with concurrent HCC; native‐liver histology had shown a tumor thrombus in the right portal vein branch. In December 2008, he had partial resection of the transplanted liver for recurrent HCC. He began sorafenib in 2009 but received it irregularly due to diarrhea. In December 2009, he developed new liver and pulmonary recurrences; he underwent transarterial chemoembolization of liver lesions and surgical resection of left‐middle‐lobe pulmonary metastases, followed by systemic liposomal doxorubicin. In July 2010, further liver and lung recurrences prompted chemotherapy with 5-fluorouracil and later capecitabine.

On examination, his visual acuity was 20/20 bilaterally. The left eye had 6 mm of proptosis with inferior displacement and superior sulcus fullness. A firm mass was palpable in the superior orbit, inseparable from the orbital rim. Left‐eye motility was limited on upward and leftward gaze. Serum α-fetoprotein was 3750 ng/mL. Computed tomography of the orbits showed an extraconal mass involving the superior and lateral walls of the left orbit, with destruction of orbital bone, incorporation of the lateral rectus muscle, and calcifications.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 12-year-old boy presented for evaluation of a scalp rash. Three years earlier, he had sustained a third-degree burn of the scalp treated with an autologous skin graft. Two years after the graft, a rash developed over the grafted area and gradually worsened over the following year. The patient reported pruritus and erythema, and he noted development of a thin film over the site. Treatment with topical 5% doxepin relieved the itch, but multiple courses of topical antibiotics and antibacterial soaps produced no improvement, prompting consideration of regrafting for presumed infection. His history was notable for atopic dermatitis; his mother had hidradenitis suppurativa. On examination, approximately 70% of the scalp radiating from the vertex showed diffuse erythema, weeping shallow erosions with overlying yellow-brown crusts, and atrophic skin bordering the graft. Prior bacterial cultures obtained at the burn hospital were all negative.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Erosive pustular dermatosis of the scalp

question:
A 48-year-old woman with autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease, hypertension, hyperlipidaemia, premature ventricular contractions, and anxiety underwent elective right renal cyst aspiration for right upper-quadrant pain. Under ultrasound guidance and moderate sedation, an 18-gauge needle yielded 170 mL of clear yellow fluid. She reported symptomatic relief and was initially stable. One hour later, she developed severe abdominal pain. Examination revealed a soft abdomen and cool, dry skin. Vital signs showed bradycardia and hypotension. Intravenous fluids and Trendelenburg positioning were started, but she became unresponsive and pulseless. Advanced cardiac life support was initiated with CPR and intravenous epinephrine. After return of spontaneous circulation, she complained of severe chest pain. Bedside ultrasound showed no right flank haematoma or pericardial effusion. An ECG demonstrated ST-segment elevations in the anterolateral leads and depressions in the inferior leads. A chest radiograph was normal. Initial laboratory studies showed normal troponin, serum glucose, and serum potassium levels. She was taken emergently for coronary angiography, which demonstrated angiographically normal coronary arteries.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 19-year-old man with depression, a history of substance use disorder, and a prior suicide attempt was brought in after being found unresponsive at home, with a scald burn to the chest and possible aspiration pneumonia following ingestion of an unknown substance. On arrival, he was unresponsive, incontinent of urine and feces, and had crackles at the left lung base with an oxygen saturation of 74%. He had blistering burns over approximately 6% of his chest and was intubated for airway protection.

Laboratory studies showed only mild elevations in liver function tests and lactate; a urine drug screen was positive for cannabis but negative for other illicit substances. Chest CT revealed left lower lobe consolidation consistent with aspiration pneumonia; noncontrast head CT was unremarkable. ECG showed sinus tachycardia.

He remained intubated for 24 hours, and burns and pneumonia were managed. After extubation on hospital day 2, he remained severely encephalopathic—opening eyes to pain, nonverbal, and disoriented. By day 3, he had nonsensical speech, appeared to respond to internal stimuli, and was not oriented to time, place, or situation. Psychiatry was consulted on day 6 with a working differential that included psychiatric disorders, opioid withdrawal psychosis, and anoxic brain injury from toxic ingestion; risperidone was started for agitation.

Brain MRI demonstrated symmetric bilateral diffusion restriction with FLAIR hyperintensities in the centrum semiovale, occipital lobes, and splenium of the corpus callosum. A lumbar puncture was nondiagnostic. A presumptive diagnosis of toxic leukoencephalopathy from synthetic opioid overdose was made. Over the next week he steadily improved—became fully oriented, regained speech and motor function, and admitted to overdosing on a “pressed pill” containing synthetic opioids.

On hospital day 13, he was alert and at his cognitive baseline but was noted to have new bilateral hearing impairment, with difficulty hearing higher-pitched sounds and requiring repeated questions at louder volumes.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 4-year-old girl presented with 4 days of worsening cough and increased work of breathing and 1 day of anuria. She had had a cough, sore throat, and rhinorrhea about one week earlier and was treated with amoxicillin. On examination, her temperature was 37.5 °C, pulse 125 beats/min, blood pressure 114/58 mmHg, respiratory rate 70 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 95% on 30 L high-flow oxygen. She had bilateral periorbital edema, crusted nasal discharge, pallor, nasal flaring, intercostal retractions, and coarse crackles bilaterally (more on the right). There was no rash or purpura.  

Initial laboratory studies revealed WBC 9.08 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 60 g/L, platelets 349 × 10^9/L, urea 16.9 mmol/L, creatinine 46 μmol/L, CRP 34.5 mg/L, potassium 5.9 mmol/L, bicarbonate 15 mmol/L, and a normal anion gap. Urinalysis showed leukocyte esterase 500 WBC/μL, protein 1.5 g/L, blood 250 RBC/μL; urine microscopy revealed 20–50 WBC/HPF, 11–20 RBC/HPF, and 3–5 granular casts/LPF.  

After >250 mL hemoptysis, she was transferred to the PICU. An arterial blood gas showed combined metabolic and respiratory acidosis (pH 7.25, pCO₂ 43 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 18 mmol/L). Chest radiography revealed bilateral consolidations with air bronchograms consistent with diffuse pulmonary hemorrhage. She was started on bilevel positive-airway pressure but progressed to intubation and high-frequency oscillatory ventilation. A nasogastric tube showed no gastric blood, and tracheal aspirates demonstrated bright red and congealed blood, confirming an alveolar source. She received two packed red-blood-cell transfusions and, given her worsening status and anuria, continuous renal replacement therapy was initiated.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 24-year-old woman presented with acute onset of severe left lower-quadrant abdominal pain that quickly became intermittent and mild (3/10). She denied fevers, vomiting, vaginal discharge or bleeding, dyspareunia, urinary symptoms, and changes in bowel habits. Her menstrual cycles were regular; her last period was 3 weeks prior. She had no history of sexually transmitted infections, pelvic masses, or prior pelvic surgery. Medical history was significant only for celiac disease.

On examination, she was afebrile and hemodynamically stable. Abdominal examination revealed a soft abdomen with mild left lower-quadrant tenderness without guarding or rebound. Speculum and pelvic examinations were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 11.4×10^9/L, neutrophils 8.9×10^9/L, and C-reactive protein <2 mg/L. A pregnancy test was negative (β-hCG <2 IU/L). Urinalysis and vaginal cultures were negative.

Transabdominal and transvaginal pelvic ultrasonography demonstrated a left ovarian volume of 4 mL (right ovary 1.9 mL), both within normal limits. There was no discrete mass or cyst. The distal left fallopian tube appeared bulky but with normal vascularity and no tubo-ovarian collection. Color Doppler flow was normal in both ovaries. The report noted that the patient was unable to empty her bladder during the study.

The gynecology team admitted her for observation and analgesia. Analgesic requirements remained minimal until day 4, when she became febrile to 38.4 °C and required more analgesia. Empiric intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics were started. On day 6, her C-reactive protein rose to 207 mg/L. The decision was made to perform an exploratory laparoscopy.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A four‐month‐old male infant was found unresponsive in his crib early one morning. The mother performed CPR until emergency responders arrived; he was transported to the hospital and died. He had been born at 33 weeks’ gestation, with an unremarkable neonatal course except for prematurity; both mother and infant tested positive for THC at birth, and an 11-day postnatal cranial ultrasound was normal. 

On external examination at autopsy, there were florid petechial hemorrhages of the conjunctivae and face, and two small contusions on the left arm. Weight and head circumference were below the 5th percentile for adjusted age. 

Postmortem skeletal radiographs showed multiple cranial vault fractures and metaphyseal fractures of the left humerus, radius, and ulna. Internal examination revealed hemorrhagic cerebrospinal fluid, two scalp soft‐tissue hemorrhages overlying corresponding skull fractures, and scant subarachnoid hemorrhage. 

Neuropathology confirmed focal subacute subarachnoid hemorrhages in bilateral fronto‐parietal regions without retinal hemorrhages. 

Anthropological examination of the skull cap revealed 14 antemortem cranial fractures—comminuted and linear fractures in the parietals and occiput with evidence of healing at varying stages, including rounded margins, subperiosteal new bone, and bridging fibrous and cartilaginous tissue. A “stair‐step” linear fracture was noted in the right parietal. 

The left upper extremity exhibited healed metaphyseal fractures of the proximal humerus, distal radius, and distal ulna, as well as a remodeled diaphyseal fracture of the ulna with medial rotation of the distal fragment. 

Histology of three sampled cranial fractures demonstrated at least three distinct healing stages, ranging from minimal fibrous connective tissue and capillary ingrowth to areas of woven bone and cartilage formation.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 27-year-old woman from Myanmar, living in Japan for 3 years, was diagnosed 2 years earlier with colonic Crohn’s disease based on discontinuous ulcers and non-caseating granulomas on colonoscopy; she responded to 5-ASA and was lost to follow-up. She now presents with a 2-week history of abdominal pain, anorexia, fatigue, and watery diarrhea (5/day). There is no fever or night sweats initially. She restarted 5-ASA without relief and underwent colonoscopy at the referral hospital, which showed non-longitudinal ulcers, inflammatory edema, pseudopolyps, and skip lesions. Prednisolone at 0.8 mg/kg was initiated but was ineffective, and she subsequently developed high fevers (up to 40 °C), hemoglobin 6.9 g/dL, and albumin 1.0 g/dL, prompting transfer. 

On transfer, prednisolone was increased to 1 mg/kg. There was no personal or family history of tuberculosis; an interferon-gamma release assay had not been performed, and the prior IGRA was negative under steroids. Physical examination: temperature 38.7 °C, pulse 102 bpm, BP 104/77 mmHg; abdominal tenderness without peritoneal signs; palpable cervical and inguinal lymph nodes; lower-leg edema. 

Contrast CT of the chest and abdomen revealed calcified mediastinal and hilar lymph nodes, atelectasis with calcification in the right middle lobe, widespread intra-abdominal lymphadenopathy (>1 cm), and pelvic ascites; findings were noted to be atypical for Crohn’s disease and suggestive of pulmonary tuberculosis. Piperacillin/tazobactam was started. 

Four days after transfer, lower gastrointestinal endoscopy up to the terminal ileum showed no longitudinal ulcers or cobblestone pattern; instead there were round or irregular transverse ulcers arranged circumferentially, a patulous ileocecal valve with pseudodiverticular deformity, pseudopolyposis, and discolored scar areas; the rectosigmoid colon was spared.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 77‐year‐old African American woman with hypertension, rheumatoid arthritis, and hypothyroidism presented with a 1‐month history of progressively worsening shortness of breath and a nonproductive cough. She denied fever, chills, chest pain, hemoptysis, leg swelling, weight loss, or appetite changes and reported no sick contacts, allergies, or exposures to hay, dust, or toxic fumes. Her rheumatoid arthritis had been well controlled on oral methotrexate, 2.5 mg daily, for more than 30 years. 

On examination, her temperature was 38.2 °C, blood pressure 116/57 mm Hg, heart rate 106 beats/min, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 88% on room air. Cardiac and lung examinations were unremarkable except for mild tachypnea. 

Laboratory studies showed a white‐cell count of 9400/µL with 18.3% eosinophils, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 111 mm/hr, serum albumin 2.9 g/dL, and lactate dehydrogenase 265 U/L. Brain‐natriuretic‐peptide levels were normal. 

Chest radiography revealed bilateral upper‐lobe hazy opacities with prominent reticulation. Computed tomography of the chest confirmed nonspecific bilateral upper‐lobe opacification. Influenza and respiratory‐syncytial‐virus tests were negative. Sputum cultures (including acid‐fast bacilli), two sets of blood cultures, and a urine Legionella antigen assay were all negative. A (1-3)-β-D-glucan assay was within normal limits. Echocardiography was normal. Pulmonary‐function testing showed no obstruction, normal maximum voluntary ventilation, no restriction, and mildly decreased diffusion capacity.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

An 81-year-old right-handed man presented with a two-week history of gradually worsening headache. His medical history included lung cancer treated with chemotherapy and radiation two years earlier, with no evidence of recurrence. On examination he was afebrile and normotensive. Neurologic examination was normal except for an active vesicular eruption over the left C4 dermatome. Initial laboratory studies showed a normal CBC and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 79 mm/hr. Noncontrast head CT showed small bilateral subdural hygromas. He was discharged with analgesics, prednisone, and famciclovir.

Twenty-four hours later he returned with visual hallucinations and progressive obtundation. Temperature was 99.0°F and blood pressure was 136/79 mm Hg. He was mildly obtunded and agitated, nonverbal, and unable to follow commands. Neck stiffness was present without overt meningeal signs. Pupils were equal and reactive; cranial nerves were otherwise intact. Motor examination revealed symmetric movements of all extremities. Reflexes were 1+ with bilateral upgoing plantar responses.

Laboratory studies on return showed a WBC count of 12,700/µL (84% neutrophils) and a negative HIV antibody test. Lumbar puncture revealed an opening pressure of 240 mm H₂O and hazy pink cerebrospinal fluid. Tube 1 contained 3600 WBCs (78% neutrophils), 2800 RBCs; Tube 3 contained 6500 WBCs (92% neutrophils), 24,900 RBCs; protein was 501 mg/dL and glucose was 31 mg/dL. CSF bacterial cultures and cytology were negative; qualitative CSF encephalitis panel was positive for varicella zoster virus antibody.

Electroencephalography showed disorganized high-amplitude delta activity with occasional theta waves. Brain MRI demonstrated symmetric T2 hyperintensities in the body and splenium of the corpus callosum extending into the posterior parietal-occipital white matter with vasogenic edema. On T1-weighted imaging with gadolinium there was enhancement of the corpus callosum. MRA revealed moderate stenosis of the right intracavernous internal carotid artery, hypoplasia of the right A1 segment, and minor irregularity of the distal right posterior cerebral artery. Magnetic resonance spectroscopy of the right parietal-occipital white matter showed elevated lipids, reduced N-acetyl aspartate, and a choline-to-creatinine ratio of 1.7. The initial radiology report concluded this appearance was most consistent with a “butterfly glioma.”

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 4‐year‐old castrated male mixed‐breed dog was referred for evaluation of an open wound overlying the left orbit. Two months earlier, the dog had undergone bilateral transconjunctival enucleation with silicone orbital implants for uveodermatologic syndrome (UDS) and secondary glaucoma. Prior to surgery, he had received oral mycophenolate and topical anti‐inflammatory and anti‐glaucoma medications. At surgery, unshaped silicone implants were placed in both orbits. The wounds were closed in multiple layers using absorbable polyglactin deep sutures and nylon skin sutures. Oral amoxicillin/clavulanate was administered for 10 days. Skin sutures were removed 14 days postoperatively, and both incisions appeared healed.

Approximately two months after surgery, marked swelling of the left periorbital region developed, followed by a draining tract. The family veterinarian prescribed oral cefpodoxime and referred the dog for further evaluation. On presentation, there was a 7-mm open wound with serosanguineous discharge at the midpoint of the left orbital incision. The right incision was well healed, and a silicone implant was palpable in the right orbit. Palpation of the left orbit revealed a firm spherical structure similar in size to the contralateral prosthesis. A preliminary diagnosis of partial incisional dehiscence with possible infection or suture reaction was made; implant extrusion was not suspected. Surgical exploration was planned, with removal of the implant and sampling for bacterial culture.

Preanesthetic physical examination and point-of-care bloodwork were unremarkable. Following intramuscular hydromorphone and acepromazine, the dog vomited, and a black spherical object consistent with a silicone implant was recovered from the vomitus. The left incision remained unchanged, and palpation still detected a firm orbital sphere. Under general anesthesia, an elliptical incision was made around the previous scar. Dissection revealed an approximately 2-cm firm, pinkish-white mass filling most of the orbital space. The mass was removed en bloc with peri-incisional skin and bisected to rule out a retained surgical sponge; no foreign material was evident grossly. Additional cultures were obtained, the orbit was flushed, and the wound was closed.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

options.B: Low-level orbital infection with biofilm formation

question:
A 70-year-old man presented with 2 weeks of abdominal pain, headache, and new melena after a family-physician trial of NSAIDs and a mucosal protectant. On arrival, he was hypotensive (80/56 mmHg), tachycardic (100 bpm), and severely anemic (hemoglobin 6.8 g/dL). After blood-transfusion stabilization, esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) showed atypical esophagitis lesions in the lower esophagus and shallow ulcers and erosions throughout the stomach and duodenum. Colonoscopy (CS) revealed extensive, map-like shallow ulcers with well-defined margins and islands of residual mucosa in the terminal ileum; biopsy showed moderate lymphocytic infiltration without malignancy or cytomegalovirus. Biopsy of endoscopically normal ileal mucosa showed only mild lymphocytic infiltration. A proton‐pump inhibitor was started, and upper GI lesions healed on follow-up EGD one month later. Capsule endoscopy had previously demonstrated multiple small aphtha-like erosions in the ileum. Stool cultures were negative. The patient’s gastrin level was 100 pg/mL (normal <200 pg/mL). Serum MPO-ANCA, PR3-ANCA, and antinuclear antibodies were negative. Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was unremarkable. Follow-up CS one month after presentation showed that the terminal ileal ulcers and erosions remained unchanged despite discontinuation of NSAIDs.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A male newborn was delivered at 40 weeks’ gestation by uneventful vaginal delivery to a 28-year-old mother. Birth weight was 3040 g; Apgar scores were 9 and 10 at 1 and 5 minutes. On initial examination, the only abnormality was an irregular, asymmetric, enlarged right testis. Scrotal ultrasonography showed a heterogeneous solid mass in the right testis without retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy; the left testis was descended and external genitalia were normal. Serum alpha-fetoprotein was 97 819 ng/mL (normal for gestational age), and total β-hCG was 31.4 mIU/mL (reference < 5 mIU/mL). At 21 hours of age, a right inguinal orchiectomy was performed. Gross pathology revealed a light-brown, round, solid mass measuring 3.0 × 2.0 × 1.5 cm that sharply replaced most of the testicular parenchyma and displaced the epididymis peripherally.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 64‐year‐old woman presented with headache and fever for 1 week. She described diffuse pain over her calvarium without focal neurologic deficits. Body temperature was 38.7 °C. Examination revealed no lymphadenopathy, no palpable breast masses, and no abdominal masses. Laboratory studies showed:
• Hemoglobin 7.0 g/dL (normocytic anemia), MCV 89.3 fL  
• White‐cell count 12 000/mm3 (neutrophils 61.7%, lymphocytes 26.7%, monocytes 10.8%)  
• Platelet count 325 000/mm3  
• Alkaline phosphatase 158 IU/L (normal 32–91)  
• Lactate dehydrogenase 292 IU/L (normal 98–192)  
• D-dimer 6570 ng/mL (normal 0–500)  
Coagulation times were normal. Chest radiograph showed no pulmonary lesions. A noncontrast CT scan of the head showed multiple osteolytic lesions in the skull compared to films 6 years earlier. A CT scan of the chest and abdomen demonstrated mixed osteolytic and osteosclerotic lesions throughout the skeleton but no clear primary tumor. Serum immunofixation electrophoresis showed no monoclonal protein; immunoglobulin G, A, and M levels were normal. Tumor markers revealed CEA 8.1 ng/mL (normal 0–5) and CA 15-3 163.2 IU/mL (normal 0–31.3), with CA 19-9 and CA-125 within normal limits.  
Bone marrow aspiration from the right posterior iliac crest revealed scant hematopoietic precursors among clusters of oval neoplastic cells with hyperchromatic nuclei and granular cytoplasm, some forming rosette‐like structures. Biopsy after decalcification showed metastatic adenocarcinoma composed of round to oval tumor cells in ovoid clusters, cribriform nests, and microacinar patterns filling the marrow. A metastatic adenocarcinoma was considered the most likely diagnosis.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 33-year-old right-handed woman has experienced spells of loss of consciousness with whole-body jerking since age 13. Multiple outpatient EEGs have been normal. Her medical history is notable for anxiety, depression, and behavioral problems attributed to childhood abuse. She reports two to three emergency visits per year due to these spells, which have caused injuries. She also describes isolated auras of a deja vu sensation occurring twice weekly, lasting about one minute without alteration of consciousness. Trials of Dilantin and phenobarbital were poorly tolerated; Depakote improved the spells but was discontinued due to weight gain; Topamax was ineffective; and a combination of Tegretol and Lamictal provided no control. There is no family history of epilepsy or neurologic disease. Neurologic examination is unremarkable. 

She was admitted to the epilepsy monitoring unit, Lamictal and Tegretol were withdrawn, but no spells were captured and her EEG remained normal. She was discharged on Lamictal and Keppra, but continued to have breakthrough spells and developed mood worsening on Keppra. Several months later, she was readmitted to the epilepsy monitoring unit to capture her spells; Lamictal and Keppra were withheld on admission.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 19-year-old man with no prior medical history presented in October 2023 with sudden onset of speech difficulties, right arm weakness, and paresthesia of the right side of his body. On arrival, he was alert and oriented but had expressive aphasia and difficulty moving his right upper and lower limbs. Vital signs were within normal limits. A noncontrast CT scan of the head showed a hypodense area in the left corona radiata. Routine blood tests, including complete blood count and metabolic panel, were within normal limits. Two days after admission, MRI of the brain and cervical spine with and without gadolinium revealed multiple demyelinating lesions both supra- and infratentorially and in the cervical and dorsal spinal cord, some of which enhanced with contrast—findings suggestive of multiple sclerosis. On hospital day 5, serum studies for MOG antibody, anti–aquaporin 4 antibody, HIV types 1 and 2, VDRL, Borrelia burgdorferi antibody, Toxoplasma antibody, cytomegalovirus antibody, anti–double-stranded DNA antibodies, and an extended ANA blot profile were obtained, and all results were negative.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 33-year-old Moroccan man presented with a left-sided laterocervical mass of four months’ duration. He had no personal or family history of note and denied smoking, alcohol use, irradiation, fever, chills, or weight loss. On examination, the left parotid gland was enlarged and firm, with cervical lymphadenopathy; no overlying skin lesions were observed. Ultrasonography and CT revealed a solid mass in the parotid gland. A biopsy showed a poorly differentiated carcinoma. The patient underwent excision of the tumor. The resected mass measured 21.5 × 9 × 6 cm, with a cut surface that was firm, tan-gray, and lobulated, measuring 6 × 5.5 × 5 cm, with apparently normal salivary gland tissue at the periphery. On histologic examination, the tumor was lobulated, with nests of large foamy cells containing centrally located nuclei and vacuolated clear cytoplasm surrounded by smaller basaloid cells, and areas of squamous islands with keratin pearl formation. Periodic acid–Schiff staining was negative in the foamy cells. Immunohistochemical studies showed expression of EMA, pancytokeratin, and p63 in all neoplastic cells and focal β-catenin, with negative staining for CK5/6, CEA, S100, CD10, vimentin, melan A, and CD45.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 65-year-old Chinese man was first seen 9 years ago for chronic floaters. At that time his visual acuity was 20/20 bilaterally. Slit-lamp examination showed multiple peripheral subepithelial corneal opacities in both eyes, with quiet anterior chambers, clear lenses, and an unremarkable fundus. He had no history of corneal injury or family history of corneal disease; his medical history included diabetes, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia.

Six years after initial presentation, he noted blurred vision in the right eye with a decrease in visual acuity to 20/30. On referral, slit-lamp biomicroscopy revealed bilateral mid-peripheral subepithelial opacities and a central mid-to-posterior stromal haze in the right eye. Fluorescein staining showed mild punctate epithelial erosions and negative staining of some subepithelial opacities. Cataracts were very mild in both eyes, and the visual decline was attributed to progressive corneal opacities.

Anterior segment optical coherence tomography demonstrated a hyperreflective band at the level of Bowman layer and anterior stroma, extending 160 µm centrally and 250 µm paracentrally; the subepithelial opacities protruded toward the surface, causing focal epithelial thinning. Hyperreflective deposits were also seen in the posterior stroma, more prominently in the right eye. In vivo confocal microscopy of both corneas revealed small, discrete hyperreflective epithelial deposits, a heterogeneous hyperreflective anterior stroma masking keratocytes, and highly reflective granular endothelial deposits with preserved cell architecture; corneal nerves were tortuous but without perineural deposits.

Given the presumed diagnosis of a Bowman layer dystrophy or epithelial recurrent erosion corneal dystrophy (ERED), genetic testing of TGFBI and COL17A1 was performed, and no pathogenic mutations were identified. Concern for paraproteinemic keratopathy was raised, and the patient was referred for hematologic evaluation.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 51-year-old woman with no prior comorbidities presented with altered sensorium of 28 days’ duration. Her initial symptoms included drowsiness, decreased voluntary eye movements, inability to eat or follow commands, absence of spontaneous speech, and bladder and bowel incontinence. After a 5-day hospitalization at a local hospital without improvement, she was transferred to a district hospital, where she tested positive for SARS-CoV-2 and was treated in the ICU. Two days later, she regained spontaneous eye opening but remained emotionally labile with inappropriate speech. After 15 days in the ICU without further improvement, she was referred to our center.

On arrival, she was alert and cooperative. She denied weakness, sensory disturbances, headache, visual changes, seizures, trauma, or other systemic symptoms. She reported an episode of fever and cough 4 days before the onset of neurologic symptoms, treated as an upper respiratory infection. Her past history was notable only for burr hole drainage of a traumatic intracranial hemorrhage 25 years earlier. Vital signs were stable except for blood pressure of 150/90 mm Hg. Respiratory and cardiovascular examinations were normal. Neurologically, she had a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 12 (E4V2M6), intact memory and comprehension, dysarthria, increased tone and 3+ reflexes in all limbs with preserved muscle bulk and power, flexor plantar responses, normal sensation, no cerebellar signs, no neck rigidity, and intact cranial nerves and facial symmetry.

She was treated acutely with intravenous hydrocortisone (100 mg) and fluids, then started on oral prednisolone (60 mg daily). Given her new-onset encephalopathy, multifocal neurologic signs, absence of prior demyelinating disease, and recent SARS-CoV-2 infection, acute disseminated encephalomyelitis was considered. 

Laboratory studies showed normal renal and liver function tests, negative erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis revealed <5 leukocytes/mm^3 (100% mononuclear), glucose 47 mg/dL, protein 36 mg/dL, lactate dehydrogenase 62 U/L, adenosine deaminase 1.839 U/L, immunoglobulin G 18.90 mg/L, and absent oligoclonal bands. The encephalitis/meningitis panel was negative. India ink staining and polymerase chain reaction tests for neurotropic viruses were negative. Serologic tests were nonreactive for antinuclear antibody, Treponema pallidum hemagglutination, and toxoplasma antibodies. NMO-IgG and MOG-IgG autoantibodies were negative. Malignancy screening, including serum tumor markers, was negative. She was not recently vaccinated against COVID-19. An MRI of the brain and spine was planned for diagnostic confirmation.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 39-year-old woman from Cali, Colombia, presented with a 6-month history of intensely itchy macules on the limbs that evolved into red-brown papules and nodules, some with mild scaling and superficial ulceration. She also reported dry skin, eyebrow hair loss, and fatigue. She had no significant medical history and took no immunosuppressive medications.

On initial evaluation, vital signs were normal. Physical examination showed skin xerosis with multiple red-brown nodules on the forearms, both ankles, and feet, some with mild scaling and superficial ulceration. There were no areas of cutaneous sensory loss near the lesions.

Two days earlier, a rapid plasma reagin (RPR) test at a primary care clinic was reactive at a 1:128 dilution. Repeat testing at a reference laboratory showed an RPR titer of 1:2 and a reactive chemiluminescent treponemal test. HIV fourth-generation ELISA was negative; hepatitis B and C serologies were negative; hematologic parameters were normal.

A presumptive diagnosis of nodular secondary syphilis was made, and she received three weekly doses of benzathine penicillin G (2,400,000 U). At 6-month follow-up, despite confirmed adherence to therapy, her skin lesions persisted without improvement, and the RPR titer remained 1:2.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 76-year-old Japanese woman with a history of stage IIB rectal adenocarcinoma treated two years earlier by resection presented with a 1-week history of productive cough and fever. Two months before presentation, she had begun FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab for peritoneal dissemination and liver metastases. After the third chemotherapy course, she developed fever up to 39.0 °C without other symptoms; physical examination showed T 39.1 °C, BP 138/82 mmHg, HR 85 bpm, RR 20 breaths/min, and a nontender subclavicular port without skin changes. Her eosinophil count rose from 171 to 607 /µL then fell to 324 /µL three days later, and the fever resolved spontaneously. A similar self-limited fever recurred after the fourth course. During the fifth course, she again developed high fever along with productive cough and chest discomfort that persisted.  
On examination she had coarse inspiratory crackles over the posterior lung fields. Laboratory tests showed WBC 5,660 /µL (eosinophils 736 /µL), hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL, platelets 288,000/µL, CRP 9.92 mg/dL, AST 15 IU/L, ALT 19 IU/L, LDH 236 IU/L, ALP 401 IU/L, γ-GTP 21 IU/L, SP-D 250 U/L, and KL-6 538 U/mL. Urine antigen tests for Legionella and Streptococcus pneumoniae were negative.  
Chest radiography and thoracic CT scans revealed bilateral, nonsegmental, subpleural-predominant areas of consolidation.  
Tazobactam/piperacillin (12.5 g/day) was administered for five days, but her symptoms and radiographic findings continued to worsen.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 34-year-old man presented to the emergency department with a 5-day history of bitemporal headache and 24 hours of intense, throbbing, continuous left neck pain radiating to the ipsilateral mandibular and auricular region. The pain was localized over the left carotid bifurcation and worsened with neck movement and palpation. Physical and neurologic examinations were normal. He denied trauma, recent infection, or chiropractic manipulation; his medical history was otherwise unremarkable. Laboratory studies—including complete blood count, coagulation tests, electrolytes, glucose, renal and liver function tests—were within normal limits. The white-cell count was 7 g/L (reference, 4–10 g/L), C-reactive protein was 6.3 mg/L (reference, 0–10 mg/L), and erythrocyte sedimentation rate was mildly elevated at 11 mm/h (reference, 0–10 mm/h). Contrast-enhanced CT of the neck revealed soft-tissue thickening around the distal common carotid artery, the carotid bulb, and the proximal internal carotid artery on the left, with no abnormalities of other supra-aortic vessels or the aortic arch. Doppler ultrasonography showed an isoechoic thickened arterial wall at the left carotid bifurcation, a peak systolic velocity of 90 cm/s, normal intraluminal flow, and “No intimal dissection or aneurysm was seen.” MRI of the neck demonstrated eccentric soft-tissue thickening at the left carotid bifurcation with a hypointense T1 signal, hyperintense T2 signal, no T1 wall hematoma on fat-saturated sequences, and intense perivascular enhancement after contrast administration. Serologic testing was negative, with “negative immunological laboratory tests including anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies (ANCA) and antinuclear antibodies (ANA).” An abdominal MRI showed “no vascular abnormality (no fibromuscular dysplasia, no vasculitis, and no stenosis).”

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 56-year-old Korean woman noted acute onset of fingertip pain, paresthesia, and color changes. Over 6 weeks, she developed digital ulcerations despite hydroxychloroquine. She was subsequently treated with an angiotensin receptor blocker, amlodipine, fluoxetine, topical nitroglycerin, and an oral prostacyclin analogue without improvement. Four months after onset, she reported malaise, fatigue, night sweats, alopecia, and dry eyes. Physical examination confirmed digital ulcerations; vital signs were unremarkable. Laboratory studies included a panel of markers for autoimmune and inflammatory disease, all within normal limits. To evaluate for malignancy, a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was obtained and revealed peritoneal carcinomatosis with omental caking. Serum CA-125 was 862 U/mL; CEA and CA19-9 were normal.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 45-year-old woman with no past medical history presented with 10 days of progressive nausea, slurred speech, and unsteady gait, beginning approximately 2 weeks after her first dose of the Sinopharm COVID-19 vaccine. She denied headache, focal weakness, vision changes, fever, respiratory or chest symptoms, and had no known COVID-19 contacts. Vital signs were normal. On examination, she was alert and oriented; there was dysarthria, left-sided dysmetria on finger–nose testing, impaired rapid alternating movements, horizontal nystagmus to the left, and marked appendicular and truncal ataxia with inability to stand unassisted. Motor strength was 5/5 throughout, and there were no meningeal signs.

Initial laboratory studies, including a complete blood count, thyroid-stimulating hormone level, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, C-reactive protein, urinalysis, and urine drug screen, were all within normal limits. Brain MRI with and without contrast demonstrated bilateral hyperintense lesions in the cerebellar hemispheres, most prominent in the left middle cerebellar peduncle, with faint enhancement; there was no cervical spinal cord involvement. Lumbar puncture revealed cerebrospinal fluid protein of 90 mg/dL, 10 white cells per mm3 with 80% lymphocytes, and negative Gram stain and culture. CSF PCR was positive for SARS-CoV-2 and negative for varicella zoster virus, herpes simplex virus types 1 and 2, cytomegalovirus, and enterovirus. Oligoclonal bands were absent.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 68-year-old man presented 4 weeks after coronary artery bypass grafting to the left anterior descending and posterior descending arteries and bioprosthetic aortic-valve replacement, with a 1-week history of dyspnoea on exertion, orthopnoea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea, and progressive oedema. His medical history included multivessel coronary artery disease, severe aortic stenosis (status post-AVR), paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, hypertension, and hypercholesterolaemia. He had no prior pericardial or myocardial disease, autoimmune conditions, or history of arrhythmia symptoms.

On examination, he was euvolaemic except for pitting oedema extending to the abdomen, jugular venous pressure markedly elevated with a positive Kussmaul’s sign, and reduced breath sounds at both lung bases. Heart sounds were dual without murmurs.

Laboratory studies showed an N-terminal pro–B-type natriuretic peptide level of 3034 ng/L (normal < 126 ng/L). A chest radiograph demonstrated bilateral pleural effusions without pericardial calcification. Electrocardiography showed sinus rhythm without ischaemic changes or low-voltage QRS complexes.

Transthoracic echocardiography revealed normal chamber sizes, preserved left ventricular systolic function, and normal prosthetic valve function without regional wall-motion abnormalities. A bright, thickened pericardium and a small pericardial effusion were noted. The inferior vena cava was dilated without respirophasic collapse. Respirophasic septal motion (“septal bounce”) indicating ventricular interdependence was observed. Mitral inflow velocity varied by > 25% with respiration, and tricuspid inflow velocity varied by > 40%.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 27-year-old gravida 4, para 3 woman was admitted at 32 weeks of gestation with premature rupture of membranes and suspected fetal malformations. Prenatal serologic testing for toxoplasmosis, rubella, syphilis, and HIV was negative. Ultrasonography at 29 weeks showed a reduced left thoracic volume and a large cystic mass occupying the right hemithorax. A cesarean delivery was performed for fetal distress, resulting in a male newborn weighing 2030 g. The infant suffered cardiorespiratory arrest immediately after birth and died at 37 minutes of life. At autopsy, the mediastinum was shifted to the left. The combined weight of the heart and lungs was 38 g. The right lung measured 9 × 7 × 3 cm, was hyperinflated, and contained multiple contiguous cystic spaces up to 7 cm in diameter. The left lung measured 4 × 2 × 1 cm and appeared immature. The heart had a 2 mm atrial septal defect; no other anomalies were identified. Histologic examination of the right lung revealed proliferation of terminal respiratory structures forming cysts lined by ciliated pseudostratified epithelium with polypoid mucosal projections, small cartilage foci in bronchial walls, clusters of goblet cells, and mucinous glands. The left lung showed immature parenchyma with mostly collapsed alveoli and cartilage around the bronchi. The placenta was histologically unremarkable.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 38-year-old woman was brought to the emergency department by her husband because of confusion and a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure at 0300 h. Approximately 30 minutes before arrival she had stiffening of her limbs followed by jerking movements, a tongue bite, and urinary incontinence. On arrival, her capillary blood glucose was 28 mg/dl. She was given 50 ml of 50% dextrose after blood samples were obtained. She had eaten at 2300 h the previous evening. She denied use of any oral or injectable medications. Her mother had type 2 diabetes treated with oral agents, but there was no personal or family history of hypoglycemia.

One week earlier she had recovered from a mild SARS-CoV-2 infection managed at home and had been taking over-the-counter grape seed extract tablets to aid her recovery. She felt well by the next evening, but at 0100 h the following night she had a second episode of loss of consciousness. Her plasma glucose at that time was 34 mg/dl, and she was treated with an intravenous bolus of dextrose followed by a 10% dextrose infusion at 75 ml/h.

Laboratory studies showed normal complete blood count, renal and liver function tests, electrolytes, ECG, chest radiograph, and brain imaging. Critical-sample measurements during the hypoglycemic episodes revealed plasma insulin levels of 324.9 µIU/ml and 412.8 µIU/ml (reference, 2.1–22 µIU/ml), C-peptide levels of 15.2 ng/ml and 14.8 ng/ml (0.8–3.85 ng/ml), cortisol levels of 920 nmol/L and 1021 nmol/L (140–690 nmol/L), and undetectable plasma ketones. A sulfonylurea screen was negative, and insulin autoantibodies were not detected. Abdominal ultrasonography showed grade I fatty liver; evaluation for chronic liver disease was unremarkable.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 2-year-old girl, the third child of healthy nonconsanguineous parents, was born at 38 weeks by elective Caesarean section weighing 3.01 kg. Apart from mild neonatal jaundice, her infancy was uneventful. By age 3 months she tracked at the 99.9th centile for height and weight and exhibited an insatiable appetite. Development was mildly delayed at 18 months but normalized by 2.5 years. For weight management, two separate TSH levels were found to be elevated with normal free T4. At referral, her weight was 20 kg (>99.6th centile), height 93 cm (>99.6th centile), and BMI 23.2 (>99.6th centile). On examination she had a round facies, flat occiput, and central obesity. Serial electrolytes showed normal calcium and phosphate with persistently elevated PTH, and thyroid tests remained abnormal. Mild primary hypothyroidism was suspected and confirmed on a TSH stimulation test.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 12-year-old girl presented with a 2-week history of progressive weakness and generalized edema, which began in the feet and later involved the abdomen, and a 1-week history of progressively worsening dyspnea at rest. There was no fever, headache, rash, or other constitutional symptoms. She had no prior serious illness. 

On examination, she was in severe respiratory distress, markedly pale, with bilateral pitting pedal edema and mild dehydration. Respiratory rate was 40 breaths per minute. Chest examination revealed stony-dull percussion notes in the mid and lower lung zones bilaterally, decreased vocal and tactile fremitus, vesicular breath sounds of reduced intensity, and a few basal crepitations. 

Cardiovascular examination showed a low pulse volume, pulsus paradoxus, raised jugular venous pressure of 11 cm H₂O, tachycardia at 120 bpm, hypotension (60/30 mmHg), an impalpable apex beat, and very distant heart sounds. Abdominal examination revealed tender hepatomegaly 7 cm below the right costal margin and mild ascites. Other systems were normal. 

An initial diagnosis of congestive cardiac failure with bilateral pleural effusions and large pericardial effusion—possibly secondary to viral myocarditis or pericarditis, with tuberculosis to be ruled out—was made, and she was started on anti–heart-failure therapy. A chest radiograph confirmed bilateral pleural effusions and an enlarged cardiac silhouette. Bilateral pleural fluid aspirations yielded clear fluid with chemical analysis suggestive of a transudate. A Mantoux test was negative. Full blood count and film were unremarkable except for a packed cell volume of 23% (hemoglobin, 7.6 g/dL).

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

options.B: Hemorrhagic pericardial effusion from blunt chest trauma

question:
An 87-year-old woman with well-controlled hypertension presented with sudden onset central chest pain that began in the afternoon and was associated with thoracic back pain. At home her daughter recorded a systolic blood pressure of 70 mmHg. On arrival to the emergency department she was alert; blood pressure 75/35 mmHg and heart rate 120 bpm. Chest auscultation was unremarkable, peripheral pulses were intact, and there was no blood pressure difference between arms. ECG showed ST-segment depression in lateral leads. Hemoglobin was 13 g/dL. Chest radiograph revealed a well-circumscribed opacity to the left of the aortic arch. During imaging she became unresponsive and more hypotensive, was intubated, ventilated, and started on inotropes. Computed tomography angiography showed a 7.3 × 6.8 cm mediastinal haematoma in the left anterior mediastinum with active contrast extravasation from the left main pulmonary artery; there was no aortic aneurysm or dissection. Bedside echocardiography demonstrated a large, non-echogenic mass external to the pericardium compressing the left atrium.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 65-year-old man presented with a 1-year history of fatigue. Laboratory studies showed alkaline phosphatase 1,805 IU/L, γ-glutamyl transferase 2,007 IU/L, aspartate aminotransferase 47 IU/L, alanine aminotransferase 81 IU/L, with normal bilirubin, platelet count, albumin, INR, and creatinine. Tests for viral hepatitis, autoimmune liver disease, serum IgG4 (41 mg/dL), and CA19-9 (121 IU/mL) were unremarkable. MRI/MRCP demonstrated subtle multifocal short-segment intrahepatic biliary dilation, mild perihilar duct wall thickening with enhancement, and a normal extrahepatic biliary tree. Abdominal CT revealed bilateral perinephric fat stranding and diffuse large-vessel inflammation. Lower-extremity radiographs showed diffuse sclerotic changes of the long bones. Positron emission tomography/CT showed no abnormal uptake in the vasculature or bone but revealed an FDG-avid left hilar mass; biopsy of that mass disclosed low-grade follicular lymphoma. A tentative diagnosis of primary sclerosing cholangitis was made, and the patient was treated with ursodeoxycholic acid, with modest improvement in pruritus. Over the next 4 months, he was hospitalized three times for suspected cholangitis, with a total bilirubin fluctuating around 2 mg/dL. Sixteen months after symptom onset, he noted dome-shaped skin lesions on his face, hands, and legs and developed worsening jaundice (total bilirubin 10.7 mg/dL). ERCP demonstrated a common hepatic duct stricture with nonfilling of the right intrahepatic duct system; the hilum was dilated and a plastic biliary stent was placed. Biliary brushing and biopsy from the stricture showed benign reactive tissue.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 57-year-old Fitzpatrick III woman with a history of numerous basal cell carcinomas since 2015, cervical polyps, uterine fibroids, Plummer-Vinson syndrome, arthritis, and decreased visual acuity presented with numerous growths on her eyelids for several years. She denied a family history of basal cell carcinoma, although her mother had breast, bladder, and colon cancer and her maternal grandmother had breast cancer. She denied lesions on the genitals, seizures, dizziness, learning difficulties, dental abnormalities, hoarseness, hypotrichosis, hypohidrosis, or macroglossia; her hair turned gray at age 13. On examination, she had numerous 2 to 6 mm pearly papules scattered on the face and bilateral strings of pearly papules along the upper and lower eyelid margins. Dermoscopic examination of the eyelid lesions revealed arborizing vessels, brown-gray globules, and aggregated yellow-white globules in all lesions. Additionally, a dermatofibroma and a plantar verruca were noted. There were no palmar pits, skeletal abnormalities, macrocephaly, or jawline deformities. Four facial papules were biopsied, each showing infundibulocystic basal cell carcinoma with follicular differentiation. A fifth lesion on the forehead demonstrated a collision of a nodular basal cell carcinoma with a blue nevus.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 56-year-old Puerto Rican man with untreated HIV infection and hypertension presented with 3 months of left upper jaw swelling and pain, difficulty swallowing, a 9-kg weight loss, and nonproductive cough. On examination, temperature was 97.8°F, blood pressure 147/95 mm Hg, heart rate 116/min, and respiratory rate 16/min. He had oral thrush, palpable cervical lymph nodes, a mass in the posterior left maxillary region, and a generalized maculopapular rash on the upper chest and back. Laboratory studies showed an HIV viral load of 162 500 copies/mL and a CD4 count of 27 cells/μL. MRI of the brain revealed a 3.9 × 5.8 × 4.3 cm soft-tissue mass in the posterior left maxilla with erosion into the maxillary sinus. Biopsy of this mass demonstrated sheets of granulomatous tissue with macrophages containing well-demarcated fungal spores. Chest CT revealed hilar lymphadenopathy and bilateral adrenal enlargement (left, 4.8 × 3.7 cm; right, 4.7 × 2.3 cm). Laboratory testing showed hyponatremia (serum sodium 126 mEq/L) and mild hyperkalemia (serum potassium 4.9 mEq/L). A 250-μg cosyntropin stimulation test yielded a baseline cortisol of 7 μg/dL, an adrenocorticotrophic hormone level of 86 pg/mL, and a 60-minute cortisol of 9 μg/dL, consistent with primary adrenal insufficiency. Twenty-one-hydroxylase antibody levels were undetectable.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 23-year-old previously healthy Caucasian man presented with a 4-day history of confusion, extreme restlessness, and diffuse cognitive deficits. He developed aggressive self-harming behavior, paranoia, suicidal thoughts, and motor restlessness. He had no history of substance use or psychiatric illness and no recent infection or vaccination. On examination he was afebrile (37.1°C), with normal vital signs. Neurologic exam revealed decreased motor function of the right lower extremity and an ataxic gait; there were no signs of myopathy. Mental status testing showed agnosia, inability to recognize his mother or girlfriend, unreal and irrelevant thoughts, and increased latency when answering. Initial laboratory studies showed normal hematology, electrolytes, infection markers, and thyroid tests (including thyroid antibodies). Cerebrospinal fluid analysis revealed 5 mononuclear leukocytes, lactate 1.6 mmol/L, protein 0.56 g/L, glucose 3.6 mmol/L, normal IgG levels, and negative oligoclonal bands; PCR for herpesviruses and Borrelia was negative, and CMV IgG was negative. CT of the head showed multiple low-density lesions without hemorrhage, ischemia, or mass effect. MRI of the brain demonstrated multiple fulminant contrast-enhancing ovoid lesions—both nodular and ring-enhancing—in both cerebral hemispheres (including the callososeptal interface) and in the left pons and medulla oblongata, raising suspicion for acute demyelination (eg, ADEM or MS), malignancy, or neuroinfection. MRI of the cervicothoracic spine showed no lesions.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man with a history of heavy smoking presented with 10 hours of chest pain, dyspnea, and general malaise. Vital signs were notable for a pulse rate of 110 beats per minute, blood pressure of 110/70 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation of 88% on room air. Cardiovascular examination was unremarkable, but fine rales were heard up to the mid-lung fields. Electrocardiography showed sinus rhythm with a QS pattern and ST-segment elevation in leads V1 through V6, consistent with an acute anterior myocardial infarction. He received 300 mg of clopidogrel, 150 mg of aspirin, subcutaneous enoxaparin, intravenous furosemide, and underwent unsuccessful fibrinolysis with tenecteplase. On transfer to the cardiology ward, he was hemodynamically stable. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 20% with akinesis of the apex and anterior and septal walls, a small pericardial effusion adjacent to the anterior and lateral right ventricle, and a 59 × 32 mm hyperechoic structure at the left ventricular apex. Color-Doppler imaging did not demonstrate any flow within this structure.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 26-year-old woman, a former health professional with a history of treated depression and a prolactin-secreting pituitary macroadenoma managed by transsphenoidal surgery in 2010 (with normalization of prolactin and no glucocorticoid replacement), presented in 2013 with episodic features of Cushing syndrome, including weight gain, new-onset hypertension, and diabetes.  
Laboratory evaluation using chemiluminescence immunoassay showed a 24-hour urinary total cortisol of 12 200 μg/24 h (normal 50–310 μg/24 h) but a normal morning serum cortisol of 12 μg/dL (normal 5–25 μg/dL). Midnight salivary cortisol by ELISA was elevated above the normal range. Plasma ACTH was 14 pg/mL (normal 10–46 pg/mL), and serum DHEA-S was low at 208 ng/mL (normal 988–3400 ng/mL). Abdominal CT revealed normal adrenal glands. A pituitary MRI showed a 1–2 cm cystic lesion in the sella, described as similar to her prior tumor but also possibly an arachnoid cyst. The patient denied any use of exogenous glucocorticoids.  
What is the most likely diagnosis?

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 14-year-old boy was referred after an incidental finding of right-sided hydronephrosis on ultrasonography during a routine health examination. He was asymptomatic with no flank pain, hematuria, or urinary symptoms. His medical history was notable for acute myeloblastic leukemia at age 8, treated per the AML-BFM-93 protocol (including cytarabine, daunorubicin, vincristine, cyclophosphamide, and other agents) with a complete remission achieved 4 years ago. Annual renal ultrasounds since treatment completion had been normal.

On examination, vital signs were stable. There were no abdominal masses or tenderness, and no edema. Laboratory studies showed a normal complete blood count, normal urinalysis, and a serum creatinine of 59 μmol/L. Renal ultrasonography demonstrated marked right hydronephrosis with an anteroposterior pelvic diameter of 50 mm, calyceal dilatation, and thinning of the renal parenchyma; the right ureter was not dilated, and the left kidney appeared normal. Doppler ultrasonography did not reveal a calculus but identified an accessory vessel crossing the pyeloureteral junction. Intravenous urography showed normal excretion on the left and no excretion on the right, even on delayed images. A 99Tc‐DMSA scan revealed no uptake in the right kidney, with preserved function of the left kidney.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old self-employed man presented with a 5-year history of “allergic rhinitis and sinusitis” managed without standard therapy and a 3-year history of an indeterminate lung shadow. Over four years he had recurrent upper abdominal pain, anorexia, and 10 kg weight loss. Laboratory testing showed a marked peripheral eosinophilia (peak 64.5 × 10^9 cells/L, 78.2%). 

Serial gastroscopy and biopsies demonstrated moderate chronic gastritis, focal glandular epithelial atypia, and eosinophil-rich exudate. Pancreatic head biopsy showed bloody effusion with eosinophils and neutrophils. Abdominal CT and PET-CT revealed a bulky pancreatic head (interpreted as pancreatitis) and mild gastric wall thickening (inflammation versus lymphoma). Chest imaging showed multiple patchy and cord-like pulmonary opacities and mildly enlarged mediastinal nodes.

On admission for evaluation of the lung shadow and hypereosinophilia, his oxygen saturation was 90% on room air, and he had a scattered erythematous rash on both lower limbs. A bronchoscopy with right lower lobe biopsy showed abundant eosinophils, histiocytes and lymphocytes in the alveolar interstitium, and eosinophilic infiltration of small vessel walls. Immunohistochemical findings were CD3(+), CD5(–), CD4(–), CD8(individual+), CD56(–), CD20(–), CD30(+), Ki-67 30%+, with EBER(–) and negative fungal and acid-fast stains. 

Based on these findings, the provisional differential diagnosis included eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis, pending characterization of sinus lesions, and eosinophilic gastritis.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 15-year-old previously healthy female presented with 3 weeks of progressive dry cough, intermittent fever to 102°F, malaise, and dyspnea on exertion that left her unable to ambulate. She had received azithromycin without improvement. She denied recent travel, current pet exposures, tobacco, vaping, or drug use. Family history was notable for an aunt with rheumatoid arthritis and uncles with asthma.

On examination, she was tachycardic and tachypneic, afebrile, and normotensive. Oxygen saturation was 80% on room air, and she was unable to speak in full sentences. Cardiac examination was normal. Pulmonary auscultation revealed coarse breath sounds bilaterally without retractions. There were no skin rashes, synovitis, or neurologic deficits.

Laboratory studies showed a normal complete blood count and metabolic panel. C-reactive protein was 65.7 mg/L and erythrocyte sedimentation rate 92 mm/h. Ferritin was 246 ng/mL and interleukin-6 was 18 pg/mL. A respiratory viral and atypical pathogen PCR panel, including two SARS-CoV-2 tests, was negative. Blood cultures and fungal serologies were negative. Autoimmune testing revealed an ANA titer of 1:80 with a nucleolar pattern; other autoimmune and myositis panels were negative. IgE was elevated at 762 kU/L without eosinophilia. Hypersensitivity pneumonitis panel results were negative.

Spirometry demonstrated a severe restrictive defect: FVC 26% predicted, FEV1 25% predicted, TLC 38% predicted, DLCO 31% predicted.

Chest radiography showed bilateral basilar interstitial infiltrates with confluent consolidations. CT of the chest revealed diffuse bilateral consolidations with central predominance and apical sparing; consolidations approached the pleural surfaces without traction bronchiectasis, fibrotic changes, or effusions.  
The initial radiologic differential diagnosis included organizing pneumonia, viral pneumonia with acute lung injury, and connective tissue disease–associated interstitial lung disease.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

options.A: Connective tissue disease-associated interstitial lung disease
options.B: Viral pneumonia with acute lung injury

question:
A 32-year-old Caucasian man with an eight-year history of well-controlled type 1 diabetes mellitus (average blood glucose 150 mg/dL; hemoglobin A₁c 7.5%) presented with a three-month history of an unintended 22-pound weight loss. He denied abdominal pain, diarrhea, changes in diet or exercise, palpitations, heat intolerance, neck soreness, or other thyroid-related symptoms. His diabetes regimen (insulin pump plus pramlintide) and glycemic control were unchanged. 

On examination, he was well-nourished and in no distress. Blood pressure was 130/80 mmHg, pulse 72 beats/minute. There was no lid lag, proptosis, thyroid enlargement, tenderness, cervical lymphadenopathy, or tremor. 

Laboratory studies showed normal complete blood count, electrolytes, liver and kidney function. Thyroid studies revealed a suppressed TSH level of 0.033 mIU/L (reference 0.4–5.0 mIU/L) and a free thyroxine level of 33 pmol/L (reference 7.7–15.4 pmol/L). An anti-thyroid peroxidase antibody was elevated at 13.2 SD (normal <2.0 SD). A thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulin (TSI) assay was undetectable. Serum thyroglobulin was within the normal range.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 57-year-old woman with a history of orthotopic heart transplantation in 2008 for ventricular arrhythmias, complicated by antibody-mediated rejection in 2013, presented in December 2020 with refractory right heart failure. Over the prior four years she experienced progressive volume overload with worsening renal function. In 2018 she underwent tricuspid valve annuloplasty for severe tricuspid regurgitation; postoperatively she developed acute kidney injury, recurrent pleural effusions, and ascites. She progressed to stage 5 chronic kidney disease and underwent deceased-donor kidney transplantation in 2020. On admission she was taking tacrolimus, prednisone, valganciclovir, and high-dose torsemide; mycophenolic acid had been discontinued for leukopenia. NT-proBNP and high-sensitivity troponin were elevated but unchanged from prior measurements.

On examination, jugular venous pressure was elevated with a prominent v-wave, an S3 gallop was present, hepatomegaly with pulsatility was noted, and there was bilateral lower extremity edema. Transthoracic echocardiography showed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 71%, right atrial enlargement greater than left atrial enlargement, and moderate tricuspid regurgitation. Mitral inflow Doppler demonstrated a large E wave without a significant A wave despite sinus rhythm at 65 bpm with a right bundle branch block. Endomyocardial and renal biopsies were negative for rejection, and recent coronary angiography showed no obstructive disease or vessel pruning. Transesophageal and intracardiac echocardiography confirmed only moderate tricuspid regurgitation, and cardiac magnetic resonance imaging demonstrated marked right atrial enlargement with abnormal atrial contraction. Right heart catheterization revealed: right atrial pressure 17 mmHg with blunted x-descent and prominent y-descent without large v-waves; right ventricular pressure 35/3/12 mmHg with a “square-root” sign; pulmonary artery pressure 37/16/24 mmHg; pulmonary capillary wedge pressure 15 mmHg; and a Fick cardiac index of 3.14 L/min/m^2. There was no evidence of ventricular interdependence.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man, a US service member, was referred for chronic neuropsychiatric and vestibular symptoms years after deployment to East Africa. Two weeks after starting mefloquine 250 mg weekly for malaria prophylaxis, he developed anxiety and vivid dreams, progressing over two months to disequilibrium and confusion. He continued mefloquine for six months; four months into deployment he experienced enemy gunfire but reported no change in symptoms after this trauma. On return, he had emotional lability (anger, irritability), difficulty concentrating, anhedonia, short-term memory loss, word-finding difficulty, persistent “sea legs,” and episodic vertigo (4–5 times daily, 20–30 min), relieved by eye closure, without autonomic symptoms. He also had constant “spinning” dizziness, falls, insomnia with vivid dreams and nightmares, and daily headaches without aura or photophobia. He denied significant environmental exposures, illicit drugs, or head trauma; alcohol use occurred during deployment but was later limited.

He was diagnosed with anxiety and PTSD by psychiatry and treated with prazosin, clonazepam, lorazepam, venlafaxine, diazepam, clonidine, and psychotherapy, with partial improvement in mood but persistent vestibular and sleep symptoms. Physical examination showed mild right cervical lymphadenopathy and balance loss on rising and tandem gait; neurologic exam was otherwise normal.

Laboratory studies were notable for negative infectious and heavy metal panels, normal inflammatory markers, and normal endocrine and tumor markers. Neck ultrasound–guided lymph node biopsy showed benign reactive tissue. MRI of the brain and internal auditory canals were unremarkable. Vestibular evaluation demonstrated hyperactive vestibulo-ocular reflex gains and abnormally low phase on rotary chair testing, consistent with a central vestibulopathy.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 77-year-old woman with hypertension, Alzheimer dementia, and adrenal insufficiency was admitted for lethargy and acute kidney injury in the setting of a urinary tract infection with Enterococcus faecalis. On admission, a SARS-CoV-2 PCR test was negative; she was afebrile, and vital signs were normal on room air. She received appropriate antibiotics and intravenous fluids, with restoration of baseline renal function and mental status.

Shortly before planned discharge, a repeat SARS-CoV-2 PCR test was positive. She remained asymptomatic from a respiratory standpoint, and a chest radiograph showed no focal findings. Laboratory studies showed elevated inflammatory markers: C-reactive protein 10.6 mg/L (normal <3 mg/L), ferritin 587 ng/mL (normal <150 ng/mL), D-dimer 1.49 mg/L (normal <0.77 mg/L), and fibrinogen 501 mg/dL (normal 136–464 mg/dL). Electrolytes, complete blood count, prothrombin time, partial thromboplastin time, and international normalized ratio were within normal limits. A cytokine panel (interleukins 2, 4, 5, 8, 12, 13, and 17; interferon γ; tumor necrosis factor α; and interleukin 2R) was unremarkable.

Six days after the positive SARS-CoV-2 test, she developed violaceous patches, some with a retiform configuration, on the dorsal and plantar aspects of both feet and toes. The lesions were nontender, and no other areas of skin were involved.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 35-year-old man presented with fever and loss of consciousness. Five days earlier, he had acute watery diarrhea. Overnight he developed a high fever (105.8 °F) and altered consciousness. He reported recent household exposure to COVID-19; his SARS-CoV-2 PCR was positive on admission. He had no underlying disease, drug allergies, or trauma.

On examination, blood pressure was 100/60 mmHg, pulse 90/min, respirations 18/min, temperature 98.6 °F, and O2 saturation 95%. He opened his eyes to pain and withdrew his limbs (GCS 10/15). Pupils were equal and reactive; gag reflex was intact. Bronchial breath sounds were heard bilaterally; abdominal and other systemic examinations were unremarkable.

Initial labs showed sodium 117 mEq/L, potassium 3.1 mEq/L, BUN 11 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, hemoglobin 13.8 g/dL, WBC 6900/μL, platelets 132 000/μL, INR 1.3. His consciousness normalized as sodium corrected to 137 mEq/L (GCS 13/15).

On hospital day 2, neurologic exam revealed bilateral facial weakness (House-Brackmann grade V), reduced eyelid closure, drooling, and inability to raise eyebrows, puff cheeks, or keep food in his mouth. A nasogastric tube was placed. Other cranial nerves, limb strength, reflexes, sensation, and gait were normal.

Brain CT and MRI without contrast performed one day after onset of facial weakness were normal. Lumbar puncture showed glucose 67 mg/dL, protein 69 mg/dL, and no cells or organisms. Serologic tests for HIV, Epstein–Barr virus, syphilis, HSV, and CMV were negative. Ear–nose–throat examination revealed no neoplasm or local infection. Rheumatological tests (ANA, ANCA, C3, C4) were negative.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 9-year-old Ugandan girl with no significant past medical history presented with an 8-day history of persistent high-grade fever unresponsive to antipyretics and severe abdominal pain localized to the periumbilical and right iliac regions. Five days after symptom onset she also developed profuse, nonbloody diarrhea (7–10 stools/day), vomiting, and malaise. On day 7 she noted a generalized maculopapular, non-pruritic rash without conjunctivitis or mucosal changes. One day before transfer she developed a dry cough and dyspnea.

Two days after initial symptoms, she was treated at an outpatient facility with paracetamol and oral amoxicillin–clavulanate for presumed infection after a CBC showed neutrophilia, but her abdominal pain, fever, diarrhea, and vomiting worsened. An abdominal ultrasound was reported as normal, yet an empiric diagnosis of appendicitis led to a laparoscopic appendectomy; the appendix appeared grossly normal. Postoperatively, she remained lethargic with persistent fevers despite IV fluids, analgesia, antipyretics, and broad-spectrum antibiotics (piperacillin–tazobactam). She developed photophobia. A nasopharyngeal swab tested positive for SARS-CoV-2. Her oxygen saturation fell from 98% to 82% on room air, and she was diagnosed with COVID-19 pneumonia and transferred for further care.

On arrival she was febrile (39.5°C), tachypneic (RR 35/min), tachycardic (PR 70/min), and required 3 L/min supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO₂ 95–97%. Examination disclosed oral thrush, a maculopapular rash, and tenderness in the right iliac fossa and epigastrium without guarding. Neck was supple; Kernig’s sign was negative.

Laboratory studies showed lymphopenia (720 cells/μL; normal 1.0–7.0), elevated C-reactive protein (103 mg/dL; normal <1.6), and ferritin (344 ng/mL; normal 20–250). A chest CT scan demonstrated multifocal peripheral ground-glass opacities and consolidation in both lungs, with basal and apical predominance.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 72‐year‐old man with metastatic castration‐sensitive prostate cancer (PSA, 89.8; Gleason 5 + 5; cT3aN0M1 with multiple bone metastases) was started on apalutamide (240 mg daily) along with a gonadotropin‐releasing hormone antagonist. His past history included transverse colon cancer, chronic glomerulonephritis, and remote cerebral infarction; he had no known drug allergies. Forty-one days after initiating apalutamide, he developed multiple small erythematous patches on his face and trunk without fever. Topical corticosteroids were ineffective, and apalutamide was discontinued 3 days later; an oral antihistamine was started. Nine days after drug discontinuation, he became febrile (38.1 °C) and hypotensive (77/50 mmHg). Examination showed edematous erythema with numerous small pustules spreading over the trunk and limbs, covering > 30% of the body surface; there were no mucosal erosions, and Nikolsky’s sign was negative. Laboratory studies revealed C-reactive protein 3.30 mg/dL, leukocytes 11 610/μL with 8 816/μL neutrophils and 8.5% eosinophils, and normal liver function tests; no atypical lymphocytes were seen. Echocardiography was unremarkable. A drug-induced lymphocyte stimulation test for apalutamide was negative, and no other medications had been introduced. He required ICU admission for hemodynamic support, and all drugs were held. A skin biopsy was obtained.  
What is the most likely diagnosis?

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

options.C: Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms
