A 27-year-old man presented with several months of progressive exertional dyspnoea and lower extremity oedema that began after a motor vehicle accident 5 months earlier. He denied any preceding viral illness, sick contacts, or travel. His initial laboratory studies showed hepatic injury (aspartate aminotransferase 632 IU/L, alanine transferase 248 IU/L), a normal troponin I level of 0.04 ng/mL, and an elevated B-type natriuretic peptide at 1787 pg/mL. A transthoracic echocardiogram demonstrated a normal left ventricular chamber size (end-diastolic diameter 5.6 cm), severely reduced systolic function (ejection fraction <20%), and an apical thrombus. He was treated with intravenous diuretics with symptomatic improvement. 

On transfer, he reported that during the prior accident he had struck his chest on the steering wheel and experienced musculoskeletal chest discomfort that resolved without evaluation. Over the ensuing months he developed orthopnoea, intermittent chest tightness, and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea. Vital signs revealed a heart rate of 109 b.p.m. and blood pressure of 98/60 mm Hg. Physical examination showed jugular venous distension, abdominal ascites, and bilateral lower extremity pitting oedema. A 12-lead electrocardiogram showed sinus tachycardia with low voltage, premature ventricular contractions, and an anterolateral infarct pattern. 

A repeat transthoracic echocardiogram confirmed severe biventricular systolic dysfunction and suggested an apical aneurysm or pseudo-aneurysm with thrombus. Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging demonstrated severe biventricular systolic dysfunction, full-thickness late-gadolinium enhancement of all apical left ventricular segments, apical aneurysm formation, and an associated layering thrombus.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 70-year-old woman presented with a 4-week history of a right-sided neck mass and a sporadic dry cough. The dry cough was unrelated to eating, talking, or movement, occurred in severe fits that sometimes induced vomiting, and was reproducibly triggered by palpation of the neck mass. She also reported intermittent hoarseness but denied dysphagia or velopharyngeal insufficiency. Her medical history was unremarkable, and there was no family history of neuroendocrine tumors.

On examination, there was a 2.5-cm right-sided level II neck mass deep to the sternocleidomastoid muscle. The mass was discrete, mobile, nonpulsatile, and palpation immediately triggered coughing. Flexible fiberoptic laryngoscopy showed normal true vocal–fold appearance and movement bilaterally, with only prominent retropharyngeal carotid arteries noted. The remainder of the examination was unremarkable except for a mildly hoarse voice.

Laboratory studies, including a complete blood panel and 24-hour urinary catecholamines, were within normal limits. 

CT angiography of the neck demonstrated a 2.4 × 2.2 × 2.2-cm heterogeneously enhancing, irregular mass in the right carotid sheath lateral to the proximal internal and external carotid arteries, with minimal arterial splaying and a central area of lower attenuation suggestive of necrosis. 

MRI of the neck revealed a 2.5 × 1.9 × 3.0-cm mass extending from the inferior margin of the right carotid to the level of the piriform sinuses within the carotid sheath that enhanced intensely after contrast administration.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 65-year-old man with hypertension and depression presented in July with 1 week of fevers and malaise. Outpatient evaluation included an unremarkable chest x-ray, a negative nasopharyngeal swab for COVID-19, and an ECG showing sinus rhythm with an incomplete right bundle branch block and left anterior fascicular block. Symptoms persisted, and he was referred from an ambulatory respiratory unit to the ED.  

On arrival: temperature 97.7 °F, heart rate 63 bpm, blood pressure 74/44 mm Hg, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Examination revealed crackles at the right lung base and a II/VI holosystolic murmur at the apex.  

Laboratory studies: AST 89 IU/L, ALT 103 IU/L, proBNP 16 093 pg/mL, troponin T 0.79 ng/mL, CRP > 300 mg/L, creatinine 2.6 mg/dL (baseline 1.1), and lactate 3.4 mmol/L. ECG showed atrial fibrillation, a non-specific intraventricular conduction delay, and minimal ST-segment elevation in the lateral leads.  

He lives in a wooded suburb of Boston and kayaks regularly. The initial differential included severe COVID-19, community-acquired pneumonia with septic shock, bacteremia/endocarditis from recent dental work, tick-borne illness, pulmonary embolism, myocarditis, and acute coronary syndrome.  

In the ED, a CT angiogram excluded pulmonary embolism. Blood cultures grew coagulase-negative staphylococci, deemed contaminants. Urine antigens for Streptococcus pneumoniae and Legionella were negative. A bedside echocardiogram revealed preserved left ventricular ejection fraction, tricuspid regurgitation, and no pericardial effusion. A formal transthoracic echocardiogram showed LVEF 54%, a dilated right ventricle with moderate-to-severe systolic dysfunction (TAPSE 1.3 cm), interventricular septal shift, mild-to-moderate tricuspid regurgitation, and no pericardial effusion. Coronary angiography demonstrated no obstructive epicardial disease. Broad infectious studies, including Lyme and anaplasma serologies, were sent.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 32-year-old gravida 1, para 0 woman at 22 weeks of gestation presented with sudden onset of severe abdominal pain immediately after a bowel movement, followed by lightheadedness. She denied trauma and had an otherwise uncomplicated antenatal course. On arrival she was pale and diaphoretic, blood pressure 77/43 mmHg, heart rate 90–110 bpm, afebrile, and oxygen saturation normal. Uterine tone was normal and there was no vaginal bleeding; the cervix was long and closed. After fluid resuscitation her blood pressure rose to 98/65 mmHg and heart rate to 110 bpm. Abdominal examination revealed distention, generalized tenderness with guarding, and absent bowel sounds. Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL (down from 12.4 g/dL in the first trimester) and a lactate level of 3 mmol/L; coagulation profile and liver and cardiac enzymes were normal. Bedside abdominal ultrasonography demonstrated a live intrauterine fetus and a 95 × 88 × 53 mm echogenic mass in the pouch of Douglas suggestive of clot, with a small amount of free fluid in the perihepatic and perisplenic spaces. An abdominal–pelvic CT scan confirmed hemoperitoneum without an identifiable bleeding source. With worsening tachycardia (150–160 bpm) and pain, the team proceeded to emergency exploratory laparotomy.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 34-year-old woman with no significant medical history presented with 5 weeks of progressively worsening pain localized to the distal left leg. She had no history of trauma. On examination, there was tenderness along the lateral aspect of the distal left leg without palpable swelling, erythema, or other abnormalities. Routine laboratory studies were unremarkable.

Plain radiographs of the left ankle showed a well-demarcated geographic osteolytic lesion in the distal fibular metaphysis, with suspected medial cortical disruption. Unenhanced CT confirmed posteromedial cortical discontinuity with extension into adjacent soft tissues, without periosteal reaction or internal mineralization. A technetium-99m bone scan demonstrated a solitary focus of increased uptake in the distal left leg.

MRI of the lesion revealed a metaphyseal mass with both solid and cystic components, posterior cortical destruction, extraosseous extension, and surrounding inflammatory changes. The lesion was isointense to muscle on T1-weighted images and predominantly hyperintense on fat-suppressed T2-weighted images, with peritumoral edema. After gadolinium administration, there was avid enhancement of the solid nodular components.

These findings raised concern for telangiectatic osteosarcoma; therefore, the patient was referred for tissue sampling.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 52-year-old Arabic man presented with a 3-year history of “annoying sounds from my jaws.” He described a popping sound without clicking or crepitation and denied any associated orofacial pain, parafunctional habits, or headache. Over the same period, he had right-sided tinnitus and progressive sensorineural hearing loss, for which he declined to use a hearing aid. He also reported intermittent headaches worsened by loud noise and dysphagia when drinking liquids. He denied otalgia, ear discharge, dizziness, vertigo, weight loss, night sweats, or fever.

He had been evaluated by two general dentists and diagnosed with temporomandibular joint dysfunction; a night guard and jaw exercises were prescribed without relief. His medical history included hypothyroidism, bronchial asthma, and cervical spondylosis at C4–C5. He had no history of maxillofacial trauma or surgery.

On examination, cranial nerves VII, VIII, IX, and XI were intact. There were no TMJ clicks or crepitus, but slight mandibular deviation to the right on opening and a 65-mm interincisal opening. The right temporalis and masseter were mildly tender; trapezius and sternocleidomastoid muscles were non-tender. Turning his head forward produced an audible bilateral popping, which recurred with yawning. A high-pitched hoarse voice was noted. Intraoral inspection was unremarkable.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 47-year-old woman reported acute left elbow pain after leaning back onto a hard object with her hand while sitting on the ground, applying axial pressure to a hyperextended elbow. She experienced severe pain along the posterior aspect of the elbow and subsequently a sensation of “catching” during flexion and extension. One year after the injury, she was referred for persistent symptoms.

On examination, left elbow extension was limited to 5°, compared with 10° on the right. Full extension of the left elbow elicited posterior tenderness and palpable crepitus. There was no instability of the ulnar collateral ligament or the lateral ulnar collateral ligament.

Magnetic resonance imaging of the left elbow revealed a hypertrophied posterior fat pad interposed between the olecranon and the olecranon fossa. MRI of the contralateral elbow showed a similarly hypertrophied posterior fat pad, although it was positioned less inferiorly. These findings were interpreted as consistent with posterior impingement caused by a hypertrophied posterior fat pad.

Conservative treatment with an anti-inflammatory agent provided no benefit. An intraarticular injection of procaine hydrochloride with dexamethasone yielded 30% pain relief for a few days, after which the pain on full extension returned. Surgical intervention was then considered.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

An 80-year-old man with a 13-year history of immunoglobulin G kappa smoldering myeloma presented with a 2-year history of dusky, erythematous, indurated patches on the lower abdomen and back and a hyperpigmented plaque on the right upper calf. Six years earlier, he had similar lesions initially diagnosed as tumid lupus that partially improved with topical corticosteroids and fully resolved after 9 months of lenalidomide therapy. On examination, indurated dusky patches were noted on the trunk and a firm hyperpigmented plaque on the calf. A biopsy of the right calf showed dermal sclerosis with hyalinized collagen bundles extending into the subcutis and absence of CD34+ dermal dendrocytes in sclerotic zones. A biopsy of the back revealed dermal sclerosis with abundant interstitial mucin confirmed by colloidal iron stain and diminished CD34+ dendrocytes in the sclerotic zone. Laboratory studies confirmed persistent IgG kappa monoclonal gammopathy; all other tests were normal. Whole-body CT showed no evidence of systemic mucinosis. At that time, a morphea/lupus erythematosus overlap syndrome and mixed connective tissue disease were considered in the histopathologic differential diagnosis.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 66-year-old man presented with respiratory difficulties during singing. He had a 45-pack-year smoking history, essential hypertension, and hyperlipidemia, with no known pulmonary, autoimmune, or occupational exposures. Initial chest CT showed mild emphysema and faint interstitial changes; pulmonary-function tests were normal. He was advised to quit smoking but continued, and over 1½ years developed a dry cough, increased serum KL-6, and worsening interstitial changes on CT, while pulmonary-function measurements remained stable.

On admission, fine inspiratory crackles were heard in both lungs. Laboratory studies showed WBC 8420/μl (normal differential), hemoglobin 16.4 g/dl, platelets 214 × 10^3/μl, CRP 0.25 mg/dl, serum KL-6 930 U/ml (normal < 500), ANA positive at 1:40 (homogeneous and speckled pattern), and negative anti-DNA, anti-RNP, anti-SS-A, anti-SS-B, and anti-Scl-70 antibodies. Chest X-ray was unremarkable. HRCT demonstrated mild upper-lobe emphysema and, in the lower lobes, ground-glass opacities with reticular patterns without honeycombing or crazy paving, consistent with an NSIP pattern.

Pulmonary-function testing: VC 3.04 L (90.7% predicted), FVC 2.84 L (86.9%), FEV1 2.30 L (86.8%), FEV1/FVC 81.0%, TLC 4.80 L (93.8%), RV 1.76 L (112%), RV/TLC 36.7%, DLCO 14.64 mL/min/mmHg (88.9%), DLCO/VA 3.73 mL/min/mmHg (84%). Bronchoalveolar lavage (recovery 63/150 ml) showed total cellularity 36 × 10^4 cells/ml with 22% lymphocytes, 2% neutrophils, and 2% eosinophils. Transbronchial biopsy from rS9 demonstrated lymphocytic infiltration without infection but was nondiagnostic.

Video-assisted thoracoscopic biopsy of rS9 revealed irregular interstitial fibrosis with mild chronic inflammation extending from the peripheral to central lobule, patchy subpleural fibrosis reminiscent of UIP but temporally homogeneous, preserved lung architecture without honeycombing, centrilobular emphysema in nonfibrotic areas, and focal intraluminal macrophage accumulation.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 44‐year‐old Hispanic woman with insulin‐dependent diabetes mellitus and hypertension presented with 3 weeks of progressively worsening nonradiating chest pain, fatigue, and exertional dyspnea. She also reported melena, gum bleeding with tooth brushing, nausea, and loss of taste; she denied fevers, chills, hemoptysis, weight loss, neurologic symptoms, or diarrhea. Her mother had myelodysplastic syndrome. 

Vital signs were notable only for a blood pressure of 138/92 mm Hg. On examination, she was obese with conjunctival pallor and multiple ecchymoses on the lower extremities; there was no hepatosplenomegaly and no neurologic deficits. 

Laboratory studies showed:  
• Hemoglobin 5.4 g/dL, hematocrit 16.4%, MCV 116.3 fL, WBC 3.5×10^3/µL, platelets 28,000/µL.  
• Peripheral smear: macrocytes, schistocytes, and teardrop cells; no hypersegmented neutrophils.  
• Troponin I <0.03 ng/mL; total bilirubin 1.0 mg/dL; AST 52 U/L; LDH 1647 IU/L; haptoglobin <30 mg/dL.  
• CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis: no splenomegaly or evidence of neoplastic disease.  
• Coagulation studies: PT 11.2 s, fibrinogen 349 mg/dL, aPTT 24.6 s.  

She received two units of packed red blood cells, was started empirically on prednisone and daily plasmapheresis due to concern for TTP, and had hematology consultation on admission day 1. On hospital day 2, ADAMTS13 activity was 85%, an ANA screen was negative, and flow cytometry showed no PNH clone. The patient had no improvement in blood counts or symptoms after two days of plasmapheresis.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 65-year-old Caucasian man with a 15-year history of hypertension and coronary artery disease (LAD stent placed 4 years earlier) was referred for evaluation of progressive bilateral lower-extremity edema. Three weeks earlier he had been discharged from the emergency department with leg swelling, positive blood, and more than 300 mg of proteinuria on urinalysis. Medications included atenolol, aspirin, irbesartan (300 mg daily), and furosemide.

On examination, his weight was 120 kg; temperature, 36.5 °C; pulse, 52 beats/min; and blood pressure, 150/98 mm Hg. Physical examination was notable for bilateral pitting edema to the thighs.

Laboratory studies revealed a spot urine protein-to-creatinine ratio of 8 g/g and a 24-hour urine protein excretion of 14 g. Urinalysis showed small hemoglobin and 0–4 red cells per high-power field. Serum creatinine was 0.85 mg/dL; C3, 112 mg/dL; C4, 17 mg/dL; uric acid, 6.7 mg/dL; hemoglobin, 13.0 g/dL; platelets, 171 000/µL; hepatitis B and C serologies were negative; total serum protein, 6.5 g/dL; and albumin, 2.0 g/dL. ANA and ANCA were negative. Renal ultrasound was unremarkable, and echocardiography showed an ejection fraction of 60%.

A renal biopsy was performed. Light microscopy of two cores (25 glomeruli, three globally sclerotic) showed diffusely and globally thickened capillary walls with segmental spikes, mild mesangial expansion, no proliferation, crescents, necrosis, or interstitial inflammation, and mild interstitial fibrosis and tubular atrophy. Immunofluorescence on fresh-frozen tissue demonstrated patchy, mild staining for IgG (1+), C3 (1+), albumin (0–1+), kappa and lambda light chains (0–1+); granular capillary loop staining with IgG4 (2+); negative IgG1, IgG2, and IgG3; and negative staining for PLA2R. Electron microscopy revealed extensive subepithelial and focal intramembranous electron-dense deposits with marked foot-process effacement.

A 29-year-old primigravida at 33 weeks’ gestation presented with a 4-day history of suprapubic pain and intermittent left flank pain, plus headache, nausea, and vomiting over the preceding 24 hours. She had no fever, vaginal bleeding, or changes in bowel habits and reported normal fetal movement. Her prenatal course was uncomplicated. She had a remote history of urolithiasis and a urinary tract infection two years earlier. 

On examination she was afebrile with normal vital signs and had mild left flank tenderness without peritoneal signs. A urine dipstick showed moderate leukocyte esterase, 6–10 squamous cells per high-power field, no blood, and no nitrites; urine culture later showed no growth. Her white blood cell count was 12 900 cells/mm3, creatinine was normal, and liver and biliary function tests were normal. Fetal fibronectin was negative, and cardiotocography showed rare contractions every 30 minutes with a reactive fetal heart rate. Betamethasone and a course of magnesium sulfate were administered for fetal lung maturity and neuroprotection, respectively, after which contractions resolved and flank pain improved.

On hospital day 3, she developed acute, colicky left flank pain with nausea and vomiting. Given the negative urine culture, absence of fever, and stable white blood cell count, infectious processes were considered less likely. A renal ultrasound showed mild left pelviectasis not out of proportion to normal pregnancy and small areas of echogenicity in the left kidney without shadowing; no definitive stones were visualized. Repeat urinalysis showed trace blood and trace leukocyte esterase. Gastrointestinal causes were deemed less likely given the location of the pain, good bowel sounds, lack of distention, and normal gastrointestinal and liver function tests. She was managed with intravenous fluids and analgesia, with symptom improvement. 

On hospital day 5, she experienced a recurrent episode of left flank pain, prompting urology consultation and a repeat renal ultrasound, which showed unchanged mild hydronephrosis without discrete stones or abscess. An MRI was obtained for further evaluation.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 61-year-old woman with diabetes mellitus, hypertension, stroke, and hypothyroidism presented with altered mental status, nausea, multiple episodes of nonbloody, nonbilious vomiting, and dizziness. She reported a three-month history of intermittent night sweats, diffuse body aches, and 10 lb of unintentional weight loss. She denied fever, chills, respiratory symptoms, excessive dairy or antacid use, recent travel, or sick contacts. Her medications included vitamin D supplements, last taken three months earlier. 

On admission, vital signs were within normal limits. Physical examination revealed dry oral mucosa and multiple painless, mobile, firm lymph nodes measuring 2–3 cm in the left anterior cervical region, right axilla, and right inguinal area. 

Initial laboratories showed: microcytic anemia, monocytosis, acute kidney injury with a prerenal pattern (BUN/creatinine ratio > 20), hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis, and a serum calcium level of 16.5 mg/dL (corrected to 16.7 mg/dL). She was started on intravenous saline, calcitonin, and bisphosphonates. 

Further evaluation of her hypercalcemia revealed:  
• PTH at the lower limit of normal  
• PTHrP within normal range  
• 25-hydroxyvitamin D at the high end of normal  
• 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D elevated

A 16-year-old male presented to the emergency department alone with an initial complaint of “homelessness.” Shortly after arrival, he collapsed and had a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure that lasted approximately five minutes. He did not meet criteria for status epilepticus. On examination, there was blood in his mouth without any visible trauma, and his oxygen saturation was 72% on room air. He was promptly intubated for airway protection, and approximately 10 mL of blood was suctioned from the endotracheal tube. 

Laboratory studies on admission showed: WBC 8300 K/µL, hemoglobin 13.9 g/dL, platelets 222 K/µL, CRP < 0.5 mg/dL, ESR 9 mm/hr, PT 14.4 sec, PTT 28 sec, INR 1.1. Serum ANA, ANCA, and anti-GBM antibodies were all negative. Blood and sputum cultures remained negative. A transthoracic echocardiogram was normal.

Chest radiography demonstrated diffuse hazy opacities and right upper lobe partial atelectasis. Chest CT revealed bilateral, diffuse ground-glass opacities, more pronounced in the apices with peripheral sparing, and consolidation in the posterior segment of the right upper lobe. Based on these imaging findings, the differential diagnosis included pulmonary edema and diffuse alveolar hemorrhage (DAH).

Additional history revealed that he had been diagnosed with a seizure disorder two years earlier and was taking levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily. Five months ago, he had a similar episode of hemoptysis (5–10 mL) immediately following a seizure that required hospitalization. At that time, CT of the chest showed similar ground-glass opacities, bronchoscopy revealed frank blood in the airways, and bronchoalveolar lavage showed no hemosiderin-laden macrophages. Rheumatologic evaluation was notable only for an ANA titer of 1:160 and a low-positive anticardiolipin IgM (20 MPL), with normal complement levels; this was deemed clinically insignificant given the absence of systemic autoimmune features. On the current admission, repeat ANA testing was normal.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 22-year-old man presented with two weeks of right supraclavicular and prelaryngeal pain aggravated by swallowing, along with sweating and palpitations. He had a preceding upper respiratory tract infection. On examination, the right thyroid lobe was enlarged; the nose, larynx, and pharynx were normal. He admitted to regular anabolic steroid use. Vital signs were normal, and he was afebrile. Laboratory tests showed: TSH 0.04 µU/mL (0.10–4.00), free T4 30.7 pmol/L (9.5–24.0), free T3 8.7 pmol/L (3.0–6.3); thyroid antibodies were negative; thyroglobulin 231 ng/mL (0–30); white blood count 11.42/µL (4.4–11.3); CRP 38.5 mg/L (0–5); liver enzymes normal. Thyroid ultrasonography revealed a markedly enlarged right lobe (2.6 × 2.8 × 5.8 cm) with inhomogeneous reduced echogenicity and decreased vascularity, and a 1.11 cm adjacent lymph node; the left lobe was normal. With thyrotoxicosis, unilateral involvement, and absence of fever, subacute thyroiditis was suspected; treatment with prednisolone 50 mg daily and propranolol was initiated, and the patient reported rapid symptomatic relief by phone.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 16-year-old girl presented with severe nausea, epigastric pain, decreased appetite, and vomiting. Two weeks earlier, she had an acute episode of nausea, non-bilious vomiting, and diarrhea that resolved in 24 hours. During the preceding week, she experienced intermittent, dull, diffuse abdominal pain and decreased appetite; over several days, emesis progressed from non-bilious to bilious. Pain was sharp, localized to the epigastrium, relieved by vomiting, and worsened with oral intake. She denied fevers, respiratory symptoms, hematemesis, hematochezia, or melena.

Further history revealed a 16-month period of 22-kg weight loss through deliberate fat and meat restriction and excessive exercise. She reported eating ~900 kcal/day, running 3.5 miles three times weekly, doing 1.5 hours of yoga daily, and playing soccer. Her menses had ceased over one year ago. She described herself as a perfectionist and enjoyed praise for her thinness but denied body-image fixation.

On examination, her weight was 39 kg (z-score –2.5), height 161 cm (z-score –0.5), BMI 15 (z-score –2.9), and pulse 45–55 beats/min. She appeared cachectic and in mild distress. Abdominal examination showed diffuse tenderness and hypoactive bowel sounds without masses or organomegaly; the remainder of the exam was unremarkable.

Laboratory studies revealed chloride 95 mmol/L and bicarbonate 36 mmol/L, consistent with contraction alkalosis, amylase 141 U/L, and lipase 164 U/L; CBC and liver tests were normal. A barium swallow with small-bowel follow-through demonstrated an extremely dilated stomach and failure of contrast to pass beyond the third portion of the duodenum. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen in the arterial phase showed severe narrowing of the third portion of the duodenum with compression between the superior mesenteric artery and the aorta.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 61-year-old man with hypertension and well-controlled type 2 diabetes presented initially with 10 days of fever, cough, and dyspnea. On admission he was febrile, tachypneic, tachycardic, and required noninvasive ventilation for critical COVID-19 pneumonia confirmed by PCR. He received high-dose dexamethasone, broad-spectrum antibiotics, a single dose of tocilizumab for cytokine release syndrome, and underwent awake proning. He was weaned from noninvasive ventilation after 2 days, continued on supplemental oxygen, and was discharged on day 10; his steroids were tapered thereafter. 

Two weeks later, at follow-up, he reported mild dyspnea on exertion and new left foot weakness. On neurologic examination, bulk and tone were normal throughout. Muscle strength was 4/5 proximally and distally in both lower limbs except for left ankle dorsiflexion, which was 2/5. Ankle and knee reflexes were absent bilaterally, plantar responses were flexor, and the rest of the neurologic examination was normal. The clinical localization favored a peripheral nerve lesion rather than a radiculopathy or Guillain–Barré syndrome.

Nerve conduction studies showed absent left superficial peroneal sensory response with normal right and normal sural responses. Motor studies revealed slowed conduction velocities in both peroneal nerves with absent F waves and normal distal latencies and CMAP amplitudes; tibial nerves had decreased CMAP amplitudes, slow conduction velocities, and absent F waves. The H reflex was absent bilaterally. Needle EMG demonstrated fibrillations and positive sharp waves with reduced recruitment in peroneal-innervated muscles bilaterally (tibialis anterior, extensor digitorum brevis, extensor hallucis longus, and peroneus longus). Upper-extremity studies were normal. The findings were consistent with acute bilateral tibial mononeuropathies at noncompressible sites of moderate severity and acute bilateral common peroneal axonal mononeuropathies proximal to the peroneus longus branch, severe in degree.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 78-year-old woman presented with acute vision loss and headache. Her ocular history was notable for pseudophakia in both eyes, dry macular degeneration, and open-angle glaucoma treated chronically with latanoprost 0.005% nightly in both eyes; three weeks earlier she was started on Simbrinza™ (brimonidine 0.1%/brinzolamide 0.1%) twice daily in both eyes. She also had chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.

On examination, visual acuity was 20/400 in the right eye and 20/200 in the left eye. Pupils were equally reactive without a relative afferent pupillary defect. Intraocular pressures were 24 mm Hg OD and 23 mm Hg OS. Slit-lamp examination revealed quiet anterior chambers that were shallow in the periphery. Indentation gonioscopy showed closed angles in both eyes. Ultrasound biomicroscopy demonstrated iridocorneal apposition with 360° choroidal effusions in both eyes. Manifest refraction revealed a myopic shift of 3 diopters in each eye.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

options.B: Buphthalmos due to primary congenital glaucoma

question:
A 56-year-old previously healthy woman presented with persistent fevers and pleural effusions. Three months earlier, she had surgical repair of a left diaphragmatic rupture sustained in a motor vehicle collision. One month postoperatively, she developed bilateral pleural effusions (left greater than right). Thoracentesis revealed an exudative effusion (protein 3.9 g/dL; LDH 205 U/L; albumin 1.7 g/dL; glucose 90 mg/dL; ADA 10.7 IU/L). Bacterial and mycobacterial cultures were negative, and cytology showed no malignancy. Echocardiography was normal.

Despite broad‐spectrum antibiotics, she developed quotidian fevers of 39–40 °C. Serologic testing for Brucella, EBV, SARS-CoV-2, hepatitis viruses, and HIV was negative. Tumor markers (CEA, CA 19-9, CA 125, CA 15-3) and rheumatologic panels were unrevealing. Endoscopic evaluation showed no malignancy. PET/CT demonstrated hypermetabolic mediastinal and cervical lymphadenopathy, intense FDG uptake in the spleen and bone marrow, and persistent pleural effusions.

On admission five months after surgery, she was alert and hemodynamically stable but continued to spike fevers. Examination revealed decreased breath sounds over the left lung; other systems were normal. Chest CT confirmed bilateral pleural effusions and mediastinal and hilar lymphadenopathy. Abdominal CT showed hepatomegaly and splenomegaly. Empiric meropenem was administered. Blood and urine cultures remained negative.

Laboratory studies showed WBC 16.3 K/μL (92% neutrophils), hemoglobin 6.4 g/dL, platelets 533 K/μL, AST 128 U/L, ALT 50 U/L, CRP 260 mg/L, procalcitonin 1.55 ng/mL, LDH 727 U/L, ferritin 3254 ng/mL, ESR 98 mm/h, D-dimer 4.85 μg/mL, and SARS-CoV-2 PCR negative. Repeat thoracentesis again yielded an exudative effusion with reactive mesothelial cells; cultures were negative. Bone marrow biopsy was normal. Autoimmune serologies, vasculitis panels, and QuantiFERON test were negative. Video-assisted thoracoscopic pleural biopsy showed chronic nonspecific pleuritis.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

An 81‐year‐old man with a history of grade 2 prostate adenocarcinoma diagnosed one year earlier (managed with watchful waiting) presented with five months of progressive dyspnea, orthopnea, peripheral edema, and painful knees and thighs that limited ambulation. He reported a 10-kg weight loss and anorexia over six months but denied chest pain or hemoptysis. His medical history included hypertension, TURP six years prior, and a 50-pack-year smoking history. On exam, blood pressure was 150/80 mmHg with an irregular pulse of 96 beats/min. He had dullness and diminished breath sounds over the left lower lung field consistent with pleural effusion, normal heart sounds, no hepatomegaly, and pitting edema of both hands and lower extremities. The hands were noted to be remarkably large. There was no vertebral tenderness. Laboratory tests showed ESR 35 mm/h, CRP 134 mg/L, hemoglobin 6.3 mmol/L (MCV 82 fl), leukocytes 8.6×10^9/L with 90% neutrophils, normal platelets, electrolytes, and liver enzymes; creatinine 77 μmol/L; alkaline phosphatase 285 U/L; calcium 1.95 mmol/L with albumin 23.9 g/L; normal phosphate; and chronic compensated respiratory acidosis with O₂ saturation 80%. ECG showed atrial fibrillation and LBBB as before. Chest X-ray confirmed a large left pleural effusion and cardiomegaly without pulmonary lesions. Thoracentesis removed 4.5 L of lymphocyte‐predominant fluid with negative cytology, biochemistry, and TB cultures. Echocardiography showed good LV function. Abdominal ultrasound showed a dilated IVC. CTA excluded pulmonary embolism but confirmed the left pleural effusion. Given the elevated alkaline phosphatase, bone pain, and known prostate cancer, a skeletal scintigraphy was obtained. It showed a “super scan,” with diffuse increased uptake throughout the entire skeleton. However, the prostate-specific antigen was within the normal range at 1.4 μg/L (normal <4.4).

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 34-year-old woman presented with recurrent left-sided cervical swelling accompanied by dysphonia and dysphagia. Initial contrast-enhanced CT imaging demonstrated extensive abscess formation, which was drained and treated with intravenous antibiotics. Her history was notable for 12 prior neck interventions dating from age 10, beginning with excision of a presumed second branchial cleft cyst. Despite multiple hospitalizations, repeat CT scans, MRIs, and barium swallow studies showed no persistent fistula or sinus tract. Two exploratory cervicotomies likewise failed to identify residual duct or cyst, and she underwent a total of seven operations under general anesthesia and four further imaging studies, accruing a cumulative radiation dose of 16 mSv. On review at our clinic, the patient reported that each new cervical abscess almost invariably followed an upper respiratory tract infection. Additionally, retrospective pathology of the original specimen revealed only pseudoepithelium rather than true cyst epithelium. Suspecting a branchial cleft anomaly of the third or fourth arch, we elected to perform a repeat barium esophagogram followed by further imaging.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 3-year-old girl presented with a 2-month history of left thigh swelling. The swelling was insidious, progressive, and painful—worse at night and aggravated by movement—and was accompanied by fever, local warmth, weight loss, and anorexia. There was no abdominal pain.  
On radiographs of the left femur, there was an ill-defined expansile lytic lesion of the diaphysis and distal metaphysis with lamellated (“onion-peel”) and focal sunburst periosteal reactions; no fracture was seen.  
Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 7220 cells/µL, hemoglobin 6.8 g/dL, hematocrit 24%, platelets 190,000/µL, C-reactive protein 81.5 mg/L, and lactate dehydrogenase 468 U/L.  
High-resolution ultrasonography of the thigh revealed a vascular soft-tissue mass with spiculated sunburst periosteal reaction.  
A provisional diagnosis of a primary bone tumor was made.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 25-year-old man presented to the emergency department with sudden, severe headache radiating to the right ear and associated nausea. He denied trauma, violent sneezing, cervical massage, or abnormal movements. He had no prior medical or surgical history, and his family history was unremarkable. Vital signs were within normal limits. On physical examination, the right tonsil was displaced toward the midline. A peritonsillar abscess was suspected, and contrast-enhanced CT of the neck was performed. CT showed a hypoattenuating, slightly enhancing mass in the right carotid space, splaying the internal and external carotid arteries, with obliteration of the parapharyngeal space and extension into the nasopharynx and superior retropharyngeal space. MRI of the neck demonstrated an infiltrative lesion within the right carotid sheath extending to the jugular foramen. The lesion was of intermediate signal intensity on T1-weighted images, high signal on T2-weighted images, and exhibited slight homogeneous enhancement. Diffusion-weighted imaging revealed marked restricted diffusion with an apparent diffusion coefficient of 458×10⁻⁶ mm²/s. No cervical lymphadenopathy was identified. The initial differential diagnosis included idiopathic inflammation, such as IgG4-related disease, and lymphoma.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 39-year-old woman presented with a 2-week history of discomfort in both eyes. Fundoscopic examination revealed bilateral papilledema, and neuro-ophthalmologic testing showed mild visual field defects. She had no headaches, diplopia, focal weakness, or sensory disturbances, and her neurologic examination was otherwise normal. She was overweight (BMI 27.2 kg/m2), with an unremarkable medical and family history and no current medications. Lumbar puncture demonstrated an opening pressure of 500 mmH2O with normal cerebrospinal fluid composition. Noncontrast CT of the head showed no space-occupying lesions or hydrocephalus, only an incidental arachnoid cyst in the left middle fossa. MR imaging revealed a 2.5 cm lesion in the right sigmoid sinus that was isointense on T1- and T2-weighted images and showed homogeneous enhancement after gadolinium administration. CT venography then demonstrated subtotal occlusion of the dominant right sigmoid sinus caused by a well-defined, homogeneous, hypodense mass.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 44-year-old man was referred for evaluation of several weeks of constipation without abdominal pain or fever. He had a history of similar symptoms 10 years earlier with a normal colonoscopy at that time. His only medication was for hypertension. On examination, a hard, palpable mass with local warmth was noted in the lower left to mid‐abdomen. The white blood cell count was 6500/μL and C‐reactive protein was 2.7 mg/dL. Colonoscopy from another hospital showed significant edema of the sigmoid colon mucosa and narrowing of the lumen, preventing passage of the scope. Initial contrast‐enhanced CT demonstrated edematous thickening of the bowel wall from the splenic flexure to the rectum, a thin, cord‐like inferior mesenteric vein, and ectatic mesenteric venous branches, while the inferior mesenteric artery was patent. There were no arterial or venous thrombi, arteriosclerotic changes, or calcifications. Clinically, inflammatory bowel disease and eosinophilic enteritis were suspected, but an endoscopic biopsy showed only mildly inflamed mucosa without significant eosinophilic infiltration. The patient was managed conservatively. One month later, he developed lower abdominal pain for 1 week and self‐medicated with NSAIDs. On examination he had left lower‐quadrant tenderness with guarding and a temperature of 38.0°C. The white blood cell count rose to 18,600/μL and CRP to 20.5 mg/dL. CT angiography was performed to evaluate suspected vascular abnormalities.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

options.A: Idiopathic myointimal hyperplasia of mesenteric veins

question:
A 63-year-old man presented with a 15-year history of various “foreign bodies” emanating from the superior fornices of both eyes, associated with blurred vision, burning, dryness, redness, and eye pain. His symptoms began in 2004 after colliding with a flowering “trumpet” plant; he reported flushing out visible plant and insect material with saline but continued to perceive persistent debris in a “permanent laceration” behind his eyes. In the days following the injury, he experienced insomnia, malaise, itching, eye pain, and, in a delirious state, rubbed his face against the floor. Over the years, he amassed a collection of self-extracted debris, including plant parts, acrylic fibers, cat hair, thread, metal filaments, and insects, retrieved through a ritual of filling and draining his kitchen sink and using a mascara brush to capture sediment.

He sought evaluation by multiple board-certified ophthalmologists and oculoplastic surgeons; records showed repeated eversion of lids and sweeping of the fornices with no evidence of retained foreign bodies. An orbital plain radiograph (March 2019) and an MRI of the orbits (June 2019) were both within normal limits.

In May 2019, corrected visual acuity was 20/20 OD and 20/30 OS. External examination revealed floppy eyelid syndrome. Slit-lamp examination demonstrated trace conjunctival injection with corneal punctate staining bilaterally; no ophthalmic masses, lesions, or ulcers were seen, and pupils, motility, iris, and lenses were unremarkable.

On presentation to our clinic in February 2023, he reported a decrease in the volume of material and ocular symptoms. Examination with eyelid eversion again showed no lesions or foreign bodies. The patient was well-groomed and cooperative, with fluent speech, full orientation, intact memory, abstract thinking, insight, and judgment, and he denied suicidal ideation.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 30-year-old G1P1 woman presented with a vaginal mass first noted at the time of a vacuum-assisted vaginal delivery 4 years earlier. She reported no change in size until 1 month of worsening watery yellow–pink discharge, occasionally blood-tinged, and entry and deep dyspareunia. She denied bowel or urinary symptoms, fevers, weight loss, or systemic complaints. On examination, there was copious serosanguinous fluid in the vaginal vault. A well-circumscribed, smooth, cystic structure approximately 4 cm in diameter was palpated along the posterior vaginal wall, with a 0.5×1.0 cm exophytic lesion overlying it, draining serosanguinous fluid. On bimanual and rectal examination, the mass was separate from the cervix and located in the rectovaginal septum; there was no rectal invasion. Biopsy specimens showed fibrous tissue with acute and chronic inflammation and squamous debris, without a definitive diagnosis. Pelvic ultrasonography revealed a complex vaginal mass inseparable from the cervix, measuring 5.1×3.8×5.4 cm, with minimal peripheral vascularity on color Doppler. MRI demonstrated a 4.7×4.8×4.9 cm mass arising from the posterior vaginal wall, separate from the cervix, heterogeneously hyperintense on T2-weighted images, hypointense on T1-weighted images, and on postcontrast sequences showing wall enhancement, absent internal enhancement superiorly, and bulky, nodular hyperenhancement inferiorly, consistent with a complex cystic mass.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presented three weeks after an insect bite to her left thigh that evolved into a deep ulcer requiring surgical debridement and antibiotics. Following treatment, she remained listless and reported bilateral proximal muscle weakness in her upper and lower limbs, with several prior exertional episodes of similar weakness.

On examination, blood pressure was 110/70 mmHg and heart rate 80 beats/min; she was normotensive. Renal function was normal (serum urea 13 mg/dL; creatinine 0.72 mg/dL). Laboratory testing revealed serum potassium 1.31 mmol/L, metabolic alkalosis on arterial blood gas analysis, hypomagnesemia, and hypocalcemia. Plasma renin activity and aldosterone levels were modestly elevated.

By history, surreptitious diuretic use, diarrhea, vomiting, and a recent hypercapnic state were excluded. Spot urine studies showed chloride excretion >20 mEq/day and potassium excretion >30 mEq/day. There was no family history of Bartter’s syndrome. Urine calcium excretion was low. Abdominal ultrasonography showed no nephrocalcinosis. Electrocardiography and stress echocardiography were normal.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 63-year-old woman presented with a 7-year history of progressive limb weakness and muscle cramps. Five years ago she had been diagnosed with possible amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) after developing intermittent cramp-like chest sensations, numb neck pain, and then objective weakness, atrophy, and fasciculations of the upper limbs with extremely brisk tendon reflexes. No sensory deficits had been documented initially. Electromyography at that time showed abnormal spontaneous activity in multiple upper-limb muscles and signs of chronic denervation; a cranial MRI was normal. A bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome was also presumed based on increased distal motor latencies of the median nerve.

On current examination, cranial nerves were intact with no bulbar signs. The arms showed distinct proximal atrophy and mild fasciculations in the supraspinatus, deltoid, biceps, and finger extensors, with motor strength 3–4/5. The legs had mild weakness (4/5 in foot dorsiflexion) and gait ataxia. Vibration sense was reduced in all limbs, and there was hyper- and dysesthesia over the shoulders and proximal arms. Deep tendon reflexes were symmetric and extremely brisk; plantar responses were flexor. Abdominal reflexes were present. There was no bladder or bowel dysfunction, but she reported persistent neck pain, numbness, and clumsiness of both arms. Proximal arm atrophy in combination with sensory dysfunction and pain prompted reconsideration of spinal pathology.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 62-year-old man with a 20-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension presented with three months of progressively worsening dyspnea. He had been treated elsewhere for presumed congestive heart failure with diuretics but had no clinical improvement. He denied chest pain, cough, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, back pain, fever, weight loss, bladder dysfunction, or sensory disturbances. He reported progressive difficulty walking and leg weakness. He was a nonsmoker, drank alcohol occasionally, and had no recent viral symptoms.

On examination, blood pressure was 161/106 mm Hg. Neurologic findings included symmetric proximal and distal muscle weakness (4/5 in the upper extremities, 3/5 in the lower extremities), decreased distal pinprick sensation, and hyporeflexia (1+ in all limbs). Cranial nerves were intact; there was no muscle atrophy or fasciculations. A fasting glucose level was 229 mg/dL. Arterial blood gas on room air revealed pO2 60 mm Hg, pCO2 58 mm Hg, and SaO2 89%, consistent with chronic respiratory insufficiency.

A chest radiograph showed clear lung fields without evidence of fluid overload. Serum electrolytes and thyroid function tests were normal. B-type natriuretic peptide level and two-dimensional echocardiogram findings were inconsistent with heart failure. Despite achieving adequate glycemic control with insulin, repeat arterial blood gases remained unchanged on hospital day 1.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 74-year-old man presented with progressive exertional dyspnea and orthopnea over 24 hours. He was afebrile (37.4°C), hemodynamically stable (heart rate 81 beats/min, blood pressure 121/58 mmHg) with a wide pulse pressure (63 mmHg), and tachypneic (22 breaths/min) without hypoxia (oxygen saturation 96%). Examination revealed central congestion, a grade 3 diastolic decrescendo murmur at the left upper sternal border, and warm extremities without edema. He had undergone transfemoral transcatheter aortic valve replacement 3 days earlier with a 34 mm self-expanding bioprosthesis and was discharged the next day without complications.

His medical history included mesenteric low-grade follicular B-cell lymphoma, nonobstructive coronary artery disease, and severe aortic stenosis with a left ventricular ejection fraction of 20–25%. 

Electrocardiography showed sinus rhythm without new ischemic changes. High-sensitive troponin levels were elevated (399 → 362 → 420 ng/L; normal <22 ng/L). Laboratory tests showed no anemia or leukocytosis, and a pro-BNP of 10,407 pg/mL. Chest radiography and CT scan demonstrated a moderate right pleural effusion and pulmonary congestion, with no evidence of pulmonary embolism, aortic dissection, or pericardial effusion.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 41-year-old man presented in May 2013 with a 20 × 18 cm area of cutaneous necrosis on the left thigh. He noted a 4 × 5 cm nodule at the same site in April without redness, pruritus, or pain, which progressed to necrosis over two weeks. He had no history of medication use, trauma, fever, or family history of thrombophilia. On admission, he reported mild right-sided chest pain and cough productive of bloody sputum but denied fever or dyspnea. Laboratory studies showed leukocytosis (36.59 × 10^9/L) with hypereosinophilia (21.15 × 10^9/L; 57.8%), thrombocytopenia (19 × 10^9/L), elevated liver enzymes (ALT 178 IU/L; AST 68 IU/L; GGT 329 IU/L), mildly increased procalcitonin (0.51 ng/mL), elevated fibrinogen (4.15 g/L), and a markedly increased D-dimer (82.20 mg/L FEU). The workup for allergic, parasitic, rheumatologic, and immunologic diseases was unremarkable. Chest CT revealed bilateral patchy pulmonary opacities and a small pleural effusion; abdominal ultrasound was normal. Skin biopsy of the necrotic lesion showed dermal necrosis and hemorrhage with perivascular lymphocytes, histiocytes, and eosinophils. Bone marrow aspiration demonstrated eosinophils comprising 27.5% of leukocytes.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 69-year-old man with advanced non–small-cell lung cancer and pleural metastasis had been treated with carboplatin, pemetrexed, and pembrolizumab for nine months, followed by pembrolizumab alone for four months, after which therapy was suspended because of good disease control. Three months after discontinuation of pembrolizumab, he remained asymptomatic without abdominal or back pain. 

Contrast-enhanced CT showed localized enlargement of the pancreatic head with delayed enhancement compared to baseline, and hypoattenuating areas in the pancreatic body. Laboratory studies revealed normal pancreatic amylase (50 IU/L), lipase (50 IU/L), and C-reactive protein (0.05 mg/dL), but an elevated carbohydrate antigen 19-9 level (148 U/mL); serum IgG4 was 22 mg/dL (normal). 

MRI demonstrated enlargement of the pancreatic head with a hypointense T1 signal, hyperintense T2 signal, and marked diffusion restriction. MR cholangiopancreatography revealed multiple stenoses of the main pancreatic duct and stenosis of the intrapancreatic bile duct. Endoscopic ultrasonography showed hypoechoic pancreatic parenchyma with scattered hyperechoic foci and stranding in the enlarged head. ERCP confirmed focal narrowing of the main pancreatic duct at the head, irregular ductal caliber at the tail, and intrapancreatic bile duct stenosis. 

Pancreatic juice cytology, bile cytology, and brushing cytology of the bile duct stricture were all negative. Based on the history, pembrolizumab-induced pancreatitis was considered highly probable, but pancreatic tumor and mass-forming pancreatitis, including autoimmune pancreatitis, could not be ruled out. Therefore, endoscopic ultrasound–guided fine-needle aspiration was performed for histopathological diagnosis.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 25-year-old woman presented with a 2-month history of worsening generalized abdominal pain and distension, accompanied by a 17-kg weight loss over 5 months. She reported no fever, cough, hemoptysis, or night sweats and had no prior history of tuberculosis or known TB contacts. An ultrasound at an outside hospital suggested ovarian malignancy with septations and irregularities.

On examination, vital signs were normal. The chest was clear; there was moderate ascites without peripheral edema. Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 8.1 g/dL (low MCV), erythrocyte sedimentation rate 95 mm/h, normal AST and ALT, and reduced serum albumin. A CA-125 level drawn 7 days later was 792.1 U/mL.

Two days after admission, 540 mL of serous ascitic fluid was removed. Analysis revealed a lymphocyte-predominant exudate (300 cells/mm3; SAAG 0.98 g/dL), with no organisms on culture or Gram stain, negative cytology for malignant cells, and a negative Gene Xpert test for Mycobacterium tuberculosis. The ascitic fluid adenosine deaminase level was 54.6 IU/L.

An initial chest radiograph showed clear lung fields with minimal left pleural effusion. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen demonstrated diffuse peritoneal thickening, diffuse small-bowel wall thickening, multiple non-specific retroperitoneal lymph nodes (average 1.2 cm), minimal pelvic and perihepatic fluid, and otherwise unremarkable solid organs.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

An 87-year-old woman was referred for progressive dyspnea. Three days earlier she had presented with high-grade fever, dyspnea, and hematuria and received levofloxacin without improvement. Her history included hypertension, diabetes, dementia, and deep vein thrombosis; medications included candesartan, teneligliptin, donepezil, digoxin, and apixaban. On examination, blood pressure was 92/66 mmHg, heart rate 92 beats/min, respiratory rate 24 breaths/min, temperature 36.7 °C, and oxygen saturation 97 % on room air. Arterial blood gas analysis showed pH 7.454, PaO2 73.7 mmHg, PaCO2 28.8 mmHg, and bicarbonate 19.7 mmol/L. Cardiac examination revealed a grade 4/6 pansystolic murmur at the apex; the abdomen had mild right-upper-quadrant tenderness. ECG showed sinus tachycardia with ST-segment elevations in leads V1–4, I, and aVL. Chest radiography was unremarkable. Laboratory tests revealed WBC 18,300/µL (78 % neutrophils), C-reactive protein 18.05 mg/dL, BNP 224 pg/mL, and troponin T 0.54 ng/mL; liver and kidney function and urinalysis were normal. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated akinesis of the apical mid-segments of the left ventricle. Given the suspicion for ST-elevation acute coronary syndrome, emergency coronary angiography was performed. 
Coronary angiography detected unremarkable findings except for 90 % focal stenosis of the mid-portion of the right coronary artery with Thrombolysis in Myocardial Infarction flow grade 2.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 43-year-old man noticed a slowly enlarging, firm mass in his right upper thigh over four months. He denied systemic symptoms such as flushing, diarrhea, or wheezing. Physical examination revealed a 3×3 cm firm mass involving the right sartorius muscle. The mass was excised en bloc.

Histopathology showed skeletal muscle and subcutaneous tissue infiltrated by polygonal cells arranged in packets separated by fine fibrovascular stroma. Immunohistochemical stains demonstrated strong, diffuse positivity for synaptophysin, chromogranin, cytokeratin, Bcl-2 protein, and neuron-specific enolase, and negativity for S100, desmin, and epithelial membrane antigen. Electron microscopy revealed polygonal cells with deeply indented nuclei and abundant neurosecretory granules in the cytoplasm. A 24-hour urinary 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid level was elevated at 92 nmol (reference range 9–31 nmol/24 h). Metaiodobenzylguanidine and octreotide scans showed no abnormal uptake outside the surgical site, and contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was unremarkable.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A female neonate was delivered at 37 weeks’ gestation via planned cesarean section because of maternal gestational diabetes, intrauterine growth restriction, polyhydramnios, and a fetal echocardiogram that showed a hypoplastic left atrium and mildly dilated right atrium. Admission to the neonatal intensive care unit had been arranged for cardiac evaluation.

At birth, her APGAR scores were 8 and 9 at 1 and 5 minutes. She had moderate respiratory distress with subcostal retractions and was placed on noninvasive positive-pressure ventilation. Vital signs included a temperature of 36.9°C, respiratory rate of 57 breaths per minute, heart rate of 139 beats per minute, and an oxygen saturation of 99–100% on a fraction of inspired oxygen of 0.30. On examination, she had craniotabes, a bell-shaped chest, mildly decreased muscle tone, and mild bowing of the bilateral femurs. A continuous machinery-type heart murmur was audible; pulses were normal. The remainder of the abdominal and skin examinations was unremarkable.

Echocardiography revealed a large patent ductus arteriosus and elevated right ventricular systolic pressure but normal cardiac structure. Chest radiography showed generalized demineralization of the bones and a bell-shaped rib cage. A skeletal survey demonstrated generalized osteopenia, metaphyseal lucencies, prominence of soft tissue over the parietal scalp, and mild bowing of the proximal humeri suggestive of healing fractures.

At 24 hours of life, laboratory studies were notable for a serum calcium concentration of 14.3 mg/dL (reference range, 8.4–10.6), ionized calcium of 2.32 mmol/L (reference range, 1.0–1.5), 25-hydroxyvitamin D level of 54.2 ng/mL (reference range, 31–80), and a parathyroid hormone level of 2116 pg/mL (reference range, 15–65). The infant was treated with intravenous fluids at 1.5 times maintenance, furosemide, and calcitonin, and transfer to a tertiary center was arranged for further evaluation.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 37-year-old Indonesian man presented with a 1-year history of exertional dyspnea.  
He had been diagnosed with bronchiectasis in 2008 based on chronic productive cough and chest radiography. He denied childhood severe respiratory infections, was a never-smoker, had no environmental tobacco or biomass exposure, and worked as an engineer without dust or fume exposures. He had no cough, hemoptysis, chest pain, wheezing, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, limb swelling, fevers, night sweats, anorexia, weight loss, skin, or joint complaints.  
Vital signs were normal. Examination showed a centrally located trachea and reduced breath sounds with dullness over the left upper chest; no clubbing.  
Chest radiograph revealed increased lucency in the upper half of the left hemithorax. Chest CT showed bronchiectatic changes in the left lower lobe, a few small cysts, and several bullae occupying almost the entire left hemithorax.  
Extensive blood tests, including immunoglobulins, connective tissue disease screen, and α₁-antitrypsin level, were unremarkable.  
Pulmonary function tests: FEV₁/FVC 0.83, FVC 2.56 L (59% predicted), FEV₁ 2.13 L (59% predicted); TLC by nitrogen washout 3.43 L (56% predicted) and by plethysmography 5.33 L (88% predicted); residual volume 2.65 L (156% predicted); DLCO 77% predicted.  
Bronchoscopy was grossly normal; malacia was seen in a segment of the left upper lobe; BAL cultures were negative; endobronchial biopsies showed acute inflammation without granulomas or malignancy.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 19-year-old woman presented after an accidental ground-level fall with direct head trauma. She noted a tender, unilateral soft-tissue swelling over the left temporoparietal region extending into the periorbital area, causing facial asymmetry and compromised opening of the left eye. On examination, she was alert, hemodynamically stable, and neurologically intact; facial sensation and motor function were preserved, though her chin deviated leftward with some dental malocclusion. Brain CT showed no intracranial injury but noted deformation of the left zygomatic arch. Further history revealed excision of a tongue neurofibroma in childhood and multiple café-au-lait spots; physical exam confirmed NF1 with a congenital lateral neck mass. Facial bone CT and orthopantomogram demonstrated left mandibular hypoplasia with osseous defects, a thinned zygomatic arch, and an enlarged mandibular canal and mental foramen without signs of infection. CT angiography excluded ruptured aneurysms but revealed a 75 × 45 × 97 mm ill-defined lobulated lesion in the left suprahyoid neck, infiltrating multiple neck spaces and encasing the jugular vein and internal carotid artery. Over the next 6 hours, the facial swelling rapidly increased in size, and the overlying skin became tense with punctate hemorrhagic spots.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 2.5-year-old boy with cystic fibrosis complicated by pancreatic insufficiency, bilateral bronchiectasis, and chronic Pseudomonas aeruginosa colonization was admitted with a 2-day history of fever and cough. He was started on intravenous piperacillin-tazobactam (100 mg/kg/day in three divided doses) and amikacin (30 mg/kg/day once daily) based on prior sputum cultures; there was no prior history of drug reactions. Home airway clearance, pancreatic enzyme replacement, and nutritional support were continued.  

Over the first 17 days, his respiratory symptoms gradually improved. On hospital day 18, he developed a high-grade fever to 39 °C. On day 19, he developed a diffuse, pruritic, erythematous maculopapular rash that began on the face and trunk and rapidly spread over his entire body; facial edema was noted. Physical examination revealed no lymphadenopathy, no mucous membrane involvement, and no organomegaly.  

Laboratory studies showed:  
• WBC 4.1 × 10^9/L with ANC 0.9 × 10^9/L and platelets 92 × 10^9/L  
• Eosinophils 1.8 × 10^9/L  
• C-reactive protein 117 mg/dL  
• AST 178 IU/L, ALT 686 IU/L (> 12× upper limit of normal)  
• Normal BUN, creatinine, and electrolytes  

Blood cultures were negative. Serum PCR was positive for Epstein–Barr virus and cytomegalovirus but negative for human herpesvirus-6, Chlamydia, and Mycoplasma pneumoniae. A respiratory viral PCR panel was negative for adenovirus, human metapneumovirus, influenza A/B, MERS-coronavirus, and parainfluenza viruses.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50‐year‐old woman on maintenance hemodialysis for end‐stage renal disease (initiated in 1987) presented during routine dialysis with several months of pruritus and productive cough. Her history was notable for hypertension and prior parathyroidectomy for hyperparathyroidism; she remained dialysis‐dependent. 

On examination, vital signs were stable. Skin turgor was normal without rash or ecchymoses. There was no jugular venous distention, lymphadenopathy, or palpable neck masses. Lung auscultation revealed bilateral rales without wheezing.

Laboratory studies showed normal blood counts, creatinine 8.4 mg/dL (0.5–0.9), parathyroid hormone 1891 pg/mL (10–69), and calcium 10.3 mg/dL (8.6–10.2). Thyroid‐function tests (TSH, free T4) and thyroglobulin were within normal limits.

A chest radiograph revealed bilateral pulmonary opacities. Contrast‐enhanced CT of the chest demonstrated multiple well‐defined nodules in both lungs, the largest measuring 1.35 cm. A 99mTc‐MIBI scan showed dominant uptake in the pulmonary lesions. Neck ultrasound identified a multinodular goiter but no discrete parathyroid glands, and CT of the neck failed to localize any parathyroid tissue.

What is your differential diagnosis?

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 53-year-old man presented with facial swelling. A chest radiograph showed a large mass projecting over the right lung field. CT of the chest demonstrated an enormous anterior mediastinal mass contiguous with the posterior sternum and right ribs, occluding the superior vena cava and compressing the heart. Routine blood tests, tumor markers, and acetylcholine receptor antibodies were all normal or negative. Bronchoscopic biopsy revealed features of a carcinoid tumor. 

He underwent en bloc excision of the mass, including resection of the superior vena cava with graft insertion, resection of the right phrenic nerve, and diaphragm plication. Histologic examination identified an atypical carcinoid of the thymus (intermediate-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma), with invasion into fat, pleura, lung lymphatics, and metastases in a hilar node and a cervical thymic node.

One month postoperatively, chest CT showed poststernotomy changes, a right basal fluid-filled cyst, bilateral atelectasis, and pleural thickening. An I-123 MIBG scan was negative for residual or recurrent disease. One year later, follow-up CT revealed no mediastinal recurrence, persistent linear atelectasis in both lower lobes, and elevated right hemidiaphragm. A bone scan showed increased uptake in the posterior arch of the right eighth rib. 

To further evaluate his status, a somatostatin receptor scintigraphy with In-111-DTPA-D-Phe^1-pentetreotide was performed. The scan demonstrated an area of abnormally increased radiotracer concentration in the mediastinum, raising the question of residual tumor, recurrence, or an inflammatory process.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 70-year-old Caucasian man with a history of essential hypertension, peripheral arterial disease, prior non–ST-elevation myocardial infarction with 3-vessel CABG, bioprosthetic aortic valve replacement, and metastatic stage IV lung adenocarcinoma treated with pembrolizumab presented with increased fatigue, weight gain, weakness, and severe hypotension. On arrival, systolic blood pressure ranged from 50 to 70 mm Hg. His baseline serum creatinine was 1.0–1.2 mg/dL; on presentation it was 7.2 mg/dL, with blood urea nitrogen of 78 mg/dL and bicarbonate of 21 mmol/L. Troponin was 2.8 ng/mL. Sodium and potassium were within normal limits. Platelet counts, historically 30,000–100,000/µL, were 77,000/µL on this admission.

Transthoracic echocardiography showed a drop in ejection fraction from 55–60 % to 35 %, without regional wall-motion abnormalities or pericardial effusion. Medication review revealed no new nephrotoxins; losartan was held. The patient denied NSAID or herbal use and had no recent contrast exposure. Renal ultrasound demonstrated no hydronephrosis; a Foley catheter was placed. Creatine phosphokinase was 277 U/L. Urinalysis showed 1+ blood, 1+ protein, 0–5 red blood cells per high-power field, and 0–5 white blood cells per high-power field; the urine protein-to-creatinine ratio was 0.06 g/g. Peripheral smear revealed no schistocytes, and bilirubin levels were normal. Hemoglobin was 10.2 g/dL, white blood cell count 5,100/µL. A fractional excretion of urea was calculated at 46 %.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 50-year-old woman presented with a left breast mass of 1 year’s duration. She first noticed a 1.5 cm firm lump in the upper quadrant of her left breast without pain, skin changes, or nipple discharge. The mass had grown slowly. She had no relevant family history and no systemic symptoms. On examination, there was asymmetry of the breasts with a firm, ill-defined 3.5 cm × 3 cm irregular mass at the 12 o’clock position, 4 cm from the nipple, fixed to deep tissues. A small amount of cloudy, light-yellow discharge was expressed from a single duct on the left; no axillary lymphadenopathy was present. 

Diagnostic imaging included:
- Mammography: partially obscured high-density mass measuring 2.0 cm × 1.7 cm with indistinct margins.  
- Ultrasound: heterogeneous hypoechoic solid mass, 30 mm × 13 mm × 20 mm, indistinct borders, with internal blood flow (BI-RADS 4C).  
- Contrast-enhanced breast MRI: oval, spiculated, heterogeneously enhancing mass (2.3 × 2.2 × 2.8 cm), isointense on T1-weighted imaging, hyperintense on T2-weighted imaging and diffusion-weighted imaging, with restricted diffusion on the ADC map. The dynamic enhancement curve showed rapid initial uptake followed by a persistent enhancement pattern (type I time-intensity curve).

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 55-year-old African American woman with a history of seropositive rheumatoid arthritis and Rai stage 1 CLL, diagnosed 8 years earlier on routine evaluation, presented with progressive lymphadenopathy, intermittent fevers, a 5-lb weight loss, and worsening respiratory status requiring airway management. Initial CLL had been managed with observation; rheumatoid arthritis therapies had included hydroxychloroquine, methotrexate, prednisone, and rituximab infusions. 

On CT imaging, there was a soft-tissue mass in the nasopharynx representing enlarged adenoidal tissue, diffuse palatine and lingual tonsillar hypertrophy with airway narrowing, and bulky cervical, supraclavicular, and axillary lymphadenopathy. PET/CT showed intense FDG uptake in the nasopharyngeal mass (SUVmax 18.7), level IIB cervical chain nodes (SUVmax 20.7), and bulky FDG-avid lymph nodes in the axillary, periaortic/retroperitoneal, and iliac regions. 

Complete blood count revealed leukocytosis (WBC 40.7 × 10^3/mm^3; 24% neutrophils, 75% lymphocytes), mild hypochromic-macrocytic anemia (Hb 11.8 g/dL; MCV 97.6 fL), and normal platelet count. The peripheral smear showed numerous atypical lymphocytes consistent with persistent CLL. Serologic and viral studies were negative, including HIV, hepatitis A/B/C, COVID-19, influenza A/B, RSV, and QuantiFERON-TB Gold. 

CLL prognostic testing revealed CD19-positive lymphoid cells (87% of lymphocytes), ZAP70 positivity 1%, CD38 positivity 20%, unmutated IGHV3-21 status, normal karyotype, and negative FISH for trisomy 12 and deletions of 6q, 11q, 13q, and 17p. Beta-2 microglobulin was 2.27 mg/L. These features indicated unfavorable CLL prognosis. 

At presentation, the leading clinical consideration was Richter transformation of her CLL, and an ultrasound-guided core biopsy of an enlarged cervical lymph node was performed.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 32-year-old man presented with a 2-day history of epigastric pain, low-grade fever, and vomiting. He drank alcohol daily (approximately 40 g/day) but had no other medical history. On examination, his temperature was 37.4 °C. Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 22,600/mm^3 and a C-reactive protein level of 3.00 mg/dL. Abdominal CT revealed a diffusely thickened, edematous gastric wall. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy demonstrated marked hyperplasia of the gastric folds with abundant mucopus, extensive mucosal redness and edema, and poor distensibility of the gastric wall.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 74‐year‐old man with a history of well-differentiated T3N1M0 pancreatic adenocarcinoma status post Whipple procedure completed six cycles of gemcitabine monotherapy (1000 mg/m2 on days 1, 8, 15 of a 28-day cycle). Two weeks after his final infusion, he noted a painful ulcer on the posterior aspect of his right calf and swelling of the right knee without preceding trauma. He denied fever, chills, or systemic symptoms.  
His medical history was notable for diet-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, benign prostatic hyperplasia, gastroesophageal reflux disease, osteoarthritis, and a remote 40-pack-year smoking history. Three days after his first gemcitabine cycle, he was admitted for fever, neutropenia, and bilateral ankle inflammation, which resolved with cefepime. He resumed gemcitabine with dexamethasone premedication. He also had a right-leg DVT treated with enoxaparin, later switched to rivaroxaban.  
On examination, he was afebrile. The right calf showed a 4 cm ulcer with a necrotic base, surrounding erythema, and no fluctuance. The right knee was warm, mildly erythematous, and tender with an effusion. Distal pulses were intact, and there were no signs of peripheral vascular insufficiency.  
Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL, leukocytes 6.8×10^9/L, platelets 130×10^9/L, ESR 85 mm/h, and CA 19-9 60 U/mL (mildly elevated). An autoimmune screen was not performed.  
A wound swab and tissue cultures revealed no bacterial or fungal growth. Skin-punch biopsy showed ulceration with tissue necrosis and acute inflammation but no evidence of infection or malignancy. Knee aspiration yielded calcium pyrophosphate crystals without microorganisms. Doppler ultrasound of the right leg showed diffuse soft-tissue edema without DVT. CT of the abdomen and pelvis revealed stable postoperative changes without new abnormalities.  
What is your differential diagnosis?

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 6‐year‐old previously healthy girl presented with a 7‐day history of sore throat and a 4‐day history of fever (up to 39.5 °C) and a spreading targetoid rash. Initial throat swab was positive for Streptococcus pyogenes, and she was started on ceftriaxone. One day after beginning ceftriaxone, she developed pedal edema and tense blisters on her feet, leading to transfer to a burn unit for concern of Stevens–Johnson syndrome. She had no pruritus, skin pain, arthralgias, or other medication exposures besides an undocumented antibiotic given 5 weeks earlier in Sri Lanka, which she had tolerated in the past. 

On examination, she was febrile with otherwise normal vital signs. Skin findings included generalized, scattered, dusky erythematous macules and patches—many targetoid—on the torso and extremities, without desquamation. She had bilateral pedal edema with tense bullae on the feet. Mucous‐membrane examination revealed strawberry tongue mucositis and bilateral nonexudative conjunctival injection. 

A punch biopsy of a foot lesion demonstrated an intraepidermal cleft with subtle vacuolar interface changes. Serologies for Mycoplasma pneumoniae and herpes simplex virus were negative. A respiratory viral panel by PCR (influenza, respiratory syncytial virus, parainfluenza, metapneumovirus, and adenovirus) was negative. The erythrocyte sedimentation rate was elevated at 95 mm/hr. Transthoracic echocardiography showed no coronary artery abnormalities.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 21-year-old woman presented with a 1-week history of painful swelling over the inner angle of the left eye. She reported occasional epiphora since childhood, without trauma or discharge. Visual acuity was 20/20 bilaterally, and ocular examination was normal. External examination of the left eye showed upper and lower eyelid edema and erythema, with superotemporal displacement of the globe; the caruncle was thickened, and the medial canthus appeared rounded. There was no conjunctival hyperemia and no discharge from either punctum. Mild mechanical limitation of adduction was noted. 

After a 1-week course of anti-inflammatory and antibiotic medications, the pain and erythema improved but the inferomedial swelling persisted and appeared to progress. Lacrimal irrigation demonstrated a patent lacrimal drainage system. Given the inferomedial location of the lesion, associated preseptal cellulitis that subsided, and the history of lifelong epiphora, a lacrimal sac diverticulum was suspected.

Computed tomography of the orbits and paranasal sinuses revealed a large, heterogeneous, radiolucent mass in the inferomedial aspect of the left orbit, indenting the globe, and containing a radiopaque solid structure attached to the inferior orbital rim near the lacrimal sac fossa, without bony remodeling.  

With a revised clinicoradiological diagnosis of an orbital foreign body granuloma or cyst, surgical exploration was planned.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old man with HIV on HAART (undetectable viral load), a history of anal condylomas, intersphincteric fistulas, perirectal abscesses, and ischemic proctitis 6 years earlier presented with 1 day of severe rectal pain, subjective fevers, nausea, multiple episodes of bloody and brown emesis, diarrhea with bright red blood mixed with clots, and fecal urge incontinence. He engages in receptive anal intercourse and takes prophylactic medications for HIV and herpes simplex virus. On examination, he was febrile (38.9°C), had abdominal distension and tenderness in the lower abdomen, and poor rectal sphincter tone. Laboratory results showed leukocytosis (WBC 29,000/mm3) and elevated lactate (2.5 mmol/L). Abdominal CT demonstrated severe proctocolitis. Empiric antibiotics were started after obtaining blood, stool, and rectal swab cultures. Sigmoidoscopy revealed a 5 cm submucosal, circumferential, nonbleeding mass 15–20 cm from the anal verge suggestive of an abscess. Pelvic MRI confirmed a 10 cm segment of circumferential intramural abscess with hemorrhage near the rectosigmoid junction. Stool studies and infectious serologies were negative. The patient improved clinically, was afebrile with normalized WBC count, and was discharged after 1 week; diverticulitis was presumed to be the etiology. Six weeks later, he underwent laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with primary stapled anastomosis. On postoperative day 3, he developed sudden abdominal pain, ileus, and rising WBC count and C-reactive protein. CT with rectal contrast showed no leak but segmental colitis. He underwent abdominal washout and diverting loop colostomy; exploration revealed ascites and adhesions without stool or purulence, and proctoscopy under water showed no air leak. Pathologic examination of the resected sigmoid colon showed patchy mild active colitis with focal perivascular lymphocytic inflammation and chronic vasculitis involving intramural and mesenteric arteries, with “no evidence of diverticular disease, granulomas, or viral cytopathic changes.” Rheumatology evaluation revealed bilateral L4 dermatome numbness. Serologic testing showed elevated p-ANCA titers, normal serum protein electrophoresis and complement levels, and negative anti-Ro, anti-LA, double-stranded DNA, and PR3-ANCA titers. Creatinine and urinalysis were normal, suggesting no renal involvement.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 55-year-old man presented with 3 days of nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and constipation, with milder similar episodes in preceding weeks. His history was notable for obesity, prediabetes, and a 30-pack-year smoking history. On examination, he was tachycardic; the abdomen was diffusely distended, mildly tender, without rebound tenderness, and had overactive bowel sounds. Blood tests, including liver and renal function, lipase, and calcium, were normal; white blood cell count was 14.9×10^9/L and C-reactive protein was 15 mg/L. Stool tests for occult blood and calprotectin, as well as QuantiFERON-TB Gold, were negative. Contrast CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed fecalization of the small bowel, dilated proximal segments, and irregular wall thickening of a distal ileal segment with partial luminal narrowing. Upper and lower endoscopies were unremarkable, and the patient improved with conservative management. Follow-up MR enterography demonstrated asymmetrical small bowel wall thickening and adjacent mesenteric lymphadenopathy, with no significant mechanical cause for intestinal obstruction. In light of a possible systemic pathology, contrast CT of the chest was obtained and showed a solid infiltrative right hilar soft tissue density mass invading the mediastinum.
