Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 72-year-old woman with a 12-year history of refractory Mycobacterium intracellulare pulmonary disease was referred for further treatment. She had a prior history of pulmonary tuberculosis. Four years ago a cavitary nodule was detected, and she subsequently received multiple macrolide-containing multidrug regimens without sustained culture conversion. At presentation, chest radiography and computed tomography showed fibrocavitary-type lesions in the upper lobes. Sputum cultures grew macrolide-resistant M. intracellulare (minimum inhibitory concentration for clarithromycin >32 μg/mL; for amikacin 8 μg/mL). She was continued on clarithromycin 400 mg/day, ethambutol 500 mg/day, and moxifloxacin 400 mg/day, and inhaled amikacin liposome suspension (ALIS, 590 mg/day) was added. Three weeks after starting ALIS, she developed fever, cough, and loss of appetite. Repeat chest radiography and CT revealed multiple infiltrative opacities in the left upper lobe and bilateral lower lobes, with decreased thickness of the cavity wall and bronchial walls.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

options.A: Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms (DRESS)

question:
A 64-year-old man presented with haematuria and urinary retention after his long-term catheter, placed for benign prostatic hyperplasia, fell out 2 days earlier; he had not passed urine for 24 hours. His history included benign prostatic hyperplasia and hypertension, treated with ramipril; he was an ex-smoker and did not consume alcohol. On examination he was diffusely tender in the lower abdomen, had an enlarged prostate on digital rectal examination, and a temperature of 37.8 °C. Laboratory tests showed a C-reactive protein level of 179 mg/L, a white-cell count of 8.5×10^9/L with neutrophils of 7.3×10^9/L, urea of 13.9 mmol/L, creatinine of 222 µmol/L, and blood glucose of 14 mmol/L; a subsequent HbA1c measurement was 7.8%. Urine dipstick revealed blood, nitrates, and white cells. Blood cultures were sterile after 5 days; urine culture showed heavy mixed growth without a definitive organism. Renal ultrasound showed no hydronephrosis, an incidental 2.7 cm renal cyst, and heterogeneous echogenicities around the bladder consistent with gas. On day 3, CT of the abdomen and pelvis demonstrated a thick-walled bladder, a large right-sided bladder diverticulum containing a gas–fluid level with intramural gas, and extraluminal free gas in the pelvis and right paracolic gutter.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 40-year-old woman presented with abdominal discomfort. She had undergone laparoscopic myomectomy 8 years earlier and was otherwise healthy with no medications. There was no known family history of endocrine or inherited disorders.

Vital signs were normal, and the physical examination was unremarkable. Routine biochemical tests, including serum calcium and intact parathyroid hormone, were within normal limits.

Contrast-enhanced abdominal CT revealed a multilocular 21 × 19 mm mass in the pancreatic tail. MRI showed the lesion to be low signal on T1-weighted images with heterogeneous high signal at the margins. Doppler endoscopic ultrasonography demonstrated increased blood flow in the lesion and identified three additional tumors in the pancreatic body and one in the uncinate process.

EUS-guided fine-needle aspiration of one of the tumors confirmed a neuroendocrine tumor. Serum levels of gastrin, glucagon, insulin, and other endocrine studies were normal.

Based on these findings, the patient was diagnosed with nonfunctional multiple pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors (PanNETs).

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 29-year-old man presented with several years of incomplete bladder emptying. He denied dysuria, hematuria, incontinence, weight loss, or bone pain.  
On CT urography, there was a 13.4 × 10.6 × 13.0 cm thick-walled, predominantly cystic pelvic mass with solid-enhancing mural nodules located inferior to the bladder. No lymphadenopathy or distant metastases were noted. He was started on tamsulosin and referred for further evaluation.  
An ultrasound-guided needle biopsy of the mass showed bland smooth muscle cells with no cytologic atypia, no necrosis, and mitotic activity <1/50 high-power fields, consistent with leiomyoma. Active surveillance with a CT in 3 months was recommended.  
Three months later, he presented with urinary retention requiring catheterization. Repeat CT showed slight increase in tumor size. His AUA symptom index was 35. Transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) performed 2 weeks later again showed leiomyoma.  
Over the next 2 years, his PSA remained 1.13 ng/mL, and his creatinine rose to 1.4 mg/dL. MRI of the pelvis demonstrated a well-marginated, complex solid, cystic, and septated mass with multiple enhancing areas and restricted diffusion, appearing to communicate with the bladder neck.  
The patient opted for surgical excision of the mass.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.B: Stump (Stromal Tumor of Uncertain Malignant Potential)

question:
A 28-year-old Polish woman who had lived in the United Kingdom for four years presented with severe ulceration of the perineal area, dysuria, soreness of the mouth, fever, nonproductive cough, and a facial rash. Two weeks earlier she had a flu-like illness and sore throat treated with antibiotics. Three days into the illness she developed burning itching of her genital region refractory to topical antifungal and antibiotic therapy for presumed urinary tract infection. She subsequently developed high fever, vomiting, and a maculopapular rash confined to her face and neck.

Her medical history was notable for von Willebrand’s disease and a caesarean delivery two years earlier; she denied relevant drug or sexual history. On examination, temperature was 38 °C, heart rate 110 bpm, blood pressure 105/60 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation 100% on room air. She had crepitations at the left lung base, right upper quadrant tenderness, mild conjunctival injection, and an ill-defined erythematous rash on her face and neck. Oral examination showed erythema at the back of the mouth with a white coating. The perineum exhibited marked erythema, excoriations, and discrete ulcerations causing severe pain.

Laboratory studies revealed lymphopenia (0.26 × 10^9/L), C-reactive protein 49 mg/L, alanine transaminase 342 IU/L, and alkaline phosphatase 185 IU/L. An infectious disease consult was obtained. Initial differential diagnoses included HIV seroconversion, herpes simplex virus, Behçet’s disease, systemic lupus erythematosus, systemic vasculitis, and Reiter’s syndrome. Viral serologies (including measles IgM and IgG), HIV PCR, anti-streptolysin O titre, complement levels, autoimmune screen, and immunoglobulin levels were ordered. She was started on intravenous acyclovir and high-dose prednisolone.

Within 20 hours she developed type 1 respiratory failure (pO2 9.5 kPa on 10 L oxygen) and required noninvasive ventilation. Chest radiography showed minimal left basal consolidation, although bronchial breathing was noted clinically. The working diagnosis was HIV seroconversion complicated by Pneumocystis carinii pneumonia, with consideration also of atypical severe bacterial infection. She received imipenem, teicoplanin, cotrimoxazole, and high-dose methylprednisolone.

By day 4 the facial rash had resolved and oxygenation improved (pO2 13.1 kPa on 40% FiO2). Repeat studies showed negative HIV PCR, negative ASO titre, normal complement and immunoglobulin levels, and a negative autoimmune screen. Steroids were tapered, antibiotics (including cotrimoxazole) were stopped, and by day 10 her symptoms had completely resolved.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 21-year-old man was admitted with a 1-week history of swelling and pain in both knees, both ankles, the right shoulder, and the right wrist, accompanied by fever. Physical examination revealed redness, swelling, and a subcutaneous hematoma over the right ankle with increased local skin temperature. He reported an antecedent “cold” and had experienced watery diarrhea 3 days before the onset of joint symptoms. Laboratory tests showed a leukocyte count of 11.78 × 10^9/L (neutrophils 82.9%), platelet count of 384 × 10^9/L, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 77 mm/h, high-sensitivity C-reactive protein of 176.49 mg/L, uric acid of 250.5 µmol/L, activated partial thromboplastin time of 44.60 s, fibrinogen of 6.74 g/L, D-dimer of 10.67 µg/mL, and factor VIII activity of 203.7%. Aspiration of the right ankle yielded bloody fluid with numerous leukocytes and red blood cells. Debridement of an ulcerated, necrotic subcutaneous hematoma was performed and cultures of joint fluid and wound secretions were negative. Magnetic resonance imaging of the right ankle suggested inflammatory arthritis. Tests for rheumatic factor, antinuclear antibodies, HLA-B27, stool cultures, and other infectious agents were negative. On hospital day 3, he developed severe left perianal pain with localized skin redness and swelling, and a diagnosis of perianal abscess was made.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A male infant was born vaginally at 38 weeks’ gestation to a 36-year-old gravida 1, para 1 mother with blood type A RhD negative. The pregnancy was uncomplicated, and routine anti-D immunoglobulin prophylaxis (300 µg) was administered at 30 weeks. Shortly after birth, the infant desaturated to 24% on pulse oximetry and required nasal continuous positive airway pressure with 100% FiO2 for 6 hours. 

At 2 hours of age, plasma glucose was 0.5 mmol/L (reference > 2.5 mmol/L), and hemoglobin was 4.5 mmol/L (reference 9.4–14.4 mmol/L); he received an umbilical vein glucose infusion and a blood transfusion. At 6 hours, he developed jaundice with a total bilirubin level of 144 µmol/L, and phototherapy was initiated. He was transferred to a tertiary care center, where a peripheral blood film showed leukoerythroblastosis and mild thrombocytopenia. 

On day 2, abdominal ultrasound revealed hepatosplenomegaly with a normal gallbladder. A direct antiglobulin test (DAT) was strongly positive. Laboratory studies showed lactate dehydrogenase 66 µkat/L (reference 2.17–12.5 µkat/L), reticulocytes 246×10^9/L (reference 79–222×10^9/L), ferritin 528 µg/L (reference 10–400 µg/L), haptoglobin < 0.1 g/L, and lactate 6.3 mmol/L (reference 0.5–2.5 mmol/L). 

On day 4, total bilirubin peaked at 462 µmol/L, with unconjugated bilirubin 167 µmol/L and conjugated bilirubin 295 µmol/L (64% of total); ursodeoxycholic acid was started. Liver enzymes and international normalized ratio were within normal limits, but a persistently high intravenous glucose requirement prompted a hypoglycemia test on day 5. Plasma glucose fell to 1.0 mmol/L with insulin 51 pmol/L (reference 10–125 pmol/L) and C-peptide 1870 pmol/L (reference 379–1630 pmol/L), consistent with hyperinsulinism. Diazoxide (10 mg/kg/day) and glucagon (2.5 µg/kg/hour) were initiated, reducing but not eliminating the need for intravenous glucose.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 61-year-old man was referred to hepatobiliary surgery for multiple giant hepatic hemangiomas discovered 5 years earlier on routine examination. In June 20XX, CT showed new multiple lung nodules, a right axillary tumor, and the known hepatic lesions; he was referred for evaluation of the axillary mass.

Laboratory studies showed a normal soluble interleukin-2 receptor level (391 U/mL) and normal carcinoembryonic antigen (2.6 ng/dL), but an elevated thyroglobulin level (226 ng/mL).

FDG–PET/CT demonstrated faint uptake in the thyroid gland, no uptake in the lung nodules, intense uptake in the right axillary tumor (SUVmax 5.1), and decreased uptake in the hepatic lesions.

Cervical ultrasonography revealed irregular, hypoechoic masses with indistinct borders in both thyroid lobes, with suspected capsular invasion in the right lobe and right cervical lymphadenopathy. Breast ultrasonography was normal.

Ultrasonography of the axilla showed a 30 mm lobulated, well-defined, hypoechoic mass with internal heterogeneity and no enlarged surrounding lymph nodes. Contrast-enhanced CT of the axilla confirmed an oval mass with high attenuation, poor contrast enhancement, no chest‐wall invasion, intact subclavian artery, and absence of the dorsal thoracic artery.

Additional findings included bilateral pulmonary nodules up to 7 mm, hepatic nodules with peripheral arterial phase enhancement spreading centrally in the portal phase (consistent with cavernous hemangioma), a left renal angiomyolipoma, a left adrenal adenoma, and a subcutaneous lipoma.

Fine-needle aspiration cytology revealed that the right thyroid tumor was class IV, the left thyroid tumor class V, the left cervical lymph node class V, and the right axillary tumor class III with epithelial-like cells in a mesenchymal background; thyroid transcription factor-1 was negative in the axillary lesion.

He was diagnosed with papillary thyroid carcinoma and an unspecified right axillary mesenchymal tumor. The differential diagnosis for the axillary lesion included lymph node metastasis from papillary thyroid carcinoma and occult breast cancer. The patient underwent total thyroidectomy, cervical lymph node dissection, and resection of the axillary tumor.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

options.C: Lymph node metastasis from papillary thyroid carcinoma

question:
A 29-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of progressive vision loss in her left eye. She had a 15-year history of headaches with documented bilateral optic nerve head swelling and possible optic disc masses. At age 14, she had been diagnosed with pseudotumor cerebri and underwent an extensive negative work-up, including left optic nerve sheath fenestration, without improvement. On presentation, visual acuity was 20/20 in the right eye and 20/150 in the left. Initial genetic testing by whole exome sequencing was negative for mutations in von Hippel-Lindau, hypoxia-inducible factor-2α, neurofibromatosis, and tuberous sclerosis genes. Over the next several years, she received monthly then biweekly alternating intravitreal bevacizumab and aflibercept injections, along with bimonthly intravitreal steroid implants, with only limited response in the right eye. The optic nerve head lesions progressed to involve the papillomacular bundles. By early 2017, visual acuity had declined to 20/400 in the right eye and no light perception in the left eye.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 44-year-old man presented in December 2020 with acute onset of fever and rash two days after completing a 14-day home isolation for RT-PCR–confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection, during which he had remained asymptomatic. He described mild, intermittent fevers with chills and developed a non-blanching, non-pruritic, maculopapular rash on his abdomen that rapidly desquamated into annular plaques with satellite lesions. He reported extreme fatigue, several episodes of vomiting and diarrhea without abdominal pain, and denied headache, myalgia, arthralgia, bleeding, mucosal involvement, or any respiratory symptoms.

On arrival, he was hypotensive (84/52 mmHg), tachycardic (137 beats/min), and febrile (38.2 °C). The lower extremities were cool, distal pulses were undetectable, and capillary refill was prolonged. His respiratory rate was 20 breaths/min with oxygen saturation of 99% on room air. Cardiac auscultation revealed tachycardia with distant heart sounds; lung fields were clear. Neurologic examination was normal.

Laboratory studies showed leukocytosis with a left shift, neutrophil predominance, and anemia; inflammatory markers were markedly elevated (see Table 1). A chest radiograph and electrocardiogram were obtained. He received bolus intravenous fluids and broad-spectrum antibiotics but developed acute pulmonary edema during resuscitation, prompting initiation of inotropic support and transfer to the ICU with noninvasive ventilatory assistance. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed global left ventricular hypokinesia, moderate systolic dysfunction, dilation of all four chambers, mitral and tricuspid regurgitation, pericardial effusion, and an ejection fraction below 30%.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 68-year-old man presented with dizziness and difficulty focusing his eyes since that morning, along with a 2-day history of headaches. He had a history of well-controlled hypertension on an angiotensin receptor blocker. On arrival, his blood pressure was 146/99 mm Hg, and he was noted to have left homonymous hemianopsia. Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain showed a relatively high signal intensity in the right occipital lobe on both diffusion-weighted imaging and fluid-attenuated inversion recovery sequences, with no apparent change on apparent diffusion coefficient maps. Magnetic resonance angiography demonstrated no steno-occlusive lesions. Based on these findings, subacute cerebral infarction was suspected, and heparin therapy was initiated.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 17-year-old girl with a history of Guillain–Barré syndrome 5 years earlier presented with 1 week of bilateral lower extremity pain and 1 day of weakness and worsening dysphagia. She reported a recent upper respiratory infection. At age 12, her initial episode required intubation; she was treated with 5 days of IVIG and recovered with minimal residual hip flexor weakness.

On examination, vital signs were normal. Neurologic evaluation revealed asymmetric weakness in both upper and lower extremities, worse on the left, with trace reflexes at the ankles and preserved reflexes at the knees and arms. Sensation to light touch, temperature, vibration, and proprioception was intact. Her negative inspiratory force was 23 cm H2O.

A T1-weighted postcontrast fat-saturated MRI of the cervical and lumbar spine showed anterior nerve-root enhancement. Cerebrospinal fluid obtained after the start of IVIG demonstrated elevated protein and lymphocytic pleocytosis. Electromyography and nerve conduction studies revealed a predominantly motor polyneuropathy with mild axonal greater than demyelinating features, intact sensory responses, preserved F waves, no conduction block, and no temporal dispersion. Infectious and rheumatologic work-ups were negative, and anti–ganglioside antibodies were absent.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 17-year-old previously healthy male presented with acute altered mental status. Initial blood glucose was 1.8 mmol/L; he received IV dextrose and improved, but his glucose then rebounded to 12.1 mmol/L over 3 hours with reactive hyperglycemia. His history was notable only for ADHD treated with dextroamphetamine-amphetamine and a family history of autoimmune thyroid disease. Thyroid‐stimulating hormone was normal. Urine screening was presumptively positive for THC; sulfonylurea screen was negative.

He was admitted NPO with glucose checks every 1–2 hours. After a 10-hour fast, glucose fell to 2.1 mmol/L; a critical sample showed insulin 158.35 pmol/L, C-peptide 1.09 nmol/L, and suppressed beta-hydroxybutyrate, indicating endogenous hyperinsulinism. Other hormonal studies (ACTH, cortisol, growth hormone) were appropriate for the clinical context. He received additional dextrose and maintained euglycemia on dextrose-containing fluids. After resuming a general diet, glucose ranged from 2.9–9.1 mmol/L.

Given reactive hyperglycemia and family history, pancreatic autoantibodies (insulin, IgG islet cell, IA-2, GAD) were obtained and all returned positive, leading to a diagnosis of stage 1 type 1 diabetes. He was discharged with self-monitoring of blood glucose, glucagon training, and nutrition education.

On outpatient follow-up, TrialNet screening confirmed multiple islet autoantibodies. An oral glucose tolerance test showed 1-hour glucose 12.0 mmol/L and 2-hour glucose 11.1 mmol/L, consistent with stage 2 type 1 diabetes. Over the next month, he gained weight, discontinued frequent SMBG, and CGM revealed prolonged fasting hypoglycemia alongside postprandial hyperglycemia. Hemoglobin A1c rose from 4.7% to 5.5%. A screening abdominal ultrasound was unremarkable, raising concern for an additional etiology of his hypoglycemia.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

options.C: Hypoglycemia due to insulin autoimmune syndrome

question:
A 75-year-old Albanian man with benign prostatic hypertrophy underwent an ultrasound for prostate evaluation, which incidentally detected a focal liver lesion. He denied symptoms, and his physical examination was unremarkable. Laboratory studies, including liver function tests, α-fetoprotein, and viral hepatitis serologies, were within normal limits or negative. Contrast‐enhanced multidetector CT of the chest and abdomen showed a solitary focal lesion in the right hepatic lobe without cirrhotic changes, other masses, or lymphadenopathy. For further characterization, a 1.5-T MRI of the upper abdomen was performed with T1-weighted in-phase and out-of-phase sequences, T2-weighted images with fat suppression, and dynamic contrast‐enhanced sequences. On MRI, the lesion was hypointense relative to liver parenchyma on T1-weighted images but isointense to spleen; on T2-weighted fat-suppressed images it was moderately hyperintense relative to liver yet isointense to spleen. In the arterial phase after gadolinium administration, the mass showed mild homogeneous enhancement, and in delayed (90-second) images it demonstrated mild perilesional enhancement.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 15-year-old previously healthy girl on a school trip presented with 3 hours of progressive shortness of breath and chest pain, following one day of cough and new-onset hemoptysis. She had experienced coryzal symptoms for one week prior but felt well enough to travel. There was no history of skin injury, immunodeficiency, or allergies.

On examination:  
• Respiratory rate 50/min, SpO2 92% on room air  
• Heart rate 120/min, blood pressure 105/74 mmHg, afebrile, cold extremities  
• Auscultation revealed crackles and reduced air entry in the right lower chest

Initial laboratory studies:  
• Arterial blood gas on supplemental oxygen: pH 7.42, PaCO2 4.0 kPa, PaO2 7.2 kPa  
• Lactate 1.7 mmol/L, bicarbonate 19.5 mmol/L, base excess –5 mmol/L  
• White blood cell count 1.5 ×10^9/L with neutrophils 0.8 ×10^9/L, C-reactive protein 0.6 mg/L

Imaging:  
• Chest radiograph showed dense consolidation of the right middle and lower zones, evolving within hours to diffuse four-quadrant opacification consistent with ARDS.

Bronchoscopy:  
• Copious yellow, protein-rich plasma-like secretions (>300 mL) with a white-speckled bronchial mucosa  
• Bronchial washings obtained for microbiologic analysis

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 51-year-old woman with a >20-year history of poorly controlled type 1 diabetes mellitus complicated by dialysis-dependent renal failure, retinopathy, and neuropathy presented with sudden onset of left thigh swelling and constant severe pain for 7 days. She denied trauma, vascular interventions, fever, or weight loss. Her medical history included coronary artery disease, stroke, hypertension, and mild cirrhosis from hepatitis C.

On examination, the left thigh was erythematous, edematous, and tender on the medial aspect, with a circumference 9 cm greater than the right. The overlying skin was otherwise normal, and she was afebrile. Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 11 000/μL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/h, a C-reactive protein level of 1.3 mg/dL, and a creatine phosphokinase level of 338 U/L.

Plain radiography of the thigh showed no gas or calcification. Lower-extremity arterial and venous Doppler studies showed no vascular occlusion, and blood cultures were negative. Contrast-enhanced CT of the left thigh demonstrated diffuse fat stranding and a heterogeneous appearance of the vastus medialis muscle. An MRI with gadolinium contrast was then obtained after hemodialysis; T2-weighted images revealed an irregular, enhancing 20 × 5 cm lesion with surrounding edema in the medial vastus medialis, containing areas of muscle breakdown. Imaging alone could not exclude a soft-tissue sarcoma, and a biopsy was planned.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 57-year-old woman with ALK-positive lung adenocarcinoma metastatic to the spine (status post T3–5 laminectomy and radiation) was started on oral lorlatinib 100 mg daily two months ago. At a follow-up visit, she reported four days of severe vision loss in both eyes, and lorlatinib was discontinued. She was referred for MRI of the brain and orbits and to ophthalmology. Six days later, she presented to the ophthalmology clinic with two weeks of painless, progressive vision loss, noting complete darkness for the last three days. She denied eye pain. On examination, visual acuity was no light perception bilaterally. Pupils were large and minimally reactive with a left relative afferent pupillary defect. Dilated fundus examination showed trace optic disc edema (left > right) and mildly dilated venules. MRI of the orbits demonstrated bilateral T1 postcontrast enhancement and increased T2 signal throughout the optic nerves up to the chiasm, more pronounced on the left. MRI of the brain revealed a small curvilinear focus of restricted diffusion and FLAIR hyperintensity in the splenium of the corpus callosum; otherwise, it was normal. Given these findings, she was admitted for expedited work-up.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 46-year-old woman presented with a 3-month history of polyuropolydipsic syndrome (urine output 5 L/24 h) and severe asthenia, without headache, visual disturbances, or focal neurologic signs. Routine blood and urinary ionograms, serum creatinine, and blood glucose levels were normal. Plasma osmolality was 292 mOsm/kg, and urinary osmolality was 138 mOsm/kg. Evaluation of the thyrotropic, corticotropic, and gonadotropic axes was unremarkable; prolactin was slightly elevated at 650 mU/L (reference range 99–495). She was started on oral desmopressin (0.1 mg twice daily), with symptomatic improvement of her polyuria and polydipsia. Brain magnetic resonance imaging revealed two intensely enhancing parietal lesions (19.3 × 17.5 × 18.2 mm on the right and 7.7 × 8.2 × 9.3 mm on the left), suggestive of metastases, and an 8-mm contrast-enhancing nodular enlargement in the supradiaphragmatic portion of the pituitary stalk, the differential diagnosis of which included a pituitary adenoma versus a secondary lesion.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 44-year-old woman presented with a 2-week history of progressive, painless bilateral visual deterioration. She had been prescribed reading glasses one month earlier but noted no improvement. On examination 9 days later, her visual acuity had declined to 6/60 bilaterally. Confrontational testing with a red pin revealed bilateral central scotomas; Goldman kinetic perimetry showed normal peripheral fields. The optic discs appeared normal and were not pale. She could not identify any Ishihara test plates.

Her medical history was unremarkable. She consumed 14 units of alcohol per week and smoked 20 cigarettes daily. She denied weight loss but admitted to a poor diet: breakfast of milk tea and toast; lunch often skipped or a packet of crisps; dinner usually meat and potatoes; green leafy vegetables only once or twice a month.

Physical examination was otherwise normal. Laboratory studies showed:
- Serum folic acid: 1.5 ng/ml (reference 2.5–18)
- Red cell folate: 208 nmol/l (reference 220–620)
- Hemoglobin: 14 g/dl; MCV: 122.8 fl (reference 78–100)
- Vitamin B12: 354 nmol/l
- Thyroid function tests, endomysial and gliadin antibodies: negative

Brain MRI revealed a tiny focus of high T2 signal in the left frontal lobe consistent with a benign perivascular space and no evidence of demyelination.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 6-month-old term female infant was brought for evaluation of a congenital scalp lesion. The lesion has been present since birth; there is no history of trauma or maternal teratogen exposure. The infant was born at term by uncomplicated vaginal delivery and had a history of a patent foramen ovale diagnosed at birth.

On dermatologic examination, there is a single, oval, midline occipital lesion measuring 0.6 × 0.7 cm with a glistening, translucent surface, absence of hair, and a distinct hair-collar sign. Dermoscopy shows a translucent epidermis, telangiectasia, absence of follicular orifices, and peripheral hypertrichosis. The remainder of the skin exam is normal.

General examination reveals normal growth and developmental milestones with no spinal or limb deformities. Lung auscultation is clear. Cardiac examination discloses a grade 3 holosystolic murmur at the lower left sternal border without a thrill. Echocardiography demonstrates an asymptomatic small ventricular septal defect.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 3-month-old male infant with failure to thrive and a cardiac murmur was found on echocardiography to have an isolated 6 mm × 9 mm perimembranous ventricular septal defect with an unrestrictive left-to-right shunt, pulmonary plethora, congestive heart failure, and left heart volume overload. There were no other cardiac or extracardiac anomalies. Despite maximal medical therapy, he remained symptomatic and underwent surgical VSD closure with a porcine pericardial patch via a right atrial approach under standard heparinization, cold-blood cardioplegia, and cardiopulmonary bypass (CPB) (CPB time 47 min, aortic cross-clamp 35 min).

After separation from CPB, a routine epicardial echocardiogram demonstrated no residual VSD and normal ventricular function but revealed an unexpected single, well-circumscribed, globular, homogeneously hyperechogenic mass within the left atrium. On multiple planes, the mobile mass measured 10 mm × 11 mm, had a tongue- or finger-like appearance without a peduncle, and extended from the free wall of the left atrium. With each cardiac cycle, it protruded closer to the mitral annulus in diastole. Colour Doppler interrogation showed no obstruction of pulmonary veins or mitral inflow. An intracardiac thrombus was the primary differential diagnosis; however, he was adequately anticoagulated, the activated coagulation time was maintained in the desired range throughout, and no thrombi were observed in the CPB circuit.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 1-year-old boy was brought to the emergency department for altered consciousness. He had no history of fever, upper respiratory symptoms, feeding intolerance, abdominal distension, bloody stools, or known trauma. His past history included prematurity at 29 + 3 weeks’ gestation and stage IA necrotizing enterocolitis. The patient’s parents were young with a history of drug abuse. On arrival his Glasgow Coma Scale score was 3 (E1V1M1). Vital signs: temperature 33.5 °C, heart rate 124 beats/min, respiratory rate 18/min, blood pressure 58/47 mmHg. Examination revealed a distended, guarded abdomen with absent bowel sounds; there were no skin bruises, and retinal examination was normal. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 13 g/dL, white blood cell count 30 240/µL (13% neutrophils), pH 6.76, pCO2 22.5 mmHg, HCO3 3.2 mmol/L, base excess –31.6, BUN 16 mg/dL, creatinine 1.07 mg/dL, AST 485 U/L, ALT 366 U/L, Na 133 mmol/L, K 6.1 mmol/L, Cl 100 mmol/L. Chest radiography demonstrated diffusely dilated bowel suggestive of ileus. A long-bone survey showed no fractures. Abdominal CT revealed pneumatosis intestinalis and portal venous gas with poor perfusion of the right-sided small bowel. Noncontrast brain CT showed diffuse cerebral swelling with no subdural or subarachnoid hemorrhage, consistent with hypoxic-ischemic changes and severe edema. Considering the clinical presentation and imaging findings, the working diagnosis was septic shock with acute ischemic bowel.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

options.C: Septic shock with acute ischemic bowel

question:
A 54-year-old woman presented with one day of unconsciousness, fever, nausea, and vomiting. Over the past decade, she had been hospitalized repeatedly for similar symptoms and complained of chronic headaches. On admission, her Glasgow Coma Scale score was 7, and meningeal signs were present. Renal function, liver biochemistry, and peripheral white-cell count were normal. A head CT scan was unremarkable. Lumbar puncture revealed an opening pressure of 200 mm H₂O; CSF analysis showed 374 white cells (80% neutrophils), protein 324.6 mg/dL, glucose 0.91 mmol/L, and chloride 109.5 mmol/L. CSF cultures were negative, and CSF metagenomic next-generation sequencing did not identify a pathogen. She was treated empirically with isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, and ceftriaxone. By day 12, her symptoms and CSF indices (pressure 190 mm H₂O; white cells 20; protein 80.8 mg/dL; glucose 2.18 mmol/L; chloride 119.6 mmol/L) had improved. On day 17, she redeveloped fever, headache, nausea, and vomiting. Repeat lumbar puncture showed an opening pressure > 300 mm H₂O; 2911 white cells; protein 178.2 mg/dL; glucose 1.82 mmol/L; chloride 118.6 mmol/L. CSF cultures grew hemolytic streptococcus, and she received meropenem and norvancomycin. To investigate recurrent meningitis, a brain MRI was obtained. It demonstrated multiple symmetric hyperintense signals in the periventricular white matter around the fourth ventricle, brainstem, posterior limbs of the internal capsules, and cerebral hemispheres. Additionally, there was a partially empty sella, distended optic nerve sheaths, flattening of the optic papillae, and narrowing of the ventricles.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 29-year-old nonpregnant woman presented with a 2-year history of low back pain that had worsened over the preceding 2 months. Her medical history was notable for a laparoscopic myomectomy for uterine leiomyoma 5 years earlier. Physical examination findings were unremarkable.  
Abdominal ultrasound revealed a 3.6 × 2.5 cm hypoechoic solid mass in the posterior right lobe of the liver; the remainder of the abdominal and pelvic ultrasound examinations was normal.  
Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen showed a patchy, slightly low-density area in the peripheral zone of segment VI of the right liver, with heterogeneous enhancement in the arterial phase.  
Laboratory studies, including liver function tests and tumor markers (AFP, CEA, CA19-9, CA125), were within normal limits. Serologies for hepatitis B surface antigen and hepatitis C virus antibodies were negative.  
What is your differential diagnosis?

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 36-year-old woman undergoing in vitro fertilization presented for ultrasound‐guided follicular aspiration under short‐term intravenous anesthesia. She denied any history of allergic reactions. Standard monitors included BIS index, pulse oximetry, end-tidal CO₂, airway pressures, blood pressure, and ECG. Anesthesia was induced with midazolam, propofol, and alfentanil, and ventilated on 40% FiO₂ with airway pressures and vital signs within normal limits (ABP 113/68 mmHg; SaO₂ 99%; heart rate 75/min; EtCO₂ 34 mmHg; Pplato 10 mbar; Ppeak 12 mbar).  
Due to a high follicle count (>30), an intravenous infusion of diclofenac sodium (75 mg) was started to prolong postoperative analgesia. Within 1–2 minutes, the patient developed a heart rate >160/min, blood pressure <70/40 mmHg, and frequent premature supraventricular and ventricular contractions. SaO₂ fell to 68% and EtCO₂ to 19 mmHg. There was pronounced oral and nasal secretions, bilateral rhonchi on auscultation, and an increase in Ppeak to 24 mbar and Pplato to 20 mbar. No erythema or urticaria was seen, but lip, periorbital, eyelid, and facial edema developed.  
The diclofenac infusion was stopped, and two doses of intramuscular adrenaline (0.5 mg each) were given. Crystalloid fluids, methylprednisolone, chlorpyramine, and aminophylline were administered intravenously, and the patient was ventilated with 100% oxygen. Airway pressures normalized, and spontaneous breathing returned. Consciousness was regained after 60 minutes. During resuscitation, blood was drawn for serum troponins, D-dimer, and tryptase measurement.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 49-year-old man was referred to rheumatology for preoperative evaluation before cervical decompression surgery. He reported paresthesia and radiating pain in all four extremities. He had a long history of pain and intermittent swelling in his knees since his teenage years, originally diagnosed as juvenile idiopathic arthritis, and at age 19 underwent bilateral total hip replacement. Since age 43, he had been treated with methotrexate and other DMARDs for presumed rheumatoid arthritis. 

On examination, his height was 164 cm, and he ambulated with crutches owing to radicular pain and knee stiffness. There were bony swellings of the bilateral metacarpophalangeal, proximal interphalangeal, and distal interphalangeal joints. Range of motion was limited in the cervical spine, shoulders, elbows, and wrists.

Laboratory studies revealed a normal complete blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, C-reactive protein level, rheumatoid factor, anti–cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody, and antinuclear antibody. 

Lumbar spine radiographs showed flattened vertebral bodies with biconcave deformities. Hand and knee radiographs demonstrated extensive osteoarthritic changes, including joint space narrowing, osteophytes, and loose bodies, along with widened epiphyses of the phalanges and periarticular osteoporosis.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 67-year-old Saudi man, retired elementary school teacher, with hypertension and longstanding benign prostatic hypertrophy, was referred to a psychosomatic service for evaluation of chronic anxiety. Three years earlier, he began repetitive checking behaviors—specifically, re-examining his underwear for residual urine—which interfered with his prayer duties. He developed progressively intrusive worries about religious rulings and family inheritance, accompanied by continuous fear and sleep disturbance that evolved from intermittent awakenings to persistent insomnia. His sons noted a diurnal pattern: severe anxiety with palpitations or occasional numbness at dawn, failure to return to sleep, gradually worsening fatigue by sunset, and appreciable relief each evening. There were no identifiable triggers, no specific phobias, flashbacks, avoidant behaviors, or irritability, and mood was not persistently low. Appetite was normal until the last few weeks. He denied suicidal ideation and had no personal or family psychiatric history. Premorbidly, he was an introverted but religiously observant man. He also described chronic sensations of “chest heat,” attributed by his family to anxiety. Over this period, he was seen by several psychiatrists and received sequential trials of paroxetine, escitalopram, mirtazapine, fluvoxamine, and sertraline at therapeutic doses; there was no meaningful clinical response. Because of his persistent, cyclically worsening anxiety, he was referred to a tertiary center and electively admitted for further evaluation.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old man, a long-term smoker, presented with a one-month history of marked pain and swelling on the left side of his neck, radiating to the left ear and sternum. He denied fever and dyspnoea. Three weeks earlier, he had been diagnosed with subacute thyroiditis by an outpatient specialist, based solely on neck pain and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 84 mm/h, and was treated with a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug without improvement. 

On examination, there was a hard, tender 6 cm mass in the left thyroid region. Laboratory tests showed ESR 77 mm/h, white blood count 8370 cells/µl, C-reactive protein 5.04 mg/dl, thyroid-stimulating hormone 1.31 mIU/l, free thyroxine 1.20 ng/dl, free triiodothyronine 3.33 pg/ml, anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies 21.49 IU/l, thyroglobulin antibodies 395 IU/l, and TSH-receptor antibodies < 0.3 IU/l.

Neck ultrasound revealed an unchanged right lobe and a markedly enlarged left lobe (44 × 61 × 100 mm) almost entirely occupied by a heterogeneous mass (44 × 55 × 70 mm) with numerous calcifications, marked reduction of vascular flow, and invasion into adjacent neck muscles. No pathological lymph nodes were seen.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 42-year-old previously healthy woman presented with a 4-day history of blurring of vision and visual loss in her left eye, associated with mild ocular pain. She denied systemic symptoms, drug use, and had no significant family, medical, or ocular history, except for a type-A behavioral pattern. She had been diagnosed elsewhere with presumed unilateral Vogt–Koyanagi–Harada disease and referred for further evaluation prior to treatment.

On examination, best-corrected visual acuity was 20/20 in the right eye and 20/40 in the left. Cycloplegic refraction was +0.50 diopters in the right eye and −1.00 diopters in the left. Intraocular pressures were normal bilaterally. Slit-lamp biomicroscopy showed a clinically shallow anterior chamber in the left eye and +1 cells in the anterior vitreous; there was no anterior chamber inflammation. Funduscopic examination of the left eye revealed large areas of neurosensory retinal elevation consistent with subretinal fluid involving the peripapillary region and extending beyond the posterior pole; the right eye fundus was unremarkable.

Spectral-domain optical coherence tomography of the left eye confirmed serous detachment of the neurosensory retina in the macular, peripapillary, and juxtapapillary regions. Optical biometry demonstrated that the anterior chamber depth was 0.62 mm shallower in the left eye than in the right, and axial length was 0.16 mm shorter in the left eye.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 77-year-old woman presented with 2 weeks of progressively worsening oral tongue pain, ulceration, and odynophagia leading to dehydration. She reported that her tongue turned blue before ulceration. She denied fevers, chills, malaise, scalp or jaw tenderness, headaches, or vision changes. Initial outpatient treatment with trimethoprim–sulfamethoxazole, then amoxicillin–clavulanate, followed by acyclovir and fluconazole, did not improve her symptoms. On examination, she had dry mucous membranes and a well-demarcated area of necrosis involving the anterior one-third to one-half of the tongue, which was tender to palpation. Laboratory testing showed a leukocyte count of 22 000/µL, a platelet count of 716 000/µL, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 65 mm/h. A CT angiogram of the head and neck revealed an undulated-beaded appearance of the distal internal carotid arteries and vertebral arteries bilaterally. A biopsy of the tongue demonstrated marked acute inflammation and granulation tissue with focal loss of the squamous mucosa; staining showed no fungal elements, and immunohistochemistry showed no cytomegalovirus, herpes simplex virus, or varicella zoster virus.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 78‐year‐old woman with multiple medical comorbidities presented with one week of progressive confusion and sleepiness. Vital signs were normal. Examination revealed a somnolent elderly female oriented only to person, with a low‐pitched coarse voice and delayed relaxation of reflexes. An admission medication history was obtained from the health system’s electronic record and then verified, dose by dose, with her daughter, who managed fourteen home medications, including levothyroxine, with no missed doses reported in the past month. Laboratory studies revealed a thyroid‐stimulating hormone concentration of 189 mU/L (normal 0.4–4.2 mU/L) and a free T4 concentration of 0.4 mg/dL (normal 4.5–11.2 mg/dL), confirming severe, symptomatic hypothyroidism. The patient was promptly treated with intravenous levothyroxine due to concerns of possible compromised oral levothyroxine absorption. Further testing, involving a levothyroxine absorption test requiring up to nine blood draws as well as brain imaging to evaluate for an intracranial etiology, was considered to determine the underlying cause of the hypothyroidism in the face of reported levothyroxine adherence.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 31-year-old non-smoking woman presented with 5 months of dull upper back pain radiating to both shoulders. The pain worsened when upright and improved when supine. She had no history of asbestos exposure. Physical examination and routine laboratory tests were unremarkable.

Chest CT showed nodular pleural thickening on the right, including a 1.5 cm extrapleural mass in the apicoposterior segment, multiple subpleural nodules in the right middle and lower lobes, and lytic lesions in the T5 and T12 vertebral bodies. A bone scan demonstrated focal uptake at T5 and a linear uptake in the left seventh anterior rib.

Thoracoscopic wedge resection of the right lower lobe revealed multiple subpleural whitish-tan plaques up to 1.2 cm in diameter. Histologically, the tumor arose from the pleura with intraparenchymal extension, composed of epithelioid cells with rounded nuclei, scant cytoplasm, intracytoplasmic vacuoles, a hyalinized stroma, few mitoses, and areas of hemorrhagic necrosis; margins were clear.

Immunohistochemistry showed the tumor cells were negative for cytokeratin, calretinin, desmin, and S-100 protein; diffusely positive for α-smooth muscle actin and vimentin; weakly positive for factor VIII and CD31; and focally positive for CD34.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 36-year-old man presented with a one-month history of worsening nasal bridge pain and intermittent fever. A primary care physician prescribed oral faropenem for presumed infection without improvement. His medical history included a childhood small intestinal obstruction requiring surgery. 

On examination, he was in moderate distress. Vital signs were: temperature 36.4°C; blood pressure 112/60 mmHg; heart rate 64 beats/min; respiratory rate 14 breaths/min; oxygen saturation 98% on room air. He had tenderness over the nasal bridge without external deformity. 

Laboratory studies showed a white blood cell count of 15 500/μL, hemoglobin 11.7 g/dL, alanine aminotransferase 53 U/L, C-reactive protein 16.4 mg/dL, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 70 mm/h. Computed tomography of the paranasal sinuses revealed thickening of the left frontal sinus mucosa. A tentative diagnosis of acute sinusitis was made, and he received oral amoxicillin–clavulanate for 10 days without improvement. 

His nasal pain worsened, and he was admitted for further evaluation. After admission, he developed generalized joint pain and severe diarrhea. Repeat sinus CT showed resolving mucosal thickening but edema and inflammation of the nasal septum and adjacent cartilage, findings that were confirmed on contrast-enhanced magnetic resonance imaging. All serological tests, including rheumatoid factor and antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies, were negative.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 12-year-old girl was referred for evaluation of a painless swelling in the left mandibular primary molar region of 6 months’ duration. She had undergone incomplete endodontic treatment of that tooth one year earlier. Her general health and oral hygiene were good. 

On extraoral examination, there was a diffuse, bony, non-tender swelling of the left side of the mandible, extending from the corner of the mouth to the angle of the jaw. Intraorally, the left primary second molar was grossly carious and mobile.

A panoramic radiograph showed a well-corticated, smooth-bordered, unilocular radiolucent lesion measuring approximately 1.8 × 2.0 cm beneath the left primary second molar, with apical displacement of the permanent second premolar. An occlusal radiograph demonstrated buccal cortical plate expansion from the distal aspect of the mandibular left lateral incisor to the mesial aspect of the mandibular left second molar, and the permanent second premolar was displaced buccolingually within the lesion.

From this information, a provisional diagnosis of a radicular cyst of the left primary second molar was made. Differential diagnoses included periapical granuloma and dentigerous cyst.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 75-year-old man from a nursing home was brought to the emergency department after a fall with a head laceration. Three weeks earlier, he had been hospitalized for a traumatic right-sided parietal skull impression fracture after a fall with transient loss of consciousness; hyponatremia was noted then but untreated. On the current presentation, he reported dizziness without syncope, chest pain, dyspnea, gastrointestinal symptoms, or fever. His history included hypertension (on candesartan) and type 2 diabetes (on metformin); he denied diuretic use and had no known chronic kidney disease.

On examination, blood pressure was 98/50 mmHg, heart rate 82/min, Glasgow Coma Scale 15, and there were no focal neurologic deficits. He had a fresh right-temple laceration requiring sutures. Mucous membranes were dry, skin turgor reduced, and there was no peripheral edema or jugular venous distention; lungs were clear and the remainder of the exam was unremarkable.

Laboratory studies on admission showed serum sodium 118 meq/L, potassium 3.6 meq/L, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen 7 mg/dL, glucose 101 mg/dL, and measured osmolality 226 mosm/kg; thyroid-stimulating hormone was normal. Ten hours after admission, urine sodium was 83 meq/L and urine osmolality 518 mosm/kg. One day later, serum uric acid was 2.6 mg/dL.

Noncontrast CT of the head revealed no acute hemorrhage or edema, but a new right frontal hygroma and a stable posterior fossa Dandy-Walker cyst; the known parietal impression fracture was unchanged. On hospital day 6, with serum sodium at 129 meq/L, an orthostatic test showed supine blood pressure 123/71 mmHg with pulse 83/min, dropping to 75/52 mmHg with pulse 100/min after 2 minutes of standing; the patient became dizzy and the test was stopped. Immediately on returning to supine, vitals returned to 115/75 mmHg and 90/min.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

options.D: Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion

question:
A 63-year-old man presented with a 2-week history of right-sided chest pain that had gradually worsened. He reported mild chest congestion with a mild cough but denied haemoptysis, dyspnoea, or fever. On presentation, his arterial oxygen tension was 84 mmHg and oxygen saturation was 95% on room air. Electrocardiography showed a left anterior fascicular block. A noncontrast CT of the chest demonstrated a suspicious filling defect in the right pulmonary artery. Computed tomographic pulmonary angiography (CTPA) revealed filling defects in the right pulmonary artery and its upper branch, with possible focal pulmonary infarction. Laboratory studies showed a D-dimer level of 0.49 mg/L (reference range 0.02–0.80 mg/L), with normal complete blood count, hepatic and renal function tests, serum electrolytes, cardiac biomarkers, autoantibodies, and coagulation parameters; there was no evidence of internal bleeding. The patient was treated with low-molecular-weight heparin for 2 weeks. On transfer to the respiratory department, the D-dimer level remained normal. Autoantibody testing, tumour markers, mycological examination, and T-spot were all normal. Doppler ultrasound of the lower extremities showed no deep vein thrombosis. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated an ejection fraction of 66%, normal chamber sizes, normal pulmonary-artery pressures, and no visible thrombus in the main or branch pulmonary arteries. Considering these atypical clinical and imaging features, (18)F-PET/CT was suggested.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 4-year-old boy was evaluated for 6 months of diarrhea and intermittent abdominal pain, accompanied for 1 month by fatigue and night sweats. He denied bleeding, steatorrhea, weight loss, fevers, vomiting, or rashes. Family history was notable only for a maternal uncle with idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura.  
On examination, he was tachycardic, pale, and had a right submandibular lymph node (1.5 × 1 cm). Abdominal examination was unremarkable. Growth charts showed a drop in weight z-score from 0.6 to –0.03 over 6 months.  
Laboratory studies revealed:  
• Absolute neutrophil count 100/µL  
• Hemoglobin 8.4 g/dL, MCV 82 fL, reticulocyte percentage 0.1%  
• Platelets 140,000/µL  
• ESR 63 mm/h, CRP 4.4 mg/dL, albumin 2.8 g/dL  
• Stool hemoccult positive; stool cultures and enteric pathogen panels negative  
A CT scan of the abdomen showed mild terminal ileal inflammation. Simultaneous esophagogastroduodenoscopy and colonoscopy revealed mucosal congestion and edema in the duodenum, with a normal terminal ileum and colon. Bone marrow biopsy demonstrated hypocellular marrow without evidence of malignancy or megaloblastic changes. Serum iron, ferritin, vitamin B12, folate, homocysteine, methylmalonic acid, copper, and zinc levels were normal, as were immunoglobulin A, M, and G levels.  
A tissue transglutaminase IgA assay and duodenal biopsy results are pending.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 33‐year‐old woman, gravida 5 para 4, with a history of attention deficit/hyperactivity disorder, treated hypothyroidism (discontinued in 2005), and previous bilateral carpal tunnel release, presented with many years of bilateral leg pain that worsened with activity and caused nocturnal cramps. She was unable to sleep more than 3 hours at a time without walking to relieve cramping of the first toes into dorsiflexion. At 30 weeks of a pregnancy due on March 23, 2008, her body mass index was 24.1 (68 kg, 168 cm). She rated her leg pain at 120% on a visual‐analogue scale and completed a pain drawing showing diffuse “toothache‐like” pain and radiating lines above the knees.

On examination, palpation elicited excessive tenderness in all muscle compartments of both legs, maximal laterally, with increased tenderness after a 3-minute exertional step test. Arterial pulses were normal in the groins, ankles, and feet, and there were no signs of venous insufficiency. Lumbar CT and MRI were normal. Neurography showed normal nerve conduction. Skin sensitivity was reduced bilaterally in the distributions of the deep fibular, superficial fibular, and sural nerves, with normal sensitivity above the knees. A neurologist excluded common neuropathies, and a physiatrist proposed a tentative diagnosis of bilateral chronic compartment syndrome.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 27-year-old primigravida at 36 weeks and 6 days’ gestation presented three hours after an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal delivery following a second stage of labor lasting 2 hours and 22 minutes. She complained of constant central chest pain and shortness of breath on standing. Her heart rate was 120–145 beats per minute; blood pressure was 130/75 mmHg; respiratory rate 18 breaths per minute; oxygen saturation 98% on room air; she was afebrile. On examination, subcutaneous emphysema was palpable over both clavicles and into her neck; chest auscultation revealed good bilateral air entry. An electrocardiogram showed sinus tachycardia. A venous blood gas sample showed pH 7.416, pO₂ 47 mmHg, lactate 4.59 mmol/L, and HCO₃⁻ 19.6 mmol/L. A chest X-ray demonstrated pneumomediastinum with subcutaneous emphysema at the base of the neck and no visible pneumothorax. A computed tomography pulmonary angiogram excluded pulmonary embolism, showed pneumomediastinum and subcutaneous emphysema within the neck and right chest wall, and revealed no pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 13-year-old boy presented with a 2-year history of multiple depressed skin lesions on his face. The lesions began as asymptomatic erythematous patches and gradually developed depressed centers. He denied any history of trauma, ulceration, fever, cough, breathlessness, sensory loss, or intralesional steroid injections. Previously, he had been diagnosed with lupus vulgaris elsewhere and received 6 months of antituberculous therapy without improvement; the lesions progressed during treatment. 

On examination, there were multiple well-defined erythematous plaques with slight atrophy on the face. A full neurological examination revealed intact sensation and no palpable peripheral nerve enlargement. Routine blood and urine tests, serum biochemistry, and antinuclear antibody testing were within normal limits. A purified protein derivative test was negative. A posterior–anterior chest radiograph showed bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. 

A skin biopsy demonstrated numerous compact epithelioid granulomas in the dermis surrounded by a sparse lymphocytic collar, with no evidence of acid-fast bacilli or fungi and no polarizable foreign material within the granulomas.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 54-year-old woman with a history of chronic kidney disease (baseline eGFR 50 mL/min/1.73 m2) and longstanding hypertension was referred for acute-onset bilateral eye pain and photophobia for two weeks. She had no history of ocular surgery and was a former contact lens user.  

One month earlier, one week after her first dose of the Pfizer COVID-19 vaccine, she was hospitalized for fever, malaise, and new-onset lower extremity edema. Her serum creatinine increased from a baseline of 1.4 mg/dL to 2.8 mg/dL, and 24-hour urine protein was 5.2 g. Initial laboratory evaluation (HIV, HBV, PLA2 antibody, ANA, dsDNA, C3, and C4) was negative. A kidney biopsy showed severe active collapsing glomerulopathy with moderate to advanced focal and segmental glomerulosclerosis and acute granulomatous interstitial nephritis. Genetic testing revealed two high-risk APOL1 alleles (G1/G1) and a homozygous FAN1 variant. She was started on oral prednisone 40 mg daily.  

On ophthalmologic examination, visual acuity was 20/25 in each eye; intraocular pressure was 10 mmHg OD and 15 mmHg OS. Slit-lamp examination showed 1+ anterior chamber cell OD and 0.5+ cell OS; there was no vitritis or retinal inflammation.  

A uveitis workup was sent, and the following results were obtained: serum RPR, FTA-antibody, quantiferon gold, Lyme Western blot, HLA-B27, ANCA, double-stranded DNA antibody, HIV, and hepatitis titers were all negative. Liver enzymes were normal. Complete blood count showed hemoglobin 10.6 g/dL and a leukocytosis with left shift. BUN was 37 mg/dL, creatinine 2.36 mg/dL (eGFR 23 mL/min/1.73 m2), ESR 51 mm/h, and serum lysozyme 13.3 µg/mL.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 21-year-old woman presented with a 12-hour history of sharp, central chest pain that awoke her from sleep and worsened to 9/10 in severity. The pain was associated with brief dyspnea on minimal exertion and three witnessed syncopal episodes. Two weeks earlier she had a viral prodrome with generalized malaise and a 30-minute episode of similar chest discomfort that resolved spontaneously. Her past medical history was significant for clinically diagnosed Cutis Laxa since age 17. She had a family history of maternal diabetes and hypertension, was an active smoker (1 pack-year), and took no regular medications.

On examination, her heart rate was 52 beats per minute, blood pressure 101/60 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. The physical examination was unremarkable. ECG showed sinus rhythm without ischemic changes. D-dimer was normal. Initial troponin T was mildly elevated at 17 ng/l (<14 ng/l), rising to 33 ng/l after 2 hours.

She underwent an ECG-gated CT angiogram of the aorta, which demonstrated no aortic dissection or central pulmonary embolus. Transthoracic echocardiography showed normal left and right ventricular systolic function without regional wall-motion abnormalities. Her chest pain resolved over 2 hours, and there were no ECG changes thereafter. She was admitted for observation on telemetry. On hospital day 2, repeat troponin T was significantly elevated at 731 ng/l, although she remained asymptomatic.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 7-year-old girl presented with involuntary movements of her upper limbs that were triggered by running. She was born full term, with normal developmental milestones and a history of febrile seizures at age 1 year. There was no family history of endocrine disorders or epilepsy. At age 7 years 2 months, she exhibited involuntary lifting of the left hand, dropping of the right hand behind her back, and flexion of both wrists with running. By 7 years 4 months, these movements increased in frequency, and jerking of the lower extremities occurred upon walking, without alteration of consciousness. An interictal electroencephalogram was normal. On the basis of these findings, carbamazepine was started, and the dyskinesia resolved.

Two months later, she was hospitalized with fever (38.6 °C) and a 3-minute generalized tonic convulsion. On examination, she was 114 cm tall (–1.4 SD), weighed 19.8 kg (–0.8 SD), and had no features of Albright’s hereditary osteodystrophy. Blood pressure was 109/73 mmHg, heart rate 127 beats/min, and SpO2 96% on room air. Laboratory studies showed sodium, glucose, and renal function within normal limits. Serum calcium was 5.5 mg/dL (normal 8.7–10.2), phosphorus 7.6 mg/dL (3.9–5.8), intact parathyroid hormone 343.1 pg/mL (10–65), 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D3 74.3 pg/mL (20–60), and 25-hydroxyvitamin D 13.5 ng/mL (>20). Thyroid function was normal. Brain CT revealed multiple bilateral calcifications in the basal ganglia and frontal cortex. Ophthalmological examination showed cataracts, and electrocardiography demonstrated QT prolongation with QTc 0.496 msec.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A woman in her 50s with a 7-year history of methotrexate therapy (8 mg weekly) for rheumatoid arthritis underwent an annual health check and was found to have abnormal chest imaging. She reported no respiratory symptoms, no fever or cough, had never smoked, and had no known exposure to pigeons.

On chest radiography, there were infiltrative shadows in the right lower lung field. Noncontrast chest CT showed two small nodules and two areas of pulmonary infiltration in the right lower lobe; there was no pleural effusion or lymphadenopathy.

Laboratory studies revealed:
• Leukocyte count 4.9 × 10^9/L (neutrophils 68.8%, lymphocytes 16.7%, eosinophils 5.1%)  
• C-reactive protein and glycosylated hemoglobin within normal limits  
• Tumor markers (CEA, SCC antigen, CYFRA 21-1, SLX, pro-GRP, NSE) within normal ranges  
• KL-6 237 U/mL  
• Interferon-γ release assay (T-SPOT.TB) negative  
• HIV antibody negative  
• Rheumatoid factor < 5 IU/mL; PR3-ANCA < 1.0; MPO-ANCA < 1.0; anti-ARS < 5.0; anti-SSA and anti-SSB negative; ANA titer 1:40  
• Soluble IL-2 receptor 547 U/mL  
• (1,3)-β-D-glucan 34.6 pg/mL (upper limit of normal 20 pg/mL)  
• Aspergillus antigen 0.3; Candida antigen < 0.02 U/mL

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old woman with juvenile myoclonic epilepsy presented with a neck mass. Her family history included a paternal abdominal aortic aneurysm, a son with tall stature and pulmonary blebs, a daughter with hypoplastic right heart syndrome, a paternal grandmother with a ruptured aortic aneurysm, and easy bruising in multiple relatives. 

Computed tomographic angiography (CTA) performed to evaluate the neck mass incidentally revealed a 45 mm ectatic aortic root. She was referred for aortic root replacement and underwent left heart catheterization, which showed an ostial left main lesion with a post-stenotic aneurysm just before the bifurcation of the left circumflex and left anterior descending arteries.

Several months later, she presented with one week of dyspnoea. Vital signs were a heart rate of 53 bpm, blood pressure of 99/64 mmHg, respiratory rate of 12 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation of 100% on room air. On examination, she was in mild distress. The oropharynx revealed a high palate; cardiac and pulmonary examinations were unremarkable. Musculoskeletal examination demonstrated pectus excavatum, positive thumb and pinky signs bilaterally, a positive knee sign bilaterally, and hyperflexibility with a Beighton score of 7.

Concerned for an anginal equivalent or progression of aortic disease with potential dissection, an echocardiogram was obtained and was interpreted as showing a dissection flap within the aortic root, consistent with Type A aortic dissection. Emergent CTA of the chest showed no dissection but confirmed an ectatic aortic root measuring 45 mm and a distal left main coronary aneurysm measuring up to 11 mm just before the bifurcation.

Laboratory studies revealed a C-reactive protein level of 3.15 mg/L (reference 0.0–10.9 mg/L), an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 8 mm/hr (reference 0–20 mm/hr), homocysteine 13.7 µmol/L (reference 5.0–15.0 µmol/L), and nonreactive treponemal, hepatitis C, and HIV antibodies.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 19-year-old woman presented with a two-week history of worsening scleral icterus, abdominal distension, and fatigue. Her only medical history was acne vulgaris treated recently with minocycline. She denied high-risk behaviors and had no family history of liver disease.

On examination, she was afebrile; blood pressure was 119/79 mmHg, heart rate 105 beats per minute, respiratory rate 20 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. She was alert and oriented. Physical findings included icteric sclera, a mildly distended abdomen without shifting dullness, and 1+ pitting edema of the lower extremities.

Initial laboratory studies revealed:
• Mild elevations in alanine aminotransferase and aspartate aminotransferase  
• Increased total bilirubin  
• Hypoalbuminemia  
• Elevated international normalized ratio  

Hemolysis laboratories (haptoglobin, direct Coombs test, and lactate dehydrogenase) were within normal limits. An abdominal ultrasound showed mild ascites but was otherwise unremarkable.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 9-year-old boy with a history of frequent-relapsing nephrotic syndrome since age 2½ was admitted for a single 375 mg/m2 infusion of rituximab while in remission off steroids and on tacrolimus. The infusion was uneventful, and he was discharged. Three days later, he returned with fever, nonproductive cough, and respiratory distress. On examination, he was tachycardic and tachypneic with intercostal retractions; bilateral coarse crepitations were heard over both lung fields; abdominal tenderness was mild; perfusion was maintained, and he was alert. Initial laboratory tests showed pancytopenia (absolute neutrophil count, 927 cells/mm3), serum albumin 3.8 g/dL, creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, normal liver enzymes, C-reactive protein 10 mg/dL, and ESR 30 mm/hr. Urinalysis showed 1+ proteinuria without pyuria. Chest radiography demonstrated diffuse bilateral hazy opacities with bullous lesions in the left upper and mid zones. Intravenous ceftriaxone was started for suspected pneumonia and sepsis. After 7 days of antibiotics, his fever improved but tachypnea and retractions persisted, and repeat radiography showed persistent hazy opacities with areas of cavitation. Tuberculosis was suspected; Mantoux test and sputum CBNAAT were negative. Sputum Gram and fungal stains and cultures were negative. High-resolution CT of the chest revealed bilateral multicystic lesions with surrounding consolidation and ground-glass attenuation. Empiric antituberculous therapy and later fluconazole were administered without clinical or radiographic improvement, and all microbiologic tests remained negative.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 69-year-old woman with a history of hypothyroidism, degenerative joint disease, and restless legs syndrome presented with a 2½-year history of intermittent abdominal pain, diarrhea, fatigue, and weight loss. One year earlier, an upper endoscopy showed patchy duodenal erythema and biopsies revealed villous blunting; celiac serologies were negative. Three months later, she was hospitalized for diarrhea, abdominal pain, and dizziness. Video capsule endoscopy demonstrated small-bowel ulcerations, and an anti-Saccharomyces cerevisiae antibody (ASCA) was positive. She was treated for presumed Crohn’s disease with mesalamine and budesonide, later switched to adalimumab, without symptom improvement. Five months later, she was readmitted with weakness, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and weight loss. CT imaging revealed abdominal lymphadenopathy; a CT-guided lymph‐node biopsy showed granulomata with rare fungal‐like organisms, and she received brief antifungal and corticosteroid therapy. Her last dose of adalimumab was one month before transfer. On examination at our hospital, she was cachectic (BMI 16.2), hypotensive at 96/56 mmHg, and afebrile. The abdomen was distended with minimal tenderness and hypoactive bowel sounds. Laboratory studies showed hypoalbuminemia (1.4 g/dL) and metabolic acidosis (bicarbonate 12 mmol/L). Noncontrast CT of the abdomen revealed colonic distention with air-fluid levels and mesenteric and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. A contrast CT 19 hours later showed bulky adenopathy, diffuse small-bowel wall thickening from duodenum to terminal ileum, and pneumatosis intestinalis. Emergent laparotomy revealed intact bowel covered with a pasty exudate. A mesenteric lymph node was excised; intraoperative brushings and frozen sections demonstrated granulomatous disease. Acid-fast bacilli (AFB) and Grocott’s methenamine silver (GMS) stains on the tissue were negative for organisms. Based on her history, the infectious disease service requested a periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) stain on lymph node tissue…

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

An 8-year-old girl presented with a 1-year history of clonic seizures involving the right upper and lower limbs, each lasting 2–5 minutes, with prolonged postictal states but no loss of consciousness; she had occasional bladder incontinence. The first seizure occurred at age 7, after which seizure frequency rose to 10–15 episodes per day, and she developed slurred speech, cognitive delay, and an abnormal gait. Her perinatal history was unremarkable, with no neonatal seizures, transfusions, neurosurgery, head trauma, allergies, or family history of epilepsy.

Initial routine electroencephalography (EEG) and brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) were normal. She was treated sequentially with oxcarbazepine, valproate sodium, and clonazepam without full seizure control. One month before admission, she developed paresthesia and twitching of the right arm. She was admitted with focal status epilepticus and generalized seizures refractory to antiepileptic drugs.

On neurologic examination, there was constant twitching of the right hand, intermittent stiffening of the right leg and pelvis, and right hemiparesis (Medical Research Council grade 3 on the right, grade 5 on the left); tendon reflexes were exaggerated on the right. She had severe global developmental delay; there were no meningeal signs and cranial nerves were intact.

Cerebrospinal fluid analysis was normal, and polymerase-chain-reaction assays for herpes simplex virus, Epstein–Barr virus, measles, rubella, and cytomegalovirus were negative. EEG showed slow activity with focal left frontal biphasic spikes and paroxysmal discharges. MRI of the brain revealed left hemispheric atrophy, including atrophy of the left insular cortex on T1-weighted images and atrophy of the left caudate and putamen nuclei on T2-weighted images.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 57-year-old woman presented with a several-day history of cough and fever up to 38 °C. Over the same period, she noted changing sputum color, back pain, limb weakness, dysphagia, hemoptysis, wheezing, chest pain, and alterations in bowel habits. She denied weight loss or facial changes. Her past medical and family histories were unremarkable. Vital signs were normal. Examination revealed clear lungs, normal heart sounds, and no lymphadenopathy or organomegaly.

Chest CT showed a 5.5 cm × 5 cm × 1.8 cm solid mass in the anterior mediastinum with adhesion to but no definite invasion of the pericardium (Masaoka IIb). The mass was completely resected thoracoscopically. Grossly, it was encapsulated, grayish-white to grayish-brown, and had an ill-defined border against surrounding adipose tissue. 

Microscopic examination of hematoxylin–eosin–stained sections revealed multiple epithelial micronodules separated by abundant lymphoid stroma forming lymphoid follicles. No necrosis was seen. Tumor cells infiltrated surrounding adipose tissue at the periphery. Residual thymic tissue lay adjacent to the lesion. The neoplastic epithelial cells exhibited marked pleomorphism with visible nucleoli and mitotic figures ranging from 1 to 9 per 10 high-power fields.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

options.C: Micronodular thymic carcinoma with lymphoid hyperplasia

question:
A 13-year-old white female was referred for evaluation of right ear and mastoid pain of 16 months’ duration. She described a constant, dull, throbbing ache rated 6–7/10 on a numeric scale, with 8–10 daily paroxysms of sharp pain rated 9/10, each lasting 1–2 minutes and awakening her once or twice nightly. No specific triggers or relieving factors were identified.

Prior workup by two otolaryngologists, a neurologist, her primary care physician, and a dentist included a brain MRI with and without contrast, a head and neck CT, and panoramic and dental periapical radiographs; all studies were reported as normal. She had been treated empirically for presumed ear infection with NSAIDs and multiple courses of oral antibiotics, and underwent placement of a tympanostomy tube without any pain relief.

Her medical history included asthma, seasonal allergies, and chronic migraine without aura managed with topiramate and other medications. She denied mood or substance-use issues.

On examination, cranial nerves II–XII were intact. Gentle cutaneous stimulation of the posterior wall of the right auditory canal elicited a familiar, sharp 9/10 pain that subsided after about 1 minute, returning to her baseline dull ache. The tympanostomy tube was in place without signs of inflammation. Palpation of the right trapezius, paracervical, and temporalis tendon was tender but did not reproduce her ear pain. A diagnostic anesthetic block of the right temporalis tendon relieved local tenderness but did not alter the ear pain. A repeat brain MRI with and without contrast was then obtained.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 27-year-old Afro-Caribbean man with a 5-year history of ulcerative colitis was admitted with a 1-week history of intermittent stabbing chest pain radiating to the neck and left arm, which worsened over the previous 24 hours. He reported no dyspnea or pleuritic component. Two and a half weeks earlier, during a flare of bloody diarrhea and malaise, his therapy was changed from steroids to azathioprine after a normal thiopurine‐methyltransferase assay; he was also taking mesalazine. He denied smoking, hypertension, dyslipidemia, diabetes, illicit drug use, and had no family history of coronary artery disease.

On examination he was afebrile; blood pressure 110/70 mm Hg; heart rate 80 bpm; respiratory rate 18 breaths/min; oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Heart sounds were normal; jugular venous pressure was not elevated; lungs were clear. The abdomen was soft with diffuse tenderness. He had reduced abduction of the right hip consistent with sacroiliitis; no rashes were present.

Electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm at 80 bpm with pathological Q waves in the inferior leads and tall R waves in leads V1–V4. Troponin I was elevated at >50.00 mg/mL. Laboratory studies revealed anemia (hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL), thrombocytosis (465×10^9/L), hypoalbuminemia (31 g/L), and a marginal eosinophilia (1.1×10^9/L). Blood cultures were negative. Serologies were negative for Echo, Coxsackie, EBV, CMV, and streptococcal infection. Rheumatoid factor was positive (31 IU/mL); antinuclear, mitochondrial, smooth muscle, and liver microsomal antibodies were negative; complement C3 and C4 were normal. High‐titer antiproteinase-3 ANCA (10.2 U/mL) was detected; perinuclear ANCA was negative. Chest radiograph was normal; urine dipstick showed no blood or protein. Flexible sigmoidoscopy revealed mildly active distal colitis without ulceration or granulomas.

Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated preserved overall systolic function, inferolateral and anterolateral wall hypokinesia, mild mitral regurgitation, and no pericardial effusion.

Differential diagnosis at this stage included inflammatory-bowel-disease–related giant cell myocarditis, drug-induced hypersensitivity myocarditis, acute lymphocytic myocarditis, and late-presentation myocardial infarction.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

Chief complaint: 39-year-old nulligravid woman with 8 months of androgenic alopecia, facial acne, and hirsutism.

History of present illness:
 • Progressive scalp hair thinning, acne, and hirsutism (Ferriman–Gallwey score 12).  
 • Over 3 months, 6 kg weight gain in the abdomen and sporadic ecchymoses without trauma.  
 • Oligomenorrhea for 9 months after stopping oral contraceptives.  
 • No changes in libido, muscle strength, or fractures.

Medical history: primary hypothyroidism, obesity (BMI 31.8 kg/m²), hypertension.

Medications: levothyroxine, nebivolol, amlodipine/valsartan.

Family history: mother with thyroid and breast cancer, hypertension, type 2 diabetes.

Physical examination:
 • Androgenic facies, alopecia, clitoromegaly.  
 • Dorsocervical fat pad, axillary acanthosis nigricans.  
 • No abdominal masses; no breast atrophy, proximal myopathy, or striae.  
 • Weight 87.6 kg; blood pressure 140/92 mm Hg.

Laboratory studies:
 • Hemoglobin 18.2 g/dL; erythrocyte count 5.84 ×10^12/L.  
 • Total testosterone 1052 ng/dL (6–82); androstenedione 5.13 ng/mL (0.3–3.3).  
 • DHEAS 246.6 µg/dL (60.9–337).  
 • FSH 0.87 mIU/mL; LH 0.61 mIU/mL.  
 • Normal prolactin, free T4, TSH.  
 • Normal 24-h urinary free cortisol and 1 mg dexamethasone suppression test.

Imaging:
 • Transvaginal ultrasound: 15×15×16 mm well-defined solid, slightly heterogeneous left ovarian lesion; no endometrial thickening.
