 • Pelvic MRI: solid left adnexal lesion with smooth margins and moderate T2 hyperintensity; no ascites or lymphadenopathy.  
 • CT of the adrenal glands: unremarkable.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Leydig cell tumor of the ovary

question:
A 9-year-old Caucasian girl presented with a 4-month history of gradually increasing swelling of both her upper and lower lips, which was distressing to her and her parents. Prior evaluations included a pediatric dentist, periodontist, and allergist. Her history was notable for excessive thumb sucking, frequent use of nail polish, and playing with homemade slime (glue, saline, shaving cream, glitter). She had mild eczematous skin lesions, vague rhinitis with nasal congestion, occasional postnasal drainage, intermittent headaches, and conjunctival pruritus, all worse in spring. There was no relevant family history, medication use, or known allergies to foods, drugs, or cosmetics. 

Physical examination revealed a symmetrically enlarged, erythematous upper lip, erythematous and edematous maxillary anterior gingiva (especially on the right), and mild lip fissures with cheilitis. Routine blood tests—including a basic metabolic panel, complete blood count with differential, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, hepatic function panel, hepatitis A/B/C serologies, C-reactive protein, immunoglobulin panel, complement levels, and relevant autoantibodies—were all within normal limits. The patient had no clinical signs or symptoms of gastrointestinal disease.

A right upper lip incisional biopsy showed submucosal lymphohistiocytic infiltrates with loose perivascular noncaseating granulomas; adjacent salivary gland tissue exhibited lymphocytic infiltrates with occasional germinal centers. Acid-fast bacilli were not seen on a concentrated smear.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 59-year-old man with longstanding fibrostenotic Crohn’s disease presented with a one-day history of fever and abdominal pain. One week earlier, he had a surveillance colonoscopy showing progression of his disease, and azathioprine 125 mg daily was started after TPMT levels were found to be intermediate. His history included iron deficiency and benign prostatic hyperplasia; he had no known allergies. Current medications were azathioprine 50 mg daily, budesonide, tamsulosin, and ferrous gluconate.

On admission, he was febrile, tachycardic, and hypotensive; intravenous fluids were given. A working diagnosis of sepsis led to empiric ceftriaxone. Chest radiograph, blood and urine cultures, and CT of the abdomen and pelvis revealed no source of infection. Azathioprine was held. Over the next three days, he remained febrile with persistent tachycardia, progressed to distributive shock and acute renal failure, and required piperacillin-tazobactam, vancomycin, stress-dose methylprednisolone, and norepinephrine.

On hospital day 4, he developed an acute erythematous rash with pustules on the torso and extremities. A skin punch biopsy was obtained. With clinical improvement and reduced concern for infection, azathioprine was restarted on ICU day 7. Twelve hours after a single re-challenge dose, he redeveloped vasodilatory shock and a decline in consciousness requiring intubation, fluid resuscitation, and vasopressors. ECG showed transient ST elevations in the inferior and septal leads, and high-sensitivity troponin T was 1290 ng/L (upper reference limit, 14 ng/L). Urgent cardiac catheterization revealed no obstructive coronary artery disease.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 72-year-old man with a history of retroperitoneal fibrosis presented with a 1-week history of swelling and pain in his left lower extremity. Magnetic resonance angiography demonstrated occlusion of the left common iliac vein. Laboratory studies showed a C-reactive protein level of 3.37 mg/dL and a D-dimer level of 3.50 µg/mL. Plain computed tomography revealed a soft-tissue mass encasing the terminal abdominal aorta. Unexpectedly, noncontrast CT also demonstrated increased wall thickness of the ascending aorta. Contrast-enhanced CT confirmed a circular high-attenuation lesion in the ascending aorta, approximately 5.3 cm in maximum diameter with a wall thickness of 15 mm; no entry tear or intimal flap was identified. The patient was afebrile, with normal blood pressure and heart rate, and denied any chest or back pain. Transthoracic ultrasound showed no intimal flap or aortic valve abnormality. Given the absence of typical features of aortic dissection and his history of retroperitoneal fibrosis, the team elected to observe the ascending aortic findings without surgical intervention.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 26-year-old previously healthy Caucasian man presented with a 3-week history of dry cough and progressive dyspnea. He reported dizziness, retrosternal chest pain, and one febrile episode. Physical examination was notable only for hypophonetic heart sounds. Chest radiography showed an enlarged cardiac silhouette. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a large pericardial effusion without signs of tamponade. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography of the abdomen revealed multiple rounded hepatic lesions, all hypodense and hypovascular, the largest measuring 3 cm in segment IVa. Laboratory studies showed normocytic anemia and elevated inflammatory markers (erythrocyte sedimentation rate, 95 mm/h; C-reactive protein, 92 mg/L); liver and thyroid function tests were normal. Serologic tests for hepatitis B and C viruses and HIV were negative; rheumatoid factor and antinuclear antibody tests were also negative. An ultrasound-guided percutaneous liver biopsy showed lobular necrosis and perisinusoidal fibrosis without granulomas or neoplastic cells. During hospitalization he developed fever, night sweats, and weight loss, and was started on empiric therapy for extrapulmonary tuberculosis with corticosteroids. Two months later, pericardial effusion had resolved but liver lesions persisted. He was readmitted in hemorrhagic shock due to bleeding from the hepatic lesions, underwent unsuccessful embolization of the right hepatic artery, and died shortly thereafter.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man (160 cm, 65 kg) with hepatitis B–related cirrhosis and previous esophageal varices underwent laparoscopic subsegmental resection of segment 7 for hepatocellular carcinoma under combined general and epidural anesthesia. His postoperative course was complicated by refractory abdominal pain, managed with patient-controlled epidural, morphine infusion, celecoxib, loxoprofen, and tramadol. He was discharged on postoperative day 10 but readmitted for a wound infection, which resolved with antibiotics. On postoperative day 15, he was referred to the pain clinic for severe recurrent upper abdominal pain unrelieved by tramadol and loxoprofen.

At the first pain clinic visit, his Numerical Rating Scale score was 2 at rest and 8 with movement. He described constant aching with posture-induced paroxysms in the right epigastric region. Rotation and lateral bending provoked severe pain, yielding a positive Carnett’s sign. Examination revealed a hypersensitive tender spot with underlying muscle stiffness forming a taut band in the right lateral subcostal area; palpation reproduced radiating pain to the epigastrium. Clinical criteria for a myofascial trigger point were met.

After initial relief with a blind mepivacaine injection into the tender spot, pain recurred 19 days after the first visit. We localized the tender area approximately 5 cm from the nearest port site on the right upper abdomen and performed B-mode ultrasonography with an 11–3 MHz linear probe. Imaging demonstrated a 5 mm diameter hyperechoic spot surrounded by a hypoechoic area within the internal oblique abdominal muscle. The surrounding muscle fibers were continuous with no loss or abrupt discontinuation as seen at port sites, and the echogenic focus lay within the muscle fibers rather than along a vertical nerve path.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 77-year-old woman presented with a 1-month history of a left breast lump. She had a history of heart disease, treated for 4 years. On examination, there was a 3 × 2 cm firm, mobile, non-tender mass in the outer quadrant of the left breast; the skin, nipple, and areola were unremarkable. There was no axillary or cervical lymphadenopathy; the right breast was normal. Mammography showed a 2 × 2 cm ill-defined mass with irregular margins in the left upper outer quadrant and no microcalcifications; the right breast was normal. Fine-needle aspiration cytology was inconclusive. She underwent a lumpectomy with frozen-section analysis.

On gross examination, the specimen revealed a firm, grey-white, fibrous, unencapsulated nodular tumor measuring 2 × 1.2 × 0.8 cm with infiltrative borders; the closest margin was 0.5 cm from the base. Frozen sections showed a spindle cell proliferation with mild atypia in a sclerotic stroma infiltrating adjacent fat; a low-grade sarcoma was favored, and no axillary staging was performed at that time.

Permanent sections demonstrated an infiltrative spindle cell tumor with prominent sclerosis reminiscent of keloid formation. The cells were arranged in fascicles with tapering nuclei, mild anisonucleosis, and inconspicuous mitoses. Occasional plump epithelioid cells formed small clusters. Interspersed foci of benign ductal hyperplasia and papillary hyperplasia, including a micropapilloma without atypia, were noted. No squamous differentiation or ductal carcinoma in situ was seen.

Immunohistochemistry revealed tumor cells diffusely positive for cytokeratin (CK), CK7, high molecular weight cytokeratin, epithelial membrane antigen, and vimentin. They were negative for gross cystic disease fluid protein, estrogen receptor, progesterone receptor, S100, desmin, CD34, and HER-2/neu. Smooth muscle actin and p63 showed focal positivity. Ki-67 labeled fewer than 5% of tumor cells.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 49-year-old woman was brought to the emergency department with 48 hours of rapidly progressing, ascending weakness of all four extremities and increasing difficulty breathing. She had no prior similar episodes, no recent trauma, vaccinations, infections, or toxin exposures, and denied drug use. On examination, she was alert but in acute respiratory distress, using accessory muscles and receiving oxygen via face mask. Vital signs were: blood pressure 140/90 mmHg, heart rate 98 beats/min, respiratory rate 40 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 92% on supplemental oxygen. Chest auscultation was clear. Neurologically, she had hypotonia in all limbs (lower > upper), muscle strength of 1/5 in the lower limbs and 3/5 in the upper limbs, absent deep tendon reflexes, and equivocal plantar responses. Sensation and cranial nerves were normal. Her abdomen was distended with absent bowel sounds without tenderness. A provisional diagnosis of Guillain–Barré syndrome was made, and she was intubated for worsening respiratory distress. Laboratory studies revealed: serum potassium 1.72 mEq/L (arterial blood gas potassium 1.38 mEq/L), metabolic acidosis (pH 7.12, HCO3- 12.5 mmol/L, BE –15.8 mmol/L, pCO2 38.5 mmHg), sodium 149 mEq/L, calcium 8.8 mg/dL, and normal renal, liver, coagulation, and glucose profiles. Hemoglobin showed moderate anemia; ELISA tests for hepatitis B, C, and HIV were negative. ECG demonstrated ST depression and T-wave inversion.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 65-year-old woman with nonischemic cardiomyopathy (LV ejection fraction 30%), complete left bundle branch block (QRS duration 160 ms), and recurrent heart failure was referred for cardiac resynchronization therapy. Coronary angiography two years earlier had shown normal coronary arteries. A left bundle branch pacing lead (3830 SelectSecure) was advanced into the right ventricular septum 1 cm apical and inferior to the distal His region, with 4–5 rapid rotations to penetrate deep into the septum. During advancement, unipolar pacing impedance rose from 350 Ω to 700 Ω and then fell to 400 Ω. The patient then developed acute chest pain, diaphoresis, and hypotension. A 12-lead ECG showed ST-segment elevation in leads I, aVL, and V1–V3 with reciprocal ST depression in leads II, III, and aVF. The lead was immediately withdrawn and repositioned 1.5 cm posterior and apical to the initial site. Urgent coronary angiography revealed a large septal branch at the initial lead site and TIMI 3 flow in the left anterior descending artery. The ST-segment changes and symptoms resolved within 10 minutes.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

options.A: Left bundle branch block myocardial infarction pattern

A 40-year-old man was referred for evaluation of a 6-week history of exertional dyspnea and cough. He had worked in construction for 18 years, had a 23 pack-year smoking history (quit 6 weeks earlier), and a 12-year history of allergic rhinitis. He was afebrile. Vital signs: heart rate 86/min, blood pressure 137/87 mmHg, respiratory rate 27/min, oxygen saturation 91% on room air. Lung auscultation was unremarkable. Laboratory tests showed leukocyte count 3150/µL, lactate dehydrogenase 394 U/L, C-reactive protein 4.48 mg/dL, and Krebs von den Lungen-6 435.2 U/mL. Immunologic studies were normal except an antinuclear antibody titer of 1:40. Sputum and blood cultures were negative for pathogens. High-resolution CT of the chest revealed centrilobular ground-glass opacities predominantly in the right lung. Pulmonary function tests showed a mixed ventilatory impairment (VC 53.6% predicted; FEV1/FVC 64.3%). Bronchoalveolar lavage fluid cultures were negative for bacteria, viruses, and fungi. BALF analysis demonstrated a total cell count of 6.994 × 10^5/mL with 61.3% lymphocytes, 37.5% macrophages, and a CD4/CD8 ratio of 0.81; there was no alveolar hemorrhage. Transbronchial lung biopsy revealed alveolitis without malignancy, granulomas, or fibrosis.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 19-year-old man presented with a 6-year history of occasional right thigh pain and a 5-year history of right thigh weakness. He denied night pain, and his pain worsened with activity. There was no history of trauma or systemic disease. On examination, the right thigh circumference was approximately 2 cm less than that of the left thigh; hair distribution was symmetric. Hip and knee range of motion were normal. Mild tenderness was noted on palpation of the right thigh.  
Plain radiographs of the right femur showed cortical thickening in the distal posterolateral diaphyseal region.  
Magnetic resonance imaging revealed cortical thickening (approximately 9.5 mm) at the metadiaphyseal level of the distal posterolateral right femur. Adjacent to this area was an ill-defined soft-tissue lesion measuring about 18 × 12 mm that was hypointense on T1-weighted sequences, hyperintense on T2-weighted sequences, and showed marked contrast enhancement.  
Computed tomography confirmed the posterolateral cortical thickening but demonstrated no central nidus.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 53-year-old man was brought to the hospital with progressive confusion over several days and was found unconscious in his apartment. His family reported that he had congenital hypothyroidism treated with levothyroxine but had become malnourished and stopped his medication after his primary caregiver died several months earlier, with an unintended weight loss of more than 30 kg. On admission, he was somnolent with incomprehensible speech, hypothermic (33.1 °C), borderline bradycardic (62 beats/min), and hypotensive (89/52 mm Hg) but responsive to fluid resuscitation. His skin appeared pale; there was no evidence of pericardial or pleural effusions or ascites.  

Initial laboratory studies revealed hyponatremia (sodium 127 mmol/L) and pancytopenia (white blood cell count 4.3×10^3/μL, hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, platelets 23×10^3/μL). The peripheral blood smear showed normocytic, normochromic cells without macrocytosis, hypersegmentation, or schistocytes. Lactate dehydrogenase and bilirubin levels were normal, and coagulation parameters were within reference ranges. Cortisol was 10.5 μg/dL (normal). Ferritin was elevated at 2385 pg/mL, but triglycerides (134 mg/dL), fibrinogen (271 mg/dL), and spleen size on computed tomography were not consistent with hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis. Tests for COVID-19 and urine toxicology were negative. C-reactive protein was 5.8 mg/dL on admission. Chest radiograph and chest CT were unremarkable, with no signs of infection or malignancy. Head CT showed no acute intracranial pathology, and there were no meningeal signs. Vitamin B12 was 1024 pg/mL and folate was low at 1.8 ng/mL. Thyroid studies demonstrated a thyroid-stimulating hormone level of 71 μIU/mL, free thyroxine 0.23 ng/dL, and free triiodothyronine 0.96 pg/mL. An autoimmune panel and bone marrow biopsy were not performed at this time.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 46-year-old Moroccan man with a history of cocaine and alcohol abuse and a 10–pack-year smoking history, detained for 3 months, was referred after prison doctors noted severe anemia (hemoglobin 43 g/L). He reported 2 months of progressive fatigue, arthromyalgia, mild abdominal pain, upper finger paresthesia, left ear tinnitus, and recurrent headaches. He denied fever, bleeding, or bowel changes.

On examination he was afebrile (36 °C), blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, heart rate 80 bpm, and oriented. Findings included pallor, slight epigastric tenderness, and a left tympanic membrane perforation. There was no lymphadenopathy, purpura, or hepatosplenomegaly. Neurologic exam was normal except for mild finger paresthesia.

Laboratory tests showed: hemoglobin 36 g/L, MCV 87 fl, hematocrit 10.8%, RDW 27%, neutropenia (0.59×10⁹/L), and a normal platelet count (15×10⁹/L). Creatinine was 176.8 μmol/L with azotemia of 24.9 mmol/L. Peripheral smear showed marked anisopoikilocytosis and 10% schistocytes. LDH was 19.7 μkat/L, haptoglobin <1 mg/L, and total bilirubin 17.1 μmol/L. Coagulation studies were normal except for D-dimer 2.63 nmol/L. Direct Coombs test was negative. Reticulocyte production index was 0.061. Ferritin was normal.

He received intravenous saline and five units of packed red cells. Renal function improved, but anemia persisted with ongoing hemolysis (schistocytes, rising LDH, low haptoglobin). Platelet count fell to 46×10⁹/L and neutropenia persisted, without peripheral blasts.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

36-year-old gravida 2 para 1 woman at 30 weeks’ gestation presented with fever, productive cough, and central cyanosis for 2 days. Two weeks earlier, she was diagnosed with multibacillary leprosy based on hypoaesthetic annular plaques on the trunk and limbs, a positive slit-skin smear (morphological index 3.2%; bacteriological index 4.3%), and was started on clofazimine, rifampicin, and dapsone 100 mg daily. She denied headache, chest pain, dyspnea, hemoptysis, chemical exposures, or unprescribed medications. On examination, her temperature spiked once on admission; vital signs were otherwise stable without respiratory distress. Oxygen saturation by pulse oximetry was 84–88% on room air, and both radial arterial and venous blood appeared dark. Arterial blood gas on room air showed PaO₂ 111 mmHg and calculated SaO₂ 98%. Laboratory tests revealed normal hemoglobin, bilirubin, LDH, and leukocyte count. G6PD activity was normal. Chest radiograph and sputum studies for bacterial culture and acid-fast bacilli were unremarkable. Administration of 100% oxygen failed to correct the cyanosis, and repeat arterial blood gas values remained unchanged.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A previously healthy boy was noted to have mild splenomegaly at his 6-month well-child visit, which was confirmed by ultrasound.  
At 12 months of age, repeat abdominal ultrasound showed:  
• Hepatosplenomegaly with heterogeneous, hyperechoic liver parenchyma  
• Mildly elevated hepatic arterial resistive index of 0.8  
Laboratory studies revealed pancytopenia:  
• White blood cell count 3.91 × 10^3/mm^3 (absolute lymphocyte count 1820/mm^3)  
• Hemoglobin 9.7 g/dL  
• Platelet count 70,000/mm^3  
He was admitted with fever, pancytopenia, and splenomegaly for evaluation of possible leukemia, bone marrow infiltration, or viral suppression.  
• Viral PCR testing for enterovirus, cytomegalovirus, adenovirus, and parvovirus was negative.  
• Bone marrow biopsy showed mildly hypocellular marrow (80%), trilineage hematopoiesis, and no evidence of acute leukemia or lymphoma.  
• Targeted metabolic workup for lysosomal storage diseases (including Gaucher disease, Niemann-Pick disease types A and B, Wolman disease, mucopolysaccharidosis I, GM1 gangliosidosis, and β-mannosidosis) was negative.  
His pancytopenia transiently improved but recurred intermittently over the next several months, always normalizing spontaneously except for mild thrombocytopenia.  
At 15 months of age, he had persistent hepatosplenomegaly despite normal liver enzymes except a mildly elevated aspartate transaminase. Gastroenterology consultation was obtained for further diagnostic evaluation.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 41-year-old man presented with mandibular discomfort and pus discharge beneath the chin. Four years earlier, he had been diagnosed with mandibular osteomyelitis and underwent sequestrectomy; since then he had received antibiotics and conservative care. On examination, a fistula with purulent discharge was present in the mental region. He had no significant medical history. He exhibited macrocephaly, frontal bossing, a broad nasal bridge, and ocular hypertelorism; hearing and facial nerve function were normal. Intraorally, the maxillary teeth were very mobile with sequestra noted in the mandibular midline; a retained deciduous tooth and a few permanent teeth were present. Oral hygiene was poor, and there was an anterior crossbite with a narrow maxillary arch. 

Panoramic radiography showed decreased radiolucency on the left mandible compared with four years earlier, increased radiolucency on the right, and generalized increased radiopacity of the maxilla and mandible; several maxillary teeth were impacted. A whole-skeleton radiograph demonstrated linear sclerotic striations in the long bones, and bone scan revealed increased uptake in both the maxilla and mandible. Maxillofacial CT confirmed mandibular and maxillary sequestra and revealed marked sclerosis of the cranial base, including the Eustachian tube and internal auditory canal.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

An 11-year-old boy presented with a history of recurrent respiratory tract infections and known tuberculosis exposure in the family. On examination, he appeared in good health with no abnormal lung or heart sounds. Tuberculin skin testing was positive. Chest radiography revealed a heterogeneous consolidation in the left lower lobe. A subsequent non-contrast CT scan showed a heterogeneous consolidation with cystic masses containing mixed fluid and air in the posterior segment of the left lower lobe, with no communication to the bronchial tree. Contrast-enhanced CT angiography demonstrated an artery originating from the descending thoracic aorta that divided into two 1.2-cm branches supplying this dysplastic area. These findings suggested intralobar pulmonary sequestration with associated bronchial atresia, superinfection, and contiguous parenchymal trapping.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 26-year-old man presented with a 1-week history of a confluent red macular rash on his face, neck, and upper chest, associated with sore throat, nausea, palpitations, fever, and malaise. He had fever to 38.7 °C, tachycardia to 130 bpm with a systolic murmur, and left hypochondrial pain. Examination revealed multiple tender head-and-neck lymph nodes. The rash, which began on the neck, spread to the face and back; it was blanching and mildly pruritic, with less pronounced involvement of the arms, legs, and abdomen.  
One month earlier, he had developed right-knee monoarthritis with elevated ESR (65 mm/h), CRP (92 mg/L), and synovitis on arthrocentesis; ANA and ANCA were negative. Sulfasalazine (500 mg five times daily) was initiated but stopped by the patient after 23 days when fever and rash appeared. He had no other chronic medications or allergies.  
Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL; AST 78 U/L, ALT 224 U/L, LDH 533 U/L, GGT 132 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 145 U/L; albumin 29.4 g/L; direct bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL; ferritin 1325 µg/L; INR 1.4; CRP 78 mg/L; WBC 10.3×10^3/mm^3 with eosinophils 4.5%. He was immune to EBV, influenza, measles, rubella, hepatitis A and B; hepatitis C and E, HIV, and paracetamol toxicity were excluded. Group A strep testing was negative. Echocardiography showed pericardial effusion without endocarditis; abdominal ultrasound was unremarkable.  
A presumptive diagnosis of delayed-onset sulfasalazine toxicity was made, with consideration of Kawasaki disease, Hodgkin lymphoma, adult Still’s disease, hypereosinophilic syndrome, and various infections. He met all seven RegiSCAR criteria for DRESS syndrome. Despite withdrawal of sulfasalazine and supportive care, he developed facial and pharyngeal swelling, hypoxia, and leukocytosis (17.1×10^3/mm^3 with 11% eosinophils); hepatosplenomegaly, ascites, and pleural effusion ensued.  
CT of the chest and abdomen revealed extensive bilateral lymphadenopathy in the mediastinum, pulmonary hila, axillae, and abdomen, hepatosplenomegaly, right pleural effusion, and massive ascites. FDG-PET CT demonstrated multiple intensely hypermetabolic lymph nodes in the head, neck, and axillary regions.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 5-year-old mare was evaluated for a mass over the right distolateral femur that had been present for approximately 3 months and had slowly increased in size. Over the same period, the mare developed right hind–limb lameness of increasing severity. On examination there was a firm, smooth, subcutaneous nodular mass, 4 × 5 cm, adherent to but not ulcerating the skin. The mare resented local palpation but had no other abnormalities on general physical examination. In-hand trot on a hard surface showed a 3/5 right hind–limb lameness. Radiographs of the distal right femur were unremarkable. Ultrasonography revealed a well-defined, homogeneous subcutaneous mass with moderate echodensity. Regional lymph nodes were normal on palpation and ultrasound.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 42-year-old Asian woman presented with a 5-day history of fever and chills and a 2-day history of jaundice. Three months earlier she had undergone laparoscopic cholecystectomy for cholangitis due to gallstones. She had no family history of hemolytic disease. On examination, she was alert, temperature 39.3 °C, blood pressure 122/61 mmHg, pulse 119 beats/min, and respiration rate normal; skin and sclera were icteric, with no abdominal or costovertebral-angle tenderness. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 9.0 g/dL, reticulocyte count 25.0%, white-cell count 10,580/µL, platelets 125,000/µL; total bilirubin 10.9 mg/dL (direct 6.0 mg/dL), AST 49 IU/L, ALT 65 IU/L, ALP 441 IU/L, LDH 550 IU/L, and GGT 301 IU/L; coagulation tests were normal. Blood cultures grew Escherichia coli. Abdominal contrast CT revealed splenomegaly and a distended left kidney, with no biliary-tract obstruction. Urinalysis showed leukocyte esterase 3+, nitrite 2+, hemoglobin 3+, bilirubin 2+, and urobilinogen 3+; sediment contained white cells, bacteria, and 5–9 erythrocytes per high-power field, and urine culture grew E. coli. Further testing showed haptoglobin 8 mg/dL and negative direct and indirect Coombs tests. A peripheral blood smear revealed spherocytes.

A previously healthy 3-year-old boy from Gorakhpur was admitted with 12 days of high-grade fever, generalized tonic–clonic seizures for 6 days, and altered sensorium for 1 day. On examination, his temperature was 103°F, pulse 158/min and feeble, respirations 54/min, blood pressure 56/32 mm Hg, and Glasgow Coma Score 4. He had hepatosplenomegaly, bilateral chest rhonchi, tachypnea, tachycardia, extensor plantar responses, and diminished deep tendon reflexes. Initial laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 9.5 g/dL, normal leukocyte count, platelets 180 000/mm^3, SGPT 109 U/L, blood urea 87 mg/dL, and blood glucose 50 mg/dL. CSF analysis revealed 68 cells/mm^3 (72% lymphocytes), protein 68 mg/dL, and a normal glucose level. Given the prolonged fever with hepatosplenomegaly, a rapid diagnostic test for malaria was performed and was positive for Plasmodium vivax and P. falciparum; peripheral thick and thin smears confirmed both species with a parasitic count of 6%. Widal test, acute hepatitis viral markers, and blood cultures were negative, and chest radiography showed right-sided heterogeneous opacities. A head CT scan on day 3 was normal. The child received a 7-day course of intravenous quinine and doxycycline, but on day 8 of admission his fever persisted. A repeat peripheral smear on day 7 of antimalarial therapy was negative for malarial parasites.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 57-year-old woman with no prior cardiac history was admitted for newly diagnosed acute myeloid leukemia. On arrival, laboratory studies showed a leukocytosis of 31.9×10^9/L with an eosinophil count of 7.66×10^9/L and normocytic anemia. Baseline echocardiography demonstrated preserved biventricular function without valvular disease. She began induction chemotherapy with cytarabine and daunorubicin. Two days after starting systemic chemotherapy, she developed chest pain. She had cardiovascular risk factors of hypertension and a smoking history. An ECG showed anterior and inferior ST-segment depressions, and troponin I was elevated at 690 ng/L (normal <14 ng/L) alongside an eosinophil count of 33.3×10^9/L. Because of thrombocytopenia, she underwent cardiac magnetic resonance imaging instead of immediate catheterization. Stress perfusion CMR revealed a new left ventricular ejection fraction of 32% with global hypokinesia and extensive sub-endocardial hypoperfusion in all coronary territories. T2 mapping was at the upper limit of normal. The study was terminated early due to severe dyspnea, and no late gadolinium enhancement images were obtained. Given her risk factors and perfusion findings, a working diagnosis of severe triple-vessel CAD or vasospasm was made. Coronary angiography demonstrated no atherosclerotic disease. Despite medical therapy for heart failure, the patient’s severe LV dysfunction and troponin elevations persisted.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 56-year-old man was evaluated for diffuse abdominal pain, progressive fatigue, and painful swelling of the second and third metacarpophalangeal joints of both hands. Two years earlier, he had been diagnosed with gout involving the knees and ankles without significant response to treatment. His medical history was notable for well-controlled stage III hypertension and newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes, managed with metformin. He denied tobacco or toxin exposure and reported only occasional alcohol use; family history was noncontributory.

On examination, he had a severe bilateral Dupuytren’s contracture, symmetric gynecomastia, and hyperpigmentation of sun-exposed skin on the face and hands. The spleen was mildly enlarged, and there was tenderness in the right upper quadrant. He later admitted to a one-year history of low libido.

Hand radiographs showed juxta-articular osteopenia, large bony proliferations at the proximal interphalangeal joints, erosion of the fourth digit of the left hand, and fixed flexion deformities. Ultrasound of the hands confirmed osteophytes with a step-up lesion, a large erosion in the right hand, and signs of calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate deposition. Foot ultrasound revealed a double-contour sign at the first metatarsal head, consistent with his gout history.

Laboratory tests showed no elevation of inflammatory markers, negative rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies, slight vitamin D deficiency, and mild thrombocytopenia. Cholestatic liver enzymes (alkaline phosphatase, γ-glutamyl transferase) and total bilirubin were approximately three times the upper limit of normal. Lipid profile, reticulocyte count, and blood glucose were within normal limits.

Abdominal ultrasound demonstrated a hyperechoic, steatotic liver with nodularity, irregular margins, reduced vascular flow, and no focal lesions; the spleen measured 14 cm. FibroScan indicated stage III steatosis and grade IV fibrosis. Upper endoscopy showed mild gastritis and a Schatzki ring. Contrast CT of the abdomen revealed diffuse liver microcalcifications and a spleen of 13.7 cm.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 22-year-old Nepalese man presented with a more than 5-year history of insidious onset weakness of his right upper limb. He initially noted difficulty sustaining his grip when holding objects, which gradually progressed to impairments in fine motor tasks, including buttoning clothes, writing, combing his hair, and using utensils. Over the same period, he observed a tremor and progressive reduction in muscle bulk of his right hand. After 2 years of right-hand symptoms, he began to notice tremors in his left hand without accompanying weakness. His symptoms progressed over the first 3 years but have been static since then. He denied neck pain, sensory changes, autonomic symptoms, or bowel or bladder dysfunction. There was no history of trauma, surgery, medication use, vaccination, or toxin exposure. He is a university student working part-time and has no family history of neurologic disease.

On examination, vital signs were stable. General examination was unremarkable. Neurologic examination revealed normal cranial nerves, coordination, sensation, and gait. Motor testing showed atrophy of the right hand, most prominently in the first dorsal interosseous, with sparing of the brachioradialis. Muscle strength (Medical Research Council scale) on the right was 2/5 in the interossei, 3/5 in wrist flexion and extension, and 4/5 in elbow flexion and extension; left upper-limb strength was normal. Tone was reduced around the right elbow and wrist. Minipolymyoclonus was present in the fingers bilaterally (more pronounced on the right). Deep‐tendon reflexes were symmetrical and normal except for a brisk right supinator response. Plantar responses were flexor bilaterally, and Hoffman signs were absent. Sensation and gait were intact.

Laboratory studies, including complete blood count, renal and liver function, electrolytes, thyroid function, inflammatory markers, creatine phosphokinase, viral serologies, and autoantibodies (ANA, anti-dsDNA, ANCA), were within normal limits. A cervical spine radiograph was unremarkable. Nerve conduction studies demonstrated motor axonal neuropathy with reduced compound motor action potentials in the right median and ulnar nerves; findings were normal on the left. Needle EMG showed neurogenic discharges with fibrillations and fasciculations in bilateral first dorsal interossei and the right biceps and triceps. Cervical spine MRI, including flexion T1-weighted contrast and neutral T2-weighted sequences, showed no dural enhancement, no anterior displacement of the posterior dural sac, and no cord atrophy or signal abnormality. A prior MRI performed 2 years earlier was also normal.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 50-year-old man with no known past medical history was transferred to the ICU for organ donation after the declaration of brain death. Eight days earlier, he had undergone decompressive craniectomy and hematoma evacuation for spontaneous intracerebral and intraventricular hemorrhage, followed by a second surgery for rebleeding. On ICU admission he was apneic, his pupils were fixed and dilated at 7 mm, and his vital signs were: blood pressure 75/50 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm, and pulse oximetry 75% on room air. Dopamine infusion (15 µg/kg/min) was started, and mechanical ventilation was initiated with FiO₂ 1.0, PIP 26 cmH₂O, PEEP 10 cmH₂O, and RR 20/min. Generalized edema was noted, and pink frothy secretions were suctioned from the endotracheal tube; bilateral crackles were heard on auscultation. Chest radiography demonstrated bilateral alveolar infiltrates. Transthoracic echocardiography showed concentric left ventricular hypertrophy with preserved contractility and no pulmonary hypertension. The first arterial blood gas on FiO₂ 1.0 revealed pH 7.31, PaCO₂ 40.5 mmHg, PaO₂ 84.9 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 19.9 mEq/L, and SaO₂ 94.3%. Besides the central nervous system insult, there was no other existing cause for pulmonary edema such as heart failure, pneumonia, or pulmonary embolism.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 32-year-old Persian woman presented with 3 weeks of progressive abdominal pain. A colonoscopy performed elsewhere revealed a cecal mass and a presumptive diagnosis of colon cancer. She was referred with new‐onset ascites, dyspnea, weakness, and malaise. Her medical history was unremarkable, with no medications or family history of malignancy.  
On examination, she was tachycardic, tachypneic, and hypoxic; breath sounds were decreased at both lung bases without rales. Venous blood gas showed pH 7.31, PCO2 21.6 mm Hg, and PO2 98 mm Hg.  
Laboratory studies on admission revealed: WBC 7.1 × 10^3/μL, hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL, platelets 91 × 10^3/mm^3; serum creatinine 5.6 mg/dL, potassium 5.6 mEq/L, calcium 10.9 mg/dL, and uric acid 18.1 mg/dL.  
Contrast‐enhanced CT of the chest and abdomen showed a cecal mass, omental infiltration, hypodense lesions in the right hepatic lobe, and bilateral pleural effusions.  
Thoracentesis yielded 400 mL of sanguineous fluid containing 11,200 WBCs (80% neutrophils), pleural fluid LDH 15,930 U/L (serum LDH 9900 U/L).  
Her electrolyte abnormalities (hyperuricemia, hyperphosphatemia, hyperkalemia) met the Cairo and Bishop criteria for tumor lysis syndrome, but the presence of hypercalcemia and very high LDH in pleural fluid was considered atypical for TLS arising from a solid tumor.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 13-year-old previously healthy boy presented with three weeks of mild dyspnea, chest pain on exertion, and a newly detected cardiac murmur. He denied syncope, palpitations, fever, weight loss, recent infections, and had no history of central lines or tattoos. Family history was negative for congenital heart disease, cardiomyopathy, sudden death, or thrombophilia.

On examination, he was afebrile, heart rate 95 bpm, blood pressure 96/72 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 100%. He had a grade 3/6 systolic murmur at the left upper sternal border, normal S1 and S2, no gallops or rubs, and mild hepatomegaly. Peripheral pulses were normal. 

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 12.9 g/dL, aspartate aminotransferase 43 U/L, normal renal function, coagulation profile, C-reactive protein, and troponin, but an elevated NT-proBNP of 4019 pg/mL. Chest radiograph was unremarkable. 

Electrocardiogram demonstrated sinus rhythm, right bundle-branch block with repolarization abnormalities in the right precordial leads, and isolated monomorphic premature ventricular contractions often in a bigeminy pattern. 

Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a 3.5 × 4.0 cm rounded, lobulated mass with clear margins and inhomogeneous content, attached by a pedicle to the infundibular septum and causing severe obstruction of the right ventricular outflow tract with a peak gradient of 80 mmHg. The right atrium and right ventricle were dilated, with severe right ventricular systolic dysfunction (fractional area change 18.6%). 

Contrast-enhanced computed tomography confirmed a heterogeneously enhancing soft-tissue mass occupying the right ventricular outflow tract without evidence of pulmonary embolism.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 70-year-old man with a history of clear cell renal cell carcinoma treated by nephrectomy and multiple prior lines of therapy for spinal metastases was started on axitinib 5 mg twice daily for progressive disease. Ten days later, he reported severe fatigue; a complete blood count showed a platelet count of 4 × 10^9/L (baseline 245 × 10^9/L). Axitinib was discontinued, and he was admitted for evaluation. Viral serologies (hepatitis, CMV, HIV) were negative; renal and hepatic function were normal. Prothrombin time, INR, activated partial thromboplastin time, fibrinogen, and D-dimer levels were normal. Direct and indirect Coombs tests were negative. ADAMTS13 activity was 42.5% (normal > 10%). The serotonin-release assay was normal (<1). Abdominal ultrasound demonstrated a spleen of normal size. Bone marrow biopsy was normocellular, without metastatic disease or increased blasts, and showed a normal proportion of megakaryocytes. He received six units of platelet transfusions, but the platelet count remained between 12 and 15 × 10^9/L.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 31-year-old man with no history of atopy or chronic illness presented with sudden-onset, intensely pruritic skin lesions. He denied fevers, arthralgias, muscle weakness, or recent medication use. On examination, there were erythematous to violaceous linear streaks in a flagellate pattern symmetrically over his arms and legs, coalescing over his chest and scalp. Discrete pustules were noted on his trunk and in intertriginous areas; there was no mucosal involvement. He reported eating partially cooked shiitake mushrooms 10 days before presentation and consuming raw shiitake mushrooms 6 days later. The rash appeared 24 hours after the raw-mushroom exposure. Complete blood count and liver and renal function tests were within normal limits. A skin biopsy showed follicular sterile pustules with a mild mixed perivascular inflammatory infiltrate.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 42‐year‐old woman living on Reunion Island, with no significant medical history, presented after 3 weeks of a persistent, generalized rash. Initially, she noted a pruritic maculopapular eruption on her lower limbs, later extending to her trunk and upper extremities but sparing the palms. She denied fever or lymphadenopathy. Over the preceding 48 hours, she also developed a painful, well‐demarcated, inflammatory nodule on her right forearm, clinically consistent with erythema nodosum.

On examination, vital signs were normal. Skin examination confirmed widespread maculopapular lesions on the legs and a tender, erythematous subcutaneous nodule on the right forearm. No mucosal lesions were seen.

Two skin biopsies were obtained: one from a papular lesion on the left leg and one from the forearm nodule. Pending results, and with a working diagnosis of cutaneous sarcoidosis, topical dermocorticoids were started.

Pathology of the leg biopsy showed granulomatous dermatitis with a lichenoid infiltrate, and the arm biopsy confirmed septal panniculitis consistent with erythema nodosum. Despite topical steroids, her skin lesions showed no improvement.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 16-year-old male presented to the emergency department after losing consciousness during a school test. He described a brief prodrome of dizziness, and witnesses noted tonic–clonic movements during the event. His history included febrile seizures and parieto-occipital epilepsy in childhood, but he had been seizure-free and off medication for over a year. He also had mild cognitive impairment and cognitive visual dysfunction; family history was unremarkable.

While under evaluation, he experienced a second syncopal episode with spontaneous recovery. On examination, a cardiologist noted an irregular pulse and obtained a 12-lead ECG, which showed sinus rhythm, widespread J waves, a normal QT interval, and frequent monomorphic premature ventricular complexes with right bundle–branch-block morphology. Continuous telemetry captured frequent PVCs that degenerated into polymorphic ventricular tachycardia and ventricular fibrillation, requiring six defibrillation shocks over one hour. After intravenous magnesium, potassium, and a labetalol infusion, both PVC frequency and J-wave amplitude decreased.

Physical examination was otherwise normal, with a body temperature of 36.8 °C. Laboratory tests including electrolytes, acid–base status, thyroid function, and drug screening were unremarkable; free carnitine was slightly decreased and alanine slightly elevated (both deemed non-significant). Lactate and pyruvate levels were normal, and immune and metabolic screens were negative. Transthoracic echocardiography, cardiac magnetic resonance imaging, and brain MRI (including pituitary) were normal. A treadmill stress test provoked no arrhythmias.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 40-year-old man with well-controlled hypertension presented in Pemba, Mozambique, with a 2-day history of fever, chills, malaise, general weakness and prostration. He denied headache, arthralgia or myalgia at onset. On admission, temperature was 38.2 °C, blood pressure 110/75 mmHg, and a diffuse, undifferentiated rash was noted without hemorrhages or edema. A rapid diagnostic test for Plasmodium falciparum was negative. Laboratory studies showed: white blood cell count 15.7 × 10^3/mm^3 (lymphocytes 6.9 × 10^3/mm^3), hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, platelets 33 × 10^3/mm^3, alanine aminotransferase 245 U/L, glucose 9.11 mmol/L, and urea 14.2 mmol/L. No blood cultures were available. He was started on ceftriaxone, ciprofloxacin and ampicillin. Over the next two days, fever persisted and he developed headache, dizziness, symmetrical polyarthralgia and melaena. On hospital day 2, hemoglobin fell to 6.2 g/dL and platelets rose to 56 × 10^3/mm^3. On day 3 he was transferred to the ICU and received three units of whole blood. On day 4 he became comatose. Repeat malaria tests remained negative, and urinalysis was unremarkable. Broad-spectrum antibiotics were continued without improvement.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

Ms L is a 47-year-old woman referred for respiratory assessment and flight testing because of recurrent, predictable symptoms occurring only during airplane flights. She had flown in the past without incident but, beginning in 2003, developed on five separate flights a sudden, severe, left-sided, stabbing pleuritic chest pain radiating to her back, associated with a severe tightness across her forehead and painless left arm weakness preventing her from lifting a cup. There were no facial weakness, dysphasia, visual disturbances, or arm discoloration. Symptoms began as the airplane reached cruising altitude (flights lasted 2.5–4.5 hours) and fully resolved on descent; forehead tightness occurred in only one episode, while chest pain and arm weakness were always present. She did not receive oxygen or other treatment in flight. Medical history included eczema, treated depression (fluoxetine), mild childhood asthma, and social smoking (one cigarette per month); she denied anxiety attacks or claustrophobia. Family and occupational histories were noncontributory. On examination, respiratory, cardiovascular, and neurological findings were normal; oxygen saturation was 100% on room air and did not change with posture or exertion. A chest radiograph showed a subtle reduction of vascular markings in the left mid and upper zone. CT pulmonary angiography revealed a solitary left apical bulla measuring 10×8×8 cm and mild peripheral bronchiectasis in the middle and right upper lobes. A head MRI was normal. Pulmonary function tests showed normal spirometry, normal lung volumes by helium dilution, and a normal transfer factor.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 25-year-old man with no prior medical history presented 3 days after the sudden onset of involuntary motor tics and vocalizations. He initially developed facial twitches and left-sided winking up to 12 times per minute, followed by whistling sounds. He denied initial stressors but later disclosed a marital separation four months earlier, his daughter’s move out of the home, and that he had entered his daughter’s room—his first return since the separation—one week before tic onset. He also noted a former coworker with Tourette’s syndrome.

Review of systems was otherwise negative. On examination, vital signs were normal. Neurologic evaluation showed intact cranial nerves II–XII, full symmetric strength, normal reflexes, sensation, coordination, and gait. During the exam, he exhibited frequent involuntary facial movements, left-sided hemifacial spasms, blinking, grimacing, clicking, and whistling. Psychiatric assessment revealed clear and fluent speech without suicidal or homicidal ideation. On further probing he endorsed increased jumpiness, decreased sleep, persistent ruminations, and an impending court date for alleged domestic abuse. His Patient Health Questionnaire-9 score was 4 and his Generalized Anxiety Disorder-7 score was 15.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

An 81-year-old woman (ASA class III) with chronic kidney disease stage III and anxiety managed with sertraline underwent resection of a recurrent retroperitoneal liposarcoma. Preoperatively, an epidural catheter with 0.125% bupivacaine was placed. She received general anesthesia with isoflurane, 300 µg fentanyl, 0.4 mg hydromorphone, 53.35 mg ketamine, and cisatracurium for intubation; a phenylephrine infusion maintained her blood pressure during the 7-hour procedure. She was extubated and transferred to recovery. Thirty minutes later, she became unresponsive with rigidity and extensor posturing, prompting a code stroke. Examination revealed fixed, pinpoint pupils, and she received three 0.04 mg doses of naloxone, after which she became fully responsive. A head CT scan was unremarkable. Two hours later, she again became rigid and unresponsive, and the epidural infusion was discontinued. On examination, blood pressure was 185/93 mm Hg, heart rate 66 bpm, respiratory rate 13 breaths/min, temperature 36.5 °C, and oxygen saturation 95–100% on 2 L nasal cannula. Laboratory studies showed glucose 227 mg/dL, sodium 134 mmol/L, potassium 4 mmol/L, creatinine 0.76 mg/dL, lactic acid 2.3 mmol/L, with other electrolytes, blood counts, and arterial blood gas values within or near reference ranges. She had a blank stare, pupils 1–2 mm slowly reactive, no nystagmus or eye deviation, a clenched jaw, sustained extensor posturing of all extremities, was nonverbal, followed minimal commands, had no withdrawal to pain, and exhibited no clonus.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 53-year-old woman with no significant medical history presented with a 2-week history of headache and vertigo. She was alert and oriented, with mild truncal ataxia and gait unsteadiness; nystagmus was absent. Noncontrast CT of the head demonstrated a hyperdense 30 × 31 × 36 mm mass in the anteromedial cerebellar hemispheres adjacent to the medial tentorium cerebelli, with mild ventriculomegaly. Contrast-enhanced CT showed the lesion abutting the lower surface of the medial tentorium between the vein of the cerebellomesencephalic fissure and the declival vein and compressing the dorsal brainstem, causing aqueductal stenosis. With a presumptive diagnosis of tentorial meningioma, she was scheduled for MRI 10 days later. By that time, her symptoms had worsened, and she required assistance to walk. MRI revealed an extra-axial tumor with broad attachment to the medial tentorium, hypointense on T1-weighted images, mixed intensity on T2- and diffusion-weighted images with corresponding hypointensity on the ADC map, extensive perilesional edema, and intense gadolinium enhancement measuring 40 × 41 × 46 mm. Cerebral angiography showed no obvious feeding vessels or tumor blush from the internal carotid, external carotid, or vertebrobasilar systems. The serum level of soluble interleukin-2 receptor was 470 U/mL (normal range, 157–474 U/mL).

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 53-year-old Afro-Caribbean woman with no history of cardiac disease presented with 3 weeks of confusion, anorexia, a 20-lb weight loss, and fatigue. She denied fever, headache, chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, or edema. In the emergency department her blood pressure was 92/52 mm Hg, heart rate 129 beats/min, and she was lethargic but without focal neurologic deficits. Examination revealed small, painless supraclavicular and inguinal lymph nodes; heart and lung exams were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed a white-blood-cell count of 88 000/μL, creatinine 4.8 mg/dL, and corrected calcium 21 mg/dL. Peripheral smear demonstrated atypical lymphocytes. Additional studies were consistent with tumor lysis syndrome. Imaging revealed diffuse lytic bone lesions and enlarged lymph nodes. Bone marrow biopsy and flow cytometry confirmed a T-cell clonal neoplasm, and HTLV-1 serology was positive, establishing acute adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma (ATLL). A baseline transthoracic echocardiogram showed normal left ventricular size and function, with an ejection fraction of 65%.

After stabilization of tumor lysis syndrome, she was started on inpatient EPOCH chemotherapy: continuous infusions of etoposide, vincristine, and doxorubicin, followed by cyclophosphamide and prednisone. Ten hours into the infusion of doxorubicin, etoposide, and vincristine, she developed acute hypoxia (70% on room air), tachycardia (152/min), and tachypnea (40/min), prompting endotracheal intubation and discontinuation of chemotherapy.

An electrocardiogram showed new nonspecific T-wave inversions in leads V2-V3. A bedside echocardiogram revealed apical ballooning with severe hypokinesis of the anterior and anteroseptal walls, mild hypokinesis of other walls, and an estimated ejection fraction of 20%. Chest radiography demonstrated pulmonary edema and an enlarged cardiac silhouette. N-terminal pro–B-type natriuretic peptide was 7000 pg/mL (normal <125 pg/mL), and peak troponin was 0.7 ng/mL (normal <0.01 ng/mL). 

She was started on intravenous dobutamine for hemodynamic support.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

An 87-year-old man from Isfahan presented with asymptomatic, progressive facial lesions of 3 years’ duration. He noted onset after an overnight outdoor camping trip; his son concurrently developed a leishmanial nodule on his leg. Lesions gradually extended over the nose, cheeks, upper lip, and periorbital areas, with confluent erythematous plaques, induration, ulceration, crusting, and purulent discharge compromising vision. Routine blood tests and chest radiography were normal. PPD was negative. Over 3 years, multiple skin biopsies showed pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia and mixed inflammatory infiltrates with occasional noncaseating granulomas; special stains and cultures for fungi and mycobacteria, Giemsa smears, and PCR for Leishmania were repeatedly negative. Empiric courses of antibiotics, antifungals, intralesional antimonials, antituberculous therapy, and systemic corticosteroids produced no durable improvement. The most recent biopsy of the upper lip lesion revealed actinic cheilitis. In our clinic, three punch biopsies again showed noncaseating tuberculoid granulomas without identifiable organisms. A nasal skin biopsy demonstrated prominent epidermal hyperplasia with cellular atypia, increased mitoses, invasion of squamous nests, and keratin pearls, consistent with well-differentiated squamous cell carcinoma. Staging revealed no metastases. Culture of purulent discharge grew Staphylococcus aureus, but a 10-day course of cloxacillin yielded no significant change; botryomycosis was excluded due to antibiotic nonresponse. Otolaryngology and oncology consultations were obtained, and a surgical plan for SCC was scheduled.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 63-year-old obese African American woman with asthma, hypertension, congestive heart failure, obstructive sleep apnea, and obesity presented with sudden onset of bilateral facial and tongue swelling and progressive shortness of breath. She had minor fruit allergies but no history of anaphylaxis. Initial vital signs were blood pressure 127/67 mmHg, pulse 98 beats/minute, respiratory rate 18 breaths/minute, and oxygen saturation 100% on 6 L nasal cannula. She received epinephrine, diphenhydramine, and solumedrol for presumed anaphylaxis and was intubated for airway protection. On examination, there was extensive laryngeal edema. The right pupil was dilated and unresponsive; the left pupil was pinpoint but reactive. Emergent head CT showed bilateral effaced sulci with small ventricles and narrowed basilar cisterns, consistent with increased intracranial pressure. Initial arterial blood gas analysis on controlled ventilation (tidal volume 600 mL, respiratory rate 12, FiO2 50%) revealed pH 7.22, pCO2 85 mmHg, pO2 109 mmHg, and bicarbonate 33 mEq/L. A neurosurgeon recommended no surgical intervention. Mannitol was not administered, as there were no clinical or laboratory signs of an osmotic disturbance. The patient remained unresponsive with a dilated right pupil and blurred disc margins bilaterally.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

options.A: Brain tumor with increased intracranial pressure

question:
An 18-year-old man presented for a routine dental examination. He was the first child of healthy, unrelated parents; prenatal history was unremarkable. Developmental milestones, growth, and intelligence were normal.

Physical examination showed facial asymmetry with left malar and mandibular hypoplasia and deviation of the chin to the left. Intraoral findings included a high-arched palate, palatally erupted maxillary left second premolar, ankyloglossia with frenulum attachment to the mandible, and oligodontia (congenital absence of multiple maxillary and mandibular teeth). Surgical release of the frenulum restored normal tongue function and speech.

Panoramic radiograph confirmed reduced height of the left mandible, a supernumerary tooth between the maxillary left premolars, bifurcated styloid processes bilaterally, and multiple missing teeth. Study models demonstrated a maxillary high-arched palate and a reduced mandibular alveolar ridge on the left.

Examination of the limbs revealed bilateral rudimentary thumbs attached to the distal radius, absence of most carpal, metacarpal, and phalangeal bones except two carpals on the left (one fused), and complete absence of these bones on the right. Both forefeet were absent; a rudimentary toe was present on the right. Radiographs showed only two tarsals on the left with absent metatarsals and phalanges, and on the right four deformed tarsals, a rudimentary first metatarsal, and a single phalanx.

Skull and chest radiographs were normal. Abdominal ultrasonography showed normal kidneys and other organs. Chromosomal analysis revealed a 46,XY karyotype. Sequencing of GLI3 showed no mutation, and analysis of LMBR1 revealed no deletions or point mutations.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 61-year-old woman presented with a 5-year history of discomfort and a gradually enlarging lump at the base of her right thumb and thenar eminence. Her medical history included osteoarthritis of the right thumb, hypertension, and hypercholesterolemia; she was an ex-smoker with moderate alcohol use. Family history was noncontributory.

On examination, there was a mildly tender, circumferential mass extending from the thumb base to the proximal phalanx with no movement at the metacarpophalangeal or interphalangeal joints; neurovascular function was intact.

A radiograph of the right thumb showed soft-tissue swelling over the dorsolateral aspect without bony scalloping or erosion and degenerative changes at the metacarpophalangeal joint. Ultrasound revealed a multilobulated hypoechoic lesion up to 6 cm surrounding the flexor pollicis longus tendon. MRI demonstrated a 4.6 × 2.9 × 6.7 cm multilobulated mass encasing the flexor pollicis longus tendon and surrounding the first proximal phalanx, metacarpophalangeal joint, and first metacarpal, with intermediate T1 signal, T2 hyperintensity, and heterogeneous postcontrast enhancement.

An ultrasound-guided biopsy showed an atypical cartilaginous lesion with mild-to-moderate cytological atypia abutting bone but without bony infiltration; high-grade malignant features were not identified.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

options.C: Giant cell tumor of the tendon sheath

question:
A 50-year-old Chinese woman with a 9-month history of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) was admitted for fever and relapsing abdominal pain over the same period. Initial SLE had been diagnosed on the basis of fever, recurrent abdominal pain, bloating, leukopenia, positive antinuclear and anti–double-stranded DNA antibodies, anti-Smith and anti-RNP antibodies, and polyserositis, and responded to prednisolone and mycophenolate mofetil. As steroids were tapered, she redeveloped fever (38.5°C), abdominal pain, hair loss, dry mouth, and reduced defecation; she denied photosensitivity, oral ulcers, arthralgias, or Raynaud’s phenomenon. Examination was notable only for tenderness in the right lower quadrant. Her history included osteoporosis and a tuberculous infection treated with rifampicin and isoniazid for 3 months without relief of abdominal pain. Laboratory studies showed leukopenia (WBC 2.56 × 10^9/L), anemia (hemoglobin 102 g/L), normal ESR, CRP, and procalcitonin, and negative blood cultures. Immunologic tests revealed rheumatoid factor 153 IU/mL, low complement C3 (0.71 g/L), positive ANA (1:320), and positive anti-Smith, anti-dsDNA, and anti-RNP antibodies; a TB-spot test was positive. A PET-CT scan showed no abnormalities. After exclusion of infection, intestinal tuberculosis, and malignancy, SLE-related mesenteric vasculitis and intestinal obstruction were considered. Treatment with methylprednisolone (120 mg/day for 11 days), cyclophosphamide, gastrointestinal decompression, and nutritional support resolved her fever and normalized her WBC count, but her incomplete intestinal obstruction and decreased bowel movements persisted. An abdominal X-ray showed multiple air-fluid levels. Enteroscopy demonstrated a small intestinal ulcer with luminal stenosis. Contrast CT of the small intestine revealed segmental dilatation with wall enhancement and multiple air-fluid levels.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 59-year-old man was followed for mild bilateral renal cystic disease when surveillance imaging showed interval enlargement of an endophytic right interpolar Bosniak III cyst measuring 1.9 × 2.0 × 1.6 cm. He was asymptomatic, with no hematuria or flank pain. His medical history included hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type II diabetes, and gout; his baseline glomerular filtration rate was 77 mL/min. He had a 22–pack-year smoking history but no family history of genitourinary malignancy. Laboratory studies were normal. He elected to undergo a robot-assisted right partial nephrectomy without complication. 

Gross examination revealed a well-circumscribed renal mass composed of multiple cysts ranging from closely packed small tubules to larger simple cysts within fibrous stroma; focal papillary architecture was noted. On light microscopy, the cysts were lined by predominantly a single layer—and occasionally multiple layers—of cells with variable eosinophilic cytoplasm resembling oncocytic cells. In some areas, cells exhibited a hobnail appearance and focal intracytoplasmic vacuoles. Nucleoli were prominent (grade 3). Ovarian-type stroma was absent.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 56-year-old man from Sri Lanka presented with 3 weeks of high-grade fevers, drenching night sweats, anorexia, and a 6-kg weight loss. He denied cough, bowel changes, headache, or known tuberculosis exposure. His past medical history was unremarkable. On examination, he was febrile (39.8 °C), mildly pale, and had soft, nontender hepatosplenomegaly without lymphadenopathy or meningism. Blood pressure was 100/60 mmHg; other systems were normal.

Laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 7.6 g/dL, leukocytes 840/μL (neutrophils 640/μL), platelets 112×10^3/μL, C-reactive protein 206 mg/L, and ESR 135 mm/h. Blood and urine cultures were sterile. Chest radiograph was normal. Renal function and coagulation profile were within normal limits. Serum albumin was 26 g/L. Liver enzymes were elevated (ALT 318 U/L, AST 444 U/L), with normal bilirubin. LDH was 1620 U/L. CSF analysis was unremarkable. Serologies for melioidosis, hepatitis, HIV, and a malaria smear were negative. Mantoux test was negative. Ultrasound and contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis showed only mild hepatosplenomegaly and no lymphadenopathy or lesions. 

He was treated empirically with meropenem and teicoplanin but remained febrile with worsening cytopenias. A bone marrow biopsy was obtained.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman with a history of intermittent headaches and a half–pack-per-day smoking habit presented with subacute intermittent right-sided hemi–body numbness and recurrent transient word-finding difficulties. She had an intrauterine device, was not taking oral contraceptives, and reported multiple sexual partners; her last syphilis screen 6 years earlier had been negative. On arrival, her blood pressure was 138/50 mm Hg; during hospitalization it ranged from the 90s to the 130s systolic. Neurologic examination revealed expressive aphasia, dysarthria, and decreased fine-touch sensation over the right forearm. 

Noncontrast head CT showed ill-defined hypodensities in the left posterior parietal lobe with hemorrhagic transformation and additional hypodensities in the left frontal lobe. CT angiography demonstrated focal moderate-to-severe stenosis of the left M1 segment of the middle cerebral artery. Brain MRI with and without contrast revealed numerous diffusion-restricted foci involving both white and gray matter of the left cerebral hemisphere, with correlating FLAIR hyperintensity suggestive of acute to subacute infarcts, some with hemorrhagic transformation; a left basal ganglia focal enhancing lesion with T1 hyperintensity and no diffusion restriction was also noted. 

Routine stroke workup, including transthoracic echocardiography with bubble study, 48-hour Holter monitoring, and laboratory tests (CBC with differential, serum electrolytes, CRP, ESR, and liver function tests), was normal. Given her young age and unusual presentation, vasculopathy versus vasculitis was suspected; HIV and Lyme disease antibody screening, antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies (ANCA), proteinase 3 (PR3), myeloperoxidase (MPO), and anti–double-stranded DNA antibodies were all negative. She was started on verapamil for concern of reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS). 

Diagnostic cerebral angiography showed a distal left M1 segment stenosis with partial improvement in caliber after administration of 10 mg of intra-arterial verapamil.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A middle-aged man with no past medical history presented with a 2-month history of left-sided sciatica-like pain progressing to lower back discomfort, without bowel or bladder symptoms. HIV testing was negative. CT of the lumbar spine showed a lytic lesion in the left half of the L2 vertebral body and pedicle. MRI demonstrated a T1 hypointense, T2 isointense, contrast-enhancing lesion of the left half of the L2 vertebral body and pedicle. He underwent posterior spinal fixation with lateral extracavitary approach cage fusion and decompression. Intraoperatively, the lesion was fleshy, fibrous, and highly vascular. Histopathology revealed plasmacytoma; immunophenotyping was CD138 positive with a high MIB-1 index. Bone marrow aspirate showed no increased plasma cells, no clonality, and normal cytogenetics. Serum protein electrophoresis and serum-free light chains were negative for paraprotein or light-chain excess; the blood count was normal, and myeloid markers were negative. He received 45 Gy in 25 fractions to the L2 region, with complete pain resolution and stable implant positioning on follow-up imaging.

Six months after radiotherapy (8 months after the initial diagnosis), he developed acute numbness and weakness of both lower limbs over 2 days, accompanied by bladder dysfunction. Examination indicated a T6 myelopathy. CT of the thoracic spine revealed a lytic lesion with collapse of the T4 vertebral body. MRI showed a T1 hypointense, T2 isointense, contrast-enhancing T4 vertebral lesion with collapse and an anterior epidural soft-tissue mass compressing the spinal cord; additional contrast-enhancing lesions were seen in the pelvic bones and ribs. Repeat myeloma workup remained negative. Due to progressive neurologic deficits, he underwent posterior spinal fixation with lateral extracavitary approach cage fusion and decompression at T4. The lesion appeared similarly fleshy, fibrous, and highly vascular. Biopsy of the T4 lesion was interpreted as plasmacytoma by one center, whereas a second center reported a malignant lymphoproliferative disorder with B-cell predominance. Immunohistochemical stains were positive for CD138, CD20, and CD3; negative for CD56; and showed a high MIB-1 labeling index. Comparison with the original L2 specimen demonstrated identical histological and immunophenotypic features.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

An 81-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer and newly diagnosed acute myeloid leukemia, managed palliatively, presented with bilateral painful skin lesions of the thumbs. On admission she was febrile (38.2 °C). Examination revealed well-demarcated, tender, violaceous plaques on the dorsal and palmar surfaces of both thumbs, with pus at the left thumbnail base. Laboratory studies showed a white blood cell count of 19.8 × 10^9/L and a C-reactive protein level of 121 mg/L. She was started on intravenous clindamycin, acyclovir, and fluconazole. Over the next 2 days, the plaques extended to the bases of both thumbs and both index fingers. Wound swabs yielded no growth at 40 hours. Orthopaedic debridement removed necrotic fat tissue; histology demonstrated haemorrhage with fibrinopurulent exudate, florid reactive atypia and epidermal hyperplasia, and an acute inflammatory infiltrate without neoplastic cells. Subsequently, the lesions evolved into haemorrhagic bullae and new lesions appeared on the little fingers. Nonadjacent digit involvement and failure to respond to antibiotics suggested a noninfectious process.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?
